[F01B 31/04] Means for equalising torque in reciprocating-piston machines or engines -- This subgroup covers mechanisms that balance or equalise torque fluctuations in reciprocating-piston machines or engines, such as flywheels, balancing gears or torque converters, to smooth power delivery and reduce vibration.
[F01B 3/105] by moving the swash plate in a direction perpendicular to the axis of rotation of the cylinder barrel -- The F01B 3/105 subclass covers reciprocating-piston machines whose swash plate is actuated in a direction transverse to the cylinder barrel's rotational axis, thereby varying the piston stroke or timing. It is part of the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for engines or pumps, specifically positive-displacement machines such as steam engines.
[F01B 3/106] by changing the inclination of the swash plate -- F01B 3/106 covers reciprocating-piston machines whose cylinder axes are coaxial with, parallel to, or inclined from the main shaft and that vary performance by altering the inclination angle of a swash plate. The classification applies to engines and pumps that use a tilted swash plate to change piston stroke length, thereby adjusting displacement or power output. This mechanism is common in axial-piston hydraulic pumps and certain variable-displacement steam or internal-combustion engines.
[F01B 31/06] Means for compensating relative expansion of component parts -- This subclass covers devices or arrangements that allow parts of a machine or engine to accommodate differential thermal or load-induced expansion, such as flexible couplings, expansion joints, or adjustable brackets. It applies to any component in engines or pumps where relative movement must be managed to maintain alignment and seal integrity.
[F01B 3/107] using wedges -- F01B 3/107 classifies the use of wedges in reciprocating-piston machines or engines whose cylinder axes are coaxial, parallel or inclined to the main shaft, typically to modify stroke, timing or force transmission. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and positive-displacement engine types such as steam engines.
[F01B 3/108] by turning the swash plate (with fixed inclination) -- This subgroup covers reciprocating-piston engines or machines where the driving element is a swash plate whose angle is fixed and does not vary during operation; the plate rotates with the output shaft to impart the piston motion. It is used in positive-displacement engines such as axial-piston pumps or certain steam engines.
[F01B 31/08] Cooling of steam engines -- F01B 31/08 relates to the cooling of steam engines, encompassing condensers, cooling water passages, radiators and other heat-removal arrangements that are not covered in other groups. It addresses component parts and accessories designed to dissipate engine heat and maintain operating temperature in general or positive-displacement steam engines.
[F01B 3/109] by changing the inclination of the axis of the cylinder barrel relative to the swash plate -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[F01B 31/10] Lubricating arrangements of steam engines -- The subclass F01B 31/10 covers devices, methods and accessories that provide lubrication within steam-engine systems, such as oil galleries, drip-feed or spray lubricators, and automatic systems for feeding oil to cylinders, pistons, bearings and valve gear to minimise wear. It pertains specifically to component parts and arrangements dedicated to lubrication in positive-displacement steam engines.
[F01B 31/12] Arrangements of measuring or indicating devices -- F01B 31/12 covers the design and layout of instruments and displays that monitor or indicate operating parameters of engines or pumps, such as pressure gauges, temperature sensors, speedometers, and their mounting or integration within the machine. It applies to any arrangement of these devices on general or positive-displacement engines, regardless of the specific type of engine.
[F01B 31/14] Changing of compression ratio -- F01B 31/14 covers devices, components or methods for varying or adjusting the compression ratio of reciprocating engines, such as mechanisms that alter cylinder volume, piston stroke or combustion chamber shape, allowing the ratio to be changed during operation or between runs.
[F01B 31/16] Silencers specially adapted for steam engines -- F01B 31/16 designates silencers that are specifically designed for use with steam engines, intended to attenuate the acoustic emissions from the engine's exhaust or steam discharge. These devices are considered accessory components distinct from the main engine structure and are covered under the mechanical engineering subclass for positive-displacement machines.
[F01B 31/18] Draining -- F01B 31/18 covers the drainage features of positive-displacement machines such as steam, internal-combustion or hydraulic engines. It includes drains, pipelines, valves and related components used to remove condensate, oil, water or other fluids from the machine housing or crankcase. The subclass is used for accessories that are not otherwise classified in the main engine groups.
[F01B 31/20] of cylinders -- This CPC subgroup concerns the design, construction and arrangement of cylinders in positive-displacement engines such as steam or internal-combustion types. It includes cylinder components, details or accessories not covered elsewhere, such as heads, liners, cooling passages and mounting features, and falls under mechanical engineering → engines or pumps → general positive-displacement machines.
[F01B 31/22] Idling devices, e.g. having by-passing valves -- The F01B 31/22 subclass covers idling devices for reciprocating piston engines, such as mechanisms that introduce by-passing valves to allow the engine to run at low speed or no-load conditions. These devices divert fluid or steam around the working chambers, providing controlled idle operation and reducing wear while the engine remains ready for full power.
[F01B 31/24] Disengagement of connections between pistons and main shafts -- F01B 31/24 covers mechanisms that allow the piston to be separated from the main driving shaft, such as couplings, clutches or release devices used in reciprocating engines and similar machines. It includes designs for disengaging the piston during start-up, shutdown or fault conditions and related accessories not covered elsewhere.
[F01B 31/26] Other component parts, details, or accessories, peculiar to steam engines -- A subclass that groups specialised parts, details and accessories that are unique to steam-engine construction, such as particular valves, condensers, feedwater devices and other elements not covered by broader groups. It captures components whose design or function is peculiar to steam-driven machines.
[F01B 31/28] Cylinders or cylinder covers -- F01B 31/28 covers the design and construction of cylinders and their protective covers in positive-displacement engines such as steam or internal-combustion engines. It includes details of the cylinder barrel, liner, piston housing and any removable or fixed cover that encloses the cylinder to contain working fluid and maintain alignment.
[F01B 31/30] Arrangements of steam conduits -- The F01B 31/30 class covers the design and layout of steam-conduit systems in steam-driven machines, including the routing of pipes, headers and passages that carry steam from the boiler to the engine's cylinders or other work-producing elements. It addresses the overall arrangement, grouping, and interconnection of these steam lines to optimise flow, efficiency and maintenance access.
[F01B 31/32] Arrangements or adaptations of vacuum breakers -- F01B 31/32 covers the design, mounting and integration of vacuum-breaker devices that protect steam-engine or pump systems from vacuum formation, including relief valves, condensate-return arrangements and related adaptations. It is a component-level subclass within mechanical-engineering categories for engines or pumps, specifically for parts not classified elsewhere.
[F01B 31/34] Safety means against water hammer or against the penetration of water -- The F01B 31/34 entry classifies safety devices that protect engines, pumps or related machinery from water-hammer shocks and from water ingress. It covers protective valves, cushions, surge absorbers and other accessories designed to mitigate sudden pressure surges in piping or to stop water from penetrating the equipment.
[F01B 31/36] automatically cutting-off steam supply -- F01B 31/36 relates to devices that automatically cut off the supply of steam to a machine or cylinder when a preset condition-such as excess pressure, temperature, or speed-is reached. These safety or efficiency mechanisms are used in steam engines and related mechanical systems to prevent over-pressure, conserve energy, or protect equipment.
[F01B 5/00] Reciprocating-piston machines or engines with cylinder axes arranged substantially tangentially to a circle centred on main shaft axis -- F01B 5/00 covers reciprocating-piston engines or machines in which the cylinder axes are positioned roughly tangentially around a circle whose centre lies on the main shaft axis, typical of radial-type engines where pistons fire outward from a central crankcase. This arrangement allows compact, balanced designs often used in aircraft, large compressors and some marine power units.
[F01B 5/003] the connection of the pistons with an actuated or actuating element being at the outer ends of the cylinders -- This subclass covers reciprocating-piston machines where each piston is connected to an actuating or driven element located at the outer end of its cylinder, typical of radial or other engines with cylinder axes arranged tangentially around the main shaft.
[F01B 5/006] the connection of the pistons with an actuated or actuating element being at the inner ends of the cylinders -- The subclass covers reciprocating-piston machines whose cylinder axes are arranged tangentially around a main shaft and where the pistons are linked to an actuated or actuating element at the inner ends of the cylinders. It includes connections such as crankpins, cams or levers positioned near the cylinder centre that transmit motion to or from the piston.
[F01B 7/00] Machines or engines with two or more pistons reciprocating within same cylinder or within essentially coaxial cylinders -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Coaxial cylinders in opposite arrangement relative to main shaft -> F01B1/08
[F01B 7/02] with oppositely reciprocating pistons -- F01B 7/02 covers machines or engines in which two or more pistons move in opposite directions within the same cylinder or in essentially coaxial cylinders, such as opposed-piston engines. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, specifically machines or engines of positive-displacement type.
[F01B 7/04] acting on same main shaft -- F01B 7/04 covers machines or engines in which two or more pistons reciprocate within a single cylinder or within essentially coaxial cylinders and transmit their power to the same main shaft. The classification applies to designs such as multi-piston steam or internal-combustion engines where the pistons share a common crankshaft or drive shaft.
[F01B 7/06] using only connecting-rods for conversion of reciprocatory into rotary motion or -- F01B 7/06 covers engine designs that convert the linear motion of pistons into rotary motion solely through the action of connecting rods, without additional linkages such as levers or cranks. The mechanism links each reciprocating piston directly to a rotating shaft, providing a simple and compact power transmission.
[F01B 7/08] with side rods -- F01B 7/08 classifies positive-displacement engines that have two or more pistons moving in the same cylinder or in essentially coaxial cylinders and are equipped with side rods that connect the pistons for synchronized motion. This sub-group falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and general or steam-type machines.
[F01B 7/10] having piston-rod of one piston passed through other piston -- This CPC subclass concerns positive-displacement engines in which the rod of one piston passes through another piston within the same cylinder or essentially coaxial cylinders, enabling two or more pistons to reciprocate together. It applies to machines such as steam or internal-combustion engines that use this dual-piston arrangement.
[F01B 7/12] using rockers and connecting-rods -- F01B 7/12 covers machines or engines of the positive-displacement type that employ two or more pistons moving reciprocally within a single cylinder or coaxial cylinders, with the motion transmitted by rockers and connecting-rods. Such arrangements are used in steam and internal-combustion engines to balance forces and improve efficiency.
[F01B 7/14] acting on different main shafts -- This CPC subclass covers reciprocating-piston machines where two or more pistons operate in the same cylinder or essentially coaxial cylinders but transmit power to different main shafts, such as compound steam engines with separate crankshafts. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, general positive-displacement machines.
[F01B 7/16] with pistons synchronously moving in tandem arrangement -- This subgroup covers machines or engines in which two or more pistons occupy the same cylinder or coaxial cylinders and move together in a synchronized, tandem fashion, with their strokes occurring simultaneously rather than alternately. It applies to positive-displacement engines such as steam or internal-combustion types that employ synchronized pistons to improve balance or power delivery.
[F01B 7/18] with differential piston -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - With two or more pistons reciprocating one within another -> F01B7/20
[F01B 7/20] with two or more pistons reciprocating one within another, e.g. one piston forming cylinder of the other -- F01B 7/20 pertains to engines or machines where two or more pistons reciprocate in a nested arrangement, with one piston serving as the cylinder for another, a configuration often seen in positive-displacement types such as specialized steam or internal-combustion engines.
[F01B 9/00] Reciprocating-piston machines or engines characterised by connections between pistons and main shafts, not specific to groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Reciprocating-piston machines or engines characterised by connections between pistons and main shafts and not specific to groups F01B1/00 - F01B7/00 , e.g. when no recognisable crank shaft is present or the main shaft is a cam shaft.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[F01B 9/02] with crankshaft -- F01B 9/02 pertains to reciprocating-piston machines or engines whose pistons are connected to a crankshaft that transforms the pistons' linear motion into rotary motion of the main shaft, encompassing steam, internal-combustion and other positive-displacement engines that use this basic crank-linkage arrangement.
[F01B 9/023] of Bourke-type or Scotch yoke -- F01B 9/023 covers reciprocating-piston machines in which the piston is linked to the crankshaft by a Bourke-type or Scotch-yoke mechanism, a sliding yoke that converts the piston's linear motion into rotary motion without a conventional crank. This arrangement is used in various positive-displacement engines and pumps.
[F01B 9/026] Rigid connections between piston and rod; Oscillating pistons -- The subclass F01B 9/026 relates to reciprocating-piston machines and concerns rigid connections between the piston and its connecting rod, as well as pistons that oscillate rather than translate linearly. It addresses design features of the linkage that transmit motion and force within engines such as steam or positive-displacement types.
[F01B 9/04] with rotary main shaft other than crankshaft -- F01B 9/04 covers reciprocating-piston machines or engines whose pistons are linked to a rotary main shaft that is not a crankshaft, such as those using wobble, oscillating or cam-type shafts to convert piston motion into rotation. This subgroup lies under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and machines or engines in general or of positive-displacement type.
[F01B 9/042] the connections comprising gear transmissions -- This subclass covers gear transmission connections between pistons and the main shaft in reciprocating-piston machines, including gear sets, gear trains and related coupling mechanisms used to transmit motion or power. It applies to positive-displacement engines such as steam engines.
[F01B 9/047] with rack and pinion -- A reciprocating-piston engine or machine that employs a rack-and-pinion gear set to transform the pistons' linear motion into rotary motion of the main shaft, typically for speed control, positioning or power transmission, is classified under CPC code F01B 9/047.
[F01B 9/06] the piston motion being transmitted by curved surfaces -- F01B 9/06 covers reciprocating-piston machines whose piston motion is transferred to the crankshaft by curved surfaces, such as cams, eccentric or arcuate guides, rather than by straight connecting rods or pins. The classification includes any arrangement where the piston's linear motion is converted to rotary motion through these shaped interfaces. It is used for engines and pumps that employ such curved-surface transmission mechanisms.
[F01B 9/08] with ratchet and pawl -- F01B 9/08 covers reciprocating-piston engines or machines that incorporate a ratchet and pawl mechanism, used to permit motion in one direction while preventing reverse movement, typically to lock the piston or crankshaft at a selected position during operation. This feature is applied in various steam, internal-combustion or other positive-displacement engines to control stroke timing or provide safety stops.
[F01C] ROTARY-PISTON OR OSCILLATING-PISTON MACHINES OR ENGINES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Positive displacement machines, i.e. devices which could be equally an engine or pump, with rotary or oscillating pistons for elastic fluids or for combination of liquid and elastic fluid.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Internal-combustion aspects of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines -> F02B53/00 , F02B55/00 - Engines with reciprocating pistons or rotary or oscillating pistons wherein the working fluid is a liquid. -> F03C - Positive displacement machines for liquids, or pumps in which the working-fluid is displaced by one or more reciprocating pistons or by flexible working members -> F04B  **Glossary:** - Cooperating members: means the "oscillating piston" or "rotary piston" and another member, e.g., the working-chamber wall, which assists in the pumping action or machine's action - Internal axis type: means that the rotational axes of the inner and outer co-operating members remain at all times within the outer member, e.g., in a similar manner to that of a pinion meshing with the internal teeth of a ring gear - Machine: means a device that could equally be both an engine and a pump and not a device which is restricted to an engine or one which is restricted to a pump - Movement of the cooperating members: is to be interpreted as relative, so that one of the "cooperating members" may be stationary, even though reference may be made to its rotational axis, or both may move - Oscillating piston machine: means a positive-displacement machine in which a fluid-engaging, work-transmitting member oscillates, e.g. a vane piston swinging back and forth about a fixed axis - Positive displacement engines: the energy of a working fluid is transformed into mechanical energy, in which variations of volume created by the working fluid in a working chamber produce equivalent movement of mechanical members, e.g. pistons transmitting the energy, the dynamic effect of the fluid being of minor importance - Positive displacement pumps: pumps using pistons or other mechanical members to displace a working fluid in a working chamber, the dynamic effect on the fluid being of minor importance - Pump: means a device for continuously raising, forcing, compressing, or exhausting fluid by mechanical means - Reciprocating piston: means a fluid-engaging, work-transmitting member of an reciprocating-piston type machine or pump that slides alternately back and forth usually along a straight line or path - Rotary piston: means a fluid engaging, work-transmitting member of a rotary-piston machine or pump that can completely rotate about a fixed axis or about an axis moving along a circular or similar orbit when operating, e.g. rotor having vanes or teeth - Rotary piston machine: means a positive-displacement machine in which a liquid-engaging, work-transmitting member rotates about a fixed axis or about an axis moving along a circular or similar orbit, e.g. machine with a rotor having vanes or teeth - Teeth or tooth equivalents: include lobes, projections or abutments - Working fluid: means the driven fluid in a pump or driving or driven liquid in a machine. The working fluid can be in a compressible, gaseous state, e.g. steam, called elastic fluid, a liquid state, or a state where there is coexistence of elastic fluid and liquid state
[F01C 1/00] Rotary-piston machines or engines -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - With axes of co-operating members non parallel -> F01C3/00 - With the working-chamber walls at least partly resiliently deformable -> F01C5/00 - With fluid ring or the like -> F01C7/00 - Rotary-piston machines or engines in which the working fluid is exclusively displaced by, or exclusively displaces, one or more reciprocating pistons -> F01B13/00
[F01C 1/02] of arcuate-engagement type, i.e. with circular translatory movement of co-operating members, each member having the same number of teeth or tooth-equivalents -- Rotary-piston engines in which the cooperating members move in a circular translatory path and engage each other by an arcuate-engagement arrangement of matching teeth or equivalent features are covered. The class includes machines where all members have the same number of teeth and the engagement is of the arcuate-engagement type.
[F01C 1/0207] both members having co-operating elements in spiral form -- F01C 1/0207 classifies rotary-piston machines in which the two interacting members are formed as cooperating spiral elements, a design used to transmit motion or fluid within the engine. This falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and specifically rotary-piston or oscillating-piston machines.
[F01C 1/0215] where only one member is moving -- F01C 1/0215 covers rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines in which only a single moving member-such as one rotor or piston-provides the power-stroke while all other components remain stationary, exemplified by single-rotor Wankel-type engines.
[F01C 1/0223] with symmetrical double wraps -- The F01C 1/0223 subclass covers rotary-piston engines whose rotors have symmetrical double wraps, meaning the rotor's lobes are formed by two identical, mirror-image spirals that encircle the central shaft. This design features two concentric paths that create balanced sealing surfaces and uniform combustion chambers, typically used to improve efficiency and reduce vibration in engines such as the Wankel type.
[F01C 1/023] where both members are moving -- This subclass covers rotary-piston engines in which both the rotating member and the complementary member such as a housing, stator or crank move relative to each other during the power cycle. It includes designs where the rotor and a secondary moving component work together to create combustion chambers and transfer work.
[F01C 1/0238] with symmetrical double wraps -- F01C 1/0238 covers rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines in which the piston or rotor incorporates two symmetrical wraps around the axis. The double-wrapped design provides balanced forces and improved sealing between chambers, and is a specific variant of rotary-piston machines within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy.
[F01C 1/0246] Details concerning the involute wraps or their base, e.g. geometry -- The F01C 1/0246 classification covers specifications of the involute wraps or their bases in rotary-piston engines, including the geometric shape, dimensions and profile characteristics that define how the wraps interact with the rotor and housing. This entry is used to classify technical information on the design and analysis of these wrap geometries.
[F01C 1/0253] Details concerning the base -- F01C 1/0253 addresses the base of rotary-piston machines or engines, covering the design, construction and mounting of the supporting structure, methods of fixing the unit to a foundation, and measures for vibration isolation or load distribution. It includes features such as base plates, foundations, mounting brackets and related structural details that secure the engine and manage mechanical stresses.
[F01C 1/0261] Details of the ports, e.g. location, number, geometry -- F01C 1/0261 pertains to rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines and specifies the design features of the fluid-exchange ports. It includes the position, number and geometric shape of inlet and outlet ports that affect charging, scavenging and exhaust processes.
[F01C 1/0269] Details concerning the involute wraps -- F01C 1/0269 covers rotary-piston machines and engines and deals with the geometry and construction of involute-shaped wraps that form rotor or stator lobes, including their dimensions, manufacturing methods and sealing features that influence fluid flow and compression.
[F01C 1/0276] Different wall heights -- F01C 1/0276 covers rotary-piston machines where the cylinder walls or housing sections have different heights, creating varying chamber volumes during rotation. This variation affects compression ratios, power output and thermal behavior, and is employed in specialized pump or engine designs.
[F01C 1/0284] Details of the wrap tips -- F01C 1/0284 covers the specific design and construction features of the wrap tips in rotary-piston machines, including their shape, material, sealing surfaces and cooling provisions. The subclass addresses variations intended to enhance tip sealing, wear resistance and thermal management within the engine.
[F01C 1/0292] Ports or channels located in the wrap -- F01C 1/0292 covers the arrangement of intake, exhaust or fluid-transfer ports and channels that are incorporated in the peripheral wrap of a rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engine, allowing gas or fluid to flow between the working chambers and external systems. This includes the design and positioning of these passages within the engine housing to optimise sealing, timing and flow characteristics.
[F01C 1/04] of internal-axis type -- F01C 1/04 covers rotary-piston machines of the internal-axis type, where the rotor rotates inside a stationary housing and the power-producing pistons turn about an axis that lies within the engine casing. Typical examples are Wankel-type internal combustion engines and similar designs that use revolving chambers for compression and expansion strokes.
[F01C 1/045] having a C-shaped piston -- F01C 1/045 covers rotary-piston machines whose pistons are shaped like the letter C, forming a crescent-type chamber that rotates within a housing. This C-shaped piston geometry is typical of engines such as the Wankel, enabling continuous combustion and power output with fewer moving parts than conventional reciprocating pistons.
[F01C 1/06] of other than internal-axis type -- F01C 1/06 covers rotary-piston machines or engines that do not use an internal-axis configuration, meaning the pistons rotate about an external or alternative axis rather than being arranged around a central shaft. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically within engines or pumps and the rotary-piston or oscillating-piston machine category.
[F01C 1/063] with coaxially-mounted members having continuously-changing circumferential spacing between them -- Rotary-piston machines in which the coaxially mounted components, such as rotor and stator, are designed so that the circumferential spacing between them changes continuously as they rotate. This variable geometry improves sealing, compression or power-delivery characteristics throughout the cycle.
[F01C 1/067] having cam-and-follower type drive -- F01C 1/067 relates to rotary-piston machines or engines that employ a cam-and-follower type drive, where cam profiles are used to convert rotational motion into the reciprocating motion of the pistons. This subclass covers configurations in which the cam mechanism directly powers the piston movement in such engines.
[F01C 1/07] having crankshaft-and-connecting-rod type drive -- F01C 1/07 covers rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines that use a conventional crankshaft and connecting-rod arrangement to transfer motion, converting the rotary motion of the piston into reciprocal motion for output shafts. The subclass is applied to machines where the drive follows the same principle as typical piston engines rather than alternative transmission methods.
[F01C 1/073] having pawl-and-ratchet type drive -- A rotary-piston engine that uses a pawl-and-ratchet mechanism to transmit motion, allowing unidirectional drive or preventing reverse rotation of the rotor. This arrangement is employed to control power delivery or to lock the engine against back-driving forces.
[F01C 1/077] having toothed-gearing type drive -- This subclass concerns rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines whose power is transferred to the output shaft by a toothed-gear drive, such as spur, helical or bevel gears, rather than direct coupling or other transmission types.
[F01C 1/08] of intermeshing engagement type, i.e. with engagement of co- operating members similar to that of toothed gearing -- F01C 1/08 covers rotary-piston engines or machines where the moving parts engage one another by intermeshing, similarly to toothed gears. The design uses gear-like profiles on the cooperating members to transmit motion and power within the rotary-piston mechanism.
[F01C 1/082] Details specially related to intermeshing engagement type machines or engines -- F01C 1/082 pertains to rotary-piston machines that operate by intermeshing engagement of their moving elements, such as gear or lobed-rotor engines. The subclass includes technical details of tooth geometry, timing, sealing and bearing arrangements that are specific to the interlocking motion of the pistons.
[F01C 1/084] Toothed wheels -- F01C 1/084 covers toothed wheels used in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston machines and engines. It includes gears, ratchets and similar wheel components that transmit or modify rotary motion within the engine's internal mechanism.
[F01C 1/086] Carter -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Outer members cooperating with the rotary pistons of intermeshing engagement type machines.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Non intermeshing-engagement type machines -> F01C21/10
[F01C 1/088] Elements in the toothed wheels or the carter for relieving the pressure of fluid imprisoned in the zones of engagement -- F01C 1/088 relates to rotary-piston engines and covers components such as specially shaped teeth on wheels or features in the carter that allow fluid compressed in the gear-meshing zones to be released, preventing pressure build-up and improving efficiency. This entry falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for engines and pumps.
[F01C 1/10] of internal-axis type with the outer member having more teeth or tooth-equivalents, e.g. rollers, than the inner member -- Rotary-piston engines of the internal-axis type in which the outer housing or stator carries a greater number of teeth, rollers or equivalent projections than the inner rotor. This configuration creates a differential gear-like interaction that drives the pistons around the axis, typical of certain compact internal-combustion or hydraulic power units.
[F01C 11/00] Combinations of two or more machines or engines, each being of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combinations of two or more machines or engines, each being of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston type, such as multistage machines and parallel operating machines.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Rotary-piston machines or engines with non-parallel axes of rotation between co-operating members -> F01C3/00 - Combinations of engines with devices driven thereby -> F01C13/00 - Combinations of two or more rotary-piston pumps -> F04C
[F01C 11/002] of similar working principle -- F01C 11/002 covers arrangements that combine two or more rotary-piston or oscillating-piston machines or engines, where the machines operate on a similar working principle, such as sharing the same combustion cycle or fluid-power concept. It applies to integrated systems that unite these engines to achieve a combined function.
[F01C 11/004] and of complementary function, e.g. internal combustion engine with supercharger -- F01C 11/004 covers arrangements where two or more rotary-piston or oscillating-piston machines are combined to perform complementary functions, such as an internal-combustion engine driving a supercharger or a similar auxiliary device. The classification applies to integrated systems that enhance performance or efficiency.
[F01C 11/006] of dissimilar working principle -- F01C 11/006 covers hybrid arrangements that combine two or more rotary-piston or oscillating-piston machines, each operating on a different working principle. The classification is used for engines or pumps where dissimilar mechanisms are integrated to achieve combined performance, such as pairing a reciprocating cylinder with a rotary screw device. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the subgroup for rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines.
[F01C 11/008] and of complementary function, e.g. internal combustion engine with supercharger -- F01C 11/008 relates to devices that combine two or more rotary-piston or oscillating-piston machines where each unit serves a complementary role, such as pairing an internal combustion engine with a supercharger to boost performance.
[F01C 1/101] Moineau-type -- The Moineau-type entry covers rotary-piston machines that use a helical rotor and matching stator to compress gases, commonly known as screw compressors. It includes designs where the rotor-stator geometry creates a continuous sealing chamber for gas flow, used for pressure boosting, vacuum generation and fluid transfer in various industrial applications.
[F01C 1/102] with a crescent shaped filler element located between the intermeshing elements -- The F01C 1/102 subclass covers rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines that incorporate a crescent-shaped filler element positioned between the intermeshing rotor elements. This filler typically serves to fill gaps, improve sealing and reduce wear within the rotating assembly.
[F01C 1/103] the two members rotating simultaneously around their respective axes -- The code F01C 1/103 covers rotary-piston engines in which two rotor members turn at the same time about their own separate axes, providing synchronized motion for power conversion. This arrangement is typical of dual-rotor designs such as certain Wankel-type or oscillating-piston machines, where each rotor's rotation contributes to the engine's combustion cycle.
[F01C 1/104] one member having simultaneously a rotational movement about its own axis and an orbital movement -- A rotary-piston engine element that both spins on its own shaft and travels in an orbital path is covered by CPC code F01C 1/104. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and specifically rotary-piston or oscillating-piston machines.
[F01C 1/105] and having an articulated driving shaft -- This subclass covers rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines in which the power-transmitting shaft is articulated, meaning it includes a joint or flexible connection that allows angular movement between the shaft and the driven components, facilitating alignment or varying geometry within the machine.
[F01C 1/107] with helical teeth -- This subclass covers rotary-piston engines that employ helical teeth on the rotor, gear or other moving parts to convert rotational motion, enhance torque transmission, or provide sealing benefits. It includes designs where the teeth are cut in a spiral form along the axial direction.
[F01C 1/113] the inner member carrying rollers intermeshing with the outer member -- This subgroup covers rotary-piston or oscillating-piston machines in which the inner rotating member is equipped with rollers that mesh with corresponding rollers or lobes on the outer member, providing the power-transmitting contact between the two parts.
[F01C 1/12] of other than internal-axis type -- F01C 1/12 covers rotary-piston machines or engines that are not of the internal-axis type, such as external-axis rotary engines and related mechanisms. It is situated in the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Engines or Pumps → Rotary-piston or Oscillating-piston Machines or Engines.
[F01C 1/123] with tooth-like elements, extending generally radially from the rotor body cooperating with recesses in the other rotor, e.g. one tooth -- The group F01C 1/123 covers rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines in which one rotor bears radially extending tooth-like elements that engage complementary recesses on the opposite rotor, providing positive mechanical coupling between the rotors.
[F01C 1/126] with elements extending radially from the rotor body not necessarily cooperating with corresponding recesses in the other rotor, e.g. lobes, Roots type -- F01C 1/126 covers rotary-piston machines or engines in which protruding elements such as lobes extend radially from the rotor body without requiring matching recesses on the opposite rotor, exemplified by Roots-type blowers and similar devices. This sub-class classifies designs where the rotor's radial extensions operate independently of complementary cut-outs on the mating rotor.
[F01C 1/14] with toothed rotary pistons -- F01C 1/14 pertains to rotary-piston machines or engines that use toothed rotary pistons, where the pistons are fitted with gear-like teeth that mesh with a counterpart to transmit torque and enhance sealing during fluid compression or power generation, as seen in rotary compressors and some internal-combustion engines.
[F01C 1/16] with helical teeth, e.g. chevron-shaped, screw type -- Rotary-piston machines or engines in which the pistons have helical, screw-type teeth-often chevron-shaped-allowing continuous rotation and power transmission within the cylinder. The helical teeth convert linear motion into rotary motion and improve sealing and torque characteristics. This subclass covers such rotary-piston designs.
[F01C 1/165] having more than two rotary pistons with parallel axes -- This subgroup covers rotary-piston engines that employ more than two pistons arranged with parallel axes, allowing multiple pistons to rotate in sync within the same housing. The feature is intended to increase power output or balance forces compared with two-piston configurations.
[F01C 1/18] with similar tooth forms -- F01C 1/18 covers rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines in which the pistons use identical or substantially similar tooth profiles for meshing with the housing or cam elements, enabling uniform power transmission and reduced wear across the rotating components.
[F01C 1/20] with dissimilar tooth forms -- Rotary-piston machines or engines in which the rotor and the surrounding housing use teeth of different shapes or sizes, creating non-matching tooth geometries that affect sealing, torque transmission or wear distribution.
[F01C 1/22] of internal-axis type with equidirectional movement of co-operating members at the points of engagement, or with one of the co-operating members being stationary, the inner member having more teeth or tooth- equivalents than the outer member -- This subclass concerns internal-axis rotary-piston engines in which the cooperating components move in the same direction at their points of engagement, or where one component remains stationary, and the inner component carries more teeth or equivalent projections than the outer component.
[F01C 1/24] of counter-engagement type, i.e. the movement of co-operating members at the points of engagement being in opposite directions -- This subclass covers rotary-piston engines where the cooperating members engage so that their contact points move in opposite directions, such as in certain helical gear or screw-type compressors. It pertains to designs where the pistons or rotors are arranged to provide counter-engagement motion, improving sealing or power transmission.
[F01C 1/26] of internal-axis type -- F01C 1/26 classifies rotary-piston machines or engines in which the rotor axis is fixed within the housing, i.e., internal-axis types such as Wankel or cycloidal engines, where the piston rotates around a stationary shaft rather than moving along an external axis.
[F01C 1/28] of other than internal-axis type -- The F01C 1/28 subclass covers rotary-piston machines whose rotating axis is not internal to the cylinder, such as external-axis rotary engines and pumps. These devices employ a piston that moves in a circular path around an axis positioned outside the working chamber, differing from conventional internal-axis designs. This subclass groups all non-internal-axis rotary-piston configurations under the broader rotary-piston engine category.
[F01C 1/30] having the characteristics covered by two or more groups -- F01C 1/30 covers rotary-piston engines that combine features defined in two or more CPC sub-groups. It applies to designs that integrate elements from different categories within rotary-piston machines, such as mixed displacement mechanisms or combined valve arrangements.
[F01C 13/00] Adaptations of machines or engines for special use -- F01C 13/00 covers the design changes made to rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines to meet particular operating requirements, such as marine, aerospace, or specialized industrial uses. It includes custom mounting, cooling, fuel-delivery, or control adaptations that tailor the engine's performance for a specific application.
[F01C 13/02] for driving hand-held tools or the like -- F01C 13/02 covers rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines that are specially adapted to drive hand-held tools such as drills, impact wrenches, sanders or similar portable equipment, providing the power source for these devices in a compact, mobile form.
[F01C 13/04] for driving pumps or compressors -- F01C 13/04 covers adaptations of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines that are specifically configured to drive pumps or compressors. It classifies machines where the engine's mechanical output is used to power fluid-moving equipment, distinguishing these arrangements from general engine designs. This falls within the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and rotary-piston or oscillating-piston machines.
[F01C 1/32] having both the movement defined in group -- F01C 1/32 designates rotary-piston engines in which the pistons perform both types of motion defined in the preceding group, i.e., they rotate about an axis while also executing an oscillating or reciprocating stroke. This dual motion is employed to obtain specific power-output, efficiency or compactness characteristics.
[F01C 1/321] with vanes hinged to the inner member and reciprocating with respect to the inner member -- F01C 1/321 covers rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines in which the vanes are attached to the inner rotating member by hinges and move reciprocally relative to that member, allowing the vane tips to slide against the outer housing while the inner rotor turns. This arrangement is used to improve sealing and efficiency in engines such as certain Wankel-type or vane-compressor designs.
[F01C 1/322] with vanes hinged to the outer member and reciprocating with respect to the outer member -- F01C 1/322 covers rotary-piston machines in which the vanes are hinged to the outer member and reciprocate relative to it as the rotor turns. The hinged arrangement improves sealing and reduces wear in devices such as vane compressors and rotary engines.
[F01C 1/324] with vanes hinged to the inner member and reciprocating with respect to the outer member -- This subclass covers rotary-piston machines in which the vanes are attached to the inner rotor by hinges, allowing them to move back and forth relative to the stationary outer housing. The design enables the vanes to expand and contract during rotation, improving sealing and volumetric efficiency.
[F01C 1/328] and hinged to the outer member -- F01C 1/328 pertains to rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines in which a component-such as a valve, timing gear, or power-output link-is hinged directly to the outer housing or casing of the machine, allowing pivoted movement relative to the rotating internal parts. This arrangement is used to transmit motion, control fluid flow, or support sealing elements within the engine.
[F01C 1/332] with vanes hinged to the outer member and reciprocating with respect to the inner member
[F01C 1/336] and hinged to the inner member -- The F01C 1/336 subclass concerns rotary-piston engines in which a component is hinged to the inner rotating member, typically to allow controlled movement or sealing of the rotor's internal parts. It addresses the hinge mechanism that links the inner element to adjacent structures within the engine.
[F01C 1/34] having the movement defined in group -- F01C 1/34 covers rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines in which the piston's movement is defined by the characteristics set out in the referenced group, distinguishing these machines from other rotary-piston designs with different motion patterns.
[F01C 1/344] with vanes reciprocating with respect to the inner member -- The F01C 1/344 subclass covers rotary-piston machines or engines in which vanes move back and forth relative to the inner member, such as a rotor or housing. These reciprocating vanes create variable volumes for compression, pumping or power generation within the rotating chamber. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and rotary-piston or oscillating-piston machines.
[F01C 1/3441] the inner and outer member being in contact along one line or continuous surface substantially parallel to the axis of rotation -- This subclass deals with rotary-piston machines in which the inner and outer members touch each other along a single line or a continuous surface that runs substantially parallel to the axis of rotation, defining the axial sealing or guiding arrangement of the engine or pump.
[F01C 1/3442] the surfaces of the inner and outer member, forming the working space, being surfaces of revolution -- The subclass F01C 1/3442 covers rotary-piston machines or engines in which the inner member (such as a rotor) and the outer member (such as a housing) that define the working space possess surfaces of revolution. It applies to designs where both surfaces are generated by rotating a profile about an axis, influencing sealing, clearance and motion characteristics.
[F01C 1/3443] with a separation element located between the inlet and outlet opening -- F01C 1/3443 pertains to rotary-piston engines that incorporate a separation element placed between the inlet and outlet openings, serving to isolate the incoming fluid from the exhaust stream, improve efficiency and prevent backflow.
[F01C 1/3445] the vanes having the form of rollers, slippers or the like -- This subclass covers rotary-piston machines in which the vanes are formed as rollers, slippers or similar elements that roll or slide against the cylinder wall, providing sealing and load transmission. Such roller-type vanes are employed in certain rotary engines or compressors to reduce friction and wear.
[F01C 1/3446] the inner and outer member being in contact along more than one line or surface -- It covers rotary-piston engines in which the moving inner member and the stationary outer housing are in contact along more than a single line or surface, providing continuous sealing and power transfer during rotation. Such multi-line or surface contact improves load distribution and reduces wear compared with point-contact designs.
[F01C 1/3447] the vanes having the form of rollers, slippers or the like -- This subclass covers rotary-piston machines in which the internal vanes are formed as rollers, slippers or similar shaped elements that slide or roll inside the housing, providing sealing and transfer of motion. It is situated under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, rotary-piston machines.
[F01C 1/3448] with axially movable vanes -- This subclass covers rotary-piston engines in which the vanes can move along the axis of rotation. The axially movable vanes adjust their position during operation to vary the chamber volume, improve sealing and enhance performance in machines such as Wankel-type or similar rotary engines.
[F01C 1/348] the vanes positively engaging, with circumferential play, an outer rotatable member -- This subclass covers rotary-piston engines where the internal vanes are designed to engage a surrounding rotating component in a positive manner while allowing limited circumferential movement. The arrangement ensures a secure seal during operation yet provides the necessary play to accommodate thermal expansion or manufacturing tolerances.
[F01C 1/352] the vanes being pivoted on the axis of the outer member -- A rotary-piston engine in which the working vanes are hinged so they can pivot about an axis that coincides with the axis of the outer rotating member, allowing the vanes to adjust to the chamber shape during rotation.
[F01C 1/356] with vanes reciprocating with respect to the outer member -- The code F01C 1/356 designates rotary-piston machines in which the internal vanes move back and forth while the outer housing remains stationary or rotates. It covers engines where the vanes slide reciprocally relative to the surrounding member, such as certain vane-type rotary compressors or engines with reciprocating vanes inside a rotating rotor.
[F01C 1/3562] the inner and outer member being in contact along one line or continuous surface substantially parallel to the axis of rotation -- The code refers to rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines in which the inner rotating member and the outer stationary member meet along a line or a continuous surface that runs essentially parallel to the axis of rotation, providing longitudinal contact for power transmission or sealing.
[F01C 1/3564] the surfaces of the inner and outer member, forming the working space, being surfaces of revolution -- The subclass F01C 1/3564 relates to rotary-piston engines in which the working chamber is bounded by an inner and an outer member whose contacting faces are generated as surfaces of revolution. It includes designs where both surfaces are produced by rotating a profile around a common axis, defining the space in which the rotor moves.
[F01C 1/3566] the inner and outer member being in contact along more than one line or surface -- F01C 1/3566 covers rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines in which the inner cylinder and the outer housing engage each other over more than a single line or point, i.e. the contact occurs along multiple lines or over a surface area. This design improves sealing and load distribution within the engine.
[F01C 1/3568] with axially movable vanes -- This CPC subgroup covers rotary-piston engines in which one or more vanes slide along the axis of the rotor, altering the chamber volume during rotation to achieve compression or power output. It applies to designs such as axial-moveable vane pumps or engines where the vanes are not fixed radially.
[F01C 1/36] having both the movements defined in sub-groups -- F01C 1/36 covers rotary-piston or oscillating-piston machines that incorporate the two distinct motion types defined in the preceding sub-groups, such as a combination of rotary and reciprocating actions within a single engine or pump. This subclass is used for designs where the piston simultaneously follows both rotational and linear paths, enabling hybrid operation in the same device.
[F01C 1/38] having the movement defined in group -- F01C 1/38 is a CPC subclass for rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines in which the piston movement is explicitly defined by the classification group. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and covers machines whose kinematic cycle is described within this specific subgroup.
[F01C 1/39] with vanes hinged to the inner as well as to the outer member -- F01C 1/39 covers rotary-piston machines, such as engines or pumps, where the vanes are hinged to both the inner rotor and the outer stator, allowing the vanes to swing as the rotor turns and maintain sealing or fluid transfer.
[F01C 1/40] having the movement defined in group -- F01C 1/40 designates rotary-piston engines whose kinematic motion is defined by reference to another group in the classification. It covers machines such as Wankel-type engines where the rotor follows a specific trochoidal or similar path, with the exact movement pattern detailed in the referenced subclass rather than repeated in this entry.
[F01C 1/44] with vanes hinged to the inner member -- F01C 1/44 covers rotary-piston machines or engines in which the vanes are hinged to the inner rotating member, allowing the vanes to swing outward and maintain contact with the housing during rotation. This arrangement is used in devices such as vane pumps, rotary compressors and certain types of internal-combustion engines to improve sealing and efficiency.
[F01C 1/46] with vanes hinged to the outer member -- F01C 1/46 covers rotary-piston machines in which the vanes are hinged to the outer housing, allowing each vane to pivot as the rotor turns and maintain contact with the inner wall. This arrangement improves sealing and reduces wear, and is used in engines and compressors that employ rotating vanes.
[F01C 17/00] Arrangements for drive of co-operating members, e.g. for rotary piston and casing -- The classification covers mechanical arrangements that transmit driving motion between cooperating components of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines, such as the linkage of a rotary piston to its surrounding casing, enabling synchronized operation of the engine's moving parts.
[F01C 17/02] of toothed-gearing type -- F01C 17/02 covers drive arrangements in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines where the cooperating members, such as the piston and its casing, are linked by a toothed-gearing system to transmit torque and synchronize motion.
[F01C 17/04] of cam-and-follower type -- F01C 17/04 relates to cam-and-follower mechanisms used to drive cooperating components in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines, such as arrangements that synchronize the motion of the piston with its surrounding casing.
[F01C 17/06] using cranks, universal joints or similar elements -- The entry defines mechanical arrangements that employ cranks, universal joints or comparable linkages to transmit motion between the cooperating parts of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines, such as between the rotor and its casing. It focuses on the drive mechanisms that coordinate the movement of these members.
[F01C 17/063] with only rolling movement -- F01C 17/063 covers arrangements for driving cooperating members in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines where the relative motion is purely rolling. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, rotary-piston or oscillating-piston machines, and arrangements for drive of co-operating members.
[F01C 17/066] with an intermediate piece sliding along perpendicular axes, e.g. Oldham coupling -- This subclass covers drive arrangements for rotary-piston or casing members that use an intermediate element moving along two mutually perpendicular axes, as in an Oldham coupling. The sliding intermediate piece transfers torque while allowing relative angular or axial misalignment between the driven parts.
[F01C 19/00] Sealing arrangements in rotary-piston machines or engines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Any seal construction or mode not provided by the groups, e.g. labyrinth.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Sealing by liquid injection -> F01C21/001
[F01C 19/005] Structure and composition of sealing elements such as sealing strips, sealing rings and the like; Coating of these elements  vane construction  F01C21/0809 ; piston rings and ring sealings of similar construction in general  F16J9/00 -- F01C 19/005 covers the design and material makeup of sealing components used in rotary-piston machines, including sealing strips, rings and similar elements, as well as methods for coating these parts to improve performance and durability. It also relates to vane constructions and piston-ring type seals that have comparable construction. The code is applied to inventions that modify the structure, composition or surface treatment of such seals to enhance reliability in rotary engines.
[F01C 19/02] Radially-movable sealings for working fluids
[F01C 19/025] Radial sealing elements specially adapted for intermeshing engagement type machines or engines, e.g. gear machines or engines -- F01C 19/025 covers radial sealing elements designed for intermeshing-engagement machines such as gear engines, where sealing rings or pads are positioned radially to prevent fluid leakage between tightly meshing teeth or rotors. These seals are specially shaped to accommodate the relative motion and pressure conditions of gear-type rotary-piston mechanisms.
[F01C 19/04] of rigid material -- This subclass covers sealing arrangements that use rigid (non-elastic) components in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines and pumps. It includes designs where hard materials such as metal, ceramic or composite are employed to create tight seals between moving and stationary parts, preventing fluid leakage while withstanding high pressures and temperatures.
[F01C 19/06] of resilient material -- This subclass covers sealing arrangements in rotary-piston engines that employ resilient materials such as rubber, elastomers or polymers. It includes designs of O-rings, lip seals and other flexible elements used to maintain pressure boundaries between the rotating housing and the rotor.
[F01C 19/08] Axially-movable sealings for working fluids -- F01C 19/08 relates to sealing arrangements in rotary-piston machines where the seal moves axially to control the working fluid. It covers devices such as sliding or telescoping seals in rotary-piston compressors or engines that prevent leakage while accommodating piston motion.
[F01C 19/085] Elements specially adapted for sealing of the lateral faces of intermeshing-engagement type machines or engines, e.g. gear machines or engines -- The code F01C 19/085 covers specially designed sealing elements that protect the lateral faces where gear-type or intermeshing rotary-piston components engage. These elements are tailored to prevent leakage in engines or machines that use interlocking gear or rotor teeth. The classification addresses the geometry and material features required for effective sealing of those side contacts.
[F01C 19/10] Sealings for working fluids between radially and axially movable parts -- F01C 19/10 deals with seals that isolate the working fluid in rotary-piston machines where components shift both radially and axially. It includes gaskets, labyrinth or other sealing devices that prevent leakage between moving parts such as pistons, housings or shafts, and is relevant to engines, compressors and pumps employing rotary-piston mechanisms.
[F01C 19/12] for other than working fluid -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sealing arrangements for fluids other than the working fluid, e.g. lubricant, coolant. Sealing in other locations of the machine, i.e. not between successive working chambers of the machine, e.g. between housing parts, towards the external space etc.
[F01C 19/125] Shaft sealings specially adapted for rotary or oscillating-piston machines or engines -- F01C 19/125 covers shaft sealing systems designed specifically for rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines and machines. The entry includes seals that prevent fluid or gas leakage while accommodating the rotating shaft's motion and operating conditions.
[F01C 20/00] Control of, monitoring of, or safety arrangements for, machines or engines -- F01C 20/00 covers devices and methods that control, monitor or provide safety for rotary-piston or oscillating-piston machines and engines, including governors, speed regulators, temperature or pressure sensors, automatic shutdown systems and other protective arrangements that ensure reliable and safe operation.
[F01C 20/02] specially adapted for several machines or engines connected in series or in parallel -- F01C 20/02 covers control, monitoring or safety systems that are designed to operate with multiple rotary- or oscillating-piston engines or machines linked together in series or parallel configurations. The classification applies to devices or methods that manage the coordinated performance of several such engines, ensuring proper operation and protection.
[F01C 20/04] specially adapted for reversible machines or engines -- This group deals with control, monitoring, or safety devices that are specifically designed for engines that can run in both directions, such as reversible pistons, including mechanisms that shut off, regulate, or protect the machine during forward and reverse operation.
[F01C 20/06] specially adapted for stopping, starting, idling or no-load operation -- F01C 20/06 covers rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines that are specially designed or fitted with mechanisms to facilitate stopping, starting, idling or no-load operation, often for improved control, efficiency or safety. These adaptations may include starter systems, idle-speed regulators, shut-off devices or other arrangements that enable the engine to run briefly without load or to be quickly brought to or from a stopped condition.
[F01C 20/08] characterised by varying the rotational speed -- F01C 20/08 includes devices and methods that vary the rotational speed of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines, such as variable-speed governors, electronic controllers, or mechanical arrangements that adjust speed for performance or safety.
[F01C 20/10] characterised by changing the positions of the inlet or outlet openings with respect to the working chamber -- A machine or engine in which the inlet or outlet openings are repositioned relative to the working chamber during operation, affecting flow timing or volume. The classification covers control, monitoring or safety arrangements that adjust these opening positions in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston devices.
[F01C 20/12] using sliding valves -- F01C 20/12 deals with the use of sliding valves in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines and pumps, focusing on how these valves regulate fluid flow. It falls under control, monitoring, or safety arrangements for such machines.
[F01C 20/125] with sliding valves controlled by the use of fluid other than the working fluid -- F01C 20/125 covers rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines in which sliding valves are actuated by a fluid other than the engine's working fluid, such as hydraulic, pneumatic or other auxiliary fluids used for valve timing, monitoring or safety functions.
[F01C 20/14] using rotating valves -- This subgroup deals with the use of rotating valves to control the intake and exhaust flow in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines, covering valve timing, actuation mechanisms and related monitoring or safety features. It includes designs where a rotating valve replaces conventional reciprocating valve gear, providing improved reliability and the possibility of variable timing.
[F01C 20/16] using lift valves -- F01C 20/16 relates to the use of lift valves in control, monitoring or safety arrangements for rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines and machines, covering their operation and regulation within mechanical engineering systems.
[F01C 20/18] characterised by varying the volume of the working chamber -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Changing the stroke of a moving element of machines other than those meant for the sub-groups, e.g. with non parallel axes of movement.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - By changing the positions of inlet or outlet openings -> F01C20/10
[F01C 20/185] by varying the useful pumping length of the cooperating members in the axial direction -- This CPC subclass addresses control, monitoring or safety arrangements for rotary-piston or oscillating-piston machines that adjust operation by varying the useful pumping length of the cooperating members along the axial direction. It concerns mechanisms that change the effective stroke of these components to regulate flow, pressure or power output.
[F01C 20/20] by changing the form of the inner or outlet contour of the working chamber
[F01C 2021/12] Control of working fluid admission or discharge -- F01C 2021/12 relates to machines that use fluid power and covers the regulation of fluid flow into or out of a system. It includes valves, regulators and other control devices that manage the admission or discharge of working fluid in hydraulic or pneumatic installations.
[F01C 2021/125] Arrangements for supercharging the working space -- The CPC subclass F01C 2021/125 covers devices and arrangements that increase the pressure or flow of a working fluid within a machine's operating chamber, typically by using superchargers, blowers, compressors or similar mechanisms to enhance performance, cooling or power output.
[F01C 2021/14] for variable fluid distribution -- F01C 2021/14 covers pump or fluid-handling devices that provide variable fluid distribution, meaning they can adjust flow rates, pressures or volume among multiple outlets or sections to match changing demand. It includes mechanisms such as adjustable valves, multiple-stage designs or control systems that modulate the delivery of a fluid.
[F01C 2021/16] Other regulation or control -- The CPC entry F01C 2021/16 covers inventions that provide regulation or control features for machines classified in F01C, such as control systems, sensors, actuators, or safety devices used to monitor and adjust operation, when the specific type of control is not captured by a more detailed subclass.
[F01C 2021/1606] Variation of the working chamber -- F01C 2021/1606 covers pumps whose working chamber can be altered in shape, size or configuration during operation, allowing the displacement volume to be varied to match changing flow or pressure requirements. This includes mechanisms that adjust the chamber geometry to optimise performance.
[F01C 2021/1612] by changing the eccentricity of an element with respect to another element -- The entry F01C 2021/1612 designates technologies in pumps, compressors or similar rotating machines that modify the eccentricity between one component and another to alter performance characteristics such as flow or pressure, for example by offsetting a rotor relative to a stator or adjusting the position of a crankshaft within a housing.
[F01C 2021/1618] by changing the positions of the inlet and outlet openings with respect to the working chambers -- F01C 2021/1618 covers fluid-handling machines that achieve a functional effect by altering the relative position of inlet and outlet openings in relation to the working chambers. The modification of these openings can change flow direction, pressure distribution or mixing characteristics within the device.
[F01C 2021/1625] with sliding or rotating valves, adjustable in position -- F01C 2021/1625 covers hydraulic or pneumatic pumps that incorporate sliding or rotating valves whose position can be adjusted to regulate flow or pressure. The code is used for designs where the valve geometry is movable, allowing the pump to be tuned for different operating conditions. It falls under the broader CPC group for pumps with variable-type valve mechanisms.
[F01C 2021/1631] with sliding valves controlled by the use of fluid other than the working fluid -- This sub-group of CPC F01C covers fluid-power machines such as pumps, compressors or turbines that incorporate sliding valves whose movement is actuated by a control fluid that is distinct from the primary working fluid of the machine. The definition excludes valve actuation by the same fluid that performs the main power function, focusing on auxiliary-fluid control systems.
[F01C 2021/1637] by changing the form of the radially inner or the radially outer contour of the working chamber -- F01C 2021/1637 classifies inventions that modify the shape of the working chamber by altering either its radially inner contour or its radially outer contour, typically to improve heat transfer, pressure distribution or overall efficiency in steam generators and related equipment.
[F01C 2021/1643] by using valves regulating pressure and flow rate, e.g. discharge valves -- The CPC entry F01C 2021/1643 covers fluid-handling apparatus that controls pressure and flow by means of regulating valves, such as discharge valves, typically employed in pumps, compressors, or similar devices to adjust output conditions.
[F01C 2021/165] using a by-pass channel -- F01C 2021/165 relates to wind turbines that employ a bypass channel to divert part of the airflow around the rotor or through the nacelle. The channel is used to control turbine performance, reduce mechanical loads or improve efficiency under varying wind conditions.
[F01C 2021/1656] being obtained by displacing a lateral sealing face -- This CPC subclass falls under F01C pumps and concerns sealing techniques in which a seal is achieved by moving a lateral sealing face relative to the pump housing or rotor. The method is used to maintain fluid tightness during operation, typically in reciprocating or rotary pump designs.
[F01C 2021/1662] with venting means -- F01C 2021/1662 covers pumps that include venting means, such as openings, valves or passages, designed to discharge air or trapped fluid from the pump housing or suction line. The venting feature helps prevent cavitation, maintain flow stability and improve overall efficiency of the pump system.
[F01C 2021/1668] with several machines or engines connected in series or in parallel -- The code applies to steam-turbine installations in which two or more turbines or engines are linked together in series or in parallel, enabling shared load, staged expansion, or redundant power generation. It covers arrangements where the output of one unit feeds another or where multiple units operate jointly on a common shaft or system.
[F01C 2021/1675] with reversible machines or engines -- The CPC subclass F01C 2021/1675 covers machines or engines that are capable of operating in reverse, such as reversible turbines, compressors, pumps and similar devices. It includes structural features, control methods and operating arrangements that enable the machine to deliver power in both forward and reverse directions. This subclass is used to classify inventions where bidirectional mechanical energy conversion is a key technical element.
[F01C 2021/1681] by varying the rotational speed -- F01C 2021/1681 falls under the CPC class for machines that generate or use mechanical energy, specifically devices whose operation is controlled by varying the rotational speed. It covers turbines, compressors, fans and similar equipment equipped with variable-speed drives or control systems that adjust speed to regulate output, efficiency or load.
[F01C 2021/1687] Safety arrangements -- F01C 2021/1687 classifies safety arrangements for pump installations, covering devices, systems and methods that protect pumps, associated pipelines and equipment from hazards such as over-pressure, overheating, leakage or unauthorised operation. It includes safety valves, alarms, automatic shutdowns, protective housings and monitoring or control mechanisms designed to ensure safe operation.
[F01C 2021/1693] Stopping or starting, idling or no-load operation -- The classification covers pumps and related apparatus that include means for stopping, starting, idling or running without load. It addresses devices, control systems and methods that manage these operating states to reduce wear, conserve energy or facilitate maintenance. The entry applies to any pump where such start-stop or no-load functionality is integral to its design.
[F01C 20/22] by changing the eccentricity between cooperating members -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Internal gear machines with variable eccentricity (i.e. variable in direction and not in quantity)(no change of volume but only relative displacement of the working chamber to the inlet and outlet openings) -> F01C20/10
[F01C 20/24] characterised by using valves for controlling pressure or flow rate, e.g. discharge valves -- The subclass concerns rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines that use valves to regulate pressure or flow, such as discharge valves, as part of the control, monitoring or safety arrangements of the machine.
[F01C 20/26] using bypass channels -- F01C 20/26 covers methods and devices that employ bypass channels in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines for control, monitoring or safety functions, such as diverting fluid flow to adjust pressure, temperature or lubrication during operation.
[F01C 20/265] being obtained by displacing a lateral sealing face -- F01C 20/265 covers sealing arrangements for rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines that achieve a seal by laterally displacing a sealing face, such as moving sealing rings or pads that shift sideways to maintain contact with the cylinder wall. The technique is used to improve sealing reliability and accommodate wear.
[F01C 20/28] Safety arrangements -- F01C 20/28 covers safety arrangements for rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines, encompassing devices and methods that monitor, control, or shut down the machine to prevent hazardous conditions. It includes protective systems, fault detection, and automatic stop mechanisms integrated within mechanical engineering.
[F01C 21/00] Component parts, details or accessories not provided for in groups -- F01C 21/00 classifies any component parts, details or accessories for rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines that are not specifically listed in other groups, such as auxiliary valves, seals, mounting brackets, or specialized fittings designed for these machines.
[F01C 21/001] Injection of a fluid in the working chamber for sealing, cooling and lubricating  sealing only  F01C17/00 ; lubrication only  F01C21/04 ; cooling only  F01C21/06 ; injecting water or steam in internal combustion engines  F02B47/02 ,  F02D21/00 ,  F02M25/00 -- This subclass deals with the introduction of a fluid into the working chamber of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines to perform sealing, cooling or lubrication functions, either alone or in combination. It includes specific provisions for sealing only, lubrication only, cooling only, and the injection of water or steam in internal-combustion engines, cross-referencing related groups such as F01C17/00, F01C21/04, F01C21/06 and the F02 series.
[F01C 21/002] with control systems for the injection of the fluid -- F01C 21/002 covers component parts, details or accessories for rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines that incorporate control systems for injecting fluid. It includes devices such as injection timing valves, electronic or hydraulic controllers, and related mechanisms that regulate the quantity, timing or pressure of the injected fuel or working fluid.
[F01C 21/003] Systems for the equilibration of forces acting on the elements of the machine  interstice adjustment other than by fluid pressure  F01C21/102 -- F01C 21/003 covers mechanical arrangements that equalise forces on components of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines by adjusting clearances or gaps rather than using hydraulic or pneumatic means. These systems typically employ springs, elastomeric elements or geometric compensation to maintain balance and reduce wear.
[F01C 21/005] Internal leakage control
[F01C 21/006] Equalization of pressure pulses  silencing for compressors  F04C29/06
[F01C 21/007] General arrangements of parts; Frames and supporting elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Supports, elastic suspension, outer frame elements of units, foundations.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Flow-sheets, multiple unit assemblies -> F01C11/00 , F04C11/00 , F04C23/00
[F01C 21/008] Driving elements, brakes, couplings, transmissions specially adapted for rotary or oscillating-piston machines or engines  brakes, couplings, transmissions  per se   F16 ,  B60 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All kinds of transmissions e.g. couplings, timing gears, gear boxes, shafts (as for example elastic, hollow), brakes.
[F01C 21/02] Arrangements of bearings -- F01C 21/02 covers the specific layout and mounting of bearings in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines, detailing how bearings are positioned to support shafts, rods or other rotating components. It includes inventive bearing configurations and support structures that are not addressed in broader bearing groups.
[F01C 21/04] Lubrication -- F01C 21/04 covers lubrication arrangements for rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines and pumps, including devices, methods and accessories that supply, distribute or control oil or other lubricants to moving parts.
[F01C 21/045] Control systems for the circulation of the lubricant -- It covers devices and mechanisms that regulate the flow of oil or other lubricants within rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines, including pumps, valves, sensors, and control circuitry designed to maintain proper lubrication pressure and distribution. These systems are not classified elsewhere in the CPC.
[F01C 21/06] Heating -- F01C 21/06 concerns heating devices and elements that are part of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston machines and engines, such as water jackets, heating coils or other means used to raise the temperature of the working fluid or engine components, when these are not covered by other groups.
[F01C 21/08] Rotary pistons -- F01C 21/08 covers the rotary pistons themselves in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines, describing the design, structure, materials and mounting of the piston elements that convert pressure into rotary motion, and includes related accessories that are not classified elsewhere in the group.
[F01C 21/0809] Construction of vanes or vane holders -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of vanes of engines but also of pumps and compressors.
[F01C 21/0818] Vane tracking; control therefor -- F01C 21/0818 covers the tracking of vanes in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines and the control devices that adjust their motion. It includes mechanisms that monitor vane position and regulate it to maintain proper clearance, timing or sealing within the engine housing.
