[F01D 5/187] Convection cooling -- F01D 5/187 deals with convection cooling methods for turbine blades in non-positive displacement machines such as steam turbines. It includes designs that use airflow or gas streams to remove heat from blade surfaces, enhancing efficiency and material durability.
[F01D 5/188] with an insert in the blade cavity to guide the cooling fluid, e.g. forming a separation wall -- F01D 5/188 covers turbine or pump blades that incorporate an internal insert within the blade cavity to direct cooling fluid, such as a separation wall that channels the fluid along a defined path. The feature improves thermal management and structural integrity of non-positive displacement machines like steam turbines.
[F01D 5/189] the insert having a tubular cross-section, e.g. airfoil shape -- F01D 5/189 covers inserts used in turbine blades of non-positive displacement machines such as steam turbines that have a tubular or airfoil cross-section to form the flow passage. The insert is fitted within the blade structure to guide fluid and improve aerodynamic performance.
[F01D 5/20] Specially-shaped blade tips to seal space between tips and stator -- This subclass deals with blade tips that are shaped to create a seal with the surrounding stator, limiting fluid leakage around the rotating blades in steam turbines and similar non-positive displacement machines. The design improves efficiency and reduces wear by maintaining a controlled clearance between the tip and the casing.
[F01D 5/22] Blade-to-blade connections, -- A blade-to-blade connection refers to the method of joining adjacent turbine blades directly to each other rather than to a disc or hub, providing structural continuity and load transfer across the blade row. Techniques include dovetail interlocks, bolted or welded joints, and interlocking geometries that maintain alignment and permit thermal expansion.
[F01D 5/225] by shrouding -- The F01D 5/225 subclass refers to turbine blades that are provided with a shroud, meaning an outer covering or sealing ring surrounding the blade. The shrouding is used to limit coolant or working-fluid leakage between blade rows, increase structural stiffness and improve aerodynamic efficiency in non-positive displacement machines such as steam turbines. This feature is applied to blade designs, manufacturing methods and related assemblies.
[F01D 5/24] using wire or the like -- F01D 5/24 covers methods of constructing or securing turbine or pump blades by employing wire, cords or similar flexible members to hold, reinforce or attach the blade elements, typically in non-positive-displacement machines such as steam turbines. This includes the use of wire loops, strands or woven structures integrated into the blade or its mounting to provide strength, shape retention, or vibration damping.
[F01D 5/26] Antivibration means not restricted to blade form or construction or to blade-to-blade connections -- F01D 5/26 covers antivibration features for turbine or similar non-positive-displacement machine blades that are not tied to a particular blade shape, material or blade-to-blade joint. The provision applies to any device or arrangement that reduces blade vibration regardless of the blade's construction.
[F01D 5/28] Selecting particular materials; Measures against erosion or corrosion -- The F01D 5/28 subclass addresses the selection of specific blade materials and the implementation of protective measures to prevent erosion or corrosion in non-positive-displacement machines such as steam turbines. It covers material choice, surface treatments, coatings and other techniques aimed at preserving blade integrity during operation.
[F01D 5/282] Selecting composite materials, e.g. blades with reinforcing filaments -- The class covers criteria and methods for choosing composite materials for turbine blades and similar components, focusing on the use of reinforcing filaments to achieve required strength, stiffness, heat resistance and durability in non-positive displacement machines such as steam turbines.
[F01D 5/284] Selection of ceramic materials -- The F01D 5/284 subclass covers technical guidance for choosing ceramic materials used in the blades of non-positive displacement machines such as steam turbines. It addresses criteria like high-temperature resistance, strength, wear and corrosion performance, and manufacturing considerations to optimise blade durability and efficiency.
[F01D 5/286] Particular treatment of blades, e.g. to increase durability or resistance against corrosion or erosion  F01D5/288  takes precedence -- The CPC subclass F01D 5/286 covers methods for treating turbine or pump blades to improve their durability, corrosion resistance, or erosion resistance. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, non-positive displacement machines, blades. It is superseded by subclass F01D 5/288.
[F01D 5/288] Protective coatings for blades
[F01D 5/30] Fixing blades to rotors -- The subclass covers methods and devices for attaching blades to the rotors of non-positive-displacement machines such as steam turbines. It includes welding, brazing, bolting, adhesives, mechanical clamping and other fixing techniques that secure blades to the rotor shaft or hub.
[F01D 5/3007] of axial insertion type -- The subclass F01D 5/3007 addresses turbine blades that are mounted by axial insertion, meaning the blades are introduced along the shaft axis into the rotor body. This mounting method is used in non-positive-displacement machines such as steam or gas turbines to simplify blade replacement and improve alignment.
[F01D 5/3015] with side plates -- The CPC subclass F01D 5/3015 covers steam turbine blades equipped with side plates that provide structural support and sealing between the blade and the casing. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, non-positive displacement machines or engines, steam turbines, blades.
[F01D 5/3023] of radial insertion type, e.g. in individual recesses -- The subclass concerns blades for non-positive displacement machines such as steam turbines that are mounted by radial insertion, meaning each blade is placed from the side into a separate recess or slot in the rotor or stator. This arrangement allows individual positioning and replacement of blades within the turbine assembly.
[F01D 5/303] in a circumferential slot -- The classification F01D 5/303 covers turbine or pump blades that are situated in a circumferential slot around the rotor or runner. It includes the design, positioning and manufacturing of blades mounted within such slots in non-positive-displacement machines such as steam turbines.
[F01D 5/3038] the slot having inwardly directed abutment faces on both sides -- F01D 5/3038 covers blade designs in non-positive displacement machines such as steam turbines where a slot is provided with inwardly directed abutment faces on both sides, serving to guide, support or limit blade movement.
[F01D 5/3046] the rotor having ribs around the circumference -- F01D 5/3046 covers rotors that incorporate circumferential ribs as part of the blade structure in non-positive displacement machines such as steam turbines. The ribs reinforce the rotor and may improve aerodynamic or mechanical performance.
[F01D 5/3053] by means of pins -- The subclass concerns non-positive displacement machines such as steam turbines where the blades are mounted, supported or guided by pins, which act as pivots or fastening elements for individual or grouped blades. It includes designs that use pin-based attachment or positioning of the blades.
[F01D 5/3061] by welding, brazing -- F01D 5/3061 covers turbine blades for non-positive displacement machines such as steam turbines that are joined to a disc or other parts by welding or brazing. The code applies to blade attachment methods that use fusion or filler-metal processes.
[F01D 5/3069] between two discs or rings -- A blade arrangement where the blades are mounted in the space between two concentric discs or rings, typically used in non-positive displacement machines such as steam turbines. The configuration defines the rotor-stator geometry for directing fluid flow across the blades. It falls under the CPC class for mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, non-positive displacement machines, and specifically blade constructions.
[F01D 5/3076] Sheet metal discs -- F01D 5/3076 pertains to sheet metal discs employed as blades in non-positive displacement machines such as steam turbines. It includes flat or slightly curved metal discs that are mounted on a rotor or stator to interact with fluid flow and extract or impart energy. The classification covers the design, material and arrangement of these disc-type blade components.
[F01D 5/3084] the blades being made of ceramics -- This entry classifies ceramic blades used in non-positive displacement machines such as steam turbines. It falls under mechanical engineering → engines or pumps → non-positive displacement machines → blades and is identified by the code F01D 5/3084, which specifies the material of the blades as ceramics.
[F01D 5/3092] Protective layers between blade root and rotor disc surfaces, e.g. anti-friction layers  F01D5/288  takes precedence -- It concerns protective or anti-friction layers applied at the interface between turbine blade roots and the rotor disc surfaces. Such layers reduce wear and friction, helping maintain the integrity of the attachment. The entry falls under non-positive displacement machines, specifically steam turbines.
[F01D 5/32] Locking, e.g. by final locking blades or keys -- F01D 5/32 concerns locking devices for non-positive displacement machines such as steam turbines, specifically mechanisms that secure rotor or stator blades in place. The entry includes systems employing final locking blades, keys, or similar components that prevent blade movement during operation or maintenance.
[F01D 5/323] Locking of axial insertion type blades by means of a key or the like parallel to the axis of the rotor -- The entry F01D 5/323 covers a method of securing axial-insertion type blades in non-positive displacement machines such as steam turbines by using a key or similar device arranged parallel to the rotor axis. This locking approach ensures the blades remain firmly attached during operation.
[F01D 5/326] Locking of axial insertion type blades by other means -- The subclass covers ways of fixing axial-insertion turbine blades by means other than the usual attachment, such as mechanical locks, retaining rings, clamping devices or other securing elements. It is part of the mechanical-engineering branch for engines or pumps, specifically non-positive-displacement machines like steam turbines, under the blades group.
[F01D 5/34] Rotor-blade aggregates of unitary construction -- A rotor-blade aggregate of unitary construction is a single, integral assembly in which the rotating blades are formed as one piece with the hub, typically by casting or forging, rather than being separately attached. This design is used in non-positive displacement machines such as steam turbines to improve balance, strength and reduce assembly complexity.
[F01D 7/00] Rotors with blades adjustable in operation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Rotors with blades adjustable in operation, e.g. unshrouded fans with a single rotor or two counter-rotating rotors  taken from US2006188375  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Rotors for reversing -> F01D1/30
[F01D 7/02] having adjustment responsive to speed -- F01D 7/02 refers to rotors with blades whose angles can be varied automatically according to the operating speed of the machine, enabling performance optimisation during run-time. The provision applies to non-positive displacement engines such as steam turbines, where blade adjustment is responsive to speed changes.
[F01D 9/00] Stators -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Stators, i.e. stationary fluid guiding arrangements such as nozzles etc
[F01D 9/02] Nozzles -- F01D 9/02 covers nozzles that form part of the stator assembly in non-positive displacement machines such as steam turbines. The classification includes designs for directing fluid flow onto moving blades or other components and related features that affect performance and efficiency.
[F01D 9/023] Transition ducts between combustor cans and first stage of the turbine in gas-turbine engines; their cooling or sealings -- F01D 9/023 covers the design and treatment of transition ducts that link the combustor cans to the first-stage turbine in gas-turbine engines, focusing on methods for cooling these ducts and providing seals to prevent gas leakage and maintain efficient flow.
[F01D 9/026] Scrolls for radial machines or engines -- F01D 9/026 covers scroll devices used in radial machines or engines, such as centrifugal pumps and turbines, where a stationary and a rotating element form a spiral passage that guides fluid to add or extract energy. These scrolls are applied in non-positive displacement installations within the stator section of steam-turbine-type machines.
[F01D 9/04] forming ring or sector -- The F01D 9/04 subclass deals with the manufacturing of stator components that are formed as rings or sector-shaped sections for non-positive-displacement machines like steam turbines. It includes processes such as casting, forging, machining, and assembling of these stator rings or sectors, which provide the stationary part of the turbine's rotor-stator assembly.
[F01D 9/041] using blades  F01D5/148  takes precedence -- The subclass F01D 9/041 relates to stators of non-positive displacement machines, such as steam turbines, that employ blades to guide the working fluid. This group is overridden by the more specific subclass F01D 5/148.
[F01D 9/042] fixing blades to stators  fixing stator-rings in the casing or to each other  F01D25/246
[F01D 9/044] permanently, e.g. by welding, brazing, casting or the like -- F01D 9/044 relates to stators of non-positive displacement machines such as steam turbines that are permanently attached to the rotor or housing by processes like welding, brazing, casting or similar methods, rather than being removable or loosely fitted. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering → engines or pumps → non-positive displacement machines → stators.
[F01D 9/045] for radial flow machines or engines -- The F01D 9/045 subclass deals with stators specifically designed for radial-flow machines or engines, such as steam turbines. It covers the stationary components that direct fluid or gas radially through the rotor, including the geometry, mounting, and flow-guiding features that optimize performance in non-positive-displacement, radial-flow power devices.
[F01D 9/047] Nozzle boxes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Nozzle boxes, i.e. usually a bladed section of a guiding apparatus with inner and outer shrouds segments that when assembled with several other nozzle boxes results in a stator structure. Two or four nozzle boxes form a complete circular fluid supply assembly
[F01D 9/048] for radial admission -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Nozzles or nozzles boxes forming a ring or a sector and in which working fluid is supplied radially onto a turbine stage or a turbine wheel
[F01D 9/06] Fluid supply conduits to nozzles or the like -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The fluid conduits themselves, i.e. anything that supplies fluid into the casing of the turbine or ducts fluid around the casing, e.g. from a bleed valve to the user or a hot section of the turbine
[F01D 9/065] Fluid supply or removal conduits traversing the working fluid flow, e.g. for lubrication-, cooling-, or sealing fluids  see also  F01D25/16 ,  F01D25/24  and  F01D25/26 -- F01D 9/065 addresses conduits that intersect the working-fluid flow in non-positive displacement machines such as steam turbines, providing paths for lubrication, cooling or sealing fluids. The passages are incorporated in the stator and traverse the main fluid stream.
[F01K] STEAM ENGINE PLANTS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All aspects of power plants using steam, vapour or special working fluids in a thermodynamic cycle. It covers also steam or vapour accumulators and power plants which are characterised by steam or heat accumulators, by the use of specific types of engines and by condensers and by the use of steam or condensate extracted from the power plant. Furthermore general lay-out, control and methods of operation of complete power plants and methods of converting heat or fluid energy into mechanical energy are covered.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Non-positive displacement machines or engines, like steam turbines -> F01D - Internal-combustion piston engines -> F02B - Gas turbine plants -> F02C - Jet propulsion plants -> F02K - Refrigeration machines, plants and systems -> F25B - Nuclear power plants and engine arrangements therein -> G21D  **Glossary:** - CHP: Combined Heat and Power, Cogeneration - Trigeneration: Combined production of heat, power and cold - HRSG: Heat Recovery Steam Generator - CCS: Carbon capture and storagecarbon capture and sequestration - Combined cycle: The exhaust of one heat engine is used as the heat source for another - FBC: Fluidized Bed Combustion - PFBC: Pressurised Fluidized Bed Combustion - APFBC: Advanced Pressurised Fluidized Bed Combustion - GFBCC: Gasification Fluidized Bed Combustion Combined cycle systems - CHIPPS: Combustion-based High Performance Power System
[F01K 1/00] Steam accumulators -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Steam or vapour accumulators acting as an energy storage device including:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Use of accumulators in steam engine plants -> F01K3/00
[F01K 1/02] for storing steam otherwise than in a liquid -- F01K 1/02 covers arrangements for retaining steam in the gaseous phase rather than condensing it, such as steam accumulators, pressurised vessels or insulated chambers that hold super-heated or saturated steam for later use in engines or processes. The code belongs to the CPC subclass for steam engine plants and addresses non-liquid storage of steam.
[F01K 1/04] for storing steam in a liquid, e.g. Ruth's type -- F01K 1/04 covers steam accumulators that store steam within a liquid medium, a technology used in steam engine plants to smooth out pressure fluctuations and improve efficiency. Such accumulators, exemplified by Ruth's type, allow rapid release of steam and can act as buffers between boiler output and engine demand.
[F01K 1/06] Internal fittings facilitating steam distribution, steam formation, or circulation -- F01K 1/06 designates the internal components of steam accumulators that assist in distributing, forming, or circulating steam, including pipes, valves, baffles and similar fittings integrated within the accumulator vessel.
[F01K 1/08] Charging or discharging of accumulators with steam -- A method or device for filling or emptying steam accumulators by means of steam, including the associated valves, controls and feed-or discharge arrangements that manage stored steam energy within a steam-engine plant. This covers both charging the accumulator with steam and drawing steam from it for use.
[F01K 1/10] specially adapted for superheated steam -- F01K 1/10 designates steam accumulators specially adapted for superheated steam, covering devices that store and release superheated steam within steam engine plant systems. These accumulators are built to withstand higher temperatures and pressures than standard steam accumulators, enabling efficient energy storage and delivery in high-temperature applications.
[F01K 11/00] Plants characterised by the engines being structurally combined with boilers or condensers -- The entry F01K 11/00 covers plant designs in which the engine is structurally integrated with a boiler or a condenser, typical of steam-engine installations where the power unit and its heat source are combined into a single compact assembly.
[F01K 11/02] the engines being turbines -- F01K 11/02 pertains to steam-engine plant arrangements in which the engine portion is a turbine. It covers configurations where the turbine is structurally integrated with a boiler or condenser as part of the overall steam-engine system. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and steam-engine plants.
[F01K 11/04] the boilers or condensers being rotated in use -- F01K 11/04 covers steam engine plants where the boiler or condenser is mounted on a rotating frame and turns together with the engine during operation. This arrangement allows the heat source or heat sink to move with the crankshaft, often used in marine or mobile applications.
[F01K 1/12] Multiple accumulators -- F01K 1/12 covers steam-engine installations that incorporate two or more steam accumulators. The subclass deals with the design, arrangement, linking and control of multiple accumulators to improve steam supply stability, pressure regulation, or energy storage in steam plants.
[F01K 1/14] Circulation -- F01K 1/14 pertains to the circulation of fluid in steam accumulators, covering the flow paths and distribution systems that move water or steam between the accumulator and the engine or other plant components. It includes methods and apparatus for ensuring efficient transfer and uniform temperature throughout the accumulator.
[F01K 1/16] Other safety or control means -- The CPC subclass F01K 1/16 addresses safety and control devices employed in steam accumulators, covering equipment that monitors or regulates pressure, temperature or flow to prevent hazardous conditions. It includes pressure relief valves, safety shut-off mechanisms, alarm systems and other auxiliary means that protect the accumulator system during operation.
[F01K 1/18] for steam pressure -- Steam accumulators are vessels that store steam under pressure for later discharge, providing a reserve that smooths fluctuations in supply and enhances the efficiency and responsiveness of steam engine plants. They are used to maintain stable steam pressure, reduce load variations on boilers, and improve overall system performance.
[F01K 1/20] Other steam-accumulator parts, details, or accessories -- F01K 1/20 relates to the ancillary components of steam accumulators, covering miscellaneous parts, details and accessories such as fittings, valves and other auxiliary elements that are not classified elsewhere in steam-accumulator technology.
[F01K 13/00] General layout or general methods of operation of complete plants -- F01K 13/00 covers the overall arrangement and operating procedures for complete steam-engine installations, including how the various components such as boilers, cylinders, condensers and auxiliary equipment are integrated and coordinated. It addresses design choices that affect plant efficiency, control strategies and the layout of piping, drive, and supporting systems for the entire plant.
[F01K 13/003] Arrangements for measuring or testing  in general  G01 -- F01K 13/003 covers general arrangements for measuring or testing in steam-engine plant installations, encompassing the overall layout of sensors, gauges and diagnostic equipment used to monitor performance, efficiency and safety of a complete steam-engine system. It addresses the integration of measurement devices into the plant's operational scheme rather than specific test procedures.
[F01K 13/006] Auxiliaries or details not otherwise provided for -- F01K 13/006 classifies the auxiliary components and miscellaneous details of complete steam-engine plants that are not covered by more specific sub-categories, such as supporting equipment, ancillary devices or particular arrangements that facilitate the overall operation of the plant.
[F01K 13/02] Controlling, e.g. stopping or starting -- This CPC subclass covers control mechanisms for steam engine plants, describing methods and devices used to start, stop, or regulate the operation of complete installations. It includes control systems, sensors and actuators that manage plant performance.
[F01K 13/025] Cooling the interior by injection during idling or stand-by -- F01K 13/025 covers methods for cooling the interior of a steam engine plant by injecting a cooling medium while the engine is idling or on standby. The technique is applied to reduce temperature buildup in the boiler or internal components during periods of no-load, improving efficiency and preventing damage.
[F01K 15/00] Adaptations of plants for special use -- The code F01K 15/00 covers modifications and special fittings of steam-engine installations intended to meet particular operating conditions or applications, such as mobile, marine, high-altitude, or uniquely constrained environments. It includes structural changes, auxiliary equipment, and control adaptations that tailor a plant's performance, safety or efficiency for these specialized uses.
[F01K 15/02] for driving vehicles, e.g. locomotives -- F01K 15/02 pertains to steam-engine plant adaptations designed for vehicle propulsion, notably locomotives. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, steam engine plants, and adaptations of plants for special use, covering modifications that enable steam engines to drive transport machines.
[F01K 15/025] the vehicle being a steam locomotive -- F01K 15/025 covers steam locomotives as a specific adaptation of steam-engine plants for vehicular use. The classification addresses the design, construction and operation of locomotive units that convert steam power into motive force for rail transport, including associated auxiliary components and control systems.
[F01K 15/04] the vehicles being waterborne vessels -- F01K 15/04 classifies steam-engine installations that are specially adapted for waterborne vessels, covering propulsion units and auxiliary machinery designed to operate in marine environments such as ships, boats and other floating craft.
[F01K 15/045] Control thereof  F01K3/22 ,  F01K7/00 ,  F01K13/02  take precedence -- F01K 15/045 covers the control of steam-engine plant adaptations for special uses, falling under Mechanical Engineering → Engines or Pumps → Steam Engine Plants → Adaptations of plants for special use. It specifies control methods that take precedence over the related subclasses F01K 3/22, F01K 7/00 and F01K 13/02.
[F01K 17/00] Using steam or condensate extracted or exhausted from steam engine plant -- F01K 17/00 covers methods and arrangements for utilizing the steam or condensate that is taken from or released by a steam-engine system, such as recirculating exhaust steam for heating, feedwater pre-heating, or power generation. It pertains to the efficient use of waste steam in mechanical engineering contexts.
[F01K 17/005] by means of a heat pump  heat pumps systems  per se   F25B
[F01K 17/02] for heating purposes, e.g. industrial, domestic -- F01K 17/02 covers the use of steam or condensate taken from a steam-engine plant to provide heat, including systems that channel waste steam for industrial or domestic heating purposes. The subclass addresses methods and apparatus that recycle engine exhaust steam for space-heating, process-heat, or similar thermal applications.
[F01K 17/025] in combination with at least one gas turbine, e.g. a combustion gas turbine -- This subclass covers steam-engine power plants that are combined with at least one gas turbine, such as a combustion gas turbine, to form hybrid or combined-cycle systems. The arrangement typically uses the gas turbine's exhaust heat to generate steam for the steam engine, improving overall efficiency. It applies to configurations where both types of turbines operate together in a single power-generation installation.
[F01K 17/04] for specific purposes other than heating -- F01K 17/04 covers steam-engine installations that use the steam or condensate for purposes other than heating, such as driving machinery, generating power, or providing propulsion. It is nested under mechanical engineering, then engines or pumps, and finally steam-engine plants.
[F01K 17/06] Returning energy of steam, in exchanged form, to process, e.g. use of exhaust steam for drying solid fuel or plant -- It covers techniques that harness the energy of steam leaving a steam-engine plant and apply it in another process, such as using exhaust steam to dry solid fuel, heat material or operate auxiliary equipment. The focus is on energy recovery and reuse rather than further power generation.
[F01K 19/00] Regenerating or otherwise treating steam exhausted from steam engine plant -- F01K 19/00 covers technologies for handling the exhaust steam from steam-engine installations, such as recovering, reheating, or otherwise treating the steam to improve efficiency or reduce emissions. It includes condensing, reheating, and regeneration processes applied after the steam has performed work.
[F01K 19/02] Regenerating by compression -- F01K 19/02 covers methods for recovering energy from exhaust steam of steam-engine or turbine installations by compressing the steam, raising its pressure and temperature so it can be reused in the cycle or for other purposes. It is situated under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, steam engine plants, and deals specifically with regeneration of exhausted steam.
[F01K 19/04] in combination with cooling or heating -- The F01K 19/04 subclass covers arrangements for regenerating or otherwise treating steam exhausted from a steam-engine plant by linking the exhaust system with a cooling or heating circuit, so that the waste steam is reused for temperature control or energy recovery within the same installation.
[F01K 19/06] in engine cylinder -- F01K 19/06 covers features or processes applied directly inside the cylinder of a steam engine to regenerate, treat or otherwise manage the exhaust steam as it leaves the piston chamber. This includes devices or methods that condition the spent steam before it is released to the exhaust system, aiming to improve efficiency, reduce heat loss, or recover energy within the cylinder itself.
[F01K 19/08] compression done by injection apparatus, jet blower, or the like -- F01K 19/08 classifies methods for compressing steam in steam-engine plant exhaust treatment using injection apparatus, jet blowers or similar devices. The entry covers techniques that increase the pressure of the exhausted steam to facilitate regeneration or other processing steps.
[F01K 19/10] Cooling exhaust steam other than by condenser -- F01K 19/10 covers methods for lowering the temperature of exhaust steam from steam-engine plants without using a condenser. It includes direct water sprays, indirect cooling in heat exchangers and other techniques that dissipate heat to the environment.
[F01K 21/00] Steam engine plants not otherwise provided for -- F01K 21/00 covers steam engine plants that do not fit into any other more specific categories. It includes the overall arrangement and auxiliary components of steam engines not otherwise provided for, such as general plant layouts and supporting infrastructure.
[F01K 21/005] using mixtures of liquid and steam or evaporation of a liquid by expansion -- The entry covers methods in steam-engine plants that employ a mixture of liquid and steam or that evaporate a liquid by means of expansion. It includes techniques that use combined phases to improve the working fluid's properties or to generate power, where such processes are not otherwise classified.
[F01K 21/02] with steam-generation in engine-cylinders -- This CPC subclass F01K 21/02 covers steam engine plants where the boiler or steam-generating component is integrated into the engine cylinder, producing steam directly inside the cylinder during operation. It falls under Mechanical Engineering to Engines or Pumps to Steam Engine Plants and is used for inventions that locate the heat source within the cylinder itself.
[F01K 21/04] using mixtures of steam and gas -- F01K 21/04 covers steam-engine plants that operate with a working medium formed by a mixture of steam and another gas, such as combustion gases or air, rather than pure steam. The classification applies to designs that use the combined fluid to improve start-up, efficiency or power output in engines or pumps not otherwise specified.
[F01K 21/042] pure steam being expanded in a motor somewhere in the plant  F01K21/045  takes precedence -- F01K 21/042 concerns steam engine plants where pure steam, free of water, is expanded in a motor located elsewhere in the plant and not covered by more specific groups. It addresses configurations that use the steam directly for mechanical work in a separate motor unit.
[F01K 21/045] Introducing gas and steam separately into the motor, e.g. admission to a single rotor through separate nozzles -- The code relates to steam engine plants where gas and steam are supplied to the same motor via distinct inlet passages or nozzles, allowing separate control of each fluid. This arrangement is used to improve start-up, efficiency or power output by combining combustion gases with steam.
[F01K 21/047] having at least one combustion gas turbine -- This subclass covers steam engine installations that incorporate at least one combustion gas turbine, either to drive the steam generator or to provide supplemental power. The gas turbine may be directly coupled to the steam cycle or used for auxiliary functions within the plant.
[F01K 21/06] Treating live steam, other than thermodynamically, e.g. for fighting deposits in engine -- The F01K 21/06 subclass covers techniques for treating live steam in steam-engine installations that are not based on thermodynamic changes, such as cleaning, preventing or removing deposits, and other non-thermal conditioning methods applied to the steam before or during use.
[F01K 23/00] Plants characterised by more than one engine delivering power external to the plant, the engines being driven by different fluids -- The class refers to industrial installations that employ two or more separate engines whose output is delivered outside the plant, each engine being driven by a different working fluid such as steam, gas, hydraulic oil or another medium. It covers combined-engine arrangements for power generation or drive functions.
[F01K 23/02] the engine cycles being thermally coupled -- This subclass refers to steam-engine installations that use more than one engine powered by different working fluids, with the cycles thermally linked so that heat from one engine is transferred to another. The arrangement improves overall efficiency by reusing waste heat.
[F01K 23/04] condensation heat from one cycle heating the fluid in another cycle -- A steam-engine plant that includes two or more engines powered by different working fluids, where the heat released during condensation in one cycle is transferred to heat the fluid of another cycle, improving overall efficiency.
[F01K 23/06] combustion heat from one cycle heating the fluid in another cycle -- F01K 23/06 relates to multi-engine steam plant arrangements in which the combustion heat produced in one thermodynamic cycle is transferred to the working fluid of a second cycle, allowing engines driven by different fluids to share energy and improve overall efficiency.
[F01K 23/061] with combustion in a fluidised bed  plants with a fluidised-bed combustor comprising only gas-turbines  F02C3/205 ; fluidised-bed apparatus  per se   B01J8/18 ; fluidised-bed combustors  F23C10/00 ; fluidised-bed steam-boilers  F22B31/0007
[F01K 23/062] the combustion bed being pressurised  pressurised fluid bed combustion  per se   F23C10/16
[F01K 23/064] in combination with an industrial process, e.g. chemical, metallurgical -- F01K 23/064 classifies steam-engine plant configurations that employ two or more engines driven by different fluids to supply power external to the plant, and that are integrated with an industrial operation such as a chemical or metallurgical process. It covers combined-engine systems used to augment or drive such processes.
[F01K 23/065] the combustion taking place in an internal combustion piston engine, e.g. a diesel engine -- The CPC subclass F01K 23/065 covers the combustion process that occurs in internal-combustion piston engines, for example diesel engines, within steam-engine plant arrangements that employ multiple engines driven by different fluids. It focuses on how fuel is ignited and burned to produce power in such piston-type engines.
[F01K 23/067] the combustion heat coming from a gasification or pyrolysis process, e.g. coal gasification  gas turbines with fuel gasifiers  F02C3/28
[F01K 23/068] in combination with an oxygen producing plant, e.g. an air separation plant -- This subclass addresses steam-engine plants that employ two or more engines driven by different fluids and are combined with an oxygen-producing facility such as an air-separation plant, covering the integration of the power units with the oxygen-generation process.
[F01K 23/08] with working fluid of one cycle heating the fluid in another cycle -- F01K 23/08 covers steam engine installations in which the working fluid of one thermodynamic cycle transfers heat to the working fluid of a second cycle, allowing the latter to be heated by the former. This inter-cycle heat exchange is used in combined-cycle or cogeneration plants where multiple engines driven by different fluids share heat to improve overall efficiency.
[F01K 23/10] with exhaust fluid of one cycle heating the fluid in another cycle -- F01K 23/10 covers combined-cycle power plants that use two or more external engines each driven by a different working fluid, where the exhaust from one cycle is employed to heat the fluid of another cycle, improving overall efficiency.
[F01K 23/101] Regulating means specially adapted therefor  F01K23/105 ,  F01K23/108  take precedence
[F01K 23/103] with afterburner in exhaust boiler -- F01K 23/103 covers steam plant arrangements that employ multiple external engines powered by different fluids and incorporate an afterburner in the exhaust boiler to enhance combustion and improve heat recovery.
[F01K 23/105] Regulating means specially adapted therefor -- This CPC subclass F01K 23/105 concerns control or regulating devices that are specifically designed for steam engine plants that use more than one engine powered by different fluids. The devices adjust parameters such as speed, load distribution or fluid flow to coordinate the multiple engines.
[F01K 23/106] with water evaporated or preheated at different pressures in exhaust boiler -- F01K 23/106 covers steam-engine plant arrangements that use multiple engines powered by different fluids, where the exhaust boiler evaporates or preheats water at varying pressures. This sub-class specifies plants in which each engine's exhaust steam is treated under distinct pressure conditions to improve overall efficiency.
[F01K 23/108] Regulating means specially adapted therefor -- The subclass F01K 23/108 covers regulation devices that are specially designed for steam-engine plants that use two or more engines powered by different fluids to deliver external power, addressing control mechanisms adapted to the unique operating conditions of such multi-engine systems.
[F01K 23/12] the engines being mechanically coupled -- This CPC subclass F01K 23/12 covers steam-engine plants that use more than one engine to supply power external to the plant, where each engine is operated by a different working fluid. The subclass specifically addresses arrangements in which the separate engines are mechanically coupled together.
[F01K 23/14] including at least one combustion engine -- F01K 23/14 covers steam-engine plant arrangements that use more than one engine to deliver power externally, where the engines are driven by different working fluids and at least one of the engines is a combustion engine. This subclass refines the broader category of steam engine plants with multiple power sources, specifying mixed-fluid configurations that combine combustion-driven and other types of engines.
[F01K 23/16] all the engines being turbines -- F01K 23/16 covers steam-engine plants that employ multiple external power units, where every unit is a turbine and each turbine is driven by a different fluid. The classification applies to arrangements in which two or more turbines supply power to the plant rather than a single engine. This sub-group falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and steam engine plants.
[F01K 23/18] characterised by adaptation for specific use -- F01K 23/18 covers steam-engine installations that use two or more engines, each driven by a different fluid, and that are specially adapted for a particular function such as heating, propulsion or process work. The code addresses how these multi-engine plants are configured and integrated to meet the requirements of the designated use.
[F01K 25/00] Plants or engines characterised by use of special working fluids, not otherwise provided for -- The F01K 25/00 subclass covers mechanical plants or engines that employ special working fluids-such as unconventional liquids, gases, or two-phase media-to achieve power generation or motion, where the fluid's properties are essential to the device's operation and are not addressed in other CPC groups.
[F01K 25/005] the working fluid being steam, created by combustion of hydrogen with oxygen -- This subclass covers steam engine plants whose working fluid is steam generated through direct combustion of hydrogen with oxygen, rather than from conventional fuels. The system produces water vapor as the driving medium for the engine.
[F01K 25/02] the fluid remaining in the liquid phase -- F01K 25/02 covers steam-engine or related plant where the working fluid is retained in the liquid phase, such as condensate that is collected, stored or recirculated rather than being exhausted as vapor. It applies to systems using special liquids that are not otherwise specified in the classification. This includes apparatus for handling, re-using, or conditioning the liquid working medium within the engine plant.
[F01K 25/04] the fluid being in different phases, e.g. foamed -- F01K 25/04 covers steam-engine or related plants where the working fluid exists in more than one phase during operation, such as mixtures of liquid and vapor or foamed fluids. This subclass addresses the design and control features needed to handle phase-changing or foaming media within the engine system.
[F01K 25/06] using mixtures of different fluids -- F01K 25/06 relates to steam-engine or plant designs that employ mixtures of two or more working fluids. It covers devices and methods where blended fluids are introduced or circulated to modify properties such as heat-transfer efficiency, lubricity, pressure control or safety.
[F01K 25/065] with an absorption fluid remaining at least partly in the liquid state, e.g. water for ammonia  F01K5/00  takes precedence -- F01K 25/065 covers steam engine plants that employ a special working fluid in which the absorption fluid remains at least partially liquid, for example water used with ammonia cycles. This subgroup falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and steam engine plants, and it is a child of F01K5/00.
[F01K 25/08] using special vapours -- This subclass covers steam-engine installations that use unconventional or specially selected vapours as the working fluid, such as organic or mixed-fluid vapours rather than water. It includes designs where the choice of vapor is central to the plant's operation and not covered by other more specific categories.
[F01K 25/085] the vapour being sulfur -- F01K 25/085 covers steam-engine plants that employ sulfur vapour as the working fluid. It includes engines or boilers where sulfur, rather than water, is vaporised to transmit energy and related arrangements for handling the fluid.
[F01K 25/10] the vapours being cold, e.g. ammonia, carbon dioxide, ether -- This subclass concerns steam engine plants that use cold vapours - such as ammonia, carbon dioxide or ether - as the working fluid, a special fluid not otherwise covered elsewhere in the classification.
[F01K 25/103] Carbon dioxide  F01K25/065  takes precedence -- This subclass covers steam engine or pump installations that employ carbon dioxide as the working fluid, used in place of water or other fluids. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, steam engine plants, and is superseded by the broader carbon dioxide subclass F01K25/065.
[F01K 25/106] Ammonia  F01K25/065  takes precedence
[F01K 25/12] the vapours being metallic, e.g. mercury -- F01K 25/12 covers steam-engine or pump plants that employ metallic vapours, such as mercury, as the working fluid. It applies to engines or systems where the fluid's metallic nature is a distinguishing feature not addressed by other categories. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and steam-engine plants.
[F01K 25/14] using industrial or other waste gases -- F01K 25/14 covers steam engine plants that utilize industrial or other waste gases as the working fluid, converting the energy in these gases into steam to drive the engine. The classification applies to designs where waste gases are fed to a boiler or heat exchanger to generate steam for power generation.
[F01K 27/00] Plants for converting heat or fluid energy into mechanical energy, not otherwise provided for -- F01K 27/00 covers plant designs that transform heat or fluid energy into mechanical power where the conversion method is not covered by other more specific subclasses. It includes unconventional or integrated systems for steam, hot-water, or other thermal-fluid driven engines that do not fit existing categories. The class sits under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, steam engine plants.
[F01K 27/005] by means of hydraulic motors -- This subclass includes steam-engine installations that generate mechanical power by driving hydraulic motors, using pressurised fluid from the plant to produce rotational motion instead of direct piston-crank mechanisms. It applies to systems where hydraulic actuation converts heat or fluid energy into mechanical output.
[F01K 27/02] Plants modified to use their waste heat, other than that of exhaust, e.g. engine-friction heat -- Plants that recover and utilize waste heat-such as heat generated by engine friction rather than exhaust gases-to improve overall efficiency, typically by diverting that energy to auxiliary processes or additional power generation within steam-engine type systems. This modification enhances performance without altering the primary engine cycle.
[F01K 3/00] Steam engine plants -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This group covers the use of steam/vapour accumulators, in general heat accumulators and intermediate steam/vapour heaters in power plant arrangements and in arrangements adapted for a specific use like for vehicle drive. Control of said devices is also covered.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Regenerating exhaust steam -> F01K19/00 - Heat-transfer, heat-exchange or heat-storage materials -> C09K5/00  **Glossary:** - Ruth's type steam accumulator: accumulator for storing steam in a liquid (variable pressure system)
[F01K 3/002] Steam conversion -- F01K 3/002 - Steam conversion refers to the part of steam engine plant technology that deals with converting the thermal energy of steam into mechanical motion. It includes designs, devices and processes that transform steam pressure or temperature into rotational or linear output, such as pistons, turbines, and associated control systems.
[F01K 3/004] Accumulation in the liquid branch of the circuit -- The code F01K 3/004 covers provisions for collecting and storing liquid within the hydraulic circuit of a steam-engine plant, such as condensers, reservoirs or feed-water accumulators that maintain steady flow and pressure in the system.
[F01K 3/006] Accumulators and steam compressors -- This subclass covers devices that store or raise the pressure of steam within steam-engine installations, including steam accumulators that receive and discharge steam at a roughly constant pressure and steam compressors that increase steam pressure for reuse or distribution. It relates to the handling of pressurised steam in mechanical-engineering contexts.
[F01K 3/008] Use of steam accumulators of the Ruth type for storing steam in water; Regulating thereof  Ruth accumulators  per se   F01K1/04 -- The entry covers the use of Ruth-type steam accumulators to store steam in water within steam engine plants and the methods for regulating their operation. It falls under the broader class of steam engine plant technologies dealing with accumulator devices for steam storage and control.
[F01K 3/02] Use of accumulators and specific engine types -- The subclass F01K 3/02 addresses steam-engine installations that incorporate energy-storage devices such as accumulators or employ particular engine configurations. It covers the use of accumulators to smooth power delivery, store surplus steam energy, or assist auxiliary functions, and includes specialized engine types like compound or double-acting steam engines within steam-engine plants.
[F01K 3/04] the engine being of multiple-inlet-pressure type -- F01K 3/04 covers steam engine plants whose cylinders admit steam at more than one pressure level, typically using separate inlet ports for high- and low-pressure steam. This multiple-inlet-pressure arrangement improves efficiency by allowing staged expansion and better control of the engine's power output.
[F01K 3/06] the engine being of extraction or non-condensing type -- F01K 3/06 covers steam-engine plants whose engines operate as extraction or non-condensing types, meaning the steam is expanded and exhausted without a separate condenser, often to provide lower-pressure power or to make use of waste heat directly in the process.
[F01K 3/08] Use of accumulators and the plant being specially adapted for a specific use -- The CPC code F01K 3/08 covers steam-engine installations that incorporate accumulators-devices for storing steam, water or other working fluid-to improve performance, and where the entire plant is engineered for a specific purpose such as heating, power generation or process integration. It includes designs that adapt the engine, its auxiliary equipment and control systems to the particular operational requirements of the intended application.
[F01K 3/10] for vehicle drive, e.g. for accumulator locomotives -- The subclass F01K 3/10 covers steam-engine plants that provide propulsion for vehicles, including specialized arrangements such as accumulator locomotives. It pertains to the integration of a steam engine into a vehicle's drive system for direct mechanical movement.
[F01K 3/12] having two or more accumulators -- F01K 3/12 covers steam engine installations that incorporate two or more accumulators, devices that store steam or hot water to regulate pressure and provide reserve energy. Such systems are used to smooth load fluctuations, improve start-up performance, and enhance overall efficiency of the plant.
[F01K 3/14] having both steam accumulator and heater, e.g. superheating accumulator -- The F01K 3/14 subclass covers steam-engine plant designs that incorporate a steam accumulator together with a heater, such as a superheating accumulator, to store and further raise the temperature of steam before it is used in the engine.
[F01K 3/16] Mutual arrangement of accumulator and heater -- The subclass F01K 3/16 addresses steam-engine plant designs in which a steam or water accumulator is integrated with a heater, allowing the stored fluid to be pre-heated before re-entry into the engine cycle, thereby improving temperature control, reducing condensation losses and enhancing overall efficiency.
[F01K 3/18] having heaters -- The F01K 3/18 subgroup concerns steam-engine plants that incorporate heaters, such as feed-water, combustion-air or exhaust-gas heaters, to raise temperature of working fluids and improve thermal efficiency. It covers various heater types - economizers, steam-driven or fire-tube heaters and similar devices - that are integral to the plant's operation.
[F01K 3/181] using nuclear heat  F01K3/26  takes precedence -- F01K 3/181 relates to steam-engine installations in which the heat source is produced by nuclear reactions. It classifies plants that generate steam from nuclear heat for driving pistons, turbines or other mechanical work, and is subordinate to subclass F01K 3/26 covering steam-engine plants using alternative heat sources.
[F01K 3/183] one heater being a fired superheater -- F01K 3/183 covers steam-engine installations in which a single heater functions as a fired superheater, raising the temperature of saturated steam above its boiling point before it reaches the cylinder. The heater is directly heated by combustion, providing superheated steam that improves thermal efficiency and reduces condensate formation in the engine. This sub-class falls under steam engine plants within the broader categories of mechanical engineering and engines or pumps.
[F01K 3/185] using waste heat from outside the plant  F02G5/00  takes precedence -- F01K 3/185 covers methods for supplying a steam-engine plant with waste heat that originates outside the plant, such as heat recovered from external processes or ambient sources. The classification is subordinate to F02G 5/00, which has priority for the same subject matter. Use of external waste heat to improve efficiency or augment steam generation is the focus of this entry.
[F01K 3/186] using electric heat -- F01K 3/186 pertains to steam-engine plants in which electric heating elements are employed to generate the required steam, either as the sole heat source or in combination with conventional fuel-based boilers. This subclass addresses the integration of electrical heat for producing steam in mechanical engine systems.
[F01K 3/188] using heat from a specified chemical reaction -- F01K 3/188 covers steam-engine plant arrangements that obtain the required heat from a specified chemical reaction, such as the exothermic conversion of a fuel or reagent, instead of a conventional boiler. The code includes designs that integrate the reaction source with steam generation or use, addressing heat supply, control and recovery.
[F01K 3/20] with heating by combustion gases of main boiler -- The subclass F01K 3/20 pertains to steam-engine plants whose main boiler is heated by combustion gases. It includes designs in which hot gases from the burning of fuel are directed to the boiler to generate steam, a configuration common in locomotive and stationary steam engines.
[F01K 3/205] more than one circuit being heated by one boiler -- F01K 3/205 concerns steam engine plant designs in which a single boiler provides heat to multiple separate circuits, allowing two or more steam or hot-water loops to be served from one boiler unit.
[F01K 3/22] Controlling, e.g. starting, stopping -- F01K 3/22 pertains to control systems for steam-engine installations, covering devices and methods that start, stop and regulate engine operation. The subclass includes both automatic and manual mechanisms for managing speed, load, and safety functions of the plant.
[F01K 3/24] with heating by separately-fired heaters -- F01K 3/24 designates steam-engine plant arrangements in which the boiler is heated by one or more separately-fired heaters, such as auxiliary burners or fire-tubes, rather than by the primary engine combustion. It covers configurations that use external heat sources to raise steam temperature or aid start-up.
[F01K 3/242] delivering steam to a common mains -- The subclass F01K 3/242 covers systems that distribute generated steam from a boiler to a shared main pipe serving multiple machines or processes. It includes arrangements for collecting, routing, and controlling steam flow to ensure uniform pressure and temperature across the network.
[F01K 3/245] delivering steam at different pressure levels  F01K3/247  takes precedence -- F01K 3/245 refers to arrangements in steam-engine plants that deliver steam at two or more pressure levels, typically by routing high-pressure steam to one circuit and lower-pressure steam to another for different functions. This subgroup is located under the broader class for steam-engine plant distribution systems and is superseded by the more specific F01K 3/247.
[F01K 3/247] one heater being an incinerator -- F01K 3/247 covers steam-engine plant designs in which a single heater also functions as an incinerator, using the heat from waste combustion to produce steam for the engine.
[F01K 3/26] with heating by steam -- F01K 3/26 covers steam-engine plants in which the engine is heated directly by steam, meaning the working fluid that drives the pistons or rotors is generated by a steam source and used to produce mechanical power.
[F01K 3/262] by means of heat exchangers -- This subclass covers steam engine installations in which the boiler or heat source is connected to the engine through heat exchangers rather than direct combustion. It includes arrangements where the working fluid receives thermal energy via secondary circuits, recuperators or economisers, and related control devices.
[F01K 3/265] using live steam for superheating or reheating -- The F01K 3/265 subclass covers steam-engine plant arrangements in which live steam is directed to a superheater or reheater to raise its temperature before or after expansion. It includes methods and devices for using the same steam stream to provide additional heating to improve efficiency or increase output.
[F01K 3/267] by mixing with steam, e.g. LOFFLER-boiler -- This subgroup covers steam-mixing techniques in boiler systems, such as the Loffler boiler where fuel or water is introduced and mixed with steam to enhance heat transfer and combustion efficiency. It belongs to steam engine plants dealing with the generation of steam by direct mixing methods.
[F01K 5/00] Plants characterised by use of means for storing steam in an alkali to increase steam pressure, e.g. of Honigmann or Koenemann type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Storing steam based on thermo chemical energy storage and conversion. Absorbing and desorbing vapour into an alkali solution for discharging heat or producing cold taking into account pressure changes. Furthermore regenerative installations using absorption and desorption are also covered.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Thermodynamic cycles with an absorption fluid remaining at least partly in the liquid state, like Kalina cycles -> F01K25/065  **Glossary:** - Accumulator of Honigmann type: Steam or vapour accumulator based on a process based on the vapour pressure depression of a concentrated solution or adsorbed vapour in comparison to the pure working fluid
[F01K 5/02] used in regenerative installation -- F01K 5/02 classifies steam engine plants that employ regenerative installations where steam is stored in an alkali bath to raise pressure, as in Honigmann or Koenemann systems. The code applies to the use of such alkali-based steam storage means within the overall plant.
[F01K 7/00] Steam engine plants characterised by the use of specific types of engine -- F01K 7/00 relates to steam-engine installations that are distinguished by the particular type of engine employed, such as single-acting reciprocating, double-acting, compound or rotary steam engines, and includes the arrangement of auxiliaries and auxiliary plant adapted to those specific engine designs.
[F01K 7/02] the engines being of multiple-expansion type -- F01K 7/02 refers to steam-engine plants whose engines operate on a multiple-expansion principle, using successive cylinders at decreasing pressures such as double-, triple- or quadruple-expansion arrangements to increase thermal efficiency. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and steam engine plants.
[F01K 7/025] Consecutive expansion in a turbine or a positive displacement engine -- F01K 7/025 relates to steam engine plants that use consecutive expansion of the working fluid, either by passing steam through multiple turbine stages or by employing a series of positive-displacement chambers that expand the fluid in steps to improve overall thermodynamic efficiency.
[F01K 7/04] Control means specially adapted therefor -- F01K 7/04 relates to control means specially adapted for steam engine plants, including regulators, governors and other devices that automatically adjust speed, pressure or load to maintain proper operation. These mechanisms are designed to work directly with the engine's specific characteristics.
[F01K 7/06] the engines being of multiple-inlet-pressure type -- F01K 7/06 classifies steam-engine plants that employ engines of the multiple-inlet-pressure type, meaning the machinery admits steam at more than one pressure level to enhance performance. It is a subclass within mechanical engineering → engines or pumps → steam engine plants, and is used to identify patents dealing with this specific engine configuration.
[F01K 7/08] Control means specially adapted therefor -- This subclass covers devices and systems that regulate or operate steam-engine plants and are specially designed for those engines, including governors, speed-controllers, throttle mechanisms and automatic shut-off devices built into the plant; it does not include generic control gear that is not tailored to steam engines.
[F01K 7/10] characterised by the engine exhaust pressure -- F01K 7/10 covers steam engine plants that are defined by the pressure of the exhaust from the engine, such as systems where the exhaust pressure is a key parameter for operation, control or performance. It includes designs that deliberately regulate or make use of specific exhaust pressure levels to improve efficiency or to meet particular functional requirements.
[F01K 7/12] of condensing type -- F01K 7/12 pertains to steam engine plants of the condensing type, in which exhaust steam is condensed to create a pressure differential that drives the piston or turbine, thereby improving efficiency by recovering latent heat. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and steam engine plants.
[F01K 7/14] Control means specially adapted therefor -- F01K 7/14 covers control devices that are specially designed for steam-engine plants, such as governors, throttles, and other regulating mechanisms that adjust speed, pressure or power output. These means are adapted to the specific operating characteristics of steam engines.
[F01K 7/16] the engines being only of turbine type -- F01K 7/16 classifies steam-engine plant arrangements where the power-producing unit is solely a turbine, not a reciprocating engine, and includes any associated generators, condensers and auxiliary components. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engines or Pumps → Steam Engine Plants.
[F01K 7/165] Controlling means specially adapted therefor -- F01K 7/165 covers control devices that are specially designed for steam-engine plants, such as regulators, governors, speed-control or safety mechanisms that are adapted to the particular characteristics of the engine type they serve.
[F01K 7/18] the turbine being of multiple-inlet-pressure type -- F01K 7/18 denotes steam-engine plant designs in which the turbine receives steam at several inlet pressures, allowing staged expansion for improved efficiency. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, steam engine plants, and covers multiple-inlet-pressure turbine configurations.
[F01K 7/20] Control means specially adapted therefor -- F01K 7/20 covers control devices that are specially designed for steam-engine installations, such as regulators, governors or automatic shut-off mechanisms tailored to the particular characteristics of the plant. These means adjust speed, load or safety functions to optimise performance and protect the engine, and are considered a sub-category of steam-engine plant controls within mechanical engineering.
[F01K 7/22] the turbines having inter-stage steam heating -- This CPC subclass pertains to steam-engine plant configurations in which the turbine incorporates inter-stage steam heating, i.e., steam is reheated between turbine stages to raise efficiency and output. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engines or Pumps → Steam Engine Plants.
[F01K 7/223] Inter-stage moisture separation -- F01K 7/223 covers devices that remove water from the steam between successive expansion stages of a compound steam engine, typically by means of a separator or drain installed in the intermediate pipework. The inter-stage moisture removal improves efficiency and reduces wear by preventing liquid carry-over into downstream cylinders.
[F01K 7/226] Inter-stage steam injection -- Inter-stage steam injection refers to the practice of adding steam between successive stages of a multi-stage steam engine or turbine to boost efficiency, manage temperature, and enhance power output in steam engine plant configurations.
[F01K 7/24] Control or safety means specially adapted therefor -- F01K 7/24 covers control or safety devices specially designed for steam engine plants, such as governors, pressure-relief valves, automatic shut-off mechanisms and other systems that monitor and regulate speed, pressure or load to prevent unsafe operating conditions.
[F01K 7/26] the turbines having inter-stage steam accumulation -- The subclass F01K 7/26 refers to turbines that include steam accumulation chambers or reservoirs between successive stages, allowing steam to be stored temporarily to smooth flow and improve efficiency during operation. This feature is used in steam-engine plant designs to optimise energy conversion and reduce pulsations.
[F01K 7/28] Control means specially adapted therefor -- F01K 7/28 covers control devices designed specifically for steam engine installations, such as governors, throttles or automated regulators that adjust speed, load or fuel intake to optimise performance and safety. These means are tailored to the characteristics of steam engines and differ from generic control systems.
[F01K 7/30] the turbines using exhaust steam only -- F01K 7/30 covers turbines that are powered only by the exhaust steam from a preceding engine, so they do not receive fresh steam directly from a boiler. These turbines are used to recover waste heat and improve overall efficiency of steam engine plants, often in combined-cycle or cogeneration systems.
[F01K 7/32] the engines using steam of critical or overcritical pressure -- This CPC subgroup covers steam engine plants that operate with steam at critical or supercritical pressure, i.e. pressures at or above the fluid's thermodynamic critical point. It pertains to designs where the steam does not condense during expansion.
[F01K 7/34] the engines being of extraction or non-condensing type -- F01K 7/34 designates steam-engine plants whose engines operate as extraction or non-condensing types, meaning the steam is not condensed after expansion and is either exhausted or used directly for other purposes. This subclass covers arrangements without a conventional condenser, typical in certain locomotive, marine or industrial installations.
[F01K 7/345] Control or safety-means particular thereto -- F01K 7/345 covers control and safety devices specially adapted for steam-engine installations, such as governors, regulators, safety valves and other mechanisms that monitor, adjust or protect the plant's operation. The subclass applies to steam-engine plants where these specialised means are integral to ensuring reliable and safe performance.
[F01K 7/36] the engines being of positive-displacement type -- F01K 7/36 designates steam-engine plants in which the engine operates on a positive-displacement principle, meaning fluid is moved by the mechanical displacement of a fixed volume within the cylinder. This sub-class falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engines or Pumps → Steam Engine Plants.
[F01K 7/38] the engines being of turbine type -- F01K 7/38 covers steam engine plants whose primary motive unit is a turbine rather than a reciprocating piston. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, steam engine plants and is used by the EPO and USPTO to classify turbine-type steam engines.
[F01K 7/40] Use of two or more feed-water heaters in series -- Steam engine plants that employ two or more feed-water heaters arranged in series to preheat the water before it enters the boiler, thereby enhancing thermal efficiency and reducing fuel consumption. This configuration is used in condensatory or non-condensatory steam cycles where staged heating improves overall plant performance.
[F01K 7/42] Use of desuperheaters for feed-water heating -- The CPC subclass F01K 7/42 covers steam engine plants that incorporate desuperheaters to heat the feed-water. Desuperheaters remove superheat from steam before it enters the boiler, improving thermal efficiency and reducing the risk of overheating. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, steam engine plants.
[F01K 7/44] Use of steam for feed-water heating and another purpose -- F01K 7/44 relates to steam-engine plants that use the generated steam to preheat the feed water and simultaneously carry out another function, such as producing power, providing heating, or driving auxiliary equipment. This subclass addresses the dual use of steam within the same system for both feed-water heating and an additional purpose.
[F01K 9/00] Plants characterised by condensers arranged or modified to co-operate with the engines -- F01K 9/00 covers steam-engine plant configurations in which condensers are arranged or adapted to work in direct cooperation with the engine, enhancing condensation and overall efficiency. The classification includes designs where the condenser location, structure or control is specifically coordinated with the engine's operation.
[F01K 9/003] condenser cooling circuits -- F01K 9/003 refers to condenser cooling circuits in steam-engine plants, specifically the arrangements or modifications that enable condensers to work in cooperation with the engines. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and addresses the water-circulation systems that improve condensation and overall plant efficiency.
[F01K 9/006] Vacuum-breakers -- F01K 9/006 covers vacuum-breakers used in steam engine installations where condensers are linked to the engine. These devices release vacuum in the condenser or steam circuit to prevent excessive negative pressure that could impair condensate flow or damage components. They are integral to plants whose condensers are arranged to cooperate with the engine.
[F01K 9/02] Arrangements or modifications of condensate or air pumps -- This CPC subclass F01K 9/02 covers arrangements or modifications of condensate or air pumps in steam engine plants where condensers are integrated with the engine. It concerns mechanical-engineering designs that improve the handling of condensate or air to enhance the efficiency of steam-driven systems.
[F01K 9/023] Control thereof -- This classification covers control systems for steam-engine plants whose condensers are arranged or modified to cooperate with the engines. It includes methods and devices for regulating temperature, pressure, flow and overall operation of the combined condenser-engine system to improve efficiency and safety.
[F01K 9/026] Returning condensate by capillarity -- The F01K 9/026 class covers steam-engine plant arrangements in which condensate is returned to the boiler or system by capillary action rather than by pumps or gravity. It applies to designs where condensers are integrated with the engine to facilitate this passive fluid transport.
[F01K 9/04] with dump valves to by-pass stages -- A subclass of steam-engine plant arrangements where condensers are coordinated with the engine and equipped with dump valves that can bypass intermediate stages, allowing rapid release of condensate or steam to control pressure and improve efficiency. This feature is used especially in engines with multiple expansion stages to simplify operation and protect the system during overloads.
[F01L] CYCLICALLY OPERATING VALVES FOR MACHINES OR ENGINES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Glossary:** - Engine: A device for continuously converting fluid energy into mechanical power; thus this term includes, for example, steam piston engines or steam turbine or internal combustion engines. - Machine: A device that could equally be an engine and a pump, and not a device that is restricted to an engine or one that is restricted to a pump.
[F01L 1/00] Valve-gear or valve arrangements for positive-displacement machines or engines other than steam engines, e.g. for internal-combustion piston engines, without provision for variable fluid distribution -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Valve drive units, e.g. transmission between crankshaft and camshaft, camshaft arrangements, cam followers, camshaft phasers.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Lift-valve and valve-seat assemblies per se -> F01L3/00 - Slide-valve gear -> F01L5/00 - Actuated non-mechanically -> F01L9/00 - Valve arrangements in working piston or piston rod -> F01L11/00 - Modifications of valve-gear to facilitate reversing, braking, starting, changing compression ratio, or other specific operations -> F01L13/00
[F01L 1/02] Valve drive -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Transmitting-gear between valve drive and valve -> F01L1/12
[F01L 1/022] Chain drive -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example  US4750455
[F01L 1/024] Belt drive -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example  US4716864
[F01L 1/026] Gear drive -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example  US5598630
[F01L 1/04] by means of cams, camshafts, cam discs, eccentrics or the like -- F01L 1/04 classifies valve-gear or valve arrangements for positive-displacement machines such as internal-combustion engines that are operated by cams, camshafts, cam discs, eccentrics or similar mechanisms, without provisions for variable fluid distribution. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and cyclically operating valves for machines or engines.
[F01L 1/042] Cam discs -- F01L 1/042 covers cam discs, the rotating elements that form part of valve-gear arrangements for positive-displacement machines such as internal-combustion piston engines. The discs convert the cam's rotation into the precise opening and closing of intake or exhaust valves without providing variable fluid distribution.
[F01L 1/044] Reciprocating cams -- The subclass F01L 1/044 covers reciprocating cams used in valve gear for positive-displacement engines, where the cam converts rotational motion into a straight-line reciprocating movement to open or close valves. These cams are employed in internal-combustion piston engines and similar machines, providing fixed timing without variable fluid distribution.
[F01L 1/047] Camshafts -- The CPC subclass F01L 1/047 covers camshafts used in valve-gear arrangements for positive-displacement engines other than steam, such as internal-combustion piston engines. It relates to components that operate cyclically to control valve timing without variable fluid distribution.
[F01L 1/053] overhead type -- The F01L 1/053 entry covers overhead-type valve-gear arrangements used in positive-displacement machines such as internal-combustion piston engines. It classifies configurations where the valve mechanism is positioned above the cylinder to control intake and exhaust, but does not include provisions for varying fluid distribution.
[F01L 1/0532] the cams being directly in contact with the driven valve -- F01L 1/0532 covers valve-gear arrangements for positive-displacement engines where the cam acts directly on the driven valve, without any intermediary levers or rocker arms. It applies to internal-combustion piston engines and similar machines that use cams in direct contact to open and close the valve.
[F01L 1/06] the cams, or the like, rotating at a higher speed than that corresponding to the valve cycle, e.g. operating fourstroke engine valves directly from crankshaft -- F01L 1/06 pertains to valve-gear arrangements for positive-displacement engines in which cams or similar rotating components operate at a speed higher than the valve cycle, for example driving four-stroke engine valves directly from the crankshaft without a provision for variable fluid distribution.
[F01L 1/08] Shape of cams -- The F01L 1/08 subclass covers the geometric design of cams used in valve-gear arrangements for positive-displacement engines such as internal-combustion pistons. It includes cam profiles, lobes and surfaces that determine valve lift, timing and motion characteristics.
[F01L 1/10] by means of crank-or eccentric-driven rods -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F01L 11/00] Valve arrangements in working piston or piston-rod -- It covers the design and placement of valves that control fluid flow in pistons or piston-rods of engines or pumps, including valve seats, guides, and timing mechanisms integrated with the moving piston assembly. These arrangements affect sealing, lubrication, and power transmission.
[F01L 11/02] in piston -- F01L 11/02 covers valve arrangements that are built into the piston or piston-rod of a reciprocating engine. It includes designs where intake, exhaust, or lubrication valves are integrated directly in the moving piston structure, allowing cyclic operation without separate external valve gear.
[F01L 11/04] operated by movement of connecting-rod -- F01L 11/04 covers valve arrangements in a working piston or piston-rod where the valve is actuated directly by the connecting-rod's motion, such as in reciprocating engines or pumps that use the rod's linear movement to open or close the valve. This sub-class falls under cyclically operating valves for machines or engines within the mechanical engineering hierarchy.
[F01L 11/06] operating oscillatory valve -- F01L 11/06 covers operating oscillatory valve arrangements in working pistons or piston-rods of engines or pumps. It pertains to cyclically operating valves that move back and forth to control fluid flow within the cylinder, typically for starting, stopping or regulating pressure. The classification is used in mechanical engineering patent documents.
[F01L 1/12] Transmitting gear between valve drive and valve -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Simultaneously operating two or more valves -> F01L1/26
[F01L 1/14] Tappets -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Simultaneously operating two or more valves -> F01L1/24
[F01L 1/143] for use with overhead camshafts -- F01L 1/143 designates valve-gear or valve arrangements for positive-displacement engines that are intended for use with overhead camshafts. The subclass covers cyclically operating valve mechanisms in internal-combustion piston engines that do not provide variable fluid distribution.
[F01L 1/146] Push-rods -- Push-rods are rigid links that transmit the reciprocating motion of a cam, crank or rocker lever to the valve stem in positive-displacement engines such as internal-combustion pistons. By converting rotational or oscillating input into precise linear movement, they operate the intake and exhaust valves without varying the fluid flow distribution. This component is classified under CPC F01L 1/146 for valve-gear arrangements that lack variable distribution mechanisms.
