[F01L 2313/00] Rotary valve drives -- Rotary valve drives are mechanisms that convert rotary motion into the cyclic opening and closing of valves in engines or pumps, providing timed control of fluid flow without reciprocating linkages. By rotating the valve rather than sliding it, these drives enhance sealing, speed and reliability.
[F01L 25/00] Drive, or adjustment during the operation, or distribution or expansion valves by non-mechanical means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Types of non-mechanical valve drives like for F01L9/00 , but for steam engines
[F01L 25/02] by fluid means -- F01L 25/02 pertains to cyclically operating valves for machines or engines in which the drive, adjustment, distribution or expansion of the valve is achieved by fluid power rather than by mechanical means. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering → engines or pumps → cyclically operating valves for machines or engines.
[F01L 25/04] by working-fluid of machine or engine, e.g. free-piston machine -- F01L 25/04 covers cyclically operating valves for engines or machines that are driven, adjusted, or expanded by the working fluid of the same machine, such as the fluid in a free-piston engine, rather than by separate mechanical means. This sub-class falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and valves that operate cyclically.
[F01L 25/06] Arrangements with main and auxiliary valves, at least one of them being fluid-driven
[F01L 25/063] the auxiliary valve being actuated by the working motor-piston or piston-rod -- This CPC subclass (F01L 25/063) relates to auxiliary valves that are driven directly by the working motor-piston or piston-rod, without separate mechanical actuation. It concerns cyclically operating valves for engines or pumps where the valve motion is derived from the main piston's movement.
[F01L 25/066] piston or piston-rod being used as auxiliary valve -- F01L 25/066 relates to cyclically operating valves in engines or pumps where the piston or its rod itself serves as an auxiliary valve, controlling fluid flow, timing or pressure without a separate mechanical valve mechanism. It falls under drive, adjustment, distribution or expansion valves operated by non-mechanical means.
[F01L 25/08] by electric or magnetic means -- The CPC class F01L 25/08 covers cyclically operating valves in engines or pumps whose drive, adjustment, distribution or expansion is performed by electric or magnetic actuation rather than by mechanical means. It includes any valve that uses electromagnetic devices to control flow during operation.
[F01L 27/00] Distribution or expansion valve-gear peculiar to free-piston machines or engines and not provided for in -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Distribution or expansion valve-gear peculiar to free-piston machines or engines and not provided for in F01L21/00 - F01L25/00
[F01L 27/02] the machine or engine having rotary or oscillatory valves -- F01L 27/02 covers machines or engines that employ rotary or oscillatory valves, specifically distribution or expansion valves designed for free-piston machines and not otherwise classified.
[F01L 27/04] Delayed-action controls, e.g. of cataract or dashpot type -- F01L 27/04 covers delayed-action control devices, such as cataract or dashpot types, that are used in the distribution or expansion valve gear of free-piston machines and engines. The subclass addresses mechanisms that provide timed or progressive movement of cyclically operating valves within such systems.
[F01L 2710/00] Control of valve gear, speed or power
[F01L 2710/003] Control of valve gear for two stroke engines -- The CPC subclass F01L 2710/003 covers systems that control the valve gear of two-stroke internal-combustion engines. It includes mechanisms and electronic methods for adjusting valve timing, lift or power to optimise performance, emissions and fuel efficiency in engines that use cyclically operating valves.
[F01L 2710/006] Safety devices therefor -- F01L 2710/006 covers safety devices associated with the control of valve gear, speed or power in cyclically operating valves for engines or pumps. These devices are intended to prevent over-pressure, overspeed or other hazardous conditions by monitoring and interrupting valve operation when unsafe parameters are detected.
[F01L 2740/00] Control of slide-valve gear -- F01L 2740/00 covers systems that regulate the motion of slide-valve gear in engines or pumps, including mechanisms that adjust valve timing, lift and duration to optimise performance. It addresses the design of linkages, cam profiles and hydraulic or pneumatic actuators that directly control the sliding action of the valve. This subclass falls under cyclically operating valves for machines or engines within mechanical engineering.
[F01L 2740/003] more than one slide-valve, e.g. for four stroke engines -- This subclass covers mechanical arrangements that employ two or more slide-valves in the timing gear of reciprocating engines, such as the four-stroke type, where separate valves manage intake and exhaust phases. It pertains to the control mechanisms that coordinate the motion of multiple slide-valves within the engine's valve gear.
[F01L 2740/006] more than one slide-valve, e.g. for two stroke engines -- F01L 2740/006 covers mechanisms that use two or more slide valves to control the timing of inlet and exhaust ports in reciprocating engines, such as two-stroke engines. The classification pertains to the control of slide-valve gear within cyclically operating valve systems for engines or pumps.
[F01L 2750/00] Control of valve gear for four stroke engines directly driven by the crankshaft -- The entry covers mechanisms that control the valve gear of four-stroke internal-combustion engines when the drive is taken directly from the crankshaft, such as camshafts, tappets or timing gears that operate intake and exhaust valves in sync with piston movement. It pertains to the design and regulation of those directly crank-driven valve-control systems.
[F01L 2760/00] Control of valve gear to facilitate reversing, starting, braking of four stroke engines -- The F01L 2760/00 entry covers mechanisms that control the valve gear of four-stroke engines to enable reversing, starting and braking functions. It addresses devices that adjust timing or position of valves for changing engine direction, initiating motion, or applying engine brakes, typically used in marine or land-based mechanical power plants.
[F01L 2760/001] for starting four stroke engines -- This class covers devices and methods that control the valve gear of four-stroke engines to facilitate starting. It includes mechanisms that adjust valve timing or position to initiate engine rotation during start-up, and is part of the broader category of cyclically operating valves for machines or engines.
[F01L 2760/002] for reversing or starting four stroke engines -- F01L 2760/002 designates valve-gear control arrangements that enable reversing or starting of four-stroke engines, covering devices that adjust timing or position of cyclically operating valves to provide forward-or-reverse motion, initiate start-up, or aid braking in such engines.
[F01L 2760/003] for switching to compressor action in order to brake -- This classification covers valve-gear arrangements that switch a four-stroke engine to compressor operation for braking. It relates to cyclically operating valves in mechanical engineering, enabling the engine to act as a brake by converting kinetic energy into compressed fluid.
[F01L 2760/004] whereby braking is exclusively produced by compression in the cylinders -- F01L 2760/004 covers mechanisms in which the braking of a four-stroke engine is achieved solely by using the compression of the pistons in the cylinders. This control method is applied to valve gear for reversing, starting or braking, without external brake components.
[F01L 2760/005] in cooperation with vehicle transmission or brakes -- It covers valve-gear control systems for four-stroke engines that operate in coordination with a vehicle's transmission or braking system, enabling functions such as reversing, starting and braking.
[F01L 2760/006] for reversing two stroke engines -- The CPC subclass F01L 2760/006 relates to control mechanisms that enable reversal of two-stroke engines, allowing the valve gear to change rotation direction for starting, braking or changing travel direction. It is nested under mechanical engineering → engines or pumps → cyclically operating valves for machines or engines.
[F01L 2760/007] for starting two stroke engines -- The CPC subclass F01L 2760/007 covers devices that control valve gear to start two-stroke engines. It addresses mechanisms that manage inlet and exhaust valve timing during engine start-up, enabling reliable ignition and initial operation of the engine.
[F01L 2760/008] for reversing and restarting two stroke engines -- F01L 2760/008 covers valve-gear control systems that enable a two-stroke engine to be reversed or restarted, providing the means to change direction of rotation and to re-ignite the engine after a stop. It is a subcategory of cyclically operating valves for machines or engines within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy.
[F01L 2800/00] Methods of operation using a variable valve timing mechanism -- The entry F01L 2800/00 covers methods of operating engines that employ a variable valve timing mechanism, where the timing of valve opening and closing is adjusted during operation to optimise power output, fuel efficiency or emissions. It includes control strategies, timing schedules and actuation techniques used in such engines.
[F01L 2800/01] Starting -- F01L 2800/01 covers methods for starting machines or engines by employing a variable valve timing mechanism to control cyclically operating valves. It includes techniques that adjust valve timing during start-up to improve engine performance, reduce load, or ensure reliable ignition.
[F01L 2800/02] Cold running -- The CPC class F01L 2800/02 covers methods for operating internal combustion or other engines that employ a variable valve timing mechanism during cold start conditions. It relates to cyclically operating valves in engines and pumps, focusing on techniques that improve performance or reduce wear when the engine is first started while cold.
[F01L 2800/03] Stopping -- The F01L 2800/03 entry concerns methods for halting the operation of engines or pumps by employing a variable valve-timing mechanism that adjusts the timing of cyclically operating valves. It covers techniques that stop power flow or piston motion through controlled valve closure or deactivation.
[F01L 2800/04] Timing control at idling -- The F01L 2800/04 subclass covers methods that adjust the timing of engine valves while the engine is idling, using variable-valve-timing mechanisms to optimise performance, emissions or fuel consumption during low-speed operation. It falls under cyclically operating valves for machines or engines.
[F01L 2800/05] Timing control under consideration of oil condition -- This subclass covers methods for controlling valve timing in engines or pumps that adjust operation based on the condition of lubricating oil, such as viscosity, temperature, or contamination. It includes sensor monitoring and control algorithms that modify timing to maintain performance and protect components.
[F01L 2800/06] Timing or lift different for valves of same cylinder -- F01L 2800/06 covers methods of operating a machine or engine in which the valve timing or lift varies among the valves of the same cylinder, typically using a variable valve-timing mechanism to improve performance, efficiency, or emissions. This subclass falls under cyclically operating valves for engines within the broader mechanical engineering domain.
[F01L 2800/08] Timing or lift different for valves of different cylinders -- This subclass covers engine designs in which the timing or lift of intake or exhaust valves is deliberately varied between cylinders, such as by using separate cam profiles, hydraulic or electronic variable-valve-timing systems, or other mechanisms that give each cylinder a different valve actuation schedule to improve performance, efficiency or emissions.
[F01L 2800/09] Calibrating -- This CPC subclass concerns methods for calibrating variable valve-timing mechanisms that operate cyclically on engine or pump valves. It includes procedures for measuring and adjusting valve opening and closing events to attain desired performance, fuel efficiency, or emissions goals. The emphasis is on calibration techniques rather than the valve hardware itself.
[F01L 2800/10] Providing exhaust gas recirculation [EGR] -- F01L 2800/10 relates to methods of operating engines or pumps that employ a variable valve timing system to provide exhaust-gas recirculation. The classification covers techniques for adjusting valve actuation so that a portion of the exhaust gas is redirected back into the intake charge, improving combustion control and emissions performance.
[F01L 2800/11] Fault detection, diagnosis -- The classification covers techniques for identifying and diagnosing faults in variable valve timing systems that control cyclically operating valves in engines or pumps. It includes sensors, signal analysis, and diagnostic algorithms used to monitor valve behavior and detect malfunctions.
[F01L 2800/12] Fail safe operation -- The F01L 2800/12 sub-class covers methods that ensure a valve's timing system continues to operate safely even if a control fault occurs. It relates to cyclically operating engine or pump valves whose timing can be varied, describing fail-safe strategies that maintain proper valve actuation and prevent engine damage when the variable-timing mechanism malfunctions.
[F01L 2800/13] Throttleless -- F01L 2800/13 (Throttleless) relates to methods of operating engines or pumps that employ cyclically operating valves with a variable valve timing mechanism to control airflow without a conventional throttle, thereby enhancing efficiency and reducing emissions. This entry falls within mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and cyclically operating valves for machines or engines.
[F01L 2800/14] Determining a position, e.g. phase or lift -- F01L 2800/14 covers methods for determining the angular position, phase or lift of a cyclically operating valve in an engine or pump, often by using sensors, reference marks or electronic feedback to enable variable valve timing control. The techniques are applied to improve performance, efficiency and emissions of mechanical systems.
[F01L 2800/15] Balancing of rotating parts -- F01L 2800/15 concerns techniques for balancing rotating parts in engines or pumps that employ cyclically operating valves with variable timing, aiming to reduce vibration and improve efficiency. It includes methods such as adjusting mass distribution, adding counterweights or using dynamic balancing devices during operation.
[F01L 2800/16] Preventing interference -- F01L 2800/16 relates to methods for preventing interference between valves in engines or pumps that use variable valve timing. It covers control strategies, timing adjustments, and sensor-based interventions that avoid valve collisions or overlap during operation. The subclass is part of the CPC group dealing with cyclically operating valves and their timing mechanisms.
[F01L 2800/17] Maintenance -- A method for maintaining cyclically operating valves in engines or machines that employ a variable valve-timing mechanism, covering adjustment, cleaning, repair and routine servicing procedures to ensure proper timing and performance of the valves.
[F01L 2800/18] Testing or simulation -- F01L 2800/18 covers methods for testing or simulating the operation of variable valve timing mechanisms in cyclically operating valves of engines and pumps. The classification includes laboratory bench tests, engine dynamometer runs, and computer-based models that evaluate timing adjustments, performance, and durability. It is used to catalogue inventions that improve valve control through experimental or virtual analysis.
[F01L 2800/19] Valves opening several times per stroke -- F01L 2800/19 classifies engines or pumps that use valves which open more than once during a single piston stroke, employing variable valve timing to adjust lift, timing or duration for each opening. Such multi-opening valves enable better control of flow, power or emissions.
[F01L 2810/00] Arrangements solving specific problems in relation with valve gears -- This subgroup deals with specialized designs of valve gear mechanisms that address particular operational issues, such as timing accuracy, wear, lubrication, noise, or thermal effects, in engines and other cyclic machines. The inventions focus on arranging camshafts, pushrods, rocker arms or related components to overcome these problems.
[F01L 2810/01] Cooling -- F01L 2810/01 refers to cooling arrangements that are integrated with the valve gear of cyclically operating valves in engines or pumps, addressing specific thermal management problems of those mechanisms. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and cyclically operating valves for machines or engines. It covers designs that dissipate heat generated by valve motion or combustion to maintain performance and reliability.
[F01L 2810/02] Lubrication -- F01L 2810/02 covers lubrication arrangements that address specific problems associated with valve gears in cyclically-operating valves for machines or engines. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and cyclically-operating valves for machines or engines.
[F01L 2810/03] Reducing vibration -- This CPC subclass covers design features of valve-gear arrangements that aim to reduce vibration in cyclically operating valves of engines or pumps, such as dampening mechanisms, balancing methods, or structural modifications to minimize oscillatory forces. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, cyclically operating valves, and arrangements solving specific problems in valve gears.
[F01L 2810/04] Reducing noise -- The code F01L 2810/04 covers arrangements that reduce noise produced by valve gears in cyclically operating engines or pumps. It includes measures such as damping devices, acoustic insulation, optimized valve geometry or material selection designed to minimise sound radiation from the valve mechanism.
[F01L 2810/05] Related to pressure difference on both sides of a valve -- F01L 2810/05 relates to designs that manage the pressure differential across a valve in cyclically operating valve gear for engines or pumps. It includes arrangements that balance or compensate pressure on both the inlet and outlet sides to improve operation or reduce stress.
[F01L 2820/00] Details on specific features characterising valve gear arrangements -- F01L 2820/00 covers the detailed design of valve-gear mechanisms that control the timing and motion of cyclically operating valves in engines or pumps. It classifies specific features such as cam profiles, eccentric arrangements, linkages, and gear trains that define how the valves open and close during each cycle. This subclass groups inventions that focus on the geometry and interaction of these components to achieve desired valve timing and performance.
[F01L 2820/01] Absolute values -- F01L 2820/01 covers the specification of absolute numerical values that define the geometry and timing of valve-gear arrangements for cyclically operating valves in engines or pumps, such as angles, clearances, lift heights and timing intervals. It is used to describe the exact quantitative parameters of valve mechanisms within the broader class of mechanical engineering.
[F01L 2820/02] Formulas
[F01L 2820/03] Auxiliary actuators -- The CPC class F01L 2820/03 covers auxiliary actuators used in cyclically operating valve gear arrangements for engines or pumps. It refers to secondary devices that assist the primary valve mechanism in opening, closing or regulating flow, such as hydraulic, pneumatic or electric actuators that supplement the main valve drive.

[F01L 2820/034] Pneumatic engines -- F01L 2820/034 classifies pneumatic engines, i.e., machines that generate mechanical power from compressed air or other gases. This entry is part of the broader group dealing with cyclically operating valves in mechanical engineering.
[F01L 2820/035] Centrifugal forces -- The subclass F01L 2820/035 pertains to valve-gear arrangements where centrifugal forces influence operation, covering design features, balancing measures and performance aspects that arise from the rotation-induced forces acting on cyclically operating valves in engines or pumps.
[F01L 2820/04] Sensors -- F01L 2820/04 covers sensors used in cyclically operating valve gear arrangements for engines or machines, focusing on devices that monitor position, speed, pressure or other operating parameters of the valves. It classifies technologies that integrate detection elements into the valve system to enable control, diagnostics or safety functions. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and cyclically operating valves for machines or engines.
[F01L 2820/041] Camshafts position or phase sensors -- The classification F01L 2820/041 covers devices that detect the angular position or phase of camshafts in internal-combustion engines, typically employing magnetic, optical or Hall-effect sensors to provide timing information for valve-train control or fuel injection. It includes sensor arrangements, mounting methods and signal-processing elements specifically designed for camshaft position monitoring.
[F01L 2820/042] Crankshafts position -- The subclass F01L 2820/042 pertains to inventions that define the location or orientation of the crankshaft within valve-gear arrangements of engines or pumps, detailing how crankshaft position affects valve timing and operation. It is used to classify mechanical-engineering solutions that specify crankshaft placement to control cyclically operating valves.
[F01L 2820/043] Pressure -- The CPC code F01L 2820/043 covers features of valve-gear arrangements that are defined by pressure conditions in cyclically operating valves for engines or pumps. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engines or pumps, and concerns the way pressure influences the design and operation of these cyclic valves.
[F01L 2820/044] Temperature -- The subclass F01L 2820/044 covers temperature-related aspects of valve-gear arrangements for cyclically operating valves in engines or pumps, including thermal control, temperature-dependent actuation, compensation for thermal expansion and heating or cooling of valve components.
[F01L 2820/045] Valve lift -- F01L 2820/045 covers the technical features of valve lift in cyclically operating engine valves, specifying how the geometry and actuation of valve gear determine the amount and timing of valve opening. It relates to the design of lift mechanisms that optimise airflow and performance in mechanical engineering engines.
[F01L 29/00] Reversing-gear -- F01L 29/00 relates to reversing gears employed in cyclically operating valves for engines or machines, enabling the direction of rotation to be changed so that valve timing can be reversed during a pump or engine cycle. This subclass deals with the gear mechanisms that control the reversal of motion in such valve systems.
[F01L 29/02] by displacing eccentric -- This group covers reversing-gear arrangements that achieve valve actuation by shifting an eccentric member, causing the valve to open and close cyclically in engines or pumps. The eccentric displacement is used to reverse flow direction or timing of the valve operation.
[F01L 29/04] by links or guide rods -- F01L 29/04 refers to reversing-gear mechanisms for cyclically operating valves in engines or pumps in which the direction of valve motion is altered by means of links or guide rods that transmit and change the motion between the cam and the valve. This subclass includes arrangements that use such linkages to reverse stroke direction without requiring separate cams.
[F01L 29/06] by interchanging inlet and exhaust ports -- F01L 29/06 covers valve mechanisms that reverse flow direction by swapping the inlet and exhaust ports, typically used in engines or pumps to allow operation in reverse. This subcategory falls under cyclically operating valves for machines or engines and relates to reversing-gear systems.
[F01L 29/08] specially for rotary or oscillatory valves -- F01L 29/08 covers valve arrangements that are designed specifically for rotary or oscillatory motion, where the valve element pivots or turns to control fluid flow in engines, pumps or other cyclically operating machines. The subclass includes mechanisms such as rotating disc, paddle or swinging gate valves that replace linear reciprocating types, providing alternative timing and sealing solutions for reversing-gear applications.
[F01L 29/10] Details, e.g. drive -- F01L 29/10 covers the specific details of drive mechanisms for reversing gears that operate cyclically in engine or pump valves. It includes the design of actuators, coupling elements and control features that enable the gear to change rotation direction during each cycle. This subclass focuses on the technical solutions of the drive itself rather than the valve or gear geometry.
[F01L 29/12] Powered reverse gear -- F01L 29/12 designates powered reverse-gear mechanisms used with cyclically operating valves in engines or pumps, enabling the direction of rotation to be changed by an external power source. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and cyclically operating valves for machines or engines.
[F01L 3/00] Lift-valve, i.e. cut-off apparatus with closure members having at least a component of their opening and closing motion perpendicular to the closing faces -- F01L 3/00 covers lift-valves, also called cut-off devices, whose closing members move at least partly perpendicular to the sealing faces during opening and closing. These valves are used in engines or pumps to control fluid flow by lifting the valve seat away from the flow path and then returning it to block the passage. The classification applies to cyclically operating valve mechanisms in mechanical engineering.
[F01L 3/02] Selecting particular materials for valve-members or valve-seats -- F01L 3/02 covers the choice of specific materials for the moving parts and sealing surfaces of lift-type, cyclically operating valves used in engines or pumps, focusing on the composition of valve members and seats.
[F01L 3/04] Coated valve members or valve-seats -- F01L 3/04 pertains to lift-valves whose closure members move perpendicularly to the sealing faces and includes valve bodies or seats that are provided with a protective coating to enhance wear resistance, corrosion protection and sealing performance.
[F01L 3/06] Valve members or valve-seats with means for guiding or deflecting the medium controlled thereby, e.g. producing a rotary motion of the drawn-in cylinder charge -- F01L 3/06 covers lift-valves in which the valve members or seats incorporate guides or deflectors that shape the flow of the medium, typically causing a rotary motion of the cylinder charge drawn in during operation. The design includes closure elements whose opening-closing movement has a component perpendicular to the sealing faces, improving control of the cut-off process.
[F01L 3/08] Valves guides -- F01L 3/08 relates to guide devices for lift-valves in cyclically operating engines or pumps, providing alignment and control of the valve's opening and closing motion, especially where the motion is perpendicular to the sealing faces. These guides ensure smooth operation of cut-off apparatus in mechanical engineering applications.
[F01L 3/085] Valve cages -- Valve cages are structural components that surround and support the moving parts of lift-valves, providing a guide and retaining the valve disc or poppet within the valve housing while allowing the required perpendicular opening-closing motion. They ensure proper alignment, limit lateral displacement, and often incorporate seals or bearing surfaces to maintain reliable cut-off performance in engines or pumps.
[F01L 3/10] Connecting springs to valve members -- The CPC entry F01L 3/10 covers the means of linking springs to the moving parts of lift-valves used in engines or pumps, where the valve's opening and closing motion is at least partly perpendicular to its sealing surfaces. It includes designs that attach, anchor or integrate springs with valve stems, rocker arms or other actuating members to provide the required force for valve operation.
[F01L 31/00] Valve drive, valve adjustment during operation, or other valve control, not provided for in groups -- F01L 31/00 covers devices that provide power to open or close valves in engines or pumps, and mechanisms that adjust valve timing or lift while the machine is running. It includes any valve control solutions not specifically listed in narrower CPC groups.
[F01L 31/02] with tripping-gear -- This subclass covers valve drive and adjustment mechanisms for cyclically operating engine or pump valves that incorporate a tripping gear to automatically shut off or modify valve motion under preset conditions.
[F01L 31/04] with positively-driven trip levers -- The subclass F01L 31/04 covers valve-drive or valve-adjustment mechanisms for cyclically operating engine or pump valves that incorporate positively-driven trip levers to actuate, release or modify valve motion during operation. These levers are mechanically linked to the valve system to provide controlled, repeatable trips not covered in other groups.
[F01L 31/06] with tripping-gear specially for oscillatory valves -- This subclass covers mechanisms that drive, adjust or otherwise control cyclically operating valves in engines or pumps, specifically incorporating tripping-gear designed for oscillatory valve types. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and cyclically operating valves, addressing valve-control features not covered elsewhere.
[F01L 31/08] Valve drive or valve adjustment, apart from tripping aspects -- F01L 31/08 covers mechanisms that drive or adjust the position of cyclically operating valves in engines or pumps, excluding any features related to tripping or safety release. It includes devices that vary valve lift, timing, or opening degree during operation to optimise performance, such as hydraulic, pneumatic or electromechanical actuators that continuously modify valve settings while the machine runs. This subclass is part of the broader group on valve control for mechanical engineering applications.
[F01L 31/10] the drive being effected by eccentrics -- F01L 31/10 covers valve-drive mechanisms in cyclically operating engines or pumps where the motion is generated by eccentric elements. It includes designs that use off-center shafts or cams to convert rotary motion into the linear movement needed to open and close the valve during operation.
[F01L 31/12] Valve adjustment by displacing eccentric -- F01L 31/12 covers valve-control systems in which the timing or lift of a valve is varied by shifting an eccentric component, thereby adjusting the valve's position during operation. This method is used for fine-tuning valve timing in engines or pumps without replacing the entire valve assembly.
[F01L 31/14] Valve adjustment by links or guide rods, e.g. in valve-gears with eccentric drive -- F01L 31/14 relates to valve-control mechanisms in engines or pumps where the valve timing or lift is altered by links or guide rods, such as in valve gears driven by an eccentric shaft. The subclass covers arrangements that adjust valve position during operation and are not covered by other more specific groups.
[F01L 31/16] the drive being effected by specific means other than eccentric, e.g. cams -- F01L 31/16 covers valve drive or adjustment mechanisms for cyclically operating engine or pump valves that use specific means other than an eccentric, such as cam-driven systems. It includes designs where the valve motion is generated by cams, levers or other non-eccentric devices to control timing and lift during operation. This sub-group is part of the broader class for valve control in mechanical engineering.
[F01L 31/18] specially for rotary or oscillatory valves -- The F01L 31/18 subclass pertains to valve-drive or adjustment mechanisms that are specially designed for rotary or oscillatory valves used in cyclically operating valve systems of engines or pumps, where such control methods are not covered in other groups. It includes devices that impart rotational or back-and-forth motion to the valve or modify its position during operation.
[F01L 3/12] Cooling of valves -- The entry deals with techniques and apparatus for removing heat from lift-valves that regulate flow in engines or pumps, specifically those whose opening-closing motion is at least partly perpendicular to the sealing faces. It includes internal cooling passages, external jackets, fluid circulation systems and other heat-transfer measures that keep valve components within safe temperature ranges during cyclic operation.
[F01L 3/14] by means of a liquid or solid coolant, e.g. sodium, in a closed chamber in a valve -- This subclass describes lift-valves whose opening and closing motion is perpendicular to the sealing faces and that are cooled by a liquid or solid medium, such as sodium, inside a sealed chamber. The cooling system is integrated with cyclically operating valves used in engines or pumps to manage temperature and wear.
[F01L 3/16] by means of a fluid flowing through or along valve, e.g. air -- F01L 3/16 covers lift-valve or cut-off mechanisms for engines or pumps where the valve's opening and closing motion is perpendicular to the seat and is driven by a fluid flowing through or along the valve, such as air, to produce the desired cyclic operation.
[F01L 3/18] Liquid cooling of valve -- F01L 3/18 designates systems that apply liquid cooling to lift-type cut-off valves used in engines or pumps, where the valve's opening and closing motion is perpendicular to its sealing faces. The subclass includes methods and apparatus for circulating coolant around valve components to remove operational heat and enhance durability.
[F01L 3/20] Shapes or constructions of valve members, not provided for in preceding subgroups of this group -- F01L 3/20 classifies lift-valve cut-off devices whose valve members have shapes or constructions that are not covered by the earlier sub-groups of the group. It includes novel geometries, special profiles, or unconventional arrangements of the moving parts that affect the opening and closing motion perpendicular to the sealing faces. This subgroup captures inventive designs of valve components that fall outside the standard listed configurations.
[F01L 3/205] Reed valves -- Reed valves are classified under cyclically operating valves. They have closure members with motion perpendicular to the closing faces. Code F01L 3/205 is used for these valves.
[F01L 3/22] Valve-seats not provided for in preceding subgroups of this group -- F01L 3/22 relates to lift-valve mechanisms for engines or pumps in which the valve-seat design is not covered by any of the preceding subgroups, encompassing unconventional seat shapes, materials or configurations that differ from standard cut-off apparatus.
[F01L 3/24] Safety means or accessories, not provided for in preceding sub- groups of this group -- F01L 3/24 covers safety devices or accessories for lift-valves (cut-off apparatus) used in engines or pumps that are not specified in the preceding sub-groups, such as additional guards, emergency shut-off mechanisms, or monitoring components that enhance safe operation of the valve's opening and closing motion.
[F01L 33/00] Rotary or oscillatory slide valve-gear or lift-valve-gear or such valve arrangements specially for steam engines or specially for other machines or engines with variable working-fluid distribution -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Types of rotary or oscillatory slide valve arrangements like for F01L7/00 , but for steam engines
[F01L 33/02] rotary -- F01L 33/02 covers rotary cyclically operating valves used in engines or machines where the distribution of the working fluid varies, such as steam engines. It includes rotary slide-valve gear, lift-valve gear and similar valve arrangements that replace oscillating or reciprocating valve mechanisms.
[F01L 33/04] oscillatory -- F01L 33/04 classifies oscillatory slide-valve or gear mechanisms used in cyclically operating engines or pumps, especially steam-engine types, where the valve moves back and forth to vary the distribution of working fluid. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and cyclically operating valves.
[F01L 35/00] Lift valve-gear or valve arrangements specially adapted for machines or engines with variable fluid distribution -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Types of lift valve arrangements like for F01L3/00 , but for steam engines
[F01L 35/02] Valves -- F01L 35/02 covers lift-valve gear and related valve arrangements that are specially designed for engines or machines where the fluid flow is varied during operation. These valves are used in cyclically operating valve systems to control the timing and quantity of fluid intake or exhaust, enabling variable distribution of fluid to meet changing engine or machine demands.
[F01L 35/04] Arrangements of valves in the machine or engine, e.g. relative to working cylinder -- The CPC subclass F01L 35/04 covers the geometric and functional arrangement of lift-valve gear or valve systems within engines or machines. It includes how valves are positioned relative to the working cylinder to control fluid flow in cyclic operations.
[F01L 5/00] Slide valve-gear or valve-arrangements -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Valve-arrangements with pure rotary or oscillatory movement -> F01L7/00
[F01L 5/02] with other than cylindrical, sleeve or part annularly shaped valves, e.g. with flat-type valves -- F01L 5/02 covers slide valve gear or arrangements that employ valves which are not cylindrical, sleeve or annular in shape, such as flat-type valves. It applies to engines or pumps where the valve moves linearly to control fluid flow, differing from round or sleeve valves. This subclass falls under cyclically operating valves for machines or engines.
[F01L 5/04] with cylindrical, sleeve, or part-annularly shaped valves -- F01L 5/04 refers to cyclically operating valves for engines that employ cylindrical, sleeve or part-annularly shaped valves, typically arranged in sliding-valve gear systems. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering → engines or pumps → cyclically operating valves for machines or engines.
[F01L 5/045] Piston-type or cylinder-type valves arranged above the piston and coaxial with the cylinder axis -- F01L 5/045 designates piston-type or cylinder-type valves that are mounted above the piston and share the cylinder's axis, forming a slide-valve arrangement for engines or pumps. The code is part of the hierarchical group for mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, cyclically operating valves, and slide-valve gear.
[F01L 5/06] surrounding working cylinder or piston -- F01L 5/06 refers to valve arrangements in which the valve is located around the working cylinder or piston of an engine or pump. This subclass includes designs where the valve gear encircles the cylinder, providing inlet or exhaust control as the piston moves cyclically. It is used for engines, pumps and similar machines where space-saving or protective configurations are required.
[F01L 5/08] Arrangements with several movements or several valves, e.g. one valve inside the other -- F01L 5/08 covers valve gear arrangements in engines or pumps where more than one valve motion is combined, such as nested or concentric valves that move independently or in sequence. These configurations enable complex control of fluid admission and exhaust within a single mechanism.
[F01L 5/10] with reciprocating and other movements of the same valve -- F01L 5/10 relates to slide-valve gear or valve arrangements in which a single valve performs reciprocating or other back-and-forth movements during each cycle, such as lifting, lowering or oscillating within the valve seat. It applies to cyclically operating valves for engines or pumps where the valve's motion is linear rather than rotary.
[F01L 5/12] Arrangements with part-annularly-shaped valves -- F01L 5/12 classifies valve arrangements that employ part-annularly-shaped sliding valves in cyclically operating engines or pumps. The valves are shaped like a segment of a ring and move linearly to open or close ports, offering compact sealing and flow-control advantages.
[F01L 5/14] characterised by the provision of valves with reciprocating and other movements -- F01L 5/14 classifies slide-valve gear or arrangements for engines and pumps that feature valves moving reciprocally or in other non-rotary motions. It covers designs where the valve's motion is linear, oscillatory or combined, distinguishing them from purely rotating valve mechanisms. This subclass falls under cyclically operating valves within mechanical engineering.
[F01L 5/16] with reciprocating and other movement of same valve, e.g. longitudinally of working cylinder and in cross direction -- F01L 5/16 covers slide-valve gear or valve arrangements in which the valve performs a reciprocating motion combined with additional movements, such as longitudinal travel within the working cylinder and transverse motion across the cylinder, used in cyclically operating engines or pumps.
[F01L 5/18] with reciprocatory valve and other slide valve -- F01L 5/18 designates valve gear that combines a slide valve with a reciprocating valve to regulate fluid flow in cyclically operating engines or pumps. The arrangement uses the sliding element together with a moving valve component to open and close ports during each cycle, improving timing and control of the working medium.
[F01L 5/20] specially for two-stroke engines -- The F01L 5/20 subclass covers slide-valve gear or valve arrangements that are designed specially for two-stroke engines, addressing the unique timing and motion requirements of the intake and exhaust processes in this engine type. It includes mechanisms that control port opening and closing through reciprocating slide actions adapted to the two-stroke cycle.
[F01L 5/22] Multiple-valve arrangements -- F01L 5/22 covers slide valve gears that incorporate several valves operating together in a single arrangement, such as multiple slide or poppet valves coordinated to control fluid flow in reciprocating engines or pumps. The classification includes configurations where the valves are mounted on a common gear or linked mechanisms to achieve synchronized opening and closing.
[F01L 5/24] Component parts, details or accessories, not provided for in preceding subgroups in this group -- F01L 5/24 covers component parts, details or accessories of slide-valve gear or valve arrangements that are not specified in any of the earlier subgroups of this class. It includes miscellaneous or specialized items used in cyclically operating valves for engines or pumps, such as custom fittings, adapters or ancillary mechanisms not otherwise classified.
[F01L 7/00] Rotary or oscillatory slide valve-gear or valve arrangements -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[F01L 7/02] with cylindrical, sleeve, or part-annularly shaped valves -- F01L 7/02 covers rotary or oscillating slide-valve gear arrangements in engines or pumps where the moving valves are cylindrical, sleeve-shaped, or form part of an annular ring. These valves cyclically open and close to control fluid flow, differing from flat-plate or disc-type slide valves.
[F01L 7/021] with one rotary valve
[F01L 7/022] Cylindrical valves having one recess communicating successively with aligned inlet and exhaust ports -- A cylindrical slide valve that contains a single recess which, as the valve rotates or oscillates, lines up in turn with an inlet port and an exhaust port, allowing fluid to enter and then be discharged in a cyclic sequence. This type of valve is used in engines or pumps as part of rotary or oscillatory slide-valve gear arrangements.
[F01L 7/023] Cylindrical valves having a hollow or partly hollow body allowing axial inlet or exhaust fluid circulation -- F01L 7/023 covers cylindrical valves whose bodies are hollow or partially hollow so that fluid can flow axially through the valve for inlet or exhaust. These valves are used in cyclically operating machines such as engines and pumps, and include rotary or oscillating slide valve arrangements where the valve body provides a passage for the working fluid.
[F01L 7/024] Cylindrical valves comprising radial inlet and axial outlet or axial inlet and radial outlet -- The class covers cylindrical valves that combine a radial inlet with an axial outlet, or the reverse arrangement, used in engines or pumps where fluid enters or exits at right angles to the valve axis. These designs are typically applied in rotary or oscillating slide-valve gear mechanisms to control flow direction efficiently.
[F01L 7/025] Cylindrical valves comprising radial inlet and side outlet or side inlet and radial outlet -- The CPC entry F01L 7/025 covers cylindrical valves designed with a radial inlet and a side outlet, or a side inlet and a radial outlet, used in cyclically operating valve gear for engines or pumps. These valves belong to the rotary or oscillatory slide-valve arrangements within mechanical engineering.
[F01L 7/026] with two or more rotary valves, their rotational axes being parallel, e.g. 4-stroke -- F01L 7/026 covers valve gear where two or more rotary valves are used with parallel axes, such as the valve system of a four-stroke internal-combustion engine. The arrangement allows simultaneous intake and exhaust control by rotating valves rather than traditional reciprocating ones.
[F01L 7/027] with two or more valves arranged coaxially  F01L7/045  takes precedence -- F01L 7/027 defines rotary or oscillatory slide-valve and gear arrangements that incorporate two or more valves positioned coaxially, a configuration used in engines and pumps for synchronized flow control. It is a narrower subclass of the broader coaxial valve group F01L 7/045 within cyclically operating valve technology.
[F01L 7/028] having the rotational axis coaxial with the cylinder axis and the valve surface not surrounding piston or cylinder -- A rotary or oscillatory slide valve arrangement in which the valve's rotational axis is aligned with the cylinder's axis, while the valve surface does not encircle the piston or the cylinder, falls under CPC F01L 7/028. This design is used for cyclically operating machines or engines where a coaxial rotation simplifies the valve-gear geometry without surrounding the moving components.
[F01L 7/029] having the rotational axis of the valve parallel to the cylinder axis -- The CPC class F01L 7/029 covers rotary or oscillatory slide-valve gear arrangements in engines or pumps where the valve's rotational axis runs parallel to the cylinder's axis, allowing the valve to open and close by turning about the same line as the piston movement. This geometry is used for cyclically operating valves that slide or swing to control fluid flow within the cylinder.
[F01L 7/04] surrounding working cylinder or piston -- This CPC subclass F01L 7/04 pertains to designs where the valve gear or slide-valve arrangement encloses the working cylinder or piston, providing a surrounding structure that integrates the valve mechanism with the cylinder. It includes rotary or oscillatory slide valves that form a housing around the piston, improving sealing or guiding.
[F01L 7/045] with two or more valves arranged coaxially -- F01L 7/045 covers rotary or oscillatory slide valve gear in engines or pumps where two or more slide valves are mounted on the same axis. The coaxial arrangement allows synchronized opening and closing of the valves to control fluid flow during each cycle.
[F01L 7/06] with disc type valves -- The F01L 7/06 subclass covers rotary or oscillatory slide-valve arrangements that employ disc-type valves as the flow-controlling element in engines or pumps. It includes designs where a flat or slightly curved disc rotates or pivots to open and close the passage, forming part of the valve gear that regulates fluid or gas flow in cyclically operating machinery.
[F01L 7/08] with conically or frusto-conically shaped valves -- This group covers rotary or oscillating slide valve gear where the moving valve element has a conical or frusto-conical shape, used to control flow in engines or pumps during cyclic operation. The geometry allows gradual opening and closing and efficient sealing in high-speed machinery.
[F01L 7/10] with valves of other specific shape, e.g. spherical -- F01L 7/10 covers rotary or oscillatory slide valve-gear for engines or pumps where the moving valve has a special shape, for example a spherical form. It falls under cyclically operating valves for machines or engines in the mechanical engineering field.
[F01L 7/12] specially for two-stroke engines -- F01L 7/12 pertains to rotary or oscillatory slide-valve, gear or valve arrangements that are specially designed for two-stroke engines, controlling inlet and exhaust port timing through a rotating or reciprocating slide mechanism.
[F01L 7/14] Multiple-valve arrangements -- The CPC subclass F01L 7/14 covers valve gear arrangements in which two or more valves are integrated into a rotary or oscillating slide-valve mechanism for engines or pumps. It includes designs that combine multiple valves within a single gear to control timing, flow distribution or pressure regulation during cyclic operation. This classification is used for describing multi-valve slide-valve systems in mechanical engineering.
[F01L 7/16] Sealing or packing arrangements specially therefor -- F01L 7/16 pertains to sealing or packing arrangements specifically designed for rotary or oscillatory slide valve-gear and related valve mechanisms in engines or pumps. It includes seals, packing glands and other devices that prevent leakage around moving valve components.
[F01L 7/18] Component parts, details, or accessories not provided for in preceding subgroups of this group -- F01L 7/18 designates component parts, details, or accessories for rotary or oscillatory slide valve-gear or valve arrangements that are not specified in the preceding subgroups of this group.
[F01L 9/00] Valve-gear or valve arrangements actuated non-mechanically -- F01L 9/00 refers to valve-gear or valve arrangements that are actuated by non-mechanical means, such as hydraulic, pneumatic, electrical, or magnetic forces, in cyclically operating valves for engines or pumps. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for engines or pumps and deals with alternatives to conventional mechanical actuation in valve-gear systems.
[F01L 9/10] by fluid means, e.g. hydraulic -- F01L 9/10 covers valve-gear or valve arrangements in engines or pumps that are actuated by fluid power rather than mechanical linkages. The actuation is provided by hydraulic or other fluid pressure to open, close or modulate the valve.
[F01L 9/11] in which the action of a cam is being transmitted to a valve by a liquid column -- The code F01L 9/11 covers valve-gear or valve arrangements in which a cam's motion is transmitted to a valve through a liquid column, i.e., hydraulic transmission of cam actuation for cyclically operating valves in engines or pumps.
[F01L 9/12] with a liquid chamber between a piston actuated by a cam and a piston acting on a valve stem -- In this CPC subclass, a cam drives a primary piston that pressurises a liquid chamber; the hydraulic pressure moves a secondary piston which directly operates the valve stem. The arrangement provides non-mechanical actuation of cyclic valves in engines or pumps.
[F01L 9/14] the volume of the chamber being variable, e.g. for varying the lift or the timing of a valve -- F01L 9/14 covers valve-gear or valve arrangements in engines or pumps where the chamber volume can be changed to adjust valve lift or timing, typically by non-mechanical actuation methods. This allows the valve's opening characteristics to be varied during operation.
[F01L 9/16] Pneumatic means -- The code F01L 9/16 covers valve gear or arrangements for engines that are actuated by pneumatic means, i.e. using compressed air rather than direct mechanical linkage. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, cyclically operating valves for machines or engines.
[F01L 9/18] Means for increasing the initial opening force on the valve -- The entry covers devices that boost the force required to begin opening a cyclically operated valve in engines or pumps when the valve gear is actuated by non-mechanical means such as hydraulic, pneumatic or electromagnetic energy. It includes springs, pressure-assist chambers or other mechanisms that increase the initial opening load.
[F01L 9/20] by electric means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F01L 9/21] actuated by solenoids -- F01L 9/21 is a CPC subclass concerning valve-gear or valve arrangements in engines or pumps that are actuated by solenoids rather than mechanical means. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, cyclically operating valves for machines or engines.
[F01L 9/22] actuated by rotary motors -- F01L 9/22 covers valve-gear or valve arrangements for engines that are driven by rotary motors rather than by conventional mechanical linkages such as cams or pistons. The subclass includes systems where a rotating motor directly provides the actuation needed to open and close cyclically operating valves.
[F01L 9/24] Piezoelectric actuators -- F01L 9/24 relates to piezoelectric actuators employed to drive valve-gear or valve arrangements in engines and pumps without mechanical linkage. The code covers devices that transform electrical input into precise mechanical motion for controlling cyclically operating valves, providing rapid, accurate timing and positioning in mechanical engineering applications.
[F01L 9/26] Driving circuits therefor -- F01L 9/26 covers the electrical or electronic circuits that supply power and control signals to valve-gear or valve arrangements that are actuated without mechanical linkage, typically in engines or pumps with cyclically operating valves. The class includes designs for the drive electronics that open, close, or modulate such non-mechanical valves.
[F01L 9/30] Arrangements for setting the actuator position, e.g. the initial position -- The CPC subclass F01L 9/30 covers arrangements that set the position of non-mechanically actuated valve gear, such as mechanisms that establish the initial or reference position of cyclically operating valves in engines or pumps. These solutions ensure proper timing before actuation.
[F01L 9/40] Methods of operation thereof -- F01L 9/40 covers the techniques for running and controlling valve gears or arrangements that are driven by hydraulic, pneumatic, electrical or other non-mechanical means in cyclically operating valves for engines and pumps. It includes timing, sequencing, energizing and de-energizing strategies that affect valve opening and closing during each cycle.
[F01M] LUBRICATING OF MACHINES OR ENGINES IN GENERAL -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Lubrication of machines or engines in general, where the lubrication system, or device for lubricating parts of the machine or engine, does not form part of the structure of the machine or engine.  Pressure lubrication, e.g. using pressure from pumps, crankcase pressure or the like to circulate the lubricant; including provision of lubricant passageways in crankshafts or connecting rods.  Lubrication specially adapted for engines with crankcase compression of fuel/air mixtures, or for engines in which lubricant is contained in fuel, combustion air or fuel/air mixture (e.g. two-stroke engines).  Heating, cooling or controlling temperature of lubricant.  Lubrication means facilitating engine starting, e.g. by conditioning or heating the lubricant.  Lubrication means specially adapted for facilitating the running-in or breaking-in of machines or engines.  Lubricating means having other significant or pertinent characteristics, e.g. introducing additives to lubricant, using fuel as lubricant, dip, splash or drip lubrication, non-pressurised lubrication.  Details, parts or accessories of lubricating, including controlling lubricant level or pressure, arrangement of lubricant conduits, purifying lubricant, filling or draining lubricant.  Indicating or safety features, e.g. to render machine or engine inoperative or idling on lubricant pressure failure.  Crankcase ventilating or breathing, e.g. breather valves or inlet air filters.  **Glossary:** - Engine: A device for continuously converting fluid energy into mechanical power. Thus, this term includes, for example, steam piston engines or steam turbines per se, or internal-combustion piston engines, but it excludes single-stroke devices. - Pump: A device for continuously raising, forcing, compressing or exhausting fluid by mechanical or other means. Thus, this term includes fans or blowers. This term also includes methods of operation, unless otherwise specifically provided for. - Machine: A device which could equally be an engine or a pump, but not a device which is restricted to an engine or one which is restricted to a pump. - Lubricant: Any fluid (e.g. water, oil), fluent material (e.g. particulate graphite), semi-fluid material (e.g. oil with thickener), or semisolid material (e.g. grease), or any mixture of such types of substance, which is used for the specific purpose of lubrication. - Lubrication: Apparatus, arrangements or methods for introducing or applying a thin layer of a discrete lubricant between two or more relatively-moving adjacent solid surfaces of a device or apparatus, for the specific purpose of reducing friction, heat or wear between the adjacent surfaces. - Working fluid: The driven fluid in a pump or the driving fluid in an engine. The working fluid may be in a gaseous state, i.e. compressible, or liquid. In the former case coexistence of two states is possible. - Positive displacement: The way the energy of a working fluid is transformed into mechanical energy, in which variations of volume created by the working fluid in a working chamber produce equivalent displacements of the mechanical member transmitting the energy, the dynamic effect of the fluid being of minor importance, and vice-versa. - Non-positive displacement: The way the energy of a working fluid is transformed into mechanical energy, by transformation of the energy of the working fluid into kinetic energy, and vice-versa.
[F01M 1/00] Pressure lubrication -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pressure lubrication
[F01M 1/02] using lubricating pumps -- This subclass covers inventions that employ lubricating pumps to deliver oil or other lubricants to moving parts, typically in engines or mechanical systems, ensuring continuous pressure lubrication. It lies within mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, lubricating of machines or engines in general, and pressure lubrication.
[F01M 1/04] using pressure in working cylinder or crankcase to operate lubricant feeding devices -- This subclass covers systems that use the pressure produced in a working cylinder or the crankcase to drive oil-pump or other lubricant-feeding devices, providing lubrication directly from engine-generated pressure rather than from an external pump.
[F01M 1/06] Lubricating systems characterised by the provision therein of crankshafts or connecting rods with lubricant passageways, e.g. bores -- This subclass covers lubrication systems that integrate internal passages in crankshafts or connecting rods, such as drilled bores, to feed oil directly to bearing surfaces. The design improves oil distribution and reduces wear in engines and other pressure-lubricated machines.
[F01M 1/08] Lubricating systems characterised by the provision therein of lubricant jetting means -- This subclass defines lubricating systems that employ lubricant-jetting means, such as nozzles or spray devices, to deliver a pressurized film onto moving components. It applies to engines, pumps and other mechanical devices where precise, high-speed distribution of oil is required for effective friction reduction and cooling.
[F01M 1/10] Lubricating systems characterised by the provision therein of lubricant venting or purifying means, e.g. of filters -- F01M 1/10 covers lubricating systems for engines or pumps that incorporate venting or purification devices such as filters to remove contaminants from the lubricant.
[F01M 11/00] Component parts, details or accessories, not provided for in, or of interest apart from, groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Oilsumps;  Arrangements of lubricant conduits;  Mounting or connecting of lubricant purifying means relative to the machine or engine;  Details of lubricant purifying means;  Filling or draining lubricant of or from machines or engines;  Means for keeping lubricant level constant or for accommodating movement or position of machines or engines;  Separating lubricant from air or fuel-air mixture before entry into cylinder;  Indicating devices;  Other safety devices.
[F01M 11/0004] Oilsumps -- F01M 11/0004 covers oilsumps, which are containers that collect, store, or feed lubricating oil in engines, pumps and other mechanical systems. The code pertains to component parts or accessories not otherwise classified, used to manage oil supply within a lubrication circuit.
[F01M 11/02] Arrangements of lubricant conduits -- F01M 11/02 covers the design and layout of channels, pipes and passages that transport lubricating oil or other lubricants within engines or pump assemblies. It includes the spatial arrangement, routing, connection points and distribution networks that ensure effective lubrication of moving parts.
[F01M 11/03] Mounting or connecting of lubricant purifying means relative to the machine or engine -- The entry covers the methods and structures for attaching or linking lubricant-purifying devices such as filters, separators or cleaning units to engines or other machines, including brackets, housings, couplings and mounting arrangements that position the purifying means relative to the lubricated components.
[F01M 11/04] Filling or draining lubricant of or from machines or engines -- The F01M 11/04 subclass covers methods, apparatuses and accessories used to fill or drain lubricants in machines or engines, including pumps, pipelines or reservoirs that supply or remove oil, grease or other lubricating fluids. It pertains to the general lubricating of mechanical systems and is nested under lubricating of machines or engines in general.
[F01M 11/0408] Sump drainage devices, e.g. valves, plugs -- The F01M 11/0408 class defines devices used to drain fluid from a sump in lubricated engines or pumps, such as valves, plugs or similar fittings that control or discharge accumulated oil or other lubricants. These components help remove excess fluid, prevent contamination and facilitate maintenance of the lubrication system.
[F01M 11/045] Removing lubricant by suction -- F01M 11/045 covers devices or methods that extract used lubricant from engines or other machines by suction, typically as part of a lubrication system or maintenance equipment. It relates to components that remove oil for oil-recycling or disposal, separate from the primary lubrication supply.
[F01M 11/0458] Lubricant filling and draining -- F01M 11/0458 designates devices, components or methods used to fill lubricants into, or drain them from, engines, pumps and other machines, covering fittings, valves, pumps, reservoirs or related accessories that are not classified elsewhere in the lubricating group. It addresses the handling of lubricant during maintenance or operation, ensuring proper supply and removal within the lubrication system.
[F01M 11/06] Means for keeping lubricant level constant or for accommodating movement or position of machines or engines -- F01M 11/06 covers devices that maintain a constant lubricant level in machines or engines, or that allow the lubrication system to adapt to movement or positional changes of the equipment. These mechanisms include level regulators, accumulators and flexible conduits designed to ensure consistent oil supply despite angular or linear displacement.
[F01M 11/061] Means for keeping lubricant level constant -- The F01M 11/061 subclass covers devices and mechanisms that automatically maintain a constant lubricant level in engines or pumps. This includes reservoirs, float-type valves, level-sensing probes and control systems that add or remove oil to keep the level steady during operation.
[F01M 11/062] Accommodating movement or position of machines or engines, e.g. dry sumps -- F01M 11/062 pertains to components that enable a machine or engine to accommodate movement or positional changes, for example dry sumps designed to maintain lubrication despite shifting parts. The classification falls under lubricating of machines or engines, specifically component parts not otherwise classified.
[F01M 11/064] Movement -- F01M 11/064 covers devices that generate or transmit movement for the lubrication of machines or engines, specifically component parts, details or accessories not otherwise classified. It includes mechanisms such as gears, cams, drives or feed devices that convey lubricants to moving parts.
[F01M 11/065] Position -- F01M 11/065 pertains to devices or elements that define or adjust the spatial location of lubricating components in engines or machines, such as mounts, bearings or positioning mechanisms that ensure correct oil delivery.
[F01M 11/067] inverted, e.g. for inverted flight -- F01M 11/067 concerns lubricating system components or accessories that are adapted for operation when a machine or engine is positioned upside-down, such as during inverted flight. It addresses features that maintain oil flow, pressure and distribution while the device functions in an inverted orientation.
[F01M 11/08] Separating lubricant from air or fuel-air mixture before entry into cylinder -- This subclass addresses devices or methods that remove lubricant particles from the air or fuel-air mixture before the mixture enters a cylinder, ensuring a cleaner charge for combustion or piston motion and reducing oil carry-over into the working chamber.
[F01M 11/10] Indicating devices -- F01M 11/10 covers indicating devices related to the lubrication of machines or engines, such as gauges, meters, alarms or visual displays that signal oil pressure, flow, temperature or system status. These components are treated as separate parts or accessories when they are not covered by other specific groups within the lubricating category.
[F01M 11/12] concerning lubricant level -- F01M 11/12 covers devices or arrangements that monitor or indicate the level of lubricant in machines or engines. It applies to component parts, details or accessories that are not otherwise specified in the main lubricating categories. This subclass is part of the broader group for lubricating of machines or engines in the mechanical engineering domain.
[F01M 1/12] Closed-circuit lubricating systems not provided for in groups -- F01M 1/12 refers to closed-circuit lubricating systems that do not fall into the predefined groups, describing arrangements where lubricating fluid is recirculated under pressure to supply bearings, gears or other moving parts of engines and machines.
[F01M 1/14] Timed lubrication -- Timed lubrication is a method of delivering lubricant to machines or engines at predetermined intervals or engine cycles instead of continuously. It employs mechanical, hydraulic or electronic timing mechanisms to coordinate lubrication with operational phases, improving efficiency and reducing waste in pressure-lubricated systems.
[F01M 1/16] Controlling lubricant pressure or quantity -- F01M 1/16 covers methods and devices for regulating the pressure or volume of lubricant supplied to a machine or engine in pressure-lubrication systems. It includes control mechanisms that adjust flow rates to maintain optimal film thickness and prevent wear, often using sensors, valves or electronic controllers.
[F01M 1/18] Indicating or safety devices -- F01M 1/18 pertains to indicating or safety devices for pressure-lubricated engines or pumps, including pressure gauges, warning alarms, automatic shut-off mechanisms and other components that monitor lubrication conditions and protect the machinery from damage.
[F01M 1/20] concerning lubricant pressure -- F01M 1/20 relates to pressure lubrication systems used in engines or pumps, specifically the means of generating, controlling or varying the pressure of the lubricant supplied to moving parts. It covers devices, circuits and methods that ensure adequate hydraulic pressure for effective machine lubrication.
[F01M 1/22] rendering machines or engines inoperative or idling on pressure failure -- This subclass covers devices or control methods that automatically shut down or place a machine or engine in idle when the pressure in its lubrication system drops, protecting the equipment from damage. It includes sensors, valves and control logic that detect pressure loss and trigger the inoperative or idle state.
[F01M 1/24] acting on engine fuel system -- F01M 1/24 covers inventions that modify or control the fuel delivery system of an engine to achieve pressure lubrication, such as fuel-pump actuated oil injection or fuel-mediated lubrication circuits. It is situated within mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, lubricating of machines or engines in general, and pressure lubrication.
[F01M 1/26] acting on engine ignition system -- This subclass covers inventions that link the engine's ignition control to its lubrication system, such as devices that modify oil flow or pressure based on spark timing, ignition-timing control units that coordinate lubrication to protect components during combustion, and methods for synchronising ignition events with oil delivery to enhance engine performance.
[F01M 1/28] acting on engine combustion-air supply -- The F01M 1/28 subclass covers provisions that influence the combustion-air supply to engines, such as throttle devices, air-metering or pumping mechanisms that regulate the amount, pressure or timing of intake air. These features are applied in pressure-lubricated engines to optimise combustion, improve performance and aid overall machine lubrication.
[F01M 13/00] Crankcase ventilating or breathing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Crankcase ventilating or breathing;  Blowby circuit layout;  Purification device details.
[F01M 13/0011] Breather valves -- The F01M 13/0011 subclass covers breather valves used to ventilate or "breathe" the crankcase of engines and other lubricated machines. These valves allow excess gases and vapours to escape while preventing entry of contaminants, helping maintain proper pressure and lubrication performance.
[F01M 13/0033] Breather inlet-air filters -- The CPC class F01M 13/0033 covers filters installed in breather inlet lines that clean the air drawn into a crankcase ventilation or breathing system for engines or pumps. It pertains to lubricating-related devices that remove contaminants before the air enters the crankcase.
[F01M 13/02] by means of additional source of positive or negative pressure -- F01M 13/02 concerns crankcase ventilation or breathing systems that use an auxiliary device to create a pressure differential, either suction or positive pressure, for removing gases and oil mist from the crankcase. The additional source may be a dedicated pump, blower, venturi or similar equipment that enhances lubrication hygiene beyond the engine's inherent breathing.
[F01M 13/021] of negative pressure -- The F01M 13/021 subclass covers crankcase ventilation or breathing systems that operate by creating a negative pressure within the crankcase to draw out gases and contaminants. This method uses suction rather than positive-pressure blow-out to manage lubricating-oil vapour, moisture and combustion by-products, improving engine cleanliness and reducing emissions.
[F01M 13/022] using engine inlet suction -- This subclass covers arrangements that employ the engine's inlet suction flow to ventilate the crankcase, drawing gases and oil vapors out of the crankcase through the intake system. It includes designs that channel crankcase vapors into the air-filter or combustion chamber using the engine's own suction rather than separate pumps.
[F01M 13/023] Control valves in suction conduit -- The subclass F01M 13/023 concerns control valves that are placed in the suction conduit of a crankcase ventilation or lubricating system for engines and pumps. These valves regulate the flow of air-oil mixtures drawn into the crankcase, helping to maintain proper pressure and prevent oil accumulation. The entry covers the design, function and positioning of such suction-line control devices.
[F01M 13/025] with an inlet-conduit via an air-filter -- The subclass F01M 13/025 covers crankcase ventilating or breathing arrangements for engines that incorporate an inlet conduit routed through an air-filter, ensuring that gases drawn from the crankcase are cleared of contaminants before being released.
[F01M 13/028] of positive pressure -- F01M 13/028 covers technologies that create a positive pressure in the crankcase of an internal-combustion engine to improve ventilation and remove blow-by gases. The classification includes devices and methods that pressurise the crankcase as part of the engine's lubrication and emission-control system.
[F01M 13/04] having means for purifying air before leaving crankcase, e.g. removing oil -- This subclass covers crankcase ventilation or breathing arrangements that incorporate a means to clean the exhaust air, such as filters or separators that remove oil droplets before the air leaves the crankcase. It applies to engines and other machines where lubricating oil may be entrained in the vented gases.
[F01M 13/0405] arranged in covering members apertures, e.g. caps -- F01M 13/0405 refers to crankcase ventilation or breathing arrangements that are incorporated directly into the apertures of covering members such as caps, enabling gases to be expelled or filtered through the cover itself rather than using separate external ducts.
[F01M 13/0416] arranged in valve-covers -- The subclass F01M 13/0416 covers crankcase ventilation or breathing arrangements that are incorporated into the engine's valve-covers. It includes designs where passages, filters or valves for expelling gases from the crankcase are built into the cover, improving sealing and simplifying the engine's layout.
[F01M 13/06] specially adapted for submersible engines, e.g. of armoured vehicles -- F01M 13/06 covers crankcase ventilation or breathing systems that are specially adapted for use in submersible engines, such as those installed in armored vehicles. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and lubricating of machines or engines in general.
[F01M 2001/0207] characterised by the type of pump -- F01M 2001/0207 is a CPC subgroup for pump inventions where the defining feature is the particular type of pump employed, such as axial, centrifugal, or rotary. The classification is used when the patent claims are limited or distinguished primarily by that pump type rather than by function or application.
[F01M 2001/0215] Electrical pumps -- F01M 2001/0215 designates the subclass for electrical pumps, covering devices that convert electrical energy into fluid movement, including centrifugal, axial, and positive-displacement pump types driven by electric motors. The entry encompasses pump construction, control, sealing, and integration with electrical circuits, as well as specific adaptations for pressure and flow regulation.
[F01M 2001/0223] Electromagnetic pumps -- Electromagnetic pumps are devices that move conductive fluids-typically liquid metals-by applying a magnetic field to generate a Lorentz force, causing the fluid to flow without moving mechanical parts. They are used in metallurgical processes, cooling systems for nuclear reactors, and certain aerospace applications where sealed, corrosion-free fluid transport is required.
[F01M 2001/023] Piston pumps -- F01M 2001/023 covers piston pumps, a type of positive displacement pump that uses one or more reciprocating pistons to create suction and discharge of fluid. The pistons move back and forth within cylinders, converting mechanical energy into fluid pressure, and may be driven by gears, cams or electric motors. This subclass includes designs, arrangements and operating principles specific to piston-type pumping mechanisms.
[F01M 2001/0238] Rotary pumps -- F01M 2001/0238 classifies rotary pumps, which move fluid by the rotation of an impeller, gear set or screw. The entry includes centrifugal, positive-displacement and mixed-flow pump designs, covering their operating principle, structural features and performance characteristics.
[F01M 2001/0246] Adjustable pumps -- F01M 2001/0246 covers pumps whose output can be varied mechanically or by control, including adjustable displacement, speed, or flow rate, typically by changing the geometry of the pumping elements or by using control devices. These pumps allow the user to modify performance to suit changing demand.
[F01M 2001/0253] characterised by the pump driving means -- F01M 2001/0253 is a CPC subgroup for pumps in which the driving means of the pump is a defining feature. It covers inventions that specify or improve the motor, engine, gear, or other power-transmission components that operate the pump, distinguishing them from generic pump designs. The classification is used when the pump's drive system is a pivotal element of the claimed technical solution.
[F01M 2001/0261] driven by the camshaft -- The subclass F01M 2001/0261 covers engine components or systems that are powered directly by the camshaft, such as cam-driven valves, fuel pumps or timing mechanisms. It applies to designs where the camshaft's rotational motion is the primary drive source for these auxiliary functions.
[F01M 2001/0269] driven by the crankshaft -- This CPC subclass classifies engine components and auxiliary mechanisms that obtain power directly from the rotating crankshaft of a reciprocating internal-combustion engine, such as oil pumps, compressors or other accessories driven by the crankshaft.
[F01M 2001/0276] driven by a balancer shaft -- The subgroup covers arrangements in which a balancer shaft is used as the driving element for another component, typically in internal-combustion engines to transmit power and reduce vibration. It includes gear, chain, belt or similar mechanisms that couple the crankshaft to the balancer shaft for synchronized operation.
[F01M 2001/0284] mounting of the pump -- The subclass F01M 2001/0284 covers the mounting of pumps, i.e., the arrangement, supporting structures and attachment means that secure a pump to a foundation, frame, housing or other support. It includes brackets, flanges, bases and coupling methods that provide proper alignment, stability and connection of the pump to the driven system.
