[F01N 1/10] in combination with sound-absorbing materials -- F01N 1/10 covers silencers for engines or pumps that achieve noise reduction by integrating sound-absorbing materials into the exhaust or gas-flow path. The classification applies to designs where the method of silencing relies on the material's acoustic attenuation, such as porous inserts, fibrous liners or foam layers, combined with the overall silencing geometry.
[F01N 11/00] Monitoring or diagnostic devices for exhaust-gas treatment apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices like engine control units [ECUs] or sensors used to monitor or diagnose the function of exhaust-gas treatment apparatuses, e.g. for catalytic activity.
[F01N 11/002] the diagnostic devices measuring or estimating temperature or pressure in, or downstream of the exhaust apparatus -- F01N 11/002 covers diagnostic and monitoring devices that measure or estimate temperature or pressure within, or downstream of, exhaust-gas treatment or silencing systems for engines and machinery. The classification includes sensors, transducers and electronic instruments used to assess exhaust conditions for performance monitoring, emission control and fault detection. It applies to any apparatus that provides real-time or recorded data on exhaust temperature or pressure for engines, pumps or related mechanical systems.
[F01N 11/005] the temperature or pressure being estimated, e.g. by means of a theoretical model -- F01N 11/005 relates to methods and devices that estimate the temperature or pressure inside exhaust-gas treatment systems by applying theoretical models instead of direct sensors. It falls under monitoring and diagnostic devices for exhaust-gas silencers or apparatus within the mechanical engineering hierarchy.
[F01N 11/007] the diagnostic devices measuring oxygen or air concentration downstream of the exhaust apparatus -- Monitoring or diagnostic devices that measure the oxygen or air concentration in the gas stream after it leaves an exhaust-gas treatment system, used to assess the performance of silencers, catalysts or other exhaust-control apparatus for engines and machines.
[F01N 1/12] using spirally or helically shaped channels -- F01N 1/12 covers silencing devices for engines or pumps that employ spirally or helically shaped channels to attenuate gas-flow noise. The classification groups such exhaust or gas-flow silencers under mechanical engineering, specifically where the method of silencing relies on the geometry of the internal passage.
[F01N 1/125] in combination with sound-absorbing materials -- The CPC subclass F01N 1/125 covers silencing devices for engines that incorporate sound-absorbing materials. It applies to exhaust or gas-flow silencers where the silencing effect is achieved by including absorptive liners, foams or similar media in the apparatus.
[F01N 1/14] by adding air to exhaust gases -- F01N 1/14 covers exhaust silencing devices that reduce noise by injecting additional air into the exhaust gas stream. The added air mixes with the gases, lowering velocity and turbulence and thereby diminishing sound emission. This technique is applied in various engine and pump systems to improve acoustic performance.
[F01N 1/16] by using movable parts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Exhaust gas silencing using movable elements that modify the exhaust flow and its acoustic properties. These mechanisms may function by altering the flow path, varying the flow area, modulating pressure fluctuations, disrupting sound wave propagation or dynamically adjusting flow resistance.
[F01N 1/161] for adjusting resonance or dead chambers or passages to resonance or dead chambers -- This subclass covers exhaust or gas-flow silencing devices that incorporate means for tuning resonance or dead-space chambers and passages to achieve desired acoustic cancellation. Adjustments may involve changing chamber dimensions, adding adjustable plugs, or varying passage lengths to create resonant or non-resonant conditions that reduce noise.
[F01N 1/163] by means of valves -- This subclass covers exhaust-silencing devices that employ one or more valves to modulate the flow of gases, thereby reducing noise generated by engines or pumps. The valves open, close or throttle the exhaust stream in response to operating conditions to achieve quieter operation.
[F01N 1/165] for adjusting flow area -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Silencing by using movable parts for adjusting flow area, e.g. by means of a valve.  Illustrative example of the subject matter classified in this place:
[F01N 1/166] for changing the flow path through the silencer or for adjusting the dimensions of a chamber or a pipe  F01N1/165  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of the subject matter classified in this place:  1.  2.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Silencing by using movable parts for adjusting flow area -> F01N1/165
[F01N 1/168] for controlling or modifying silencing characteristics only -- The F01N 1/168 subclass covers silencing apparatus for engines or pumps that are designed solely to control or modify the acoustic performance of the device. It includes mechanisms such as adjustable baffles, variable-area chambers or active control systems that change the muffling effect without altering the basic exhaust flow path.
[F01N 1/18] having rotary movement -- F01N 1/18 covers silencing devices for engines or pumps that employ a rotary motion to reduce noise, such as rotating baffles or chambers that modulate exhaust flow. The classification groups mechanisms where the sound-attenuation function is achieved by components that turn or spin as part of the exhaust system.
[F01N 1/20] having oscillating or vibrating movement -- This subclass covers silencers or exhaust devices that reduce noise by incorporating components that oscillate or vibrate during operation. The oscillating or vibrating action creates destructive interference of sound waves, improving acoustic attenuation in engines or pumps.
[F01N 1/22] the parts being resilient walls -- F01N 1/22 relates to gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for engines or pumps that achieve noise reduction through resilient wall components. The classification falls under mechanical engineering and addresses silencing devices whose method of silencing relies on flexible or elastically deformable walls that absorb acoustic energy.
[F01N 1/24] by using sound-absorbing materials -- F01N 1/24 covers exhaust or gas-flow silencers for engines that reduce noise by incorporating sound-absorbing materials within the apparatus. The classification applies to silencing methods that rely on absorption rather than purely geometric or kinetic means and falls under gas-flow silencers for machines or engines in general.
[F01N 13/00] Exhaust or silencing apparatus characterised by constructional features -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Joints and assembling details of exhaust parts inside a housing or between exhaust conduits. Selection of particular materials, thermal insulation details, special arrangement of the exhaust conduits or devices on the vehicle or engine. Exhaust manifolds. Exhaust details of engines adapted for particular uses, model applications, marine propulsion.
[F01N 13/001] Exhaust gas flow channels or chambers being at least partly formed in the structural parts of the engine or machine  using structural parts of the vehicle  B60K13/06 -- F01N 13/001 covers exhaust or silencing systems where the gas-flow passages or chambers are at least partially integrated into the structural components of the engine or machine, such as using the vehicle's frame or body parts as part of the exhaust routing. This integration reduces separate piping and can improve compactness and weight.
[F01N 13/002] Apparatus adapted for particular uses, e.g. for portable devices driven by machines or engines
[F01N 13/004] specially adapted for marine propulsion, i.e. for receiving simultaneously engine exhaust gases and engine cooling water  for submerged exhausting  F01N13/12 ; treating exhaust by using liquids  F01N3/04
[F01N 13/005] with parts constructed of non-metallic material, e.g. of rubber -- F01N 13/005 covers exhaust or silencing devices for engines in which one or more components are made from non-metallic materials such as rubber, plastic or composites. The classification is applied to designs that use these materials to reduce noise, vibration or corrosion, often in flexible joints, seals or muffler liners.
[F01N 13/007] Apparatus used as intake or exhaust silencer -- F01N 13/007 covers devices that function as intake or exhaust silencers for engines or pumps, reducing noise in gas-flow systems. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engines or pumps and gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus. The classification addresses constructional features of these silencing apparatuses.
[F01N 13/008] Mounting or arrangement of exhaust sensors in or on exhaust apparatus  sensor arrangements for engine control  F02D41/1439 -- F01N 13/008 covers the way exhaust-flow sensors are positioned on or within an engine's silencer or exhaust system, detailing mounting structures and layout that enable accurate measurement for engine-control functions. The subclass addresses constructional features that integrate sensors with the exhaust apparatus for optimal performance and diagnostics.
[F01N 13/009] having two or more separate purifying devices arranged in series -- F01N 13/009 covers exhaust or silencing apparatus for engines that incorporate two or more separate purifying devices arranged in series. Typical implementations use successive filters, scrubbers or catalytic units to achieve higher removal of pollutants or noise.
[F01N 13/0093] the purifying devices are of the same type -- F01N 13/0093 covers exhaust or silencing systems in which the gas-purifying elements-such as filters, scrubbers or catalytic devices-are all of the same type, sharing identical construction or functional principle. The subclass applies to mechanical engineering applications where a uniform purifying device is used throughout the apparatus.
[F01N 13/0097] the purifying devices are arranged in a single housing -- This subclass covers exhaust or silencing apparatus for machines or engines in which the purifying elements-such as filters, scrubbers or catalytic devices-are integrated into a single housing, combining silencing and cleaning functions within one enclosure.
[F01N 13/011] having two or more purifying devices arranged in parallel -- In the CPC, subclass F01N covers gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for machines or engines, with group 13/00 referring to constructional features of such devices. Subgroup 13/011 specifically defines exhaust or silencing systems that employ two or more purifying devices arranged in parallel to treat the gas stream.
[F01N 13/017] the purifying devices are arranged in a single housing -- F01N 13/017 covers exhaust or silencing systems in which the purifying elements-such as filters, catalysts or scrubbers-are incorporated into one single-housing unit, providing an integrated solution that simplifies the overall exhaust arrangement.
[F01N 13/02] having two or more separate silencers in series -- The entry covers exhaust or silencing devices for machines or engines that employ two or more distinct silencers connected in series, a constructional feature aimed at reducing noise or gas flow resistance. It resides within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under engines or pumps and gas-flow silencers.
[F01N 13/04] having two or more silencers in parallel, e.g. having interconnections for multi-cylinder engines -- F01N 13/04 covers exhaust or silencing devices that incorporate two or more silencers arranged in parallel, often with interconnections to serve multi-cylinder engines. The classification is used for designs where multiple silencing stages operate simultaneously to reduce noise or back-pressure.
[F01N 13/06] specially adapted for star-arrangement of cylinders, e.g. exhaust manifolds -- A device for directing exhaust gases from engines whose cylinders are positioned in a star arrangement, such as specially shaped exhaust manifolds, is covered by this subclass. It concerns constructional features of exhaust or silencing apparatus for machines or engines.
[F01N 13/08] Other arrangements or adaptations of exhaust conduits -- The classification F01N 13/08 covers exhaust or silencing systems for engines that involve alternative configurations or adaptations of the exhaust conduit, such as special routing, branching, or integration with other components, beyond conventional muffler designs.
[F01N 13/082] of tailpipe, e.g. with means for mixing air with exhaust for exhaust cooling, dilution or evacuation  F01N13/20  takes precedence -- F01N 13/082 covers tailpipe designs that incorporate devices for introducing air into the exhaust stream to cool, dilute, or vent the gases. It pertains to gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for engines, focusing on constructional features that improve exhaust conditioning. This subclass follows the broader F01N 13/20 category.
[F01N 13/085] having means preventing foreign matter from entering exhaust conduit -- F01N 13/085 covers exhaust or silencing devices for engines that incorporate structures to stop foreign objects such as debris, water or insects from entering the exhaust conduit, typically by using screens, baffles or filtered inlet designs. These preventative features protect downstream components and maintain proper flow.
[F01N 13/087] having valves upstream of silencing apparatus for by-passing at least part of exhaust directly to atmosphere  valves for changing flow path through the silencer  F01N1/166 -- F01N 13/087 covers exhaust or silencing devices for engines that incorporate one or more valves placed upstream of the silencer, allowing a portion of the exhaust gases to be diverted directly to the atmosphere or to reroute the flow path through the silencer. This feature enables adjustable noise control or pressure relief in mechanical engineering applications.
[F01N 13/10] of exhaust manifolds -- This CPC subclass deals with exhaust or silencing devices for engines that are characterized by the construction of exhaust manifolds. It includes designs that collect, channel, and merge gases from multiple cylinders, focusing on flow efficiency, noise control, and heat management.
[F01N 13/102] having thermal insulation -- F01N 13/102 covers exhaust or silencing devices for engines that incorporate thermal insulation as a defining constructional feature. The insulation reduces heat loss from the exhaust stream and can protect surrounding components or improve acoustic performance.
[F01N 13/105] having the form of a chamber directly connected to the cylinder head, e.g. without having tubes connected between cylinder head and chamber -- F01N 13/105 designates exhaust or silencing devices in which the silencer forms a chamber that is attached directly to the cylinder head, eliminating any intervening pipes or tubes between the head and the chamber. This construction-type feature is used to reduce noise and manage gas flow from engines or pumps.
[F01N 13/107] More than one exhaust manifold or exhaust collector -- F01N 13/107 refers to engine or pump exhaust systems that employ two or more exhaust manifolds or collectors, allowing separate routing or combined handling of exhaust gases for improved performance, emission control, or noise attenuation.
[F01N 13/12] specially adapted for submerged exhausting -- F01N 13/12 covers exhaust or silencing apparatus that is specially adapted for submerged exhausting, such as marine or underwater engine exhaust systems that discharge below the water surface to reduce noise, vibration and emissions. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and gas-flow silencers.
[F01N 13/14] having thermal insulation -- F01N 13/14 covers exhaust or silencing devices for engines that incorporate thermal-insulating elements, such as heat-shielding linings or insulated casings, to reduce heat loss and protect surrounding components. The classification applies to any construction that uses insulation as a defining feature.
[F01N 13/141] Double-walled exhaust pipes or housings -- F01N 13/141 covers double-walled exhaust pipes or housings used in engines or machines, where an inner conduit carrying the exhaust gases is surrounded by an outer layer separated by a space or insulating material. The construction aims to improve thermal insulation, reduce noise and protect the outer surface from heat and corrosion.
[F01N 13/143] with air filling the space between both walls -- F01N 13/143 covers exhaust or silencing apparatus in which the space between the inner and outer walls of the silencer is deliberately filled with air, using the air column as the primary sound-absorbing medium rather than solid or porous filler material. This design is applied to gas-flow silencers for engines or machines to reduce noise while maintaining low back-pressure.
[F01N 13/145] with a gas other than air filling the space between both walls -- This CPC subgroup defines exhaust or silencing devices whose interior cavity between the outer and inner walls is filled with a gas other than air, such as inert or heavier gases, to improve sound attenuation or thermal performance. It applies to silencers, mufflers, and other gas-flow control components for engines or pumps.
[F01N 13/146] with vacuum in the space between both walls -- F01N 13/146 covers exhaust or silencing devices for engines in which a vacuum is maintained in the cavity between the inner and outer walls, improving noise reduction and flow control. This feature is applied mainly to gas-flow silencers or mufflers used on mechanical engines or pumps.
[F01N 13/148] Multiple layers of insulating material
[F01N 13/16] Selection of particular materials -- The subclass deals with choosing specific materials for exhaust or silencing devices, focusing on how material properties affect acoustic performance, durability, heat resistance and corrosion protection in engines and machinery. It guides the selection of metals, alloys, composites or other substances to meet functional and environmental requirements of the apparatus.
[F01N 13/18] Construction facilitating manufacture, assembly, or disassembly -- F01N 13/18 relates to exhaust or silencing devices for engines or pumps where the construction includes features that simplify manufacturing, assembly or disassembly, such as modular sections, quick-release fasteners, or prefabricated components, facilitating easier production and maintenance of gas-flow silencers.

[F01N 13/1816] the pipe sections being joined together by flexible tubular elements only, e.g. using bellows or strip-wound pipes -- F01N 13/1816 covers exhaust or silencing devices in which the individual pipe sections are linked solely by flexible tubular elements, such as bellows or strip-wound pipes, providing a continuous, compliant connection without rigid joints.
[F01N 13/1822] for fixing exhaust pipes or devices to vehicle body -- F01N 13/1822 covers structural arrangements for attaching exhaust pipes or silencing devices to a vehicle's body or chassis. It includes brackets, clamps, mounting plates and other fixation elements that secure the exhaust system to the vehicle frame, focusing on the constructional features of the attachment rather than the exhaust function itself.

[F01N 13/1833] specially adapted for small internal combustion engines, e.g. used in model applications -- F01N 13/1833 covers exhaust or silencing devices that are specially adapted for small internal combustion engines, such as those used in model or hobby applications. [F01N 13/1838] characterised by the type of connection between parts of exhaust or silencing apparatus, e.g. between housing and tubes, between tubes and baffles -- F01N 13/1838 classifies exhaust or silencing systems for engines that are defined by the way their parts are joined, such as the connections between the housing and tubes or between tubes and baffles. The focus is on the type of mechanical linkage or fitting used within the apparatus.
[F01N 13/1844] Mechanical joints -- F01N 13/1844 classifies exhaust and silencing devices for engines that incorporate specific mechanical joints as part of their construction. [F01N 13/185] the connection being realised by deforming housing, tube, baffle, plate, or parts thereof -- This subclass covers exhaust or silencing devices for engines in which the connection between components is created by permanently deforming a housing, tube, baffle, plate or similar part, forming a sealed joint without separate fasteners.

The classification focuses on the method of connection rather than the overall shape or material of the silencer.
[F01N 13/1861] the assembly using parts formed by casting or moulding -- The CPC subclass F01N 13/1861 refers to exhaust or silencing apparatus for engines where the assembly is constructed from parts produced by casting or moulding. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering, Engines or Pumps, gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus, and exhaust or silencing apparatus characterised by constructional features.
[F01N 13/1866] the channels or tubes thereof being made integrally with the housing -- This subclass covers exhaust or silencing devices whose flow-guiding channels or tubes are formed as a single, integral piece with the surrounding housing rather than being separate, assembled parts, detailing constructional features that combine the conduit and enclosure into one unit.

[F01N 13/1877] the channels or tubes thereof being made integrally with the housing -- F01N 13/1877 covers exhaust or silencing devices for engines where the internal channels or tubes are formed as a single, integral part of the housing, rather than being separate components assembled later. [F01N 13/1883] manufactured by hydroforming -- The subclass F01N 13/1883 refers to exhaust or silencing devices for engines that are fabricated by hydroforming, a high-pressure fluid forming method that shapes metal parts with precise, seamless construction.

[F01N 2210/02] Resonance and interference -- F01N 2210/02 pertains to the study and mitigation of resonance and interference phenomena in gas-flow silencers and exhaust devices for engines and pumps. It addresses design strategies and analytical methods for controlling acoustic vibrations and wave interactions within such apparatus.
[F01N 2210/04] Throttling-expansion and resonance -- The F01N 2210/04 subclass covers exhaust or gas-flow silencers that employ throttling-expansion techniques and exploit resonance effects to reduce noise and control emission flow. It includes designs where pressure drops or acoustic resonance are used to dampen sound in engines or pumps.
[F01N 2210/06] Throttling-expansion and interference -- F01N 2210/06 relates to throttling-expansion and interference features in gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for engines, covering designs that control flow by throttling or expanding gases and address component interaction issues. The classification is used for mechanical engineering inventions dealing with engine exhaust noise reduction and flow regulation.
[F01N 2230/00] Combination of silencers and other devices -- F01N 2230/00 in the CPC covers the integration of gas-flow silencers with additional components such as filters, catalytic converters, or heat exchangers to form a combined system that reduces noise while performing other functions for engines or pumps. This entry applies to mechanical engineering solutions that merge exhaust-silencing technology with auxiliary devices in a single assembly.
[F01N 2230/02] Exhaust filters -- F01N 2230/02 refers to exhaust filters that are combined with silencers or other exhaust-gas treatment devices for engines or machines. [F01N 2230/04] Catalytic converters -- F01N 2230/04 classifies catalytic converters, devices that treat exhaust gases from engines by promoting chemical reactions that reduce pollutants while often being integrated with silencing elements. These components are used in exhaust apparatus for machines and engines, combining emission control with noise reduction in a single unit.
[F01N 2230/06] Spark arresters -- F01N 2230/06 covers spark arresters incorporated in gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for engines, devices that prevent hot particles or sparks from escaping the exhaust stream. [F01N 2240/00] Combination or association of two or more different exhaust treating devices, or of at least one such device with an auxiliary device, not covered by indexing codes -- The CPC code F01N 2240/00 falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and exhaust apparatus, and it designates configurations that combine two or more different exhaust-treating devices, or pair at least one such device with an auxiliary unit, when the combination is not covered by other specific indexing codes.

[F01N 2240/22] a condensation chamber -- A condensation chamber is an auxiliary component incorporated into an exhaust-treating system that cools the exhaust gases so that water vapor condenses and separates from the flow. It is used in combined or hybrid exhaust arrangements where multiple silencing or cleaning devices are integrated, providing moisture removal that complements the primary noise-reduction or pollutant-control functions.

[F01N 2250/00] Combinations of different methods of purification -- F01N 2250/00 covers exhaust or gas-flow silencers that use a combination of two or more purification techniques, such as filtration, catalytic conversion, scrubbers or electrostatic precipitation, to reduce pollutants from engine or machine emissions. [F01N 2250/02] filtering and catalytic conversion -- This subclass covers devices that combine filtration and catalytic conversion in gas-flow silencers or exhaust systems for engines, integrating purification methods to remove particulates and chemically transform pollutants. [F01N 2250/04] afterburning and catalytic conversion -- F01N 2250/04 covers the treatment of exhaust gases from engines or pumps by means of afterburning and catalytic conversion, where additional combustion or catalyst action is used to further oxidise pollutants or convert them into less harmful substances. The entry applies to gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus that integrate these purification steps to improve emission control.

[F01N 2250/12] absorption or adsorption, and catalytic conversion -- This subclass concerns exhaust or gas-flow silencing devices for engines or pumps that employ a combined purification approach, where pollutants are first absorbed or adsorbed onto a medium and then undergo catalytic conversion.
[F01N 2250/14] absorption or adsorption, and filtering -- F01N 2250/14 classifies exhaust or silencing systems that combine absorption or adsorption with a filtering stage, using materials or media to capture pollutants while also removing particulates from the gas stream. [F01N 2260/02] for cooling the device -- The F01N 2260/02 subclass covers exhaust-treating devices, such as silencers or mufflers for engines, that incorporate cooling features not otherwise specified in the main classification. It includes any provisions for reducing temperature of the exhaust flow to improve performance or durability.
[F01N 2260/022] using air -- F01N 2260/022 covers exhaust-treating devices that utilize air, such as systems that inject, mix, or otherwise introduce air into an engine's exhaust stream to aid combustion, cooling, or pollutant reduction when no other specific provision applies. It is a catch-all subclass for air-based exhaust treatment methods within gas-flow silencers or general exhaust apparatus.

[F01N 2260/06] for improving exhaust evacuation or circulation, or reducing back-pressure -- This CPC subclass deals with devices or modifications that enhance the evacuation or circulation of exhaust gases in engines or machines, primarily by reducing back pressure. [F01N 2260/08] for preventing heat loss or temperature drop, using other means than layers of heat-insulating material
[F01N 2260/10] for avoiding stress caused by expansions or contractions due to temperature variations -- The F01N 2260/10 entry covers exhaust-treating devices that incorporate features to relieve thermal-induced stress, such as expansion joints, flexible mounts or other mechanisms that accommodate dimensional changes when temperature varies. These provisions are used when standard designs do not already provide such stress-relief measures.
[F01N 2260/12] for resisting high pressure -- F01N 2260/12 covers exhaust-treating devices for engines that include features designed to withstand or resist high-pressure conditions. [F01N 2260/14] for modifying or adapting flow area or back-pressure -- F01N 2260/14 covers exhaust-treating devices that incorporate features to change the cross-sectional flow area or to adjust back-pressure within the exhaust system, such as variable-geometry silencers or adaptive throttling elements used in engines or pumps.

[F01N 2260/18] for improving rigidity, e.g. by wings, ribs -- This subgroup covers exhaust-treating devices that incorporate structural features such as wings or ribs to increase the rigidity of the component. [F01N 2260/20] for heat or sound protection, e.g. using a shield or specially shaped outer surface of exhaust device -- This CPC subgroup covers exhaust-treating devices that provide heat or sound protection, such as by using a shield or a specially shaped outer surface of the exhaust apparatus. It is nested under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for machines or engines in general.
[F01N 2260/22] for preventing theft of exhaust parts or devices, e.g. anti-theft arrangements -- F01N 2260/22 covers exhaust-treating devices that include anti-theft features, such as locking or shielding arrangements intended to prevent the removal or stealing of exhaust components. This subcategory falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for machines or engines in general.
[F01N 2260/24] for identifying exhaust parts or devices, e.g. by labels, stickers or directly printing -- F01N 2260/24 classifies exhaust-system components that are marked for identification, such as with labels, stickers or direct printing on the parts. It covers methods of applying these identifiers to any exhaust-treating device where such marking is not otherwise specified.
[F01N 2260/26] for preventing enter of dirt into the device -- The classification covers exhaust-treating devices, such as gas-flow silencers, that incorporate features specifically designed to keep foreign matter such as dirt from entering the apparatus. It falls under mechanical engineering → engines or pumps → gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for machines or engines in general. Code F01N 2260/26.
[F01N 2270/00] Mixing air with exhaust gases -- F01N 2270/00 relates to devices or methods that introduce fresh air into the exhaust stream of engines or mechanical systems to promote better mixing, flame stabilization or emission reduction. The technology is applied in exhaust apparatus such as silencers, after-burners or recirculation systems to improve combustion efficiency and control pollutants. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus.
[F01N 2270/02] for cooling exhaust gases or the apparatus -- The code F01N 2270/02 covers devices that mix air with exhaust gases to cool the exhaust stream, including the associated apparatus for this purpose, used in engines or other machinery as part of gas-flow silencers or exhaust systems.
[F01N 2270/04] for afterburning -- F01N 2270/04 covers exhaust systems that incorporate afterburning, where additional air is mixed with the engine's exhaust gases to raise temperature and improve thrust or noise reduction. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for machines or engines in general.
[F01N 2270/06] for silencing -- F01N 2270/06 designates silencing devices for gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus that work by mixing air with exhaust gases, thereby reducing acoustic emissions. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engines or pumps, and concerns general solutions for noise control in machine or engine exhaust systems.
[F01N 2270/08] for evacuation of exhaust gases, e.g. in tail-pipes -- This subclass covers devices that evacuate exhaust gases from engines, such as tail-pipe systems, often using air-mixing techniques to reduce noise or emissions. It is situated under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus that mix air with exhaust gases.
[F01N 2270/10] for rendering exhaust innocuous, e.g. by dilution -- This subclass addresses devices or methods that make engine exhaust harmless by diluting it with additional air or other gases, typically mixing the exhaust stream with ambient air before release. The goal is to lower pollutant concentration and noise, rendering the exhaust innocuous.
[F01N 2290/00] Movable parts or members in exhaust systems for other than for control purposes -- F01N 2290/00 relates to movable parts or members incorporated in exhaust or silencing systems of engines and machines that serve functions other than flow control. Examples include hinged, pivoting, or telescopic components used for access, cleaning, modular assembly or safety rather than for regulating pressure, temperature or acoustic characteristics.
[F01N 2290/02] with continuous rotary movement -- F01N 2290/02 covers movable parts in exhaust or silencing systems that operate with a continuous rotary motion, such as rotating baffles, drums or shafts that continually adjust gas flow. These elements are used for purposes other than active control, providing a steady mechanical means to influence the exhaust stream.
[F01N 2290/04] driven by exhaust gases -- This CPC subclass covers movable components of exhaust or silencing systems that are powered directly by the flow of exhaust gases rather than used for control functions. It includes turbines, pumps, actuators or other members that harvest exhaust energy to drive mechanical motion within the exhaust apparatus.
[F01N 2290/06] driven by auxiliary drive -- F01N 2290/06 refers to exhaust-system components that are moved by an auxiliary drive rather than by the engine's gas flow. These parts, such as adjustable baffles or movable silencers, are used to modify noise or flow characteristics without serving a control function directly linked to the engine.
[F01N 2290/08] with oscillating or vibrating movement -- F01N 2290/08 covers movable components in exhaust or silencer systems that are not used for flow control but perform an oscillating or vibrating motion, such as flexible joints, diaphragms, vibrating baffles or similar parts that aid noise reduction or accommodate thermal expansion.
[F01N 2290/10] actuated by pressure of exhaust gases, e.g. exhaust pulses -- The code F01N 2290/10 refers to movable components in exhaust systems that are driven by the pressure of exhaust gases, for example devices that use exhaust pulses to operate valves or other actuators. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for machines or engines in general.
[F01N 2310/00] Selection of sound absorbing or insulating material -- F01N 2310/00 covers the choice and specification of materials that absorb or insulate sound in gas-flow silencers and exhaust apparatus for engines or machines. It includes criteria, testing methods and design considerations for selecting liners, foams, fibers or composites that reduce noise transmission in exhaust or ventilation systems. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering topics dealing with engines, pumps and their acoustic control.
[F01N 2310/02] Mineral wool, e.g. glass wool, rock wool, asbestos or the like -- A sound-absorbing material of the mineral-wool type-such as glass wool, rock wool or asbestos-is specified for use in gas-flow silencers or exhaust devices for engines and machines. The entry falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and the selection of sound-absorbing or insulating material.
[F01N 2310/04] Metallic wool, e.g. steel wool, copper wool or the like -- This CPC entry covers the use of metallic wool such as steel or copper fibers as a sound-absorbing or insulating material in gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for machines or engines, providing noise reduction in the exhaust system. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and the selection of sound-absorbing material.
[F01N 2310/06] Porous ceramics -- F01N 2310/06 covers porous ceramic materials selected for their sound-absorbing or insulating properties in gas-flow silencers and exhaust apparatus for engines and pumps. It pertains to the use of these ceramics to reduce noise and vibration within mechanical-engineering exhaust systems.
[F01N 2310/08] Exfoliated vermiculite, e.g. zonolite, coke, pumice -- This subclass addresses the selection of exfoliated vermiculite, zonolite, coke, pumice and similar lightweight minerals as sound-absorbing or insulating media in gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for engines. These materials are used to damp acoustic vibrations and reduce noise in the exhaust stream.
[F01N 2310/10] Plastic foam -- F01N 2310/10 denotes plastic foam employed as a sound-absorbing or insulating material in gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for engines and machines, reducing noise and vibration in mechanical engineering applications.
[F01N 2310/12] Granular material -- The code F01N 2310/12 pertains to the use of granular material as a sound-absorbing or insulating fill in gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for engines and pumps. It includes selections such as metal wool, ceramic beads, mineral wool or similar granular inserts placed in the flow path to reduce noise emission.
[F01N 2310/14] Wire mesh fabric, woven glass cloth or the like -- F01N 2310/14 covers the use of wire-mesh fabric, woven glass cloth and similar flexible materials as sound-absorbing or insulating components in gas-flow silencers and exhaust devices for engines or pumps. It addresses the selection of these media to reduce acoustic emissions in such systems.
[F01N 2330/00] Structure of catalyst support or particle filter -- The entry covers the design and arrangement of catalyst supports or particle filters that form part of gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for engines, describing their material, geometry and integration to treat exhaust gases.
[F01N 2330/02] Metallic plates or honeycombs, e.g. superposed or rolled-up corrugated or otherwise deformed sheet metal

[F01N 2330/10] Fibrous material, e.g. mineral or metallic wool -- F01N 2330/10 covers the use of fibrous materials such as mineral or metallic wool as the structural element of catalyst supports or particle filters within gas-flow silencers and exhaust apparatus for engines. The classification addresses how these woven or non-woven fibrous media form the filter matrix that traps particles and facilitates catalytic reactions.
[F01N 2330/101] using binders, e.g. to form a permeable mat, paper or the like -- F01N 2330/101 classifies technologies that employ binding agents to build the supporting structure of catalyst supports or particle filters in gas-flow silencers and exhaust apparatus. The binders are used to form permeable mats, paper-like sheets or similar formats that provide mechanical strength while allowing exhaust gases to pass. Such approaches are relevant to the design of filtration or catalytic components in engines and pumps.
[F01N 2330/102] fibrous material being fiber reinforced polymer made of plastic matrix reinforced by fine glass or in the form of a loose mass of filaments or fibers -- This subclass covers fibrous material that is a fiber-reinforced polymer having a plastic matrix strengthened by fine glass fibers, or presented as a loose mass of filaments, used as a catalyst support or particle filter in gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus.
[F01N 2330/12] Metallic wire mesh fabric or knitting -- F01N 2330/12 covers metallic wire mesh fabrics or knitted structures used as supports for catalysts or particle filters in gas-flow silencers and exhaust systems of engines. These meshes provide a sturdy, permeable framework that traps particles while allowing gas to pass, improving filtration efficiency and durability.
[F01N 2330/14] Sintered material -- F01N 2330/14 covers exhaust or gas-flow silencers in which the catalyst support or particle filter is formed from a sintered material, i.e. a porous structure produced by compacting and heating powder particles without melting. The sintered component provides a high surface area for catalytic activity or particle capture while withstanding exhaust temperatures.
[F01N 2330/18] Composite material -- The code F01N 2330/18 designates composite materials employed as catalyst supports or particle filters within gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for engines and machines, covering their structure and composition in these mechanical engineering applications.
[F01N 2330/20] Plastics, e.g. polymers, polyester, polyurethane
[F01N 2330/22] Metal foam -- Metal foam is a porous metallic structure employed as a catalyst support or particle filter within gas-flow silencers and exhaust apparatus for engines and machines, offering high surface area and controlled flow resistance for contaminant removal and cooling.
[F01N 2330/30] Honeycomb supports characterised by their structural details -- F01N 2330/30 is a CPC subclass within mechanical engineering for engines or pumps, specifically gas-flow silencers and exhaust apparatus. [F01N 2330/32] characterised by the shape, form or number of corrugations of plates, sheets or foils -- F01N 2330/32 pertains to gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus whose catalyst support or particle filter is defined by the geometry of corrugated plates, sheets or foils, specifically the shape, form or number of corrugations employed. [F01N 2330/321] with two or more different kinds of corrugations in the same substrate -- This CPC subclass covers gas-flow silencers or exhaust systems that incorporate a catalyst support or particle filter whose substrate features at least two distinct corrugation patterns. The dual corrugation design is intended to enhance flow resistance or filtration efficiency by providing varied channel geometries within the same filter element.
[F01N 2330/322] Corrugations of trapezoidal form -- The entry covers gas-flow silencer or exhaust apparatus components that use trapezoidal-shaped corrugations as part of the catalyst support or particle-filter structure. This detail falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and exhaust apparatus categories.
[F01N 2330/323] Corrugations of saw-tooth or triangular form -- This CPC subclass (F01N 2330/323) relates to exhaust or gas-flow silencers that incorporate catalyst supports or particle filters whose internal surfaces feature saw-tooth or triangular corrugations. The corrugated geometry is intended to increase turbulence, improve flow distribution, and enhance the mechanical strength or filtering performance of the component.

[F01N 2330/34] with flow channels of polygonal cross section -- The CPC entry F01N 2330/34 falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus, specifically describing the structure of a catalyst support or particle filter that incorporates flow channels having a polygonal cross-section. [F01N 2330/36] with flow channels formed by tubes -- F01N 2330/36 covers gas-flow silencers or exhaust devices whose catalyst support or particle filter incorporates flow channels created by tubular elements. [F01N 2330/38] flow channels with means to enhance flow mixing,(e.g. protrusions or projections) -- F01N 2330/38 covers flow channels that incorporate elements such as protrusions or projections to improve fluid mixing, typically used in gas-flow silencers, exhaust apparatus, catalyst supports or particle filters for engines and pumps.
[F01N 2330/40] made of a single sheet, foil or plate -- This CPC subclass covers gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for engines in which the catalyst support or particle filter is constructed as a single sheet, foil or plate. [F01N 2330/42] made of three or more different sheets, foils or plates stacked one on the other -- This CPC subclass covers exhaust silencers or gas-flow devices whose catalyst support or particle filter is built by stacking three or more different sheets, foils or plates, each layer having distinct material or properties, and is applied in engines or pumps to reduce noise or filter particles.
[F01N 2330/44] made of stacks of sheets, plates or foils that are folded in S-form -- F01N 2330/44 covers structures for catalyst supports or particle filters that consist of stacks of sheets, plates or foils folded into an S-form. [F01N 2330/60] Discontinuous, uneven properties of filter material, e.g. different material thickness along the longitudinal direction -- This subclass covers filter elements whose material properties vary discontinuously along their length, such as sections with different thicknesses or densities, affecting flow resistance in exhaust or gas-flow silencers. [F01N 2340/00] Dimensional characteristics of the exhaust system, e.g. length, diameter or volume of the exhaust apparatus -- This group covers specifications of the physical size of exhaust components for engines and machines, including overall length, pipe diameter, cross-sectional area and internal volume, which affect flow resistance and noise control.
[F01N 2340/02] Distance of the exhaust apparatus to the engine or between two exhaust apparatuses -- The F01N 2340/02 subclass deals with the placement of exhaust components relative to the engine or to each other, covering specifications such as the distance between the exhaust manifold and the engine block or the spacing between multiple exhaust pipes. It addresses dimensional aspects that affect flow, noise reduction and overall engine integration.
[F01N 2340/04] Arrangement of the exhaust system relative to a vehicle or parts thereof -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangement of the exhaust system, e.g. of the exhaust pipe, exhaust manifold or exhaust apparatus, relative to the vehicle or parts thereof, e.g. relative to the vehicle frame or to the vehicle body.
[F01N 2340/06] Arrangement of the exhaust apparatus relative to the turbine of a turbocharger -- The subclass F01N 2340/06 covers the arrangement of an exhaust system in relation to the turbine of a turbocharger, describing how the exhaust pipe, silencer or other components are positioned to influence flow into the turbine and integrate with the overall engine exhaust geometry.
[F01N 2340/08] Series-connected exhaust apparatuses mounted in a side-by-side spatial arrangement, e.g. U- or S-shaped -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Series-connected exhaust apparatuses, e.g. series-connected purifying devices or series-connected silencers, mounted in a side-by-side spatial arrangement, e.g. U- or S-shaped. [F01N 2350/00] Arrangements for fitting catalyst support or particle filter element in the housing -- This subclass covers designs that incorporate a catalyst support or particulate filter directly into the housing of a gas-flow silencer or exhaust system for engines or machines. [F01N 2350/02] Fitting ceramic monoliths in a metallic housing -- A subclass for exhaust systems that details how a ceramic monolith, such as a catalyst or particle filter, is secured inside a metal housing. This enables efficient placement of catalyst or filter components within gas-flow silencers or engine exhaust apparatus.
[F01N 2350/04] with means compensating thermal expansion -- This subclass covers exhaust or gas-flow silencer arrangements in which a catalyst support or particulate-filter element is fitted inside the housing and includes means for compensating thermal expansion, such as flexible mounts or expansion joints.
[F01N 2350/06] with means preventing gas flow by-pass or leakage -- This subclass covers exhaust or gas-flow silencers for engines that include features to prevent gas bypass or leakage, such as seals, gaskets or other sealing means that keep flow confined within the housing around a catalyst support or filter element.
[F01N 2350/08] with means for compressing granular material -- This subgroup covers exhaust or silencer housings for engines that incorporate a catalyst support or particle-filter element and also provide a mechanism to compress the granular filter material during operation. The compression means improve sealing and maintain flow resistance as the filter material settles.
[F01N 2370/00] Selection of materials for exhaust purification -- The code F01N 2370/00 deals with the choice of materials used in exhaust purification systems such as gas-flow silencers and exhaust apparatus for engines and machines. [F01N 2370/02] used in catalytic reactors -- F01N 2370/02 covers the selection of materials used in catalytic reactors for exhaust purification, particularly in gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for engines and pumps, specifying component choices that enable effective treatment of engine exhaust gases.
[F01N 2370/04] Zeolitic material -- F01N 2370/04 covers the use of zeolitic materials in the selection of components for exhaust purification systems of engines and pumps. [F01N 2370/22] used in non-catalytic purification apparatus -- F01N 2370/22 concerns the selection of materials employed in non-catalytic exhaust purification devices such as silencers or other gas-flow cleaning apparatus for engines. It falls under CPC code F01N 2370/24, which addresses selection of materials for exhaust purification in mechanical engineering applications.

[F01N 2370/40] Activated carbon or charcoal -- F01N 2370/40 denotes the use of activated carbon or charcoal as a material for gas-flow silencers or exhaust-purification systems in engines or pumps, covering selection and application of these adsorbents to remove pollutants from exhaust streams. [F01N 2390/00] Arrangements for controlling or regulating exhaust apparatus -- This CPC subclass concerns arrangements that control or regulate exhaust apparatus, such as silencers, for machines or engines. It is located under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for machines or engines in general.
[F01N 2390/02] using electric components only -- F01N 2390/02 covers arrangements for controlling exhaust silencers or apparatus that rely solely on electric components such as sensors, switches, actuators or electronic controllers, with no mechanical, pneumatic or hydraulic elements involved.
[F01N 2390/04] using electropneumatic components -- F01N 2390/04 covers arrangements that control or regulate exhaust apparatus by employing electropneumatic components such as electrically driven pneumatic valves or actuators. It falls within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for engines or pumps and gas-flow silencers, specifically focusing on the use of combined electrical and pneumatic mechanisms to manage exhaust flow.
[F01N 2390/06] using pneumatic components only -- This subclass covers arrangements that control or regulate exhaust apparatus using only pneumatic components such as air-driven valves, actuators or compressors to manage flow, pressure or silencing in engines or pumps, without any hydraulic or electronic devices.
[F01N 2390/08] using mechanical components only, e.g. actuated manually -- F01N 2390/08 covers arrangements for controlling or regulating exhaust apparatus in engines that use only mechanical components, such as manually actuated devices, without any electronic or pneumatic assistance.
[F01N 2410/00] By-passing, at least partially, exhaust from inlet to outlet of apparatus, to atmosphere or to other device -- F01N 2410/00 covers arrangements that divert at least part of the exhaust stream from the inlet of a silencer or exhaust system directly to the atmosphere or to another device, rather than routing it wholly through the silencing chamber. It applies to gas-flow silencers and exhaust apparatus for engines or machines where partial bypass improves flow, reduces back-pressure, or enables auxiliary functions.
[F01N 2410/02] in case of high temperature, e.g. overheating of catalytic reactor -- F01N 2410/02 covers exhaust or gas-flow silencers for engines or machines that include a bypass arrangement activated under high-temperature conditions, such as when a catalytic reactor overheats, allowing exhaust gases to be diverted partially or wholly to the atmosphere or another device to protect the silencer.
[F01N 2410/03] in case of low temperature -- F01N 2410/03 concerns gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus that divert at least part of the exhaust stream from the inlet to the outlet, releasing it to the atmosphere or another device, specifically when the operation occurs at low temperature.
[F01N 2410/04] during regeneration period, e.g. of particle filter -- F01N 2410/04 relates to exhaust systems that include a bypass arrangement used while a particulate filter undergoes regeneration. During the regeneration period the exhaust is partially diverted from the filter to the atmosphere or another device to maintain engine operation and facilitate cleaning. This falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus.
[F01N 2410/06] at cold starting -- F01N 2410/06 covers exhaust or gas-flow silencers for engines that include a bypass arrangement used during cold starting, allowing part of the exhaust to be routed directly to the atmosphere or another device. The feature helps reduce engine wear, lower emissions, and improve start-up performance.
[F01N 2410/08] in case of clogging, e.g. of particle filter -- The CPC subclass F01N 2410/08 covers exhaust-silencing or gas-flow devices for engines that include a bypass system to divert exhaust around a particle filter when it becomes clogged, directing the flow to the atmosphere or another component. This arrangement is part of the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus.
[F01N 2410/10] for reducing flow resistance, e.g. to obtain more engine power -- F01N 2410/10 covers exhaust or gas-flow silencers that incorporate a bypass path allowing part of the exhaust stream to be diverted around the main conduit, reducing flow resistance and increasing engine power output. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for machines or engines in general.
[F01N 2410/12] in case of absorption, adsorption or desorption of exhaust gas constituents -- The entry covers exhaust-gas treatment devices that partially bypass the main flow and employ absorption, adsorption or desorption to remove specific gas constituents. It applies to silencers or exhaust apparatus for engines where such chemical or physical processes are used to clean the emissions.
[F01N 2410/14] in case of excessive pressure, e.g. using a safety valve -- F01N 2410/14 concerns safety mechanisms that vent excess exhaust pressure from engines or pumps, typically using a safety or relief valve to divert gas to the atmosphere or another component when a predetermined pressure is exceeded. The provision ensures controlled bypass of the exhaust flow to prevent damage or hazardous conditions.
[F01N 2430/00] Influencing exhaust purification, e.g. starting of catalytic reaction, filter regeneration, or the like, by controlling engine operating characteristics -- F01N 2430/00 covers methods and devices that influence exhaust purification, such as initiating catalytic reactions or regenerating filters, by adjusting engine operating parameters. It relates to gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for engines, focusing on control strategies that modify combustion or valve timing to improve after-treatment performance.
[F01N 2430/02] by cutting out a part of engine cylinders -- The subclass covers exhaust-purification methods that involve disabling or removing a portion of the engine's cylinders to alter combustion and gas flow. By cutting out part of the cylinders the engine's operating parameters are changed, which can trigger catalytic reactions or regenerate filters. It is applied in gas-flow silencers or exhaust systems to improve emission control.
[F01N 2430/04] by adding non-fuel substances to combustion air or fuel, e.g. additives -- This classification covers techniques that improve exhaust purification-such as catalyst activation or filter regeneration-by introducing non-fuel substances, like additives, into the combustion air or fuel of engines or similar machines. It focuses on controlling engine operating characteristics to influence the exhaust cleaning process.
[F01N 2430/06] by varying fuel-air ratio, e.g. by enriching fuel-air mixture -- A method for improving exhaust purification of engines or pumps by adjusting the fuel-air mixture, such as enriching it, to affect catalytic reactions or filter regeneration. The approach controls engine operating characteristics like the fuel-air ratio to promote cleaner exhaust emissions.
[F01N 2430/08] by modifying ignition or injection timing -- This subgroup covers methods that improve exhaust purification-such as catalyst activation or filter regeneration-by altering the engine's ignition or fuel-injection timing to influence the combustion process.
[F01N 2430/085] at least a part of the injection taking place during expansion or exhaust stroke -- F01N 2430/085 covers engine control methods where at least a portion of the fuel injection is timed to occur during the expansion or exhaust stroke, enabling effects such as catalytic ignition or filter regeneration to improve exhaust purification.
[F01N 2430/10] by modifying inlet or exhaust valve timing -- This subclass covers techniques that alter the timing of an engine's inlet or exhaust valves so that the resulting exhaust flow promotes purification processes such as catalytic ignition, filter regeneration or other emission-reducing actions. By varying valve timing, the engine's operating characteristics are used to influence the composition, temperature or flow of the exhaust gases.
[F01N 2450/00] Methods or apparatus for fitting, inserting or repairing different elements -- F01N 2450/00 covers techniques and devices used to install, replace or mend parts of gas-flow silencers or exhaust systems for engines and machines, including tools for fitting, inserting and repairing elements such as baffles, liners or connectors. The classification addresses maintenance and assembly processes that ensure proper sound attenuation and exhaust flow in mechanical power units.
[F01N 2450/02] Fitting monolithic blocks into the housing -- This subclass covers techniques for installing solid monolithic blocks within the housing of gas-flow silencers or exhaust devices for engines or pumps. It includes methods and apparatus that secure the blocks during assembly or repair, ensuring proper alignment and sealing inside the enclosure.
[F01N 2450/04] Filling or emptying a chamber with granular material -- The CPC subclass F01N 2450/04 addresses methods and apparatuses for filling or emptying a chamber with granular material, commonly applied to engines, pumps or exhaust silencers. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering group dealing with fitting, inserting or repairing components where granular substances are transferred into or out of a cavity.
[F01N 2450/06] Inserting sound absorbing material into a chamber -- F01N 2450/06 relates to methods or devices for fitting sound-absorbing material into a chamber of a gas-flow silencer or exhaust apparatus used with engines or pumps, covering the insertion, positioning and securing of the acoustic filler within the silencer structure.
[F01N 2450/08] Repairing the housing or pipe-joints -- F01N 2450/08 pertains to techniques and devices used to repair the housing or pipe joints of gas-flow silencers or exhaust systems for engines and machines, including methods for fitting, inserting, or restoring damaged components to maintain sealing and structural integrity.
[F01N 2450/10] Fitting temporarily exhaust apparatus on exhaust conduit, e.g. in confined environment, garage or the like -- This classification covers methods and devices for temporarily attaching an exhaust silencer or other exhaust apparatus to a conduit, such as when working in a confined space like a garage. The solution is designed for quick installation and removal without permanent modification of the exhaust system.
[F01N 2450/16] by using threaded joints -- The code F01N 2450/16 covers methods or devices that employ threaded joints to attach, insert, or repair components of gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for engines and machines. It falls within the mechanical engineering subclass for engines or pumps, specifically dealing with fitting and repair of such elements.
[F01N 2450/18] by using quick-active type locking mechanisms, e.g. clips -- The subclass describes methods or devices for attaching, inserting or repairing parts of gas-flow silencers or exhaust systems by means of quick-acting locking mechanisms such as clips, allowing rapid and secure assembly or maintenance.

It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and the broader class of exhaust apparatus for machines.
[F01N 2470/00] Structure or shape of exhaust gas passages, pipes or tubes -- This CPC subclass F01N 2470/00 covers the structural and geometric design of exhaust-gas passages, including the shape, layout and dimensions of pipes, tubes and ducts that convey combustion gases from engines or machines to silencing or emission-control components. It addresses how the physical form of these conduits influences flow, noise reduction and integration with the overall exhaust system.
[F01N 2470/02] Tubes being perforated -- F01N 2470/02 covers perforated tubes used in the design of exhaust gas passages, pipes or tubes for engines and other machines. The classification includes tubes that have holes or slots to modify flow, reduce noise, or improve heat dissipation within exhaust systems.
[F01N 2470/04] characterised by shape, disposition or dimensions of apertures -- This entry pertains to exhaust-gas silencers or exhaust apparatus for engines where the design focuses on the shape, arrangement or dimensions of openings in passages, pipes or tubes, affecting the flow and noise-reduction characteristics.
[F01N 2470/06] Tubes being formed by assembly of stamped or otherwise deformed sheet-metal -- The CPC subclass F01N 2470/06 pertains to tubes that are created by joining stamped or otherwise deformed sheet-metal parts, describing their structure or shape. It falls under mechanical-engineering categories for exhaust-gas passages, pipes or tubes in engines or pumps, emphasizing sheet-metal assembly methods.
[F01N 2470/08] Exhaust gas passages being formed between the walls of an outer shell and an inner chamber -- The code refers to exhaust-gas passages that are formed in the annular gap between an outer shell and an inner chamber of an engine or machine, defining the geometry of the flow path within that space. This subclass addresses the shape and arrangement of such passages in gas-flow silencers or exhaust systems.
[F01N 2470/10] Tubes having non-circular cross section -- F01N 2470/10 classifies tubes whose cross-section is not circular and are used in exhaust-gas passages, pipes or tubes of engines or pumps. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under engines or pumps, specifically gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus, and concerns the structure or shape of the exhaust-gas conduits.
[F01N 2470/12] Tubes being corrugated -- Corrugated tubes are wave-shaped hollow members used in the passageways of exhaust or gas-flow silencers for engines and machines. Their ridged profile provides flexibility, pressure resistance and improved acoustic damping while maintaining structural strength, making them suitable where the exhaust conduit must accommodate thermal expansion or vibration.
[F01N 2470/14] Plurality of outlet tubes, e.g. in parallel or with different length -- The classification addresses exhaust arrangements that employ a plurality of outlet tubes, for example parallel tubes or tubes of different lengths, to discharge gases from engines or machines. This configuration can balance flow, lower back-pressure, or modify acoustic characteristics.
[F01N 2470/16] Plurality of inlet tubes, e.g. discharging into different chambers -- The subclass F01N 2470/16 covers exhaust-gas arrangements that use several inlet tubes, each feeding a different chamber or conduit. It pertains to the structural design of the passages, pipes or tubes that split the flow, often to improve silencing, temperature control or distribution in engines or similar machines.
[F01N 2470/18] the axis of inlet or outlet tubes being other than the longitudinal axis of apparatus -- F01N 2470/18 relates to exhaust-gas passages in engines or machines where the inlet or outlet tubes are oriented at an angle other than the longitudinal axis of the apparatus. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engines or pumps and gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus, and covers the structural design of such non-aligned tubes.
[F01N 2470/20] Dimensional characteristics of tubes, e.g. length, diameter -- F01N 2470/20 deals with the dimensional characteristics of tubes used in exhaust-gas passages, such as length, diameter, wall thickness and overall shape, as applied to silencers, mufflers and other exhaust apparatus for engines and machines.
[F01N 2470/22] Inlet and outlet tubes being positioned on the same side of the apparatus -- This subclass deals with exhaust-gas passages in which the inlet and outlet tubes are mounted on the same side of the silencer or exhaust apparatus, defining a particular layout of the flow channels. It is part of the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for engines or pumps, focusing on the structure or shape of exhaust-gas pipes, tubes or passages.
[F01N 2470/24] Concentric tubes or tubes being concentric to housing, e.g. telescopically assembled -- This CPC subclass covers exhaust-gas passages built with one tube placed inside another or inside the housing in a concentric, often telescopic, arrangement. It includes designs that use nested tubes to direct or silence gas flow in engines or pumps.
[F01N 2470/26] Tubes being formed by extrusion, drawing or rolling -- This CPC subclass covers tubes used in exhaust silencers that are manufactured by extrusion, drawing or rolling processes, defining their shape and structural characteristics. It applies to components forming the gas-flow passages of engines or pumps.

[F01N 2490/02] Two or more expansion chambers in series connected by means of tubes -- This subclass covers exhaust or gas-flow silencers that employ two or more expansion chambers arranged in series and joined by tubes, a configuration used to attenuate noise and smooth pressure fluctuations. [F01N 2490/06] the gases flowing longitudinally from inlet to outlet in opposite directions -- This subclass deals with the arrangement of gas passages in which the main flow moves longitudinally from the inlet toward the outlet while a counter-flow of gas travels in the opposite direction within the same chamber, a configuration employed in silencers or exhaust apparatus for engines. It focuses on the structure, disposition or shape of such gas-chambers.

It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and gas-flow silencers.
[F01N 3/0215] the filtering elements having the form of disks or plates -- F01N 3/0215 covers exhaust or silencing devices for engines that include a treatment function, such as purifying or neutralising the exhaust, and that employ filtering elements shaped as disks or plates. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and gas-flow silencers, specifying the form of the filter components used in the treatment system.
[F01N 3/0217] the filtering elements having the form of hollow cylindrical bodies -- F01N 3/0217 classifies exhaust or silencing devices that incorporate purification functions, specifically the filter components that are made as hollow cylindrical bodies. These elements are used in gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for machines and engines to treat the emissions.

The subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus.

[F01N 3/0224] the structure being granular -- F01N 3/0224 designates exhaust or silencing apparatus that contain a granular structure, such as packed beds or granular catalytic media, used to purify or neutralize exhaust gases. The classification applies to devices where the granular material is integral to the silencer or treatment system.
[F01N 3/0226] the structure being fibrous -- This subclass deals with exhaust or silencing devices for engines that incorporate a fibrous structure, such as filter mats or felt, to treat the gas stream by removing particulates or absorbing pollutants while also reducing noise.
[F01N 3/0228] the structure being made of foamed rubber or plastics -- This subclass concerns exhaust or silencing devices for engines or pumps that incorporate foamed rubber or plastic as the structural material, often to dampen noise and assist in treating or purifying the exhaust gases. The classification emphasizes the use of these lightweight, resilient foamed compounds in the construction of the apparatus.
[F01N 3/023] using means for regenerating the filters, e.g. by burning trapped particles -- F01N 3/023 covers exhaust or silencing systems for engines that incorporate a means to regenerate the filter, for example by incinerating trapped particles to restore filtration capacity. The regeneration function is integrated into the exhaust treatment device to keep emissions low during operation.
[F01N 3/0231] using special exhaust apparatus upstream of the filter for producing nitrogen dioxide, e.g. for continuous filter regeneration systems [CRT] -- The entry covers exhaust or silencing devices that incorporate a special upstream unit to generate nitrogen dioxide, typically for continuous regeneration of downstream filters. It applies to gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for engines where the NO₂ produced helps to oxidise and clean trapped particulates, enabling ongoing filter operation.
[F01N 3/0232] removing incombustible material from a particle filter, e.g. ash -- The subclass covers devices and methods that extract solid, non-combustible residues such as ash from particle filters used in engine exhaust or silencing systems, ensuring continued filtration efficiency. It includes techniques like back-flushing, pulse-jet cleaning, mechanical agitation or other means that purge the filter of accumulated incombustible material.
[F01N 3/0233] periodically cleaning filter by blowing a gas through the filter in a direction opposite to exhaust flow, e.g. exposing filter to engine air intake -- F01N 3/0233 covers exhaust or silencing devices for engines that include a filter which is periodically cleaned by directing a gas flow opposite to the normal exhaust stream, such as using intake air to blow through the filter. The reverse-flow cleaning helps maintain filtering efficiency without removing the filter from the system.
[F01N 3/0234] using heat exchange means in the exhaust line -- F01N 3/0234 covers exhaust or silencing devices that incorporate heat-exchange components in the exhaust stream, allowing recovery or transfer of thermal energy from the gases for purposes such as improving overall engine efficiency, pre-heating intake air, or supporting after-treatment processes.
[F01N 3/0235] using exhaust gas throttling means -- The subclass pertains to exhaust or silencing apparatus that includes a throttling device to regulate the flow of exhaust gases, often to enhance treatment efficiency, reduce emissions, or lower noise in engines or pumps. It covers systems where the throttling means works together with purification or neutralisation functions.
[F01N 3/0236] using turbine waste gate valve -- F01N 3/0236 relates to exhaust or silencing devices for engines that incorporate a turbine waste-gate valve to control gas flow, allowing the exhaust to be treated, purified or rendered harmless as part of the silencing system.
[F01N 3/0237] for regenerating ex situ -- The subclass covers exhaust or silencing systems that incorporate an ex-situ regeneration process, where used filtering or catalytic media are removed from the gas stream, treated or refreshed outside the main apparatus, and then re-installed to restore their purifying effectiveness. This allows continued cleaning of emissions without shutting down the engine.
[F01N 3/0238] for regenerating during engine standstill -- F01N 3/0238 is a CPC subclass for exhaust or silencing devices that include a regeneration function for filters, catalysts or other treatment elements while the engine is at standstill, fitting under mechanical engineering → engines or pumps → gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus → exhaust or silencing apparatus having means for purifying, rendering innocuous, or otherwise treating exhaust.
[F01N 3/025] using fuel burner or by adding fuel to exhaust -- F01N 3/025 pertains to exhaust-or-silencing devices that treat emissions by introducing a fuel burner or adding fuel directly into the exhaust stream, thereby reheating the gases to promote further combustion and reduce pollutants. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering for engines or pumps, specifically gas-flow silencers or general exhaust apparatus equipped with means for purifying the exhaust. It covers designs where fuel is used as a secondary agent to improve exhaust cleaning.
[F01N 3/0253] adding fuel to exhaust gases -- The subclass F01N 3/0253 covers devices that inject fuel into the exhaust stream of engines or machines, usually within a silencer or exhaust pipe, to enable after-burning, catalytic regeneration or other treatment of the gases. It relates to exhaust-purifying or neutralising systems that incorporate fuel-addition mechanisms as part of the overall silencing or emission-control arrangement.

[F01N 3/035] with catalytic reactors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cooling, or removing solid constituents of, exhaust by means of filters in combination with catalytic reactors, e.g. catalysed diesel particulate filters.

[F01N 3/038] by means of perforated plates defining expansion chambers associated with condensation and collection chambers -- This subclass concerns exhaust or silencing apparatus that treats emissions by passing gases through perforated plates that create expansion chambers, which are linked to condensation and collection chambers where pollutants are condensed and gathered. [F01N 3/04] using liquids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cooling, or removing solid constituents of, exhaust using liquids, e.g. water, and without chemical reactions directly involved.
