The elastic components secure the catalyst while permitting movement or expansion during operation, providing a means of treating the exhaust gases. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and specifically gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus.
[F01N 3/2878] by using non-elastic means for retaining catalyst body in the housing, e.g. a metal chamfer, or by corrugation or deformation of the metal housing -- This subgroup covers exhaust or silencing devices that secure the catalyst element in the housing by means of rigid, non-elastic features such as metal chamfers, corrugations or deformed sections of the housing, rather than using springs or other elastic retainers. It applies to systems where the catalyst is held in place by shaping or forming the metal housing.
[F01N 3/2882] Catalytic reactors combined or associated with other devices, e.g. exhaust silencers or other exhaust purification devices  combined with absorbents or adsorbents only  F01N3/0814 ; combined with particulate filters  F01N3/035 -- F01N 3/2882 covers catalytic reactors that are integrated with other exhaust-treatment components, such as silencers, absorbent or adsorbent units, and particulate filters. The code applies to combined systems that purify exhaust gases while also providing noise reduction or solid-particle capture in engines or other machines.
[F01N 3/2885] with exhaust silencers in a single housing -- The code F01N 3/2885 designates exhaust-silencing systems for engines or pumps in which the silencer is integrated together with any treatment or purification device inside a single housing, providing combined noise reduction and exhaust-cleaning functions in one unit.
[F01N 3/2889] with heat exchangers in a single housing -- F01N 3/2889 covers exhaust or silencing devices for engines that incorporate heat-exchange elements within a single housing, allowing the treated exhaust gases to be cooled, heated, or otherwise thermally processed while the silencing function is performed. This integrates pollutant treatment and thermal management in one compact unit.
[F01N 3/2892] Exhaust flow directors or the like, e.g. upstream of catalytic device -- The subclass F01N 3/2892 deals with components that guide or reshape the flow of exhaust gases before they reach a catalytic device, such as ducts, vanes or mixers that direct the stream to improve subsequent treatment. It falls under exhaust-silencing apparatus that includes means for purifying or rendering the exhaust innocuous.
[F01N 3/2896] Liquid catalyst carrier -- F01N 3/2896 refers to liquid catalyst carriers employed in exhaust or silencing devices for engines. The entry covers liquid media that hold catalytic substances for treating, neutralising or otherwise processing exhaust gases, such as reducing pollutants or converting harmful components, often by circulating the liquid through a catalyst bed or injecting it into the exhaust stream.
[F01N 3/30] Arrangements for supply of additional air -- This subgroup deals with systems that introduce extra air into exhaust-treatment or silencing devices, such as induced-draft fans, air-injection ducts, or air-assist mechanisms that enhance combustion or pollutant neutralisation. The additional air is supplied to improve the effectiveness of the purifying or silencing apparatus.
[F01N 3/303] Filtering additional air -- F01N 3/303 classifies exhaust or silencing apparatus for engines that include a filter to clean additional air introduced into the system, removing particulates or pollutants before the air is mixed with combustion gases. This treatment enhances exhaust purification while maintaining normal silencing function.
[F01N 3/306] Preheating additional air -- F01N 3/306 relates to exhaust or silencing systems for engines that incorporate a means for treating the gases and includes devices that preheat additional air before it is introduced into the exhaust stream, improving combustion efficiency or reducing emissions.
[F01N 3/32] using air pump -- F01N 3/32 covers exhaust or silencing devices for engines that incorporate an air-pump to introduce additional air into the exhaust stream, thereby facilitating purification, neutralisation, or other treatment of the emitted gases. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus in general.
[F01N 3/323] Electrically driven air pumps -- F01N 3/323 designates electrically driven air pumps that are incorporated in exhaust or silencing systems equipped with means to treat, purify or otherwise render the exhaust harmless. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engines or pumps and gas-flow silencers, and covers pump designs powered by electricity for handling and conditioning exhaust streams.
[F01N 3/326] Engine-driven air pumps -- F01N 3/326 designates engine-driven air pumps, a type of exhaust or silencing device that incorporates means for treating or purifying the gas flow. It falls within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under engines or pumps and the broader class of gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus for machines or engines in general.
[F01N 3/34] using air conduits or jet air pumps, e.g. near the engine exhaust port -- F01N 3/34 relates to exhaust or silencing systems that incorporate air conduits or jet-air pumps positioned near the engine's exhaust port. The air flow is used to promote mixing, cooling or catalytic treatment of the exhaust, thereby aiding noise reduction and pollutant neutralisation. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus with purification functions.
[F01N 3/36] Arrangements for supply of additional fuel -- In this subgroup, it is desirable to add the indexing codes of F01N2610/14 .
[F01N 3/38] Arrangements for igniting -- F01N 3/38 concerns the design and placement of devices that initiate combustion within exhaust or silencing systems that also incorporate treatment functions such as purification or neutralisation. It includes spark plugs, burners, or other ignition mechanisms integrated into mufflers, catalytic converters, or after-treatment units to aid in the destruction of pollutants or to maintain temperature control.
[F01N 5/00] Exhaust or silencing apparatus combined or associated with devices profiting by exhaust energy -- The F01N 5/00 class covers exhaust or silencing systems that are integrated with or linked to devices that recover or utilize the energy of the exhaust gases. Examples include combined mufflers and heat-recovery turbines or generators that capture waste heat or pressure from engine exhaust for productive use. This classification falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and gas-flow silencers.
[F01N 5/02] the devices using heat -- F01N 5/02 covers devices that harness the heat generated by exhaust or silencing apparatus of engines or pumps, converting waste thermal energy into useful work or secondary energy forms such as power, heated fluids, or propulsion, often through heat exchangers, recuperators or thermoelectric converters.
[F01N 5/025] the device being thermoelectric generators -- F01N 5/025 relates to exhaust or silencing systems for engines or pumps that incorporate thermoelectric generators, converting waste heat from the exhaust stream into electrical power while the apparatus also reduces noise or directs flow. The invention recovers energy that would otherwise be lost, integrating power generation with the machine's exhaust-energy-management components.
[F01N 5/04] the devices using kinetic energy -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Exhaust or silencing apparatus combined or associated with devices converting the exhaust energy into kinetic energy, e.g. exhaust-driven turbines coupled to electrical generators.
[F01N 9/00] Electrical control of exhaust gas treating apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods of electrically controlling exhaust gas treating apparatus and of exhaust devices not provided elsewhere.
[F01N 9/002] of filter regeneration -- F01N 9/002 concerns the regeneration of filters used in exhaust-gas treatment systems for engines and other machines. It covers electrical control methods for cleaning or reactivating particulate or catalytic filters to restore their performance during operation. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and gas-flow silencers or exhaust apparatus.
[F01N 9/005] using models instead of sensors to determine operating characteristics of exhaust systems, e.g. calculating catalyst temperature instead of measuring it directly -- The F01N 9/005 entry covers methods that use mathematical or computational models rather than physical sensors to infer the performance of exhaust-gas treatment systems, such as estimating catalyst temperature or flow characteristics from engine operating data. This approach replaces direct measurement with predictive calculations.
[F01N 9/007] Storing data relevant to operation of exhaust systems for later retrieval and analysis, e.g. to research exhaust system malfunctions
[F01N 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- F01N 99/00 covers any gas-flow silencer or exhaust apparatus for engines that does not fit into the more specific groups of this subclass, providing a residual category for novel or uncommon designs, modifications or combinations that are not otherwise classified.
[F01P] COOLING OF MACHINES OR ENGINES IN GENERAL -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cooling of machines, where "machine" means a device which could equally be an engine and a pump, and not a device which is restricted to an engine or one which is restricted to a pump.  Cooling of engines, where "engine" means a device for continuously converting fluid energy into mechanical power, e.g. steam piston engines or steam turbines.  Cooling of internal combustion engines.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cooling of machine tools -> B23Q1/00 - Arrangements in connection with cooling of propulsion units in vehicles -> B60K11/00 - Heat-exchange or heat-storage materials -> C09K5/00 - Cooling of gas-turbine engines -> F02C7/12 - Profiting from the waste heat of the exhaust gases -> F02G5/02 , F01N5/02 - Cogeneration -> F25B27/02 - Heat exchange in general, radiators -> F28 - Cooling of electric machines -> H02K9/00  **Glossary:** - Air: gaseous cooling fluids - Liquid cooling: includes also cooling where liquid is used as the heat transferring fluid between parts to be cooled and the air, e.g. using radiators - Air cooling: direct air cooling and thus excludes indirect air cooling occurring in liquid cooling systems as explained herefore - Cooling air: directly or indirectly acting air for cooling - Engine: device for continuously converting fluid energy into mechanical power. Thus, this term includes, for example, steam piston engines or steam turbines, per se, or internal-combustion piston engines, but it excludes single-stroke devices. "Engine" also includes the fluid-motive portion of a meter unless such portion is particularly adapted for use in a meter - Machine: device which could equally be an engine and a pump, and not a device which is restricted to an engine or one which is restricted to a pump
[F01P 1/00] Air cooling -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Air cooling of the engine parts as cylinders or cylinder heads, but also cooling of the engine room, when related to the engine cooling circuit.  **Glossary:** - Engine: an internal combustion engine
[F01P 1/02] Arrangements for cooling cylinders or cylinder heads, e.g. ducting cooling-air from its pressure source to cylinders or along cylinders -- This subclass deals with air-cooling arrangements that channel pressurised air from its source to engine cylinders or cylinder heads, including ducts, passages and distribution systems that run along or between the cylinders. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and general cooling of machines or engines.
[F01P 1/04] Arrangements for cooling pistons -- F01P 1/04 covers the design and integration of systems that provide cooling to pistons in internal-combustion or similar engines. It includes features such as internal cooling passages, oil jets, coolant sprays or other mechanisms that remove heat from the piston surface to maintain operating temperature and prevent damage.
[F01P 1/06] Arrangements for cooling other engine or machine parts -- F01P 1/06 covers designs for removing heat from engine or machine components other than the main combustion or working fluid, typically using forced or natural air flow. It includes ducts, fans, radiators and other air-cooling arrangements for parts such as bearings, housings or auxiliary devices.
[F01P 1/08] for cooling intake or exhaust valves -- The CPC subclass F01P 1/08 covers methods and apparatuses that cool the intake or exhaust valves of engines, typically by directing air over these components. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and general cooling of machines or engines, specifically air cooling.
[F01P 1/10] for cooling fuel injectors or sparking-plugs -- This CPC subclass is nested under Mechanical Engineering → Engines or Pumps → Cooling of Machines or Engines in General → Air cooling. It pertains to arrangements or methods that provide air cooling specifically for fuel injectors or spark-plugs.
[F01P 11/00] Component parts, details, or accessories not provided for in, or of interest apart from, groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Venting, overflow, deaeration, filling of the circuit, expansion reservoirs, reservoir caps, draining and purging, indicating and safety devices, filtration and silencing of the cooling air and also regenerative heat accumulators.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Arrangement of the radiator in the vehicle -> B60K11/04
[F01P 11/02] Liquid-coolant , overflow, venting, or draining devices -- F01P 11/02 covers devices that manage excess liquid coolant in engines or pumps, such as overflow reservoirs, venting mechanisms, and draining systems, ensuring safe removal or release of coolant to prevent over-pressure and maintain proper cooling performance.
[F01P 11/0204] Filling -- F01P 11/0204 covers devices, methods and accessories used to fill cooling systems of machines or engines, such as reservoirs, pumps, valves and connectors that introduce coolant or lubricant into the circuit. It pertains to components not otherwise classified within the broader cooling-of-machines groups.
[F01P 11/0209] Closure caps -- F01P 11/0209 covers closure caps used in the cooling systems of engines or machines. These components seal openings such as ports, ducts or coolant reservoirs to prevent leakage or contamination. The caps are classified under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, cooling of machines or engines in general.
[F01P 11/0214] Mounting -- F01P 11/0214 - Mounting concerns the brackets, supports, fixtures and other accessories that attach cooling system components such as radiators, heat exchangers, coolant lines or related parts to engines or pumps, when those elements are not covered by more specific categories.
[F01P 11/0238] with overpressure valves or vent valves -- F01P 11/0238 relates to cooling systems for engines or machines and specifically to component parts, details or accessories that incorporate over-pressure or vent valves for releasing excess pressure.
[F01P 11/0247] Safety; Locking against opening -- F01P 11/0247 covers safety devices and mechanisms that prevent accidental opening of components related to the cooling of machines or engines. It includes locking or securing arrangements for parts such as valves, ducts, or fittings that are not otherwise classified, ensuring that the cooling system remains closed during operation.
[F01P 11/0276] Draining or purging -- F01P 11/0276 pertains to the draining or purging of cooling-system liquids or gases in machines or engines, covering methods, devices or procedures that remove residual fluid, air or contaminants from radiators, heat exchangers, jackets or related components to maintain efficient heat removal.
[F01P 11/028] Deaeration devices -- F01P 11/028 covers deaeration devices used in cooling systems of engines or pumps to remove dissolved air and gases from the circulating fluid, thereby improving heat transfer efficiency and preventing corrosion or cavitation. These components are classified as accessories not specified in other groups.
[F01P 11/0285] Venting devices -- F01P 11/0285 pertains to venting devices employed in cooling systems for engines or pumps, covering components that release trapped gases or fluids to enhance heat removal. It includes pressure relief valves, vents and blow-off mechanisms that prevent pressure buildup and improve cooling efficiency.
[F01P 11/029] Expansion reservoirs -- Expansion reservoirs are accessory components that store coolant or other working fluid and release it to maintain stable pressure and temperature in the cooling systems of engines or machines. They help smooth out temperature and pressure fluctuations during load changes and are used alongside radiators, thermostats or heat exchangers.
[F01P 11/0295] Condensers for radiators -- F01P 11/0295 designates condensers that are part of radiator cooling systems for engines or machines, covering component parts and accessories that are not specified elsewhere in the classification. It is situated in the mechanical engineering hierarchy under engines or pumps and cooling of machines or engines in general.
[F01P 11/04] Arrangements of liquid pipes or hoses -- This subclass addresses the design and layout of liquid-carrying pipes or hoses used in cooling systems for machines and engines, focusing on how the conduits are routed, connected and integrated as separate components rather than the pipes themselves.
[F01P 11/06] Cleaning -- F01P 11/06 covers cleaning of mechanical equipment, specifically the component parts, details or accessories of engines or pumps that are not otherwise classified. It includes methods, devices and arrangements used to remove contaminants from such elements, irrespective of the broader cooling or lubrication functions.
[F01P 11/08] Arrangements of lubricant coolers -- This subclass deals with how lubricant coolers are incorporated into machines or engines, covering the placement of cooler units, associated piping, heat-exchanger arrangements and control elements that direct oil through a cooling device. It includes configurations where the lubrication circuit is combined with a cooling system or where the cooler is integrated with other engine components.
[F01P 11/10] Guiding or ducting cooling-air, to, or from, liquid-to-air heat exchangers -- This subclass covers devices that direct or channel cooling-air to or from liquid-to-air heat exchangers in engines or other machines, including ducts, manifolds, shrouds and related accessory components not covered elsewhere. It is situated under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and cooling of machines or engines in general.
[F01P 11/12] Filtering, cooling, or silencing cooling-air -- F01P 11/12 covers devices that filter, cool, or reduce noise of the air used for cooling machines or engines. It includes components such as air filters, heat exchangers, and silencers that are separate from the main engine or pump assembly. The classification is part of the mechanical engineering group for engines or pumps.
[F01P 11/14] Indicating devices -- F01P 11/14 covers indicating devices that monitor and display operating conditions of cooling systems for engines or machines, such as temperature, pressure or flow gauges. These components are treated as separate accessories rather than integral parts of the cooling apparatus.
[F01P 11/16] concerning coolant temperature -- F01P 11/16 covers inventions that deal with the temperature of the coolant used in machines or engines, such as sensors, control devices, or methods for measuring, maintaining or adjusting coolant temperature. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and cooling of machines or engines, specifically for component parts or accessories not otherwise classified.
[F01P 11/18] concerning coolant pressure, coolant flow, or liquid-coolant level -- The F01P 11/18 subgroup deals with components, mechanisms and accessories that control or monitor coolant pressure, regulate coolant flow, or indicate liquid-coolant level within engine or machine cooling systems, covering items not otherwise specified in broader cooling-of-machines categories.
[F01P 11/20] concerning atmospheric freezing conditions, e.g. automatically draining or heating during frosty weather -- F01P 11/20 relates to components for engines or pumps that protect against atmospheric freezing, such as devices that automatically drain or heat parts in frosty weather to prevent ice formation.
[F01P 2001/005] Cooling engine rooms -- F01P 2001/005 relates to cooling engine rooms, covering systems that remove heat generated by ship or marine propulsion units. It includes water-cooled loops, air-flow ducts, heat exchangers and control devices designed to maintain safe operating temperatures for machinery and surrounding spaces.
[F01P 2001/023] Cooling cylinders  F01P2003/022  takes precedence -- A cooling cylinder is a device incorporated in engines or similar machines that circulates coolant through the cylinder walls to remove heat generated during operation, improving thermal management and preventing overheating. It typically includes passages, jackets or channels that allow fluid flow directly around the combustion chamber. The later CPC entry F01P 2003/022 supersedes this classification, covering more specific or advanced cooling-cylinder arrangements.
[F01P 2001/026] Cooling cylinder heads  F01P2003/025  takes precedence -- CPC F01P 2001/026 covers technologies for removing heat from engine cylinder heads, including external cooling jackets, coolant passages and related apparatus that keep the head temperature within operating limits. The more specific subclass F01P2003/025, which deals with internal coolant circulation within the head, takes precedence over this broader group.
[F01P 2003/001] Cooling liquid -- F01P 2003/001 relates to cooling liquids, the fluids employed to absorb and transfer heat in refrigeration, air-conditioning, heat-pump and other thermal-management systems. It includes substances such as water, glycol solutions, refrigerants in liquid form and any additives that modify thermal or physical properties for efficient cooling.
[F01P 2003/003] having boiling-point higher than 100°C -- The entry F01P 2003/003 in the CPC denotes inventions dealing with fluids whose boiling point exceeds 100 °C. It is used to classify technologies such as high-temperature lubricants, hydraulic or cooling media, and processes where low volatility is required.
[F01P 2003/005] the liquid being fuel -- The CPC entry F01P 2003/005 covers inventions in which the processed liquid is a fuel, such as pumps, storage, transport and conditioning devices designed specifically for combustible liquids. It applies to systems where the fuel character of the liquid influences the design, materials or safety features.
[F01P 2003/006] the liquid being oil -- F01P 2003/006 refers to machines or plant in which the conveyed fluid is oil, encompassing pumps, compressors and related handling equipment designed for the specific physical and chemical properties of oil. The classification addresses aspects such as oil viscosity, lubricating function and containment requirements in industrial oil processing.
[F01P 2003/008] the liquid being water and oil -- F01P 2003/008 pertains to power-plant or heat-exchange equipment where the working fluid is a mixture of water and oil, a design intended to enhance heat-transfer efficiency or operational stability.
[F01P 2003/021] Cooling cylinders -- Cooling cylinders are hollow members through which a fluid circulates and is cooled by heat exchange with an external medium, commonly used in refrigeration, air-conditioning, and industrial process cooling. They are typically arranged in water-cooled condensers or oil-cooled heat exchangers to maximize surface area and flow efficiency.
[F01P 2003/022] combined with air cooling -- The CPC class F01P 2003/022 covers refrigerating or heat-pump installations that also use air cooling as an auxiliary or complementary method, such as hybrid systems where a compressor-based cycle is combined with direct air-flow heat exchangers to improve efficiency or manage thermal loads.
[F01P 2003/024] Cooling cylinder heads -- F01P 2003/024 pertains to cooling of cylinder heads in internal-combustion engines. It includes designs and methods for delivering coolant to the head, internal water passages, and related heat-removal structures such as jackets, channels or external cooling devices.
[F01P 2003/025] combined with air cooling -- F01P 2003/025 covers machines whose primary function is combined with an air-cooling system, such as internal-combustion engines, gas turbines or related power units that integrate air cooling into their design to dissipate heat during operation. The classification is used for patents describing how the cooling air is routed, managed or combined with the main mechanical process.
[F01P 2003/027] Cooling cylinders and cylinder heads in parallel -- F01P 2003/027 covers engine designs in which the cooling system is arranged so that the cylinders and their heads lie side-by-side in a parallel orientation, typically using shared water jackets or passages to provide uniform temperature control and efficient heat dissipation.
[F01P 2003/028] Cooling cylinders and cylinder heads in series -- F01P 2003/028 relates to cooling systems where the coolant is passed sequentially through a series of cylinders or cylinder heads, commonly found in internal combustion engines. The classification covers designs that arrange the cylinders or heads in a chain so that heat is removed from each in turn, improving thermal efficiency and reducing hotspots.
[F01P 2003/182] with multiple heat-exchangers -- The subclass covers propulsion units that incorporate more than one heat-exchanger, such as engines or turbines using separate exchangers for cooling, pre-heating, or waste-heat recovery. Employing multiple heat-exchangers improves thermal efficiency and allows distinct temperature control for different system parts.
[F01P 2003/185] arranged in parallel -- F01P 2003/185 covers pumps in which multiple identical functional elements such as impellers, stages or flow channels are positioned side-by-side so that their flow paths run parallel to each other. This arrangement lets the pump handle larger volumes or provide redundancy while maintaining the same pressure rise. The classification is applied to parallel-arranged centrifugal, axial or mixed-flow pump designs.
[F01P 2003/187] arranged in series -- The CPC group F01P 2003/187 covers fluid-moving devices such as pumps, compressors, fans or blowers that are installed one after another so the discharge of one unit becomes the suction of the next, creating a series arrangement that raises pressure, flow or efficiency compared with a single machine.
[F01P 2003/2214] Condensers -- The class covers apparatus and methods for condensing refrigerant vapour in refrigeration or heat-pump cycles, including air-cooled, water-cooled and hybrid heat-exchanger designs such as finned tubes, shell-and-tube, plate and micro-channel condensers.
[F01P 2003/2221] of the horizontal type -- The CPC entry F01P 2003/2221 refers to wind turbines of the horizontal type, meaning the rotor axis is parallel to the ground. This group covers designs such as the common three-blade, horizontally rotating machines used for electricity generation.
[F01P 2003/2228] of the upflow type -- This CPC subclass covers bioreactors or fermentation vessels that operate with an upflow configuration, where the feed enters at the bottom and moves upward through the reaction zone. It includes designs such as upflow anaerobic sludge-blanket reactors and other upward-flow process equipment.
[F01P 2003/2235] of the downflow type -- F01P 2003/2235 refers to pumps of the downflow type, where the fluid enters the machine above the impeller and flows downward through the pump before being discharged, a configuration commonly used in vertical centrifugal or multistage pumps that exploit gravity to assist the flow.
[F01P 2003/2242] Steam-to-steam condensers -- F01P 2003/2242 relates to steam-to-steam condensers, which are heat-exchange devices that condense exhaust steam by transferring its latent heat to a separate stream of cooler steam. The condensed steam returns as liquid water while the heating steam is further utilized, improving overall thermal efficiency in power-generation and district-heating cycles. This technology is employed in combined-cycle and heat-recovery systems where steam serves both as coolant and as a source of usable heat.
[F01P 2003/225] Steam-to-liquid condensers -- F01P 2003/225 covers condensers that turn steam directly into liquid, typically integrated in steam turbine cycles for power generation or process plants. The entry includes designs that improve heat transfer, reduce fouling, and operate under high pressure and temperature, often employing shell-and-tube or plate-type configurations.
[F01P 2003/2257] Rotating condensers -- F01P 2003/2257 pertains to rotating condensers, a type of heat-exchanger where the condensing surface is set in motion to enhance heat-transfer efficiency, often used in refrigeration, air-conditioning or chemical-process equipment. The rotation may be driven by the process fluid or an external motor and can involve multiple tubes or fins arranged on a rotating drum or cylinder.
[F01P 2003/2264] Separators -- The CPC class F01P 2003/2264 concerns separators used in fluid-power systems. It includes devices that separate liquids, gases or solids from a fluid stream, such as oil-water separators, air-oil separators and filter-type separators employed in hydraulic or pneumatic circuits.
[F01P 2003/2278] Heat pipes -- F01P 2003/2278 covers heat pipes, sealed devices that transport heat by the evaporation and condensation of a working fluid within a closed circuit. The entry includes pipe designs, wick structures, working fluids, manufacturing methods and their use in cooling, thermal-management and energy-recovery applications.
[F01P 2003/2292] with thermostatically controlled by-pass -- F01P 2003/2292 classifies pumps that incorporate a by-pass circuit whose flow is automatically regulated by a thermostat. The thermostatically controlled by-pass maintains a desired temperature in the pumped fluid by diverting a portion of the flow when the set point is exceeded, and is typically used in heating, cooling or process-temperature-control systems.
[F01P 2005/025] using two or more air pumps -- F01P 2005/025 covers inventions that employ two or more air pumps working together, typically to increase pressure, flow rate, or redundancy in pneumatic, ventilation, or compression systems. The classification includes arrangements where the pumps are mechanically or electronically coordinated to achieve a combined performance not attainable by a single pump.
[F01P 2005/046] with electrical pump drive -- F01P 2005/046 covers pumping apparatus whose motive power is supplied by an electrical drive, typically an electric motor coupled directly or via gearing to the pump. The classification includes centrifugal, rotary-vane, diaphragm and other pump types where the electrical motor provides the rotating or reciprocating motion needed for fluid displacement.
[F01P 2005/105] Using two or more pumps -- The CPC class F01P 2005/105 relates to fluid-handling systems that employ two or more pumps, either in series to raise pressure or in parallel to increase flow or provide redundancy. It covers the arrangement, control and integration of multiple pumps within a single installation.
[F01P 2005/125] Driving auxiliary pumps electrically -- The classification covers machines in which auxiliary pumps are powered by an electric drive rather than a mechanical link to the main engine. It includes arrangements where the electric motor directly drives the pump for functions such as lubrication, cooling or pressure boosting, and the pump is considered a secondary or supporting unit to the primary propulsion system.
[F01P 2007/143] using restrictions -- F01P 2007/143 covers inventions that impose restrictions on the use of gas or steam turbine systems, for example by limiting operating parameters, load, or runtime to protect components or enhance safety. It is applied to designs that embed control mechanisms or procedural limits that deliberately constrain turbine operation.
[F01P 2007/146] using valves -- The CPC class F01P 2007/146 refers to the use of valves in water treatment and management installations. It covers inventions that incorporate valves to regulate flow, pressure, or direction of water or waste streams in processes such as filtration, desalination, sewage treatment and related hydraulic systems.
[F01P 2007/168] By varying the cooling capacity of a liquid-to-air heat-exchanger -- The CPC subclass F01P 2007/168 covers liquid-to-air heat exchangers whose cooling capacity can be varied, typically by changing the liquid flow rate, adjusting control valves, or altering heat-transfer surface conditions. It includes any design or method that enables the device to modulate heat removal in response to demand.
[F01P 2009/005] Cooling with melting solids -- Cooling with melting solids covers technologies that use phase-change materials to absorb heat as they melt, providing thermal regulation for systems such as refrigeration, air-conditioning, or process cooling. The approach exploits the latent heat of fusion of selected solids to maintain desired temperatures with high efficiency and compact equipment. It includes methods, apparatuses and control strategies for integrating such materials into cooling cycles.
[F01P 2011/0219] using bayonet connections -- F01P 2011/0219 classifies machines-particularly pumps, compressors or hydraulic devices-that incorporate bayonet-type connectors for the quick coupling and decoupling of fluid lines. The entry covers any use of a bayonet connection as the means of joining conduits in such equipment.
[F01P 2011/0223] Decoration -- The CPC entry F01P 2011/0223 covers patents that relate to decorative elements applied to agricultural or horticultural machinery. It includes ornamental designs, color schemes, logos or embellishments intended solely to improve the appearance of the equipment, with functional aspects excluded.
[F01P 2011/0228] Sealing -- F01P 2011/0228 covers sealing solutions for mechanical and pressure equipment, including static and dynamic seals, packing, gaskets, and sealing arrangements for shafts, housings and pressure vessels in power plants, pumps and related installations. The group addresses the design, materials and methods used to prevent leakage of fluids or gases under various operating conditions.
[F01P 2011/0233] Venting -- The CPC subclass F01P 2011/0233 covers devices and methods for venting gases, liquids or vapours from pumps, compressors, turbines or related fluid-handling equipment. It includes arrangements that relieve pressure, remove entrained air or discharge excess fluid to maintain performance or safety. The focus is on the venting function rather than the primary pumping or compressing action.
[F01P 2011/0242] setting the pressure valve -- F01P 2011/0242 covers devices or methods for setting a pressure valve, i.e., adjusting or presetting the pressure at which the valve opens or closes to regulate fluid flow. It applies to hydraulic, pneumatic or other fluid systems where precise pressure control is required for performance or safety.
[F01P 2011/0252] Venting before opening -- F01P 2011/0252 covers systems and methods that release pressure or gases from a container, pipe or vessel before it is opened, ensuring safe access and preventing sudden discharge. It includes venting devices, control procedures and safety mechanisms designed to reduce hazards during maintenance or inspection.
[F01P 2011/0257] with theft preventing means -- The CPC entry F01P 2011/0257 covers mechanical or electromechanical systems that include features designed to deter or prevent theft of the protected item. This includes integrated locks, alarm triggers, immobilizers, and other anti-theft components that activate when unauthorized access is attempted. It is used for classifying inventions where theft-prevention means are part of the device's functional design.
[F01P 2011/0261] activated by temperature -- The CPC subclass F01P 2011/0261 groups inventions where a function, operation or component is initiated or switched on by a change in temperature. It covers mechanisms, materials or control systems that respond to a specific temperature threshold, such as thermal actuators, temperature-sensitive valves or heat-triggered release devices.
[F01P 2011/0266] activated by pressure -- This CPC subclass covers pest control or plant-protection compositions and devices that are released or become active only when pressure is applied, such as pressure-activated sprayers, aerosol dispensers or pressure-rupture capsules delivering the active ingredient.
[F01P 2011/0271] Semi-permeable, e.g. using Gore-Tex c fibres -- F01P 2011/0271 covers semi-permeable structures such as Gore-Tex woven fibres that allow selective passage of gases or liquids while blocking others, commonly employed in breathable fabrics, filtration systems and fluid-control components for building, apparel or industrial uses.
[F01P 2011/061] Cleaning or combating corrosion using filters -- F01P 2011/061 covers methods and devices that use filter elements to remove corrosive substances from liquids or gases or to deliver anti-corrosion agents, thereby cleaning or protecting equipment from corrosion.
[F01P 2011/063] Cleaning  F01P2011/061  takes precedence
[F01P 2011/065] Flushing -- The CPC subclass F01P 2011/065 covers apparatus, systems and methods for flushing, i.e., the introduction of a fluid to clean or clear residues from pipelines, vessels, filters, tanks or related equipment to restore or maintain flow, remove contaminants or prevent buildup. It includes designs for flushing devices, control arrangements and procedures used in industrial, agricultural or municipal water-handling and processing installations.
[F01P 2011/066] Combating corrosion  F01P2011/061  takes precedence
[F01P 2011/068] chemically -- F01P 2011/068 - chemically - concerns the chemical treatment of gases or waste streams in processes for energy recovery or pollution control, such as scrubbing, catalytic conversion, or other chemical reactions applied to the feed or product gases.
[F01P 2011/205] using heat-accumulators -- F01P 2011/205 concerns the use of heat accumulators in mechanical or plant engineering. It includes methods and devices that store thermal energy in materials such as molten salts, phase-change substances or water to be released later for heating, power generation or process heating. Applications range from solar-thermal power plants to industrial waste-heat recovery systems.
[F01P 2023/00] Signal processing -- F01P 2023/00 is a Cooperative Patent Classification subclass under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and cooling of machines or engines in general, covering signal-processing techniques used to monitor, control or optimize cooling systems for engines, pumps or related machinery.
[F01P 2023/08] Microprocessor -- F01P 2023/08 covers microprocessors that are employed in signal-processing functions for the cooling of machines or engines. The entry relates to electronic control units, sensors and software that monitor temperature, regulate coolant flow and optimise thermal management in mechanical systems.
[F01P 2025/00] Air cooling -- F01P 2025/00 covers air cooling methods for machines and engines, including the design and arrangement of fans, ducts, heat exchangers, and other devices that use ambient air to remove heat from mechanical components. It addresses general principles and specific implementations for improving thermal management in internal combustion engines, pumps, and other rotating equipment.
[F01P 2025/04] Pressure -- The CPC subclass F01P 2025/04 pertains to the pressure of air employed in cooling engines or pumps. It includes methods, devices, or control systems that generate, regulate, or monitor the air pressure applied in air-cooling arrangements for mechanical power units.
[F01P 2025/06] for determining flow -- This subclass pertains to devices, methods or analytical techniques that determine the flow of cooling air in engines or pumps, covering sensors, measurement systems and computational approaches used to assess air-cooling performance.
[F01P 2025/08] Temperature -- F01P 2025/08 covers temperature aspects in the air-cooling of machines or engines, including the measurement, regulation and effects of temperature on cooling performance and efficiency. It relates to systems that manage heat removal from mechanical devices through forced or natural airflow.
[F01P 2025/12] Cabin temperature -- The entry covers equipment and methods for regulating the temperature inside a vehicle or machine cabin using air-cooling techniques, such as ventilation ducts, fans, or heat exchangers that condition interior air. It pertains to the broader field of cooling machines or engines by moving air and is applied to maintaining comfortable or safe cabin conditions.
[F01P 2025/13] Ambient temperature -- F01P 2025/13 deals with air-cooled engines or machines where the surrounding ambient temperature influences heat dissipation. It covers design, control and performance aspects that take the environmental temperature into account, fitting within the broader cooling-of-machines or engines category in mechanical engineering.

It is situated under mechanical engineering, specifically engines or pumps and the general cooling of machines or engines.
[F01P 2025/31] Cylinder temperature -- The CPC class F01P 2025/31 pertains to the temperature of engine cylinders and the means of controlling it by air cooling. It covers devices, structures and methods such as cooling fins, airflow ducts, and temperature-sensing arrangements that manage or monitor cylinder heat during operation.
[F01P 2025/32] Engine outcoming fluid temperature -- F01P 2025/32 covers inventions that monitor, regulate or otherwise manage the temperature of fluid leaving an engine, typically in the context of air-cooled cooling systems for mechanical engines and pumps.
[F01P 2025/33] Cylinder head temperature -- F01P 2025/33 designates inventions that monitor, control or influence the temperature of an engine's cylinder head during air-cooled operation. It covers sensors, thermostats, cooling passages or methods that keep the head temperature within optimal limits to improve performance and avoid overheating.
[F01P 2025/34] Heat exchanger incoming fluid temperature -- The CPC subclass F01P 2025/34 covers heat-exchanger arrangements that specify or control the temperature of the fluid entering the exchanger, typically in air-cooled engine or machine cooling systems. [F01P 2025/36] Heat exchanger mixed fluid temperature -- F01P 2025/36 covers heat exchangers that combine fluids of different temperatures to regulate the temperature of machines or engines, typically using air as the cooling medium. [F01P 2025/40] Oil temperature -- F01P 2025/40 covers the control, regulation and monitoring of oil temperature in engines, pumps or similar machinery, including devices, methods and systems that heat, cool or sense lubricating oil to maintain optimal operating conditions and prevent overheating.

[F01P 2025/44] Outlet manifold temperature -- The entry concerns devices or methods for measuring, monitoring or controlling the temperature of the outlet-manifold in air-cooled engine or pump systems, helping to optimise heat removal and prevent overheating.
[F01P 2025/46] Engine parts temperature -- F01P 2025/46 covers the temperature of engine components, focusing on the measurement, regulation and control of heat in parts of air-cooled engines. [F01P 2025/50] using two or more temperature sensors -- The F01P 2025/50 entry covers air-cooled engines or pumps that employ two or more temperature sensors to monitor and control the cooling process. It addresses systems that use multiple sensors for more accurate temperature measurement, feedback regulation, or safety functions in mechanical cooling applications.
[F01P 2025/52] Heat exchanger temperature -- F01P 2025/52 refers to the temperature control aspects of heat exchangers used for air cooling of machines or engines. It classifies inventions that regulate, monitor or adjust the operating temperature of air-cooled heat exchangers, such as those employed in engines, pumps or other mechanical systems, to improve efficiency and prevent overheating.
[F01P 2025/60] Operating parameters -- F01P 2025/60 covers the operating parameters relevant to air-cooling systems for engines or pumps, such as temperature limits, airflow rates, pressure drops and fan speeds. [F01P 2025/62] Load -- This subclass deals with the load applied to air-cooled engines or machines, encompassing how the cooling system is sized and operated to handle varying thermal and mechanical demands, the influence of load on airflow distribution, and methods for managing heat under different operating conditions.

[F01P 2025/70] Level -- F01P 2025/70 "Level" covers features and methods for monitoring or controlling the coolant level in air-cooled engine or machine cooling systems, including sensors, level-adjustment devices and related circuitry that ensure adequate coolant quantity for effective heat dissipation.

[F01P 2031/00] Fail safe -- The F01P 2031/00 entry concerns fail-safe features for cooling systems of engines or pumps, covering devices, methods and control strategies that detect faults and automatically maintain or shut down cooling to prevent overheating. It includes backup cooling mechanisms, protective shutdown procedures and monitoring circuits that ensure safe operation of the cooling circuit under abnormal conditions.
[F01P 2031/16] using melting materials -- The F01P 2031/16 sub-class covers fail-safe cooling methods for engines or pumps that employ materials which melt at a predetermined temperature to absorb heat and protect the machine. By using a melting medium, the system automatically limits temperature rise, providing a passive safety mechanism without active control. This approach is applied to general cooling of mechanical power units.
[F01P 2031/18] Detecting fluid leaks -- The F01P 2031/18 subclass covers inventions that monitor or locate fluid leaks in cooling systems of engines or machines, providing fail-safe detection to prevent loss of coolant or overheating. It includes sensors, diagnostic methods and leak-identifying devices integrated into the cooling circuit.
[F01P 2031/20] Warning devices -- F01P 2031/20 covers warning devices associated with the cooling of machines or engines, such as sensors, alarms or indicators that alert operators to abnormal temperature or coolant flow conditions, helping to prevent overheating and ensure safe operation.
[F01P 2031/22] using warning lamps -- F01P 2031/22 covers fail-safe systems for machine or engine cooling that employ warning lamps to signal coolant failure, overheating or other abnormal conditions. The entry pertains to visual alert devices integrated into the cooling arrangement to prompt corrective action.
[F01P 2031/24] for freezing -- The CPC subclass F01P 2031/24 covers fail-safe cooling arrangements that protect machines or engines by intentionally freezing a fluid or component. It includes designs where a freeze-induced block or thermally driven shutdown prevents overheating, ensuring safe operation of the equipment.
[F01P 2031/30] Cooling after the engine is stopped -- F01P 2031/30 relates to systems or methods that cool a machine or engine after it has been stopped, providing a fail-safe means to reduce residual heat and prevent overheating or damage during shutdown.
[F01P 2031/32] Deblocking of damaged thermostat -- Deblocking of a damaged thermostat covers methods and devices for clearing or repairing a thermostat that has become blocked or stuck, restoring its ability to regulate temperature in engine-cooling systems. Techniques may include mechanical removal of debris, flushing, or replacement of sealing elements to maintain safe and reliable cooling.

[F01P 2060/00] Cooling circuits using auxiliaries -- This subclass covers cooling systems for machines or engines that incorporate auxiliary components such as secondary pumps, heat exchangers, or control devices to regulate temperature. It includes designs where the auxiliary elements assist the primary cooling-circuit, for example by recirculating coolant or providing additional heat removal.
[F01P 2060/02] Intercooler -- An intercooler is a heat-exchange device installed in the cooling circuit of a turbocharged or supercharged engine to lower the temperature of compressed air before it enters the combustion chamber, thereby increasing efficiency and power output. It functions as an auxiliary element in the broader category of cooling systems for machines or engines.
[F01P 2060/04] Lubricant cooler -- This CPC subclass covers devices that cool lubricating oil in engines or machinery using dedicated heat exchangers. [F01P 2060/045] for transmissions -- F01P 2060/045 covers cooling circuits that use auxiliary devices specifically for transmissions. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and general cooling of machines or engines.
[F01P 2060/06] Retarder -- F01P 2060/06 covers retarders in auxiliary cooling circuits for engines or pumps. A retarder is a device that deliberately reduces flow or pressure to control temperature or limit cooling capacity. This sub-class is part of mechanical engineering, under cooling of machines or engines in general.
[F01P 2060/08] Cabin heater -- F01P 2060/08 classifies cabin-heater systems that use engine-generated heat, such as coolant or exhaust gases, to warm the interior of a vehicle.
