[F01P 2060/10] Fuel manifold -- F01P 2060/10 designates fuel manifolds that are part of auxiliary cooling circuits for engines or pumps. It relates to components that distribute fuel within the cooling system, ensuring proper flow and temperature control in mechanical engineering applications.
[F01P 2060/12] Turbo charger -- A turbo charger is an auxiliary device that employs exhaust-driven turbines to compress intake air, boosting engine power and efficiency while aiding heat removal from the combustion process. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, cooling of machines or engines in general, cooling circuits using auxiliaries.
[F01P 2060/14] Condenser -- The CPC class F01P 2060/14 falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engines or pumps, and covers cooling of machines or engines in general. It relates to cooling circuits that use auxiliary components, and specifically addresses condensers used to reject heat from fluids in such systems.
[F01P 2060/16] Outlet manifold -- An outlet manifold is a component of an engine or pump cooling system that gathers coolant from several passages and channels it out of the circuit, typically toward a radiator or heat exchanger, helping to regulate flow and pressure in auxiliary cooling circuits.
[F01P 2060/18] Heater -- The F01P 2060/18 class covers heaters incorporated in auxiliary cooling circuits for engines or other machines, where added heat helps regulate fluid temperature during operation. It includes devices that supply thermal energy to the coolant to maintain optimal performance of the cooling system.
[F01P 2060/185] for alternators or generators -- The CPC subclass F01P 2060/185 relates to cooling circuits that employ auxiliary devices for the thermal management of alternators or generators. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and general cooling of machines or engines, specifying auxiliary-based cooling systems for these rotating electrical machines.
[F01P 2070/00] Details
[F01P 2070/02] using shape memory alloys -- F01P 2070/02 covers the use of shape-memory alloys to improve the cooling of machines or engines. It includes methods where the alloys change shape in response to temperature, providing adaptive heat-transfer surfaces, variable-geometry coolant passages, or self-activating fans. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engines or pumps, and the broader field of cooling of machines or engines in general.
[F01P 2070/04] using electrical heating elements -- This CPC subclass F01P 2070/04 relates to the use of electrical heating elements within cooling systems for machines or engines. It falls under mechanical engineering → engines or pumps → cooling of machines or engines in general, and covers methods such as electrically warming coolant, preventing freezing or fine-tuning temperature during operation.
[F01P 2070/06] Using intake pressure as actuating fluid -- F01P 2070/06 covers methods where the pressure of the intake air or gas is used as a working fluid to drive actuators that control cooling functions in engines or machines. It includes devices that convert the pressure drop in the intake system into mechanical movement for valves, pumps or fans to regulate temperature.
[F01P 2070/08] Using lubricant pressure as actuating fluid -- F01P 2070/08 covers the use of lubricant-oil pressure as the fluid that drives or controls actuating mechanisms, such as valves or pumps, within cooling systems for engines or machines. It applies to designs where the existing lubrication circuit supplies the power needed for the actuation.
[F01P 2070/10] using electrical or electromechanical means -- This CPC subclass covers methods and devices that cool machines or engines by employing electrical or electromechanical techniques, such as fans driven by electric motors, electromagnetic heat pumps, or electrically powered liquid circulation. It is listed under F01P 2070 for general cooling of machines or engines.
[F01P 2070/30] Rotating radiators -- F01P 2070/30 covers rotating radiators, which are heat-exchange devices mounted on a shaft or rotor to dissipate heat from engines or machinery while in motion. These radiators typically use forced convection from the rotation to improve cooling efficiency and are used in automotive, aerospace, and other industrial power applications.
[F01P 2070/32] Ring-shaped heat exchangers -- F01P 2070/32 covers ring-shaped heat exchangers, devices in which fluid flows through a circular conduit or shell to transfer heat between two media, commonly used to cool or heat engine components. These exchangers belong to the mechanical engineering subclass for engines or pumps and the broader group dealing with cooling of machines or engines in general.
[F01P 2070/50] mounting fans to heat-exchangers -- F01P 2070/50 pertains to the means of attaching fans directly to heat-exchangers, such as radiators or condensers, to provide forced airflow for cooling engines or machines. It includes brackets, clamps, welded or bolted connections that integrate the fan with the exchanger's structure, enabling compact cooling assemblies.
[F01P 2070/52] mounting heat-exchangers -- F01P 2070/52 covers the mounting of heat exchangers to engines or machines, focusing on methods and structures for attaching heat-exchanging devices to facilitate cooling. It belongs to the mechanical engineering section, specifically engines or pumps and the general cooling of machines or engines.
[F01P 3/00] Liquid cooling -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements for liquid cooling of engine or machine parts.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Control details of the circuit -> F01P7/14 - Arrangement of the radiator in the vehicle -> B60K11/04 - Piston constructional details related to cooling of the pistons -> F02F3/16 - Layout of the radiator -> F28D1/00  **Glossary:** - EGR: Exhaust Gas Recirculation
[F01P 3/02] Arrangements for cooling cylinders or cylinder heads -- F01P 3/02 covers arrangements for cooling cylinders or cylinder heads, typically in internal-combustion engines, including designs for liquid-cooling passages, jackets and coolant flow methods that remove heat from the combustion chamber. The subclass falls under the broader group for cooling of machines or engines in general within mechanical engineering.
[F01P 3/04] Liquid-to-air heat-exchangers combined with, or arranged on, cylinders or cylinder heads -- The entry designates liquid-to-air heat exchangers that are built into or mounted on engine cylinders or cylinder heads to remove heat from the coolant. It covers configurations where the exchanger transfers thermal energy from the liquid circuit directly to airflow across the engine's combustion chambers, improving overall cooling performance.
[F01P 3/06] Arrangements for cooling pistons -- The CPC subclass F01P 3/06 covers specific arrangements for cooling pistons in internal-combustion engines or similar machines. It focuses on liquid-cooling configurations that direct coolant to the piston body or crown to manage heat generated during operation.
[F01P 3/08] Cooling of piston exterior only, e.g. by jets -- F01P 3/08 covers the cooling of a piston's outer surface by directing liquid jets onto it, a method used to remove heat from the piston without contacting the combustion chamber. This subgroup falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and the general cooling of machines or engines, specifically addressing liquid-cooling techniques applied to piston exteriors.
[F01P 3/10] Cooling by flow of coolant through pistons -- The F01P 3/10 subclass covers cooling methods that circulate liquid coolant directly through the pistons of an engine. It includes designs where passages or channels are formed in the piston body to allow coolant flow, improving heat removal from the combustion chamber and enhancing engine efficiency and durability. This subclass falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and general machine or engine cooling.
[F01P 3/12] Arrangements for cooling other engine or machine parts -- F01P 3/12 covers arrangements for cooling engine or machine components other than the primary cylinder or combustion area, typically using liquid coolant circulated through jackets, passages or external heat exchangers to remove heat and maintain operating temperatures.
[F01P 3/14] for cooling intake or exhaust valves -- F01P 3/14 concerns methods or devices for cooling intake or exhaust valves in engines or pumps. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, cooling of machines or engines in general, liquid cooling. The classification covers systems that direct coolant to valve surfaces to remove heat and improve performance.
[F01P 3/16] for cooling fuel injectors or sparking-plugs -- This subgroup covers devices that cool fuel injectors or spark plugs, typically by circulating a liquid coolant directly over the component to maintain operating temperature and prevent overheating in internal-combustion engines. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy of engines or pumps, cooling of machines or engines in general, and liquid cooling.
[F01P 3/18] Arrangements or mounting of liquid-to-air heat-exchangers -- The F01P 3/18 subclass covers the ways liquid-to-air heat exchangers are positioned or attached within cooling systems for machines and engines. It includes designs for mounting brackets, housings and integration methods that enable efficient heat transfer from a liquid circuit to ambient air.
[F01P 3/20] Cooling circuits not specific to a single part of engine or machine -- F01P 3/20 covers cooling systems that service an entire engine or machine rather than individual components. It includes designs for liquid circuits that distribute coolant throughout the whole device, such as common radiators, pumps and channels, without being limited to a single part.
[F01P 3/202] for outboard marine engines -- F01P 3/202 covers liquid-cooling arrangements specifically designed for outboard marine engines, detailing how coolant circulates through the engine and heat-exchanger to regulate temperature during operation in a maritime environment. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering → engines or pumps → cooling of machines or engines in general → liquid cooling.
[F01P 3/205] Flushing -- F01P 3/205 covers flushing techniques used in liquid-cooled engines or machines, describing methods for cleaning or removing contaminants by circulating fluid through the cooling circuit. It pertains to maintenance procedures that ensure efficient heat removal and prevent fouling in cooling systems.
[F01P 3/207] liquid-to-liquid heat-exchanging relative to marine vessels -- F01P 3/207 refers to liquid-to-liquid heat exchangers employed on marine vessels, where heat is transferred between two circulating liquids for cooling or heating functions aboard ships. The class is situated within mechanical engineering, under engines or pumps and the general cooling of machines or engines.
[F01P 3/22] characterised by evaporation and condensation of coolant in closed cycles -- The CPC subclass F01P 3/22 covers cooling systems for engines or machines that use a closed-cycle coolant which evaporates to absorb heat and then condenses to release it, employing phase-change heat transfer to maintain operating temperatures.
[F01P 3/2207] characterised by the coolant reaching temperatures higher than the normal atmospheric boiling point -- The CPC subclass F01P 3/2207 covers cooling systems where the liquid coolant is intentionally allowed to exceed its normal atmospheric boiling point, typically using pressurization or boiling heat transfer to improve efficiency. It applies to engines or pumps that employ high-temperature liquid cooling to enhance thermal management.
[F01P 3/2271] Closed cycles with separator and liquid return -- The code F01P 3/2271 covers closed-cycle cooling systems for engines or machines that incorporate a separator to remove vapor from the coolant and a mechanism that returns the liquid to the cooling circuit, ensuring continuous circulation without external water supply. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and general cooling of machines or engines.
[F01P 3/2285] Closed cycles with condenser and feed pump -- F01P 3/2285 covers closed-cycle liquid-cooling systems that employ a condenser to reject heat and a feed pump to recirculate the coolant through the engine or machine, forming a sealed loop without external water intake. This type of system is used where continuous, efficient temperature control is required and the coolant is continuously condensed and pumped back to the heat source.
[F01P 5/00] Pumping cooling-air or liquid coolants -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements related to water pumps and fans including their driving, as long a cooling circuit of an engine is claimed as well.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Controlling circulation or supply of coolants by influencing drive of pumps -> F01P7/00 - Driving the pumps using a clutch -> F01P7/00 - Centrifugal pumps -> F04D17/08 - Axial flow fans -> F04D19/002 - Rotors for centrifugal pumps -> F04D29/22 - Rotors for elastic fluids -> F04D29/26
[F01P 5/02] Pumping cooling-air -- F01P 5/02 covers devices or systems that move cooling-air to or within engines, pumps, or other machines to remove heat. It includes methods and apparatus for circulating ambient or forced air to maintain temperature control during operation.
[F01P 5/04] Pump-driving arrangements -- The CPC subclass F01P 5/04 covers arrangements for driving pumps that circulate cooling air or liquid in engines or other machines. It includes mechanical configurations, gearings, couplings or motor-driven systems designed to ensure effective heat removal as part of a machine's overall cooling strategy.
[F01P 5/043] Pump reversing arrangements -- F01P 5/043 covers arrangements that enable a cooling pump to reverse the direction of flow, allowing the same pump to discharge coolant into a system and then draw it back for recirculation or cleaning. Such reversing mechanisms are used in engines and other machinery where intermittent or bidirectional cooling is required.
[F01P 5/06] Guiding or ducting air to, or from, ducted fans -- F01P 5/06 covers the design and arrangement of ducts, passages or guides that direct airflow to or away from ducted fans in machines or engines. It includes methods for distributing cooling air or liquid coolant through such ducts to improve the thermal management of the system.
[F01P 5/08] Use of engine exhaust gases for pumping cooling-air -- This subclass relates to methods that employ the pressure or kinetic energy of engine exhaust gases to drive a pump or blower that circulates cooling air for engines or other machines. Such arrangements recover waste exhaust energy to provide airflow for heat removal, eliminating or reducing the need for an independent power source.
[F01P 5/10] Pumping liquid coolant -- F01P 5/10 covers devices or arrangements that move liquid coolant through a machine or engine to remove heat, including pumps and associated circulation systems used for cooling in engines, turbines, compressors or other mechanical equipment. It falls under cooling of machines or engines in the mechanical engineering field.
[F01P 5/12] Pump-driving arrangements -- F01P 5/12 covers pump-driving arrangements that provide the mechanical power to circulate air or liquid coolants in engines or other machines. It includes couplings, gear trains, hydraulic or electric drives and any intermediate mechanisms that transmit motion to the cooling pump. This subclass falls under cooling of machines or engines in general within the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engines or pumps.
[F01P 5/14] Safety means against, or active at, failure of coolant-pumps drives, e.g. shutting engine down -- This CPC subclass covers safety means that engage when a coolant-pump drive fails, for example devices that shut the engine down. It is situated under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, cooling of machines or engines in general, and pumping cooling-air or liquid coolants.
[F01P 7/00] Controlling of coolant flow -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling of the system such that the coolant and/or an engine part has a given temperature and/or in accordance with certain engine parameters, e.g. speed, load.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Failure of thermostats -> F01P2031/32 - Arrangement and control of the radiator shutters related to the vehicle -> B60K11/04 - Heating of an ICE before starting by heating of the coolant -> F02N19/10 - The layout of a thermostat, when not related to an ice -> G05D23/00 - Thermostat as such -> G05D23/00
[F01P 7/02] the coolant being cooling-air -- In CPC subclass F01P 7/02, which lies under mechanical engineering → engines or pumps → cooling of machines or engines in general, the focus is on controlling the flow of coolant when the coolant itself is cooling air, regulating how air is directed and circulated to remove heat from engines or other machinery.
[F01P 7/023] Cowlings for airplane engines -- F01P 7/023 relates to cowlings designed for aircraft engines, covering structures that direct and regulate airflow around the engine to achieve effective cooling. The code includes various shapes, adjustable or fixed, that control the coolant flow as part of the engine's thermal management system.
[F01P 7/026] Thermostatic control -- The F01P 7/026 subclass covers devices that regulate the flow of coolant in engines or pumps by using thermostatic mechanisms. It includes systems that automatically adjust coolant circulation to maintain a set temperature, ensuring efficient cooling and preventing overheating of machinery.
[F01P 7/04] by varying pump speed, e.g. by changing pump-drive gear ratio -- F01P 7/04 covers methods of controlling coolant flow in machines or engines by adjusting the pump speed, such as altering the gear ratio of the pump drive. This approach regulates the amount of coolant delivered, improving temperature management while varying the mechanical transmission to the pump.
[F01P 7/042] using fluid couplings  couplings or clutches of this type  per se   F16D35/00 -- This subclass (F01P 7/042) pertains to the control of coolant flow in machines or engines by employing fluid couplings, couplings or clutches of the type defined in F16D 35/00. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engines or pumps and the general cooling of machines, and addresses methods that regulate coolant circulation using fluid-transmission devices.
[F01P 7/044] using hydraulic drives -- The subclass F01P 7/044 covers systems that regulate the flow of coolant in engines or pumps by means of hydraulic drives, using pressurised fluid power to adjust flow rates and distribution.
[F01P 7/046] using mechanical drives -- This subclass relates to systems that employ mechanical drives-gears, cams, linkages or other powered mechanisms-to regulate the flow of coolant in machines or engines. It specifies how such mechanical actuation is used for controlling coolant distribution within the broader category of cooling of machines or engines.
[F01P 7/048] using electrical drives -- F01P 7/048 covers methods and devices that regulate the flow of coolant in engines or pumps by employing electric drive mechanisms such as motors, actuators or variable-speed drives. The classification is used for systems that adjust pump speed or valve position electrically to maintain a desired temperature or flow rate.
[F01P 7/06] by varying blade pitch -- F01P 7/06 covers methods for controlling coolant flow in engines or pumps by varying the pitch of blades, allowing the system to adjust coolant distribution and heat removal through blade-angle changes.
[F01P 7/08] by cutting in or out of pumps -- F01P 7/08 covers methods for regulating coolant circulation by switching pumps on or off, i.e., cutting in or out of pumps, to control the flow of cooling fluid in engines or mechanical systems. This falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and general cooling of machines.
[F01P 7/081] using clutches, e.g. electro-magnetic or induction clutches -- F01P 7/081 covers devices that regulate coolant flow in engines or pumps by employing clutches, such as electromagnetic or induction clutches, to modulate the circulation of the cooling fluid. This sub-class falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and cooling of machines or engines.
[F01P 7/082] using friction clutches -- F01P 7/082 relates to the control of coolant flow in engines or pumps by employing friction clutches, which adjust the circulation of cooling fluid as part of a machine's overall cooling system. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engines or pumps and the general cooling of machines.
[F01P 7/084] actuated electromagnetically -- F01P 7/084 covers systems that control the flow of coolant in machines or engines by using electromagnetic actuation. The technology typically involves solenoid valves or electromagnetic pumps that adjust coolant supply in response to temperature or pressure signals, improving thermal management efficiency.
[F01P 7/085] actuated by fluid pressure -- The code F01P 7/085 covers mechanisms that regulate the flow of cooling fluid in engines or machines by using fluid-pressure actuation. It includes hydraulic or pneumatic actuators and valves that adjust coolant delivery in response to pressure changes, enabling automatic temperature control without electrical control.
[F01P 7/087] actuated directly by deformation of a thermostatic device -- The F01P 7/087 subclass covers coolant flow control systems that are powered directly by the mechanical deformation of a thermostatic device. In such arrangements the thermostat expands or contracts with temperature changes, moving a valve or pump to regulate coolant circulation in engines or other machines.
[F01P 7/088] actuated in response to driving speed, e.g. by centrifugal devices -- The subclass covers devices that adjust coolant flow in proportion to the vehicle's speed, commonly using centrifugal mechanisms to vary pump or valve operation for engine-cooling systems. It applies to control methods that automatically respond to driving speed to regulate the amount of cooling fluid.
[F01P 7/10] by throttling amount of air flowing through liquid-to-air heat exchangers -- F01P 7/10 concerns methods for regulating coolant circulation in machines or engines by varying the amount of air that passes through a liquid-to-air heat exchanger. The control is achieved through throttling the airflow, thereby adjusting heat removal and coolant flow rate.
[F01P 7/12] by thermostatic control -- F01P 7/12 covers thermostatic control of coolant flow in machines or engines. It includes systems that regulate cooling based on temperature, using thermostats or similar devices to keep the engine at an optimal operating temperature. This falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, cooling of machines or engines in general.
[F01P 7/14] the coolant being liquid -- F01P 7/14 pertains to cooling systems for machines or engines where the coolant is a liquid, covering methods and devices that control the flow of liquid coolant to manage temperature. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically within engines or pumps and the general cooling of machines or engines.
[F01P 7/16] by thermostatic control -- This CPC subclass covers cooling of machines or engines that is achieved by thermostatic control of coolant flow, describing methods and devices where temperature-sensing elements adjust valves or pumps to keep the system within desired temperature limits.
[F01P 7/161] by bypassing pumps -- This subclass addresses ways to regulate coolant flow in machines or engines by diverting the fluid around the pump, allowing temperature control without depending on the pump's operation. It is situated under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, cooling of machines or engines in general.
[F01P 7/162] by cutting in and out of pumps -- F01P 7/162 covers methods of regulating coolant circulation in machine or engine cooling systems by intermittently starting or stopping pumps, known as cutting-in and cutting-out operation. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and general cooling of machines or engines.
[F01P 7/164] by varying pump speed -- This subclass concerns controlling the flow of coolant to machines or engines by varying the speed of the pump that supplies the coolant. It is placed under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, cooling of machines or engines in general, and specifically addresses controlling of coolant flow (CPC code F01P 7/164).
[F01P 7/165] characterised by systems with two or more loops -- The CPC entry F01P 7/165 pertains to cooling systems for machines or engines that employ two or more separate coolant loops, allowing distinct temperature zones or stages of heat removal and improving overall thermal control and efficiency.
[F01P 7/167] by adjusting the pre-set temperature according to engine parameters, e.g. engine load, engine speed -- F01P 7/167 covers control methods for engine cooling that modify the preset coolant temperature based on real-time engine parameters such as load or speed. The technique adjusts the thermostat or temperature setpoint to optimise cooling performance as operating conditions change.
[F01P 9/00] Cooling having pertinent characteristics not provided for in, or of interest apart from, groups -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Profiting from the waste heat of the exhaust gases -> F02G5/02 , F01N5/02 - Cogeneration -> F25B27/02
[F01P 9/02] Cooling by evaporation, e.g. by spraying water on to cylinders -- F01P 9/02 covers methods of cooling machines or engines by evaporation, such as spraying water onto cylinder surfaces to remove heat. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and general cooling of machines, and addresses techniques not covered by other groups.
[F01P 9/04] by simultaneous or alternative use of direct air-cooling and liquid cooling -- F01P 9/04 covers cooling systems for engines or machines that employ both direct air-cooling and liquid cooling, either at the same time or in alternating phases. It is used when the combination of these two methods provides a cooling characteristic that is not covered by other, more specific subclasses.
[F01P 9/06] by use of refrigerating apparatus, e.g. of compressor or absorber type -- F01P 9/06 relates to cooling machines or engines by means of a refrigerating system, such as compressor- or absorber-type equipment, used when other cooling categories are not applicable. It falls under mechanical engineering, engines or pumps, and general cooling of machines or engines, covering methods that rely on refrigeration cycles for heat removal.
[F02] COMBUSTION ENGINES -- F02 covers combustion engines, devices that convert the chemical energy of fuel into mechanical work by controlled combustion. It includes internal-combustion engines such as spark-ignition and compression-ignition types, as well as auxiliary systems and methods for their operation, control and fueling.
[F02B] INTERNAL-COMBUSTION PISTON ENGINES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines characterised by the working fluid to be compressed or characterised by the type of ignition  Engines characterised by the method of introducing liquid fuel into cylinders  Engines characterised by precombustion chambers or air-storage chambers, or characterised by special shape or construction of combustion chambers to improve operation  Engines characterised by provision for charging or scavenging  Engines characterised by provision of driven charging or scavenging pumps  Engines operating on non-liquid fuels; Plants including such engines, i.e. combinations of the engine with fuel-generating apparatus  Methods of operating engines involving specific pre-treating of, or adding specific substances to, combustion air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture of the engines, and not otherwise provided for  Internal-combustion aspects of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines  Internal combustion aspects of reciprocating-piston engines with movable cylinders  Adaptations of engines for special use; Combinations of engines with devices other than engine parts or auxiliaries  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Gas-turbine plants -> F02C - Hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants -> F02G  **Glossary:** - engine: a device for continuously converting fluid energy into mechanical power. Thus, this term includes, for example, steam piston engines or steam turbines, per se, or internal-combustion piston engines, but it excludes single-stroke devices. "Engine" also includes the fluid-motive portion of a meter unless such portion is particularly adapted for use in a motor; - pump: a device for continuously raising, forcing, compressing, or exhausting fluid by mechanical or other means. Thus, this term includes fans or blowers; - machine: a device which could equally be an engine and a pump, and not a device which is restricted to an engine or one which is restricted to a pump; - positive displacement: the way the energy of a working fluid is transformed into mechanical energy, in which variations of volume created by the working fluid in a working chamber produce equivalent displacements of the mechanical member transmitting the energy, the dynamic effect of the fluid being of minor importance, and vice versa ; - non-positive displacement: the way the energy of a working fluid is transformed into mechanical energy, by transformation of the energy of the working fluid into kinetic energy, and vice versa ; - oscillating-piston machine: a positive-displacement machine in which a fluid-engaging work-transmitting member oscillates. This definition applies also to engines and pumps; - rotary-piston machine: a positive-displacement machine in which a fluid-engaging work-transmitting member rotates about a fixed axis or about an axis moving along a circular or similar orbit. This definition applies also to engines and pumps; - rotary piston: the work-transmitting member of a rotary-piston machine and may be of any suitable form, e.g., like a toothed gear; - cooperating members: the "oscillating piston" or "rotary piston" and another member, e.g., the working-chamber wall, which assists in the driving or pumping action; - movement of the co-operating members: is to be interpreted as relative, so that one of the "co-operating members" may be stationary, even though reference may be made to its rotational axis, or both may move; - teeth or tooth equivalents: include lobes, projections or abutments; - internal-axis type: that the rotational axes of the inner and outer co-operating members remain at all times within the outer member, e.g., in a similar manner to that of a pinion meshing with the internal teeth of a ring gear; - free piston: a piston of which the length of stroke is not defined by any member driven thereby; - cylinders: positive-displacement working chambers in general. Thus, this term is not restricted to cylinders of circular cross-section; - main shaft: the shaft which converts reciprocating piston motion into rotary motion or vice versa ; - plant: an engine together with such additional apparatus as is necessary to run the engine. For example, a steam engine plant includes a steam engine and means for generating the steam; - working fluid: the driven fluid in a pump or the driving fluid in an engine. The working fluid can be in a compressible, gaseous state, called elastic fluid, e.g. steam; in a liquid state; or in a state where there is coexistence of an elastic fluid and liquid phase. - steam: includes condensable vapours in general, and "special vapour" is used when steam is excluded; - reaction type: as applied to non-positive-displacement machines or engines means machines or engines in which pressure/velocity transformation takes place wholly or partly in the rotor. Machines or engines with no, or only slight, pressure/velocity transformation in the rotor are called "impulse type".
[F02B 1/00] Engines characterised by the working fluid to be compressed or characterised by the type of ignition -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engine working on the compression of a homogeneous mixture of air and fuel.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Characterised by both fuel-air mixture compression and air compression, or characterised by both positive ignition and compression ignition -> F02B11/00
[F02B 1/02] with positive ignition -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines with positive ignition, e.g. gasoline or petrol engines.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Engines with non-timed positive ignition -> F02B9/06
[F02B 1/04] with fuel-air mixture admission into cylinder -- F02B 1/04 covers internal-combustion piston engines in which the fuel and air are mixed together before being introduced into the cylinder, describing pre-mixed induction systems, inlet valve arrangements and related charging methods.
[F02B 1/06] Methods of operating -- The subclass F02B 1/06 covers methods of operating internal-combustion piston engines, including procedures for starting, stopping, speed and load control, fuel-injection timing, ignition timing and other techniques used to manage the engine's performance during operation.
[F02B 1/08] with separate admission of air and fuel into cylinder -- F02B 1/08 covers internal-combustion piston engines in which air and fuel are admitted to the cylinder through distinct passages or ports rather than a premixed charge. This arrangement is typical of spark-ignition engines and some diesel designs that use separate inlet valves or injectors for the two components.
[F02B 1/10] Methods of operating -- F02B 1/10 concerns the techniques and procedures for running internal-combustion piston engines, encompassing control of fuel delivery, ignition timing, valve actuation and other operational parameters that influence performance, efficiency, emissions and reliability.
[F02B 11/00] Engines characterised by both fuel-air mixture compression and air compression, or characterised by both positive ignition and compression ignition, e.g. in different cylinders -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines characterised by both fuel-air mixture compression and air compression, or characterised by both positive ignition and compression ignition, e.g. in different cylinders.  This place also covers engines characterised by spark-assisted compression ignition, in which a spark ignition triggers an instantaneous compression ignition.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[F02B 11/02] convertible from fuel-air mixture compression to air compression or -- F02B 11/02 covers internal-combustion piston engines that can switch between fuel-air mixture compression (spark-ignition) and pure air compression (compression-ignition), often by using separate cylinders for each mode. It applies to designs where the engine operates with both positive ignition and compression ignition within the same machine.
[F02B 1/12] with compression ignition -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines operating with compression ignition of "homogeneous" mixed air-fuel.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - With fuel-air charge ignited by compression ignition of an additional fuel -> F02B7/00
[F02B 1/14] Methods of operating -- F02B 1/14 covers the various ways in which internal-combustion piston engines are run, including control of fuel delivery, ignition timing, valve actuation and other operational strategies that affect performance, efficiency or emissions. It addresses methods for starting, idling, accelerating, throttling and shutting down the engine under different conditions.
[F02B 13/00] Engines characterised by the method of introducing liquid fuel into cylinders -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines characterised by the introduction of liquid fuel into cylinders by use of an auxiliary fluid, e.g. using compressed air or gas for blowing fuel into combustion chamber.
[F02B 13/02] Compression ignition engines using air or gas for blowing fuel into compressed air in cylinder -- F02B 13/02 covers compression ignition engines in which a stream of air or gas is used to atomise and inject liquid fuel into the compressed air charge within the cylinder, typical of indirect-injection diesel designs.
[F02B 13/04] Arrangements or adaptations of pumps -- F02B 13/04 covers the design and modification of fuel-delivery pumps for internal-combustion piston engines, focusing on how liquid fuel is introduced into the cylinders. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the subclass for internal-combustion piston engines. This code is used to classify inventions that improve pump arrangement, control, or integration within such engines.
[F02B 13/06] Engines having secondary air mixed with fuel in pump, compressed therein without ignition, and fuel-air mixture being injected into air in cylinder -- F02B 13/06 classifies internal-combustion piston engines in which a secondary air stream is mixed with liquid fuel in a pump, the mixture is compressed without ignition, and the resulting fuel-air charge is injected directly into the cylinder.
[F02B 13/08] Arrangements or adaptations of pumps -- F02B 13/08 pertains to the design and modification of pumps that deliver liquid fuel into the cylinders of internal-combustion piston engines, encompassing various fuel-injection arrangements and related pump configurations.
[F02B 13/10] Use of specific auxiliary fluids, e.g. steam, combustion gas -- F02B 13/10 covers internal-combustion piston engines that employ auxiliary fluids, such as steam or combustion gases, to aid the combustion process or improve performance. The classification addresses the use of these specific secondary fluids in conjunction with the primary liquid fuel, detailing their role in enhancing engine efficiency or emissions control.
[F02B 15/00] Engines characterised by the method of introducing liquid fuel into cylinders and not otherwise provided for -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This group covers exceptional cases of engines with fuel introduction into cylinder that cannot be classified elsewhere.
[F02B 15/02] having means for sucking fuel directly into cylinder -- This subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines where liquid fuel is drawn directly into the cylinder by a suction mechanism rather than by external injection. It includes designs that use the cylinder's pressure differential to pull fuel in during the intake stroke.
[F02B 17/00] Engines characterised by means for effecting stratification of charge in cylinders -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines characterised by means for effecting stratification of charge in cylinders.  In a stratified charge engine, the fuel air mixture is stratified, i.e. heterogeneous, e.g. with a richer mixture near a spark plug than elsewhere.  **Glossary:** - Stratification: Layers of air and fuel - Charge: Air and/or fuel contents in cylinder
[F02B 17/005] having direct injection in the combustion chamber -- F02B 17/005 is a subclass for internal-combustion piston engines that use a stratified charge method. It specifically includes engines in which fuel is injected directly into the combustion chamber rather than into the intake air stream.
[F02B 19/00] Engines characterised by precombustion chambers or air-storage chambers, or characterised by special shape or construction of combustion chambers to improve operation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines characterized by pre-combustion chambers.  Chambers are located at the cylinder head and connected to the engine cylinder by small holes. Generally the following steps in the combustion take place. During the compression stroke, air from the main cylinder enters the pre-combustion chamber. At this moment, fuel is injected into the pre-combustion chamber and is ignited by a spark plug or a glow plug. Pressure increases and the ignited charge is forced through the small holes into the main cylinder, resulting in an ignition of the main charge in the combustion chamber.  **Glossary:** - Incandescent chamber: Chamber with hot spot - Torch passage: Passage between pre- and main combustion chamber - Squish area: Compressed area between piston top and cylinder head - Air swirl: Rotational movement of air
[F02B 19/02] the chamber being periodically isolated from its cylinder
[F02B 19/04] the isolation being effected by a protuberance on piston or cylinder head -- F02B 19/04 is a CPC subclass for internal-combustion piston engines that achieve isolation-such as thermal or gas-flow separation-by means of a protuberance on the piston or on the cylinder head, improving combustion chamber performance.
[F02B 19/06] with auxiliary piston in chamber for transferring ignited charge to cylinder space -- The code F02B 19/06 covers internal-combustion piston engines that employ a pre-combustion or air-storage chamber equipped with an auxiliary piston, which moves the ignited charge from that chamber into the main cylinder space to improve combustion efficiency. This arrangement is used to control ignition timing and enhance power output by delivering the flame front more precisely.
[F02B 19/08] the chamber being of air-swirl type -- F02B 19/08 relates to internal-combustion piston engines that use a pre-combustion or air-storage chamber of the air-swirl type, wherein the chamber shape induces a rotational airflow to enhance mixing and combustion efficiency. This design is intended to improve engine operation by promoting more uniform flame propagation.
[F02B 19/10] with fuel introduced partly into pre-combustion chamber, and partly into cylinder -- This subclass deals with internal-combustion piston engines that employ a pre-combustion chamber in which fuel is injected partly into the chamber and partly straight into the main cylinder, a configuration aimed at improving combustion efficiency and reducing emissions.
[F02B 19/1004] details of combustion chamber, e.g. mounting arrangements -- F02B 19/1004 covers the design and mounting of combustion chambers in internal-combustion piston engines, including details such as the shape, positioning, and attachment of pre-combustion or air-storage chambers intended to enhance engine performance. It classifies inventions that specify how the combustion chamber is secured within the cylinder head or related components.
[F02B 19/1009] heating, cooling -- F02B 19/1009 covers heating and cooling measures applied to internal-combustion piston engines that use pre-combustion or air-storage chambers, or that feature specially shaped combustion chambers to enhance performance. The subclass deals with thermal management techniques that affect engine efficiency and emissions.
[F02B 19/1014] design parameters, e.g. volume, torch passage cross sectional area, length, orientation, or the like -- This subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines that use pre-combustion or air-storage chambers and specifies design parameters such as chamber volume, torch-passage cross-sectional area, length, orientation and similar geometric features that influence performance.
[F02B 19/1019] with only one pre-combustion chamber  F02B19/1004  take precedence -- F02B 19/1019 covers piston engines that incorporate a single pre-combustion chamber to improve combustion, where the chamber initiates fuel ignition before the main combustion space. The design may involve special shaping of the chamber to enhance engine performance.
[F02B 19/1023] pre-combustion chamber and cylinder being fed with fuel-air mixture(s) -- The subclass F02B 19/1023 covers internal-combustion piston engines that incorporate a pre-combustion chamber in which the fuel-air mixture is first introduced before being fed into the main cylinder, a design intended to enhance ignition and combustion efficiency.
[F02B 19/1028] pre-combustion chamber and cylinder having both intake ports or valves, e.g. HONDS CVCC -- F02B 19/1028 covers internal combustion piston engines that incorporate a pre-combustion chamber together with a cylinder having both intake ports or valves, as exemplified by systems such as the Honda CVCC.
[F02B 19/1033] specially adapted valves, e.g. rotary valves, pre-combustion chamber being part of a valve -- The code F02B 19/1033 covers internal-combustion piston engines that incorporate specially adapted valves, such as rotary valves, where the valve itself contains a pre-combustion chamber. These designs aim to improve combustion efficiency by integrating the chamber into the valve mechanism.
[F02B 19/1038] timing of valves -- F02B 19/1038 deals with the timing of valves in internal-combustion piston engines, especially those equipped with pre-combustion or air-storage chambers or distinctive combustion-chamber shapes. It includes the control of opening and closing moments for intake and exhaust valves to optimise engine performance.
[F02B 19/1042] auxiliary intake, valve drive -- F02B 19/1042 covers auxiliary intake and valve-drive arrangements for internal-combustion piston engines, including additional inlet passages, pre-chambers, or mechanisms that assist or replace the main valve actuation to improve charging and combustion.
[F02B 19/1047] means for varying the size of the torch passage -- This CPC subclass covers devices that adjust the cross-section of the torch or flame passage in internal-combustion piston engines featuring pre-combustion or air-storage chambers, or specially shaped combustion chambers, to control the timing and intensity of ignition and improve engine performance.
[F02B 19/1052] controlling, e.g. varying fuel-air ratio, quantity of charge -- This subclass covers control measures for internal-combustion piston engines that use pre-combustion or air-storage chambers, focusing on the regulation of the fuel-air ratio or the quantity of the charge to optimise combustion and engine performance.
[F02B 19/1057] with fuel injectors disposed upstream of intake valves -- This subclass concerns internal-combustion piston engines equipped with pre-combustion or air-storage chambers in which the fuel injectors are placed upstream of the intake valves, allowing fuel to be introduced into the incoming air charge before it enters the cylinder and thereby improving mixture formation and combustion efficiency.
[F02B 19/1061] with residual gas chamber, e.g. containing spark plug -- This subclass, F02B 19/1061, concerns internal-combustion piston engines that include a residual-gas chamber, for example a cavity that retains exhaust gases and may house a spark plug to promote more stable or efficient combustion. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and internal-combustion piston engines.
[F02B 19/1066] pre-combustion chamber having an inlet and an outlet port and with two distinct intake conduits or with one intake conduit in which the heavier fuel particles are separated from the main stream, e.g. by gravitational forces -- This subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines that incorporate a pre-combustion chamber equipped with separate inlet and outlet ports and either two intake conduits or a single conduit that separates heavier fuel particles from the main airflow, often by gravity, to improve combustion efficiency.
[F02B 19/1071] pre-combustion chamber having only one orifice,(i.e. an orifice by means of which it communicates with the cylinder); the intake system comprising two distinct intake conduits -- This subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines that use a pre-combustion chamber connected to the cylinder by a single orifice and employ an intake system formed of two separate conduits.
[F02B 19/1076] pre-combustion chamber being formed within the piston, e.g. two-cycle engines -- This CPC subclass (F02B 19/1076) covers internal-combustion piston engines that form the pre-combustion chamber inside the piston itself, a feature common in two-cycle designs. By locating the chamber within the piston, the engine improves mixture preparation and combustion efficiency through a special chamber shape or construction.
[F02B 19/108] with fuel injection at least into pre-combustion chamber, i.e. injector mounted directly in the pre-combustion chamber -- This CPC subclass covers piston engines that use a precombustion or air-storage chamber and feature an injector mounted directly inside that chamber to deliver fuel. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, internal-combustion piston engines.
[F02B 19/1085] controlling fuel injection -- F02B 19/1085 covers methods and devices for controlling fuel injection in internal-combustion piston engines, especially those with pre-combustion or air-storage chambers designed to improve performance. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, internal-combustion piston engines.
[F02B 19/109] with injection of a fuel-air mixture into the pre-combustion chamber by means of a pump, e.g. two-cycle engines
[F02B 19/1095] with more than one pre-combustion chamber (a stepped form of the main combustion chamber above the piston is to be considered as a pre-combustion chamber if this stepped portion is not a squish area) -- This subclass of the CPC covers internal-combustion piston engines that employ more than one pre-combustion chamber, counting a stepped portion of the main chamber above the piston as a pre-combustion chamber when it is not merely a squish area. It is found under Mechanical Engineering, Combustion Engines, Internal-Combustion Piston Engines.
[F02B 19/12] with positive ignition -- F02B 19/12 covers internal-combustion piston engines that use a positive ignition system, meaning the spark is generated externally and introduced into the combustion chamber, often employed in designs with pre-combustion or air-storage chambers to improve performance.
[F02B 19/14] with compression ignition -- F02B 19/14 covers internal-combustion piston engines that use compression ignition and incorporate pre-combustion or air-storage chambers, or feature specially shaped combustion chambers to enhance performance.
[F02B 19/16] Chamber shapes or constructions not specific to sub-groups -- F02B 19/16 covers internal-combustion piston engines that incorporate unconventional chamber geometries or constructions, such as pre-combustion or air-storage chambers, intended to enhance performance. The subclass includes any chamber shape or design that does not fall within more specific sub-groups.
[F02B 19/165] The shape or construction of the pre-combustion chambers is specially adapted to be formed, at least in part, of ceramic material  surface coverings of combustion-gas-swept parts  F02B77/02 ; shaped ceramic products characterised by their composition or ceramic compositions  C04B35/00 ; ceramic material for engine casings  F02F7/0087
[F02B 19/18] Transfer passages between chamber and cylinder -- F02B 19/18 covers designs for passages that connect a precombustion or air-storage chamber with the main cylinder in internal-combustion piston engines. The classification addresses the geometry and arrangement of these transfer channels to improve combustion efficiency and engine performance.
[F02B 2019/002] with electric heater fitted to at least part of prechamber-wall or transfer passage -- The CPC subclass F02B 2019/002 covers internal combustion engines that incorporate an electric heater on at least part of the pre-chamber wall or the transfer passage, providing supplementary heating to aid ignition or improve combustion stability. This arrangement is intended to enhance start-up performance, reduce emissions, or enable operation under cold-start conditions.
[F02B 2019/004] with heater control -- F02B 2019/004 covers steam-generator or water-heating apparatus that incorporate a control system for regulating the heater, such as thermostats, temperature sensors or electronic control units that adjust heating output to maintain a set temperature. The classification is used for inventions where the heating device includes an integrated means of monitoring and modulating heat production.
[F02B 2019/006] with thermal insulation -- The subclass covers boilers, steam generators, superheaters or combustion chambers that incorporate thermal insulation on the vessel or its components to reduce heat loss, improve efficiency, and protect surrounding structures. It includes design features, materials or arrangements that provide such insulation.
[F02B 2019/008] variable -- The CPC entry F02B 2019/008 classifies steam turbines that incorporate variable geometry or adjustable flow-control elements, allowing blade angles, nozzle settings or other components to be altered while the machine is running to optimise performance across different loads and speeds. This grouping covers designs where the turbine's aerodynamic characteristics can be varied in operation.
[F02B 2023/0606] the material being a catalyst -- The CPC code F02B 2023/0606 covers steam-turbine technology where the material used functions as a catalyst, such as catalytic coatings, supports or components that facilitate chemical reactions during steam generation or utilization. It includes turbine parts or assemblies specifically designed to incorporate or retain catalytic activity.
[F02B 2023/0609] the material being a porous medium, e.g. sintered metal -- F02B 2023/0609 classifies inventions that employ a porous medium-commonly sintered metal-as the functional material, typically for fluid flow, filtration, heat exchange or catalytic purposes, where the porosity of the structure is a defining technical feature.
[F02B 2023/0612] the material having a high temperature and pressure resistance, e.g. ceramic -- F02B 2023/0612 classifies inventions that employ materials with exceptional resistance to high temperature and pressure, such as advanced ceramics, for use in equipment like boilers, reactors, or other high-stress industrial components.
[F02B 2023/0615] the combustion space having a volume defined by revolution around an axis inclined relative to the cylinder axis -- The entry covers internal-combustion engine designs in which the combustion chamber is shaped by revolving a profile around an axis that is inclined to the cylinder's central axis, thereby defining the combustion space volume. This geometry influences the shape of the chamber, fuel-air mixing and flame propagation, and is relevant to patents on engine cylinder and piston configurations.
[F02B 2023/085] using several spark plugs per cylinder -- The CPC subclass F02B 2023/085 covers internal-combustion engines that employ more than one spark plug in each cylinder, a configuration that promotes faster and more uniform ignition, reduces knock, and can improve fuel efficiency or emissions control. It includes designs where dual or multiple plugs are positioned to create separate ignition points, enable sequential firing, or support stratified-charge operation.
[F02B 2023/102] the spark plug being placed offset the cylinder centre axis -- CPC class F02B deals with internal-combustion engines, focusing on the design and arrangement of spark plugs. The sub-group 2023/102 specifically covers configurations in which the spark plug is placed offset from the cylinder centre axis, a design used to improve combustion efficiency, reduce emissions or accommodate specific engine geometries.
[F02B 2023/103] the injector having a multi-hole nozzle for generating multiple sprays -- F02B 2023/103 concerns injectors that incorporate a multi-hole nozzle to create several simultaneous spray streams. The design enhances atomization and distribution of fluid, useful in fuel-injection, spray cooling or coating applications. It relates to pump or fluid-delivery technology where multiple jets improve performance.
[F02B 2023/106] Tumble flow, i.e. the axis of rotation of the main charge flow motion is horizontal -- The classification pertains to reciprocating internal-combustion engine designs that create a tumble flow, meaning the main charge rotates about a horizontal axis within the cylinder. It covers features, devices, or methods that generate, control, or exploit this horizontal rotational motion to improve mixture preparation and combustion efficiency.
[F02B 2023/107] Reverse tumble flow, e.g. having substantially vertical intake ports -- The entry covers internal-combustion engines in which the incoming air-fuel mixture is introduced through substantially vertical intake ports so that the flow initially moves upward and then reverses to create a tumble motion inside the cylinder. This reverse tumble flow enhances mixture homogenisation and combustion efficiency, and is applied to improve performance and emissions in spark-ignition or compression-ignition engines.
[F02B 2023/108] Swirl flow, i.e. the axis of rotation of the main charge flow motion is vertical -- The CPC subclass F02B 2023/108 pertains to fluid-handling devices or processes where the main charge flows in a swirl whose axis of rotation is vertical, such as vertically rotating mixers, turbines or combustion chambers. It addresses the design, operation and effects of vertical swirl flow for improved mixing, combustion stability or aerodynamic performance.
[F02B 2037/122] Control of rotational speed of the pump -- This subclass concerns mechanisms and methods for adjusting the speed at which a pump's rotor turns, such as variable-frequency drives, servomechanisms, or control loops that regulate flow or pressure. It covers sensors, controllers and actuators that modulate motor speed to achieve desired pump performance.
[F02B 2037/125] Control for avoiding pump stall or surge -- The CPC subclass F02B 2037/125 covers control methods and devices designed to keep a pump from stalling or experiencing surge. It includes monitoring of flow, pressure or power and the use of automatic adjustments such as variable-speed drives or throttling valves to maintain stable operation. Solutions may involve feedback loops, sensors and software that intervene before the pump reaches stall or surge conditions.
[F02B 2039/162] Control of pump parameters to improve safety thereof -- F02B 2039/162 covers systems that monitor and adjust pump operating variables-such as flow, pressure, speed or temperature-to enhance safety. It includes feedback-controlled drives, alarm or automatic shut-off functions, and other methods that detect abnormal conditions and modify pump performance to prevent failures or hazards.
[F02B 2039/164] the temperature of the pump, of the pump drive or the pumped fluid being limited -- F02B 2039/164 covers protective or control measures that limit the temperature of a pump, its drive unit or the fluid being pumped, such as cooling systems, thermal sensors, heat exchangers or temperature-regulated operation to prevent overheating or maintain desired fluid temperatures.
[F02B 2039/166] the fluid pressure in the pump or exhaust drive being limited -- This CPC subclass covers pump or exhaust-drive systems in which the fluid pressure is deliberately limited, for example by pressure-relief valves, regulators or control circuits, to prevent over-pressure, protect components and improve safety or efficiency. It includes devices that monitor or control the pressure in the drive line.
[F02B 2039/168] the rotational speed of pump or exhaust drive being limited -- The CPC subclass F02B 2039/168 covers pumps, compressors or exhaust-driven machines whose rotational speed is intentionally limited, for example by governors, throttling devices, variable-speed drives or other control mechanisms that restrict the drive's rpm to regulate pressure, flow or power consumption.
[F02B 2041/12] in jet propulsion apparatus -- This CPC subclass defines inventions concerning the internal components and arrangements of jet propulsion systems, covering elements such as compressors, combustion chambers, turbines and exhaust nozzles that generate thrust by accelerating a fluid stream.
[F02B 2043/103] Natural gas, e.g. methane or LNG used as a fuel -- This CPC subgroup relates to using natural gas, including methane and liquefied natural gas, as a fuel in power-producing installations such as boilers, furnaces, and engines. It covers the handling, supply, and combustion methods specific to these gaseous fuels within the broader class of steam and gas turbines.
[F02B 2043/106] Hydrogen obtained by electrolysis -- The CPC subclass F02B 2043/106 relates to methods and apparatus for generating hydrogen by electrolytic splitting of water or other hydrogen-containing liquids, covering the design of electrolyzers, electrode materials, electrolytes and operating conditions aimed at efficient hydrogen production.
[F02B 2053/005] Wankel engines
[F02B 2063/045] Frames for generator-engine sets -- The subclass F02B 2063/045 covers frames that support and mount combined generator-engine sets, specifying the design, mounting methods and material constructions used to hold the two machines together as a single unit. It pertains to integrated power-plant structures where alignment, vibration control and transportability are achieved through a common frame.
[F02B 2063/046] Handles adapted therefor, e.g. handles or grips for movable units -- The subclass covers handles, grips or lever devices specifically designed to operate or adjust movable parts of steam turbines or related equipment, such as shafts, valves or blade assemblies. These components provide manual access for positioning, locking or inspection of the moving units.
[F02B 2075/022] having less than six strokes per cycle -- This CPC subclass covers reciprocating steam generators or boilers in which the piston or other moving element completes fewer than six strokes during each operating cycle, a design intended to simplify the mechanism and reduce wear while delivering steam.

[F02B 2075/027] four -- F02B 2075/027 designates wind turbines whose rotor comprises four blades, covering the specific geometry, arrangement and aerodynamic features of four-blade rotors used in wind-energy conversion systems.
[F02B 2075/028] five -- The CPC entry F02B 2075/028 falls under the steam-turbine-plant class and covers installations that employ five turbine units or stages, specifying how the five machines are arranged, coupled and controlled within a single plant. It deals with the mechanical and operational features unique to a five-unit configuration.
[F02B 2075/125] Direct injection in the combustion chamber for spark ignition engines, i.e. not in pre-combustion chamber -- The classification covers fuel delivery systems that inject the air-fuel mixture directly into the main combustion chamber of spark-ignition engines rather than into a pre-combustion chamber, a method used to improve efficiency, power output and reduce emissions.
[F02B 2075/1804] Number of cylinders -- F02B 2075/1804 falls under the CPC class for internal-combustion piston engines and relates specifically to the number of cylinders, covering inventions that define, vary, or optimize cylinder count and configurations in such engines.
[F02B 2075/1808] two -- CPC F02B 2075/1808 classifies steam- or gas-turbine power plants that incorporate two of a specified component, such as a pair of combustion chambers, burners, or turbine stages, arranged to operate together within the same plant. It falls under the broader group for turbine installations using multiple parallel units to improve efficiency or flexibility.

[F02B 2075/1828] seven -- F02B 2075/1828 classifies steam turbines that are configured with seven stages or sections, describing the specific arrangement, components and operation of a seven-stage turbine system.
[F02B 2075/1832] eight -- F02B 2075/1832 is a CPC subclass under the F02B group for steam boilers and related heating apparatus, specifying designs that incorporate eight of a particular element-such as eight burners, tubes, or chambers-thereby defining the structural or functional arrangement of those eight components.
[F02B 2075/1836] nine -- CPC F02B 2075/1836 designates pumps that are built with a nine-stage arrangement, meaning the fluid passes through nine successive impeller stages stacked in series to achieve the required pressure rise.
[F02B 2075/184] ten -- F02B 2075/184 classifies pump or compressor designs that incorporate ten discrete working elements, such as ten stages, chambers or impellers, in a single machine, covering their specific arrangement and functional features within the broader category of fluid-moving devices.
[F02B 2075/1844] eleven
[F02B 2075/1848] twelve -- F02B 2075/1848 is a sub-group of the CPC class for steam or water turbines that specifically relates to designs or arrangements incorporating twelve of a particular element, such as twelve blades, twelve stages, twelve cylinders or other dozen-count components, defining the technical features and configurations of such twelve-unit turbine systems.
[F02B 2075/1852] thirteen -- The CPC entry F02B 2075/1852 (thirteen) pertains to reciprocating internal-combustion piston engines that incorporate thirteen cylinders or a related thirteen-component configuration, covering the specific arrangement and operation of such multi-cylinder engines.
[F02B 2075/1856] fourteen -- The CPC entry F02B 2075/1856 relates to internal combustion engines that have fourteen working cylinders, detailing their arrangement and associated technical features. It is applied to patents describing fourteen-cylinder configurations in automotive or industrial power units.
[F02B 2075/186] fifteen -- The CPC subclass F02B 2075/186 covers steam-turbine rotors or rotor discs that are equipped with fifteen blades. It is applied to patents describing the design, arrangement or manufacture of a turbine rotor where exactly fifteen blades are mounted on the disc.
[F02B 2075/1864] sixteen -- F02B 2075/1864 covers steam turbines that employ sixteen stages or rows of blades in the flow passage, defining the configuration and arrangement of the blade sets for that specific stage count.

[F02B 2075/1872] twenty-two -- F02B 2075/1872 covers gas or steam turbines whose rotor is designed with a specific twenty-two-stage arrangement, detailing the configuration, blade geometry and aerodynamic treatment of such multi-stage axial-flow turbine assemblies.
[F02B 2075/1876] twenty-four -- F02B 2075/1876 is a CPC subclass under steam generators that pertains to designs featuring twenty-four specific elements-typically water or fire tubes, heating segments, or similar components-arranged in a defined configuration for producing steam.

[F02B 2075/1884] thirty-two -- The CPC code F02B 2075/1884 falls within the "Other engines" class and designates inventions that incorporate a feature identified as "thirty-two," typically referring to a configuration involving thirty-two identical elements such as cylinders, stages, or burners in a power-generating or propulsion system.
[F02B 2075/1888] thirty-four
[F02B 2075/1892] thirty-six -- F02B 2075/1892 is a CPC subclass in the mechanical-engineering section that covers designs or arrangements of turbine or engine systems employing a thirty-six element configuration, such as a 36-stage turbine or a 36-cell fuel layout.
[F02B 2077/045] by flushing or rinsing -- This CPC subclass covers cleaning or purifying gas streams where the contaminated component is removed by flushing or rinsing the equipment or contacting surfaces with a liquid or gas to wash away deposits or residues. It is a specific technique within gas-cleaning processes that uses a flushing action to restore the cleaning medium.
[F02B 2077/06] Arrangements of purifying apparatus for liquid fuel or lubricant filters -- F02B 2077/06 falls under the CPC group for power-plant equipment and deals with the layout of devices that clean liquid fuel or lubricating oil. It covers how filters, housings, separators and related components are arranged within the fuel or lubricant circuit, including positioning, flow-path design and integration with the engine or machinery.
[F02B 21/00] Engines characterised by air-storage chambers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines with an auxiliary air chamber, e.g. which may be connected and disconnected with the engine intake or cylinder during the compression or expansion period of the engine cycle in order to influence the compression or expansion process.  **Glossary:** - Auxiliary chamber: Chamber not being a combustion chamber
[F02B 21/02] Chamber shapes or constructions -- The F02B 21/02 subclass pertains to the design and geometry of air-storage chambers within internal-combustion piston engines, covering various shapes, partitions, and structural features that influence airflow, compression, and combustion efficiency. It includes innovations in chamber contours, liners, and internal configurations that affect engine performance.
[F02B 2201/00] Fuels -- The CPC subclass F02B 2201/00 covers fuels for internal-combustion piston engines, including the types of combustible liquids, gases or solids, their composition, physical and chemical properties, and methods of preparation or storage. It also addresses fuel handling, delivery systems and performance characteristics relevant to engine operation.
[F02B 2201/02] Liquid -- F02B 2201/02 covers liquid fuels used in internal-combustion piston engines, including gasoline, diesel, kerosene, bio-derived oils and other liquid hydrocarbon mixtures that power mechanical, automotive or stationary engines. It addresses the composition, preparation, handling and performance characteristics of these liquid energy sources.
[F02B 2201/04] Gas -- The F02B 2201/04 subclass covers the use of gaseous fuels in internal-combustion piston engines. It includes all aspects of gas selection, handling, storage, and supply systems that enable combustion in mechanical engine applications.
[F02B 2201/06] Dual fuel applications -- This subclass relates to internal-combustion piston engines where the combustion process is powered by two fuels, either mixed or sequentially injected, to improve efficiency, emissions, or performance. It includes designs, control methods, and fuel-delivery systems that enable dual-fuel operation in diesel, gasoline or alternative fuel engines.
[F02B 2201/062] Liquid and liquid
[F02B 2201/0622] Liquid and liquefied gas -- The subclass F02B 2201/0622 in the Cooperative Patent Classification relates to liquid and liquefied gas used as fuel in internal-combustion piston engines. It addresses the properties, storage, handling and supply of these fluids for mechanical engineering applications. This code is nested under mechanical engineering - combustion engines - internal-combustion piston engines - fuels.
[F02B 2201/064] Liquid and gas -- A classification for internal-combustion piston engines that addresses the use of both liquid and gaseous fuels, either separately or in combination, for supplying the combustion chamber. It covers dual-fuel systems, fuel-mixing arrangements, and related control methods that enable operation with liquid, gas, or mixed fuel streams.
[F02B 2201/066] Gas and gas
[F02B 2275/00] Other engines, components or details, not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- F02B 2275/00 covers miscellaneous internal-combustion piston engine designs, components or details that are not specifically classified in other groups of the subclass. It includes unconventional or auxiliary engine parts, novel configurations and any other engine-related features that do not fit existing categories.
[F02B 2275/02] Attachment or mounting of cylinder heads on cylinders -- The CPC group F02B 2275/02 covers methods and structures for attaching or mounting cylinder heads to engine cylinders in internal-combustion piston engines. It includes fasteners, clamps, bolts, sealing arrangements and related components that secure the head to the cylinder block. This subgroup falls under the broader category of other engine components not addressed elsewhere.
[F02B 2275/06] Endless member is a belt -- F02B 2275/06 refers to the use of an endless belt as a power-transmitting member in internal-combustion piston engines. The classification covers belts that drive auxiliary components such as pumps, generators or valve-train mechanisms when no other specific subclass applies. It is placed in the "Other engines, components or details" group of the CPC.
[F02B 2275/08] Endless member is a chain -- F02B 2275/08 designates endless members that take the form of a chain for use in internal-combustion piston engines, covering chain drives that transmit motion or timing between engine components.
[F02B 2275/10] Diamond configuration of valves in cylinder heads -- The CPC entry F02B 2275/10 covers internal-combustion piston engines in which the cylinder-head valves are arranged in a diamond pattern, providing a compact configuration that can improve airflow and combustion efficiency. This specific valve layout is classified separately from other valve-arrangement groups.
[F02B 2275/14] Direct injection into combustion chamber -- The subclass F02B 2275/14 covers piston engines in which fuel is introduced straight into the combustion chamber, without a pre-chamber or manifold. Direct injection may be timed during the compression stroke and can involve various injector designs such as spray or jet nozzles. It applies to any internal-combustion piston engine not otherwise classified in more specific groups.
[F02B 2275/16] Indirect injection -- The code F02B 2275/16 designates the use of indirect injection in internal-combustion piston engines, where fuel is first introduced into a pre-chamber, swirl chamber or intake manifold before entering the main combustion chamber. This method is applied to improve mixing, reduce emissions and enable higher compression ratios in engines not covered by more specific subclasses.
[F02B 2275/18] DOHC [Double overhead camshaft] -- The F02B 2275/18 code refers to internal-combustion piston engines with a double overhead camshaft. This classification is a subset of internal-combustion piston engines. It is used for engines with this specific component.
[F02B 2275/20] SOHC [Single overhead camshaft] -- The CPC entry F02B 2275/20 falls under mechanical engineering, internal-combustion piston engines and covers single overhead camshaft (SOHC) arrangements. It pertains to engines or components in which a single camshaft is mounted in the cylinder head and operates the intake and exhaust valves, typically via direct or rocker-lever actuation. This subclass is used for designs not allocated to other more specific groups.
[F02B 2275/22] Side valves -- F02B 2275/22 classifies side valves, a type of valve positioned on the side of a cylinder in internal-combustion piston engines. It pertains to designs where the valve opens and closes laterally rather than from the top or bottom of the combustion chamber, covering their structure, operation and integration within engine components.
[F02B 2275/26] Flame plate -- A flame plate is a component fitted within the combustion chamber of an internal-combustion piston engine to shape and stabilize the flame front, promoting more uniform burning and reducing knock or emissions. It is used where other engine subcomponents are not specifically classified, and falls under the CPC group for other engine parts not otherwise provided.
[F02B 2275/28] Timing distribution gear -- The CPC entry F02B 2275/28 covers timing distribution gears employed in internal-combustion piston engines to synchronize the rotation of the crankshaft with camshafts or other driven components. It includes gears, gear trains and associated housings that control valve timing or fuel-injection timing, distinct from other engine-drive mechanisms.
[F02B 2275/30] Inverted positioning of engines -- The CPC subclass F02B 2275/30 relates to internal-combustion piston engines that are mounted in an inverted orientation, meaning the cylinder block or combustion chambers are positioned above the crankcase rather than below. This configuration is used to improve ground clearance, streamline the vehicle layout, or facilitate cooling and maintenance. It falls under the broader groups covering mechanical engineering and combustion engines.
[F02B 2275/32] Miller cycle -- The Miller cycle is a type of internal-combustion piston engine in which the intake valve remains open for part of the compression stroke, effectively reducing the compression ratio while retaining a higher expansion ratio to improve thermal efficiency. This arrangement is used in engines that do not fall under other more specific subclasses of combustion-engine technology. It is classified under F02B 2275/32 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[F02B 2275/34] Lateral camshaft position -- F02B 2275/34 covers the placement of a camshaft on the side of an internal-combustion piston engine, describing lateral mounting arrangements that drive valve-train components. The classification includes designs where the camshaft is positioned parallel to the cylinder bank or engine block, rather than overhead or centrally located, and the associated support, drive, and timing mechanisms.
[F02B 2275/36] Modified dwell of piston in TDC -- F02B 2275/36 refers to a design feature that changes the dwell time of a piston at top dead centre (TDC) in internal-combustion piston engines. The modification can involve altering the geometry or timing of the piston's motion to increase or decrease the period the piston remains near TDC, affecting combustion efficiency or emissions.
[F02B 2275/38] Square four-cylinder configuration -- The square-four configuration refers to an internal-combustion piston engine having four cylinders arranged at the corners of a square, typically with two parallel crankshafts or a single crankshaft and intersecting cylinder banks. This layout provides a compact, balanced design used in various small-displacement engines where space efficiency and smooth operation are desired.
[F02B 2275/40] Squish effect -- The F02B 2275/40 entry covers the squish effect in internal-combustion piston engines, where the piston crown and cylinder wall are shaped to create a thin clearance that forces the air-fuel mixture to be rapidly compressed and churned during the compression stroke. This turbulence accelerates flame propagation and can improve combustion efficiency and power output.
[F02B 2275/42] Texaco combustion process -- The CPC code F02B 2275/42 falls under Mechanical Engineering ▸ Combustion Engines ▸ Internal-combustion piston engines and pertains to the Texaco combustion process, a specific method of fuel injection and air-fuel mixing employed in piston engines. It defines the particular steps, timing and conditions of this process, distinguishing it from other combustion techniques used in similar engines.
[F02B 2275/44] Tools for engines -- F02B 2275/44 covers tools specifically designed for the assembly, disassembly, adjustment, inspection or testing of internal-combustion piston engines and their parts, such as wrenches, pullers, gauges and specialty hand or power tools that are not classified elsewhere in the engine-related subclasses.
