[F02B 2275/46] Total Energy plant -- F02B 2275/46 classifies total-energy plants that incorporate internal-combustion piston engines, covering integrated systems that generate and manage overall energy output, such as combined-heat-and-power or cogeneration installations, and related components not classified elsewhere in the subclass.
[F02B 2275/48] Tumble motion in gas movement in cylinder -- The CPC subclass F02B 2275/48 covers designs or methods that induce a tumble motion of the intake or exhaust gases within the cylinder of an internal-combustion piston engine. Tumble motion is a rotational flow around an axis transverse to the cylinder bore, used to improve fuel/air mixing, combustion speed and emissions. The entry applies to any engine component or configuration that creates this swirling gas movement.
[F02B 2275/50] Walking beam arrangement of rockers in valve drive -- The code F02B 2275/50 designates a valve-actuation system in internal-combustion piston engines where rocker arms are linked by a walking-beam mechanism. This arrangement synchronises valve opening and closing by transferring motion between rockers through a pivoting beam, offering an alternative to conventional cam-or spring-driven layouts. It applies to engines, components or details not covered by other subclasses in the mechanical-engineering hierarchy.
[F02B 23/00] Other engines characterised by special shape or construction of combustion chambers to improve operation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines characterised by a special shape of the combustion chamber to improve operation. The shape of the combustion space between the cylinder head and the working piston determines how the combustible mixture of air and fuel is combusted. The group mainly deals with the configuration of the combustion space in the piston for compression ignition but also for spark ignited engines.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Engines with incandescent chambers -> F02B9/08  **Glossary:** - Squish flow: Flow through a narrowing area, e.g. between piston and cylinder head - Swirl flow: Rotational flow around the cylinder axis - Tumble flow: Rotational flow around a line perpendicular to the cylinder axis
[F02B 23/02] with compression ignition -- F02B 23/02 covers internal-combustion piston engines whose combustion chambers are specially shaped or constructed to enhance operation by using compression ignition, such as diesel-type engines. It falls under the broader groups for mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and piston engines with uniquely designed chambers.
[F02B 23/04] the combustion space being subdivided into two or more chambers -- F02B 23/04 covers piston engines where the combustion space is split into two or more chambers, such as pre-chambers or divided cylinders, to enhance combustion efficiency, emissions or power output.
[F02B 23/06] the combustion space being arranged in working piston -- This subclass concerns internal-combustion piston engines where the combustion chamber is formed within the moving piston itself rather than in a fixed cylinder head. It covers designs that shape the piston to create the combustion space, improving efficiency or reducing heat loss.
[F02B 23/0603] at least part of the interior volume or the wall of the combustion space being made of material different from the surrounding piston part, e.g. combustion space formed within a ceramic part fixed to a metal piston head -- This subclass covers piston engines whose combustion chamber includes a portion of a different material, such as a ceramic insert fixed to a metal piston head, to enhance thermal resistance or wear performance. It pertains to internal-combustion piston engines with specially shaped or constructed combustion chambers.
[F02B 23/0618] having in-cylinder means to influence the charge motion -- This subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification covers internal-combustion piston engines that include devices placed inside the cylinder to modify the flow of the fuel-air mixture during intake or compression. Such in-cylinder means - like swirl generators, tumble plates or variable-geometry chambers - aim to improve combustion efficiency, emissions or power output.
[F02B 23/0621] Squish flow -- Squish flow denotes the rapid movement of the air-fuel mixture from the narrow clearance (the "squish" region) between the piston crown and cylinder head into the combustion chamber, creating intense turbulence that improves mixing and flame propagation. This phenomenon is exploited in internal-combustion piston engines with specially shaped chambers to enhance efficiency and reduce emissions.
[F02B 23/0624] Swirl flow -- The F02B 23/0624 entry covers internal-combustion piston engines that employ a swirl flow within the combustion chamber. By inducing a rotational motion of the air-fuel mixture, swirl improves mixture homogenization, flame propagation and combustion efficiency, especially in engines with specially shaped chambers.
[F02B 23/0627] having additional bores or grooves machined into the piston for guiding air or charge flow to the piston bowl -- A feature of internal-combustion piston engines in which the piston includes extra bores or grooves that are machined to direct air or the fuel-air charge into the piston bowl, enhancing flow and combustion efficiency.
[F02B 23/063] the combustion space in the piston interacting fluid dynamically with the cylinder head, the injector body or the cylinder wall  F02B23/04  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass covers piston combustion chambers whose internal fluid flow interacts dynamically with the cylinder head, injector body or cylinder wall, affecting the combustion process. It is situated under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → internal-combustion piston engines → special-shape combustion chambers, and is subordinated to F02B 23/04.
[F02B 23/0633] the combustion space being almost completely enclosed in the piston, i.e. having a small inlet in comparison to its volume -- The entry F02B 23/0633 concerns internal-combustion piston engines in which the combustion space is largely sealed inside the piston, leaving only a small inlet relative to the chamber's volume. This design feature aims to improve operation by shaping the combustion chamber for better sealing and thermal efficiency.
[F02B 23/0636] the combustion space having a substantially flat and horizontal bottom -- This subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines whose combustion chamber is designed with a substantially flat, horizontal bottom, a shape intended to influence flame spread, fuel mixing and efficiency. It is used for engines that employ this special chamber geometry to improve operation.
[F02B 23/0639] the combustion space having substantially the shape of a cylinder -- This subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines in which the combustion chamber is essentially cylindrical in shape, a design intended to enhance engine performance. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Internal-Combustion Piston Engines, specifically addressing engines characterised by special combustion-chamber geometry.
[F02B 23/0642] the depth of the combustion space being much smaller than the diameter of the piston, e.g. the depth being in the order of one tenth of the diameter -- This subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines whose combustion chambers are shaped so that the depth of the combustion space is much smaller than the piston diameter, typically about one-tenth of the diameter. The design aims to enhance engine performance by employing a shallow, wide chamber geometry.
[F02B 23/0645] Details related to the fuel injector or the fuel spray -- This classification covers details of fuel injectors and the fuel spray they produce in internal-combustion piston engines, including design aspects that affect spray pattern, atomisation, injection timing and overall combustion efficiency.
[F02B 23/0648] Means or methods to improve the spray dispersion, evaporation or ignition -- This subclass covers devices or techniques that enhance the atomisation, evaporation or ignition of fuel spray within internal-combustion piston engines whose combustion chambers have a special shape or construction. It includes any means-such as modified injectors, air-assist nozzles, swirl-inducing features or fuel-pre-heating systems-designed to improve spray dispersion, promote faster fuel evaporation or achieve more reliable ignition.
[F02B 23/0651] the fuel spray impinging on reflecting surfaces or being specially guided throughout the combustion space -- This CPC subgroup concerns internal-combustion piston engines in which the fuel spray is directed to strike reflective surfaces or is guided in a particular way throughout the combustion chamber, a design aimed at improving combustion efficiency and reducing emissions by shaping the spray-chamber interaction.
[F02B 23/0654] Thermal treatments, e.g. with heating elements or local cooling -- F02B 23/0654 covers thermal treatment of combustion chambers in piston engines, including the use of heating elements or localized cooling to adjust chamber temperature, improve combustion efficiency, reduce emissions, or prevent hot-spot damage.
[F02B 23/0657] the spray interacting with one or more glow plugs -- F02B 23/0657 concerns the interaction of fuel spray with one or more glow plugs in internal-combustion piston engines that employ specially shaped combustion chambers to improve operation. It addresses how glow plugs affect spray penetration, ignition timing and overall combustion efficiency.
[F02B 23/066] the injector being located substantially off-set from the cylinder centre axis -- This CPC entry covers internal-combustion piston engines with specially shaped combustion chambers designed to enhance performance, specifically where the fuel injector is positioned substantially off-set from the cylinder's centre axis. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and internal-combustion piston engines.
[F02B 23/0663] having multiple injectors per combustion chamber -- This subclass defines piston engines whose combustion chambers contain more than one fuel injector, enabling finer fuel distribution and improved mixture formation. The multiple-injector arrangement is used to enhance combustion efficiency, power output, emissions control, or stability of the engine operation.
[F02B 23/0666] having a single fuel spray jet per injector nozzle -- The entry describes internal-combustion piston engines in which each injector nozzle produces a single fuel-spray jet rather than multiple jets, a design intended to enhance fuel-air mixing and combustion efficiency within specially shaped combustion chambers.
[F02B 23/0669] having multiple fuel spray jets per injector nozzle -- This subclass covers piston engines that employ injector nozzles designed to emit several fuel spray jets instead of a single stream. By delivering multiple jets, the fuel is more finely atomized, promoting better mixing with air, higher combustion efficiency, lower emissions and improved power output.
[F02B 23/0672] Omega-piston bowl, i.e. the combustion space having a central projection pointing towards the cylinder head and the surrounding wall being inclined towards the cylinder center axis  the surrounding wall being exactly vertical  F02B23/0696 -- The entry concerns internal-combustion piston engines that use an omega-piston bowl, a combustion chamber shaped with a central projection aimed at the cylinder head and a surrounding wall that is exactly vertical. This special chamber geometry is intended to enhance fuel-air mixing and combustion efficiency.
[F02B 23/0675] the combustion space being substantially spherical, hemispherical, ellipsoid or parabolic -- This subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines whose combustion chambers are designed with substantially spherical, hemispherical, ellipsoidal or parabolic shapes. The special geometry is used to enhance flame propagation, improve efficiency or reduce emissions, distinguishing these engines from those with conventional chamber forms.
[F02B 23/0678] Unconventional, complex or non-rotationally symmetrical shapes of the combustion space, e.g. flower like, having special shapes related to the orientation of the fuel spray jets -- This subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines that employ unconventional, complex or non-rotationally symmetrical combustion-chamber shapes such as flower-like forms, designed to match the orientation of fuel-spray jets and enhance combustion efficiency. It falls within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy of combustion engines and internal-combustion piston engines.
[F02B 23/0681] Square, rectangular or the like profiles -- This subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines whose combustion chambers are formed with square, rectangular or similarly shaped profiles to enhance combustion efficiency, flame propagation or heat distribution. It applies to engines that use these non-circular chamber geometries as a design feature for improved operation.
[F02B 23/0684] Ring like bowl, e.g. toroidal -- F02B 23/0684 classifies internal-combustion piston engines that employ a ring-like, toroidal bowl as the combustion chamber, a special shape intended to enhance combustion efficiency and engine performance. This falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and specially shaped chamber designs.
[F02B 23/0687] Multiple bowls in the piston, e.g. one bowl per fuel spray jet -- This subclass covers piston designs for internal-combustion engines that incorporate two or more separate combustion bowls, each positioned to receive a distinct fuel-spray jet. The multiple-bowl arrangement aims to enhance mixture formation and flame propagation, improving engine efficiency and emissions.
[F02B 23/069] characterised by its eccentricity from the cylinder axis -- This subclass covers piston engines where the combustion chamber is offset from the cylinder axis, creating an eccentric arrangement to improve performance. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, internal-combustion piston engines and addresses special chamber shapes.
[F02B 23/0693] the combustion space consisting of step-wise widened multiple zones of different depth -- F02B 23/0693 covers internal-combustion piston engines whose combustion chambers are shaped with a series of stepwise widened zones of differing depth, creating multiple zones in the combustion space to enhance flame development, fuel mixing and overall engine performance.
[F02B 23/0696] W-piston bowl, i.e. the combustion space having a central projection pointing towards the cylinder head and the surrounding wall being inclined towards the cylinder wall -- A W-piston bowl is a combustion chamber shape where a central protrusion points toward the cylinder head while the surrounding walls slope outward toward the cylinder wall, improving flame propagation and fuel mixing in internal-combustion piston engines with specially shaped chambers.
[F02B 23/08] with positive ignition -- F02B 23/08 covers internal-combustion piston engines whose combustion chambers are specially shaped or constructed and that use a positive ignition system such as spark plugs rather than compression ignition, fitting within the mechanical-engineering class for combustion engines.
[F02B 23/10] with separate admission of air and fuel into cylinder -- It covers piston engines where air and fuel are introduced into the cylinder through distinct ports or valves rather than being mixed beforehand, typically employed to improve combustion efficiency and control. These designs often feature specially shaped combustion chambers to enhance performance.
[F02B 23/101] the injector being placed on or close to the cylinder centre axis, e.g. with mixture formation using spray guided concepts -- This subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines in which the fuel injector is positioned on or near the cylinder centre axis, enabling mixture formation through spray-guided concepts that improve combustion chamber performance. It addresses special-shaped or constructed chambers that enhance operation by directing fuel sprays centrally.
[F02B 23/104] the injector being placed on a side position of the cylinder -- F02B 23/104 covers internal-combustion piston engines in which the fuel injector is mounted on the side of the cylinder, a design used to optimise fuel-air mixing and combustion chamber performance. It falls under the subclass for engines characterized by special combustion-chamber shape or construction to improve operation.
[F02B 23/105] the fuel is sprayed directly onto or close to the spark plug -- The F02B 23/105 subclass of the CPC pertains to internal-combustion piston engines where fuel is sprayed directly onto or close to the spark plug, a design intended to enhance mixture formation and combustion efficiency.
[F02B 25/00] Engines characterised by provision for charging or scavenging -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines characterized by using fresh charge for scavenging cylinders.  Mostly two-stroke engines i.e. internal combustion engines that complete the process cycle in one revolution of the crankshaft, i.e. an up stroke and a down stroke of the piston. This is accomplished by using the beginning of the compression stroke and the end of the combustion stroke to perform simultaneously the intake and exhaust or scavenging functions. In this way, two-stroke engines often provide high specific power, at least in a narrow range of rotational speeds. The functions of some or all of the valves required by a four-stroke engine are usually served in a two-stroke engine by ports that are opened and closed by the motion of the piston.  **Glossary:** - Unidirectional scavenging: Scavenging gas flows in one direction in the cylinder - Reverse-flow scavenging: Scavenging gas flows up and down again - Reed valve: Type of unidirectional valve
[F02B 25/02] using unidirectional scavenging -- F02B 25/02 covers internal-combustion piston engines that employ a unidirectional scavenging system to expel exhaust gases and admit fresh charge, typically in two-stroke designs where the gas flow moves in a single direction through the cylinder. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and internal-combustion piston engines with provisions for charging or scavenging.
[F02B 25/04] Engines having ports both in cylinder head and in cylinder wall near bottom of piston stroke -- These are internal-combustion piston engines equipped with both a cylinder-head port and a low-position cylinder-wall port, allowing the intake or exhaust gas to be introduced or removed near the bottom of the piston's travel. The arrangement is typical of certain two-stroke designs that use separate head and liner ports for charging and scavenging.
[F02B 25/06] the cylinder-head ports being controlled by working pistons, e.g. by sleeve-shaped extensions thereof -- This subclass covers piston-driven mechanisms that open or close cylinder-head intake or exhaust ports, such as piston-mounted sleeves that act as valve plugs. It applies to engines where charging or scavenging flow is regulated directly by piston movement instead of separate valve gear.
[F02B 25/08] Engines with oppositely-moving reciprocating working pistons -- Engines in this subgroup are internal-combustion piston engines that use two or more working pistons moving in opposite directions within the same cylinder or paired cylinders, providing balanced forces and often improving scavenging or power output. Typical examples include opposed-piston and double-acting piston designs where the reciprocal motion of the pistons is coordinated to compress and expand the charge simultaneously.
[F02B 25/10] with one piston having a smaller diameter or shorter stroke than the other -- F02B 25/10 classifies internal-combustion piston engines that incorporate a charging or scavenging arrangement in which one piston has a smaller diameter or a shorter stroke than the other piston.
[F02B 25/12] Engines with U-shaped cylinders, having ports in each arm -- F02B 25/12 refers to internal-combustion piston engines that employ a U-shaped cylinder in which each arm of the U contains a port for charging or scavenging the cylinder. The design enables intake and exhaust flow to be managed separately in the two arms, improving gas exchange and engine efficiency.
[F02B 25/14] using reverse-flow scavenging, e.g. with both outlet and inlet ports arranged near bottom of piston stroke
[F02B 25/145] with intake and exhaust valves exclusively in the cylinder head -- F02B 25/145 designates internal-combustion piston engines in which both the intake and exhaust valves are situated solely in the cylinder head, as in overhead-valve or overhead-cam configurations. This class excludes engines that have any valve placed in the engine block.
[F02B 25/16] the charge flowing upward essentially along cylinder wall opposite the inlet ports -- F02B 25/16 relates to internal-combustion piston engines that feature a charging or scavenging system in which the charge flows upward along the cylinder wall opposite the inlet ports, directing the mixture or exhaust upward for improved filling or scavenging.
[F02B 25/18] the charge flowing upward essentially along cylinder wall adjacent the inlet ports, e.g. by means of deflection rib on piston -- F02B 25/18 covers internal-combustion piston engines in which the fresh charge moves upward along the cylinder wall next to the inlet ports, typically aided by a deflection rib on the piston that redirects the mixture toward the top of the cylinder. This arrangement is used to improve charging or scavenging efficiency in combustion-engine designs.
[F02B 25/20] Means for reducing the mixing of charge and combustion residues or for preventing escape of fresh charge through outlet ports not provided for in, or of interest apart from, subgroups -- F02B 25/20 covers devices that limit the intermixing of fresh charge with combustion by-products or stop fresh charge from leaking through unintended outlet ports. Such means are used in internal combustion piston engines to improve scavenging efficiency and are classified separately from related subgroups.
[F02B 25/22] by forming air cushion between charge and combustion residues -- F02B 25/22 covers internal-combustion piston engines that use an air cushion between the fresh charge and combustion residues to achieve charging or scavenging. The air cushion acts as a barrier that separates incoming air-fuel mixture from exhaust gases, improving scavenging efficiency especially in two-stroke designs. This method is classified under mechanical engineering for combustion engines with provisions for charging or scavenging.
[F02B 25/24] Inlet or outlet openings being timed asymmetrically relative to bottom dead-centre -- The F02B 25/24 subclass pertains to internal-combustion piston engines whose intake or exhaust openings are timed asymmetrically with respect to bottom dead centre, meaning the charging or scavenging events occur at different crank angles before and after this reference position.
[F02B 25/26] Multi-cylinder engines other than those provided for in, or of interest apart from, groups -- F02B 25/26 covers multi-cylinder internal-combustion piston engines that are not classified in the specific sub-groups for particular cylinder arrangements or charging methods. The code applies to engines with more than one cylinder where the configuration, scavenging, or charging system falls outside the defined groups, such as unconventional layouts or hybrid arrangements.
[F02B 25/28] with V-, fan-, or star-arrangement of cylinders -- This subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines that include a charging or scavenging system and feature the cylinders arranged in a V, fan or star pattern, providing a compact layout and improved balance.
[F02B 27/00] Use of kinetic or wave energy of charge in induction systems, or of combustion residues in exhaust systems, for improving quantity of charge or for increasing removal of combustion residues -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Internal combustion engines with a variable configuration intake manifold or exhaust manifold technology. Variable configuration intake or exhaust manifold technology can vary the configuration of the intake or exhaust tract in order to optimise power and torque across the range of engine speed operation, as well as help provide better fuel efficiency. The effect is often achieved by having two separate intake ports, each controlled by a valve, that open two different manifolds - one with a short path that operates at full engine load, and another with a significantly longer path that operates at lower load.  **Glossary:** - Resonance charging: Oscillating air column charging
[F02B 2700/00] Measures relating to the combustion process without indication of the kind of fuel or with more than one fuel -- This CPC subclass covers general measures that affect the combustion process in internal-combustion piston engines without specifying a particular fuel, or that are applicable to more than one fuel type. It includes techniques such as timing adjustments, air-fuel mixture control, pre-chamber designs and other methods aimed at improving combustion efficiency, power output or emissions irrespective of the fuel used.
[F02B 2700/02] Four stroke engines -- F02B 2700/02 covers four-stroke internal-combustion piston engines and related measures of the combustion process that are not limited to a specific fuel or involve multiple fuels. It falls under Mechanical Engineering, Combustion Engines, Internal-Combustion Piston Engines.
[F02B 2700/021] with measures for removing exhaust gases from the cylinder -- F02B 2700/021 covers devices or processes that extract combustion gases from the cylinder of an internal-combustion piston engine, independent of fuel type. It includes scavenging, exhaust-gas recirculation and other techniques that reduce residual gases before the next intake stroke. The class is applied to patents describing cylinder-level exhaust-gas removal.
[F02B 2700/023] with measures for charging, increasing the power -- F02B 2700/023 covers internal-combustion piston engines where the classification relates to the combustion process without specifying a fuel type or covering multiple fuels, specifically addressing methods or devices for charging the cylinder and increasing engine power output.
[F02B 2700/025] with measures for compressing the cylinder charge -- F02B 2700/025 covers measures aimed at compressing the cylinder charge in internal-combustion piston engines, irrespective of fuel type, as part of combustion-process control. It falls under the broader category of measures relating to the combustion process without indication of the kind of fuel.
[F02B 2700/026] with measures for increasing the part of the heat transferred to power, compound engines -- The CPC subclass F02B 2700/026 covers measures that improve the proportion of heat converted into useful power in internal-combustion piston engines, specifically in compound engine arrangements. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and internal-combustion piston engines, and it does not limit the type of fuel used.
[F02B 2700/028] double-acting -- The CPC code F02B 2700/028 covers double-acting internal-combustion piston engines, in which each piston generates power strokes on both sides, and includes design and operational measures for the combustion process without specifying a particular fuel type.
[F02B 2700/03] Two stroke engines -- The CPC subclass F02B 2700/03 covers two-stroke internal-combustion piston engines, focusing on measures that affect the combustion process where the fuel type is unspecified or multiple fuels are used. It is nested under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, internal-combustion piston engines, and measures relating to the combustion process.
[F02B 2700/031] with measures for removing exhaust gases from the cylinder -- The CPC code F02B 2700/031 refers to internal-combustion piston engines and covers measures for removing exhaust gases from the cylinder, independent of the fuel used or when multiple fuels are involved. It is placed under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, internal-combustion piston engines, and measures relating to the combustion process.
[F02B 2700/032] by means of the exhaust gases -- The subclass F02B 2700/032 concerns methods for influencing the combustion process in internal-combustion piston engines by utilizing the exhaust gases, without specifying a particular fuel type and applicable to more than one fuel. It is part of the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Internal-Combustion Piston Engines.
[F02B 2700/034] with measures for charging, increasing the power -- F02B 2700/034 covers measures applied to the combustion process of internal-combustion piston engines that are not tied to a specific fuel type or involve multiple fuels, specifically those aimed at improving the charging of the cylinder and boosting engine power.
[F02B 2700/035] with reservoir for scavenging or charging air -- The code F02B 2700/035 covers methods and devices for measuring combustion characteristics in internal-combustion piston engines when the fuel type is unspecified or multiple fuels are used, specifically those employing a reservoir to supply scavenging or charging air, such as superchargers or turbochargers. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and internal-combustion piston engines.
[F02B 2700/037] Scavenging or charging channels or openings -- The code F02B 2700/037 covers features that provide pathways for fresh air or exhaust gases in internal-combustion piston engines, such as scavenging passages, charging ducts or openings that aid the combustion process without specifying the fuel type. It is used for describing any arrangement that improves the flow of charge or removal of gases.
[F02B 2700/038] with measures for compressing the cylinder charge -- This CPC subclass pertains to internal-combustion piston engines and covers any technical measures that affect the compression of the cylinder charge, without specifying the fuel used. It includes features such as varying compression ratios, supercharging, turbocharging, and other methods that increase the pressure of the air-fuel mixture before ignition.
[F02B 27/001] the system having electrically controlled acoustic pulse generating devices, e.g. loudspeakers -- It covers systems that use electrically controlled acoustic pulse generators, such as loudspeakers, to influence the kinetic energy of the intake charge or to aid removal of combustion residues in exhaust passages of internal-combustion piston engines.
[F02B 27/003] using check valves -- This subclass covers the use of check valves in induction or exhaust systems of internal-combustion piston engines to harness the kinetic or pressure wave energy of the intake charge or exhaust gases, thereby improving the amount of fresh charge entering the cylinder or enhancing removal of combustion residues.
[F02B 27/005] Oscillating pipes with charging achieved by arrangement, dimensions or shapes of intakes pipes or chambers; Ram air pipes -- This subclass deals with oscillating intake or charging pipes in internal-combustion piston engines, where the arrangement, dimensions, or shape of the intake ducts or chambers is used to enhance the charge amount or remove residues, including ram-air designs.
[F02B 27/006] of intake runners -- F02B 27/006 covers features of intake runners that exploit the kinetic or wave energy of the incoming charge or the energy of combustion residues in the exhaust to increase the amount of fresh charge entering the cylinder or to enhance residue removal. The classification is applied to designs that use pressure waves, resonance or other dynamic effects in the induction system for improved charging efficiency in internal-combustion piston engines.
[F02B 27/008] Resonance charging -- This subclass covers methods that exploit the kinetic or wave energy of the fuel charge in induction or exhaust systems to increase the amount of charge or to enhance removal of combustion residues, using resonance phenomena to charge the cylinder. It is placed under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, internal-combustion piston engines in the CPC system.
[F02B 27/02] the systems having variable, i.e. adjustable, cross-sectional areas, chambers of variable volume, or like variable means -- F02B 27/02 covers variable-geometry components in internal-combustion piston engines, such as adjustable ducts, chambers of changeable volume or other means that alter cross-sectional area. These features are used to optimise the amount of charge entering the cylinder or to enhance exhaust-gas cleaning.
[F02B 27/0205] characterised by the charging effect -- It covers internal-combustion piston engines where the charging effect is employed, i.e., the kinetic or wave energy of the fuel-air mixture in intake or of exhaust gases is harnessed to increase the amount of charge entering the cylinder or to aid removal of combustion residues. This subclass focuses on methods that use these energy forms in induction or exhaust systems to improve charge quantity or residue extraction.
[F02B 27/021] Resonance charging  combined with oscillating pipe charging  F02B27/0221 -- F02B 27/021 covers techniques that exploit resonance charging together with oscillating pipe charging to enhance the amount of charge in internal-combustion piston engines. The methods use kinetic or wave energy of the charge or combustion residues in induction or exhaust systems to improve charge filling and promote more effective residue removal.
[F02B 27/0215] Oscillating pipe charging, i.e. variable intake pipe length charging -- The entry covers systems that vary the length or geometry of the intake pipe during engine operation to create oscillating charge flow, using the kinetic or wave energy of the incoming mixture or exhaust residues to increase the amount of charge entering the cylinder or to enhance residue removal. It relates to internal-combustion piston engines.
[F02B 27/0221] Resonance charging combined with oscillating pipe charging -- This subclass deals with methods that use resonance charging together with oscillating pipe charging to harness the kinetic or wave energy of the charge in the induction system or combustion residues in the exhaust, aiming to increase the amount of charge entering the cylinder or to enhance the removal of combustion by-products.
[F02B 27/0226] characterised by the means generating the charging effect -- The classification covers technologies that harness kinetic or wave energy from the intake charge or from combustion residues in the exhaust to boost the amount of charge entering the cylinder or to enhance residue removal, specifically through mechanisms that generate a charging effect. It is applied to internal-combustion piston engines where such energy-recovery methods are employed.
[F02B 27/0231] Movable ducts, walls or the like  F02B27/0257  takes precedence -- The CPC subclass F02B 27/0231 covers devices that use the kinetic or wave energy of the air-fuel charge in the intake system, or the energy of combustion residues in the exhaust, to increase the amount of charge entering the cylinder or to enhance removal of residues. It relates to movable ducts, walls or similar components that manipulate flow, and is subordinated to the broader subclass F02B 27/0257.
[F02B 27/0236] with continuously variable adjustment of a length or width -- The CPC subclass F02B 27/0236 covers internal-combustion piston engines that harvest kinetic or wave energy from the intake charge or exhaust residues to boost charge quantity or enhance residue removal, employing a continuously variable adjustment of a length or width in the associated system.
[F02B 27/0242] Fluid communication passages between intake ducts, runners or chambers -- F02B 27/0242 addresses passages that link intake ducts, runners or chambers in internal-combustion piston engines, allowing the fuel-air charge or exhaust gases to flow between these sections. These fluid communication passages are intended to improve charge distribution, enhance induction efficiency or aid removal of combustion residues.
[F02B 27/0247] Plenum chambers; Resonance chambers or resonance pipes -- This class covers plenum or resonance chambers and pipes integrated into the intake or exhaust of internal-combustion piston engines. Such components exploit pressure-wave or kinetic energy of the charge or combustion residues to boost the amount of fresh charge entering the cylinder or to improve residue removal from the exhaust.
[F02B 27/0252] Multiple plenum chambers or plenum chambers having inner separation walls, e.g. comprising valves for the same group of cylinders -- The code covers internal-combustion piston engines that employ several plenum chambers or a single plenum with internal separating walls, often incorporating valves that service the same group of cylinders. Such arrangements are used to boost the amount of charge entering the engine or to enhance the removal of combustion residues from the exhaust system.
[F02B 27/0257] Rotatable plenum chambers -- Rotatable plenum chambers are rotating intake or exhaust components in piston engines that capture the kinetic or wave energy of the charge or combustion residues. By creating dynamic pressure variations, they enhance the amount of fresh charge entering the cylinder and promote more effective removal of exhaust by-products.
[F02B 27/0263] the plenum chamber and at least one of the intake ducts having a common wall, and the intake ducts wrap partially around the plenum chamber, i.e. snail-type  F02B27/0257  takes precedence -- This CPC entry (F02B 27/0263) covers a combustion-engine arrangement where the plenum chamber and at least one intake duct share a common wall and the duct partially encircles the chamber in a snail-type geometry. The design exploits the kinetic or wave energy of the charge or exhaust residues to boost charge density or enhance residue removal.
[F02B 27/0268] Valves -- F02B 27/0268 covers valves that exploit the kinetic or acoustic energy of the fuel-air charge in induction systems, or the energy of combustion residues in exhaust, to enhance the amount of charge entering the cylinder or to aid removal of deposits. These valve designs are employed in internal-combustion piston engines to improve charging efficiency or exhaust cleaning.
[F02B 27/0273] Flap valves -- The F02B 27/0273 entry concerns flap-type valves used in internal-combustion piston engines to harness the kinetic or wave energy of the incoming charge in induction systems, or of combustion residues in exhaust systems, with the aim of increasing the amount of charge that reaches the cylinders or enhancing the removal of residual gases. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and internal-combustion piston engines.
[F02B 27/0278] Multi-way valves -- F02B 27/0278 covers multi-way valves used in internal-combustion piston engines to control the kinetic or wave energy of the charge in induction systems or the flow of combustion residues in exhaust passages. These valves direct gases for better charge filling and more efficient removal of combustion by-products.
[F02B 27/0284] Rotary slide valves -- This CPC entry describes rotary slide valves used in internal-combustion piston engines to control charge flow and exhaust gases, employing kinetic or wave energy of the charge or combustion residues to improve charge quantity or residue removal.
[F02B 27/0289] Intake runners having multiple intake valves per cylinder -- The code addresses intake runners that supply each cylinder with more than one intake valve to improve charge filling and flow uniformity in internal-combustion piston engines. This design is used to enhance engine breathing efficiency and overall performance.
[F02B 27/0294] Actuators or controllers therefor; Diagnosis; Calibration
[F02B 27/04] in exhaust systems only, e.g. for sucking-off combustion gases -- The code covers techniques that exploit the kinetic or wave energy present in an engine's exhaust stream to draw additional fresh charge into the cylinder or to extract combustion residues, thereby improving the amount of charge retained and enhancing removal of exhaust gases.
[F02B 27/06] the systems having variable, i.e. adjustable, cross-sectional areas, chambers of variable volume, or like variable means -- F02B 27/06 covers devices in internal-combustion piston engines that modify the size of intake or exhaust passages, chambers or other flow paths. These variable-geometry elements, such as adjustable cross-sectional areas or expandable volumes, are used to optimise charge filling or enhance exhaust residue removal by exploiting the kinetic or wave energy of the gases.
[F02B 2710/00] Gas engines -- F02B 2710/00 is a CPC subgroup for gas engines, covering internal-combustion piston engines that are powered by gaseous fuels such as natural gas, LPG, biogas or hydrogen, including their construction, operation, fuel-air mixing, ignition and control methods. The code is used by the EPO and USPTO within the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Internal-Combustion Piston Engines.
[F02B 2710/02] Four stroke engines -- F02B 2710/02 covers four-stroke internal combustion piston engines, in which the combustion cycle is divided into intake, compression, power and exhaust strokes. These engines are commonly used in automotive and stationary applications and are a subcategory of gas engines within the combustion engine hierarchy.
[F02B 2710/021] with measures for removing exhaust gases from the cylinder -- The subclass F02B 2710/021 covers gas-engine piston designs that incorporate means to extract exhaust gases directly from the cylinder during operation, aiming to improve scavenging and reduce emissions. It includes mechanisms such as exhaust ports, valves or ducts positioned to remove burnt gases before the intake stroke, enhancing engine efficiency and environmental performance.
[F02B 2710/023] with measures for charging, increasing the power -- F02B 2710/023 covers gas-fuel internal-combustion piston engines that incorporate charge-enhancing features such as superchargers, turbochargers, or other methods designed to raise the amount of air-fuel mixture in the cylinders, thereby increasing the engine's power output.
[F02B 2710/025] with measures for compressing the cylinder charge -- F02B 2710/025 pertains to gas-type internal-combustion piston engines that include features for compressing the cylinder charge, such as superchargers, turbochargers, or other compression mechanisms. It classifies designs where the engine's inlet air or fuel-air mixture is pressurised before combustion.
[F02B 2710/026] with measures for improving combustion -- F02B 2710/026 covers gas piston engines that incorporate devices or methods aimed at improving the combustion process. It includes features such as optimized fuel injection, enhanced air-fuel mixing, and chamber geometries that increase efficiency, reduce emissions, or promote more complete burn.
[F02B 2710/028] with measures for increasing the part of the heat transferred to power, compound engines -- F02B 2710/028 covers internal-combustion piston engines, specifically gas engines, that employ measures to raise the fraction of heat transferred to useful power, such as compound-engine configurations.
[F02B 2710/03] Two stroke engines -- F02B 2710/03 designates two-stroke internal-combustion piston engines, a type of gas engine that completes the power cycle in two strokes of the piston instead of four. These engines are commonly used in small-medium power applications such as motorcycles, chainsaws and portable generators.
[F02B 2710/032] with measures for removing exhaust gases from the cylinder -- The subclass F02B 2710/032 covers gas-type internal-combustion piston engines that incorporate means for removing exhaust gases directly from the cylinder, such as exhaust-gas recirculation, scavenging or in-cylinder after-treatment systems, to improve emissions control and engine performance.
[F02B 2710/034] with measures for charging, increasing the power -- F02B 2710/034 relates to gas-fuel internal-combustion piston engines that incorporate charging or induction techniques-such as supercharging, turbocharging, or other methods-to increase the amount of air-fuel mixture entering the cylinders, thereby raising the engine's power output.
[F02B 2710/036] Scavenging or charging channels or openings -- The CPC subclass F02B 2710/036, under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Internal-combustion piston engines → Gas engines, covers scavenging or charging channels or openings. It pertains to the design of passages that introduce fresh charge or remove exhaust gases in gas-type piston engines, such as inlet ports, scavenging ducts, and related openings.
[F02B 2710/038] with measures for improving combustion -- F02B 2710/038 covers gas-fueled internal-combustion piston engines that incorporate measures to improve the combustion process. The classification includes features such as optimized fuel injection, enhanced air-fuel mixing, spark-timing control, exhaust-gas recirculation and other techniques that increase efficiency, reduce emissions or improve power output.
[F02B 2720/00] Engines with liquid fuel -- Engines with liquid fuel are internal-combustion piston engines that run on gasoline, diesel or other liquid hydrocarbons. The classification covers the design, operation, control and related components of such engines, including fuel injection, combustion chambers and exhaust systems. It is used in patent documents describing mechanical-engineering solutions for liquid-fuel power plants.
[F02B 2720/10] Mixture compressing engines for liquid fuel -- F02B 2720/10 covers piston engines that compress the fuel-air mixture before ignition and are designed to run on liquid fuels such as gasoline or diesel. These engines belong to the internal-combustion piston engine group within the broader mechanical engineering field.
[F02B 2720/12] Four stroke engines with ignition device -- The code F02B 2720/12 pertains to four-stroke internal-combustion piston engines that run on liquid fuel and incorporate an ignition device such as a spark plug. It covers the arrangement and operation of the ignition system within the engine's cycle, including timing, spark generation, and related components. This classification falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, internal-combustion piston engines, and engines with liquid fuel.
[F02B 2720/122] with measures for removing exhaust gases from the cylinder -- F02B 2720/122 pertains to liquid-fuel internal-combustion piston engines that incorporate features for removing exhaust gases directly from the cylinder. The sub-class covers devices such as scavenging ports, exhaust valves, or other mechanisms that expel burnt gases to improve engine breathing and efficiency.
[F02B 2720/124] with measures for charging, increasing the power -- The subclass covers liquid-fuel internal-combustion piston engines that incorporate devices or methods for charging the cylinders-such as superchargers, turbochargers, or other forced-induction systems-and other measures aimed at raising the engine's power output. It classifies technical solutions that enhance the amount of air-fuel mixture or improve combustion efficiency to increase performance.
[F02B 2720/126] with measures for compressing the cylinder charge -- F02B 2720/126 covers liquid-fuel internal-combustion piston engines that incorporate devices or methods for compressing the cylinder charge, such as superchargers, turbochargers, or other charge-compression arrangements. It applies to any technical features that increase the pressure of the air-fuel mixture before ignition to improve performance or efficiency.
[F02B 2720/128] with measures for increasing the part of the heat transferred to power, compound engines -- This CPC subclass covers piston engines that run on liquid fuel and incorporate compound cycles or other devices intended to raise the share of combustion heat converted into useful mechanical power. It includes features such as multi-stage expansion, heat-recovery systems and other efficiency-enhancing measures applied to internal-combustion engines.
[F02B 2720/13] Two stroke engines with ignition device -- F02B 2720/13 covers two-stroke internal-combustion piston engines that operate on liquid fuel and include an ignition device such as a spark plug. The class falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, internal-combustion piston engines, specifically engines with liquid fuel.
[F02B 2720/131] with measures for removing exhaust gases from the cylinder -- The F02B 2720/131 subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines that use liquid fuel and incorporate features or devices aimed at extracting exhaust gases directly from the cylinder, such as exhaust ports, valves, or specialized scavenging systems designed to improve combustion efficiency and reduce emissions.
[F02B 2720/132] by means of exhaust gases -- F02B 2720/132 relates to internal-combustion piston engines powered by liquid fuel that employ exhaust gases for engine operation or control. It covers techniques such as exhaust-gas recirculation, turbocharging, or after-treatment systems that use the spent gases to influence combustion, efficiency, or emissions.
[F02B 2720/133] with measures for charging, increasing the power -- F02B 2720/133 covers internal-combustion piston engines that run on liquid fuel and incorporates measures for charging the engine-such as enhanced fuel or air delivery systems-to boost its power output.
[F02B 2720/135] with reservoir for scavenging or charging air -- This classification covers internal-combustion piston engines that run on liquid fuel and incorporate a reservoir for scavenging or charging air. Such designs use a separate air tank to supply fresh charge or assist exhaust-gas removal, as seen in two-stroke engines with crankcase compression or supercharged systems.
[F02B 2720/136] Scavenging or charging channels or openings -- F02B 2720/136 covers the design and arrangement of passages that supply fresh charge to, or expel exhaust from, liquid-fuel internal-combustion piston engines. It includes scavenging ducts, inlet manifolds, and exhaust outlets that form the engine's charging or scavenging system.
[F02B 2720/137] with measures for improving combustion -- This CPC subclass F02B 2720/137 covers internal combustion piston engines that run on liquid fuel and includes features designed to improve combustion efficiency such as optimized air-fuel mixing, timing adjustments, or advanced ignition systems.
[F02B 2720/138] with measures for increasing the part of the heat transferred to power, compound engines -- F02B 2720/138 covers compound internal-combustion piston engines that use liquid fuel and include features designed to raise the proportion of heat that is converted into mechanical power.
[F02B 2720/15] Mixture compressing engines with ignition device and mixture formation in the cylinder -- F02B 2720/15 relates to liquid-fuel internal-combustion piston engines in which the air-fuel mixture is created inside the cylinder, compressed, and then ignited by a built-in ignition device such as a spark plug. It covers designs where fuel injection or atomisation occurs within the combustion chamber prior to compression.
[F02B 2720/151] with fuel supply and pulverisation by air or gas under pressure during the suction or compression stroke -- This subclass pertains to internal-combustion piston engines that run on liquid fuel and supply the fuel by injecting it with a stream of air or gas under pressure during the suction or compression stroke, thereby atomising and mixing the fuel with the intake charge before ignition.
[F02B 2720/152] with fuel supply and pulverisation by injecting the fuel under pressure during the suction or compression stroke -- The subclass pertains to liquid-fuel piston engines in which fuel is supplied and atomised by high-pressure injection during the suction or compression stroke, allowing the fuel to mix with air before ignition. This timing of injection is intended to improve fuel preparation and overall combustion efficiency.
[F02B 2720/153] with injection of an air-fuel mixture under pressure during the suction or compression stroke -- The code F02B 2720/153 covers internal-combustion piston engines that run on liquid fuel and inject a pre-mixed air-fuel charge under pressure during the intake (suction) or compression stroke. This injection timing differs from conventional fuel-only injection, aiming to improve mixture homogeneity and combustion efficiency. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for combustion engines.
[F02B 2720/155] with pulverisation by air sucked into the cylinder -- The CPC subclass F02B 2720/155 covers internal-combustion piston engines that run on liquid fuel and employ a method of fuel atomisation in which air is drawn into the cylinder to pulverise the fuel before ignition. It pertains to designs where the intake air stream assists the spray formation, improving mixing and combustion efficiency.
[F02B 2720/156] with pulverisation by the compressed air stream -- The code F02B 2720/156 classifies internal-combustion piston engines that run on liquid fuel and employ a compressed-air stream to atomise the fuel before injection. It falls under the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, internal-combustion piston engines, and engines with liquid fuel.
[F02B 2720/157] with means for improving the mixture in the cylinder -- This subclass covers internal combustion piston engines that run on liquid fuel and include devices that improve the homogeneity or distribution of the fuel-air mixture inside the cylinder, such as swirl generators, fuel-spray nozzles, or in-cylinder mixing aids. It is part of CPC group F02B 2720 for liquid-fuel engines.
[F02B 2720/158] with an auxiliary cylinder in which an explosion is generated -- This class covers piston engines that incorporate a secondary cylinder where a combustible mixture is ignited to provide auxiliary power, boost torque, or assist start-up. The auxiliary cylinder operates alongside the main combustion chambers and uses the same liquid fuel.
[F02B 2720/16] Mixture compressing engines with ignition by compression or other heat -- This CPC subclass concerns internal-combustion piston engines that run on liquid fuel and ignite the fuel-air mixture by compression or another heat source rather than a spark plug. It includes designs where the compression process itself raises the temperature sufficiently for self-ignition, covering diesel-type and similar compression-ignition engines.
[F02B 2720/20] Air compressing engines with ignition by the heat of compression -- F02B 2720/20 covers internal-combustion piston engines that run on liquid fuel and ignite the fuel solely by the heat produced during air compression, i.e., compression-ignition (diesel-type) engines.
[F02B 2720/22] Four stroke engines -- F02B 2720/22 classifies four-stroke internal-combustion piston engines that run on liquid fuel. It covers the complete cycle - intake, compression, power and exhaust - and associated design features such as valve timing, crankshaft layout and fuel delivery systems. The code is used in patent documentation to identify technologies in automotive, marine and stationary power applications.
[F02B 2720/221] with measures for removing exhaust gases from the cylinder -- F02B 2720/221 covers devices or methods that remove or treat exhaust gases inside the cylinder of liquid-fuel internal-combustion piston engines, such as in-cylinder scavenging, exhaust-gas recirculation or cleaning mechanisms. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → internal-combustion piston engines → engines with liquid fuel.
[F02B 2720/223] with measures for charging, increasing the power -- A subclass of liquid-fuel internal-combustion piston engines that includes devices or methods aimed at enhancing the charging process and raising output power, such as superchargers, turbochargers, forced induction systems, and related control techniques. It covers any arrangement that improves the amount of air-fuel mixture entering the cylinders to increase engine performance.
[F02B 2720/225] with measures for compressing the cylinder charge -- F02B 2720/225 covers internal-combustion piston engines that run on liquid fuel and incorporate measures to compress the cylinder charge, such as superchargers, turbochargers or other forced-induction devices. It falls under the Mechanical Engineering hierarchy, specifically within Combustion Engines and Internal-Combustion Piston Engines.
[F02B 2720/226] with measures for improving combustion -- F02B 2720/226 is a CPC subclass concerning liquid-fuel piston engines that incorporate measures to improve the combustion process, such as advanced fuel injection, optimized ignition timing, or enhanced air-fuel mixing, aimed at increasing efficiency and reducing emissions.
[F02B 2720/228] with measures for increasing the part of the heat transferred to power, compound engines
[F02B 2720/23] Two stroke engines -- F02B 2720/23 covers two-stroke internal-combustion piston engines that run on liquid fuel. In these engines the intake, compression, power and exhaust phases occur in just two strokes of the piston, so each crankshaft revolution produces a power stroke. This design yields a simple, lightweight power unit common in small-capacity applications such as motorcycles, outboard motors and handheld equipment.
[F02B 2720/231] with measures for removing exhaust gases from the cylinder -- F02B 2720/231 classifies internal-combustion piston engines that run on liquid fuel and incorporate devices for removing exhaust gases directly from the cylinder, such as exhaust gas recirculation systems, valve arrangements, or scavenging methods that improve emissions control and engine efficiency.
[F02B 2720/232] by means of the exhaust gases -- This subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines fueled by liquids where the exhaust gases are used to perform a function, for example to drive a turbo-charger, to recirculate exhaust for emission control, or to provide additional power.
[F02B 2720/233] with measures for charging, increasing the power -- This subclass covers liquid-fuel internal-combustion piston engines that incorporate features aimed at enhancing the charging process and boosting output power, such as superchargers, turbochargers, variable-geometry intake systems or other mechanisms that increase the amount of air-fuel mixture introduced into the cylinders.
[F02B 2720/235] with reservoir for scavenging or charging air -- This subclass covers liquid-fuel piston engines that incorporate a dedicated air-reservoir to facilitate scavenging or to charge the cylinder with fresh air during operation. The reservoir may be used for supercharging, blow-by reduction, or improving charge-air management in two-stroke or four-stroke cycles.
[F02B 2720/236] scavenging or charging channels or openings -- In liquid-fuel internal-combustion piston engines, scavenging or charging channels or openings are passages that admit fresh air-fuel mixture to the cylinder and allow exhaust gases to be expelled, enhancing combustion efficiency. These openings may be formed in the cylinder liner, valve ports, crankcase or other flow-routing components and are shaped to promote effective flow during each engine cycle.
[F02B 2720/237] with measures for improving combustion -- This subclass covers liquid-fuel piston engines that incorporate features aimed at enhancing the combustion process, such as optimized fuel injection, improved air-fuel mixing, ignition timing or combustion-chamber design. It falls under Mechanical Engineering - Combustion Engines - Internal-combustion piston engines in the CPC.
[F02B 2720/238] with measures for increasing the part of the heat transferred to power, compound engines -- This subclass describes liquid-fuel internal-combustion piston engines that incorporate measures to raise the fraction of waste heat converted into useful power, such as compound engine arrangements or heat-recovery devices. It is nested under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, internal-combustion piston engines and engines with liquid fuel.
[F02B 2720/25] Supply of fuel in the cylinder -- F02B 2720/25 relates to the supply of fuel into the cylinder of liquid-fuel internal-combustion piston engines. It encompasses injection, metering, timing and distribution mechanisms that deliver fuel directly into the combustion chamber during engine operation.
[F02B 2720/251] Fuel supply by high pressure gas
[F02B 2720/252] with air pump fixed to engine cylinder -- F02B 2720/252 covers liquid-fuel internal-combustion piston engines that have an air pump permanently attached to the engine cylinder to provide direct charging or scavenging.
[F02B 2720/253] with high pressure air reservoir close to the point of injection -- This subclass covers liquid-fuel internal-combustion piston engines that incorporate a high-pressure air reservoir positioned close to the fuel injection point, typically used to improve atomisation, combustion stability or power output. The arrangement is applied in combustion-engine designs where precise timing and mixing of air and fuel are critical.
[F02B 2720/255] with mixture compressing pump -- The CPC subclass F02B 2720/255 refers to internal-combustion piston engines that employ a mixture-compressing pump, meaning a pump that both mixes liquid fuel with air and raises the mixture pressure before it enters the combustion chamber. It covers designs where this combined mixing and compression function is integral to the engine's fuel-delivery system, typical of certain two-stroke and specialized diesel engines.
[F02B 2720/256] using steam or other gas as high pressure gas -- The subclass F02B 2720/256 deals with internal-combustion piston engines that run on liquid fuel but employ steam or another gas as a high-pressure working fluid, such as by injecting the gas into the cylinder or using it to boost combustion. It covers methods that use the auxiliary gas to increase efficiency, power output or performance of the engine.
[F02B 2720/257] Supply of fuel under pressure in the cylinder without blowing fluid -- The F02B 2720/257 class covers methods for delivering liquid fuel into the cylinder of a piston-type internal-combustion engine under pressure while preventing the fuel from escaping or blowing back into the supply system. It applies to engine designs where the fuel is injected directly into the combustion chamber without a separate venting or flushing step. This ensures precise fuel metering and maintains system integrity during high-pressure operation.
[F02B 2720/258] with compression and ignition exclusively in the cylinder -- The subclass F02B 2720/258 covers liquid-fuel piston engines in which both the compression of the charge and its ignition occur solely inside the cylinder, without external ignition devices. It applies to engines that rely on auto-ignition of the fuel by compression, such as conventional diesel engines.
[F02B 2720/27] Air compressing engines with hot-bulb ignition -- Air-compressing internal-combustion piston engines that use liquid fuel and ignite the charge with a hot-bulb system, where combustion is initiated by heating a bulb cup to a high temperature before fuel injection. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and specifically engines with liquid fuel.
[F02B 2720/272] Supply of all the fuel into the prechamber -- It covers methods or devices for delivering the whole fuel charge directly into the pre-chamber of a liquid-fuel internal-combustion piston engine, often to create a stratified or pre-ignition mixture. The classification includes fuel-injection arrangements that feed the pre-chamber before the main combustion event.
[F02B 2720/274] with injection of all the fuel into the prechamber -- This subclass covers liquid-fuel internal-combustion piston engines in which the entire fuel charge is injected into a prechamber before entering the main combustion chamber. It is part of the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for combustion engines, focusing on prechamber injection designs.
[F02B 2720/276] Supply of only a part of the fuel into the prechamber -- F02B 2720/276 covers liquid-fuel piston engines where only a portion of the total fuel is introduced into a prechamber or pilot combustion zone before the main charge. Supplying a partial fuel amount to the prechamber is used to initiate or stabilize combustion, improve efficiency or reduce emissions. This entry lies within mechanical engineering, combustion engines, internal-combustion piston engines, engines with liquid fuel.
[F02B 2720/278] with injection of only a part of the fuel into the prechamber -- The subclass covers liquid-fuel piston engines in which only a portion of the total fuel is injected into a prechamber before the main combustion chamber, a technique used to enhance fuel-air mixing, promote stable flame initiation and lower emissions.
[F02B 2720/30] Engines with air compression and ignition device -- F02B 2720/30 classifies piston engines that compress a charge of air, mix it with liquid fuel, and ignite the mixture using a spark plug or other ignition device, covering spark-ignition internal-combustion engines.
[F02B 2730/00] Internal-combustion engines with pistons rotating or oscillating with relation to the housing -- The CPC subclass F02B 2730/00 covers internal-combustion piston engines in which the pistons rotate or oscillate relative to the engine housing. It includes designs such as rotary, wobble-plate or oscillating-piston configurations used to convert combustion pressure into mechanical motion.
[F02B 2730/01] with one or more pistons in the form of a disk or rotor rotating with relation to the housing -- This CPC subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines in which at least one piston is shaped as a disk or rotor that rotates with respect to the housing instead of moving linearly. It includes designs where the rotating piston performs power strokes while the housing remains stationary.
[F02B 2730/011] with vanes sliding in the housing -- F02B 2730/011 designates internal-combustion piston engines in which the pistons are replaced or complemented by vanes that slide within the housing, converting combustion pressure into motion. The classification applies to engine concepts where sliding vanes perform the power-stroke instead of conventional reciprocating pistons.
[F02B 2730/012] with vanes sliding in the piston -- This subclass relates to internal-combustion piston engines in which the piston incorporates one or more vanes that slide relative to the piston body during operation, often to improve gas flow, sealing or energy transfer within the cylinder. It falls under the hierarchy of mechanical engineering → combustion engines → internal-combustion piston engines with rotating or oscillating pistons.
[F02B 2730/013] Vanes fixed in the centre of the housing -- The subclass F02B 2730/013 covers internal-combustion piston engines in which one or more vanes are permanently fixed at the centre of the engine housing, influencing the motion of the pistons or rotating components. It pertains to designs where the fixed vanes define the clearance and flow paths within the cylinder space.
[F02B 2730/015] with vanes hinged to the housing -- F02B 2730/015 classifies internal-combustion piston engines in which the pistons are replaced by vanes that are hinged to the housing, allowing them to swing or oscillate instead of rotating with a conventional crankshaft. This sub-group falls under combustion-engine technology for rotary or wobble-plate engines that derive power from hinged vanes moving within the engine housing.
[F02B 2730/016] with vanes hinged to the piston -- This subgroup covers internal-combustion piston engines where the piston carries hinged vanes that move relative to the piston to improve gas flow or sealing. These vanes are attached to the piston and can swing about a hinge as the piston moves within the cylinder. The design is used to enhance scavenging or combustion efficiency.
[F02B 2730/017] with rotating elements fixed to the housing or on the piston -- F02B 2730/017 pertains to internal-combustion piston engines in which rotating components-such as gears, cams or other rotary elements-are mounted directly on the engine housing or attached to the piston, rather than being integral to the crankshaft. This sub-class addresses designs where the rotation is fixed to the housing or piston for power transmission or valve actuation.
[F02B 2730/018] with piston rotating around an axis passing through the gravity centre, this piston or the housing rotating at the same time around an axis parallel to the first axis -- This CPC subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines in which the piston rotates about an axis that passes through its centre of gravity while the engine housing simultaneously rotates about a parallel axis. The arrangement allows combined rotational motion of both piston and housing for specialized power-transmission or balance purposes.
[F02B 2730/02] with piston rotating around its axis and having a reciprocating movement in a cylinder -- This CPC subclass covers internal combustion piston engines where the piston rotates about its own axis while also moving back and forth within the cylinder. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, internal-combustion piston engines. It is used to classify patents dealing with such combined rotary-reciprocating piston designs.
[F02B 2730/03] with piston oscillating in a housing or in a space in the form of an annular sector -- A subclass of internal-combustion piston engines in which the piston does not rotate but oscillates back-and-forth within a housing or a space shaped like an annular sector. It covers designs such as wobble-type or oscillating-piston mechanisms used in specialized engines or pumps.
[F02B 2730/05] with pistons intermeshing as gear wheels -- This subclass defines internal-combustion piston engines in which the pistons are arranged to mesh together like gear wheels, rotating or oscillating within the housing as a means of power transmission.
[F02B 2730/09] Arrangements or specially formed elements for engines according to the preceding groups -- F02B 2730/09 pertains to the design of arrangements or specially formed elements for piston-type internal-combustion engines, such as modified cylinder heads, intake or exhaust manifolds and other components that influence flow, cooling or combustion within the engine housing. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and internal-combustion piston engines.
[F02B 2730/095] Hydraulic pistons -- F02B 2730/095 is a subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification for mechanical engineering, specifically under combustion engines and internal-combustion piston engines that have pistons rotating or oscillating relative to the housing. It covers inventions concerning hydraulic pistons, detailing their design, operation, and integration within such engines. This code is used by the EPO and USPTO to index patent documents dealing with hydraulic piston technology.
[F02B 29/00] Engines characterised by provision for charging or scavenging not provided for in groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means not otherwise provided for, for improving the engine's volumetric efficiency by increasing the air intake density. The pressure of ambient air drawn in is increased and the temperature is decreased before it enters into the engine cylinder. This results in a greater mass of air entering the cylinder on each intake stroke.
[F02B 29/02] Other fluid-dynamic features of induction systems for improving quantity of charge -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - for also imparting a rotation to the charge in the cylinder -> F02B31/00
[F02B 29/04] Cooling of air intake supply -- F02B 29/04 relates to cooling the air supplied to the intake of internal-combustion piston engines, particularly those with special charging or scavenging arrangements not covered elsewhere. It includes devices or processes that lower intake-air temperature to improve engine performance, efficiency or emissions.
[F02B 29/0406] Layout of the intake air cooling or coolant circuit -- F02B 29/0406 covers the arrangement of the intake-air cooling or coolant circuit in internal-combustion piston engines that have special charging or scavenging features not addressed in other groups, focusing on how air-to-air or air-to-liquid cooling pathways are integrated into the engine's intake system. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for combustion engines, specifically the subcategory for layouts of intake-air cooling circuits.
[F02B 29/0412] Multiple heat exchangers arranged in parallel or in series -- F02B 29/0412 covers internal-combustion piston engines that use more than one heat-exchanger for charging or scavenging, where the exchangers are set up either side-by-side in parallel or linked one after another in series. It applies to designs that provide additional cooling or heat-recovery beyond the standard configurations. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, internal-combustion piston engines, and the group for engines with special charging or scavenging provisions.
[F02B 29/0418] the intake air cooler having a bypass or multiple flow paths within the heat exchanger to vary the effective heat transfer surface -- An intake-air cooler designed with an internal bypass or several flow channels can change the effective heat-transfer surface, allowing the cooling capacity to be varied according to engine operating conditions. This feature is used in internal-combustion piston engines to optimise charge temperature and scavenging efficiency.
[F02B 29/0425] Air cooled heat exchangers -- F02B 29/0425 refers to air-cooled heat exchangers used in internal-combustion piston engines that have a charging or scavenging arrangement not covered by other groups. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion engines and internal-combustion piston engines. It addresses designs where heat is dissipated by air flow rather than liquid cooling.
[F02B 29/0431] Details or means to guide the ambient air to the heat exchanger, e.g. having a fan, flaps, a bypass or a special location in the engine compartment -- F02B 29/0431 relates to internal-combustion piston engines and defines devices that guide ambient air to a heat exchanger, including fans, flaps, bypass passages or special placement within the engine compartment.
[F02B 29/0437] Liquid cooled heat exchangers -- F02B 29/0437 refers to liquid-cooled heat exchangers used in internal-combustion piston engines that have special charging or scavenging arrangements not covered by other groups. These devices transfer heat from the engine coolant to a secondary fluid, typically water or glycol, to maintain operating temperature. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, internal-combustion piston engines.
[F02B 29/0443] Layout of the coolant or refrigerant circuit -- F02B 29/0443 is a CPC subclass for internal-combustion piston engines that include a charging or scavenging system, focusing on the design and arrangement of the coolant or refrigerant circuit that removes heat from the engine components. It addresses the routing, components, and integration of the cooling network within the engine's overall layout.
[F02B 29/045] Constructional details of the heat exchangers, e.g. pipes, plates, ribs, insulation, materials, or manufacturing and assembly -- This subclass covers the design and construction of heat exchangers used in internal-combustion piston engines with special charging or scavenging arrangements, including the choice of pipes, plates, ribs, insulation, materials and methods of manufacturing and assembly. It addresses structural aspects rather than thermal performance.
[F02B 29/0456] Air cooled heat exchangers -- F02B 29/0456 covers air-cooled heat exchangers employed in internal-combustion piston engines that have special charging or scavenging arrangements not covered elsewhere. These devices remove heat from engine components using ambient air flow rather than liquid coolant, enhancing temperature control in such engines.
[F02B 29/0462] Liquid cooled heat exchangers -- In the CPC scheme the code F02B 29/0462 covers liquid-cooled heat exchangers that are part of internal-combustion piston engines which have charging or scavenging arrangements not covered by other groups. These devices transfer heat from the engine coolant to a liquid medium, typically for temperature control in specialized engine designs.
[F02B 29/0468] Water separation or drainage means -- The F02B 29/0468 entry covers water-separation or drainage devices used in internal-combustion piston engines that have special charging or scavenging arrangements not covered by other groups. It pertains to mechanisms that remove condensate or sprayed water from the intake or combustion chambers to prevent performance loss or damage.
[F02B 29/0475] the intake air cooler being combined with another device, e.g. heater, valve, compressor, filter or EGR cooler, or being assembled on a special engine location
[F02B 29/0481] Intake air cooling by means others than heat exchangers, e.g. by rotating drum regenerators, cooling by expansion or by electrical means -- This subclass of internal-combustion piston engines covers methods of cooling the intake air that do not employ conventional heat exchangers, such as rotating-drum regenerators, cooling by expansion, or electrical cooling techniques. It is used for engines with special charging or scavenging provisions.
[F02B 29/0493] Controlling the air charge temperature -- F02B 29/0493 covers methods or apparatus for regulating the temperature of the inlet air in internal-combustion piston engines that have special charging or scavenging arrangements not covered in other groups. It includes heating, cooling or pre-conditioning of the charge to improve performance, emissions or durability.
[F02B 29/06] After-charging, i.e. supplementary charging after scavenging -- F02B 29/06 pertains to internal-combustion piston engines that incorporate a provision for after-charging, i.e., a supplementary charge of fuel or air introduced after the scavenging phase. It covers mechanisms and methods used to supply additional charge to improve combustion efficiency or power output.
[F02B 29/08] Modifying distribution valve timing for charging purposes -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - After-charging, i.e. supplementary charging after scavenging -> F02B29/06
[F02B 29/083] Cyclically operated valves disposed upstream of the cylinder intake valve, controlled by external means -- The code F02B 29/083 covers piston engines in which a valve placed upstream of the cylinder intake valve is operated cyclically by an external mechanism to manage charging or scavenging functions.
