Such arrangements are used to improve airflow timing or to enable special intake strategies not covered by other groups.
[F02B 29/086] the engine having two or more inlet valves -- F02B 29/086 pertains to internal-combustion piston engines that incorporate two or more inlet valves, enabling additional pathways for charging or scavenging beyond the basic single-valve arrangements. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and internal-combustion piston engines.
[F02B 3/00] Engines characterised by air compression and subsequent fuel addition -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines characterised by air compression and subsequent fuel addition. E.g. diesel engines, but this group also covers engines with air compression and subsequent gasoline fuel addition as in gasoline direct injection.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Characterised by both fuel-air mixture compression and air compression, or characterised by both positive ignition and compression ignition -> F02B11/00
[F02B 3/02] with positive ignition -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Air compressing engines with spark ignition.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - With non-timed positive ignition -> F02B9/06
[F02B 3/04] Methods of operating -- F02B 3/04 concerns the methods of operating internal-combustion piston engines in which air is compressed before fuel is introduced. It includes control strategies, timing, load management and techniques for starting, throttling and shutting down such engines.
[F02B 3/06] with compression ignition -- **Definition:** This place covers:  In short: The Diesel engine. Preferably only deviations of the Diesel engine should be classified here.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Engines with positive injection -> F02B13/02
[F02B 3/08] Methods of operating -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods of operating which would be a deviation from the "normal" diesel cycle. Usually, the method of operating is already embodied in the engine itself.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Methods of operating compression ignition engines with intermittent fuel introduction -> F02B3/12
[F02B 3/10] with intermittent fuel introduction -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Compression ignition engines where more than one injection per cycle or very closely spaced multiple injections are introduced.
[F02B 31/00] Modifying induction systems for imparting a rotation to the charge in the cylinder -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Modifications of the induction system of internal combustion engines to improve the mixing and distribution of air and fuel in the cylinder. The group covers modifications to the intake passages up until the intake port of the cylinder of the engine.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Air induction systems structural features -> F02M35/10  **Glossary:** - Swirl: Rotation around cylinder axis - Tumble: Rotation around transverse cylinder axis
[F02B 31/02] in engines having inlet valves arranged eccentrically to cylinder axis -- F02B 31/02 covers modifications to the induction system of internal-combustion piston engines that create a swirl or rotation of the air-fuel charge within the cylinder, specifically for engines whose inlet valves are positioned off-centre relative to the cylinder axis. This classification captures designs such as offset valve placement, shaped ports, or swirling devices that improve mixture homogenisation and combustion efficiency.
[F02B 31/04] by means within the induction channel, e.g. deflectors -- F02B 31/04 covers features placed inside the induction channel of an internal-combustion piston engine that create a swirl of the incoming charge, such as flow-deflecting vanes or guides. These devices are used to improve mixture homogeneity and combustion efficiency by imparting rotation before the mixture enters the cylinder.
[F02B 31/042] induction channel having a helical shape around the intake valve axis -- The code F02B 31/042 covers intake passages that are formed with a helical geometry wrapping around the axis of an intake valve, causing the incoming air-fuel charge to rotate as it enters the cylinder. This swirling motion promotes better mixture homogeneity and more efficient combustion.
[F02B 31/06] Movable means, e.g. butterfly valves -- F02B 31/06 covers movable components, for example butterfly valves, incorporated in the induction system of internal-combustion piston engines to impart a rotation to the charge within the cylinder.
[F02B 31/08] having multiple air inlets -- F02B 31/08 covers internal-combustion piston engines whose induction system uses more than one air inlet to create a swirling motion of the intake charge within the cylinder. The multiple inlet design imparts rotation to improve mixing and combustion efficiency.
[F02B 31/085] having two inlet valves -- This subclass (F02B 31/085) addresses internal-combustion piston engines that employ two inlet valves to create a rotating or swirling motion of the intake charge within the cylinder, improving mixture formation and combustion performance. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → internal-combustion piston engines and concerns modifications to the induction system for charge rotation.
[F02B 31/087] having three or more inlet valves -- This CPC subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines that use three or more inlet valves per cylinder to improve the induction of the air-fuel mixture. By increasing the number of intake valves, the design enhances swirling or rotational motion of the charge, aiding mixing and combustion efficiency.
[F02B 3/12] Methods of operating -- F02B 3/12 covers methods of operating internal-combustion piston engines that compress air before fuel injection, including control strategies for start-up, speed regulation, load management and fuel-air mixture adjustment.
[F02B 33/00] Engines characterised by provision of driven charging or scavenging pumps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines characterised by provision of pumps for charging or scavenging.
[F02B 33/02] Engines with reciprocating-piston pumps -- F02B 33/02 pertains to internal-combustion piston engines that incorporate driven reciprocating-piston pumps for charging or scavenging the cylinders, using the engine's own motion to supply fresh charge or expel exhaust gases.

[F02B 33/12] the rear face of working piston acting as pumping member and co-operating with a pumping chamber isolated from crankcase, the connecting-rod passing through the chamber and co-operating with movable isolating member -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:

It covers designs where the pump pistons are not synchronized with the power pistons, allowing distinct movement patterns for combustion and gas-handling functions.

The pumps may be of gear, vane, or rotary type and are integrated in the crankcase to move charge air or exhaust gases. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, internal-combustion piston engines.

[F02B 33/443] Heating of charging air, e.g. for facilitating the starting -- F02B 33/443 deals with methods for heating the air supplied to internal-combustion piston engines, especially to improve cold-start performance. It includes systems that pre-heat charging air using external heat sources, exhaust gases, or electrically driven heaters to facilitate engine starting.
[F02B 33/446] having valves for admission of atmospheric air to engine, e.g. at starting -- F02B 33/446, within the mechanical engineering, combustion engines, internal-combustion piston engines hierarchy, covers piston engines that include valves for admitting atmospheric air, typically used during engine start-up. The valves provide a means to charge the cylinder with air when external charging or scavenging pumps are not operating.

[F02B 37/00] Engines characterised by provision of pumps driven at least for part of the time by exhaust -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines with turbocharging.
[F02B 37/001] using exhaust drives arranged in parallel -- F02B 37/001 relates to internal-combustion piston engines that employ one or more pumps powered partly by exhaust-gas energy, with the exhaust-driven pumps configured in parallel so each pump receives exhaust flow independently rather than in series. This arrangement improves pumping efficiency and assists engine performance.
[F02B 37/002] the exhaust supply to one of the exhaust drives can be interrupted -- This subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines in which the exhaust gas flow to an exhaust-driven pump or turbine can be selectively cut off, allowing the exhaust-driven device to be stopped or throttled while the engine continues running.
[F02B 37/004] with exhaust drives arranged in series -- This subclass refers to internal-combustion piston engines that use exhaust-driven pumps, with the pumps linked one after another so the output of one drive feeds the next in a series arrangement, commonly for supercharging, scavenging or auxiliary lubrication functions.
[F02B 37/005] Exhaust driven pumps being combined with an exhaust driven auxiliary apparatus, e.g. a ventilator -- F02B 37/005 covers internal-combustion piston engines in which a pump is powered at least partly by exhaust gas flow and is integrated with an additional exhaust-driven device, such as a ventilator or other auxiliary apparatus. The classification addresses the combined operation of these exhaust-driven components within the engine system.
[F02B 37/007] with exhaust-driven pumps arranged in parallel -- **Definition:** This place covers:  with exhaust-driven pumps arranged in parallel. E.g. at least one parallel pump being driven alternatively.
[F02B 37/013] with exhaust-driven pumps arranged in series -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[F02B 37/02] Gas passages between engine outlet and pump drive, e.g. reservoirs -- F02B 37/02 covers internal-combustion piston engines that employ exhaust-driven pumps and includes arrangements where a gas passage connects the engine's exhaust outlet to the pump drive, often through a reservoir, to stabilise pressure or enhance pump operation.
[F02B 37/025] Multiple scrolls or multiple gas passages guiding the gas to the pump drive -- This subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines where exhaust gas drives a pump and specifically relates to designs that use multiple scrolls or several gas passages to direct the exhaust flow to the pump drive, improving efficiency and durability.
[F02B 37/04] Engines with exhaust drive and other drive of pumps, e.g. with exhaust-driven pump and mechanically-driven second pump -- This CPC subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines where the exhaust gas drives a pump and a second pump is powered mechanically, such as by a crankshaft, to supplement functions like lubrication or fuel delivery. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, internal-combustion piston engines. Code F02B 37/04.
[F02B 37/10] at least one pump being alternatively driven by exhaust and other drive, -- These are internal-combustion piston engines in which at least one auxiliary pump, such as an oil or fuel pump, is powered alternately by exhaust-gas energy and by a separate mechanical drive.
[F02B 37/105] exhaust drive and pump being both connected through gearing to engine-driven shaft -- F02B 37/105 concerns piston engines where an exhaust-driven pump and an engine-driven pump are both linked to the same engine shaft by gearing, allowing the pump to receive power at least partially from exhaust energy.
[F02B 37/11] driven by other drive at starting only -- F02B 37/11 covers internal-combustion piston engines in which a pump is powered by exhaust gases for most of the operation, but a separate drive mechanism-such as an electric motor or starter-gear-is employed solely to start the engine before the exhaust-driven system takes over. This subclass distinguishes engines that rely on an auxiliary starting drive rather than continuous external propulsion.
[F02B 37/12] Control of the pumps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of turbocharging of engines. These groups cover also electronic control of turbochargers.
[F02B 37/14] of the alternation between exhaust drive and other drive of a pump, e.g. dependent on speed -- This CPC subclass F02B 37/14 covers piston engines in which a pump is intermittently driven by exhaust energy and at other times by an alternative source, the drive mode alternating according to engine speed.

[F02B 37/164] the bypassed air being used in an auxiliary apparatus, e.g. in an air turbine -- A subclass of internal-combustion piston engines in which a pump is driven partly by exhaust gases and the bypassed air is diverted to power an auxiliary device such as an air turbine. This arrangement captures otherwise wasted exhaust-driven airflow to perform useful work, improving overall engine efficiency.
[F02B 37/166] the auxiliary apparatus being a combustion chamber, e.g. upstream of turbine -- This subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines that use exhaust-driven pumps to operate auxiliary equipment, specifically a combustion chamber placed before the turbine. The auxiliary chamber receives fresh charge or recirculated gases to improve power output, efficiency or emissions control.

[F02B 37/183] Arrangements of bypass valves or actuators therefor -- The entry F02B 37/183 covers the design and layout of bypass valves and the mechanisms that operate them in internal-combustion piston engines that use exhaust-driven pumps. It includes any arrangement that controls the flow of gases or fluids around components such as turbochargers, superchargers or exhaust gas recirculation systems.
[F02B 37/186] Arrangements of actuators or linkage for bypass valves -- The subclass covers the design of mechanisms that operate bypass valves in internal-combustion piston engines where pumps are driven at least partially by exhaust gas. It includes the layout and interconnection of actuators, linkage and related components that control the opening and closing of the bypass valve.
[F02B 37/20] by increasing exhaust energy, e.g. using combustion chamber -- **Definition:** This place covers:  by increasing exhaust energy, e.g. using combustion chamber upstream of turbine or injecting water.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Using an auxiliary combustion chamber supplied by charging air -> F02B37/166

[F02B 37/225] air passages -- F02B 37/225 covers the design and arrangement of air passages within internal-combustion piston engines that use exhaust-driven pumps, such as those supplying scavenging or intake air. It includes ducts, manifolds and channels that convey air to or from the combustion chambers.
[F02B 37/24] by using pumps or turbines with adjustable guide vanes -- The CPC subclass F02B 37/24 falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → internal-combustion piston engines and concerns engines that employ exhaust-driven pumps or turbines whose flow is controlled by adjustable guide vanes, typically to enhance scavenging, supercharging or variable boost control.

[F02B 39/06] the engine torque being divided by a differential gear for driving a pump and the engine output shaft -- F02B 39/06 covers internal-combustion piston engines in which a differential gear divides the engine's torque between the main output shaft and a driven pump, enabling simultaneous power delivery to both. It falls under component parts and accessories for charging or scavenging pumps that are not classified elsewhere in the mechanical-engineering hierarchy.
[F02B 39/08] Non-mechanical drives, e.g. fluid drives having variable gear ratio -- This subclass concerns charging or scavenging pumps in internal-combustion piston engines that are powered by non-mechanical means, for example fluid-driven or hydraulic drives whose transmission ratio can vary. It excludes conventional mechanical drive arrangements.
[F02B 39/085] the fluid drive using expansion of fluids other than exhaust gases, e.g. a Rankine cycle -- F02B 39/085 covers fluid-drive systems for piston engines that utilise the expansion of a working fluid other than the engine's exhaust gases, such as a Rankine-cycle loop, to power charging or scavenging pumps not classified elsewhere.

[F02B 41/00] Engines characterised by special means for improving conversion of heat or pressure energy into mechanical power -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines characterised by special means for improving conversion of heat or pressure energy into mechanical power, e.g. modification of compression ratio to enhance thermal efficiency or by using "turbocompound" i.e. using the exhaust to drive a turbine connected to the crankshaft.
[F02B 41/02] Engines with prolonged expansion -- **Glossary:** - Prolonged expansion: relative to the compression - Modified dwell: The piston remains longer than usual at TOP DEAD CENTRE - Miller cycle: A modified four stroke cycle where the compression stroke is made shorter by blow back through late inlet valve closing, for turbo charged engines - Atkinson cycle: the same as Miller cycle, but for normally aspirated engines
[F02B 41/04] in main cylinders -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines with means and ways to make the compression stroke and the expansion stroke different.  E.g. engines using the Miller or Atkins cycles or using mechanical means in order to have special stroke of piston.
[F02B 41/06] in compound cylinders -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines with more than one expansion stroke, i.e. the exhaust is allowed to expand in an additional cylinder. The compounding is similar to the compounding in a steam engine.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  Valves 23 and 24 allow expansion of exhaust gases to adjacent cylinder through channels 21 and 22  1a.  1b.
[F02B 41/08] Two-stroke compound engines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Two stroke engines with second expansion in an expansion cylinder.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:

[F02B 43/00] Engines operating on non-liquid fuels -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Engines characterised by the gas-air charge being ignited by compression ignition of an additional fuel -> F02B7/06 - Engines convertible from gas to other fuel consumption -> F02B69/04  **Glossary:** - LNG: Liquefied Natural Gas. It behaves like an inert gas. It has a high critical compression ratio. Therefore, only used with spark ignition or pilot fuel ignition - LPG: Liquefied Petroleum Gas - CNG: Compressed Natural Gas - DME: Dimethyl ether
[F02B 43/02] Engines characterised by means for increasing operating efficiency -- The F02B 43/02 subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines that run on solid or gaseous fuels and includes any features that improve their efficiency, such as turbo- or super-charging, exhaust-gas recirculation, variable valve timing, or advanced fuel-air mixing systems. These measures are intended to increase power output, reduce fuel consumption, or lower emissions while the engine operates on non-liquid fuels.
[F02B 43/04] for improving efficiency of combustion -- The CPC subclass F02B 43/04 covers inventions that improve the efficiency of combustion in internal-combustion piston engines that operate on non-liquid fuels such as gases, gas mixtures or solid fuels, using methods, device modifications or control strategies.
[F02B 43/06] for enlarging charge -- F02B 43/06 relates to internal-combustion piston engines that use non-liquid fuels and includes features for increasing the amount of the fuel-air charge introduced into the cylinder, such as larger intake passages, expanded combustion chambers or auxiliary charging devices that enlarge the charge.
[F02B 43/08] Plants characterised by the engines using gaseous fuel generated in the plant from solid fuel, e.g. wood -- This subclass covers power plants whose engines run on a gas generated on-site from solid fuels such as wood. It includes systems that gasify the solid fuel to produce a gaseous fuel for the internal-combustion piston engine, integrating fuel conversion and engine operation within the same installation.
[F02B 43/10] Engines or plants characterised by use of other specific gases, e.g. acetylene, oxyhydrogen -- This subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines and related plants that are designed to operate on specific gaseous fuels other than conventional liquids, such as acetylene or oxyhydrogen mixtures, emphasizing their distinct combustion methods and equipment arrangements.
[F02B 43/12] Methods of operating -- **Definition:** This place covers:  methods of operating such engines.
[F02B 45/00] Engines characterised by operating on non-liquid fuels other than gas -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines operating on non-liquid fuels other than gas, such as solid or semi-solid fuels.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Plants involving generation of gaseous fuels from solid fuel -> F02B43/08 - Engines convertible from gas to other fuel consumption -> F02B69/04
[F02B 45/02] operating on powdered fuel, e.g. powdered coal -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines operating on powdered fuel, e.g. powdered coal.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Operating on fuel containing oxidant -> F02B45/06
[F02B 45/04] Plants, e.g. having coal-grinding apparatus -- F02B 45/04 covers installations for internal-combustion piston engines that run on solid fuels other than gas, specifically plants equipped with apparatus for grinding coal to supply the engine.
[F02B 45/06] operating on fuel containing oxidant -- F02B 45/06 designates internal-combustion piston engines that run on fuels which already include an oxidising agent, such as solid or liquid monopropellants, rather than using external air. It falls under the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering - Combustion Engines - Internal-Combustion Piston Engines - Engines characterised by operating on non-liquid fuels other than gas.
[F02B 45/08] operating on other solid fuels -- F02B 45/08 covers internal-combustion piston engines that are designed to run on solid fuels other than liquid or gaseous fuels, such as coal, wood, or other particulate fuels. It is a subcategory of combustion engines within the mechanical-engineering field, specifically addressing engines that operate on non-liquid, non-gas solid fuels.
[F02B 45/10] operating on mixtures of liquid and non-liquid fuels, e.g. in pasty or foamed state -- F02B 45/10 refers to internal-combustion piston engines that operate on mixtures of liquid and non-liquid fuels, such as pasty or foamed blends. The classification covers designs where such fuel combinations are supplied to the combustion chamber to modify combustion behavior or enable alternative fuel use.
[F02B 47/00] Methods of operating engines involving specific pre-treating of, or adding specific substances to, combustion air, fuel or fuel-air mixture of the engines, and not otherwise provided for -- This subclass concerns methods of running internal-combustion piston engines in which the intake air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture is pre-treated or has specific substances added, where such measures are not covered by other categories. It includes techniques such as air enrichment, fuel additives, or recirculation applied before combustion.
[F02B 47/02] the substances being water or steam -- This subclass covers methods of operating internal-combustion piston engines in which water or steam is introduced into the combustion air, fuel or fuel-air mixture to modify combustion characteristics, such as cooling, power boosting or emission reduction, and includes water-injection and steam-injection techniques.
[F02B 47/04] the substances being other than water or steam only -- F02B 47/04 classifies methods for running internal-combustion piston engines in which substances other than water or steam are introduced into the intake air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture. The entry covers the use of additives, gases, vapours or other compounds to modify combustion characteristics, improve performance, reduce emissions, or protect engine components.
[F02B 47/06] the substances including non-airborne oxygen -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The added substance including non-airborne oxygen, e.g. N 2 O or laughing gas. [F02B 47/08] the substances including exhaust gas -- This subclass covers methods for operating internal-combustion piston engines in which substances, including recirculated exhaust gases, are added to the intake air, fuel or fuel-air mixture. It includes techniques such as exhaust-gas recirculation, lean-burn control, or other pre-treatment of the combustion charge.
[F02B 47/10] Circulation of exhaust gas in closed or semi-closed circuits, e.g. with simultaneous addition of oxygen -- The subclass F02B 47/10 covers techniques for routing exhaust gases through closed or semi-closed circuits in internal-combustion piston engines, often combined with the simultaneous addition of oxygen to the intake stream. These methods aim to modify the combustion process by pre-treating the exhaust-air mixture.
[F02B 49/00] Methods of operating air-compressing compression-ignition engines involving introduction of small quantities of fuel in the form of a fine mist into the air in the engine's intake -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods of operating air-compressing compression-ignition engines involving introduction of small quantities of fuel in the form of a fine mist into the air in the engine's intake, for example. as a starting aid. [F02B 5/00] Engines characterised by positive ignition -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines characterised by positive ignition.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Engines characterised by fuel-air mixture compression with positive ignition -> F02B1/02 - Engines characterised by air compression and subsequent fuel addition with positive ignition -> F02B3/02 - Engines with non-timed positive ignition, e.g. with hot-spots -> F02B9/06 - Engines characterised by both fuel-air mixture compression and air compression, or characterised by both positive ignition and compression ignition -> F02B11/00
[F02B 5/02] Methods of operating -- F02B 5/02 covers methods of operating internal-combustion piston engines that use positive ignition, such as spark-ignited gasoline engines. It includes procedures for starting, controlling speed, load management, fuel-air mixture regulation, ignition timing and other operational strategies aimed at optimizing performance, efficiency and emissions.
[F02B 51/00] Other methods of operating engines involving pretreating of, or adding substances to, combustion air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture of the engines -- F02B 51/00 relates to methods of operating internal-combustion piston engines that involve pretreating or adding substances to the intake air, fuel, or their mixture. It covers techniques such as fuel additives, air enrichment, exhaust-gas recirculation, or other treatments aimed at improving performance, emissions or reliability.
[F02B 51/02] involving catalysts -- F02B 51/02 covers methods for operating internal-combustion piston engines in which a catalyst is introduced to the intake air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture to modify combustion. The technique is used to improve efficiency, reduce emissions, or control ignition by chemically treating the combustion process.
[F02B 51/04] involving electricity or magnetism -- This subgroup covers techniques for influencing the combustion process in internal-combustion piston engines by applying electrical or magnetic fields to the intake air, fuel or their mixture. It includes methods such as plasma ignition, electromagnetic fuel atomisation, or magnetic-field-enhanced combustion.
[F02B 51/06] involving rays or sound waves -- F02B 51/06 covers internal-combustion piston engines in which the combustion air, fuel or their mixture is pretreated or modified by means of rays, such as laser or electromagnetic radiation, or by sound waves, to influence ignition, combustion efficiency, emissions or performance. This subclass falls under methods of operating engines that add or alter substances in the intake or fuel stream.
[F02B 53/00] Internal-combustion aspects of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Internal-combustion aspects of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Internal combustion aspects of rotary pistons or outer members of co-operating therewith -> F02B55/00
[F02B 53/02] Methods of operating -- F02B 53/02 covers the operational procedures for rotary-piston or oscillating-piston internal-combustion engines, including timing, fuel injection, ignition control, and load management. It addresses how these engines are started, run, and shut down to optimize performance, efficiency, and emissions.
[F02B 53/04] Charge admission or combustion-gas discharge -- F02B 53/04 pertains to the means for introducing the fuel-air mixture into, or exhausting combustion gases from, rotary-piston or oscillating-piston internal-combustion engines. It includes designs for inlet and outlet ports, timing mechanisms, valve arrangements and any control devices that regulate charge admission or gas discharge during engine operation.
[F02B 53/06] Valve control therefor -- F02B 53/06 covers systems and methods for operating and timing valves in rotary or oscillating piston internal-combustion engines. It includes devices that open, close, or adjust valve position to control fuel intake, exhaust, or compression, as well as the associated control mechanisms such as cam, hydraulic, pneumatic or electronic actuation.
[F02B 53/08] Charging, e.g. by means of rotary-piston pump -- The CPC subclass F02B 53/08 pertains to the charging phase of internal-combustion piston engines that employ rotary or oscillating pistons, focusing on methods of introducing the working fluid, such as air or fuel-air mixtures, by means of a rotary-piston pump. This entry falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, internal-combustion piston engines.
[F02B 53/10] Fuel supply -- F02B 53/10 pertains to the fuel-supply arrangements for rotary-piston or oscillating-piston internal-combustion engines, covering devices and methods that store, meter, transport and inject fuel into the combustion chambers, as well as the control systems that regulate fuel flow for these engine types.
[F02B 53/12] Ignition -- The CPC entry F02B 53/12 covers ignition mechanisms for rotary-piston or oscillating-piston internal-combustion engines, including spark generation, timing control and associated components that initiate combustion in these engine types.
[F02B 53/14] Adaptations of engines for driving, or engine combinations with, other devices -- The F02B 53/14 class covers adaptations of internal-combustion piston engines-particularly rotary-piston or oscillating-piston types-so that they drive other devices or are combined with auxiliary machines, including hybrid or dual-function arrangements used for power transmission or mechanical assistance.
[F02B 55/00] Internal-combustion aspects of rotary pistons -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Internal combustion aspects of rotary pistons or outer members for co-operation with rotary pistons.
[F02B 55/02] Pistons -- F02B 55/02 covers pistons used in internal-combustion piston engines, focusing on their design, materials, motion and sealing within rotary mechanisms. It includes features such as cooling passages, ring arrangements and methods to reduce friction and wear.
[F02B 55/04] Cooling thereof -- The code F02B 55/04 covers methods and devices for cooling rotary pistons in internal-combustion piston engines, including techniques that remove heat from the piston surfaces or internal chambers to maintain temperature limits during operation. It falls under the broader classes of mechanical engineering and combustion-engine technology.
[F02B 55/06] by air or other gas -- F02B 55/06 refers to internal-combustion piston engines where the working charge is supplied by air or another gas, typically describing systems that introduce the gas directly into the combustion chamber or cylinder. It covers mechanisms for charging, mixing or delivering the gas to the rotary pistons, including air-intake ducts, blow-by arrangements and related control devices. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, internal-combustion piston engines, specifically rotary piston aspects.
[F02B 55/08] Outer members for co-operation with rotary pistons
[F02B 55/10] Cooling thereof -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:
[F02B 55/12] by air or other gas -- The F02B 55/12 subgroup covers internal-combustion piston engines in which the working fluid is air or another gas, addressing the charging, ignition and combustion processes that use gaseous fuels rather than liquid fuels. It relates to rotary piston arrangements and the specific methods of introducing and burning the gas within the engine.
[F02B 55/14] Shapes or constructions of combustion chambers -- F02B 55/14 concerns the geometric design and structural features of combustion chambers in internal-combustion piston engines. It covers various shapes and configurations intended to influence flame propagation, fuel-air mixing, and overall efficiency.
[F02B 55/16] Admission or exhaust passages in pistons or outer members -- The code F02B 55/16 covers features of admission or exhaust passages that are incorporated into pistons themselves or into surrounding components of internal-combustion piston engines, particularly those with rotary piston designs. It addresses how gases enter or leave the combustion chamber via channels cut or formed in the moving piston or its adjacent parts. This classification is used for innovations involving the geometry, sealing or flow-control of such passages.

[F02B 63/00] Adaptations of engines for driving pumps, hand-held tools or electric generators -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Adaptations of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines -> F02B53/14
[F02B 63/02] for hand-held tools -- F02B 63/02 covers internal-combustion piston engines that have been adapted to power hand-held tools such as drills, saws or sanders. The classification focuses on design features that make the engine portable, lightweight and suitable for direct integration into handheld equipment. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and adaptations of engines for driving pumps, handheld tools, or electric generators.
[F02B 63/04] for electric generators -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[F02B 63/041] Linear electric generators -- Linear electric generators are devices that convert the reciprocating motion of a piston or other linear drive directly into electrical energy without intermediate rotary conversion, often used when adapting internal-combustion engines for pumps, hand-held tools or auxiliary power generation. The classification covers designs, configurations and operating principles of such generators, including magnetic, electrodynamic and piezoelectric implementations that produce electricity from linear motion.
[F02B 63/042] Rotating electric generators -- F02B 63/042 covers rotating electric generators driven by internal-combustion piston engines, including adaptations of these engines for power generation in pumps, hand-held tools or similar applications. The entry sits within mechanical engineering, combustion engines and internal-combustion piston engines.
[F02B 63/043] Electric generators using oscillating movement -- F02B 63/043 covers electric generators that derive power from oscillating motion, such as the reciprocal movement of a piston in internal-combustion engines adapted to drive generators or similar equipment. The class includes designs where the linear or back-and-forth motion is directly converted into electrical energy rather than via a rotating shaft.
[F02B 63/044] the engine-generator unit being placed on a frame or in an housing -- F02B 63/044 covers internal-combustion piston engines that are permanently coupled to an electric generator and installed as a single unit on a frame or within a protective housing. The classification applies to engine-generator assemblies used to drive pumps, hand-held tools or other equipment, where the engine and generator are integrated for compact, ready-to-use operation.
[F02B 63/047] Movable engine-generator combinations on wheels -- F02B 63/047 covers self-propelled units that combine an internal-combustion piston engine with an electric generator and are mounted on wheels for easy transport, such as mobile power packs, pump-driven engines or handheld-tool generators.
[F02B 63/048] Portable engine-generator combinations -- This CPC subclass F02B 63/048 covers portable engine-generator combinations where the internal-combustion piston engine is adapted to directly drive a generator for electricity, typically used in mobile or hand-held power tools.
[F02B 63/06] for pumps -- F02B 63/06 refers to internal-combustion piston engines that are specifically adapted to drive pumps. It covers designs where the engine's mechanical power is used directly for pumping applications such as water, oil or other fluid transfer.
[F02B 65/00] Adaptations of engines for special uses not provided for in groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Adaptations of engines for special uses not provided for in groups F02B61/00 or F02B63/00 ; Combinations of engines with other devices, e.g. with non-driven apparatus. For example, internal combustion engines where an engine cylinder may act as an air compressor.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Adaptations of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston engines -> F02B53/14 - Combinations of prime-movers consisting of electric motors and internal combustion engines for mutual or common propulsion -> B60K6/20
[F02B 67/00] Engines with pertinent characteristics other than those provided for in or of interest apart from, preceding main groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines characterised by the arrangement of auxiliary apparatuses not being otherwise provided for, e.g. the apparatus having different functions; Driving auxiliary apparatus from engines, not otherwise provided for.
[F02B 67/04] of mechanically-driven auxiliary apparatus -- F02B 67/04 covers mechanically driven auxiliary apparatus for internal-combustion piston engines that are not addressed in the main groups, such as oil pumps, water pumps, compressors and other devices powered directly from the engine's crankshaft or camshaft.
[F02B 67/06] driven by means of chains, belts, or like endless members -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Auxiliary devices driven by means of chains, belts, or like endless members.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:

[F02B 69/00] Internal-combustion engines convertible into other combustion-engine type, not provided for in -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Internal combustion engines convertible into other combustion-engine type not provided for in F02B11/00 ; Internal-combustion engines of different types characterised by constructions facilitating use of same main engine-parts in different types.  For example, engines convertible from two stroke to four stroke, or convertible for use with different fuels.
[F02B 69/02] for different fuel types, other than engines indifferent to fuel consumed, e.g. convertible from light to heavy fuel -- This subclass covers internal-combustion piston engines that can be adapted or converted to operate on fuel types other than those for which they were originally designed, when such conversion is not covered by other subclasses. Typical examples include engines that can be switched from light fuels to heavy fuels.
[F02B 69/04] for gaseous and non-gaseous fuels -- The classification F02B 69/04 covers internal-combustion piston engines that can be converted to run on different types of fuel, including both gaseous and liquid fuels not otherwise specified. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, internal-combustion piston engines.
[F02B 69/06] for different cycles, e.g. convertible from two-stroke to four stroke -- The subclass F02B 69/06 refers to internal-combustion piston engines that can be altered to operate on different cycles, for example a design that allows conversion between two-stroke and four-stroke operation. It is listed under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → internal-combustion piston engines.
[F02B 7/00] Engines characterised by the fuel-air charge being ignited by compression ignition of an additional fuel -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines characterised by the fuel-air charge being ignited by compression ignition of an additional fuel. E.g. a compressed fuel-air mixture being ignited by directly injecting a diesel fuel.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Engines characterised by both fuel-air mixture compression and air compression, or characterised by both positive ignition and compression ignition -> F02B11/00  **Glossary:** - Pilot injection: The injection of an additional fuel for ignition of a compressed fuel-air mixture
[F02B 7/02] the fuel in the charge being liquid -- Internal-combustion piston engines in which the fuel-air mixture contains liquid fuel, such as diesel or heavy oil, are classified under F02B 7/02. This subgroup addresses engines where the charge's fuel component is delivered in liquid form prior to compression ignition.
[F02B 7/04] Methods of operating -- F02B 7/04 covers methods of operating internal-combustion piston engines in which the fuel-air mixture is ignited by compression ignition of an added fuel, i.e., diesel-type engines. The subclass includes control techniques, timing of fuel injection, load regulation and other operational strategies for these engines.
[F02B 7/06] the fuel in the charge being gaseous -- F02B 7/06 classifies piston engines of the compression-ignition type in which the fuel present in the fuel-air charge is a gas rather than a liquid, covering engines that run on gaseous fuels such as natural gas, propane or hydrogen. This subgroup falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and internal-combustion piston engines.
[F02B 7/08] Methods of operating -- This CPC subclass concerns operating methods for compression-ignition piston engines, where the fuel-air mixture is ignited by the heat generated during compression. It includes control strategies, timing, injection and load regulation.
[F02B 71/00] Free-piston engines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[F02B 71/02] Starting -- F02B 71/02 covers starting techniques for free-piston internal-combustion engines. It includes methods and devices that initiate piston motion using external energy, pre-compression, or electrical ignition, without a conventional crankshaft. The class is nested under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, internal-combustion piston engines, and free-piston engines.
[F02B 71/04] Adaptations of such engines for special use -- F02B 71/04 pertains to modifications of free-piston internal-combustion engines that are made for specific purposes or operating environments, such as specialized fuel delivery, cooling, or integration into particular machinery. The subclass captures adaptations that tailor the engine's performance, durability, or configuration to meet unique functional requirements.
[F02B 71/045] with hydrostatic transmission -- F02B 71/045 designates free-piston internal-combustion engines that employ a hydrostatic transmission, using pressurised fluid to convey power from piston motion to a drivetrain or auxiliary system. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and internal-combustion piston engines.
[F02B 71/06] Free-piston combustion gas generators -- Free-piston combustion gas generators are devices that produce high-pressure gas by combusting fuel in a piston that moves without a crankshaft, directly driving a load such as a turbine or compressor. They fall under the internal-combustion piston-engine branch of mechanical engineering.

[F02B 75/021] having six or more strokes per cycle -- F02B 75/021 designates piston engines that execute six or more strokes in each operating cycle, adding extra intake, compression, power, or exhaust phases beyond the conventional four-stroke process. Such multi-stroke cycles are used to enhance thermodynamic efficiency, power density, or emissions performance in specialized engine designs.
[F02B 75/04] Engines with variable distances between pistons at top dead-centre positions and cylinder heads -- The classification pertains to internal-combustion piston engines that incorporate a mechanism allowing the clearance between the piston at top dead centre and the cylinder head to be altered during operation, typically to change compression ratio or improve performance under different loads.

[F02B 75/042] the cylinderhead comprising a counter-piston -- F02B 75/042 covers cylinder heads that incorporate a counter-piston, a secondary piston located within the head that moves opposite the main piston to modulate charge motion, improve scavenging or increase compression. The feature is used in internal-combustion piston engines to enhance performance or reduce emissions.

These means smooth the power delivery, reduce fluctuations and improve engine performance and durability.

[F02B 75/12] Other methods of operation -- F02B 75/12 designates patents that disclose alternative ways of running internal-combustion piston engines, beyond conventional steady-state operation. [F02B 75/16] Engines characterised by number of cylinders, e.g. single-cylinder engines -- This subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification covers internal-combustion piston engines that are defined primarily by the count of their cylinders, including single-cylinder and multi-cylinder configurations, and relates to design considerations, performance, and applications tied to cylinder number. It is used to locate patents that focus on cylinder-number-specific innovations such as balancing, cooling, and power output.

[F02B 77/086] Sensor arrangements in the exhaust, e.g. for temperature, misfire, air/fuel ratio, oxygen sensors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sensor arrangements in the exhaust, e.g. for temperature, misfire, air/fuel ratio, oxygen sensors.
