[F02B 77/087] determining top dead centre or ignition-timing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Safety, indicating or supervising devices determining top dead centre or ignition-timing
[F02B 77/088] relating to tightness -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Safety, indicating or supervising devices relating to tightness.
[F02B 77/089] relating to engine temperature  concerning coolant temperature  F01P11/16 -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Indicating and safety devices concerning coolant temperature -> F01P11/16
[F02B 77/10] Safety means relating to crankcase explosions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Safety means relating to crankcase explosions, e.g. explosion relief valves or shrapnel containment arrangements.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[F02B 77/11] Thermal or acoustic insulation -- F02B 77/11 covers thermal or acoustic insulation used on component parts, details or accessories of internal-combustion piston engines that are not otherwise specified. It pertains to measures that reduce heat loss or noise transmission in engine assemblies.
[F02B 77/13] Acoustic insulation -- F02B 77/13 pertains to acoustic insulation for internal-combustion piston engines, covering sound-absorbing components, liners, blankets or housings that reduce engine noise and vibration. This classification falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and component parts not otherwise specified.
[F02B 77/14] Engine-driven auxiliary devices combined into units -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engine-driven auxiliary devices combined into units, e.g. a water pump and an alternator combined in one unit.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  2.
[F02B 79/00] Running-in of internal-combustion engines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Running-in of internal-combustion engines.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Lubrication of internal combustion engines -> F01M7/00
[F02B 9/00] Engines characterised by other types of ignition -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines characterised by other types of ignition.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Engines characterised by both fuel-air mixture compression and air compression, or characterised by both positive ignition and compression ignition -> F02B11/00
[F02B 9/02] with compression ignition -- F02B 9/02 designates internal-combustion piston engines in which the fuel is ignited by the heat generated during compression rather than by a spark. This subclass covers diesel-type and other compression-ignition engines, detailing their construction, operation and related control methods.
[F02B 9/04] Methods of operating -- F02B 9/04 covers methods of operating internal-combustion piston engines that are characterised by ignition types other than conventional spark-ignition, including compression-ignition, controlled auto-ignition or other alternative ignition schemes. It addresses control strategies, timing adjustments and operational procedures aimed at optimizing performance, efficiency or emissions for these specially ignited engines.
[F02B 9/06] with non-timed positive ignition, e.g. with hot-spots -- F02B 9/06 covers internal-combustion piston engines that use a non-timed positive ignition system, such as hot-spot ignition, where combustion is initiated by localized high-temperature areas rather than a spark or timed fuel injection.
[F02B 9/08] with incandescent chambers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  Hot bulb engine
[F02B 9/10] Chamber shapes or constructions -- F02B 9/10 covers the design and geometry of combustion chambers in internal-combustion piston engines that use non-spark-ignition methods. It includes variations in shape, partitioning, and structural features intended to influence fuel-air mixing, flame propagation, and pressure development for engines such as compression-ignition or homogeneous-charge compression-ignition types.
[F02C] GAS-TURBINE PLANTS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Construction of turbines -> F01D - Steam turbine plants -> F01K - Special vapour plants -> F01K - Jet-propulsion plants -> F02K - Construction of compressors or fans -> F04D - Gas-turbine combustion chambers -> F23R - Using gas turbines in compression refrigeration plants -> F25B11/00
[F02C 1/00] Gas-turbine plants characterised by the use of hot gases or unheated pressurised gases, as the working fluid -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gas-turbine plants characterised by the use of hot gases or unheated pressurised gases, as the working fluid, e.g. heated indirectly by solar power, nuclear power or the like; unheated pressurized gas that is stored and prior to use the expansion of the pressurized gas is used to drive a turbine.
[F02C 1/002] using an auxiliary fluid -- F02C 1/002 is a CPC subgroup within mechanical engineering → combustion engines → gas-turbine plants that covers gas-turbine installations in which an auxiliary fluid is introduced into the working-fluid stream. The auxiliary medium may be used to boost power, control temperature, aid start-up or enhance efficiency by mixing with the hot or pressurised gases that drive the turbine.
[F02C 1/005] being recirculated -- This subclass covers gas-turbine installations in which the working gas is routed back into the cycle rather than being exhausted, forming a closed or recirculating flow. Such arrangements often use heat exchangers or compressors to maintain pressure and temperature for continuous operation.
[F02C 1/007] combination of cycles -- F02C 1/007 refers to gas-turbine plants that use a combination of thermodynamic cycles, typically coupling a gas-turbine (Brayton) cycle with a secondary cycle such as a steam-Rankine cycle to recover waste heat and raise overall efficiency. The classification includes the design, integration and operation of such combined-cycle systems and related control or heat-recovery equipment.
[F02C 1/02] the working fluid being an unheated pressurised gas -- The classification refers to gas-turbine plants that use an unheated, pressurised gas as the working fluid instead of hot combustion gases. It is situated in the hierarchy under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and specifically gas-turbine plants.
[F02C 1/04] the working fluid being heated indirectly -- This CPC subgroup F02C 1/04 belongs to mechanical engineering → combustion engines → gas-turbine plants and addresses gas-turbine installations where the working fluid is heated indirectly, meaning the fluid gains heat from a separate source rather than direct combustion.
[F02C 1/05] characterised by the type or source of heat, e.g. using nuclear or solar energy -- F02C 1/05 covers gas-turbine plants that are distinguished by the type or source of heat used to generate the working fluid, including systems powered by nuclear, solar, geothermal or other specific energy sources rather than conventional combustion.
[F02C 1/06] using reheated exhaust gas -- F02C 1/06 pertains to gas-turbine plants in which the exhaust gas is reheated before further expansion or use, typically to raise cycle efficiency or power output. The classification applies to designs that incorporate reheating devices such as combustion chambers, heat exchangers or fuel injectors that increase the temperature of the turbine's working fluid after the primary combustion stage.
[F02C 1/08] Semi-closed cycles -- Semi-closed cycles are gas-turbine plant configurations in which the working fluid-typically hot gases or pressurised inert gases-is partially retained and recirculated through the system rather than being fully exhausted. This approach reduces loss of the working medium, enables better control of temperature and pressure, and can improve overall efficiency of the turbine cycle.
[F02C 1/10] Closed cycles -- Closed-cycle gas-turbine plants operate with a working fluid that is continuously recirculated within the system rather than being expelled, using heat exchangers to transfer energy from an external source to the fluid. This arrangement allows the turbine to generate power with either hot gases or compressed inert gases while maintaining a sealed loop.
[F02C 1/105] construction; details -- F02C 1/105 covers the structural design and specific construction features of gas-turbine plants that operate with hot gases or unheated pressurised gases, including the arrangement of casings, shafts, bearings, sealing systems and assembly methods for the turbine, compressor and related auxiliaries.
[F02C 3/00] Gas-turbine plants characterised by the use of combustion products as the working fluid -- This subclass covers gas-turbine plants where the hot gases generated by combustion are used directly as the working fluid to drive the turbine, instead of a separate medium. It includes open-cycle and combined-cycle arrangements in which the combustion products expand through the turbine to produce mechanical or electrical power.
[F02C 3/02] using exhaust-gas pressure in a pressure exchanger to compress combustion-air -- The code F02C 3/02 covers gas-turbine systems that recover the pressure of the exhaust gases in a pressure-exchanger and use it to compress the incoming combustion air. This method improves overall efficiency by reusing waste energy rather than venting it.
[F02C 3/04] having a turbine driving a compressor -- F02C 3/04 covers gas-turbine installations in which the turbine is mechanically coupled to drive a compressor, forming a single unit that compresses intake air before it enters the combustion chamber. This configuration is typical of gas-turbine plants that use the turbine's output to power the compressor required for the combustion process.
[F02C 3/045] having compressor and turbine passages in a single rotor-module -- F02C 3/045 covers gas-turbine plants that use combustion products as the working fluid and integrate the compressor and turbine flow passages within a single rotor-module, meaning the same rotating assembly contains both the compressor and turbine stages. This arrangement is typical of compact, high-speed turbo-machinery.
[F02C 3/05] the compressor and the turbine being of the radial flow type -- This CPC subclass F02C 3/05 covers gas-turbine plants where both the compressor and turbine are of the radial flow type, meaning the working fluid moves outward radially through the stages. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, gas-turbine plants, and pertains to designs that use combustion products as the working fluid.
[F02C 3/055] the compressor being of the positive-displacement type -- F02C 3/055 covers gas-turbine plants in which the compressor is a positive-displacement type, used when the combustion products themselves serve as the working fluid. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → gas-turbine plants characterised by combustion-product flow.
[F02C 3/06] the compressor comprising only axial stages -- F02C 3/06 covers gas-turbine plants that use combustion products as the working fluid and specifically refers to compressors built solely from axial stages. These axial compressors provide continuous flow and are typical in high-speed turbine cycles.
[F02C 3/062] the turbine being of the radial-flow type -- This subgroup covers gas-turbine plants that employ combustion products as the working fluid and where the turbine is of the radial-flow type, meaning the working fluid moves radially outward from the centre of rotation.
[F02C 3/064] the compressor having concentric stages -- The CPC entry F02C 3/064 covers compressors in gas-turbine plants that employ concentric stages, where each stage shares a common axis and is arranged one inside the other. It applies to designs that use the combustion products as the working fluid for power generation.
[F02C 3/067] having counter-rotating rotors -- This CPC subclass F02C 3/067 covers gas-turbine plants in which the rotors rotate in opposite directions, i.e. counter-rotating rotors, used to extract energy from combustion products as the working fluid.
[F02C 3/073] the compressor and turbine stages being concentric -- F02C 3/073 covers gas-turbine plants that use the combustion products themselves as the working fluid, specifically where the compressor and turbine stages are arranged concentrically around a common axis. This configuration is typical in combined-cycle or direct-drive turbines, allowing the same shaft to house both compression and expansion components in a compact layout.
[F02C 3/08] the compressor comprising at least one radial stage -- F02C 3/08 covers compressors in gas-turbine plants that use combustion products as the working fluid and that incorporate at least one radial-stage stage, describing the design of the compression section that pressurises the flow before it enters the turbine.
[F02C 3/085] the turbine being of the radial-flow type (radial-radial)  F02C3/05  takes precedence -- F02C 3/085 covers gas-turbine plants that use combustion gases directly as the working fluid and feature a radial-flow turbine where both inlet and outlet flow are radial, a radial-radial configuration. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, gas-turbine plants.
[F02C 3/09] of the centripetal type -- F02C 3/09 refers to gas-turbine plants of the centripetal type, in which the combustion products serve as the working fluid and flow inward from the outer rim toward the centre of the rotor. The subclass is used for designs that employ a centripetal flow path within the turbine stage.
[F02C 3/10] with another turbine driving an output shaft but not driving the compressor -- This CPC subclass covers gas-turbine plants where a second turbine is used to drive an output shaft while the main turbine does not drive the compressor. It applies to arrangements in which the power-producing turbine is separate from the compressor-driving turbine, such as combined-cycle or power-boost configurations.
[F02C 3/103] the compressor being of the centrifugal type -- F02C 3/103 pertains to gas-turbine plants that use combustion products as the working fluid, specifically where the compressor is of the centrifugal type.
[F02C 3/107] with two or more rotors connected by power transmission -- F02C 3/107 covers gas-turbine plants that use the combustion products directly as the working fluid and incorporate two or more rotors linked together by a power-transmission system. This arrangement enables mechanical power to be transferred between stages, improving efficiency and flexibility in turbine operation.
[F02C 3/113] with variable power transmission between rotors -- This subclass deals with gas-turbine plants that use combustion gases as the working fluid and incorporate a variable power transmission system between the rotors, allowing the torque or speed transferred from one rotor to another to be adjusted during operation to match changing load demands.
[F02C 3/13] having variable working fluid interconnections between turbines or compressors or stages of different rotors -- F02C 3/13 classifies gas-turbine plants that use variable interconnections to route the combustion-product working fluid between turbines, compressors or stages of different rotors, allowing the flow path to be changed according to operating conditions. This flexibility enables the plant to switch or combine stages for performance optimisation.
[F02C 3/14] characterised by the arrangement of the combustion chamber in the plant -- This subclass covers gas-turbine installations whose defining feature is the arrangement of the combustion chamber within the plant, such as axial, annular or can-type configurations and their placement relative to compressors, turbines and exhaust. It is applied when the layout of the combustor, rather than the cycle type, is the primary distinguishing characteristic.
[F02C 3/145] the combustion chamber being in the reverse flow-type -- This subclass covers gas-turbine installations where the combustion chamber is arranged in a reverse-flow configuration, meaning fresh air enters opposite to the direction of exhaust gases, enhancing compactness and heat exchange. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and gas-turbine plants.
[F02C 3/16] the combustion chambers being formed at least partly in the turbine rotor -- The CPC entry F02C 3/16 covers gas-turbine plants in which the combustion chamber is at least partly integrated into the turbine rotor itself. This design uses the combustion products directly as the working fluid that expands through the rotating blades, combining combustion and expansion functions within the rotor structure.
[F02C 3/165] the combustion chamber contributes to the driving force by creating reactive thrust -- F02C 3/165 covers gas-turbine plants where the combustion chamber itself supplies part of the thrust by directing the high-temperature combustion gases to produce a reactive force that assists the turbine's drive. This subcategory falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, gas-turbine plants and deals with designs that use the combustion products as the working fluid.
[F02C 3/20] using a special fuel, oxidant, or dilution fluid to generate the combustion products -- F02C 3/20 covers gas-turbine plants that generate their working fluid by employing a special fuel, oxidant or dilution fluid in the combustion process. The classification is used for designs where the combustion products themselves are the turbine's working medium, rather than conventional air-fuel mixtures.
[F02C 3/205] in a fluidised-bed combustor  in combination with a steam cycle  see   F01K23/061 ; fluidised-bed apparatus in general  B01J8/18 ; fluidised-bed combustors in general  F23C10/00 -- F02C 3/205 covers gas-turbine power plants that use the combustion gases directly as the working fluid and incorporate a fluidised-bed combustor together with a steam cycle. It links to related fluidised-bed technology in subclasses B01J8/18, F01K23/061 and to general fluidised-bed combustors in F23C10/00.
[F02C 3/22] the fuel or oxidant being gaseous at standard temperature and pressure -- F02C 3/22 covers gas-turbine plants in which the fuel or oxidant is supplied in a gaseous state at standard temperature and pressure. It relates to combustion-engine systems where the working fluid consists of the combustion products, emphasizing the handling and injection of gaseous fuels or oxidants in the turbine cycle.
[F02C 3/24] the fuel or oxidant being liquid at standard temperature and pressure -- This subclass refers to gas-turbine plants in which the fuel or oxidant is supplied as a liquid at standard temperature and pressure, covering the methods and equipment for storing, delivering, and vaporising liquid working substances in the turbine cycle. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → gas-turbine plants.
[F02C 3/26] the fuel or oxidant being solid or pulverulent, e.g. in slurry or suspension -- F02C 3/26 covers gas-turbine plants where the fuel or oxidant is supplied in a solid or pulverised form, such as powders, slurries or suspensions, and the combustion products themselves serve as the working fluid. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and gas-turbine plants that use solid or pulverulent reactants.
[F02C 3/28] using a separate gas producer for gasifying the fuel before combustion -- This subclass covers gas-turbine installations where the fuel is first gasified in a dedicated gas producer before being burned to drive the turbine. The approach separates the gasification step from the main combustion chamber, allowing the combustion products to serve as the working fluid.
[F02C 3/30] Adding water, steam or other fluids -- F02C 3/30 pertains to gas-turbine plants that use the combustion products as the working fluid and incorporate the addition of water, steam or other fluids to improve performance, control temperature or enhance efficiency. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → gas-turbine plants.
[F02C 3/305] Increasing the power, speed, torque or efficiency of a gas turbine or the thrust of a turbojet engine by injecting or adding water, steam or other fluids  F01K21/04  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass F02C 3/305 covers methods for boosting the output of gas-turbine or turbojet engines by injecting water, steam or other fluids into the combustion stream, thereby raising power, speed, torque or thrust. It applies to gas-turbine plants that use combustion products as the working fluid and is subordinate to the broader class F01K21/04.
[F02C 3/32] Inducing air flow by fluid jet, e.g. ejector action -- F02C 3/32 covers gas-turbine plants that employ a fluid-jet or ejector mechanism to draw in air, using the momentum of a high-speed jet of combustion products or other fluid to induce the required airflow for combustion. This technique improves air-fuel mixing and plant efficiency.
[F02C 3/34] with recycling of part of the working fluid, i.e. semi-closed cycles with combustion products in the closed part of the cycle -- This CPC subclass covers gas-turbine plants where part of the combustion products are recirculated, forming a semi-closed cycle in which the working fluid is partially reused. It relates to mechanical-engineering combustion engines that recycle a portion of the working fluid within the closed part of the cycle.
[F02C 3/36] Open cycles -- F02C 3/36 pertains to open-cycle gas-turbine plants in which the combustion products themselves act as the working fluid and are expelled after driving the turbine. These systems operate without recirculating the exhaust, typical of simple-cycle power generation.
[F02C 3/365] a part of the compressed air being burned, the other part being heated indirectly  in a fluidised-bed combustor  F02C3/205
[F02C 5/00] Gas-turbine plants characterised by the working fluid being generated by intermittent combustion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gas-turbine plants characterised by the working fluid being generated by intermittent combustion, i.e. non continuous combustion e.g. in a combustion chamber having valves  taken from FR2210718
[F02C 5/02] characterised by the arrangement of the combustion chamber in the chamber in the plant -- F02C 5/02 refers to gas-turbine plants in which the combustion chamber is integrated within the turbine housing itself, rather than being a separate unit. This arrangement is used for plants that generate the working fluid by intermittent combustion, allowing a compact layout and often simplifying the flow path between combustion and expansion stages.
[F02C 5/04] the combustion chambers being formed at least partly in the turbine rotor -- F02C 5/04 covers gas-turbine plants in which the working fluid is generated by intermittent combustion and the combustion chambers are at least partially formed within the turbine rotor, allowing heat addition directly in the rotating part of the engine.
[F02C 5/06] the working fluid being generated in an internal-combustion gas generated of the positive-displacement type having essentially no mechanical power output -- F02C 5/06 covers gas-turbine plants in which the working fluid is produced by an internal-combustion gas generator of the positive-displacement type that delivers essentially no mechanical power output, the turbine being driven solely by the intermittent combustion-generated fluid. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and gas-turbine plants with intermittent-combustion fluid generation.
[F02C 5/08] the gas generator being of the free-piston type -- This subclass classifies gas-turbine plants whose working fluid is produced by intermittent combustion, specifically when the gas generator is of the free-piston type, a configuration used to drive the turbine.
[F02C 5/10] the working fluid forming a resonating or oscillating gas column, i.e. the combustion chambers having no positively actuated valves, e.g. using Helmholtz effect -- The entry F02C 5/10 covers gas-turbine plants where the working fluid is produced by intermittent combustion that creates a resonating or oscillating gas column, eliminating positively actuated valves. It includes designs that exploit the Helmholtz effect in the combustion chambers.
[F02C 5/11] using valveless combustion chambers -- F02C 5/11 covers gas-turbine plants whose working fluid is produced by intermittent combustion and that use valveless combustion chambers, where flame initiation and termination are controlled without mechanical valves, relying on pressure, flow or acoustic effects to regulate the combustion process. This subclass lies within the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and gas-turbine plants.
[F02C 5/12] the combustion chambers having inlet or outlet valves, e.g. Holzwarth gas-turbine plants -- F02C 5/12 covers gas-turbine plants in which the working fluid is generated by intermittent combustion and the combustion chamber is equipped with inlet or outlet valves, such as the Holzwarth-type gas-turbine configuration.
[F02C 6/00] Plural gas-turbine plants -- The CPC entry F02C 6/00 covers designs and configurations that use multiple gas-turbine units operating together as a plural plant. It addresses how several turbines are arranged, coupled, or coordinated to generate power, drive machinery, or provide combined-cycle performance. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Gas-Turbine Plants.
[F02C 6/003] Gas-turbine plants with heaters between turbine stages -- A gas-turbine plant that incorporates heating devices installed between successive turbine stages to raise the temperature of the working gas, thereby increasing expansion work and overall cycle efficiency. This arrangement is used to improve performance in combustion-engine driven turbine installations.
[F02C 6/006] Open cycle gas-turbine in which the working fluid is expanded to a pressure below the atmospheric pressure and then compressed to atmospheric pressure -- It covers open-cycle gas-turbine systems that expand the working fluid to a pressure below atmospheric and then use a downstream compressor to raise the pressure back to ambient. This configuration can improve net work extraction or meet specific process constraints. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, gas-turbine plants.
[F02C 6/02] Plural gas-turbine plants having a common power output -- The class F02C 6/02 covers gas-turbine installations that use two or more turbine units operating together to provide a single, common power output. It includes arrangements where the turbines are connected in parallel or otherwise coordinated so that their combined output drives the same shaft, generator or power-distribution system, effectively forming one plant despite comprising multiple machines.
[F02C 6/04] Gas-turbine plants providing heated or pressurised working fluid for other apparatus, e.g. without mechanical power output -- Gas-turbine installations that are designed primarily to heat or pressurise a working fluid for use in other equipment, rather than to deliver mechanical power themselves, fall under this subclass. The turbines act as energy-transfer devices supplying hot or high-pressure fluid to secondary processes such as chemical reactors, power-generation cycles, or propulsion auxiliaries. This entry is within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for combustion engines and gas-turbine plants.
[F02C 6/06] providing compressed gas -- This CPC subclass covers the supply of compressed gas to multiple gas-turbine installations, including the distribution network, compressors, and control arrangements that feed several turbine units simultaneously. It addresses systems that provide the necessary pressurised fuel or working gas for plural gas-turbine plants.
[F02C 6/08] the gas being bled from the gas-turbine compressor -- This subclass deals with methods and devices for bleeding off compressed gas from a gas-turbine compressor, often to control pressure, temperature, or to supply cooling or start-up air. It includes bleed valves, ducts and control systems that remove a portion of the working fluid before it enters the combustion chamber.
[F02C 6/10] supplying working fluid to a user, e.g. a chemical process, which returns working fluid to a turbine of the plant -- This CPC entry covers arrangements that deliver the working fluid of a gas-turbine plant to an external user, such as a chemical process, and then return the fluid back to the turbine. It includes systems for supplying, routing and recirculating the fluid between the turbine and the user.
[F02C 6/12] Turbochargers, i.e. plants for augmenting mechanical power output of internal-combustion piston engines by increase of charge pressure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Either turbochargers and details thereof if not covered elsewhere or plants/cycles with supercharging apparatuses or processes  taken from US2006016196  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Seals -> F01D11/00 - Variable geometry turbines -> F01D17/14 , F01D17/16 - Bearings -> F01D25/16 - Lubrication -> F01D25/18 - Casings -> F01D25/24  **Glossary:** - VGT: variable turbine geometry
[F02C 6/14] Gas-turbine plants having means for storing energy, e.g. for meeting peak loads -- F02C 6/14 covers gas-turbine power plants equipped with energy-storage devices, such as batteries, flywheels or thermal reservoirs, that can supply additional power during peak-load periods or store excess output for later use. The classification groups these plants within mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and gas-turbine systems.
[F02C 6/16] for storing compressed air -- F02C 6/16 relates to gas-turbine plant designs that include means for storing compressed air, enabling functions such as turbine start-up assistance, load-leveling or energy recovery. The subclass addresses how compressed-air reservoirs are integrated into the overall combustion-engine system.
[F02C 6/18] using the waste heat of gas-turbine plants outside the plants themselves, e.g. gas-turbine power heat plants -- **Glossary:** - HRSG: heat recovery steam generator
[F02C 6/20] Adaptations of gas-turbine plants for driving vehicles -- The classification covers modifications of gas-turbine power plants that enable them to propel vehicles, detailing design changes, integration methods, and performance adaptations required for automotive or aerospace use. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and gas-turbine plants.
[F02C 6/203] the vehicles being waterborne vessels -- F02C 6/203 covers gas-turbine propulsion plants installed in waterborne vessels such as ships, boats and barges. It classifies the arrangement, coupling and operation of multiple gas-turbine units used to drive the vessel's propellers or waterjets within the broader mechanical-engineering hierarchy of combustion and gas-turbine plant technology.
[F02C 6/206] the vehicles being airscrew driven -- A subclass of gas-turbine plants in which the turbine's power is transmitted to an airscrew (propeller) that drives the vehicle, covering aircraft, helicopters and similar propulsion systems where a gas turbine directly powers a rotating propeller for thrust.
[F02C 7/00] Features, components parts, details or accessories, not provided for in, or of interest apart form groups -- F02C 7/00 pertains to gas-turbine plants and covers any features, components, parts or accessories that are not specifically dealt with in other subclasses, or that are of interest apart from the main groups. It includes auxiliary devices, special fittings, and miscellaneous items that support the operation of the turbine system but are not otherwise classified.
[F02C 7/04] Air intakes for gas-turbine plants or jet-propulsion plants -- The subclass concerns the design, components and accessories of air-intake systems for gas-turbine or jet-propulsion plants, such as ducts, filters and inlet devices that are not covered in other groups.
[F02C 7/042] having variable geometry -- This subgroup includes gas-turbine components whose geometry can be changed during operation, such as adjustable inlet guide vanes, stator blades or other flow-path elements that allow the shape of passages to be varied for performance control.
[F02C 7/045] having provisions for noise suppression -- F02C 7/045 refers to features on gas-turbine plants that provide noise suppression, such as silencers, acoustic liners, mufflers or other devices designed to reduce sound emitted by the turbine or its exhaust system.
[F02C 7/047] Heating to prevent icing -- The code designates heating arrangements used to prevent ice formation on gas-turbine plant components such as inlet ducts, blades or other parts, thereby safeguarding reliable operation in icing conditions. It covers devices, methods or systems that provide thermal protection for these elements.
[F02C 7/05] having provisions for obviating the penetration of damaging objects or particles -- F02C 7/05 covers protective provisions in gas-turbine plant components that prevent the entry of damaging objects or particles, such as debris, birds, or hail, into the combustion or airflow paths. It includes designs of screens, filters, inlet cages and related accessories that safeguard engine parts from impact or contamination.
[F02C 7/052] with dust-separation devices -- F02C 7/052 is a CPC subclass for gas-turbine installations that includes dust-separation devices applied to the plant's airflow or exhaust. It covers components, accessories or details of dust-removal systems that are not classified elsewhere within the gas-turbine group. This entry falls under the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Gas-Turbine Plants.
[F02C 7/055] with intake grids, screens or guards -- This CPC subclass falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → gas-turbine plants and covers any features, components, parts, details or accessories related to intake grids, screens or guards that are not otherwise specified in the classification. It is used for describing protective or filtering elements positioned at the inlet of a gas-turbine system.
[F02C 7/057] Control or regulation -- F02C 7/057 classifies the control and regulation systems for gas-turbine plants, including devices, methods or arrangements that adjust fuel, air, temperature, speed or other operating parameters to manage performance, efficiency or safety, separate from the specific components or accessories covered in other groups.
[F02C 7/06] Arrangements of bearings -- F02C 7/06 covers the design and layout of bearing systems used in gas-turbine plants. It includes the positioning, mounting and support methods for bearings that hold rotating shafts and related components within combustion-engine machinery.
[F02C 7/08] Heating air supply before combustion, e.g. by exhaust gases -- This subclass covers devices or methods that preheat the combustion-air supply in gas-turbine plants, typically by recovering heat from exhaust gases. It relates to accessories or components that improve engine efficiency by raising inlet air temperature before combustion.
[F02C 7/10] by means of regenerative heat-exchangers -- The subclass F02C 7/10 covers gas-turbine plant features that employ regenerative heat-exchangers, such as designs where compressed air, combustion gases or turbine exhaust are pre-heated by a regenerator to improve cycle efficiency. It includes components, arrangements and accessories specifically related to these regenerative heat-exchange systems.
[F02C 7/105] of the rotary type -- F02C 7/105 covers features, components, parts, details or accessories of rotary-type gas-turbine plants that are not classified elsewhere. It includes elements such as rotors, shafts, bearings and other rotating machinery specific to these engines. This sub-class is used when the item is of interest apart from the main functional groups of the turbine.
[F02C 7/12] Cooling of plants -- The F02C 7/12 subclass covers cooling arrangements for gas-turbine plants, including systems, devices and accessories used to remove excess heat from the turbine, generator, exhaust or auxiliary components. It addresses water, air, oil or mixed-medium cooling methods, heat-exchanger designs and related control equipment.
[F02C 7/125] by partial arc admission of the working fluid or by intermittent admission of working and cooling fluid -- This subclass defines gas-turbine configurations that introduce the working fluid over only a segment of the annular flow path or that admit the working and cooling fluids intermittently, allowing partial-arc or pulsed flow control to improve performance or thermal management.
[F02C 7/14] of fluids in the plant -- The F02C 7/14 subclass covers components, parts and accessories related to the handling of fluids within gas-turbine plants, such as fluid conduits, pumps, valves, heat-exchangers and related fluid-distribution equipment that are not classified elsewhere. It addresses fluid-related features of the plant's combustion-engine system.
[F02C 7/141] of working fluid -- The subclass F02C 7/141 concerns features, components, parts or accessories associated with the working fluid of gas-turbine plants, including fluid handling, conditioning, circulation, injection or removal, that are not covered by other more specific groups. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, gas-turbine plants and addresses fluid-related details of interest apart from the main turbine assemblies.
[F02C 7/143] before or between the compressor stages -- This subgroup covers parts, components or accessories that are situated in the flow path before a compressor stage or in the inter-stage region of a gas-turbine plant, such as guide vanes, diffusers, seals or ducting that are not otherwise classified. It relates to mechanical engineering of combustion engines, specifically gas-turbine compressors.
[F02C 7/1435] by water injection -- The F02C 7/1435 subclass concerns gas-turbine plants that employ water injection, a technique used to cool combustion, increase mass flow and improve efficiency or emissions. It covers features, components, parts and accessories specifically related to the water-injection system in such turbines.
[F02C 7/16] characterised by cooling medium -- This subclass covers gas-turbine plant features that involve the use of a cooling medium to control the temperature of components or sections of the engine. It includes arrangements, devices, or accessories that provide cooling, such as air, water, oil or other fluids, applied to blades, combustors, liners or other parts, where the cooling function is not otherwise specified in broader subclasses.
[F02C 7/18] the medium being gaseous, e.g. air -- F02C 7/18 covers aspects of gas-turbine plants where the working medium is a gas such as air, including components, accessories or details that are not classified elsewhere in the system.
[F02C 7/185] Cooling means for reducing the temperature of the cooling air or gas -- This CPC subclass covers devices and methods that lower the temperature of cooling air or gas used in gas-turbine plants, such as heat exchangers, chillers or other cooling systems integrated with the turbine. It pertains to ancillary cooling components not otherwise classified.
[F02C 7/20] Mounting or supporting of plant -- F02C 7/20 relates to the mounting or supporting of gas-turbine plant equipment, covering foundations, frames, brackets, supports and other accessories that secure the plant in place. It addresses the ways components and subsystems are attached, positioned or held within a gas-turbine power plant.

[F02C 7/232] Fuel valves -- Fuel valves are devices that regulate the delivery of fuel to the combustion chambers of gas-turbine plants, encompassing various valve types, actuation mechanisms, sealing arrangements and safety features. The classification covers their design, installation, control, monitoring and maintenance as components of combustion-engine systems.
[F02C 7/236] Fuel delivery systems comprising two or more pumps -- The CPC class F02C 7/236 relates to fuel delivery systems for gas-turbine plants that employ two or more pumps. [F02C 7/2365] comprising an air supply system for the atomisation of fuel -- The CPC subclass F02C 7/2365 falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and gas-turbine plants and covers gas-turbine systems that include an air-supply arrangement specifically for atomising the fuel. It pertains to auxiliary components that deliver pressurised air to a fuel-spray device, improving fuel-air mixing before combustion. [F02C 7/24] Heat or noise insulation -- The code F02C 7/24 covers heat or noise insulation applied to parts, components, accessories or details of gas-turbine plants that are not covered elsewhere. It includes the choice of insulating materials, installation methods and design features intended to limit thermal losses or reduce acoustic emissions in combustion-engine installations.
[F02C 7/25] Fire protection or prevention -- The F02C 7/25 subclass covers measures and devices that protect gas-turbine plant components from fire damage or prevent fire from occurring, such as fire-suppressing systems, shielding, and monitoring equipment specific to combustion-engine installations. It addresses accessories and details not covered elsewhere in the broader mechanical engineering and combustion-engine hierarchy.
[F02C 7/26] Starting -- F02C 7/26 deals with the starting of gas-turbine plants, covering devices and procedures used to bring the turbine up to self-sustaining speed. It includes electric, hydraulic, pneumatic and mechanical starter systems and their control elements.
[F02C 7/262] Restarting after flame-out -- The subclass F02C 7/262 covers methods, devices and control arrangements used to restart a gas-turbine plant after a flame-out event. It includes ignitors, bleed-air or fuel-spray systems, timing controls and other accessories that enable re-ignition when such features are not classified elsewhere in the gas-turbine groups.
[F02C 7/264] Ignition -- F02C 7/264 covers ignition devices and methods used in gas-turbine plants, including spark-plugs, igniters, and associated control systems that initiate combustion in turbine combustors. The class addresses the design, placement, and operation of these components when they are not covered by more specific sub-categories within gas-turbine engineering.
[F02C 7/266] Electric -- F02C 7/266 covers electric elements, systems and accessories specifically related to gas-turbine plants, such as generators, power converters, control electronics and wiring that are not covered by other subclasses. It includes the electrical integration of turbine drive units, power supply arrangements and ancillary electric equipment used in combustion-engine installations.
[F02C 7/268] Starting drives for the rotor -- F02C 7/268 refers to the starting drives used to rotate the rotor of a gas-turbine plant, including electric, pneumatic, hydraulic or other starter mechanisms that initiate turbine operation. It falls under mechanical engineering > combustion engines > gas-turbine plants and covers features, components, parts or accessories not classified elsewhere.
[F02C 7/27] Fluid drives -- F02C 7/27, under Mechanical Engineering > Combustion Engines > Gas-turbine plants, classifies fluid-drive systems and related components used in gas-turbine installations, including auxiliary pumps, compressors, hydraulic drives and similar accessories not covered elsewhere.
[F02C 7/272] generated by cartridges -- This subclass concerns parts or accessories of gas-turbine plants that are produced by cartridge mechanisms, such as ignition or fuel-cartridge units, which are not otherwise specified in the main CPC groups. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → gas-turbine plants.

[F02C 9/00] Controlling gas-turbine plants -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling gas-turbine plants, controlling fuel supply in air- breathing jet-propulsion plants
[F02C 9/16] Control of working fluid flow -- F02C 9/16 covers methods and devices for regulating the flow of the working fluid in gas-turbine plants. It includes control of pressure, temperature, speed and distribution of air, fuel or combustion gases to optimise performance, protect components and meet operational targets.
[F02C 9/18] by bleeding, bypassing or acting on variable working fluid interconnections between turbines or compressors or their stages -- The CPC subclass F02C 9/18 covers control techniques for gas-turbine plants that use bleeding, bypassing, or other actions on variable working-fluid interconnections between turbines, compressors or their stages, adjusting flow paths to regulate performance.
[F02C 9/20] by throttling -- This subgroup covers control of gas-turbine plants by varying the flow of working fluid through a throttle device, such as a valve or nozzle, to adjust pressure or mass flow and thus regulate turbine speed and output power. It includes methods that use throttling as the primary means of controlling the plant's performance.
[F02C 9/22] by adjusting turbine vanes -- This CPC subclass covers methods of regulating a gas-turbine plant by changing the angle or position of its turbine vanes, which alters airflow and power output. Adjusting the vanes provides control of speed, thrust, or efficiency.
[F02C 9/24] Control of the pressure level in closed cycles -- The entry F02C 9/24 covers systems and methods for regulating the pressure level in closed-cycle gas-turbine plants. It includes sensors, control algorithms, and actuators that maintain steady pressure during operation, ensuring efficient power output and safe performance. The classification is used for patents dealing with pressure-control strategies in closed-cycle turbine cycles.
[F02C 9/26] Control of fuel supply -- This classification covers technologies and methods for regulating the amount of fuel delivered to a gas-turbine plant, including devices, control strategies, and automation systems that adjust fuel flow to meet performance, efficiency, and emission targets.
[F02C 9/263] by means of fuel metering valves -- F02C 9/263 covers control methods for gas-turbine plants that use fuel-metering valves to regulate the amount of fuel supplied to the combustor. It relates to devices and systems that adjust valve position to achieve desired power output, efficiency, or emissions, forming a subcategory of combustion-engine control within mechanical engineering.
[F02C 9/266] specially adapted for gas turbines with intermittent fuel injection -- This CPC subclass deals with control techniques and apparatus for gas-turbine installations that are designed to operate with fuel supplied intermittently. It includes methods for managing combustion stability, fuel-metering and transient response when fuel is pulsed rather than continuous.
[F02C 9/28] Regulating systems responsive to plant or ambient parameters, e.g. temperature, pressure, rotor speed -- The subclass deals with control mechanisms for gas-turbine plants that automatically adjust operation in response to measured plant or ambient conditions such as temperature, pressure, or rotor speed, thereby maintaining optimal performance, efficiency, and safety.
[F02C 9/285] Mechanical command devices linked to the throttle lever -- The code F02C 9/285 covers mechanical command devices that are directly connected to the throttle lever for controlling gas-turbine plants, enabling the operator to adjust fuel or air flow by moving the lever and thereby regulating engine speed and output.
[F02C 9/30] characterised by variable fuel pump output -- The CPC subclass F02C 9/30, within mechanical engineering → combustion engines → gas-turbine plants, concerns control of gas-turbine installations that use a fuel pump whose output can be varied. It covers systems that adjust pump speed or flow rate to match load demand or operating conditions, enhancing efficiency and response.
[F02C 9/32] characterised by throttling of fuel -- F02C 9/32 pertains to control of gas-turbine plants in which the fuel flow is regulated by throttling the fuel rather than altering air flow or other parameters. This method is used to adjust turbine power output and speed while maintaining stable combustion.
[F02C 9/34] Joint control of separate flows to main and auxiliary burners -- This subclass covers gas-turbine control schemes that synchronise the flow rates to the main combustion chamber and the auxiliary burner using a single control system, allowing coordinated operation and balanced load distribution.
[F02C 9/36] characterised by returning of fuel to sump -- This subclass covers methods or devices that recycle unburned or partially burned fuel from a gas-turbine plant back to the fuel sump for re-use, improving overall efficiency and reducing emissions. It includes fuel-recirculation systems, control strategies and piping arrangements that return fuel to the storage tank within the turbine's fuel circuit.
[F02C 9/38] characterised by throttling and returning of fuel to sump -- F02C 9/38 relates to control methods for gas-turbine plants where fuel flow is regulated by throttling and any unburned or excess fuel is directed back to a sump, allowing its reuse or safe disposal. This approach helps maintain stable turbine operation and protect equipment during load changes.
[F02C 9/40] specially adapted to the use of a special fuel or a plurality of fuels -- F02C 9/40 covers control systems for gas-turbine plants that are specially adapted to operate on a particular fuel type or on several different fuels. The classification includes strategies, devices, and algorithms that manage combustion, fuel supply, and performance under these fuel-specific conditions.
[F02C 9/42] specially adapted for the control of two or more plants simultaneously -- F02C 9/42 covers control systems that are specially designed to operate two or more gas-turbine plants at the same time, allowing coordinated regulation of speed, load, and performance across the combined units. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and gas-turbine plant control.
[F02C 9/44] responsive to the speed of aircraft, e.g. Mach number control, optimisation of fuel consumption -- This subclass addresses control methods for gas-turbine plants that adjust performance according to aircraft speed, such as Mach-number regulation, aiming to optimise fuel consumption and overall efficiency during flight.
[F02C 9/46] Emergency fuel control -- F02C 9/46 covers systems and methods for emergency fuel control in gas-turbine plants, including rapid fuel shut-off, backup fuel provision, and safety mechanisms that limit or stop fuel flow during abnormal or failure conditions to protect the turbine and plant.
[F02C 9/48] Control of fuel supply conjointly with another control of the plant -- This CPC class, F02C 9/48, concerns the coordinated control of fuel supply together with another plant function in gas-turbine installations. It includes schemes that adjust fuel flow while simultaneously regulating parameters such as turbine speed, temperature, or air-flow to optimise performance and safety. The class falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, gas-turbine plants.
[F02C 9/50] with control of working fluid flow -- F02C 9/50 designates inventions that regulate the flow of the working fluid in gas-turbine plants, including valves, flow-control devices and associated control systems used to adjust air, fuel or exhaust streams for improved performance, safety or efficiency. It falls under the CPC hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Gas-Turbine Plants → Controlling gas-turbine plants.
[F02C 9/52] by bleeding or by-passing the working fluid -- F02C 9/52 covers control techniques for gas-turbine plants that involve bleeding or bypassing the working fluid to adjust performance, speed, temperature or load. The method is used to regulate turbine output, protect components, or improve efficiency within combustion-engine systems.
[F02C 9/54] by throttling the working fluid, by adjusting vanes -- F02C 9/54 covers methods of regulating gas-turbine plants by reducing the flow of the working fluid or by changing the position of guide or inlet vanes. These techniques adjust pressure or mass flow to control speed, power output and combustion stability. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, gas-turbine plants.
[F02C 9/56] with power transmission control -- F02C 9/56 covers control mechanisms that manage the transmission of power in gas-turbine plants, such as regulating gearboxes, shafts and other drivetrain components to ensure efficient and safe operation of the turbine's output.
[F02C 9/58] with control of a variable-pitch propeller -- The CPC subclass F02C 9/58 covers control methods and devices for gas-turbine power plants that incorporate a variable-pitch propeller. It addresses regulation of engine speed, thrust or power output by adjusting propeller blade angle, including feedback and automation schemes that coordinate turbine operation with propeller pitch changes.
[F02D] CONTROLLING COMBUSTION ENGINES -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Vehicle fittings, acting on a single sub-unit only, for automatically controlling vehicle speed -> B60K31/00 - Conjoint control of vehicle sub-units of different type or different function, road vehicle drive control systems for purposes other than the control of a single sub-unit -> B60W  **Glossary:** - Engine: "engine" means a device for continuously converting fluid energy into mechanical power. Thus, this term includes, for example, internal-combustion piston engines, but it excludes single-stroke devices
[F02D 1/00] Controlling, e.g. regulating, fuel injection -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling fuel-injection pumps, e.g. of the high pressure injection type and pertains in particular to mechanical controlling and linkage of mechanically controlled fuel injection pumps.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Controlling low-pressure fuel injection, i.e. where the air-fuel mixture containing fuel thus injected will be substantially compressed by the compression stroke of the engine, by means other than controlling only an injection pump -> F02D3/00
[F02D 1/02] not restricted to adjustment of injection timing, e.g. varying amount of fuel delivered -- This subclass covers methods and devices that regulate fuel injection in combustion engines by varying the quantity of fuel supplied, without being limited to changes in injection timing. It includes systems that modify the amount of fuel delivered per cycle to control engine performance and emissions.
[F02D 1/025] by means dependent on engine working temperature  F02D1/08  takes precedence -- F02D 1/025 covers control mechanisms for combustion engines that adjust operation according to the engine's working temperature, such as temperature-dependent fuel injection or ignition timing. It is a sub-group of F02D 1/08, which deals with temperature-based control methods, within the CPC hierarchy for mechanical engineering and engine control.
[F02D 1/04] by mechanical means dependent on engine speed, e.g. using centrifugal governors -- This subclass concerns mechanical control of combustion engines that varies with engine speed, such as devices that use centrifugal forces to adjust fuel injection or other parameters. Typical examples include centrifugal governors that modulate operation based on rotational speed.
[F02D 1/045] characterised by arrangement of springs or weights -- The F02D 1/045 subclass covers inventions that regulate combustion-engine operation by means of springs or weights, such as mechanical fuel-injection control devices where the timing or quantity of fuel is set through calibrated spring forces or weighted levers. It falls under controlling combustion engines within mechanical engineering.
[F02D 1/06] by means dependent on pressure of engine working fluid -- This CPC subclass deals with control methods for combustion engines that operate according to the pressure of the engine's working fluid, such as fuel-injection or regulation systems that adjust delivery based on cylinder or crankcase pressure. It is used to improve efficiency, emissions, and performance by linking control actions directly to fluid-pressure measurements.
[F02D 1/065] of intake of air -- The F02D 1/065 entry covers methods and devices for controlling the intake of air into a combustion engine, such as regulating airflow to achieve desired fuel-air mixture and engine performance. It is part of the broader hierarchy for mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the control of those engines.
[F02D 1/08] Transmission of control impulse to pump control, e.g. with power drive or power assistance
[F02D 1/10] mechanical -- F02D 1/10 covers mechanical methods for controlling combustion engines, specifically devices that regulate fuel injection and other engine parameters through physical components such as pistons, levers, cams or mechanical governors. It pertains to engine-control mechanisms that are actuated without electronic or hydraulic assistance.
[F02D 11/00] Arrangements for, or adaptations to, non-automatic engine control initiation means, e.g. operator initiated -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hand- and/or foot pedal operated engine control initiation means and control linkages between the hand and/or foot pedal and the controlled device, e.g. throttle valve, carburettor or fuel injector. The control linkage can be mechanical, e.g. combined with power drive assistance, or non-mechanical, e.g. hydraulic linkage e.g. combined with power drive assistance.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Engine control initiation means for reversing -> F02D27/00  **Glossary:** - Power drive assistance: Drive assistance for hand or foot
[F02D 11/02] characterised by hand, foot, or like operator controlled initiation means -- The subclass F02D 11/02 covers engine-control arrangements where the initiation of combustion is performed manually by the operator, using hand, foot or similar controls, rather than by automatic devices. It includes adaptations that allow such non-automatic, operator-initiated start of a combustion engine.
[F02D 11/04] characterised by mechanical control linkages -- This subclass covers non-automatic engine-control initiation arrangements that rely on mechanical control linkages such as levers, cams, rods or gears to start or modify combustion processes. It applies to manually operated systems where the operator directly activates the control mechanism rather than using automatic electronic or hydraulic devices.
[F02D 11/06] characterised by non-mechanical control linkages, e.g. fluid control linkages or by control linkages with power drive or assistance -- F02D 11/06 covers arrangements or adaptations for non-automatic initiation of engine control that are operator-initiated, where the control linkages are not purely mechanical. Examples include fluid-based control linkages or linkages assisted by powered drives, providing alternative means to start or adjust engine operation.
[F02D 11/08] of the pneumatic type -- This subclass covers pneumatic arrangements that enable an operator to manually initiate or control a combustion engine, such as hand-operated air-pressure valves, push-button blow-by devices, or other air-driven mechanisms that start or modulate engine operation without automatic control. It also includes adaptations of the engine or auxiliary components to accommodate such non-automatic pneumatic control.
