It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and controlling combustion engines.
[F02D 1/12] non-mechanical, e.g. hydraulic -- F02D 1/12 covers non-mechanical means, for example hydraulic systems, used to regulate fuel injection and other control functions in combustion engines. It falls under the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and controlling combustion engines, focusing on fluid-based or other non-mechanical actuation methods for engine control.
[F02D 1/122] control impulse depending only on engine speed -- F02D 1/122 refers to control of combustion engines where the fuel-injection impulse is varied exclusively according to engine speed. Such systems adjust the amount of fuel delivered in each cycle based solely on the measured rotational speed, without using other parameters such as load or temperature.
[F02D 1/125] using a centrifugal governor -- The subclass F02D 1/125 covers the use of a centrifugal governor to regulate fuel-injection or other operating parameters in combustion engines, providing automatic control of engine speed and performance. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Controlling Combustion Engines.
[F02D 1/127] using the pressure developed in a pump -- F02D 1/127 covers methods that exploit the hydraulic pressure generated by a pump to control fuel injection or other combustion-engine functions. The pressure is used as a feedback or actuation source to regulate the amount or timing of fuel delivered, thereby influencing engine performance and emissions.
[F02D 1/14] pneumatic -- F02D 1/14 refers to pneumatic control systems for combustion engines, covering devices that use air pressure to regulate engine functions such as fuel injection, valve timing or idle speed. The classification groups any pneumatic-actuated mechanisms that adjust engine performance.
[F02D 1/16] Adjustment of injection timing -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Not restricted to adjustment of injection timing, e.g. varying amount of fuel delivered -> F02D1/02
[F02D 1/162] by mechanical means dependent on engine speed for angular adjustment of driving and driven shafts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  E.g. using a centrifugal governor to adjust the angular timing of pump shaft.
[F02D 1/18] with non-mechanical means for transmitting control impulse -- F02D 1/18 covers methods for controlling combustion engines that use non-mechanical means to transmit a control impulse, such as electronic, hydraulic or pneumatic signals instead of direct mechanical linkages.
[F02D 1/183] hydraulic -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hydraulic adjustment of injection timing. E.g. using a hydraulic phase shifter device between pump and pump drive or angularly adjusting a cam profile to adjust the timing.
[F02D 13/00] Controlling the engine output power by varying inlet or exhaust valve operating characteristics, e.g. timing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling the engine output power by varying inlet or exhaust valve operating characteristics, e.g. timing. The inlet and exhaust valves within an internal combustion engine are used to control the flow of the intake and exhaust gases into and out of the combustion chamber. The timing, duration and lift of these valve has a significant impact on engine performance. In a standard engine, the valve events are fixed, so performance at different loads and speeds is always a compromise between driveability, e.g. power and torque, fuel economy and emissions. An engine equipped with a variable valve actuation system is freed from this constraint, allowing performance to be improved over the engine operating range.  **Glossary:** - Miller-Atkinson cycle: Combustion cycle characterised by extending the stroke and raising the expansion and compression ratio. Premature detonation is overcome by using valve timing variations to make the effective compression and expansion strokes asymmetrical.
[F02D 13/02] during engine operation -- F02D 13/02 covers techniques for controlling a combustion engine's output power by adjusting inlet or exhaust valve timing while the engine is operating. The classification addresses dynamic valve-actuation strategies used during normal running conditions to modulate performance.
[F02D 13/0203] Variable control of intake and exhaust valves -- F02D 13/0203 covers technologies that vary the operating characteristics of intake and exhaust valves, such as timing, to control the output power of combustion engines. The classification addresses mechanisms for adjusting valve opening and closing in real time to optimise engine performance.
[F02D 13/0207] changing valve lift or valve lift and timing -- F02D 13/0207 relates to methods of controlling a combustion-engine's output power by varying the lift of inlet or exhaust valves, the lift together with valve timing, or other valve-operating characteristics, as part of the broader category of mechanical-engineering combustion-engine control.
[F02D 13/0211] the change of valve timing is caused by the change in valve lift, i.e. both valve lift and timing are functionally related -- This CPC subclass covers devices that vary engine output power by linking valve lift and timing, so that a change in lift automatically changes the timing of inlet or exhaust valves. It falls under controlling combustion engines within mechanical engineering.
[F02D 13/0215] changing the valve timing only -- F02D 13/0215 covers methods for adjusting a combustion engine's output power solely by altering the timing of its inlet or exhaust valves. This includes any technique that changes valve opening or closing moments without modifying lift or duration, used to control engine performance.
[F02D 13/0219] by shifting the phase, i.e. the opening periods of the valves are constant -- The subclass F02D 13/0219 covers control of a combustion-engine's output by altering the phase of the valve timing while the valve opening periods remain constant. It concerns shifting the opening and closing moments of inlet or exhaust valves to vary power without changing lift duration.
[F02D 13/0223] Variable control of the intake valves only -- The code F02D 13/0223 covers technologies that regulate a combustion engine's output power by adjusting only the intake-valve characteristics, such as timing or lift. It pertains to variable control of the inlet valves without altering exhaust-valve operation, enabling precise management of engine performance.
[F02D 13/0226] changing valve lift or valve lift and timing -- F02D 13/0226 covers methods for controlling the output power of combustion engines by altering the lift of inlet or exhaust valves, or by adjusting both valve lift and timing. These techniques modify valve operating characteristics to influence engine performance.
[F02D 13/023] the change of valve timing is caused by the change in valve lift, i.e. both valve lift and timing are functionally related -- F02D 13/023 covers engine control techniques in which the timing of inlet or exhaust valves is varied as a direct result of changing valve lift, so that lift and timing are functionally linked to regulate output power.
[F02D 13/0234] changing the valve timing only -- This subclass covers methods for adjusting engine output power by altering only the timing of inlet or exhaust valves, without changing other valve parameters, to control combustion processes in mechanical engines.
[F02D 13/0238] by shifting the phase, i.e. the opening periods of the valves are constant -- The subclass deals with methods of controlling the power output of internal-combustion engines by shifting the phase of inlet or exhaust valve operation while keeping the valve opening periods constant, i.e., adjusting valve timing without altering lift duration.
[F02D 13/0242] Variable control of the exhaust valves only -- The code F02D 13/0242 defines the variable control of exhaust valves only, a method for adjusting engine output power by changing the timing, lift or duration of the exhaust valves without altering inlet valve operation. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, controlling combustion engines, focusing on exhaust-valve-only strategies for power regulation.
[F02D 13/0246] changing valve lift or valve lift and timing -- This subclass covers techniques for controlling a combustion engine's output power by altering the lift of inlet or exhaust valves, or by varying valve lift together with timing. It includes mechanisms that adjust the magnitude of valve lift, the timing of valve opening or closing, or both, to modify air-fuel flow and thereby influence engine performance.
[F02D 13/0249] changing the valve timing only -- The entry F02D 13/0249 covers techniques for controlling a combustion-engine's output power by adjusting only the timing of inlet or exhaust valves, without changing lift or other valve parameters. It is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and controlling combustion engines.
[F02D 13/0253] Fully variable control of valve lift and timing using camless actuation systems such as hydraulic, pneumatic or electromagnetic actuators, e.g. solenoid valves -- This CPC subclass covers technologies that provide fully variable valve lift and timing without a camshaft, using actuators such as hydraulic, pneumatic, or electromagnetic solenoid devices to adjust inlet or exhaust valve operation for precise engine power control. Such systems enable continuous adjustment of valve opening characteristics to optimize combustion across operating conditions.
[F02D 13/0257] Independent control of two or more intake or exhaust valves respectively, i.e. one of two intake valves remains closed or is opened partially while the other is fully opened -- F02D 13/0257 covers mechanisms that independently control two or more intake or exhaust valves, permitting one valve to remain closed or partially opened while another is fully opened, thereby adjusting engine output power through variations in valve timing.
[F02D 13/0261] Controlling the valve overlap -- The F02D 13/0261 classification covers methods for controlling valve overlap in combustion engines, that is, adjusting the simultaneous opening period of inlet and exhaust valves. By varying the timing or duration of this overlap, the engine's output power and efficiency can be managed without altering other valve parameters. This is a sub-category of techniques that regulate engine performance through valve-operating characteristics.
[F02D 13/0265] Negative valve overlap for temporarily storing residual gas in the cylinder -- The subclass deals with controlling engine output power by using a negative valve-overlap strategy, where the inlet and exhaust valves are timed so that they do not overlap and residual exhaust gas is temporarily retained in the cylinder. This retained gas influences combustion and torque characteristics, providing a means to adjust power delivery.
[F02D 13/0269] Controlling the valves to perform a Miller-Atkinson cycle -- This subclass covers methods of regulating inlet or exhaust valve timing so the engine operates on a Miller-Atkinson cycle, which lengthens expansion and reduces effective compression to improve efficiency. It pertains to control strategies that adjust valve opening and closing events to achieve the desired cycle.
[F02D 13/0273] Multiple actuations of a valve within an engine cycle -- This subclass covers techniques that open or close an inlet or exhaust valve more than once in a single engine cycle to regulate output power, typically by adjusting timing, lift or duration. It is situated under controlling combustion engines in mechanical engineering.
[F02D 13/0276] Actuation of an additional valve for a special application, e.g. for decompression, exhaust gas recirculation or cylinder scavenging -- This subclass covers mechanisms that actuate an auxiliary valve in a combustion engine for special purposes such as decompression, exhaust-gas recirculation or cylinder scavenging, thereby influencing inlet or exhaust timing to control output power and emissions.
[F02D 13/028] for two-stroke engines -- F02D 13/028 relates to controlling the output power of two-stroke internal combustion engines by varying inlet or exhaust valve operating characteristics such as timing, lift or duration, thereby adjusting the engine's performance.
[F02D 13/0284] Variable control of exhaust valves only -- The CPC class F02D 13/0284 covers methods for regulating a combustion engine's power output by adjusting only the exhaust-valve timing or lift characteristics. It pertains to variable control strategies that modify exhaust-valve operation without altering inlet-valve settings.
[F02D 13/04] using engine as brake -- The subclass covers techniques that reduce or absorb engine power by adjusting inlet or exhaust valve timing, duration or lift so the engine acts as a brake. It includes control methods that use valve-operating changes to dissipate kinetic energy and slow the vehicle, rather than delivering positive torque.
[F02D 13/06] Cutting-out cylinders -- This subclass covers methods of reducing engine output by disabling one or more cylinders, typically by altering valve timing or lift so that the cylinder does not participate in the combustion cycle. It is employed for fuel-saving or emissions-control purposes in multi-cylinder engines.
[F02D 13/08] for rendering engine inoperative or idling -- A subclass for controlling combustion-engine output that covers methods of rendering the engine inoperative or keeping it at idle by varying inlet or exhaust valve operating characteristics such as timing. It is used to describe techniques that deliberately shut down or reduce power output without stopping the engine entirely.
[F02D 15/00] Varying compression ratio -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Varying compression ratio to adjust internal combustion engine cylinder compression ratios. For automotive use this needs to be done dynamically in response to the load and driving demands. Higher loads require lower ratios to be more efficient and vice versa. Variable compression engines allow for the volume above the piston at TDC to be changed.  **Glossary:** - TDC: Top Dead Centre
[F02D 15/02] by alteration or displacement of piston stroke -- The CPC subclass F02D 15/02 relates to methods of varying a combustion engine's compression ratio by altering or displacing the piston stroke, such as adjusting stroke length or piston travel to change compression during operation.
[F02D 15/04] by alteration of volume of compression space without changing piston stroke -- The subclass covers engines that vary their compression ratio by changing the volume of the compression space while keeping the piston stroke fixed, such as through adjustable cylinder heads, movable liners, or variable geometry chambers, allowing the engine to adapt performance without altering stroke length.
[F02D 17/00] Controlling engines by cutting out individual cylinders -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling engines by cutting out individual cylinders; Rendering engines inoperative or idling. Cylinder deactivation is used to reduce the fuel consumption and emissions of an internal combustion engine during light-load operation. In typical light-load driving the driver uses only around 30 percent of an engine's maximum power. In these conditions the throttle valve is nearly closed and the engine needs to work to draw air. This causes an inefficiency known as pumping loss. Some large capacity engines need to be throttled so much at light load that the cylinder pressure at top dead centre is approximately half that of a small 4-cylinder engine. Low cylinder pressure means low fuel efficiency. The use of cylinder deactivation at light load means there are fewer cylinders drawing air from the intake manifold, which works to increase its air pressure. Operation without variable displacement is wasteful because fuel is continuously pumped into each cylinder and combusted even though maximum performance is not required.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Varying inlet or exhaust valve operating characteristics -> F02D13/00 - Cutting out cylinders by varying inlet or outlet valve operating characteristics -> F02D13/06 - Rendering engine inoperative or idling by varying inlet or outlet valve operating characteristics -> F02D13/08
[F02D 17/02] Cutting-out
[F02D 17/023] the inactive cylinders acting as compressor other than for pumping air into the exhaust system
[F02D 17/026] delivering compressed fluid, e.g. air, reformed gas, to the active cylinders other than during starting -- This subclass deals with supplying a compressed fluid such as air or reformed gas to the cylinders that are actually firing, while the engine is running, and explicitly excludes any delivery that occurs during the start-up phase. It applies to systems that manage cylinder deactivation by providing the required intake flow directly to the active cylinders to sustain combustion and performance.
[F02D 17/04] rendering engines inoperative or idling, e.g. caused by abnormal conditions -- The CPC subclass F02D 17/04 covers techniques for rendering an engine inoperative or causing it to idle, such as cutting out individual cylinders in response to abnormal conditions. It falls under controlling combustion engines within mechanical engineering, focusing on selective cylinder shutdown to protect or manage engine performance.
[F02D 19/00] Controlling peculiar to specified types or adaptations of engines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mechanical and control aspects of engines using gaseous fuels, solid fuels or a combination of gaseous and non gaseous fuels. The latter simultaneously or non simultaneously used. Important aspects of the group are: configuration and control of the fuel supply system, special operating modes depending on the type of fuel and measuring and estimating of the fuel and engine parameters.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Controlling engines being supplied with gaseous non-fuel substances -> F02D21/00 - Electronic control of engines using non-liquid fuels -> F02D41/0025  **Glossary:** - Non-fuel gas: Oxygen, exhaust gas etc.
[F02D 19/02] peculiar to engines working with gaseous fuels -- F02D 19/02 deals with control methods that are specific to combustion engines designed to run on gaseous fuels, including adaptations of fuel injection, ignition timing and air-fuel ratio management that are unique to gas-fueled operation.
[F02D 19/021] Control of components of the fuel supply system -- The code F02D 19/021 covers systems and techniques for regulating fuel-supply components in combustion engines, such as pumps, injectors, filters, carburetors and fuel lines. It includes devices that adjust fuel flow, pressure or mixture to achieve desired engine performance or emissions.
[F02D 19/022] to adjust the fuel pressure, temperature or composition -- F02D 19/022 covers methods and devices for adjusting fuel pressure, temperature or composition in combustion engines, usually as part of control systems designed for specific engine types or adaptations.
[F02D 19/023] to adjust the fuel mass or volume flow -- The CPC class F02D 19/023 covers devices or methods that regulate the amount of fuel supplied to a combustion engine by adjusting its mass or volume flow, aimed at controlling engine performance in particular engine types or adaptations.
[F02D 19/024] by controlling fuel injectors -- F02D 19/024 concerns techniques for managing combustion-engine operation by controlling the fuel injectors. It includes methods that adjust injector timing, duration or quantity to influence engine performance, emissions and fuel efficiency.
[F02D 19/025] Failure diagnosis or prevention; Safety measures; Testing -- F02D 19/025 relates to control measures for combustion engines that focus on diagnosing failures, implementing safety provisions and conducting testing, especially for engines of particular types or adaptations. It encompasses methods and devices that prevent malfunctions and ensure safe operation of these engines.
[F02D 19/026] Measuring or estimating parameters related to the fuel supply system -- The F02D 19/026 subclass covers techniques for measuring or estimating any parameter of a fuel-supply system in a combustion engine, such as flow rate, pressure, temperature, or fuel composition. It is used when controlling the engine's performance, emissions, or efficiency through precise monitoring of the fuel delivery network.
[F02D 19/027] Determining the fuel pressure, temperature or volume flow, the fuel tank fill level or a valve position -- This subclass covers methods and devices for measuring fuel pressure, temperature, volume flow, fuel-tank fill level or valve position in combustion engines. It is part of the controlling combustion engines group, specifically for particular engine types or adaptations.
[F02D 19/028] by estimation, i.e. without using direct measured parameter of a corresponding sensor -- This sub-class covers control strategies for combustion engines that rely on calculated or inferred values rather than direct sensor readings. It includes approaches that estimate parameters such as air-fuel ratio, temperature or pressure to adjust engine operation. The method is used when a corresponding sensor is unavailable or impractical.
[F02D 19/029] Determining density, viscosity, concentration or composition -- This subgroup covers methods and devices for measuring fluid properties such as density, viscosity, concentration or composition to control combustion engines, especially those with particular adaptations. It includes sensors, analytical techniques and on-board diagnostics that provide data for adjusting fuel-air mixtures, lubrication or emissions control.
[F02D 19/04] peculiar to engines working with solid fuels, e.g. pulverised coal -- F02D 19/04 covers control measures that are specific to combustion engines operating on solid fuels, such as pulverised coal. It includes techniques for regulating fuel delivery, air-fuel mixing, ignition timing and combustion monitoring that are adapted to the characteristics of solid-fuel combustion. This subgroup falls under controlling combustion engines within the broader mechanical engineering hierarchy.
[F02D 19/06] peculiar to engines working with pluralities of fuels, e.g. alternatively with light and heavy fuel oil, other than engines indifferent to the fuel consumed -- F02D 19/06 refers to control mechanisms for combustion engines that are specifically adapted to handle multiple fuel types, such as alternating between light and heavy fuel oil, and excludes engines that are indifferent to the fuel used.
[F02D 19/0602] Control of components of the fuel supply system -- The entry F02D 19/0602 covers methods and devices that regulate elements of a combustion engine's fuel supply system, such as injectors, pumps, or pressure regulators, and is intended for particular engine types or special adaptations of those engines. It falls within the broader category of controlling combustion engines in mechanical engineering.
[F02D 19/0605] to adjust the fuel pressure or temperature -- F02D 19/0605 falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → controlling combustion engines → controlling peculiar to specified types or adaptations of engines. It designates features that adjust the fuel pressure or temperature to regulate engine operation, covering devices or methods that modify these parameters for optimal combustion control.
[F02D 19/0607] to adjust the fuel mass or volume flow -- F02D 19/0607 covers techniques for adjusting the fuel mass or volume flow in combustion engines, aimed at controlling engine performance in specific engine types or adapted configurations. It includes devices or methods that regulate fuel delivery to achieve desired combustion characteristics.
[F02D 19/061] by controlling fuel injectors -- The F02D 19/061 subclass covers methods and devices that regulate fuel injectors in internal combustion engines, including control of injection timing, quantity and pressure to optimise performance, fuel-economy and emissions. It belongs to the CPC hierarchy under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → controlling combustion engines, specifically for controlling peculiar to particular engine types or adaptations.

[F02D 19/0618] depending on the engine's or vehicle's position, e.g. on/off road or proximity to a harbor -- F02D 19/0618 covers control strategies for combustion engines that are selected according to the engine's or vehicle's position or operational environment, such as distinguishing between on-road and off-road use or proximity to a harbor, influencing fuel, ignition or emission settings.
[F02D 19/0621] Purging of the fuel system -- F02D 19/0621 concerns methods and apparatus for removing unwanted substances from a fuel system in combustion engines, such as flushing, venting or using cleaning agents to maintain fuel purity. It is used to ensure proper fuel flow, prevent contamination and support reliable engine performance.
[F02D 19/0623] Failure diagnosis or prevention; Safety measures; Testing -- F02D 19/0623 covers techniques for detecting and preventing failures in combustion engines, including safety devices and testing methods used to ensure reliable operation of specific engine types or adaptations. It is applied when a system's control strategy must incorporate diagnostic or protective measures to avoid malfunctions.

These diagnostics support precise control of engine performance and emissions.

[F02D 19/0634] Determining a density, viscosity, composition or concentration  F02D19/087  takes precedence -- F02D 19/0634 is a sub-group of the mechanical-engineering class for controlling combustion engines that deals with methods for determining the density, viscosity, composition or concentration of fuels, lubricants or other working fluids. It applies to specific engine types or adaptations where such property measurements are essential for performance control or emission management.
[F02D 19/0636] by estimation, i.e. without using direct measurements of a corresponding sensor -- This CPC subclass concerns methods for controlling combustion engines by estimating operating parameters instead of relying on direct sensor readings. [F02D 19/0639] characterised by the type of fuels -- This subclass concerns control techniques or devices for combustion engines that are defined by the specific type of fuel employed, such as gasoline, diesel, bio-fuels, hydrogen or other alternatives. [F02D 19/0642] at least one fuel being gaseous, the other fuels being gaseous or liquid at standard conditions -- F02D 19/0642 concerns control strategies for combustion engines that use at least one gaseous fuel while the remaining fuels may be gaseous or liquid under standard conditions. [F02D 19/0644] the gaseous fuel being hydrogen, ammonia or carbon monoxide -- This subclass covers control methods for combustion engines that use hydrogen, ammonia or carbon monoxide as their gaseous fuel. [F02D 19/0647] the gaseous fuel being liquefied petroleum gas [LPG], liquefied natural gas [LNG], compressed natural gas [CNG] or dimethyl ether [DME]
[F02D 19/0649] Liquid fuels having different boiling temperatures, volatilities, densities, viscosities, cetane or octane numbers -- This subclass covers control strategies for combustion engines that use liquid fuels with varying boiling points, volatilities, densities, viscosities, or cetane and octane numbers, such as blended or multi-grade fuels. It includes techniques for adjusting injection timing, pressure, or mixture composition to maintain optimal performance and emissions across the differing fuel characteristics.

[F02D 19/0655] at least one fuel being an alcohol, e.g. ethanol  F02D19/084  takes precedence -- The CPC subclass F02D 19/0655 falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and the control of combustion engines, specifically covering control methods for engines that use at least one alcohol fuel such as ethanol. It is a more specific sub-group subordinate to F02D 19/084, which has priority over it.
[F02D 19/0657] Heavy or light fuel oils; Fuels characterised by their impurities such as sulfur content or differences in grade, e.g. for ships -- This subclass deals with control strategies and apparatus for combustion engines that run on heavy or light fuel oils, especially when the fuels are defined by impurity levels such as sulfur content or by different grades, as commonly required for marine propulsion.
[F02D 19/066] Retrofit of secondary fuel supply systems; Conversion of engines to operate on multiple fuels -- F02D 19/066 addresses modifications that add a secondary fuel supply to an existing combustion engine or convert the engine to operate on two or more fuels, encompassing the hardware and control changes needed for dual-fuel operation. It is used for retrofitting engines to increase fuel flexibility, improve efficiency, or meet emission requirements.
[F02D 19/0663] Details on the fuel supply system, e.g. tanks, valves, pipes, pumps, rails, injectors or mixers -- F02D 19/0663 covers the components and arrangements of fuel supply systems for combustion engines, including tanks, valves, pipes, pumps, rails, injectors and mixers. It is used to classify inventions that focus on how fuel is stored, delivered and mixed within engines for improved control and performance.
[F02D 19/0665] Tanks, e.g. multiple tanks -- F02D 19/0665 relates to the control of combustion engines through the use of one or more tanks, such as multiple fuel or fluid reservoirs, designed to manage engine operation, fuel supply, or related parameters. It covers specific arrangements, monitoring, and switching techniques that adapt engine performance by employing distinct tank configurations.
[F02D 19/0668] Treating or cleaning means; Fuel filters -- F02D 19/0668 concerns devices and methods for treating or cleaning fuel, especially fuel filters, used to remove contaminants and improve the performance of combustion engines. [F02D 19/0671] Means to generate or modify a fuel, e.g. reformers, electrolytic cells or membranes -- This CPC subclass covers devices that produce or alter fuel for combustion engines, such as fuel reformers, electrolytic cells and membrane systems, used to improve engine performance or emissions. It is positioned in the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under combustion-engine control and specific adaptations.
[F02D 19/0673] Valves; Pressure or flow regulators; Mixers -- The subclass F02D 19/0673 relates to valves, pressure or flow regulators and mixers that are specially designed for particular types or adaptations of combustion engines. [F02D 19/0676] Multi-way valves; Switch-over valves -- The CPC subclass F02D 19/0676 covers multi-way and switch-over valves used to direct fluid flow in combustion-engine systems. These components enable rapid changeover between different inlet or exhaust passages, supporting engine control strategies such as variable timing, load adaptation or startup-shutdown sequencing.
[F02D 19/0678] Pressure or flow regulators therefor; Fuel metering valves therefor -- F02D 19/0678 covers pressure or flow regulators and fuel-metering valves that are used to control the operation of combustion engines, especially those designed for particular engine types or specialized adaptations. It resides within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under combustion-engine control.
[F02D 19/0681] Shut-off valves; Check valves; Safety valves; Pressure relief valves -- It covers valve devices used to control flow and pressure in combustion engines, including shut-off, check, safety and pressure-relief valves. The class is limited to valves designed for specific types or adaptations of engines within the controlling-engine subgroup.
[F02D 19/0684] High pressure fuel injection systems; Details on pumps, rails or the arrangement of valves in the fuel supply and return systems -- F02D 19/0684 covers high-pressure fuel injection systems for combustion engines, focusing on the design and configuration of pumps, fuel rails and the valve arrangement in both supply and return circuits. It relates to methods of controlling engine combustion through specialized fuel-delivery hardware.
[F02D 19/0686] Injectors -- The F02D 19/0686 subclass concerns fuel injectors that are specially designed or adapted for particular combustion-engine types. It includes devices that precisely meter and introduce fuel into the cylinder, combustion chamber or nozzle to control the engine's combustion process.
[F02D 19/0689] for in-cylinder direct injection -- F02D 19/0689 covers control techniques applied to in-cylinder direct injection systems in combustion engines. [F02D 19/0692] Arrangement of multiple injectors per combustion chamber -- The F02D 19/0692 subclass deals with designs that place more than one fuel injector within a single combustion chamber to improve fuel distribution, mixture formation, and combustion efficiency. It covers the geometric layout, positioning, and interaction of multiple injectors in engines where precise control of the combustion process is required.
[F02D 19/0694] operating with a plurality of fuels -- F02D 19/0694 covers methods and systems for controlling combustion engines that are designed to run on more than one type of fuel simultaneously or by switching between fuels. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and specific control adaptations.
[F02D 19/0697] Arrangement of fuel supply systems on engines or vehicle bodies; Components of the fuel supply system being combined with another device -- The CPC class F02D 19/0697 covers the layout and integration of fuel-supply equipment on internal-combustion engines or vehicle bodies, specifically when fuel-system parts are combined with other devices such as pumps, filters, injectors or control units. It addresses arrangements that merge fuel-delivery components with additional functions to optimise space, performance or safety in specialised engine adaptations.
[F02D 19/08] simultaneously using pluralities of fuels -- The subclass defines control techniques for combustion engines that operate on two or more fuels at the same time, such as combined gasoline-LPG or diesel-bio-fuel systems. [F02D 19/081] Adjusting the fuel composition or mixing ratio; Transitioning from one fuel to the other -- F02D 19/081 concerns techniques for modifying the fuel make-up or the ratio in which different fuels are blended, and for smoothly switching a combustion engine from one fuel type to another during operation. It addresses control strategies that manage these adjustments to optimise performance, emissions or adaptability of the engine.
[F02D 19/082] Premixed fuels, i.e. emulsions or blends -- This subclass covers the use of premixed fuels-emulsions or fuel blends-to control combustion in engines, including methods and apparatus for preparing, storing, and delivering such mixtures to improve efficiency, performance, or emissions characteristics.

The subclass falls under controlling combustion engines adapted to particular engine types or modifications.
[F02D 19/085] Control based on the fuel type or composition -- The F02D 19/085 subclass covers control strategies for combustion engines that are tailored to the type or composition of the fuel being used, such as different fuels, blends or additives. It includes systems that modify ignition timing, air-fuel ratio, injection parameters or other operating conditions to optimise performance, efficiency and emissions based on the specific fuel characteristics.
[F02D 19/087] with determination of densities, viscosities, composition, concentration or mixture ratios of fuels -- The code covers control systems for combustion engines that incorporate measurement of fuel properties-density, viscosity, composition, concentration or mixture ratios-to adjust engine operation. It applies to methods that determine these parameters as part of the engine-control strategy.
[F02D 19/088] by estimation, i.e. without using direct measurements of a corresponding sensor -- F02D 19/088 covers control of combustion engines that is performed by estimation, using model-based or observer techniques rather than direct measurements from sensors. This entry applies to methods that infer engine states or parameters to regulate performance without relying on actual sensor data.
[F02D 19/10] peculiar to compression-ignition engines in which the main fuel is gaseous -- F02D 19/10 covers control strategies that are unique to compression-ignition engines whose primary fuel is a gas. It includes methods for regulating fuel injection, ignition timing and air-fuel mixture specific to gas-fueled diesel engines.
[F02D 19/105] operating in a special mode, e.g. in a liquid fuel only mode for starting -- This subclass deals with control strategies that run a combustion engine in a designated special mode, for example switching to liquid-fuel-only operation during start-up. It covers methods and systems that temporarily alter the fuel or operating parameters to facilitate ignition or initial motion. Under the CPC hierarchy it falls under mechanical engineering - combustion engines - controlling combustion engines - controlling peculiar to specified types or adaptations of engines.
[F02D 19/12] peculiar to engines working with non-fuel substances or with anti-knock agents, e.g. with anti-knock fuel -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The group covers the control of engines, which are supplied with non-fuel substances or anti-knock agents. This includes controlling engines wherein engines are supplied with water.
[F02D 2001/0005] Details, component parts or accessories of centrifugal governors -- The CPC subclass F02D 2001/0005 covers the specific details, individual component parts and accessories that make up or are fitted to centrifugal governors, such as springs, shafts, linkages, pivots, adjusting mechanisms and protective housings. It includes designs and variations of these elements intended to regulate speed in rotating machinery.
[F02D 2001/001] Arrangement of centrifugal weights -- F02D 2001/001 relates to the design and positioning of centrifugal weights used to influence the dynamics of rotating components. It covers the arrangement of such masses to achieve balance, stabilize motion, or generate controlled centrifugal forces in machines such as rotors, turbines, gyroscopes and amusement-ride devices.
[F02D 2001/0015] the weights being cup-shaped and carrying governor springs -- F02D 2001/0015 relates to hydraulic pumps that incorporate a cup-shaped weight which also carries the governor spring. The entry specifies pump designs where the cup-shaped weight serves as the load-bearing element for the speed-governing spring mechanism.
[F02D 2001/002] Arrangement of governor springs -- The CPC subclass F02D 2001/002 covers the design and placement of springs that provide the restoring force in speed-governors for rotating machinery such as turbines, pumps and engines. It includes arrangements where springs are positioned in series, parallel, pre-loaded or otherwise configured to control governor response and maintain constant speed.
[F02D 2001/0025] having at least two springs, one of them being idling spring -- The subclass F02D 2001/0025 covers hydraulic or pneumatic power units that incorporate at least two springs, with one designated as an idling or standby spring. The idling spring is engaged only when the primary spring reaches the end of its stroke, helping to maintain smoother operation and improve the unit's performance.
[F02D 2001/003] the main spring being active at maximum speed only -- F02D 2001/003 designates internal combustion engines in which the primary spring is configured to become operative only at the engine's maximum speed, typically to regulate valve timing or limit overspeed conditions. The feature is used to provide a safety or performance function that activates exclusively at high-rotational rates.
[F02D 2001/0035] the main spring being active at all speeds, e.g. its tension varying with the load, i.e. the position of pump control -- This subgroup concerns pumps that keep the main spring engaged at every speed, allowing the spring tension to vary with the load and thereby affect the pump control position. It relates to designs where the spring's active role continuously adapts to operating conditions.
[F02D 2001/004] Arrangement of linkages between governor sleeve and pump control -- This subclass covers the mechanical arrangement of linkages that connect a governor sleeve to the pump-control mechanism in hydraulic or fluid-power pumps, detailing the geometry, pivot points and motion-transmission features used to regulate pump output.
[F02D 2001/0045] Arrangement of means for influencing governor characteristics by operator -- This subclass covers arrangements that enable an operator to modify governor parameters such as speed, load or fuel flow in engines, turbines, compressors or similar machines. It includes manual or electronic interfaces-levers, knobs, switches or control units-that let the operator influence the governor's characteristics.
[F02D 2001/005] varying main spring tension -- F02D 2001/005 covers inventions in which the tension of a primary spring can be varied, allowing the stored energy to be adjusted for different operating conditions. It applies to mechanisms such as clocks, watches, door closers or other devices that rely on a main spring to deliver controlled motion. Adjustability of the spring's force is the defining feature of this subclass.
[F02D 2001/0055] varying pivotal point of linkages between governor sleeve and pump control -- The CPC subclass F02D 2001/0055 covers pump governor mechanisms in which the pivot point of the linkage between the governor sleeve and the pump control can be varied or adjusted. By changing this geometric position, the governor's response and speed-regulation characteristics of the pump can be modified, affecting performance in centrifugal or similar pumps.
[F02D 2001/006] Assembling; Disassembling; Replacing -- F02D covers devices for handling or treating liquids or gases in heating, cooling, and ventilation systems. The subgroup 2001/006 relates to methods and mechanisms for assembling, disassembling, or replacing components of such installations, including modular sections, fittings, and serviceable parts.
[F02D 2001/0065] Selection of particular materials -- The CPC subclass F02D 2001/0065 covers methods and apparatuses that identify, separate or otherwise choose specific materials from a mixture during a hydraulic or fluid-based process. It includes techniques for selecting particular substances based on properties such as composition, density, particle size or chemical reactivity.
[F02D 2001/007] Means for adjusting stops for minimum and maximum fuel delivery -- F02D 2001/007 covers mechanisms that adjust the stopping points of fuel-metering devices to set the minimum and maximum amounts of fuel delivered to an internal-combustion engine. It includes adjustable cams, levers or electronic controls that limit fuel flow at the low-end and high-end of the engine's operating range.
[F02D 2001/0075] using engine temperature, e.g. to adjust the idling speed at cold start -- The CPC entry F02D 2001/0075 covers control methods for internal combustion engines that use measured engine temperature to modify the idling speed during a cold start, such as adjusting fuel or air delivery to improve warm-up, reduce emissions or enhance drivability. It falls under class F02D for controlling combustion engines, specifically the 2001 group dealing with engine temperature-based regulation.
[F02D 2001/008] using intake air pressure, e.g. adjusting full load stop at high supercharging pressures -- F02D 2001/008 covers methods and devices that exploit the pressure of air entering an engine, such as adjusting or terminating full-load operation when supercharging generates high intake pressures, thereby improving control, safety or efficiency of highly boosted internal-combustion engines.
[F02D 2001/0085] Arrangements using fuel pressure for controlling fuel delivery in quantity or timing -- F02D 2001/0085 covers fuel-delivery arrangements that use the pressure of the fuel itself as a control variable to set the quantity or timing of injection. The code includes pressure-regulated pumps, accumulators, sensors and valves that modulate injection events based on measured or preset pressure levels, typically found in diesel or gasoline direct-injection engines.
[F02D 2001/009] Means for varying the pressure of fuel supply pump according to engine working parameters -- F02D 2001/009 classifies devices that adjust the pressure output of a fuel-supply pump in response to engine operating conditions such as speed, load or temperature. It covers control mechanisms, sensors and actuators that continuously vary pump pressure to optimise combustion efficiency and emissions.
[F02D 2001/0095] Mounting of control means with respect to injection apparatus or the engine -- This subgroup addresses the ways control devices-such as sensors, actuators or electronic modules-are mounted or positioned relative to a fuel-injection system or the engine itself. It includes brackets, housings and integration methods that ensure proper alignment, vibration damping and accessibility for adjustment or replacement.
[F02D 2001/082] electric -- The code refers to pumps that are powered by electricity, encompassing electric motors, drive mechanisms and associated control devices used to operate the pump. It covers any pumping apparatus whose energy source is an electric motor, regardless of the fluid being moved.
[F02D 2001/085] using solenoids -- This CPC subgroup refers to pump designs that employ solenoids to drive or control moving parts such as valves, pistons or rotors, using electromagnetic force for starting, stopping or speed regulation. It covers any arrangement where a solenoid provides the primary actuation in a liquid-moving device.
[F02D 2001/087] using step motors -- This CPC subclass covers centrifugal pump designs that use stepper motors as the drive source, providing precise speed and positional control for fluid handling.
[F02D 2001/165] by means dependent on engine load -- This subclass covers arrangements or methods that control or adjust engine functions according to the engine's load, such as varying fuel supply, air intake, ignition timing or auxiliary systems in response to load signals. It includes devices that detect load and modulate operation based on that information.
[F02D 2001/167] by means dependent on engine working temperature, e.g. at cold start -- The entry covers devices or methods that adjust engine operation according to the engine's working temperature, such as controls activated during a cold start. It includes temperature-sensitive pumps, valves, fuel-injection or ignition systems that modify supply or timing based on measured heat levels. This classification is used for patents dealing with temperature-dependent engine-starting technologies.
[F02D 2001/186] using a pressure-actuated piston for adjustment of a stationary cam or roller support -- The CPC subclass F02D 2001/186 covers devices in which a pressure-actuated piston is employed to adjust the position of a fixed cam or roller support, typically to vary clearance or alignment in hydraulic power-transmission systems. This adjustment is achieved by applying fluid pressure to move the piston, thereby shifting the cam or roller without manual intervention.
[F02D 2007/025] Controlling compressed air quantity or pressure -- The CPC entry F02D 2007/025 covers devices and methods for regulating the amount or pressure of compressed air in pneumatic systems, such as pressure regulators, flow-control valves and related control mechanisms. It includes any arrangement that adjusts air supply to maintain desired pressure levels or flow rates in industrial or commercial applications.
[F02D 2009/0201] Arrangements; Control features; Details thereof -- The CPC code F02D 2009/0201 pertains to the structural layout of a hydraulic, pneumatic or mechanical system, focusing on the arrangement of its control components such as valves, actuators, sensors and related circuitry. It also covers the specific design details of these control features, including mounting, interconnections, and protective housings, as they relate to the overall functioning of the system.
[F02D 2009/0203] Mechanical governor -- The CPC entry F02D 2009/0203 covers mechanical governors that automatically regulate the speed of rotating machines such as engines, turbines or generators. It includes centrifugal, spring-loaded or friction-type devices that adjust fuel, steam or electrical input to maintain a set rotational speed.
[F02D 2009/0205] working on the throttle valve and another valve, e.g. choke -- This CPC subgroup covers methods and devices for adjusting a throttle valve together with a second valve such as a choke in internal combustion engines, enabling coordinated control of airflow and mixture during start-up or varying load conditions.
[F02D 2009/0206] specially positioned with relation to engine or engine housing -- F02D covers pumps that are specially positioned in relation to an engine or its housing, such as pumps integrated into the engine block, mounted on the crankcase, or attached to the cylinder head for lubrication, cooling, fuel delivery or similar functions. The classification groups designs where the pump's location is defined by its direct connection to or placement within the engine structure.
[F02D 2009/0208] for small engines -- F02D 2009/0208 designates patents concerning reciprocating piston internal-combustion engines that are intended for small-engine applications such as hand-held tools, lawn mowers, generators and similar equipment. The subclass addresses design, control, fuel and lubrication features optimized for low-power, lightweight engines.
[F02D 2009/021] combined with an electromechanical governor, e.g. centrifuged governor and electric governor acting on the governor lever -- The code refers to control mechanisms for reciprocating engines where an electromechanical governor, for example a centrifugal governor combined with an electric governor, is linked to the same lever to provide combined speed regulation. It covers hybrid mechanical-electrical governor arrangements.
[F02D 2009/0211] combined with another mechanical or pneumatic governor -- The CPC subclass F02D 2009/0211 covers devices that integrate a mechanical or pneumatic speed-governing system with an additional governor of a different type, forming a combined control arrangement for internal-combustion engines or similar power units. This classification is used for inventions that coordinate two governor mechanisms to improve speed regulation, load response, or safety.
[F02D 2009/0213] Electronic or electric governor -- F02D 2009/0213 refers to electronic or electric governors, devices that regulate the speed or output of engines by using electronic signals or electric actuation. The classification covers systems that replace mechanical or pneumatic control with sensors, microprocessors, or electric motors to adjust fuel delivery, ignition timing, or valve operation, ensuring stable performance under varying loads.
[F02D 2009/0215] Pneumatic governor -- The CPC class F02D pertains to governors for internal-combustion engines, and the 2009/0215 entry specifically covers pneumatic governors. These devices regulate engine speed by employing air or gas pressure to adjust fuel delivery or load, and are used in marine, automotive and other industrial power-plant applications.
[F02D 2009/0216] of the air-vane type -- The CPC entry F02D 2009/0216 covers fans or air-moving devices that employ an air-vane design, where stationary or rotating vanes shape and direct the airflow through the machine. It is used to classify inventions that use such vanes to improve pressure, flow uniformity or efficiency in axial or radial fans.
[F02D 2009/0218] Details of governor springs -- F02D 2009/0218 pertains to the technical details of springs used in governors, which regulate the speed of rotating machines such as engines or turbines. The entry covers design features, material choices, dimensions and performance characteristics of these springs, and how they affect the operation and stability of the governing mechanism.
[F02D 2009/022] Throttle control function parameters -- The CPC subgroup F02D 2009/022 covers the definition and use of parameters that govern throttle control in internal-combustion engines. It includes specifications of throttle opening, response characteristics, mapping functions and related control logic used to optimise engine speed, torque and emissions.
[F02D 2009/0222] Exhaust gas temperature -- The CPC subclass F02D 2009/0222 covers inventions that measure, monitor or control the temperature of exhaust gases from internal-combustion engines. It includes sensors, thermocouples, infrared devices and related circuitry or methods used to detect exhaust-gas temperature for performance optimisation, emissions compliance or safety monitoring.
[F02D 2009/0223] Cooling water temperature -- F02D 2009/0223 pertains to inventions that monitor, regulate or otherwise manage the temperature of cooling water in mechanical or thermal systems, such as engines, turbines or heat exchangers. It includes sensors, control devices and methods for maintaining optimal cooling-water temperature to ensure efficient operation and prevent overheating.
[F02D 2009/0225] Intake air or mixture temperature -- This CPC subgroup deals with measures that condition the temperature of the air or fuel-air mixture before it enters a combustion chamber. It includes heating, cooling, pre-heating, temperature regulation devices, sensors and control strategies intended to improve engine performance, efficiency or emissions.
[F02D 2009/0227] Atmospheric pressure -- The CPC class F02D 2009/0227 - Atmospheric pressure - covers inventions that involve creating, using, controlling or measuring pressure at or near ambient atmospheric levels. It includes devices such as atmospheric-pressure pumps, compressors and related control mechanisms that operate without pressure amplification.
[F02D 2009/0228] Manifold pressure -- The CPC entry F02D 2009/0228 pertains to steam-turbine installations and concerns the measurement, regulation or utilization of pressure within the manifold that supplies steam or gas to the turbine. It includes sensors, control devices and pressure-control arrangements designed to maintain optimal manifold pressure for efficient turbine operation.
[F02D 2009/023] Engine speed -- The sub-group relates to the control, measurement or regulation of an engine's rotational speed, covering devices, methods and systems for detecting, limiting or adjusting speed in various types of engines.
[F02D 2009/0232] Fuel pump rack position -- The class pertains to mechanisms that set, adjust, or detect the position of a fuel pump rack, ensuring proper alignment of the dispensing nozzle and controlling fuel flow during operation.
[F02D 2009/0233] Engine vibration -- F02D 2009/0233 relates to technologies for detecting, analyzing, and mitigating vibration in internal-combustion engines. The classification includes vibration-damping devices, engine-mount designs, balancing methods, and diagnostic systems aimed at improving engine durability, performance and passenger comfort.
[F02D 2009/0235] Throttle control functions -- F02D 2009/0235 pertains to fan and blower technology and defines throttle control functions, i.e., mechanisms that regulate airflow by varying fan speed or blade opening. It includes designs of throttling devices, control strategies, and associated electronic or mechanical arrangements used to adjust performance in response to operating conditions.
[F02D 2009/0237] Increasing combustion chamber gas temperature -- F02D 2009/0237 relates to methods and apparatuses for raising the temperature of the gas within a combustion chamber, including techniques such as pre-heating the incoming mixture, using radiant or catalytic elements, injecting hot gases or flames, and other means that improve combustion efficiency by increasing chamber gas temperature.
[F02D 2009/0238] Increasing ignition delay -- F02D 2009/0238 covers internal combustion engine methods and devices that deliberately increase the ignition delay, the period between fuel injection and the start of combustion. It includes approaches such as altering fuel composition, adjusting injection timing, or modifying chamber geometry to control combustion phasing for improved efficiency or reduced emissions.
[F02D 2009/024] Increasing intake vacuum -- The CPC subclass F02D 2009/024 covers inventions that increase the intake vacuum of internal-combustion engines, such as suction-enhancing devices, valve timing adjustments, or control systems that raise the pressure differential between the intake manifold and atmospheric pressure to improve fuel-air mixing and engine efficiency.
[F02D 2009/0242] Increasing exhaust brake effect -- The CPC subclass F02D 2009/0242 covers technologies that enhance the performance of an exhaust brake on internal-combustion engines, such as modifications to exhaust flow, valve timing or added devices that raise back-pressure to improve engine-braking efficiency. It is situated within class F02D, which deals with combustion engines and their exhaust systems.
[F02D 2009/0244] Choking air flow at low speed and load -- The F02D 2009/0244 entry covers technologies that limit or restrict airflow through rotating machines such as compressors or turbines when they operate at low rotational speeds and under light-load conditions, addressing performance losses and stability problems associated with choke flow.
[F02D 2009/0245] Shutting down engine, e.g. working together with fuel cut-off -- The CPC group F02D 2009/0245 covers devices and methods for stopping an engine, typically by coordinating a fuel-cutoff or similar interruption of the power supply. It includes systems that automatically or manually shut down internal combustion engines used in ships, submarines or other marine vessels. The classification is used for inventions that manage engine shutdown to improve safety, fuel efficiency or operational control.
[F02D 2009/0247] Opening the throttle a little on engine shutdown -- F02D 2009/0247 relates to internal combustion engines, specifically methods that adjust the throttle slightly during engine shutdown to improve performance or reduce emissions. The entry covers control strategies that open the throttle a small amount as the engine is turned off, aiding in smoother shutdown and preventing fuel cut-off issues. It is part of the CPC group for engine start-up and shutdown procedures.
[F02D 2009/0249] Starting engine, e.g. closing throttle in Diesel engine to reduce starting torque -- The CPC entry F02D 2009/0249 covers techniques for initiating the operation of an internal combustion engine, such as closing the throttle in a diesel engine to lower the torque required for starting. It relates to start-up control measures that improve engine start reliability and reduce mechanical stress.
[F02D 2009/025] Opening the throttle a little during starting -- The CPC subclass F02D 2009/025 covers arrangements or control strategies that slightly open the engine throttle when the engine is being started. It addresses methods for adjusting the throttle opening during start-up to improve ignition, reduce load on the starter, and lower emissions before the engine reaches normal operating speed.
[F02D 2009/0252] Opening a special valve-controlled intake passage (by-pass) during starting -- The CPC subclass F02D 2009/0252 covers mechanisms that open a valve-controlled bypass intake passage during engine start, allowing additional air or mixture to flow to facilitate ignition and reduce starter load. It relates to internal combustion engines where a dedicated bypass valve is actuated at start-up.
[F02D 2009/0254] Mechanical control linkage between accelerator lever and throttle valve -- The entry covers mechanical devices that transmit motion from an accelerator lever to a throttle valve in an internal-combustion engine. It includes linkages such as rods, cables, levers or gear systems that provide a direct, non-electronic means of controlling engine speed.
[F02D 2009/0255] with means for correcting throttle position, e.g. throttle cable of variable length -- F02D 2009/0255 covers internal-combustion engines that incorporate a device for adjusting the throttle position, such as a throttle cable whose length can be varied to maintain correct engine speed or load. This subclass groups inventions that provide automatic or manual means to correct throttle settings for improved performance or emissions control.
[F02D 2009/0257] having a pin and slob connection ("Leerweg") -- F02D 2009/0257 classifies pump constructions that use a pin-and-slob (loose-fit) connection, commonly called "Leerweg", to join the drive shaft and the pump rotor. The arrangement permits limited axial movement of the shaft to absorb thermal expansion or misalignment while maintaining torque transmission. It is applied mainly in hydraulic and rotary pumps where such flexibility is required.
[F02D 2009/0259] having a dashpot, e.g. working in the throttle opening and closing directions -- This subclass refers to fluid-power devices, especially valves, that incorporate a dash-pot to provide resistance or damping during throttle movement, controlling the speed of opening and closing to prevent shock. It covers mechanisms where the dash-pot works in both the opening and closing directions.
[F02D 2009/0261] having a specially shaped transmission member, e.g. a cam, specially toothed gears, with a clutch -- This class covers power-transmission mechanisms that employ a specially shaped transmission element such as a cam or uniquely toothed gear, combined with a clutch. The design enables controlled engagement or disengagement of motion, often for timing, synchronization, or variable speed functions in machinery.
[F02D 2009/0262] having two or more levers on the throttle shaft -- This CPC entry relates to reciprocating internal-combustion engines that feature two or more levers mounted on the throttle shaft, allowing simultaneous or independent adjustment of the throttle position. The multiple-lever arrangement enables separate control of fuel, air or other operating parameters, providing finer engine response and greater flexibility.
[F02D 2009/0264] in which movement is transmitted through a spring -- F02D 2009/0264 pertains to machines or mechanisms in which mechanical motion is conveyed by a spring, using the elastic deformation of the spring to transmit force or displacement, as found in shock absorbers, suspension components, energy-storage devices and similar spring-based transmission systems.
[F02D 2009/0266] in which movement is transmitted through a vacuum motor -- The CPC entry F02D 2009/0266 pertains to machines in which the output motion is conveyed by means of a vacuum motor, i.e., a device that creates a partial vacuum to drive a piston, diaphragm or rotor and thereby transmit power to the working mechanism. It covers arrangements where the vacuum-generated force is used as the intermediate medium for motion transmission.
[F02D 2009/0267] for simultaneous action of a governor and an accelerator lever on the throttle -- CPC class F02D deals with speed control of reciprocating engines, and subgroup 2009/0267 specifically covers mechanisms that enable a governor and an accelerator lever to act together on the throttle, providing coordinated regulation of engine speed and rapid response.
[F02D 2009/0269] Throttle closing springs; Acting of throttle closing springs on the throttle shaft -- The subclass deals with throttle mechanisms that use springs to return or close the throttle, specifically detailing how such springs are applied to the throttle shaft to move it toward a closed or idle position. It includes designs where spring force is the primary actuation for throttling control.
[F02D 2009/0271] with means for closing the throttle other than throttle closing springs -- This CPC group covers machines in which the throttle is closed by a mechanism other than a spring, such as hydraulic, pneumatic, electric or other actuation means, to control the flow or speed of the working medium.
[F02D 2009/0272] Two or more throttles disposed in series -- The code refers to steam-turbine designs that use two or more throttling valves arranged one after another to regulate steam admission. This series configuration allows finer flow control, reduces pressure loss across each valve, and can improve turbine efficiency and response.
[F02D 2009/0274] one being controlled by pressure in intake conduit, e.g. for slowly opening the throttle as the other valve is suddenly opened -- The CPC entry F02D 2009/0274 covers valve arrangements in internal-combustion engines where the motion of one valve is governed by pressure in the intake conduit, allowing a gradual opening of the throttle while a second valve is actuated abruptly. This pressure-controlled scheme improves engine response during sudden load changes or start-up.
[F02D 2009/0276] Throttle and EGR-valve operated together -- The classification covers arrangements for gas-turbine engines in which the throttle and the exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) valve are controlled in a coordinated manner, allowing simultaneous adjustment of air-flow and recirculated exhaust to optimise combustion, improve fuel efficiency and meet emission limits.
[F02D 2009/0277] Fail-safe mechanisms, e.g. with limp-home feature, to close throttle if actuator fails, or if control cable sticks or breaks -- The CPC subclass F02D 2009/0277 covers fail-safe devices that automatically close a vehicle's throttle when the actuator malfunctions or the control cable sticks or breaks, often employing a limp-home feature to allow limited engine power for safe stopping or continued operation. It is used for safety provisions in automotive throttle-control systems.
[F02D 2009/0279] Throttle valve control for intake system with two parallel air flow paths, each controlled by a throttle, e.g. a resilient flap disposed on a throttle
[F02D 2009/0281] with means for detecting malfunction of one throttle and actuating only the correctly working throttle -- This CPC group relates to throttle control mechanisms that include a fault-detection arrangement for identifying a defective throttle and that automatically operate only the throttle that is still functional, ensuring continued regulation of flow or engine power despite a malfunction.
[F02D 2009/0283] Throttle in the form of an expander -- The CPC subclass F02D 2009/0283 covers throttling devices that operate as expanders, where the flow-control element expands to reduce pressure and limit fluid or gas flow, commonly applied in engines, turbines or other fluid-power systems. It defines the structural and functional features of expander-type throttles.
[F02D 2009/0284] Throttle control device with means for signalling a certain throttle opening, e.g. by a steplike increase of throttle closing spring force -- A throttle control device that includes a signalling mechanism indicating a specific throttle opening, achieved by producing a step-like increase in the force of the throttle-closing spring. The change in spring force provides a detectable signal that corresponds to a predetermined throttle position.
[F02D 2009/0286] Throttle control device with accelerator lever defining a stop for opening the throttle, e.g. the throttle itself being opened by air flow, a spring -- The entry covers throttle control mechanisms for internal-combustion engines where the accelerator lever also functions as a mechanical stop that limits throttle opening. The throttle may be actuated by airflow pressure or a spring, with the lever-defined stop providing a defined maximum opening position.
[F02D 2009/0288] Throttle control device specially adapted for spark-assisted compression-ignition engine (Diesel engine) -- The entry F02D 2009/0288 covers throttle-control devices that are specially adapted for spark-assisted compression-ignition (SACI) diesel engines, where a pilot spark assists the conventional diesel cycle. It includes mechanisms and systems that regulate intake airflow or fuel delivery to control engine speed, torque and emissions in these combined-combustion engines.
[F02D 2009/0289] Throttle control device with means for establishing a variable resistance torque during throttle opening -- The classification covers throttle control devices that incorporate a mechanism to provide a variable resistance torque while the throttle opens, allowing the resistance to be adjusted in response to operating conditions in order to regulate engine speed or load.
[F02D 2009/0291] Throttle control device for throttle being disposed in a two-stroke engine transfer passage -- The entry covers a throttle control mechanism that is installed directly within the transfer passage of a two-stroke internal-combustion engine. It details how the device regulates the amount of fresh charge flowing to the cylinder, improving engine speed control and efficiency while fitting into the engine's existing airflow path.
[F02D 2009/0293] Throttle control device adapted to limit power development at low attitude -- The F02D 2009/0293 subclass covers throttle-control devices that automatically reduce or limit engine power when an aircraft is operating at a low attitude, such as during take-off, landing or steep descents. It includes mechanical, hydraulic or electronic systems that sense pitch or climb angle and adjust the throttle position to prevent excessive thrust and improve safety.
[F02D 2009/0294] Throttle control device with provisions for actuating electric or electronic sensors -- The class covers throttle control devices that include means for actuating electric or electronic sensors, such as throttle-position or airflow sensors, to supply feedback for engine management. It includes mechanisms where the throttle actuator provides motion or a signal to drive or calibrate the sensor, together with associated mounting or linkage arrangements.
[F02D 2009/0296] Throttle control device with stops for limiting throttle opening or closing beyond a certain position during certain periods of operation -- This CPC subclass relates to throttle control mechanisms that incorporate mechanical or electronic stops to prevent the throttle from opening or closing beyond a predetermined position for defined time intervals. The stops may be engaged during specific operating modes to ensure safety, fuel efficiency, or compliance with emissions regulations. Applications include internal combustion engine control systems where precise throttle limiting is required.
[F02D 2009/0298] Throttle control device with holding devices, i.e. to hold throttle in a predetermined position -- The CPC class F02D 2009/0298 covers throttle control devices that incorporate holding mechanisms to maintain the throttle at a selected position. It includes systems that lock or latch the throttle valve for idle stabilization, cruise control, or load reduction, typically used in internal combustion engines.
[F02D 2011/101] characterised by the means for actuating the throttles -- F02D 2011/101 groups patent disclosures that specifically define the means for actuating throttles in marine propulsion systems, such as hydraulic, electric, pneumatic or mechanical devices that adjust engine throttle position. The classification is used for inventions that focus on the design, arrangement or control of these throttle-actuation mechanisms on ships or watercraft.
[F02D 2011/102] at least one throttle being moved only by an electric actuator -- The CPC subclass F02D 2011/102 covers internal-combustion engine arrangements in which at least one throttle valve is moved only by an electric actuator, such as a motor or stepper, without any mechanical linkage, providing electronic control of air-fuel intake. This classification is used for designs that rely on purely electric actuation to regulate engine speed and emissions.
[F02D 2011/103] at least one throttle being alternatively mechanically linked to the pedal or moved by an electric actuator -- The CPC subclass F02D 2011/103 covers power-train arrangements where a throttle valve is controlled either by a conventional mechanical link to the accelerator pedal or by an electric actuator that moves the throttle independently. It relates to internal combustion engine control systems that provide alternate or combined mechanical and electronic actuation.
[F02D 2011/104] using electric step motors -- F02D 2011/104 covers inventions that incorporate electric step motors, a type of motor that moves in discrete, precise steps rather than continuous rotation. It includes the design, control, and use of these motors in mechanical systems such as positioning devices, printers, and robotics.
[F02D 2011/108] with means for detecting or resolving a stuck throttle, e.g. when being frozen in a position -- F02D 2011/108 covers fuel-injection or fuel-distribution systems that incorporate a mechanism to detect a throttle that has become jammed or frozen in a particular position and to intervene automatically, for example by unlocking, resetting or otherwise correcting the throttle's operation to restore normal engine control. This sub-group focuses on safety-related features that prevent engine damage or loss of power due to a stuck throttle.
[F02D 2013/005] of throttleless spark ignited engines -- CPC F02D 2013/005 relates to control systems for spark-ignited internal combustion engines that operate without a throttle, covering methods such as direct fuel injection or electronic engine management that regulate the air-fuel mixture and ignition timing to replace the conventional throttle valve.
[F02D 2013/0288] for cleaning the valves -- CPC F02D 2013/0288 relates to technologies for cleaning the valves of pumps, compressors or similar fluid-handling equipment, covering methods and apparatus that remove deposits, scale or fouling from valve seats and passages. The entry includes mechanical, chemical, ultrasonic or combined cleaning actions and may describe integrated cleaning cycles within the valve operation.
[F02D 2013/0292] in the start-up phase, e.g. for warming-up cold engine or catalyst -- The code refers to technologies used in the start-up phase of an engine or catalyst, specifically methods for warming a cold engine or catalyst before normal operation. It includes heating devices, control strategies and procedures that raise temperature to operating levels.
[F02D 2013/0296] Changing the valve lift only -- F02D 2013/0296 pertains to internal combustion engines where the valve lift is altered without changing the timing, typically by using adjustable cam profiles, hydraulic or pneumatic devices, or variable lift mechanisms. The classification covers methods and devices that modify the maximum valve opening height to improve performance, emissions or fuel efficiency.
[F02D 2021/083] controlling exhaust gas recirculation electronically -- F02D 2021/083 covers inventions that regulate the flow of exhaust-gas recirculation using electronic means, typically involving sensors, electronic control units and actuators to adjust EGR rates for emissions reduction and engine performance. The classification includes methods, devices and control strategies that replace or supplement mechanical or hydraulic EGR control, often integrating real-time data from engine parameters to optimize combustion and meet regulatory standards.
[F02D 2021/086] the exhaust gas recirculation valve being controlled by fuel pressure, e.g. indirectly -- This CPC entry falls under class F02D for internal combustion engines and covers arrangements where the exhaust-gas recirculation valve is operated by the pressure of the fuel, typically through an indirect linkage or hydraulic circuit. The control method uses fuel pressure variations to modulate the EGR valve opening, integrating the valve actuation with the engine's fuel system.
[F02D 2041/001] for engines with variable valve actuation -- The CPC subclass F02D 2041/001 covers internal-combustion engines whose valve timing, lift or duration is varied during operation, typically by hydraulic, electronic or pneumatic mechanisms. It includes systems that adjust valve opening and closing moments to optimise power output, fuel consumption or emissions under changing operating conditions.
[F02D 2041/0012] with selective deactivation of cylinders -- F02D 2041/0012 covers internal combustion engines that can selectively deactivate individual cylinders during operation to improve fuel efficiency or reduce emissions. The classification includes mechanisms such as valve actuation, fuel-injection cut-off or piston deactivation that allow specific cylinders to be turned off while the engine continues running.
[F02D 2041/0015] for engines with means for controlling swirl or tumble flow, e.g. by using swirl valves -- The F02D 2041/0015 subclass groups internal-combustion engines equipped with devices that control swirl or tumble flow of the charge, such as swirl valves. It covers mechanisms that create or adjust rotational or transverse air-fuel motion to improve mixing, combustion stability or emissions.
[F02D 2041/0017] by simultaneous control of throttle and exhaust gas recirculation -- F02D 2041/0017 covers techniques for regulating an internal-combustion engine by jointly adjusting the throttle opening and the rate of exhaust-gas recirculation. The simultaneous control aims to improve combustion efficiency, reduce emissions and enhance fuel economy under varying operating conditions.
[F02D 2041/002] by simultaneous control of throttle and variable valve actuation -- The CPC subclass F02D 2041/002 pertains to internal combustion engines in which the throttle and a variable-valve system are operated together in a coordinated manner. It covers methods or devices that simultaneously adjust fuel flow and valve timing to optimise power output, fuel efficiency, or emissions, typically using electronic or hydraulic control strategies. This joint control approach is applied to improve engine performance across varying operating conditions.
[F02D 2041/0022] for diesel engines by throttle control -- The CPC subclass F02D 2041/0022 covers diesel internal-combustion engines that are regulated by a throttle mechanism. It includes inventions related to throttle-controlled fuel-air mixture regulation, power-output adjustment and associated control systems for diesel engines. This heading is used to classify patents dealing specifically with throttling devices or methods applied to diesel engine operation.
[F02D 2041/0067] Determining the EGR temperature -- The code F02D 2041/0067 covers techniques for measuring the temperature of exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) streams in internal-combustion engines. It includes sensors, probes and electronic methods that determine the EGR temperature for control, diagnostics or emission-reduction purposes.
[F02D 2041/007] by estimation -- F02D 2041/007 is a sub-group of the CPC class for devices that control drying, dewatering or spreading operations based on an estimated value of a process parameter rather than a direct measurement, such as estimating moisture or temperature to set the machine's action.
