[F02D 2041/0075] by using flow sensors -- F02D 2041/0075 covers aircraft control or monitoring systems that employ flow sensors to detect the quantity, direction or speed of air or gas flow, using that information for functions such as flight-control adjustments, engine-performance regulation or aerodynamic optimisation. It is part of the CPC section for aeronautical devices and methods.
[F02D 2041/0092] Synchronisation of the cylinders at engine start -- The subclass covers devices or methods that ensure the proper timing relationship among engine cylinders during start-up, aligning ignition or fuel-injection events to achieve smooth acceleration and reduce misfires. It includes control systems, sensors and algorithms that coordinate cylinder firing sequences as the engine transitions from rest to operation.
[F02D 2041/0095] Synchronisation of the cylinders during engine shutdown -- The code covers devices or methods that coordinate the timing of multiple engine cylinders as the power unit is stopped, ensuring pistons reach predetermined positions to prevent damage, reduce wear, or enable controlled shutdown. It includes control systems that align valve or fuel-injection timing during engine termination.
[F02D 2041/026] using an external load, e.g. by increasing generator load or by changing the gear ratio -- This subclass pertains to wind-turbine control techniques that employ an external load to influence operation. It includes methods that raise the electrical load on the generator or modify the gear ratio, thereby adjusting rotor speed or power output for optimized performance.
[F02D 2041/0265] to decrease temperature of the exhaust gas treating apparatus -- The subclass F02D 2041/0265 covers measures that lower the temperature of an exhaust-gas treatment unit, such as cooling channels, heat exchangers, spray nozzles or auxiliary fluids applied to the catalyst, filter or scrubber. It includes designs that protect downstream components by reducing the gas temperature during or after treatment.
[F02D 2041/1409] using at least a proportional, integral or derivative controller -- F02D 2041/1409 covers control arrangements for fluid or pneumatic systems that incorporate at least a proportional, integral or derivative (PID) controller to regulate pressure, flow or speed. The classification applies to devices where such controllers are used to maintain set-point conditions in hydraulic or pneumatic mechanisms.
[F02D 2041/141] using a feed-forward control element -- The code refers to pump devices that incorporate a feed-forward control element, meaning the system adjusts its operation in anticipation of a measured change rather than reacting after an error occurs. This classification covers designs where the control action is based on a known disturbance or set-point variation to improve responsiveness and stability.
[F02D 2041/1411] using a finite or infinite state machine, automaton or state graph for controlling or modelling -- F02D 2041/1411 covers inventions that employ finite or infinite state machines, automata or state-graph structures as a means of control or to model a process. The classification includes use of such formal models for implementing control logic, simulation of system behaviour, or representation of functional states in mechanical, electrical or electromechanical devices.
[F02D 2041/1412] using a predictive controller -- The entry covers the use of predictive control methods for turbine operation, where a controller employs a model of the turbine system to forecast future states and adjust inputs in advance, improving efficiency, stability and response to disturbances.
[F02D 2041/1413] Controller structures or design -- The CPC entry F02D 2041/1413 pertains to the structural design of controllers for pumps, especially variable-displacement types. It includes mechanical or electronic arrangements that adjust the pump's output, such as swash-plate angle mechanisms, pressure-feedback valves or integrated control units, and the way these components are incorporated into the pump housing.
[F02D 2041/1415] using a state feedback or a state space representation -- This subclass covers control techniques for engines or power-plant systems that employ a state-feedback or state-space model, where the controller computes actions based on the system's internal state variables. It includes methods for designing, analyzing or implementing such controllers to achieve desired performance, stability or efficiency in the regulated machine.
[F02D 2041/1416] Observer -- The CPC code F02D 2041/1416 relates to observers incorporated in rotary positive-displacement machines such as pumps or compressors. It covers sensors, transducers, visual windows and other monitoring devices that measure internal or external parameters like pressure, flow, temperature or speed for control, diagnostic or safety purposes.
[F02D 2041/1417] Kalman filter -- F02D 2041/1417 relates to the application of Kalman-filter techniques for monitoring, control and fault detection in steam generators, boilers and similar thermal-energy equipment. It covers state-estimation, parameter identification and predictive diagnostics that improve the operation and safety of these systems.
[F02D 2041/1418] Several control loops, either as alternatives or simultaneous -- F02D 2041/1418 covers turbine control arrangements that incorporate two or more control loops which can be used either as alternative options or run simultaneously. The classification applies to systems that coordinate multiple feedback or feed-forward loops to regulate parameters such as speed, pressure, temperature or load, enhancing stability and performance under varying operating conditions.
[F02D 2041/1419] the control loops being cascaded, i.e. being placed in series or nested -- F02D 2041/1419 covers cascade control in hydraulic or fluid-power systems, where two or more control loops are arranged in series or nested to improve stability, accuracy and response speed. It includes designs and methods that implement such cascaded loop structures.
[F02D 2041/142] using different types of control law in combination, e.g. adaptive combined with PID and sliding mode -- The subclass F02D 2041/142 covers control systems that employ a combination of different control laws, such as integrating an adaptive controller with a proportional-integral-derivative (PID) regulator and a sliding-mode controller, to enhance stability, robustness, or performance across varying operating conditions.
[F02D 2041/1422] Variable gain or coefficients -- F02D 2041/1422 relates to hydraulic turbine control systems that incorporate adjustable gain or coefficient settings. It covers mechanisms and methods for varying the amplification or weighting factors in the turbine's regulation circuitry to optimise performance under changing load or flow conditions. This includes variable-gain amplifiers, adaptive control loops, and adjustable coefficient algorithms used in power-generation turbine installations.
[F02D 2041/1423] Identification of model or controller parameters -- The CPC subgroup F02D 2041/1423 covers methods and apparatus for identifying the parameters of a model or controller in fluid-power systems, such as hydraulic or pneumatic circuits. It includes techniques for system identification, parameter estimation, and adaptive tuning based on observed performance data to improve control accuracy.
[F02D 2041/1424] Pole-zero cancellation -- Pole-zero cancellation is a control-system technique used in steam-turbine applications where a pole of the turbine's transfer function is deliberately eliminated by introducing a matching zero, simplifying the dynamic response and enhancing stability.
[F02D 2041/1425] using a bond graph model or models with nodes -- This CPC subgroup covers inventions that apply bond-graph modeling techniques to pump technology, using nodes to represent the energy-flow relationships among mechanical, hydraulic and electrical elements in order to analyse, simulate or control pump performance.
[F02D 2041/1426] taking into account control stability -- The subclass covers control or regulation methods for rotating machines in which the stability of the control system is explicitly taken into account, such as designs that ensure stable torque or speed response under disturbances, employ stability criteria, or use adaptive or robust control techniques to maintain stable operation.
[F02D 2041/1427] Decoupling, i.e. using a feedback such that one output is controlled by only one input -- The subclass F02D 2041/1427 covers control arrangements in which a feedback loop is designed so that each controlled variable is influenced by only one independent input, a technique known as decoupling. It is applied to hydraulic, pneumatic or electrical systems to simplify regulation and improve stability.
[F02D 2041/1429] Linearisation, i.e. using a feedback law such that the system evolves as a linear one -- The entry covers techniques that apply a feedback law to a nonlinear system so that its closed-loop behavior follows a linear model, enabling the use of linear control methods for analysis and design. It includes methods such as state-feedback and feedback-linearisation to simplify system dynamics.
[F02D 2041/143] the control loop including a non-linear model or compensator -- F02D 2041/143 covers control systems for fluid power or hydraulic machines that incorporate a non-linear model or compensator within the feedback loop. It includes designs where the loop uses non-linear mathematical representations or compensating elements to improve stability or performance.
[F02D 2041/1431] the system including an input-output delay -- The subclass F02D 2041/1431 relates to pump arrangements that incorporate an intentional input-output delay within the system. It covers designs where the time between a control signal or demand and the resulting fluid delivery is deliberately delayed to improve stability, reduce surge, or coordinate with other processes.
[F02D 2041/1432] the system including a filter, e.g. a low pass or high pass filter -- F02D 2041/1432 classifies control systems for reciprocating internal-combustion engines that incorporate a filter-such as a low-pass or high-pass filter-in the signal path. The filter is used to condition measurement or control signals, smoothing out high-frequency noise or attenuating unwanted frequencies to improve engine regulation and performance.
[F02D 2041/1433] using a model or simulation of the system -- F02D 2041/1433 covers techniques that employ a model or computer simulation of a fluid or gas handling system to predict its behaviour, test control strategies, or optimise performance. The approach is used in design, monitoring, and automation of such systems.
[F02D 2041/1434] Inverse model -- F02D 2041/1434 covers inverse-model techniques applied to hydraulic machines such as turbines, pumps or compressors, where measured performance data are used to back-calculate design or operating parameters of the machine. It includes analytical, numerical or experimental methods that adjust a model until its predicted behaviour matches the observed results, facilitating diagnostics, optimization or reconstruction of internal flow characteristics.
[F02D 2041/1436] Hybrid model -- F02D 2041/1436 covers hybrid models that integrate two or more distinct thermodynamic cycles or energy-conversion principles within a single air-conditioning or refrigeration system, such as combined heat-pump and vapor-compression arrangements, to improve efficiency or operational flexibility.
[F02D 2041/1437] Simulation -- This CPC subclass covers methods and computer models used to simulate the operation, performance and dynamics of steam turbines, including virtual testing and predictive modeling that replicate turbine behavior for design optimization, control strategy evaluation and fault analysis.
[F02D 2041/1468] the characteristics being an ammonia content or concentration of the exhaust gases -- This subclass deals with methods, devices, or systems that determine or regulate the ammonia content or concentration in exhaust-gas streams, typically in selective catalytic reduction or emission control. It includes sensors, analytical techniques and control strategies aimed at monitoring ammonia levels.
[F02D 2041/1469] with determination means using an estimation -- F02D 2041/1469 covers internal combustion engines equipped with determination means that estimate a variable such as fuel consumption, load, or emissions - rather than directly measuring it. The classification is applied to control or monitoring systems that rely on calculated approximations to adjust engine operation.
[F02D 2041/147] the characteristics being a hydrogen content or concentration of the exhaust gases -- The subclass covers technologies that determine, monitor or control the hydrogen content or concentration in exhaust gases, typically from internal-combustion engines or related processes. It includes methods, sensors and control systems used for assessing hydrogen levels as part of emission analysis or optimisation of combustion.
[F02D 2041/1472] the characteristics being a humidity or water content of the exhaust gases -- F02D 2041/1472 covers methods and apparatus for determining the humidity or water content present in exhaust gases, typically in combustion or industrial processes. It includes techniques for measuring moisture levels to monitor efficiency, emissions compliance, or to control drying and condensation in exhaust streams. This sub-group falls under the broader category of internal-combustion or steam-engine exhaust gas analysis.
[F02D 2041/2003] using means for creating a boost voltage, i.e. generation or use of a voltage higher than the battery voltage, e.g. to speed up injector opening -- The subclass F02D 2041/2003 pertains to engine fuel-injection systems that incorporate a boost-voltage generator, creating a voltage higher than the battery supply to accelerate injector opening and improve fuel delivery timing.
[F02D 2041/2006] by using a boost capacitor -- The subclass refers to electric-power devices or methods that achieve voltage or energy increase by inserting a boost capacitor into the circuit, such as in pulsed-power, ignition or auxiliary-power systems, where the capacitor stores charge and releases it to raise the output voltage.
[F02D 2041/201] by using a boost inductance -- F02D 2041/201 covers pump arrangements that employ a boost-inductor circuit in the drive electronics, typically to raise voltage or current for the pump motor and improve start-up torque or efficiency. The classification applies to systems where the inductive boost is integral to the pump's power-supply architecture.
[F02D 2041/2013] by using a boost voltage source -- F02D 2041/2013 covers rotating electrical machines that are driven or controlled by employing a boost voltage source, such as a circuit that raises the supply voltage to improve starting torque, increase output power, or enhance efficiency. The classification is used for patents describing the use of auxiliary high-voltage supplies in the operation of generators, motors or similar rotating devices.
[F02D 2041/2017] using means for creating a boost current or using reference switching -- The subclass covers control arrangements that generate a temporary boost current or use reference-switching techniques to raise the motor or converter current, typically to increase torque or improve acceleration in electric drive applications. It describes methods for creating a higher current pulse by altering reference signals in the control circuitry.
[F02D 2041/202] characterised by the control of the circuit -- The F02D 2041/202 subclass covers internal-combustion engines whose invention is defined by the way the electrical circuit that drives the engine is controlled. It includes arrangements of electronic control units, sensors, switches or actuators that manage ignition, fuel-injection, starter-motor or other engine functions through a specific circuit-control strategy.
[F02D 2041/2024] the control switching a load after time-on and time-off pulses -- The entry relates to devices or methods that control a load by alternating its connection on and off in response to predetermined time-on and time-off pulse signals. It covers timing circuits, control logic and switching arrangements that generate and apply the pulses to switch the load automatically, typically for engine or auxiliary equipment regulation.
[F02D 2041/2027] Control of the current by pulse width modulation or duty cycle control -- The CPC subclass F02D 2041/2027 covers methods and devices for controlling electric current using pulse-width modulation or duty-cycle control, typically applied in power converters and motor drives. It includes techniques that adjust the width of voltage pulses to regulate output power or speed.
[F02D 2041/2031] Control of the current by means of delays or monostable multivibrators -- This subclass covers methods for regulating the electric current in electrochemical apparatus by employing timing circuits such as delay elements or monostable multivibrators, which generate a single pulse of predetermined duration to control charging, discharging or electro-processing operations.
[F02D 2041/2034] Control of the current gradient -- The code covers technologies that regulate the spatial variation of electric current in electrochemical systems, ensuring a uniform or programmed current density across electrodes. It includes control methods, sensors and feedback mechanisms applied to electrolyzers, plating baths, fuel cells and similar devices.
[F02D 2041/2037] for preventing bouncing of the valve needle -- F02D 2041/2037 concerns arrangements that prevent the valve needle from rebounding after it seats, typically using damping, hydraulic or spring devices to control needle motion and improve valve timing accuracy.
[F02D 2041/2041] for controlling the current in the free-wheeling phase -- The CPC entry F02D 2041/2041 covers technologies that regulate the current flowing during the free-wheeling phase of an electric motor drive or power converter, i.e., the period when the main switch is off and the load current recirculates through a diode or other path. It includes control schemes, sensor-based feedback, and circuit arrangements designed to limit, shape or stabilize that recirculating current.
[F02D 2041/2044] using pre-magnetisation or post-magnetisation of the coils -- This CPC subgroup covers techniques that magnetise the windings of an electric machine either before the coil is installed (pre-magnetisation) or after the coil has been assembled (post-magnetisation). The methods are used to create or adjust the magnetic field in rotating-field machines such as permanent-magnet or superconducting generators, improving performance or simplifying construction.
[F02D 2041/2048] said control involving a limitation, e.g. applying current or voltage limits -- The subclass relates to control arrangements that include a limitation function, such as imposing preset current or voltage limits on the power delivered to an electric machine or drive. These limits are used to protect components, regulate performance or ensure safe operation.
[F02D 2041/2051] using voltage control -- F02D 2041/2051 relates to methods and apparatus that regulate fluid-handling equipment, such as pumps or water-treatment systems, by adjusting the applied voltage to control motor speed or pump output. The entry captures inventions that use voltage-based control to improve efficiency, flow stability or energy consumption in such systems.
[F02D 2041/2055] with means for determining actual opening or closing time -- F02D 2041/2055 refers to pump-related assemblies that incorporate a device or sensor for measuring the real-time duration a valve, gate, or similar closure element is opened or closed. Such means allow precise monitoring or control of fluid flow timing, which is important for safety, performance optimisation or diagnostics in hydraulic systems.
[F02D 2041/2058] using information of the actual current value -- This CPC group covers systems or methods that adjust a water turbine's operation by processing the measured instantaneous electrical current produced, using that real-time value to control speed, load or efficiency.
[F02D 2041/2062] the current value is determined by simulation or estimation -- F02D 2041/2062 covers methods for obtaining the value of an electric current by means of simulation, modelling or computational estimation rather than direct measurement, typically using observer algorithms, Kalman filters or other model-based techniques applied to drives, converters or power-electronics systems. It includes approaches that infer current from voltage, speed, torque or other sensor data to provide real-time estimates for control or monitoring purposes.
[F02D 2041/2065] the control being related to the coil temperature -- This group covers arrangements in which the control system regulates operation according to the temperature of a heating coil, such as adjusting fluid flow, power input or coil positioning to keep the coil within a set temperature range in boiler or heating-coil installations.
[F02D 2041/2068] characterised by the circuit design or special circuit elements -- This subclass of F02D covers steam turbine inventions that are distinguished by the particular arrangement of the electrical circuit or by the inclusion of special circuit components used for control, monitoring or protection.
[F02D 2041/2072] Bridge circuits, i.e. the load being placed in the diagonal of a bridge to be controlled in both directions -- This CPC subgroup concerns bridge circuit configurations in which the load is connected across the diagonal of the bridge and can be driven in both directions, enabling bidirectional control of the load. It covers designs such as Wheatstone-type bridges, full-bridge converters and similar arrangements where the diagonal placement allows the load to be powered or measured from either side of the bridge.
[F02D 2041/2075] Type of transistors or particular use thereof -- This group includes patents that specify a certain transistor technology such as MOSFETs, BJTs or IGBTs or describe a distinct application of those transistors, for example in fire-detection circuits or fire-suppression control electronics.
[F02D 2041/2079] the circuit having several coils acting on the same anchor -- F02D 2041/2079 covers electrical arrangements in wind-turbine generators where multiple coils are connected to a single magnetic anchor so that their magnetic fields act together, a configuration frequently employed for electromagnetic braking or pitch-control functions.
[F02D 2041/2082] the circuit being adapted to distribute current between different actuators or recuperate energy from actuators -- This subclass covers circuits that are designed to allocate electrical current among multiple actuators or to capture and reuse energy generated by actuators during operation, such as regenerative braking or load-sharing networks in electric or hybrid drive systems.
[F02D 2041/2086] with means for detecting circuit failures -- F02D 2041/2086 covers pumps that incorporate sensors or monitoring systems to detect failures in the hydraulic or electrical circuit, such as loss of pressure, flow interruptions, or component malfunctions, and to trigger alarms or shutdown actions. This subclass addresses the integration of fault-detection means within the pump's design for improved safety and reliability.
[F02D 2041/2089] detecting open circuits -- The CPC entry F02D 2041/2089 relates to hydraulic fluid-power systems and covers methods or devices that sense a break in an electrical or hydraulic circuit, such as monitoring pressure, flow or voltage to identify an open-circuit condition. It includes sensors, control circuits and diagnostic techniques used to detect and signal loss of continuity in hydraulic power units.
[F02D 2041/2093] detecting short circuits -- The CPC subclass F02D 2041/2093 covers methods and devices for detecting short-circuit faults in electric machines, such as stator or rotor windings. It includes monitoring techniques, sensor arrangements, and signal-analysis approaches used to identify abnormal current paths before damage occurs.
[F02D 2041/223] Diagnosis of fuel pressure sensors -- F02D 2041/223 covers methods and apparatus for detecting faults, calibrating and monitoring the performance of fuel-pressure sensors in internal-combustion engines, including diagnostic algorithms, signal-processing techniques and self-test procedures that assess sensor output to determine health or malfunction.
[F02D 2041/224] Diagnosis of the fuel system -- The CPC subclass F02D 2041/224 covers techniques for detecting and diagnosing faults in the fuel system of internal-combustion engines. It includes diagnostic methods, monitoring of fuel pressure, flow, composition, injector performance and related sensor-based or software analyses used to assess system health and identify malfunctions.
[F02D 2041/225] Leakage detection -- F02D 2041/225 covers methods and apparatus for detecting leaks in steam-turbine systems, including sensors, monitoring techniques and diagnostic procedures that identify loss of fluid or pressure to prevent efficiency loss and safety hazards. It encompasses both early-warning and continuous-inspection technologies used in turbine operation and maintenance.
[F02D 2041/226] Fail safe control for fuel injection pump -- The CPC subclass F02D 2041/226 covers safety-oriented control systems for fuel-injection pumps in internal-combustion engines. It includes mechanisms that detect faults, isolate malfunctioning components, and automatically shut down or switch to a backup mode to prevent dangerous fuel delivery. The focus is on fail-safe designs that maintain engine safety despite pump failures.
[F02D 2041/227] Limping Home, i.e. taking specific engine control measures at abnormal conditions -- F02D 2041/227 pertains to strategies for limiting or adapting the operation of an internal combustion engine when a fault or abnormal condition is detected, often called "limp home" mode. The measures may include reducing power output, altering fuel injection, ignition timing, or other control parameters to enable the vehicle to reach a safe stop while preventing further damage.
[F02D 2041/228] Warning displays -- F02D 2041/228 pertains to visual warning displays on agricultural or forestry tractors and similar self-propelled machines, covering devices that present visual signals to alert the operator or nearby persons about operating conditions, hazards or machine status. It includes electronic, electromechanical or purely mechanical display elements that convey such warnings directly on the vehicle.
[F02D 2041/281] between sensors and control unit -- The CPC group F02D 2041/281 covers the interface between sensors and the control unit in steam-turbine or hydraulic-turbine systems, including the hardware and signal paths that convey sensor data to the controller for monitoring and regulation of turbine operation.
[F02D 2041/283] the sensor directly giving at least one digital reading -- F02D 2041/283 covers sensors that directly produce at least one digital output, meaning the measured physical quantity is converted to a digital signal inside the sensor without analogue intermediate stages. This subclass includes devices where the conversion to digital data occurs within the sensing element itself, enabling immediate digital reading for further processing or display.
[F02D 2041/285] the sensor having a signal processing unit external to the engine control unit -- F02D 2041/285 covers engine management systems where the sensor's signal-processing unit is located separate from the engine control unit. The configuration allows the sensor to transmit raw data to an external processor that performs filtering, calibration or diagnostics before the information is used by the ECU for fuel, ignition or emission control. This arrangement can improve modularity, reduce ECU load and enable easier upgrades of sensor algorithms.
[F02D 2041/286] comprising means for signal processing -- F02D 2041/286 is a CPC subgroup that covers wind-turbine designs which include means for signal processing, such as sensors, controllers or software algorithms used to analyse wind, blade-pitch, rotor-speed or other operational data for monitoring, optimisation or control of the turbine.
[F02D 2041/288] for performing a transformation into the frequency domain, e.g. Fourier transformation
[F02D 2041/3052] the mode being the stratified charge compression-ignition mode -- The CPC subclass F02D 2041/3052 covers internal combustion engines that operate in a stratified-charge compression-ignition (SCCI) mode, wherein fuel is injected into a pre-charged air-rich zone creating stratified layers that ignite under compression without a separate spark. It pertains to engine designs that achieve controlled combustion by managing fuel-air stratification to improve efficiency and emissions.
[F02D 2041/3088] for air assisted injectors -- F02D 2041/3088 classifies devices that employ air-assisted injectors, where a stream of air is used to atomise or deliver a fluid such as fuel, coolant or lubricant into a chamber, conduit or combustion space. The subclass covers injector designs that combine a pressurised fluid source with a secondary air flow to improve spray pattern, penetration and mixing efficiency. It is applied to internal-combustion engine fuel systems, spray cooling equipment and similar fluid-handling installations.
[F02D 2041/3881] with multiple common rails, e.g. one rail per cylinder bank, or a high pressure rail and a low pressure rail -- This subclass concerns internal-combustion engines that employ more than one common fuel rail, for example a separate rail for each cylinder bank or distinct high-pressure and low-pressure rails, to distribute fuel to the injectors. It relates to fuel-injection arrangements using multiple rails to manage pressure or cylinder grouping.
[F02D 2041/389] for injecting directly into the cylinder -- CPC class F02D 2041/389 covers technologies that introduce a fluid, typically fuel or additives, directly into the combustion chamber of an internal-combustion engine. The entry includes injection nozzles, high-pressure pumps, and control methods designed for direct-cylinder delivery to improve mixing, efficiency, or emissions.
[F02D 21/00] Controlling engines characterised by their being supplied with non-airborne oxygen or other non-fuel gas -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control systems in which oxygen or secondary air and/or exhaust or other non-fuel gases are being fed to the engine in order to increase the engine output power and/or to clean the exhaust gases.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Methods and electrical aspects of controlling exhaust gas recirculation -> F02D41/0047  **Glossary:** - Non-airborne oxygen: Oxygen not from the air - Secondary air: Air not entering main intake
[F02D 21/02] peculiar to oxygen-fed engines -- F02D 21/02 covers combustion engines that are controlled by supplying oxygen or other non-air gases instead of atmospheric air, a method used to optimise combustion in specialised engines such as oxygen-fed power units. This subclass falls under controlling combustion engines within mechanical engineering.
[F02D 21/04] with circulation of exhaust gases in closed or semi-closed circuits -- The CPC subclass F02D 21/04 deals with control of combustion engines that receive oxygen or other non-fuel gases supplied from sources other than ambient air, using systems that recirculate exhaust gases in a closed or semi-closed circuit. It is part of the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Controlling Combustion Engines.
[F02D 21/06] peculiar to engines having other non-fuel gas added to combustion air -- F02D 21/06 covers features specific to combustion-engine control systems that operate with an added non-fuel gas such as enriched oxygen, steam or inert gases mixed with the intake air. It includes methods, devices and strategies for regulating fuel-air mixture, ignition timing and combustion stability under these conditions. The subclass is used for inventions that improve efficiency, emissions or performance when supplemental gases are introduced.
[F02D 21/08] the other gas being the exhaust gas of engine -- The code F02D 21/08 denotes methods for controlling combustion engines that use a supplemental gas other than air, specifically when the additional gas is the engine's own exhaust gas. It covers systems that recirculate exhaust to influence combustion, such as exhaust gas recirculation for emissions reduction or performance control.
[F02D 21/10] having secondary air added to the fuel-air mixture -- This subclass covers internal-combustion engines whose combustion is regulated by injecting extra air into the fuel-air mixture, typically through secondary-air-injection systems that augment the primary charge to improve combustion control.
[F02D 2200/00] Input parameters for engine control -- F02D 2200/00 covers the input parameters used for controlling combustion engines, such as temperature, pressure, fuel flow, and sensor data that inform engine management systems. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and controlling combustion engines categories.
[F02D 2200/02] the parameters being related to the engine -- F02D 2200/02 covers the parameters that serve as inputs for controlling combustion engines, such as temperature, pressure, speed, and fuel flow. It relates to the measurement, monitoring or specification of these engine-control variables.
[F02D 2200/021] Engine temperature -- F02D 2200/021 - Engine temperature refers to the measurement and use of a combustion engine's temperature as an input parameter for its control system, including sensors, monitoring and feedback to adjust fuel injection, ignition timing and emission management.
[F02D 2200/022] Estimation of engine temperature -- This subclass covers methods and systems for estimating the temperature inside a combustion engine as part of the input data used for engine control. It includes approaches that use sensor measurements, mathematical models or algorithmic predictions to determine cylinder or overall engine temperature for optimizing performance, emissions and safety.
[F02D 2200/023] Temperature of lubricating oil or working fluid -- This subclass covers features related to measuring or controlling the temperature of the lubricating oil or other working fluid in a combustion engine, typically for engine performance and durability. It includes sensors, thermostats and control strategies that monitor oil temperature as an input parameter for engine control.
[F02D 2200/024] Fluid pressure of lubricating oil or working fluid -- The code F02D 2200/024 designates the use of fluid-pressure data from the lubricating-oil or other working-fluid circuit as an input parameter for controlling a combustion engine. It covers measuring, monitoring and applying oil-pressure information to regulate fuel injection, ignition timing or other engine-management functions.
[F02D 2200/025] Engine noise, e.g. determined by using an acoustic sensor -- The CPC entry F02D 2200/025 falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and controlling combustion engines, covering input parameters for engine control specifically related to engine noise measured with an acoustic sensor. It addresses methods and devices for detecting and using sound data to optimise engine performance.
[F02D 2200/04] Engine intake system parameters -- F02D 2200/04 covers parameters of the engine intake system that serve as input data for controlling combustion engines. These may include air-flow rate, pressure, temperature and valve timing settings used to optimise fuel injection and combustion performance.
[F02D 2200/0402] the parameter being determined by using a model of the engine intake or its components -- This classification covers the parameter that is obtained by applying a mathematical or physical model of the engine intake system or its components to estimate its value for engine control. It serves as an input metric for strategies that regulate combustion processes.
[F02D 2200/0404] Throttle position -- The code F02D 2200/0404 designates the throttle position, an input parameter used to regulate air or fuel flow in combustion engines. It covers methods and devices that sense or adjust the throttle setting to control engine speed and output.
[F02D 2200/0406] Intake manifold pressure -- Intake-manifold pressure is the measured pressure of the air-fuel mixture within the intake manifold of a combustion engine and is used as an input parameter for engine-control systems to optimise fuel delivery, timing and overall performance.
[F02D 2200/0408] Estimation of intake manifold pressure -- This subgroup deals with techniques for determining the pressure inside an engine's intake manifold, such as using sensor data, mathematical models or inference methods, to support real-time control of combustion processes. It falls under controlling combustion engines, specifically input parameters for engine control.
[F02D 2200/0411] Volumetric efficiency -- Volumetric efficiency refers to the ratio of the actual amount of air-fuel mixture drawn into a combustion engine to the theoretical maximum based on cylinder displacement, reflecting how effectively the engine fills its cylinders. In the CPC hierarchy it is an input parameter used for controlling combustion engines, influencing performance, fuel consumption and emissions.
[F02D 2200/0414] Air temperature -- F02D 2200/0414 deals with air temperature as an input parameter for controlling combustion engines, encompassing methods and devices that measure ambient or intake air temperature to adjust fuel delivery, ignition timing or other engine settings for optimal performance and emissions.
[F02D 2200/0416] Estimation of air temperature -- This class concerns techniques for estimating the temperature of intake or ambient air used as an input parameter in the control of combustion engines. It includes sensor-based, model-based and algorithmic methods that provide air-temperature data for engine management systems.
[F02D 2200/0418] Air humidity -- Air humidity denotes the moisture content of the intake air supplied to a combustion engine, a key input parameter for engine control systems. Measuring this variable allows the control unit to adjust fueling, timing and other settings to maintain optimal performance, efficiency and emissions under varying atmospheric conditions.
[F02D 2200/06] Fuel or fuel supply system parameters -- The subgroup F02D 2200/06 covers input parameters that describe the fuel or fuel-supply system for controlling combustion engines, including fuel pressure, flow rate, injection timing, fuel composition and related characteristics used by engine-control strategies.
[F02D 2200/0602] Fuel pressure -- F02D 2200/0602 covers the measurement, regulation, and monitoring of fuel pressure as an input parameter used to control combustion engine operation. It relates to systems that sense or adjust fuel pressure to optimize performance, emissions, and fuel efficiency within engine control strategies.
[F02D 2200/0604] Estimation of fuel pressure -- This subclass covers methods and devices for estimating the pressure of fuel supplied to a combustion engine as an input parameter for engine control. It includes sensor-based measurement, model-based calculation and data processing techniques that provide real-time fuel pressure information for optimizing combustion.
[F02D 2200/0606] Fuel temperature -- F02D 2200/0606 pertains to the measurement and use of fuel temperature as an input parameter for controlling combustion engines. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the control of such engines, addressing how fuel temperature affects engine performance, efficiency and emissions.
[F02D 2200/0608] Estimation of fuel temperature -- This subclass concerns methods for determining the temperature of fuel supplied to a combustion engine, used as an input parameter in engine control strategies. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and their control.
[F02D 2200/0611] Fuel type, fuel composition or fuel quality -- F02D 2200/0611 pertains to input parameters for engine control that describe the fuel's type, composition or quality, focusing on how these characteristics influence the operation of combustion engines. It is used to classify technical information that links fuel properties to control strategies within mechanical engineering.
[F02D 2200/0612] determined by estimation -- This subclass covers techniques for obtaining engine-control input values by estimation rather than direct measurement, including model-based calculations, sensor-fusion algorithms and predictive methods used to infer parameters such as air-fuel ratio, temperature or pressure for combustion-engine regulation.
[F02D 2200/0614] Actual fuel mass or fuel injection amount -- F02D 2200/0614 covers the measurement, determination, or specification of the actual mass of fuel delivered to a combustion engine or the quantity injected per cycle, used as an input parameter for engine control systems. It relates to methods and devices that monitor or calculate fuel flow to optimise combustion, emissions and performance.
[F02D 2200/0616] determined by estimation -- F02D 2200/0616 covers the use of estimated values for input parameters that are used to control combustion engines. It includes methods that derive required data such as temperature, pressure, fuel flow or speed by calculation or inference rather than direct measurement.
[F02D 2200/0618] Actual fuel injection timing or delay, e.g. determined from fuel pressure drop -- The code F02D 2200/0618 covers techniques for obtaining the real-time fuel injection timing or its delay in combustion engines, typically by monitoring the drop in fuel pressure. This information is used as an input parameter for engine-control strategies.
[F02D 2200/0625] Fuel consumption, e.g. measured in fuel liters per 100 kms or miles per gallon
[F02D 2200/063] Lift of the valve needle -- F02D 2200/063 relates to the lift of the valve needle, an input parameter that influences fuel injection timing and valve operation in combustion engines. It is classified under mechanical engineering, then combustion engines, and finally controlling combustion engines, defining how needle movement is measured and used for engine control.
[F02D 2200/08] Exhaust gas treatment apparatus parameters -- The subclass F02D 2200/08 covers the input parameters that are used to govern exhaust-gas-treatment devices on combustion engines. It includes measured or calculated values such as temperature, pressure, flow rate, composition and other sensor data that feed engine-control strategies for after-treatment systems.
[F02D 2200/0802] Temperature of the exhaust gas treatment apparatus -- The classification covers the measurement or monitoring of the temperature of an exhaust-gas treatment device, such as a catalytic converter or filter, as an input parameter for controlling a combustion engine. It relates to how thermal data from these components are used in engine-control strategies to optimize performance, emissions, and reliability.
[F02D 2200/0804] Estimation of the temperature of the exhaust gas treatment apparatus -- The code F02D 2200/0804 pertains to techniques for estimating the temperature of exhaust-gas treatment components such as catalytic converters or filters within a combustion engine. It includes sensor-based or model-based methods that provide temperature input data for engine-control strategies aimed at optimizing emissions and performance.
[F02D 2200/0806] NOx storage amount, i.e. amount of NOx stored on NOx trap -- The code F02D 2200/0806 designates the amount of nitrogen oxides stored on a NOx trap, an input parameter used by engine control units to manage exhaust after-treatment and regeneration in combustion engines.
[F02D 2200/0808] NOx storage capacity, i.e. maximum amount of NOx that can be stored on NOx trap -- The code F02D 2200/0808 identifies the parameter defining the maximum amount of nitrogen oxide that a NOx trap can store during engine operation. This metric is used to assess and optimise combustion-engine control strategies for emission reduction.
[F02D 2200/0811] NOx storage efficiency -- The entry covers methods for assessing how efficiently a nitrogen-oxide storage device in a catalytic after-treatment system captures NOx, providing quantitative data used as input parameters for engine-control strategies aimed at reducing emissions from combustion engines.
[F02D 2200/0812] Particle filter loading -- It refers to the measurement or estimation of the amount of particulate matter accumulated in a vehicle's exhaust filtration system used as a signal for engine management strategies. Data on filter loading helps adjust fuel injection, regeneration timing, and emission control to maintain performance and meet regulations.
[F02D 2200/0814] Oxygen storage amount -- The subclass F02D 2200/0814 defines the amount of oxygen stored in a reservoir or fuel-system component as an input parameter for controlling a combustion engine. It covers methods, sensors, or devices that determine or manage that stored oxygen to optimise engine performance, fuel-air mixture and emissions.
[F02D 2200/0816] Oxygen storage capacity -- The CPC subclass F02D 2200/0816 concerns the oxygen storage capacity of components such as intake manifolds, fuel tanks or storage devices used in combustion engine control. It relates to quantifying how much oxygen can be retained for later use, an input parameter that affects fuel-air mixture regulation and engine performance.
[F02D 2200/0818] SOx storage amount, e.g. for SOx trap or NOx trap
[F02D 2200/10] Parameters related to the engine output, e.g. engine torque or engine speed -- This CPC subclass covers input parameters that describe a combustion-engine's output, such as engine torque or engine speed, which are used for engine control. It is nested under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, controlling combustion engines, and input parameters for engine control.
[F02D 2200/1002] Output torque -- The code F02D 2200/1002 designates the parameter of output torque used to control combustion engines. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the sub-section for controlling engines by monitoring input variables that affect performance. This classification covers how torque information is employed in engine-control strategies.
[F02D 2200/1004] Estimation of the output torque -- This subclass covers methods and devices that calculate or predict the torque produced by a combustion engine based on input data used for engine control. Techniques include sensor fusion, model-based estimation, and real-time algorithms to provide torque feedback for control strategies.
[F02D 2200/1006] Engine torque losses, e.g. friction or pumping losses or losses caused by external loads of accessories -- The subclass deals with the determination, estimation or compensation of torque losses in combustion engines, encompassing friction losses, pumping losses and losses caused by external accessory loads such as alternators or water pumps, and is used as an input parameter for engine control.
[F02D 2200/101] Engine speed -- This sub-group addresses engine speed as an input parameter for controlling combustion engines, covering how rotational speed is measured, set, and regulated within engine management systems.
[F02D 2200/1012] Engine speed gradient -- F02D 2200/1012 covers parameters that describe the rate of change of engine speed used for controlling combustion engines. It relates to input variables for engine control systems, such as acceleration or deceleration rates, which influence fuel injection and ignition timing.
[F02D 2200/1015] Engines misfires -- F02D 2200/1015 covers input parameters related to engine misfires, encompassing the detection, measurement and reporting of incomplete or uneven combustion events in combustion-engine control systems. It includes sensors, signal processing, threshold settings and data used to trigger corrective actions such as fuel-trim adjustments or ignition timing changes.
[F02D 2200/50] said parameters being related to the vehicle or its components -- F02D 2200/50 defines input parameters used for controlling a combustion engine that are directly related to the vehicle or its components, such as vehicle speed, gear position, throttle setting, brake status or load on the drivetrain. These parameters inform the engine-management system to adjust fuel delivery, ignition timing, or other operating conditions.
[F02D 2200/501] Vehicle speed -- F02D 2200/501 covers vehicle speed as an input parameter used in the control of combustion engines. It relates to how the speed of a vehicle is measured or estimated to adjust fuel delivery, ignition timing, or other engine functions for optimal performance.
[F02D 2200/502] Neutral gear position -- Neutral gear position refers to the state of a vehicle's transmission when no forward or reverse gear is engaged, serving as an input parameter for engine control systems in combustion engines. It informs control strategies such as idle speed regulation, fuel cut-off, or torque management to ensure safe and efficient operation while the drivetrain is disengaged. This parameter is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, controlling combustion engines, and input parameters for engine control.
[F02D 2200/503] Battery correction, i.e. corrections as a function of the state of the battery, its output or its type -- This subclass addresses adjustments to engine-control input parameters that are derived from the battery's condition, such as its state of charge, output voltage or specific type, enabling the combustion engine's control system to compensate for variations in battery performance.
[F02D 2200/60] said parameters being related to the driver demands or status -- F02D 2200/60 refers to input parameters for engine control that are linked to the driver's demands or the vehicle's current status, such as accelerator pedal position, gear-selection signals or load requests. It is situated in the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Controlling Combustion Engines.
[F02D 2200/602] Pedal position -- Pedal position refers to the location of the accelerator or brake pedals, which provides a direct driver input used to modulate fuel injection, airflow, and ignition timing in combustion engines. As an input parameter, it influences engine speed and power output.
[F02D 2200/604] Engine control mode selected by driver, e.g. to manually start particle filter regeneration or to select driving style -- This entry covers engine-control modes that are selected by the driver, such as manually initiating diesel-filter regeneration or choosing a specific driving-style setting. It pertains to the input parameters used for controlling combustion-engine operation.
[F02D 2200/606] Driving style, e.g. sporty or economic driving -- The classification F02D 2200/606 relates to engine-control input parameters that reflect the driver's chosen style, such as sporty or economic driving. It covers how a vehicle's control system adjusts fuel, ignition or other settings to achieve the desired performance or fuel-efficiency characteristics.
[F02D 2200/70] said parameters being related to the vehicle exterior -- F02D 2200/70 is a CPC subgroup under Mechanical Engineering, Combustion Engines, Controlling Combustion Engines, Input parameters for engine control. It covers engine-control input data that are linked to the vehicle exterior, such as aerodynamic drag, frontal area, or external temperature sensors.
[F02D 2200/701] Information about vehicle position, e.g. from navigation system or GPS signal -- This subclass covers the use of vehicle-position data, such as from a navigation system or GPS signal, as an input parameter for controlling a combustion engine. It is applied when engine management systems adjust fuel injection, ignition timing, or emissions based on the vehicle's location.
[F02D 2200/702] Road conditions
[F02D 2200/703] Atmospheric pressure -- F02D 2200/703 identifies atmospheric pressure as an input parameter used for controlling combustion engines. It is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and engine-control topics, indicating that the surrounding air pressure is taken into account when adjusting fuel delivery, ignition timing, or other control functions.
[F02D 2200/704] Estimation of atmospheric pressure -- F02D 2200/704 deals with techniques for estimating the ambient atmospheric pressure as an input parameter for controlling combustion engines. Accurate pressure estimation enables the engine management system to adjust fuel delivery, ignition timing and other settings, improving performance, efficiency and emissions control.
[F02D 2250/00] Controlling, e.g. regulating, fuel injection -- The F02D 2250/00 class covers methods and devices for controlling combustion-engine operation by regulating the fuel-injection process. It includes systems that adjust fuel quantity, timing or pressure to optimise engine performance, emissions and efficiency.
[F02D 2250/02] Fuel evaporation in fuel rails, e.g. in common rails -- The entry F02D 2250/02 covers methods and devices that control fuel evaporation within fuel rails, such as common-rail systems, to maintain optimal fuel temperature and pressure for accurate injection. It addresses the regulation of fuel feed and vapor management to improve combustion efficiency and engine performance.
[F02D 2250/04] Fuel pressure pulsation in common rails -- This subclass deals with the occurrence of pressure fluctuations in the fuel supply line of common-rail injection systems, addressing their causes, detection techniques and mitigation measures to maintain stable fuel delivery for combustion engine control.
[F02D 2250/06] Reverse rotation of engine -- The F02D 2250/06 subclass covers techniques for deliberately causing a combustion engine to rotate opposite its normal direction, such as during testing, start-stop operation or control of the crankshaft. It includes methods that regulate fuel injection or other control measures to achieve reverse rotation, as part of broader systems for controlling combustion engines.
[F02D 2250/08] Engine blow-by from crankcase chamber -- This CPC subclass covers measures for handling blow-by gases that escape from a crankcase chamber of a combustion engine, including control techniques or devices that reduce emissions, protect lubrication, and maintain performance.
[F02D 2250/11] Oil dilution, i.e. prevention thereof or special controls according thereto -- The CPC entry F02D 2250/11 pertains to control measures that prevent oil from diluting fuel in combustion engines, covering methods, devices and special control strategies designed to keep oil and fuel separate. It is used for patents involving fuel-injection regulation, oil-separation systems or monitoring techniques that maintain oil-free combustion.
[F02D 2250/12] Timing of calculation, i.e. specific timing aspects when calculation or updating of engine parameter is performed -- F02D 2250/12 relates to the timing of calculations or updates of engine parameters in controlling combustion engines, such as when fuel injection or other control actions are computed. It covers methods that specify at which point in the engine cycle the calculation occurs, enabling precise regulation of engine performance. This subclass falls under controlling combustion engines within mechanical engineering.
[F02D 2250/14] Timing of measurement, e.g. synchronisation of measurements to the engine cycle -- F02D 2250/14 pertains to the timing of measurements in combustion engines, specifying how data acquisition can be synchronised with the engine cycle. It falls under controlling combustion engines, particularly the regulation of fuel injection, by ensuring measurements are taken at precise points in the cycle.
[F02D 2250/16] End position calibration, i.e. calculation or measurement of actuator end positions, e.g. for throttle or its driving actuator -- The F02D 2250/16 entry concerns the calibration of actuator end positions in combustion-engine control systems, such as determining or measuring the extreme travel limits of a throttle or its driving actuator. It addresses methods and devices used to calculate or verify these positions to ensure precise fuel-injection or throttle regulation.
[F02D 2250/18] Control of the engine output torque -- The CPC code F02D 2250/18 covers methods and devices for regulating the output torque of combustion engines, typically by adjusting fuel injection parameters. It falls under the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and controlling combustion engines.
[F02D 2250/21] during a transition between engine operation modes or states -- The CPC subclass F02D 2250/21 covers methods and devices for controlling a combustion engine while it shifts between operating modes or states, such as during startup, shutdown, load change, or mode transition. It includes regulation of fuel injection and related parameters during these transitions.
[F02D 2250/22] by keeping a torque reserve, i.e. with temporarily reduced drive train or engine efficiency -- The F02D 2250/22 subclass refers to control strategies for combustion engines that maintain a torque reserve, allowing the drivetrain or engine to operate at reduced efficiency for short periods. This approach is used to manage power output, emissions or fuel consumption by temporarily limiting engine performance.
[F02D 2250/24] by using an external load, e.g. a generator -- F02D 2250/24 covers methods of controlling a combustion engine by applying an external load, for example a generator, to regulate fuel injection or other operating parameters.
[F02D 2250/26] by applying a torque limit -- This subclass covers control techniques for combustion engines that limit the engine's torque output, usually by regulating fuel injection or related parameters to prevent the engine from exceeding a preset torque value.
[F02D 2250/28] Control for reducing torsional vibrations, e.g. at acceleration -- F02D 2250/28 covers control systems that reduce torsional vibrations in combustion engines, particularly during acceleration. It includes mechanisms such as fuel-injection regulation or other actuation methods designed to dampen twisting oscillations of the crankshaft.
[F02D 2250/31] Control of the fuel pressure -- This subclass concerns methods and devices that regulate the pressure of fuel supplied to a combustion engine, including fuel-pump control, pressure-sensor feedback and pressure-maintaining valves. It covers systems that adjust fuel pressure to achieve proper fuel injection and engine performance.
[F02D 2250/32] Air-fuel ratio control in a diesel engine -- This subclass addresses the regulation of the air-fuel mixture in diesel engines, covering sensors, electronic control units and injection-timing strategies used to maintain optimal combustion and emissions performance.
[F02D 2250/34] Control of exhaust back pressure, e.g. for turbocharged engines -- The entry relates to systems and methods that regulate the exhaust back pressure in combustion engines, especially turbocharged ones, in order to optimise performance and emissions. It includes devices such as waste-gates, exhaust-valve actuators and control algorithms that adjust back-pressure during operation.
[F02D 2250/36] Control for minimising NOx emissions -- F02D 2250/36 covers devices or control strategies in combustion engines that specifically aim to reduce nitrogen-oxide (NOx) emissions. It includes methods such as precise fuel-injection timing, exhaust-gas recirculation and after-treatment control to minimise NOx formation.
[F02D 2250/38] Control for minimising smoke emissions, e.g. by applying smoke limitations on the fuel injection amount -- A control system that reduces smoke emissions from combustion engines by imposing limits on the amount of fuel injected, thereby regulating the injection quantity to meet smoke-reduction targets. It is classified under controlling combustion engines in the mechanical-engineering hierarchy.
[F02D 2250/41] Control to generate negative pressure in the intake manifold, e.g. for fuel vapor purging or brake booster -- The entry covers methods or devices that generate negative pressure in an engine's intake manifold, typically to purge fuel vapour or to assist a brake-booster system, as part of controlling fuel injection in combustion engines.
[F02D 23/00] Controlling engines characterised by their being supercharged -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control systems for controlling the power output or rotational speed of the engine by other means than the throttle valve. In case of a supercharged engine it is possible for the power output or rotational speed to be additionally influenced for example by way of a controllable valve which is arranged parallel to the compressor in the induction track of the engine.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Electrical control of supply of combustible mixture or its constituents -> F02D41/00  **Glossary:** - Supercharged: Turbocharged
[F02D 23/005] with the supercharger being mechanically driven by the engine  supercharger drives  F02B39/00 -- F02D 23/005 covers methods or devices for controlling a combustion engine in which the supercharger is driven directly by the engine itself, such as via gears, shafts or belt drives. This sub-group is part of the CPC hierarchy for mechanical engineering, focusing on supercharged engines where the charging unit receives its power mechanically from the engine it assists.
[F02D 23/02] the engines being of fuel-injection type -- This CPC subclass concerns supercharged combustion engines that are controlled by fuel-injection systems, covering methods and apparatus for injecting fuel into the cylinders. It belongs to the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, controlling combustion engines, and specifically supercharged engine control.
[F02D 2400/00] Control systems adapted for specific engine types -- The CPC subclass F02D 2400/00 covers control systems that are customized for particular types of combustion engines, such as diesel, gasoline, or rotary engines, enabling optimized performance, fuel efficiency and emissions control. It belongs to the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering - Combustion Engines - Controlling Combustion Engines.
[F02D 2400/02] Four-stroke combustion engines with electronic control -- This subclass covers four-stroke internal-combustion engines that incorporate electronic control units for managing fuel injection, ignition timing, and valve operation. The classification applies to systems designed specifically for four-stroke engines, integrating sensors and micro-processors to optimize performance and emissions.
[F02D 2400/04] Two-stroke combustion engines with electronic control -- The class F02D 2400/04 pertains to two-stroke combustion engines that employ electronic control systems to regulate functions such as fuel injection, ignition timing and exhaust handling. It addresses engine-specific electronic strategies for managing performance, emissions and efficiency within the broader category of controlling combustion engines.
[F02D 2400/06] Small engines with electronic control, e.g. for hand held tools -- This CPC subclass covers small internal combustion engines equipped with electronic control units, typically used in handheld power tools. It pertains to control systems specifically adapted to these compact engines, addressing electronic regulation of fuel injection, ignition timing, and speed.
[F02D 2400/08] Redundant elements, e.g. two sensors for measuring the same parameter -- The subclass F02D 2400/08 covers control systems for combustion engines that incorporate redundant elements, for example employing two sensors to measure the same parameter, thereby enhancing fault tolerance and reliability.
[F02D 2400/11] After-sales modification devices designed to be used to modify an engine afterwards -- This CPC subclass covers devices supplied after a vehicle's sale that can be installed to alter the operation of a combustion engine, typically by adjusting its control system for a particular engine type. The devices are intended for retrofitting existing engines to change performance, emissions, or fuel management.
[F02D 2400/12] Engine control specially adapted for a transmission comprising a torque converter or for continuously variable transmissions -- F02D 2400/12 refers to engine control systems specially adapted for a transmission that incorporates a torque converter or a continuously variable transmission, focusing on the coordination of engine management with the transmission's operating characteristics to improve performance, efficiency and emissions.
[F02D 2400/14] Power supply for engine control systems -- F02D 2400/14 covers power supply devices specifically designed for engine control systems, providing the electrical energy needed to operate electronic control units in combustion engines. It includes batteries, generators, converters and related components tailored to the particular requirements of different engine types.
[F02D 2400/16] Adaptation of engine control systems to a different battery voltages, e.g. for using high voltage batteries -- This subclass deals with modifications to engine control systems that allow them to operate with battery supplies of different voltages, such as adapting the electronics for high-voltage batteries, ensuring proper function of the combustion engine's control hardware.
[F02D 2400/18] Packaging of the electronic circuit in a casing -- This classification covers the enclosure of electronic control modules used to manage combustion engine operation. It includes design and integration of protective casings that house circuitry for engine-specific control systems.
[F02D 2400/21] Engine cover with integrated cabling -- F02D 2400/21 relates to engine covers that incorporate integrated cabling, providing built-in routes or connectors for sensors, actuators and control units within the cover itself. By embedding the wiring, the design reduces external harnesses, protects cables from heat and debris, and streamlines installation in combustion-engine control systems.
[F02D 2400/22] Connectors or cables specially adapted for engine management applications -- F02D 2400/22 covers electrical connectors and cable assemblies designed specifically for use in engine management systems of combustion engines. The entry includes connections that handle signals, power or data between sensors, actuators and control units, and are tailored to the harsh operating conditions of engines.
[F02D 25/00] Controlling two or more co-operating engines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods and devices for controlling engine arrangements comprising at least a first and a second internal combustion engine, wherein each engine comprises a crankshaft and at least one cylinder with a piston connected to said crankshaft, a controllable clutch arranged between the crankshafts, an engine control unit (ECU) for controlling the first and second engine and sensors for monitoring at least one operating parameter for the said engines connected to the engine control unit.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cutting out individual cylinders -> F02D17/00 - Controlling engines, such controlling being peculiar to the devices driven thereby -> F02D29/00 - Conjoint control of vehicle sub-units of different type or different function -> B60W10/00  **Glossary:** - Conjoint control: Control of 2 or more cooperating engines
[F02D 25/02] to synchronise speed -- F02D 25/02 covers technical solutions for synchronising the speed of two or more cooperating internal-combustion engines, including control mechanisms, feedback loops and actuating devices that keep the engines operating at a common rotational rate to improve performance and reliability.
[F02D 25/04] by cutting-out engines -- F02D 25/04 covers methods for controlling multiple cooperating engines by selective deactivation or cutting out of one or more engines to optimise performance, fuel consumption or emissions. This technique is used in vehicles or power plants where engines operate in parallel and can be shut down when not needed.
[F02D 27/00] Controlling engines characterised by their being reversible -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines that can be operated in two directions, i.e. of which the crankshaft can turn in two directions during running of the engine. These engines require amongst others displaceable camshafts. The reversing requires a control program during which several parameters of the engine, e.g. speed, throttle- position, fuel supply should be controlled.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Toothed gearings for reversing rotary motion -> F16H3/00
[F02D 2700/00] Mechanical control of speed or power of a single cylinder piston engine -- This CPC subclass deals with mechanical means for regulating the speed or output power of single-cylinder piston engines, such as governors, throttles, and linkage systems that adjust fuel or air supply without electronic control.
[F02D 2700/02] Controlling by changing the air or fuel supply -- The entry covers methods for controlling the speed or power of a single-cylinder piston engine by varying the amount of air or fuel supplied, encompassing devices or strategies that adjust the air-fuel mixture to regulate combustion. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Controlling Combustion Engines in the CPC system.
[F02D 2700/0202] for engines working with gaseous fuel, including those working with an ignition liquid -- F02D 2700/0202 pertains to mechanical devices that regulate the speed or power of a single-cylinder piston engine powered by gaseous fuel, and also includes engines that use a liquid for ignition. The classification falls under controlling combustion engines within mechanical engineering.
[F02D 2700/0205] Controlling the air supply as well as the fuel supply -- F02D 2700/0205 covers systems that adjust both the intake air and the fuel delivery to a single-cylinder piston engine in order to regulate its speed or output power. The classification lies under mechanical control of combustion engines within the CPC.
[F02D 2700/0207] Controlling the air or mixture supply -- The entry covers methods and devices that regulate the quantity of air or fuel-air mixture supplied to a single-cylinder piston engine in order to control its speed or power output. It applies to mechanical systems that adjust intake flow as part of combustion-engine control.
[F02D 2700/021] Engines without compressor -- F02D 2700/021 covers single-cylinder piston engines in which speed or power is regulated mechanically without the use of a compressor, such as simple reciprocating engines that rely on direct mechanical control rather than forced induction.
[F02D 2700/0212] Engines with compressor -- A CPC subclass for engines that integrate a compressor to raise intake air pressure, enhancing combustion efficiency and delivering greater power or speed control in single-cylinder piston engines. It sits within the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and controlling combustion engines.
[F02D 2700/0215] Controlling the fuel supply -- F02D 2700/0215 concerns systems and methods for regulating the fuel flow to a single-cylinder piston engine in order to control its speed or power output. [F02D 2700/0217] for mixture compressing engines using liquid fuel
[F02D 2700/022] Controlling the air or the mixture supply as well as the fuel supply -- This CPC subgroup covers systems or methods that simultaneously control the airflow or air-fuel mixture together with the fuel delivery to regulate speed or power in a single-cylinder piston engine. It belongs to the hierarchy mechanical engineering - combustion engines - controlling combustion engines.
[F02D 2700/0223] Engines with fuel injection -- Engines with fuel injection are piston engines that use injected fuel rather than a carburetor to manage ignition and combustion, allowing precise control of speed and power in single-cylinder configurations. This subclass addresses the mechanical systems and methods that regulate fuel delivery to achieve desired performance in combustion engines.
[F02D 2700/0225] Control of air or mixture supply -- This CPC subclass deals with methods and devices that regulate the air or fuel-air mixture supplied to a single-cylinder piston engine to control its speed or power output. It includes mechanical or electronic controls that adjust throttles, carburetors, fuel-injection or comparable supply systems.
[F02D 2700/0228] Engines without compressor -- A single-cylinder piston engine in which the intake air is not pressurised by a compressor, relying on natural aspiration or other non-compressive methods to supply the combustion mixture. This sub-category falls under controlling combustion engines within mechanical engineering, covering speed or power regulation for such uncontrolled-air-flow engines.
[F02D 2700/023] by means of one throttle device -- This subclass covers mechanical control of speed or power of a single-cylinder piston engine by means of one throttle device. It belongs to the CPC hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Controlling Combustion Engines and is identified by the code F02D 2700/023.
[F02D 2700/0233] depending on several parameters -- The entry covers mechanical methods for regulating the speed or power output of a single-cylinder piston engine by varying controls according to multiple operating parameters, such as load, temperature, fuel rate, or ignition timing, to achieve desired performance.
[F02D 2700/0235] depending on the pressure of a gaseous or liquid medium -- The code F02D 2700/0235 refers to devices that regulate the speed or power of a single-cylinder piston engine by sensing the pressure of a gas or liquid and adjusting operation accordingly. Such pressure-dependent control systems include governors, fuel-delivery regulators, or hydraulic actuators that vary engine output in response to pressure changes.
[F02D 2700/0238] depending on the number of revolutions of a centrifugal governor -- This CPC subclass pertains to mechanical control of speed or power in single-cylinder piston engines by varying operation according to the number of revolutions measured by a centrifugal governor. It is located in the hierarchy under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and controlling combustion engines.
[F02D 2700/0241] depending on another parameter -- This CPC subclass concerns methods and devices that regulate the speed or power of a single-cylinder piston engine by reference to a secondary variable such as load, temperature, fuel quality or another operating condition. It includes the sensors, control algorithms and mechanical linkages that adjust engine output in response to that additional parameter.
[F02D 2700/0243] by means of a plurality of throttle devices -- A method for mechanically controlling the speed or power of a single-cylinder piston engine by employing more than one throttle device, allowing coordinated regulation of airflow or fuel to achieve desired engine performance.
[F02D 2700/0246] for engines with compressor -- F02D 2700/0246 designates mechanical control systems for the speed or power of single-cylinder piston engines that incorporate a compressor, such as superchargers or forced-induction mechanisms used to boost intake pressure.
[F02D 2700/0248] by means of throttle devices -- The entry covers mechanical control of speed or power for a single-cylinder piston engine using throttle devices. It is classified under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → controlling combustion engines, and specifies the method of regulating engine output by adjusting the throttle.
[F02D 2700/0251] in the intake conduit -- The CPC subclass F02D 2700/0251 covers methods or devices that adjust the flow of the intake conduit to control speed or power of a single-cylinder piston engine. It pertains to mechanical control of combustion engine performance by regulating the amount or pressure of the air-fuel mixture entering the cylinder. This subclass is part of the broader group for controlling combustion engines.
[F02D 2700/0253] in the outlet conduit -- The F02D 2700/0253 subclass concerns means of mechanically controlling the speed or power of a single-cylinder piston engine by acting on the outlet conduit, such as exhaust or discharge piping, to modify flow or pressure and thereby influence engine performance.
[F02D 2700/0256] by changing the speed of the compressor -- This subclass covers mechanical methods for regulating the speed or power of a single-cylinder piston engine by varying the speed of its compressor, a technique used to control combustion-engine performance.
[F02D 2700/0258] by other means -- The subcategory F02D 2700/0258 covers mechanical methods for regulating the speed or power of a single-cylinder piston engine by means other than the usual throttle, valve timing or fuel-metering devices, such as alternative mechanical governors, variable-stroke mechanisms or other unconventional control arrangements.
[F02D 2700/0261] Control of the fuel supply -- This CPC subclass concerns methods and devices for regulating the fuel feed to a single-cylinder piston engine in order to control its speed or output power. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and controlling combustion engines.
[F02D 2700/0264] for engines with a fuel jet working with depression -- This CPC entry covers mechanical control of speed or power for single-cylinder piston engines that use a fuel-jet operated by a vacuum or depression. It falls under the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Controlling Combustion Engines.
[F02D 2700/0266] for engines with fuel injection -- The CPC code F02D 2700/0266 classifies mechanical arrangements that regulate speed or power in a single-cylinder piston engine when the engine employs fuel injection, such as governors, throttles or injection-timing mechanisms that adjust the amount or timing of injected fuel.
[F02D 2700/0269] for air compressing engines with compression ignition -- This CPC subclass addresses the mechanical control of speed or power for a single-cylinder piston engine that functions as an air-compressing engine using compression ignition.
[F02D 2700/0271] Controlling the air supply as well as the fuel supply -- The CPC entry F02D 2700/0271 covers methods and devices that regulate both the air-fuel mixture and the air flow into a single-cylinder piston engine to control its speed or power output, falling under mechanical control of combustion engines.
[F02D 2700/0274] Controlling the air supply -- The entry covers systems and methods that regulate the amount of air entering a single-cylinder piston engine in order to control its speed or power output, encompassing mechanical devices such as throttle valves, air-control levers and related actuation mechanisms.
