[F02D 2700/0276] Engines without compressor -- F02D 2700/0276 covers single-cylinder piston engines whose speed or power is mechanically regulated but that operate without a compressor, i.e., naturally aspirated engines. It falls under the CPC hierarchy for controlling combustion engines within mechanical engineering.
[F02D 2700/0279] Engines with compressor -- This subclass covers combustion engines that incorporate a compressor as part of their design, typically to increase air-charge pressure for improved performance. It applies to single-cylinder piston engines where the compressor is integrated for speed or power control.
[F02D 2700/0282] Control of fuel supply -- This CPC subclass covers methods and devices that regulate the fuel supplied to a single-cylinder piston engine in order to control its speed or power output. It is situated within the mechanical control of combustion engines category of mechanical engineering.
[F02D 2700/0284] by acting on the fuel pump control element -- This CPC subclass covers mechanical methods for regulating the speed or power of a single-cylinder piston engine by adjusting the fuel pump control element. It belongs to the controlling combustion engines group within mechanical engineering.
[F02D 2700/0287] depending on several parameters -- A control method for a single-cylinder piston engine that varies its speed or power by simultaneously monitoring several engine parameters such as pressure, temperature, load or fuel flow, and adjusting operation accordingly.
[F02D 2700/0289] depending on the pressure of a gaseous or liquid medium -- The entry covers mechanisms that regulate the speed or power output of a single-cylinder piston engine by varying the pressure of a supplied gas or liquid, such as using pressure-controlled fuel or hydraulic actuation to adjust combustion or piston motion. It pertains to mechanical control systems for combustion engines.
[F02D 2700/0292] depending on the speed of a centrifugal governor -- F02D 2700/0292 covers mechanical control systems for single-cylinder piston engines that adjust operation according to the speed of a centrifugal governor. The device varies fuel, air or ignition parameters in response to governor speed, thereby regulating engine power or speed.
[F02D 2700/0294] depending on another parameter -- This CPC subclass covers mechanical methods for regulating the speed or power of a single-cylinder piston engine by linking the control to a secondary parameter such as temperature, pressure, load, or another operating condition.
[F02D 2700/0297] by control means in the fuel conduit between pump and injector -- This subclass covers devices that regulate the operation of a single-cylinder piston engine by adjusting the fuel flow in the line that connects the fuel pump to the injector. The control means are placed in the fuel conduit and affect engine speed or power output.
[F02D 2700/03] Controlling by changing the compression ratio -- The entry covers techniques for regulating the speed or power of a single-cylinder piston combustion engine by altering its compression ratio, such as adjusting cylinder volume, clearance, or piston position to change the pressure developed during compression. This method is used as a mechanical means of controlling engine performance.
[F02D 2700/035] without modifying the volume of the compression space, e.g. by changing the valve timing -- F02D 2700/035 covers mechanical control of speed or power of a single-cylinder piston engine achieved without changing the compression-space volume, for example by altering valve timing. This sub-group falls under combustion-engine controlling methods within the mechanical engineering hierarchy.
[F02D 2700/04] Controlling by throttling the exhaust conduit -- This classification covers the mechanical regulation of speed or power in a single-cylinder piston engine by restricting the flow of exhaust gases. It pertains to exhaust-throttling devices that adjust engine output in combustion-engine control systems.
[F02D 2700/05] Controlling by preventing combustion in one or more cylinders -- The subclass covers techniques that regulate the speed or power of a single-cylinder piston engine by disabling combustion in one or more cylinders, such as cylinder-deactivation strategies used to improve fuel efficiency or reduce emissions. It is part of the broader group on controlling combustion engines.
[F02D 2700/052] Methods therefor -- F02D 2700/052 covers methods for mechanically controlling the speed or power output of a single-cylinder piston engine. It falls under the CPC hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Controlling Combustion Engines and pertains to techniques used to adjust engine performance.
[F02D 2700/054] by keeping the exhaust valves open -- This subclass addresses controlling the speed or power of a single-cylinder piston engine by keeping the exhaust valves open, which limits the effective compression and expansion strokes and thus reduces the engine's output. It is applied to mechanical systems that use valve-opening timing as a means of regulating combustion-engine performance.
[F02D 2700/056] by interrupting the medium supply -- This classification covers methods that regulate the speed or output of a single-cylinder piston engine by periodically stopping or reducing the flow of the working fluid (fuel, air, steam, etc.) to the cylinder, typically using devices such as cut-off valves, injection timing control, or equivalent mechanisms.
[F02D 2700/058] by another method -- The CPC subclass F02D 2700/058 covers mechanical means for controlling the speed or power output of a single-cylinder piston engine when the control is achieved by a method other than the usual throttle or fuel-injection adjustments. It falls within the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and controlling combustion engines.
[F02D 2700/07] Automatic control systems according to one of the preceding groups in combination with control of the mechanism receiving the engine power -- This CPC subclass covers automatic control systems that are integrated with the control of the driven mechanism receiving power from a single-cylinder piston engine. It applies to devices that regulate engine speed or power while simultaneously managing the load or machine powered by the engine.
[F02D 2700/09] Other ways of controlling -- F02D 2700/09 refers to mechanical control of a single cylinder piston engine. It involves other ways of controlling speed or power. This is a subset of controlling combustion engines.
[F02D 2700/10] Control of the timing of the fuel supply period with relation to the piston movement -- This CPC subclass concerns mechanical control of the timing of fuel supply in relation to piston movement for a single-cylinder piston engine. It covers methods that adjust when fuel is injected during the engine cycle to regulate speed or power.
[F02D 27/02] by performing a program -- The subclass F02D 27/02 concerns control of reversible combustion engines by means of a programmed operation, where a software routine directs engine functions such as start-stop, load management or fuel regulation. This approach applies to engines that can run in both directions and uses a computer-based program to achieve the desired control.
[F02D 28/00] Program control of engines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Program-control of engines. Although operator control is suitable for automobile engines, there are many systems, such as standby emergency electric power systems, where this type of control is impractical and expensive. For these applications, automatic program control systems have been devised which perform necessary engine control functions without operator attendance. Program-control is also used in remote starting systems for automobile engines for example heating up the passenger cabin.
[F02D 29/00] Controlling engines, such controlling being peculiar to the devices driven thereby, the devices being other than parts or accessories essential to engine operation, e.g. controlling of engines by signals external thereto -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control systems characterised by the device driven by the engine. This device can be for example a vehicle, a pump or an electric generator.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Arrangement or mounting of plural diverse prime-movers for mutual or common propulsion, e.g. hybrid propulsion systems comprising electric motors and internal combustion engines -> B60K6/00 - Conjoint control of vehicle sub-units of different type or different function -> B60W10/00 - Control systems specially adapted for hybrid vehicles -> B60W20/00
[F02D 29/02] peculiar to engines driving vehicles -- F02D 29/02 covers control methods and devices that are specific to engines used to drive vehicles, excluding the core engine components themselves. It includes external signals or auxiliary systems that regulate engine performance, speed or emissions in automotive applications.
[F02D 29/04] peculiar to engines driving pumps -- F02D 29/04 concerns control mechanisms that are specific to engines used to drive pumps, where the control is applied to the engine rather than to the pump itself. These controls involve signals or devices external to the engine and are not part of the essential engine components.
[F02D 29/06] peculiar to engines driving electric generators
[F02D 3/00] Controlling low-pressure fuel injection, i.e. where the fuel-air mixture containing fuel thus injected will be substantially compressed by the compression stroke of the engine, by means other than controlling only an injection pump -- The F02D 3/00 subclass covers methods for controlling low-pressure fuel injection in combustion engines, where the injected fuel-air mixture is mainly compressed during the engine's compression stroke, using techniques other than simple injection-pump regulation. It addresses timing, quantity and distribution of the injected charge to optimise combustion without relying solely on pump control. This falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and controlling combustion engines.
[F02D 3/02] with continuous injection or continuous flow upstream of the injection nozzle -- This CPC subclass concerns mechanisms that regulate low-pressure fuel delivery to a combustion engine by providing a continuously supplied fuel stream before the injector, rather than merely controlling the pump. It applies to systems where the fuel-air mixture is still compressed during the compression stroke. It falls under controlling combustion engines in mechanical engineering.
[F02D 3/04] Controlling fuel-injection and carburation, e.g. of alternative systems -- F02D 3/04 covers methods and devices that manage low-pressure fuel injection or carburation in combustion engines, where the injected fuel-air mixture is primarily compressed during the engine's compression stroke. It includes control strategies beyond simple injection-pump regulation, such as alternative injection timing, metering, and atomisation techniques for improved engine performance.
[F02D 31/00] Other non-electrical control of combustion engines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mechanical governors, e.g. Watt type
[F02D 31/001] Electric control of rotation speed -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Electrical control of engine rotation speed, e.g. by including a speed feedback loop
[F02D 31/002] controlling air supply -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling the air supply, mainly by changing the throttle position, in order to control the engine speed  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Vehicle cruise control -> B60K31/00
[F02D 31/003] for idle speed control -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Throttle opening control after engine speed in idle mode; also setting of idle speed
[F02D 31/004] by controlling a throttle stop -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control whereby an electric actuator urges the throttle, or the throttle lever from its closed position
[F02D 31/005] by controlling a throttle by-pass -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control whereby in a bypass the air flow must be controlled after engine speed and throttle position
[F02D 31/006] for maximum speed control -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control whereby throttle is used to limit engine speed.
[F02D 31/007] controlling fuel supply -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Rotation speed control by fuel amount, e.g. Diesel governors
[F02D 31/008] for idle speed control -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feedback control of idling speed using fuel supply
[F02D 31/009] for maximum speed control -- F02D 31/009 concerns non-electrical mechanisms that limit or regulate the maximum speed of combustion engines. It includes mechanical governors, hydraulic limiters or pneumatic devices that intervene to prevent the engine from exceeding a set speed, typically for safety or performance reasons.
[F02D 33/00] Controlling delivery of fuel or combustion-air, not otherwise provided for -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling the feeding of liquid fuel from storage containers to carburettors or fuel-injection apparatus or the controlling the delivery of combustion air.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Controlling air supply using exhaust gas sensors -> F02D35/0023 - Controlling fuel supply using exhaust gas sensors -> F02D35/0046  **Glossary:** - Storage containers: Fuel tanks
[F02D 33/003] Controlling the feeding of liquid fuel from storage containers to carburettors or fuel-injection apparatus  control of electrical fuel pumps  F02D41/3082 , controlling fuel flow to a common rail  F02D41/3845 ; Failure or leakage prevention; Diagnosis or detection of failure; Arrangement of sensors in the fuel system; Electric wiring; Electrostatic discharge -- F02D 33/003 covers systems that regulate the transfer of liquid fuel from storage tanks to carburettors or fuel-injection units, including the control of electric fuel pumps and fuel flow to common-rail systems. It also addresses measures for preventing leaks or failures, diagnostic and detection methods, sensor placement in the fuel circuit, electric wiring considerations and protection against electrostatic discharge.
[F02D 33/006] depending on engine operating conditions, e.g. start, stop or ambient conditions -- The classification covers methods for adjusting the supply of fuel or combustion air to an engine according to its operating state, such as start-up, shutdown or surrounding temperature and pressure. It applies when the control strategy is not covered by more specific provisions in the CPC.
[F02D 33/02] of combustion-air -- The CPC subclass F02D 33/02 addresses methods or devices for controlling the supply of combustion-air to an internal-combustion engine, covering arrangements that are not specifically covered by other subclasses. It falls under mechanical engineering, within the broader categories of combustion engines and their control systems.
[F02D 35/00] Controlling engines, dependent on conditions exterior or interior to engines, not otherwise provided for -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling engines using information, e.g. sensor signals, about conditions exterior or interior to engines
[F02D 35/0007] using electrical feedback  F02D35/0015  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Position feedback on an electrical actuator.
[F02D 35/0015] using exhaust gas sensors  F02D41/14  takes precedence
[F02D 35/0023] Controlling air supply -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of air supply as a function of an air/fuel sensor signal
[F02D 35/003] by means of by-pass passages -- The subclass F02D 35/003 covers control of combustion engines that relies on bypass passages to direct gases or fluids, enabling regulation of engine performance based on interior or exterior conditions not otherwise specified in the classification. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → controlling combustion engines.
[F02D 35/0038] by means of air pumps -- The entry F02D 35/0038 falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, controlling combustion engines and covers control of engines by means of air pumps, i.e., using pumps to vary the amount or pressure of air supplied to influence combustion conditions.
[F02D 35/0046] Controlling fuel supply -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fuel supply control after an air/fuel sensor.
[F02D 35/0053] by means of a carburettor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Carburettor control after an air/fuel sensor
[F02D 35/0061] Controlling the emulsifying air only  F02D35/0076 ,  F02D35/0084  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling the emulsifying air only wherby most of the carburettors have a sub carburettor and it is the air/fuel mixture made in this sub carburettor (easier to control) which is drawn into the main venturi of the carburettor.
[F02D 35/0069] Controlling the fuel flow only  F02D35/0076 ,  F02D35/0084  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fuel flow control via a valve in series in the fuel alimentation of the said sub carburettor or via a valve controlling the supply of air above the constant level fuel reservoir. If this valve is closed, less fuel can be sucked into the engine.
[F02D 35/0076] using variable venturi carburettors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fuel flow using variable venturi carburettors, i.e. a special type of horizontal carburettor still used in the 80s.
[F02D 35/0084] using two barrel carburettors -- F02D 35/0084 covers methods of controlling combustion engines by employing a dual-barrel carburetor arrangement, allowing separate fuel metering for different operating conditions such as load or speed. This subclass is part of the broader group for engine control based on internal or external conditions not covered elsewhere.
[F02D 35/0092] by means of fuel injection -- This subclass covers methods of controlling combustion engines by means of fuel injection, where injection timing, quantity, or pressure is varied to achieve desired engine performance. It falls under controlling engines within mechanical engineering, specifically under combustion engines.
[F02D 35/02] on interior conditions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control according to parameters interior to the combustion chamber that may be used to influence this combustion  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Knock control using ignition timing -> F02P5/152 - Ignition timing control based on combustion pressure -> F02P5/153
[F02D 35/021] using an ionic current sensor -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Ionic sensor for testing of ignition -> F02P17/12
[F02D 35/022] using an optical sensor, e.g. in-cylinder light probe -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Optical sensors in the exhaust system -> F02D41/1451
[F02D 35/023] by determining the cylinder pressure -- F02D 35/023 covers methods for controlling combustion engines that use cylinder-pressure measurements as feedback. It pertains to systems that adjust fuel, air, ignition or valve timing based on the detected pressure within each cylinder to optimise performance, emissions or efficiency. This subclass falls under controlling engines dependent on internal or external conditions not otherwise specified.
[F02D 35/024] using an estimation -- This subclass pertains to methods for controlling combustion engines that depend on estimated values of operating or environmental parameters rather than direct measurement. It covers control strategies that infer conditions such as load, speed, or emissions to adjust fuel injection, ignition timing, or air-fuel ratio.
[F02D 35/025] by determining temperatures inside the cylinder, e.g. combustion temperatures -- A method for controlling a combustion engine that monitors the temperature inside the cylinder, such as the combustion temperature, and uses that information to adjust engine operation. This approach relies on internal temperature measurements rather than external conditions to optimise performance and emissions.
[F02D 35/026] using an estimation -- This subgroup covers control methods for combustion engines that rely on estimated engine or operating parameters rather than direct measurement. It includes algorithms and models that infer variables such as load, speed, temperature or emissions to adjust fuel, air, ignition timing or other control actions.
[F02D 35/027] using knock sensors -- This subclass addresses the control of combustion engines by employing knock sensors that detect abnormal combustion events and trigger adjustments to ignition timing, fuel injection or other operating parameters. It covers systems and methods that use sensor output to improve engine performance, efficiency and emissions.
[F02D 35/028] by determining the combustion timing or phasing -- The subclass F02D 35/028 deals with control of combustion engines by measuring or setting the timing or phase of the combustion process. It includes methods that adjust engine operation based on when fuel ignites relative to the crankshaft position, aiming to improve performance, efficiency or emissions.
[F02D 37/00] Non-electrical conjoint control of two or more functions of engines, not otherwise provided for -- F02D 37/00 covers methods for controlling two or more engine functions together without using electrical systems, where the combined control is not specified in other subclasses. It applies to mechanical or hydraulic schemes that manage parameters such as fuel, air, or valve timing simultaneously in combustion engines.
[F02D 37/02] one of the functions being ignition -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Conjoint control using ignition timing and at least another parameter, e.g. air amount or fuel injection.
[F02D 39/00] Other non-electrical control -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-electrical control of four- or two-stroke engines; of engines adding the fuel substantially at the end or before the compression stroke; of free piston engines.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Controlling engines, such controlling being peculiar to the devices driven thereby -> F02D29/00 - Two stroke engines -> F02B25/00 - Rotary engines -> F02B53/00 - Free piston engines -> F02B71/00  **Glossary:** - Free piston engines: Engines without rotary main shaft
[F02D 39/02] for four-stroke engines -- The CPC subclass F02D 39/02 pertains to non-electrical control mechanisms used in four-stroke internal combustion engines, covering mechanical, hydraulic or pneumatic systems that regulate engine functions such as valve timing, fuel delivery or exhaust management. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and controlling combustion engines.
[F02D 39/04] for engines with other cycles than four-stroke, e.g. two-stroke -- This subclass relates to mechanical or hydraulic control systems used to regulate the operation of internal-combustion engines that run on cycles other than the conventional four-stroke, such as two-stroke engines. It includes devices that adjust fuel, air or ignition timing without employing electrical actuation.
[F02D 39/06] for engines adding the fuel substantially at the end of compression stroke -- F02D 39/06 defines non-electrical methods for controlling combustion engines by introducing fuel very late in the compression phase, typically at or just before top-dead-centre. It covers mechanical or hydraulic injection systems that add the fuel substantially at the end of the compression stroke to improve combustion timing and efficiency.
[F02D 39/08] for engines adding the fuel substantially before compression stroke -- F02D 39/08 covers non-electrical control methods for combustion engines in which the fuel is introduced substantially before the compression stroke, such as early-timed injection or pre-combustion-chamber fueling systems used to influence combustion characteristics and improve performance. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → controlling combustion engines → other non-electrical control.
[F02D 39/10] for free-piston engines -- The CPC subclass F02D 39/10 covers non-electrical control measures applied to free-piston engines, such as hydraulic, pneumatic or purely mechanical mechanisms that regulate piston motion, start-stop sequencing and energy conversion. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Controlling Combustion Engines → Other non-electrical control.
[F02D 41/00] Electrical control of combustion engines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Electrical or electronical control of combustion engines  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Conjoint electrical control in which the control of air amount follows the control of fuel amount -> F02D43/00 - Circuits or control means specially adapted for starting of engines -> F02N11/08 - Electrical control of engine ignition timing -> F02P5/145  **Glossary:** - EGR: Exhaust gas recirculation
[F02D 41/0002] Controlling intake air -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of intake air amount, e.g. by using:  Throttle  Swirl throttle (Dralldrossel) on the side of intake pipe or in a second special intake pipe. It makes air flow to be twisted at low speeds to promote homogenization between air and fuel mist.  Variable valve timing and/or lift
[F02D 41/0005] during deceleration -- The classification covers electrical control strategies applied to combustion engines while the vehicle is decelerating, such as adjustments to spark timing, fuel injection, or valve operation to maintain stability, reduce emissions and improve fuel efficiency during speed reduction. It relates to systems that modulate engine parameters in response to throttle closing or braking conditions.
[F02D 41/0007] for control of turbo-charged or super-charged engines  control of the pumps  per se   F02B37/12 -- This CPC subclass covers electrical control systems for turbo-charged or super-charged internal combustion engines, specifically the regulation of the pumps that supply air or exhaust gases, including methods and devices that adjust pump operation to optimise performance and emissions.
[F02D 41/0025] Controlling engines characterised by use of non-liquid fuels, pluralities of fuels, or non-fuel substances added to the combustible mixtures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling engines running with:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Gas engines -> F02D41/0027 - Control using fuel type or fuel quality sensor -> F02D2200/0611 - Control using fuel type or fuel quality estimation -> F02D2200/0611
[F02D 41/0027] the fuel being gaseous  non-electrical control  F02D19/02
[F02D 41/003] Adding fuel vapours, e.g. drawn from engine fuel reservoir -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of the supply of fuel vapours which are normally drawn from a canister which adsorbs fuel vapours generated in the fuel tank
[F02D 41/0032] Controlling the purging of the canister as a function of the engine operating conditions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engine conditions are taken into account and purging is controlled
[F02D 41/0035] to achieve a special effect, e.g. to warm up the catalyst -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Purging is used for a particular effect on the engine, e.g. to warm-up catalyst
[F02D 41/0037] for diagnosing the engine  diagnosis of purge control systems  F02M25/0809 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of purging to achieve conditions which are suitable for engine Diagnosis. - If the expected effect due to purging does not occur, failure is determined- Switch off of purging to prevent influence on diagnosis  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Diagnosis of purge control systems -> F02M25/0809
[F02D 41/004] Control of the valve or purge actuator, e.g. duty cycle, closed loop control of position -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details specific to the control of the purge valve itself
[F02D 41/0042] Controlling the combustible mixture as a function of the canister purging, e.g. control of injected fuel to compensate for deviation of air fuel ratio when purging -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of the engine mixture is adapted to purging taking place
[F02D 41/0045] Estimating, calculating or determining the purging rate, amount, flow or concentration -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details specific to the estimation, calculation or any kind of determination of the purge quantity
[F02D 41/0047] Controlling exhaust gas recirculation [EGR]  temperature control with cooler in recirculation circuit  F02M26/33 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Electrical control of exhaust gas recirculation (EGR)
[F02D 41/005] according to engine operating conditions -- The CPC subgroup F02D 41/005 covers electrical control methods for combustion engines that adapt the engine's operation according to real-time conditions such as load, speed, temperature or emissions. It pertains to systems that modify fuel injection, ignition timing or air-fuel ratios to optimise performance and efficiency under varying operating states. This entry is nested under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Controlling Combustion Engines.
[F02D 41/0052] Feedback control of engine parameters, e.g. for control of air/fuel ratio or intake air amount -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feedback control using EGR for all parameter not directly related to EGR. This might include:  intake amount amount  intake manifold pressure  air/fuel ratio  combustion timing  engine roughness  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Internal EGR rate or amount feedback -> F02D41/0062 - External EGR rate or amount feedback -> F02D41/0072
[F02D 41/0055] Special engine operating conditions, e.g. for regeneration of exhaust gas treatment apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Special control of EGR for non-ordinary operating conditions, e.g.:
[F02D 41/0057] Specific combustion modes  combustion modes  per se   F02D41/3017 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  EGR control according to or during engine operation with specific combustion modes, e.g. special engines with two EGR modes on both sides of the peak of smoke which appears when the EGR rate is increased in a diesel engine or HCCI without anything specific to EGR control
[F02D 41/006] using internal EGR  control of valve overlap for internal EGR  F02D13/0261 ; arrangements for internal EGR  F02M26/01 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Exhaust gases are redirected into the combustion chamber or kept as residual gases during positive or negative valve overlap
[F02D 41/0062] Estimating, calculating or determining the internal EGR rate, amount or flow -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Determination of the EGR rate or residual gas amount remaining in the cylinder when internal EGR is used
[F02D 41/0065] Specific aspects of external EGR control  constructional details of EGR system  F02M26/00 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  something specific to external EGR, e.g. considering transport delays or when using low pressure and high pressure EGR circuits
[F02D 41/0072] Estimating, calculating or determining the EGR rate, amount or flow  sensors in EGR systems  F02M26/45 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Determination of the EGR rate in external EGR circuits
[F02D 41/0077] Control of the EGR valve or actuator, e.g. duty cycle, closed loop control of position  EGR valve position sensor  F02M26/48 -- This classification covers electronic methods for regulating the exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) valve or its actuator in internal combustion engines, including duty-cycle modulation and closed-loop control of valve position using feedback from an EGR position sensor. It relates to systems that adjust valve operation to manage exhaust flow for emissions control and engine performance.
[F02D 41/008] Controlling each cylinder individually -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Operation of individual engine cylinders with different operating parameters, e.g. different fuel amounts or air amounts
[F02D 41/0082] per groups or banks  F02D41/0087  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Operation of cylinder groups with different operating parameters, e.g. first cylinder group operating lean and second cylinder group operating rich to achieve heating or regeneration of catalysts
[F02D 41/0085] Balancing of cylinder outputs, e.g. speed, torque or air-fuel ratio -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control to equalise engine operating parameter for all cylinders
[F02D 41/0087] Selective cylinder activation, i.e. partial cylinder operation  deceleration cut-off  F02D41/123 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Selective activation or deactivation of cylinders: every cylinder is fed with an optimal fuel quantity for an optimal combustion or with no fuel at all. The engine power also being controlled by the number of fed cylinders and not by the throttle and the air/fuel per cylinder.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Complete fuel-cut in all cylinders during deceleration -> F02D41/123
[F02D 41/009] using means for generating position or synchronisation signals -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Detection of engine crank angle position and generation of synchronisation signals, i.e. information about which cylinder is in which cycle (intake, compression, expansion or exhaust cycle).
[F02D 41/0097] using means for generating speed signals -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Detection and measurement of engine speed.
[F02D 41/02] Circuit arrangements for generating control signals -- F02D 41/02 pertains to circuit arrangements that generate control signals for the electrical management of combustion engines, such as timing, fuel-injection or ignition control. It falls under the CPC hierarchy for mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and their electrical control.
[F02D 41/0205] using an auxiliary engine speed control  engine speed control  per se   F02D31/00 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Generator, pump or PTO (e.g. agricultural Power Take Off) engines with an externally needed regulated speed control.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Vehicle cruise speed control -> B60K31/00
[F02D 41/021] Introducing corrections for particular conditions exterior to the engine  conjoint control of vehicle sub-units for propelling the vehicle  B60W30/18 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control according to particular conditions exterior to the engine, e.g.
[F02D 41/0215] in relation with elements of the transmission -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of the engine in relation with gearbox and/or clutch
[F02D 41/022] in relation with the clutch status -- F02D 41/022 covers electrical control strategies for combustion engines that are linked to the clutch status, including monitoring clutch engagement, disengagement and slip, and using that information to adjust fuel injection, ignition timing, or torque output for smoother operation and improved performance. It pertains to systems that modify engine control parameters based on whether the clutch is engaged or released.
[F02D 41/0225] in relation with the gear ratio or shift lever position -- Covers electrical control strategies for combustion engines that modify fuel injection, ignition timing or other engine functions in response to the current gear-ratio or shift lever position, enabling optimized performance and efficiency during gear changes.
[F02D 41/023] in relation with the gear ratio shifting  conjoint control for improving gear change  B60W30/19 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Special control during the gear shift. Usually torque or speed control to target values
[F02D 41/0235] in relation with the state of the exhaust gas treating apparatus  control of exhaust gas treating apparatus  per se   F01N -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of engine in order to achieve specific effect on exhaust gas treatment device, e.g. regeneration or warming-up
[F02D 41/024] to increase temperature of the exhaust gas treating apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engine control to increase the temperature of an exhaust gas catalyst or filteralso given for regeneration, when method for temperature increase is important
[F02D 41/0245] by increasing temperature of the exhaust gas leaving the engine -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for raising catalyst temperature wherein additional heat is generated by combustion inside cylinder, e.g. by early post injection and delaying (main) injection timing.
[F02D 41/025] by changing the composition of the exhaust gas, e.g. for exothermic reaction on exhaust gas treating apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for raising catalyst temperature wherein additional heat is generated in exhaust pipe or on catalyst, e.g. by late post injection with surplus of oxygen from same/other cylinder, or by driving cylinders with different air/fuel ratios so that exhaust gases from these cylinders mix in exhaust pipe.
[F02D 41/0255] to accelerate the warming-up of the exhaust gas treating apparatus at engine start -- This class concerns electrical methods for quickly heating the exhaust-gas after-treatment system when a combustion engine is started, such as applying electric power or resistive heaters to raise temperature rapidly. The aim is to reduce cold-start emissions by accelerating the warm-up of catalytic converters, filters or similar components.
[F02D 41/027] to purge or regenerate the exhaust gas treating apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engine control without special control according to the type of catalyst
[F02D 41/0275] the exhaust gas treating apparatus being a NOx trap or adsorbent
[F02D 41/028] Desulfurisation of NOx traps or adsorbent -- The CPC subclass F02D 41/028 covers techniques for reducing the sulfur content of nitric-oxide (NOx) traps or adsorbent materials used in exhaust-after-treatment of combustion engines, typically to restore or maintain their effectiveness. It includes processes, additives or regeneration methods that desulphurise the trap while it is electrically controlled in the engine system.
[F02D 41/0285] the exhaust gas treating apparatus being a SOx trap or adsorbent -- A system for treating the exhaust of a combustion engine that incorporates a sulfur-oxide trap or adsorbent, designed to capture and retain SOx compounds from the engine's emission stream. This apparatus is part of the electrical control strategy for managing combustion engine outputs and reducing harmful pollutants.
[F02D 41/029] the exhaust gas treating apparatus being a particulate filter -- This subclass defines exhaust-gas treating devices that incorporate a particulate filter, used to capture solid particles from engine emissions. It covers filter designs, materials and configurations intended for combustion engines whose operation is electrically controlled, focusing on the integration of the filter with the engine's electronic management system.
[F02D 41/0295] Control according to the amount of oxygen that is stored on the exhaust gas treating apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of engine air/fuel ratio so that amount of oxygen that is stored on (three-way)-catalyst with oxygen storage capacity meets target value.
[F02D 41/04] Introducing corrections for particular operating conditions -- The CPC code F02D 41/04 covers electrical control strategies for combustion engines that apply specific corrections according to operating conditions such as load, speed, temperature or fuel quality. It includes devices or algorithms that adjust fuel injection, ignition timing or valve operation to optimise performance, emissions or efficiency under those particular circumstances.
[F02D 41/042] for stopping the engine -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control for stopping the engine or during the engine stopping period
[F02D 41/045] Detection of accelerating or decelerating state  detection thereof in general  G01P -- F02D 41/045 covers electrical methods for detecting whether a combustion engine is accelerating or decelerating, providing general-purpose signals used in the electronic control of engine operation. This subclass is part of the hierarchy for mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and controlling combustion engines, linking engine dynamics to sensor-based feedback.
[F02D 41/047] Taking into account fuel evaporation or wall wetting;  special correction after fuel cut-off  F02D41/126 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control combining partial pressures of vapour fuel, fresh air, recycled exhaust gas, less deposited fuel to calculate real air/fuel ratio taken in each cylinder  Control using models to determine fuel film on intake and cylinder walls
[F02D 41/06] for engine starting or warming up -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Corrections during engine start or warm-up.
[F02D 41/061] the corrections being time dependent -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Corrections during engine start linked to a delay or timer.
[F02D 41/062] for starting  F02D41/061  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Corrections during engine start.
[F02D 41/064] at cold start  F02D41/067  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engine control during engine start when engine is cold, e.g. increase of fuel injection amount to compensate increased fuel demand.
[F02D 41/065] at hot start or restart  F02D41/067  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engine start when engine is warm or immediate restart, e.g. after stall or during engine start/stop control
[F02D 41/067] with control of the choke  non electronic control of choke  see   F02M1/10 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Purely electrical chokes as well as mechanical automatic chokes where the bimetallic spiral or wax actuator is electrically heated when the engine has started.
[F02D 41/068] for warming-up -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Warming-up measures, e.g. fast idle to accelerate warming-up
[F02D 41/08] for idling -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engine control during idling
[F02D 41/083] taking into account engine load variation, e.g. air-conditionning -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engine control during idling considering external loads, e.g.
[F02D 41/086] taking into account the temperature of the engine -- This CPC subclass covers electrical control strategies for combustion engines that incorporate engine temperature data to adjust parameters such as fuel injection, ignition timing or air-fuel ratio, thereby optimizing performance, efficiency and emissions. It is located under Mechanical Engineering - Combustion Engines - Controlling Combustion Engines - Electrical control of combustion engines.
[F02D 41/10] for acceleration -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engine control during acceleration, e.g. increase of fuel injection amount to meet increased demand.
[F02D 41/102] Switching from sequential injection to simultaneous injection -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Increase of the number of injectors in intake low pressure injection to increase fuel flow
[F02D 41/105] using asynchronous injection -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control with extra fuel pulse at start of acceleration in addition to ordinary fuel pulses which are sequentially injected at regular intervals
[F02D 41/107] and deceleration -- The F02D 41/107 subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Controlling Combustion Engines and covers electrical control measures specifically aimed at decelerating combustion engines, such as retardation of fuel injection, spark timing, or motor-generator braking to reduce engine speed.
[F02D 41/12] for deceleration -- F02D 41/12 covers electrical control techniques that provide deceleration of combustion engines, such as electronic engine-braking, retardation of fuel injection or ignition timing, and other circuit-based methods used to reduce speed or torque. It includes the devices, controllers and software that implement these slowing-down functions.
[F02D 41/123] the fuel injection being cut-off -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The fuel being cut off in all cylinders during deceleration as long as there is no serious reason to restart.
[F02D 41/126] transitional corrections at the end of the cut-off period -- **Definition:** This place covers:  When the engine is decelerating it is necessary to restart the engine before idle speed is reached to avoid stalling if injection is restarted too late. Transitional measures may also be taken before. Special control to bring back catalyst to normal working range
[F02D 41/14] Introducing closed-loop corrections -- F02D 41/14 covers methods and devices that apply closed-loop corrections to the electrical control of combustion engines, using sensor-based feedback to adjust parameters such as fuel injection, ignition timing or air-fuel ratio in order to maintain optimal performance and emissions.
[F02D 41/1401] characterised by the control or regulation method  F02D41/1473 ,  F02D41/1477  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Documents giving details on the feedback loop should be put in this group and groups when it is not a PID or some basic controller, then prefer to classify in F02D41/1473 and F02D41/1477
[F02D 41/1402] Adaptive control -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The parameters/coefficients of the controller are set/tuned on-line according to the output of the system (there must be a feedback). Also called Self-Tuning regulator.
[F02D 41/1403] Sliding mode control -- F02D 41/1403 refers to the use of sliding mode control, a robust nonlinear control method, for the electrical regulation of combustion engine performance. It addresses how the controller maintains desired speed or torque despite disturbances, fitting within the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and their electrical control.
[F02D 41/1404] Fuzzy logic control -- Fuzzy logic control refers to the use of fuzzy set theory and reasoning to manage the electrical systems that regulate combustion-engine operation, enabling smoother, more adaptive responses to variable conditions such as load, speed and emissions. It replaces conventional binary control logic with graded, linguistic rules that better mimic human decision-making in engine management.
[F02D 41/1405] Neural network control -- F02D 41/1405 covers the use of neural networks to control combustion engines, focusing on electrical control strategies that regulate fuel injection, ignition timing, and emissions. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, controlling combustion engines, and electrical control of combustion engines.
[F02D 41/1406] with use of a optimisation method, e.g. iteration -- This subclass pertains to electrical control of combustion engines that employ optimisation techniques, such as iterative algorithms, to refine performance, fuel efficiency or emissions. It includes methods that repeatedly adjust control parameters based on successive calculations to achieve the desired engine operation.
[F02D 41/1408] Dithering techniques -- F02D 41/1408 covers dithering techniques applied in the electrical control of combustion engines, where small, rapid variations in control signals are introduced to smooth engine response, reduce torque ripple, and improve combustion stability.
[F02D 41/1438] using means for determining characteristics of the combustion gases; Sensors therefor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control according to a characteristic of the combustion gases.
[F02D 41/1439] characterised by the position of the sensor -- The subclass F02D 41/1439 covers combustion-engine control systems in which the electrical control is distinguished by the location of the sensor used to monitor engine parameters. It applies to designs where the sensor's placement, such as in the intake, exhaust, or cylinder, is a defining feature of the control strategy.
[F02D 41/144] Sensor in intake manifold -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Use of a gas concentration sensor in the intake manifold, e.g. to measure the O2/fuel ratio before the mixture enters the combustion chambers on order to avoid the two-revolutions delay.
[F02D 41/1441] Plural sensors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Multiple sensor arrangements in the exhaust system, e.g. one sensor before the catalyst converter and one after to check its functioning  Downstream sensor might be used for tuning the air/fuel ratio loop based on the upstream sensor
[F02D 41/1443] with one sensor per cylinder or group of cylinders -- **Definition:** This place covers:  A sensor is provided for each cylinder group or bank, e.g.in the case of V- or flat engines for example
[F02D 41/1444] characterised by the characteristics of the combustion gases -- This subgroup deals with control strategies for internal combustion engines that adjust operation according to measured properties of the exhaust-gas, such as temperature, pressure, density or composition. It covers sensors and electronic control units that use these gas characteristics to optimise fuel injection, ignition timing or emission reduction.
[F02D 41/1445] the characteristics being related to the exhaust flow -- F02D 41/1445 concerns the electrical control of combustion engines with respect to exhaust-flow characteristics, including sensing, monitoring and adjusting parameters such as pressure, temperature, composition or flow rate to improve performance, emissions or fuel efficiency.
[F02D 41/1446] the characteristics being exhaust temperatures -- The subclass addresses electrical control of combustion engines where exhaust-gas temperature characteristics are considered, including the sensing, monitoring and use of exhaust temperature data to influence engine-management strategies such as fuel injection, ignition timing and emission control.
[F02D 41/1447] with determination means using an estimation -- The subclass F02D 41/1447 covers electrical control systems for combustion engines that incorporate determination means based on estimation rather than direct measurement, used to infer parameters such as load, speed or fuel-air ratio for engine management.
[F02D 41/1448] the characteristics being an exhaust gas pressure -- The F02D 41/1448 subclass covers electrical control systems that monitor and regulate the pressure of exhaust gases in combustion engines. It deals with the use of sensor data and control algorithms to optimise engine performance, emissions and fuel efficiency by adjusting parameters such as valve timing or boost pressure based on the measured exhaust gas pressure.
[F02D 41/145] with determination means using an estimation -- The group F02D 41/145 concerns electrical control systems for combustion engines that determine operating parameters by estimating values such as load, speed or fuel-air ratio instead of measuring them directly, and then using those estimates to adjust ignition timing, fuel injection or other engine functions.
[F02D 41/1451] the sensor being an optical sensor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Use of an optical sensor in the exhaust system, e.g. for smoke measurements by evaluating the color/transparency of the exhaust gases.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Optical sensors in combustion chamber -> F02D35/022
[F02D 41/1452] the characteristics being a COx content or concentration -- F02D 41/1452 concerns the electrical control of combustion engines where the control parameter is the carbon-oxide (COx) content or concentration in the exhaust or combustion gases. It includes methods and devices that monitor or regulate engine operation based on COx levels to optimize performance or emissions.
[F02D 41/1453] the characteristics being a CO content or concentration
[F02D 41/1454] the characteristics being an oxygen content or concentration or the air-fuel ratio -- F02D 41/1454 covers electrical control systems for combustion engines that monitor or adjust the oxygen content, oxygen concentration, or the air-fuel ratio of the intake mixture. It pertains to methods and devices that use sensor feedback to optimise combustion efficiency, emissions or performance in mechanically driven engines.
[F02D 41/1455] with sensor resistivity varying with oxygen concentration  F02D41/1456  takes precedence
[F02D 41/1456] with sensor output signal being linear or quasi-linear with the concentration of oxygen -- F02D 41/1456 covers electrical control systems for combustion engines that use oxygen sensors whose output signal is linear or quasi-linear with the oxygen concentration. These sensors are employed to regulate the fuel-air mixture for optimal combustion.
[F02D 41/1458] with determination means using an estimation -- This subclass covers electrical control systems for combustion engines that determine operating conditions by estimating variables rather than measuring them directly. It includes sensors, algorithms or model-based approaches that infer parameters such as load, speed or fuel demand to adjust ignition, injection or valve timing. The focus is on indirect determination methods to improve engine performance and emissions.
[F02D 41/1459] the characteristics being a hydrocarbon content or concentration -- F02D 41/1459 pertains to electrical control of combustion engines that uses the hydrocarbon content or concentration as a measured characteristic, such as sensing fuel-air mixture or exhaust hydrocarbons to adjust ignition timing, fuel injection or air-fuel ratio for optimal performance and emissions.
[F02D 41/146] the characteristics being an NOx content or concentration -- F02D 41/146 pertains to the electronic management of internal-combustion engines in which the system monitors or controls the nitrogen-oxides (NOx) content or concentration of the exhaust. It includes sensors, algorithms, and control strategies that use NOx data to meet emission limits, optimise combustion efficiency, or adjust fueling and ignition timing.
[F02D 41/1461] of the exhaust gases emitted by the engine -- This subcategory deals with electronic systems that regulate or process the exhaust gases produced by a combustion engine, including sensors, actuators and control algorithms aimed at reducing emissions and meeting standards.
[F02D 41/1462] with determination means using an estimation -- F02D 41/1462 relates to electrical control systems for combustion engines that include means for determining engine parameters by estimation rather than direct measurement, using algorithms, models or sensor-fusion to infer variables such as load, speed or fuel consumption for performance control.
[F02D 41/1463] of the exhaust gases downstream of exhaust gas treatment apparatus -- It concerns monitoring, regulation or conditioning of exhaust-gas parameters such as temperature, composition or flow after the gas has passed through treatment components like catalytic converters or particulate filters, using electrical control systems to improve engine performance, emissions compliance or safety.
[F02D 41/1465] with determination means using an estimation
[F02D 41/1466] the characteristics being a soot concentration or content -- F02D 41/1466 covers electrical control functions that use the soot concentration or soot content of the combustion gases as a measurable characteristic. It includes sensors, signal processing and control strategies that adjust engine operation based on detected soot levels to improve combustion efficiency and emissions compliance.
[F02D 41/1467] with determination means using an estimation -- The subclass F02D 41/1467 pertains to electrical control of combustion engines that employ determination means based on estimation rather than direct measurement, using estimated parameters such as load, speed or torque to regulate fuel injection or ignition timing. It addresses control strategies that infer engine states through calculation or modeling to achieve optimal performance and emissions compliance.
[F02D 41/1473] characterised by the regulation method -- F02D 41/1473 covers combustion engine control arrangements that are distinguished primarily by the method used to regulate engine operation, such as electronic feedback, pulse-width modulation, or adaptive algorithms that adjust fuel injection, ignition timing or valve actuation.
[F02D 41/1474] by detecting the commutation time of the sensor -- This CPC subgroup concerns electrical control of combustion engines achieved by detecting the commutation time of a sensor, enabling precise timing of ignition, fuel injection or other engine functions.
[F02D 41/1475] Regulating the air fuel ratio at a value other than stoichiometry -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engine control to achieve an air-fuel ratio deviating from the stoichiometric air/fuel ratio, e.g. by:
[F02D 41/1476] Biasing of the sensor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Two-states sensors wherein a flow of O2 ions across the porous sensor is simulated by simply sending electrons
[F02D 41/1477] characterised by the regulation circuit or part of it,(e.g. comparator, PI regulator, output) -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Air/fuel ratio control circuits and its components
[F02D 41/1479] Using a comparator with variable reference -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Air/fuel ratio control with two-state air/fuel ratio sensor wherein the air/fuel ratio sensor signal is compared with a variable reference to have a lambda regulation value different from 1  Adaptation of the comparator value to variation of sensor characteristics, e.g. with temperature or age
[F02D 41/148] Using a plurality of comparators -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Air/fuel ratio control using a plurality of comparators, e.g. by selecting among them according to circumstances, to have a lambda regulation value different from 1.
[F02D 41/1481] Using a delaying circuit -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control related to the problem caused by the delay between the fuel injection and the sensing of exhaust gas properties due to travel in intake, compression, expansion, exhaust and travel in exhaust. To reduce the generated oscillation in steady some delays are introduced in the regulation loop.
[F02D 41/1482] Integrator, i.e. variable slope -- F02D 41/1482 designates an integrator with a variable slope used in the electrical control of combustion engines. The device processes signal inputs to produce a proportional output that changes according to a programmable slope, enabling precise regulation of fuel injection or ignition timing. It falls under the CPC group for controllers that shape engine performance.
[F02D 41/1483] Proportional component -- Pertains to components that provide a continuously variable output proportional to an input signal for regulating combustion-engine functions, such as proportional valves, actuators or electronic circuits used in engine control systems.
[F02D 41/1484] Output circuit -- The CPC entry F02D 41/1484 defines the output circuit used in electrical control systems for combustion engines, covering the wiring and components that transmit control signals or power from the engine's electronic control unit to actuators, sensors or other load devices.
[F02D 41/1486] with correction for particular operating conditions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Air/fuel ratio control during particular operating conditions, e.g. during transient conditions.
[F02D 41/1487] Correcting the instantaneous control value -- The entry covers methods and devices that correct the instantaneous control value in the electrical management of combustion engines, ensuring real-time adjustment of fuel, ignition or other parameters to maintain optimal engine performance.
[F02D 41/1488] Inhibiting the regulation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Deactivation of air/fuel control based on exhaust sensor measurements, e.g. during transitions
[F02D 41/1489] Replacing of the control value by a constant -- This CPC subclass addresses methods in which a variable control parameter of an electrically controlled combustion engine is replaced by a fixed constant, often to simplify the control strategy or increase reliability. It includes devices or procedures that set a constant reference value for parameters such as fuel quantity, spark timing or boost pressure.
[F02D 41/149] Replacing of the control value by an other parameter -- This subclass covers techniques where the primary control variable used to regulate engine operation is substituted with an alternative parameter, such as using pressure, temperature, or speed instead of a fuel-injection setpoint. It includes systems that dynamically replace the original control value with another measurable quantity to achieve the desired combustion performance.
[F02D 41/1491] Replacing of the control value by a mean value -- F02D 41/1491 - Replacing of the control value by a mean value covers electrical control of combustion engines where a measured control parameter is substituted with an averaged reference value, smoothing transient responses or compensating for sensor noise.
[F02D 41/1493] Details -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Constructional details of the air/fuel control circuit or its componentsAir/fuel ratio sensor input circuits
[F02D 41/1494] Control of sensor heater -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of heating of an exhaust gas sensor to its operating temperature
[F02D 41/1495] Detection of abnormalities in the air/fuel ratio feedback system -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Detection of abnormalities in the air/fuel ratio feedback system, especially air/fuel ratio sensor failure detection, e.g. by checking its temperature, its resistivity, its speed of reaction or its output voltage range.
[F02D 41/1496] Measurement of the conductivity of a sensor  F02D41/1455  takes precedence -- This subclass covers techniques for determining the electrical conductivity of sensors employed in the electronic control of combustion engines, such as oxygen or temperature probes. It includes methods, apparatus and circuitry that measure sensor resistance or conductance to monitor and optimise engine performance and emissions.
[F02D 41/1497] With detection of the mechanical response of the engine -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control using demanded and measured torque, power etc...
[F02D 41/1498] measuring engine roughness -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Detection of roughness caused by misfires due to insufficient fuel in the cylinder or due to a too late ignition not able to reach the fuel cloud. Misfire is a lack of combustion. Detection of roughness caused by different contributions of individual cylinders
[F02D 41/16] for idling -- The subclass F02D 41/16 covers electrical control systems that regulate a combustion engine while it is idling, maintaining a low and stable speed or temporary shut-off while keeping the engine ready to run. It includes methods and devices that adjust fuel, air, spark or other parameters to achieve and sustain the idle condition.
[F02D 41/18] by measuring intake air flow -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Direct air mass measurement  Three types of sensors:  Computed air flow after manifold depression or throttle opening may also be here with the keyword "parameter measurement - air model"
[F02D 41/182] for the control of a fuel injection device -- F02D 41/182 relates to electric control systems for fuel-injection devices in combustion engines. It addresses how electrical signals are used to command the timing, quantity and pressure of fuel delivered to the engine, enabling precise regulation of combustion for improved performance and emissions. This subclass is part of the hierarchy mechanical engineering → combustion engines → controlling combustion engines → electrical control of combustion engines.
[F02D 41/185] using a vortex flow sensor -- This subclass covers methods and devices that employ a vortex flow sensor to monitor or regulate airflow in combustion engines as part of an electrical control system. The sensor detects vortex shedding to provide real-time flow data used for fuel-air mixture or exhaust management, improving engine performance and emissions.
[F02D 41/187] using a hot wire flow sensor -- The entry relates to employing hot-wire flow sensors to monitor air flow in combustion engines, providing electronic feedback that the engine's control system uses to adjust fuel-air mixture, improve performance, and reduce emissions.
[F02D 41/20] Output circuits, e.g. for controlling currents in command coils -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Circuits for driving injection valves but also gas exchange valves in engines
[F02D 41/2096] for controlling piezoelectric injectors  drive and control circuit for piezoelectric devices in general  H10N30/802 -- The subclass F02D 41/2096 addresses electrical drive and control circuits used to operate piezoelectric fuel injectors in combustion engines, encompassing general-purpose circuitry for managing piezoelectric devices. It is situated under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → controlling combustion engines → electrical control of combustion engines.
[F02D 41/22] Safety or indicating devices for abnormal conditions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices and methods for diagnosing the engine or specific components, e.g. actuators or sensors  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Electric control linkage, e.g. throttles -> F02D11/107 - Air/fuel ratio feedback systems including air/fuel ratio sensor diagnosis -> F02D41/1495 - Fuel vapour purge systems -> F02M25/0809 - EGR systems -> F02M26/49 - Exhaust gas treatment systems, e.g. catalytic converters -> F02N11/00
[F02D 41/221] relating to the failure of actuators or electrically driven elements -- The subclass deals with electrical control of combustion engines and specifically addresses the failure of actuators or electrically driven elements, covering detection, protection and remedial measures for malfunctioning solenoids, motors, valves or other powered components used to regulate engine operation.
[F02D 41/222] relating to the failure of sensors or parameter detection devices -- The F02D 41/222 subclass concerns electrical control systems for combustion engines that address failures of sensors or other parameter-detection devices, including diagnostic methods, fault-tolerant architectures, redundancy schemes and techniques for identifying missing, erroneous or out-of-range signals.
[F02D 41/24] characterised by the use of digital means -- This CPC subclass covers combustion-engine control systems that use digital technology such as microprocessors, digital signal processors or software to manage functions like fuel injection, ignition timing and emissions control, replacing or augmenting analog circuitry and allowing programmable, adaptive or networked operation.
[F02D 41/2403] using essentially up/down counters -- F02D 41/2403 belongs to the mechanical-engineering class for combustion engines, specifically the electrical control of combustion engines. It pertains to the use of essentially up/down counters in engine control systems, such as counting pulses to monitor speed, position or fuel-injection timing for precise combustion regulation.
[F02D 41/2406] using essentially read only memories -- This subclass addresses the use of essentially read-only memory, such as ROM or PROM, for the electrical control of combustion engines, including storage of calibration data, control maps, or diagnostic information that is not intended to be altered during normal operation.
[F02D 41/2409] Addressing techniques specially adapted therefor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Choice of addressing parameters for parameter retrieval using maps, e.g. rpm, throttle opening and intake pressure, for example
[F02D 41/2412] One-parameter addressing technique -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Maps with only one input parameter
[F02D 41/2416] Interpolation techniques -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Use of interpolation for operating points which are grid points of the maps
[F02D 41/2419] Non-linear variation along at least one coordinate -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Choice of a logarithmic or an inverted value for a parameter to save memory space
[F02D 41/2422] Selective use of one or more tables -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Choice of different tables according to certain criteria, e.g. fuel type, to combustion modes
[F02D 41/2425] Particular ways of programming the data -- F02D 41/2425 covers electrical control of combustion engines with a focus on particular ways of programming the data used by engine management systems. It includes software algorithms, data structures and programming techniques that regulate fuel injection, ignition timing, emission control and other engine functions.
[F02D 41/2429] Methods of calibrating or learning -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Learning of engine, sensor or actuator characteristics, e.g. by learning an additive or multiplicative correction value to displace a complete table or a plurality of points of a complete table depending on the results of the learning.  Learning to consider dispersions between actuators or sensors.  Learning of control values to considers changes of engine, sensor or actuator characteristics due to aging
[F02D 41/2432] Methods of calibration -- F02D 41/2432 covers techniques for calibrating the electronic control systems that manage combustion-engine operation, including adjustment of sensor outputs, fuel-injection timing and ignition maps to achieve desired performance and emissions.
[F02D 41/2435] characterised by the writing medium, e.g. bar code -- The subclass F02D 41/2435 relates to electrical control of combustion engines in which the operating data, identification or configuration information is provided by a writing medium such as a bar-code, QR-code, RFID tag or similar imprint on a component, enabling the engine management system to read and act on that encoded information.
[F02D 41/2438] Active learning methods
[F02D 41/2441] characterised by the learning conditions -- This subclass pertains to electrical control systems for combustion engines that are defined by learning conditions, meaning the controller adapts its behaviour based on operating data, environmental inputs or feedback using adaptive, self-tuning or machine-learning algorithms to optimise engine performance.
[F02D 41/2445] characterised by a plurality of learning conditions or ranges -- F02D 41/2445 covers electrical control strategies for combustion engines that employ multiple learning conditions or ranges, allowing the control system to adapt its parameters across varied operating states. Such methods use adaptive algorithms to optimise fuel injection, ignition timing or airflow based on several predefined learning zones.
[F02D 41/2448] Prohibition of learning -- F02D 41/2448 covers systems that deliberately prevent a combustion-engine control unit from using adaptive or learning algorithms, ensuring that the control strategy remains fixed and does not modify its parameters based on past operating data. This prohibition is applied to avoid unintended performance changes or safety risks.
[F02D 41/2451] characterised by what is learned or calibrated -- F02D 41/2451 covers electrical control devices for combustion engines that operate on information obtained through learning or calibration, such as adaptive algorithms, stored reference maps, or parameters derived from test data, to adjust fuel, air, ignition or other engine functions for optimal performance.
[F02D 41/2454] Learning of the air-fuel ratio control -- The CPC subclass F02D 41/2454 covers methods for learning and adapting the air-fuel ratio control in internal combustion engines, typically using electronic control units that adjust fuel injection based on sensor feedback. It pertains to electrical control strategies that enable the engine to optimise combustion performance and emissions through adaptive algorithms.
[F02D 41/2458] with an additional dither signal -- F02D 41/2458 covers electrical control strategies for combustion engines that employ an additional dither signal, a low-amplitude modulation superimposed on the primary control input to improve engine response, reduce hunting, and enhance stability. The technique is used in engine management systems to fine-tune fuel injection or ignition timing.
[F02D 41/2461] by learning a value and then controlling another value
[F02D 41/2464] Characteristics of actuators -- The subclass covers the performance and design features of actuators employed in electrically controlled combustion engines, including force, speed, response time, power use and reliability as they affect fuel injection, valve timing and other engine functions.
[F02D 41/2467] for injectors -- This sub-class concerns electrical control of fuel injectors in combustion engines, covering electronic systems that regulate injection timing, quantity and pressure to optimise performance, efficiency and emissions.
[F02D 41/247] Behaviour for small quantities -- The CPC subclass F02D 41/247 covers electrical control strategies that address the behavior of combustion engines when operating with very small quantities of fuel, air, or other working fluids. It includes methods and devices for monitoring, regulating and optimizing engine performance during low-flow, start-up or transient conditions.
[F02D 41/2474] Characteristics of sensors -- F02D 41/2474 defines the technical characteristics of sensors employed in the electrical control of combustion engines, encompassing their functional parameters, performance limits, and design features. It is used to classify documentation that details sensor types, measurement ranges, response times, and reliability aspects relevant to engine control systems.
[F02D 41/2477] characterised by the method used for learning -- The CPC subclass F02D 41/2477 pertains to electrical control systems for combustion engines that are characterised by the use of a learning method, such as adaptive algorithms, neural-network or other machine-learning techniques that modify control parameters based on operating data.
[F02D 41/248] using a plurality of learned values -- The subclass F02D 41/248 covers methods for electrically controlling combustion engines that employ several learned parameters or models, such as adaptive or machine-learning based values, to optimise fuel injection, ignition timing or other engine functions. It focuses on using multiple stored or continuously updated data points to improve engine performance and emissions.
[F02D 41/2483] restricting learned values -- This subclass covers methods that limit or adjust set points or parameters learned by adaptive control systems in combustion engines, such as fuel injection timing, air-fuel ratio, or ignition timing, to ensure safe or efficient operation. It includes strategies for bounding, clipping or saturating learned values in engine control algorithms.
[F02D 41/2487] Methods for rewriting -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Rewriting part of the controller memory with new data, e.g. during engine inspections, and associated methods
[F02D 41/249] Methods for preventing the loss of data -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Use of (semi-)permanent memories or using permanent batteries to feed normal memories
[F02D 41/2493] Resetting of data to a predefined set of values -- The CPC class F02D 41/2493 covers methods and devices for resetting engine-control data to a predetermined set of values, typically used in electrical management systems for combustion engines to restore calibration parameters or fault-free operating conditions.
[F02D 41/2496] the memory being part of a closed loop -- F02D 41/2496 covers systems in which a memory element is incorporated into the closed-loop electrical control of a combustion engine, enabling the controller to store and retrieve data such as sensor readings, operating parameters or diagnostic information for use in subsequent control cycles. This classification relates to electronic strategies that improve engine performance, emissions control or fault handling by retaining information across engine operating periods.
[F02D 41/26] using computer, e.g. microprocessor -- This subclass relates to the use of a computer or microprocessor to electrically control a combustion engine, such as managing fuel injection, ignition timing, air-fuel mixture, or other engine parameters. It covers electronic control units and software that monitor engine performance and adjust operation for efficiency, emissions, or power output.
[F02D 41/263] the program execution being modifiable by physical parameters -- F02D 41/263 covers electrical control systems for combustion engines in which the software governing engine operation can be dynamically adjusted based on real-time physical parameters such as temperature, pressure, or load, allowing the program's execution to be modified to optimise performance or emissions.
[F02D 41/266] the computer being backed-up or assisted by another circuit, e.g. analogue -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Analog or digital circuits in addition to the main control unit, e.g.
[F02D 41/28] Interface circuits -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Interface circuit between the control unit and associated elements, e.g. multiplexers or signal processing units between sensors and control unit.
[F02D 41/30] Controlling fuel injection -- The F02D 41/30 subclass covers electronic methods, devices and systems that regulate the amount, timing and pressure of fuel delivered to internal combustion engines. It includes electronic fuel-injection control units, injector actuation mechanisms, sensor-based feedback loops and software strategies aimed at optimizing performance, emissions and fuel consumption.
[F02D 41/3005] Details not otherwise provided for -- F02D 41/3005 covers miscellaneous aspects of electrical control of combustion engines that are not specified elsewhere, including auxiliary sensors, actuators, wiring, or control algorithms that do not fit into more detailed sub-classes. It is used for any electrical engine-management features that lack a dedicated classification.
[F02D 41/3011] according to or using specific or several modes of combustion -- F02D 41/3011 covers combustion engines that operate using specific or several modes of combustion, such as homogeneous charge compression ignition, dual-fuel, or stratified-charge operation. The classification addresses electrical control systems that select or switch between these modes to improve performance, emissions or efficiency.
[F02D 41/3017] characterised by the mode(s) being used -- The subclass F02D 41/3017 pertains to electrical control of combustion engines where the control strategy is defined by the particular operating mode of the engine, such as start-up, idle, load or shut-down. It includes devices or methods that select or modify control actions based on the mode being used.
[F02D 41/3023] a mode being the stratified charge spark-ignited mode -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gasoline engines with a stratified charge, which is usually caused by injection late in the compression stroke and subsequent ignition.
[F02D 41/3029] further comprising a homogeneous charge spark-ignited mode -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines with stratified and homogeneous modes
