[F02D 41/3035] a mode being the premixed charge compression-ignition mode -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engine control for engines wherein the mixture is partly or homogeneously premixed and then ignited by compression.  Also called HCCI for homogeneous charge compression ignition.
[F02D 41/3041] with means for triggering compression ignition, e.g. spark plug -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means used to trigger the compression-ignition in premixed charge compression ignition engines, e.g.:
[F02D 41/3047] said means being a secondary injection of fuel -- **Definition:** This place covers:  A second, small injection shortly before the compression stroke TDC to get local air-fuel ratio above self-ignition limit. Said fuel might also be ignited by spark to cause pressure increase and subsequent self-ignition of the homogeneous mixture
[F02D 41/3058] the engine working with a variable number of cycles -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines controlled to change from 2-cycle to 4-cycle or 6-cycle mode, e.g. by changing valve actuation and injection frequency
[F02D 41/3064] with special control during transition between modes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Special measures to avoid problems during combustion mode transitions, e.g. considering the delay between different actuators
[F02D 41/307] to avoid torque shocks -- F02D 41/307 addresses methods and devices for avoiding torque shocks in combustion engines by using electrical control systems. It falls under the subclass for controlling combustion engines via electrical means, focusing on smoothing torque variations during operation.
[F02D 41/3076] with special conditions for selecting a mode of combustion, e.g. for starting, for diagnosing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Conditions other than load/speed map for selecting one of a plurality of combustion modesAdaptation of load/speed maps for selecting combustion modes
[F02D 41/3082] Control of electrical fuel pumps -- The F02D 41/3082 entry covers methods and devices for electrically-driven fuel pumps that are used to regulate fuel flow in combustion engines. It addresses control strategies, circuit designs and feedback mechanisms that adjust pump operation to meet engine performance and emission requirements.
[F02D 41/3094] the fuel injection being effected by at least two different injectors, e.g. one in the intake manifold and one in the cylinder -- This subclass covers systems where fuel delivery is achieved by employing at least two separate injectors, such as one positioned in the intake manifold and another directly in the cylinder, with the injection actions coordinated electrically.
[F02D 41/32] of the low pressure type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of fuel injection into intake manifold which is not air-mass based fuel injection control, i.e. essentially air density control after intake pressure and rpm is classified here
[F02D 41/34] with means for controlling injection timing or duration -- F02D 41/34 covers electrical systems that control the timing or duration of fuel injection in combustion engines. The classification includes devices such as electronic injectors, timing modules, and control circuits that adjust injection events to optimise performance, emissions, and efficiency. It falls under mechanical engineering and the electrical control of combustion engines.
[F02D 41/345] Controlling injection timing  F02D41/365  takes precedence -- F02D 41/345 covers electrical methods for controlling the timing of fuel injection in combustion engines, focusing on how electronic signals adjust when fuel is delivered to the cylinders. This subclass deals specifically with injection-timing control strategies, and it is superseded by the more detailed subclass F02D 41/365.
[F02D 41/36] with means for controlling distribution -- F02D 41/36 covers electrical control systems for combustion engines that include means for managing the distribution of fuel, air or mixture within the engine, such as electronically regulated injection timing, valve actuation or charge-air distribution, aimed at optimizing combustion performance and emissions.
[F02D 41/365] with means for controlling timing and distribution -- This subgroup addresses combustion-engine systems that incorporate electrical devices for controlling spark or fuel-injection timing and distribution, such as electronic control units, sensors and actuators that adjust ignition or injection events to optimise performance, emissions and fuel efficiency.
[F02D 41/38] of the high pressure type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  High pressure fuel injection control, e.g. control of fuel amount of electronically controlled direct injectors.
[F02D 41/3809] Common rail control systems  common rail apparatus  F02M55/025 ,  F02M63/0225 -- F02D 41/3809 covers electrical control systems for common-rail fuel injection in combustion engines, including electronic management of pressure and injection timing in the rail and associated apparatus. It relates to the broader field of controlling combustion engines through electrical means.
[F02D 41/3818] for petrol engines -- This CPC subclass covers electrical control systems specifically designed for petrol (spark-ignition) engines, including sensors, actuators and electronic modules that regulate fuel injection, ignition timing or emissions in such engines.
[F02D 41/3827] for diesel engines -- F02D 41/3827 covers electrical control systems, circuits or methods used to regulate the operation of diesel engines, such as fuel injection timing, air-fuel mixture, or emission control.
[F02D 41/3836] Controlling the fuel pressure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling the fuel pressure in the common rail, also setting of target values
[F02D 41/3845] by controlling the flow into the common rail, e.g. the amount of fuel pumped -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This includes both diverting the fuel directly from the high pressure pump and the control of fuel amount directed to the pump inlet via the so-called suction control valves
[F02D 41/3854] with elements in the low pressure part, e.g. low pressure pump -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The amount of fuel supplied to the high pressure pump is (indirectly) controlled using the low pressure fuel pump, fuel pressure regulators in the low pressure part of the common rail system or other elements in the low pressure circuit  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Suction control valve in the pump inlet -> F02D41/3845
[F02D 41/3863] by controlling the flow out of the common rail, e.g. using pressure relief valves -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Common rail pressure control via pressure relief valves.
[F02D 41/3872] characterised by leakage flow in injectors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Short actuations of the injector which cause some fuel to leak out of the injector and return to the fuel tank. These actuations are usually too short to cause fuel injector opening and therefore supply of fuel to engine.
[F02D 41/40] with means for controlling injection timing or duration -- The subclass F02D 41/40 covers combustion engines that use electrical systems to manage fuel injection, specifically providing mechanisms that adjust the timing or duration of injection events. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the broader group of controlling combustion engines, focusing on electronic control of injection parameters.
[F02D 41/401] Controlling injection timing  F02D41/402  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass covers electrical methods for adjusting the timing of fuel injection in combustion engines to optimise performance, emissions and efficiency. It resides under mechanical engineering - combustion engines - controlling combustion engines - electrical control of combustion engines. The narrower subclass F02D41/402, which deals with specific injection-timing control techniques, takes precedence.
[F02D 41/402] Multiple injections -- F02D 41/402 covers systems and methods that deliver more than one fuel injection during a single combustion cycle of an engine, using electrical control to vary the timing, quantity or pressure of each injection. The multiple-injection approach aims to improve combustion efficiency, reduce emissions and enhance power output.
[F02D 41/403] with pilot injections -- This subclass covers electrical control strategies for combustion engines that employ a small pilot injection to initiate combustion before the main fuel charge, improving ignition timing and stability. It includes systems and methods that manage the timing, quantity and pressure of the pilot injection as part of overall engine control.
[F02D 41/405] with post injections
[F02D 41/406] Electrically controlling a diesel injection pump  F02D41/401  takes precedence -- The entry covers electric-based methods for regulating a diesel injection pump, including the use of electronic signals to adjust fuel-delivery timing, quantity and pressure in order to control engine combustion. It falls within mechanical engineering → combustion engines → controlling combustion engines and supersedes the broader subclass F02D41/401.
[F02D 41/407] of the in-line type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fuel amount control in in-line injection pumps
[F02D 41/408] of the distributing type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fuel amount control in distributor injector pumps
[F02D 43/00] Conjoint electrical control of two or more functions, e.g. ignition, fuel-air mixture, recirculation, supercharging or exhaust-gas treatment -- F02D 43/00 covers systems that electrically coordinate two or more engine functions such as ignition timing, fuel-air mixture, exhaust gas recirculation, supercharging or after-treatment. The classification addresses integrated control strategies that manage these processes simultaneously to optimise performance, emissions or efficiency in combustion engines.
[F02D 43/02] using only analogue means -- F02D 43/02 covers systems that control two or more engine functions-such as ignition timing, fuel-air mixture, EGR, supercharging or exhaust treatment-by means of purely analogue electronics, without digital processors or software. The classification addresses hardware-only solutions for coordinated control in combustion engines.
[F02D 43/04] using only digital means -- The code F02D 43/04 covers digital-only control strategies that simultaneously manage two or more engine functions such as ignition timing, fuel-air mixture, exhaust-gas recirculation, supercharging or exhaust-gas treatment, using purely electronic or software-based means without analogue components. It applies to systems that integrate these controls in a single digital controller for combustion engines.
[F02D 45/00] Electrical control not provided for in groups -- F02D 45/00 covers electrical control measures for combustion-engine operation that are not specifically listed in any other CPC subgroup. It includes miscellaneous or novel electrical control solutions applied to fuel injection, ignition timing, air-fuel ratio or other engine-governing functions, where no dedicated classification exists.
[F02D 7/00] Other fuel-injection control -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Control of fuel injection peculiar to engines characterised by their use of non-liquid fuels, pluralities of fuels, or non-fuel substances added to the combustible mixtures -> F02D19/00 - Control of supercharged engines -> F02D23/00 - Electric fuel-injection control -> F02D41/00
[F02D 7/002] Throttling of fuel passages between pumps and injectors or overflow passages  low-pressure fuel injection  F02M69/00 -- The F02D 7/002 subclass deals with throttling of fuel passages between pumps and injectors or through overflow channels in low-pressure fuel-injection systems, providing means to control the amount of fuel delivered to a combustion engine. This falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion-engine control and other fuel-injection control methods.
[F02D 7/005] by mechanical means, e.g. using a centrifugal governor -- F02D 7/005 addresses mechanical methods for regulating fuel injection in combustion engines, for example using a centrifugal governor that adjusts fuel flow in response to engine speed. These devices provide direct, non-electronic control of engine performance.
[F02D 7/007] by fluid actuated means, e.g. slide valves -- The F02D 7/007 subclass covers fuel-injection control devices that operate by fluid-actuated mechanisms, such as slide-type valves, used to regulate the amount and timing of fuel delivered to combustion engines. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines and their control.
[F02D 7/02] Controlling fuel injection where fuel is injected by compressed air -- F02D 7/02 designates techniques for controlling fuel injection in combustion engines when the fuel is delivered by compressed air. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of Mechanical Engineering - Combustion Engines - Controlling Combustion Engines - Other fuel-injection control.
[F02D 9/00] Controlling engines by throttling air or fuel-and-air induction conduits or exhaust conduits -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Valves for controlling the air or air and fuel admission into or the exhaust out of the engine and the arrangement of such valves in intake or exhaust conduits.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Lift valves -> F01L3/00 - EGR valves -> F02M26/52 - Lift valves in general -> F16K1/00  **Glossary:** - Pivotally-mounted flaps: Flaps rotating around an axis
[F02D 9/02] concerning induction conduits -- F02D 9/02 covers features that control a combustion engine by throttling its induction conduits, including air or fuel-air passages that feed the cylinders. The entry includes throttle valves, variable-geometry inlet passages, and other mechanisms that regulate the amount of mixture entering the engine to manage power output and efficiency.
[F02D 9/04] concerning exhaust conduits -- F02D 9/04 covers methods and devices that regulate a combustion engine by controlling the flow of exhaust gases. This includes throttling or varying exhaust conduit dimensions, using valves, shutters or variable geometry to adjust back-pressure, thereby influencing engine speed, torque and emissions.
[F02D 9/06] Exhaust brakes -- An exhaust brake is a device that creates engine braking by partially closing the exhaust pipe, increasing back-pressure and slowing the engine's rotation. It is used on combustion engines to control speed without using the vehicle's service brakes and falls under the CPC subclass for throttling air or fuel-air induction and exhaust conduits.
[F02D 9/08] Throttle valves specially adapted therefor -- Throttle valves specially adapted for controlling combustion engines regulate the flow of air, fuel-air mixture, or exhaust gases to manage engine speed and power output. They are used where throttling of induction or exhaust conduits is required for engine control.
[F02D 9/10] having pivotally-mounted flaps -- This subclass details combustion-engine control techniques that employ pivotally-mounted flaps to throttle air, fuel-air induction or exhaust passages, regulating engine operation through adjustable flap positions.
[F02D 9/1005] Details of the flap -- F02D 9/1005 pertains to the specific design and operation of throttle flaps that regulate the flow of air, fuel-air mixture, or exhaust in combustion engines. It covers details such as flap geometry, positioning, actuation mechanisms and control strategies used to modulate engine performance.
[F02D 9/101] Special flap shapes, ribs, bores or the like -- F02D 9/101 covers the design of special flap shapes, ribs, bores or similar features used in throttling or valve devices that regulate air, fuel-air mixture or exhaust flow in combustion engines, enabling precise control of engine performance.
[F02D 9/1015] Details of the edge of the flap, e.g. for lowering flow noise or improving flow sealing in closed flap position -- This subclass covers the geometry and treatment of the edge of throttling flaps used in air, fuel-air or exhaust passages of combustion engines, aiming to lower flow-induced noise and improve sealing when the flap is closed. It includes edge profiles, sealing lips and surface modifications designed for noise attenuation and tighter shut-off.
[F02D 9/102] the flap having movable parts fixed onto it -- F02D 9/102 covers a flap that incorporates one or more movable elements fixed to its surface, used to regulate the passage of air, fuel-air mixture or exhaust gases in a combustion engine. The movable parts adjust the effective opening of the conduit, providing precise throttling for engine speed or emissions control. This technology is applied in intake or exhaust systems where variable geometry is required.
[F02D 9/1025] the rotation axis of the flap being off-set from the flap center axis -- F02D 9/1025 covers throttling devices for combustion engines in which the pivot or rotation axis of a flap is deliberately offset from the flap's geometric centre. The offset axis changes the opening characteristics of the air or exhaust passage, allowing finer control of mass flow and engine performance.
[F02D 9/103] the rotation axis being located at an edge -- F02D 9/103 covers combustion engines whose rotating axis is placed at the edge of the cylinder or housing rather than centrally, involving designs such as peripheral-axis pistons or edge-mounted crankshafts for throttling air or fuel-air flow.
[F02D 9/1035] Details of the valve housing -- F02D 9/1035 relates to the design and construction of valve housings that regulate air or fuel-air flow in internal combustion engines. The entry covers the geometry, materials, sealing methods and mounting of housings for throttle, intake or exhaust valves used to control engine operation.
[F02D 9/104] Shaping of the flow path in the vicinity of the flap, e.g. having inserts in the housing -- This class covers features that shape the flow path near a throttle or valve flap, such as inserting housings or other inserts, to alter air or exhaust conduit characteristics and enhance combustion-engine control.
[F02D 9/1045] for sealing of the flow in closed flap position, e.g. the housing forming a valve seat -- This CPC subclass covers sealing of the flow when a flap is fully closed, for example by a housing that forms a valve seat, used in throttle or exhaust control of combustion engines.
[F02D 9/105] having a throttle position sensor  detection of actuation  F02D11/106 -- F02D 9/105 relates to control of combustion engines by throttling air, fuel-air mixture, or exhaust flow and includes a throttle position sensor that detects actuator movement. The sensor provides feedback to enable precise engine regulation, enhancing performance and emissions control.
[F02D 9/1055] having a fluid by-pass -- The subclass F02D 9/1055 covers combustion-engine control systems that incorporate a fluid bypass, allowing part of the intake air, fuel-air mixture, or exhaust gas to be diverted around a primary flow path. This bypass can be used to modulate engine performance, reduce emissions, or protect components by adjusting flow rates without altering the main conduit.
[F02D 9/106] Sealing of the valve shaft in the housing, e.g. details of the bearings -- The F02D 9/106 subclass covers methods and devices for sealing the valve shaft within the engine housing, focusing on bearing arrangements and related details that prevent leakage of air, fuel-air mixture or exhaust gases while the engine is throttled for combustion control.
[F02D 9/1065] Mechanical control linkage between an actuator and the flap, e.g. including levers, gears, springs, clutches, limit stops of the like -- A mechanical linkage transmits motion from an actuator to a throttle or exhaust flap, using components such as levers, gears, springs, clutches or limit stops to regulate the opening and closing of the conduit. This arrangement forms part of the control system for combustion engines that modulates air, fuel-air mixture or exhaust flow.
[F02D 9/107] Manufacturing or mounting details -- This subclass covers the manufacturing and mounting features of components that control combustion-engine operation by throttling the air or fuel-air mixture or exhaust flow, such as throttle bodies, intake manifolds, and exhaust valves. It includes details of how these parts are produced, assembled, or attached within the engine system.
[F02D 9/1075] Materials, e.g. composites -- The CPC subclass F02D 9/1075 covers the use of specific materials, particularly composite materials, in components that throttle air, fuel-air mixtures or exhaust gases to control combustion engines. It addresses the selection, design and performance of such materials within throttling devices for engine regulation.
[F02D 9/108] Plastics
[F02D 9/1085] Non-organic materials, e.g. metals, alloys, ceramics
[F02D 9/109] having two or more flaps -- F02D 9/109 covers methods for controlling combustion engines by throttling the air or fuel-air mixture or exhaust flow using two or more movable flaps. The flaps are positioned in induction or exhaust conduits to regulate engine output.
[F02D 9/1095] Rotating on a common axis, e.g. having a common shaft -- The subclass F02D 9/1095 covers arrangements in which rotating elements share a common axis, such as a single shaft that drives multiple throttling or valve components. It applies to systems that control combustion-engine operation by adjusting air, fuel-air or exhaust flow through coaxial rotating mechanisms.
[F02D 9/12] having slidably-mounted valve members -- F02D 9/12 covers combustion engines that control speed or power by using valves that slide within their seats, such as sliding or plunger type valves rather than rotary or poppet types. These valve members are mounted so they can move linearly to adjust the flow of air, fuel-air mixture or exhaust gases.
[F02D 9/14] the members being slidable transversely of conduit -- F02D 9/14 covers devices that control a combustion engine by moving a throttle element sideways across an intake or exhaust passage, varying the flow of air, fuel-air mixture, or exhaust gases. It falls under the broader group for controlling engines through conduit throttling.
[F02D 9/16] the members being rotatable -- The entry covers combustion-engine control methods that use rotating elements-such as valves, shutters or rotors-to throttle the intake of air or fuel-air mixture, or the exhaust flow, thereby regulating engine operating conditions. It concerns mechanical means for adjusting flow rates by turning parts within the conduit system.
[F02D 9/18] having elastic-wall valve members -- F02D 9/18 covers combustion-engine control systems that use valve members with an elastic wall, such as diaphragm or membrane valves, to modulate air, fuel-air mixture, or exhaust flow. These flexible valves replace rigid seats to provide throttling action and improve sealing under varying pressure conditions.
[F02F] CYLINDERS, PISTONS OR CASINGS, FOR COMBUSTION ENGINES -- F02F relates to the design, construction and material aspects of cylinders, pistons and casings employed in internal combustion engines, encompassing their geometry, cooling features, sealing arrangements and manufacturing processes as components of the engine's combustion chamber.
[F02F 1/00] Cylinders -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cylinders and cylinder heads for combustion engines
[F02F 1/002] Integrally formed cylinders and cylinder heads -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All applications of a single piece forming the cylinder and the cylinder head.
[F02F 1/004] Cylinder liners  F02F1/08 ,  F02F1/16  take precedence -- The CPC subclass F02F 1/004 covers cylinder liners used in combustion-engine cylinders, defining the inner sleeves that form the working surface for pistons. It falls under the hierarchy of mechanical engineering → combustion engines → cylinders, pistons or casings for combustion engines → cylinders. The related sub-entries F02F 1/08 and F02F 1/16 have priority over this class.
[F02F 1/02] having cooling means -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cylinder heads -> F02F1/26
[F02F 1/04] for air cooling -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for air cooling of cylinder heads
[F02F 1/045] Attachment of cylinders to crankcase -- F02F 1/045 covers the means for securing engine cylinders to the crankcase, including fastening systems, bolts, welding or other attachment structures that align and lock the cylinder block or individual cylinders to the crankcase for proper load transfer and sealing.
[F02F 1/06] Shape or arrangement of cooling fins -- F02F 1/06 covers the shape and layout of cooling fins applied to cylinders, pistons or casings of combustion engines, addressing fin geometry, spacing, orientation and pattern to enhance heat removal and engine performance.
[F02F 1/065] with means for directing or distributing cooling medium -- The subclass F02F 1/065 covers cylinder constructions that incorporate means for directing or distributing a cooling medium, such as passages, channels, jackets or internal flow paths that supply coolant to the cylinder walls or interior to manage temperature during operation.
[F02F 1/08] running-liner and cooling-part of cylinder being different parts or of different material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Running-liner and cooling-part of cylinder being different parts or of different material:  Only for air cooled engines, e.g. different materials used for the liners and the cooling fins.
[F02F 1/10] for liquid cooling -- The CPC subclass F02F 1/10 covers cylinders for combustion engines that are equipped with liquid cooling, describing designs where coolant circulates within the cylinder walls to remove heat and improve thermal management. It applies to piston or cylinder liners in internal-combustion engines that use water or other liquid coolants.
[F02F 11/00] Arrangements of sealings in combustion engines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangement of sealings in combustion engines  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Piston rings for combustion engines -> F16J9/00
[F02F 11/002] involving cylinder heads -- This CPC subclass (F02F 11/002) covers inventions dealing with the sealing arrangements of cylinder heads in internal combustion engines, including designs and methods that prevent leakage between the combustion chamber and adjacent engine components. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → cylinders, pistons or casings.
[F02F 11/005] involving cylinder liners -- This CPC subclass covers technical features related to the arrangement of seals in cylinder liners of combustion engines, including methods and designs for sealing between the liner and the piston or other components to prevent gas leakage and improve efficiency.
[F02F 11/007] involving rotary applications -- The CPC subgroup F02F 11/007 covers sealing arrangements in internal combustion engines that employ rotary motion. It includes designs where seals rotate relative to the cylinder or housing, such as in rotary pistons, Wankel engines or other rotating-piston concepts, aiming to maintain compression and prevent leakage.

[F02F 1/108] Siamese-type cylinders, i.e. cylinders cast together -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Siamese-type cylinders, i.e. cylinders cast together:
[F02F 1/12] Preventing corrosion of liquid-swept surfaces -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All measures taken for combating corrosion in water cooled engines.
[F02F 1/14] Cylinders with means for directing, guiding or distributing liquid stream -- This subclass covers cylinder designs for combustion engines that incorporate features to control the flow of liquid fuel or coolant, such as internal channels, ports, or passages that direct, guide or distribute the liquid within the cylinder. The purpose is to improve fuel atomisation, cooling efficiency or combustion stability.
[F02F 1/16] Cylinder liners of wet type -- Cylinder liners of wet type are components that line the cylinder bore and are internally water-cooled, providing efficient heat removal and reduced wear in combustion engines. These liners are surrounded by coolant directly, improving temperature control and enabling higher power output.
[F02F 1/163] the liner being midsupported -- The code F02F 1/163 refers to cylinder liners for combustion engines that are midsupported, meaning the liner is reinforced or attached at its middle rather than only at the ends. This arrangement provides improved heat-transfer and structural stability while allowing the liner to accommodate thermal expansion. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the subclass concerning cylinders, pistons or casings.
[F02F 1/166] Spacer decks -- Spacer decks are structural components placed between the cylinder head and the engine block or between stacked cylinder sections to maintain precise alignment and spacing of the combustion chambers. They reinforce the assembly, distribute clamping forces from bolts, and help prevent distortion under thermal and mechanical loads, thereby improving sealing and durability of the engine.
[F02F 1/18] Other cylinders -- This group covers cylinders used in combustion-engine systems that are not specified in other more detailed subclasses, such as auxiliary, experimental or hybrid cylinders for steam, gas-turbine or other non-standard configurations.
[F02F 1/183] Oval or square cylinders -- F02F 1/183 covers combustion engine cylinders with non-circular cross-sections such as oval or square shapes, describing design variations intended to improve combustion efficiency, cooling, or structural strength.
[F02F 1/186] for use in engines with two or more pistons reciprocating within same cylinder -- F02F 1/186 covers cylinder designs intended for engines in which two or more pistons reciprocate within the same bore, such as opposed-piston or multi-piston arrangements. The classification applies to mechanical-engineering solutions that integrate shared cylinder walls, sealing systems and cooling features for these configurations. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and cylinder or piston casings.
[F02F 1/20] characterised by constructional features providing for lubrication -- The F02F 1/20 subclass pertains to cylinders, pistons or casings for combustion engines that incorporate specific constructional features designed to supply lubrication. It classifies designs where the lubrication system is integrated into the cylinder's geometry or components to ensure effective oil delivery during operation.
[F02F 1/22] characterised by having ports in cylinder wall for scavenging or charging -- The subclass F02F 1/22 covers cylinder designs in combustion engines that incorporate one or more ports cut into the cylinder wall to allow fresh charge to enter or exhaust gases to be removed, a feature used for scavenging or charging the cylinder. Such ported cylinders are typical of two-stroke engines and certain four-stroke configurations and are distinguished from solid-wall cylinder constructions.
[F02F 1/24] Cylinder heads -- Cylinder heads are removable components that close the top of an engine's cylinders, containing the combustion chambers, intake and exhaust ports, and often the spark plugs or fuel injectors. They provide sealing, aid heat dissipation, and manage airflow, thereby influencing engine performance, efficiency and emissions control.
[F02F 1/242] Arrangement of spark plugs or injectors -- It covers the spatial arrangement and positioning of spark plugs or fuel injectors within cylinder heads of internal combustion engines, including relative placement to optimise combustion efficiency and emissions. The classification also addresses design considerations for accessibility, cooling and durability of these components.
[F02F 1/243] Cylinder heads and inlet or exhaust manifolds integrally cast together -- The CPC subclass F02F 1/243 covers components for combustion-engine cylinders where the cylinder head and either the inlet or exhaust manifold are formed as a single, integrally cast unit, improving structural rigidity and simplifying assembly. This applies to pistons, casings and related parts in mechanical engineering.
[F02F 1/26] having cooling means -- The classification covers cylinders for internal combustion engines that incorporate cooling means such as water jackets, external fins or other heat-dissipating structures to control the temperature of the cylinder and piston during operation. It applies to designs where the cooling system is integral to the cylinder component.
[F02F 1/28] for air cooling -- F02F 1/28 covers cylinders for combustion engines that are cooled by air, i.e., designs where airflow directly removes heat from the cylinder walls or surrounding components without liquid coolant. It pertains to engine cylinders, pistons or casings employing air-cooling methods such as fins or forced-air ducts.
[F02F 1/30] Finned cylinder heads -- F02F 1/30 denotes finned cylinder heads, which are cylinder heads equipped with external fins to increase heat dissipation in internal combustion engines. These components are part of the cylinder assembly and are used to improve cooling efficiency, especially in high-performance or heavy-duty engines.
[F02F 1/305] the cylinder heads being of side valve type -- This subclass covers cylinder heads that use a side-valve (flathead) arrangement, with the valves situated beside the combustion chamber in the engine block rather than in the head itself. It pertains to the design of cylinder heads for combustion engines where the valve layout follows the side-valve configuration.
[F02F 1/32] the cylinder heads being of overhead valve type -- This subclass concerns cylinder heads of the overhead-valve type for combustion-engine cylinders, describing designs where the valves are situated in the head above the combustion chamber. It includes configurations, valve-actuation arrangements and related cooling or sealing features.
[F02F 1/34] with means for directing or distributing cooling medium -- The CPC subclass F02F 1/34 covers cylinders for combustion engines that incorporate means for directing or distributing a cooling medium, such as coolant passages, jets or channels integrated into the cylinder wall or liner to control temperature and improve heat dissipation.
[F02F 1/36] for liquid cooling -- This subgroup covers cooling arrangements that direct liquid-such as water or coolant-through passages or jackets attached to the cylinder walls of combustion engines to dissipate heat and keep temperatures uniform. It includes designs for integral liquid-flow channels, external jackets, and related structures that provide direct liquid cooling of the cylinder.
[F02F 1/365] the cylinder heads being of side valve type -- The subgroup F02F 1/365 covers cylinder heads that use a side-valve arrangement, where the intake and exhaust valves are mounted in the engine block beside the cylinder rather than above it. This design is typical of early internal-combustion engines and influences valve placement, airflow paths and cooling characteristics.
[F02F 1/38] the cylinder heads being of overhead valve type -- F02F 1/38 covers cylinder heads that employ an overhead valve arrangement in combustion engines, where the valves are positioned above the cylinder and actuated by a camshaft in the cylinder head. This design improves airflow and efficiency compared to side-valve configurations.
[F02F 1/40] cylinder heads with means for directing, guiding, or distributing liquid stream -- A cylinder head designed with integrated passages, ducts or nozzles that channel a liquid-such as coolant, fuel or lubricating oil-through the head to control its flow over combustion chambers, valve seats or exhaust ports, improving heat removal, fuel distribution or lubrication.
[F02F 1/42] Shape or arrangement of intake or exhaust channels in cylinder heads -- The subclass F02F 1/42 covers the geometry and layout of the intake and exhaust passages integral to cylinder heads, specifying how the channels that admit the air-fuel mixture and discharge combustion gases are shaped, positioned and routed within the head. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, cylinders, pistons or casings, and specifically cylinders.
[F02F 1/4214] specially adapted for four or more valves per cylinder -- The CPC subclass F02F 1/4214 designates cylinder constructions that are specifically adapted to house four or more valves per cylinder, a feature used to enhance airflow and combustion efficiency in internal-combustion engines. It falls within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under combustion engines, cylinders, pistons or casings, and covers design aspects that enable multi-valve arrangements.
[F02F 1/4221] particularly for three or more inlet valves -- This subclass covers cylinder designs in internal combustion engines that are specially adapted to accommodate three or more inlet valves per cylinder, improving airflow and efficiency. It includes configurations of piston heads, valve seats and related features that enable multi-valve arrangements.
[F02F 1/4228] Helically-shaped channels   imparting a rotation to the charge in the cylinder  F02B31/00
[F02F 1/4235] of intake channels -- F02F 1/4235 covers features of intake channels incorporated in the cylinders, pistons or casings of combustion engines. It pertains to the design, geometry or arrangement of the passages through which the air-fuel mixture enters the combustion chamber, influencing flow characteristics and engine performance. This subclass falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines and cylinder-related components.
[F02F 1/4242] with a partition wall inside the channel -- The CPC subclass F02F 1/4242 pertains to cylinders used in combustion engines that incorporate an internal partition wall within the combustion channel. The partition separates the channel into distinct zones, often to improve combustion efficiency, reduce emissions, or control heat transfer.
[F02F 1/425] with a separate deviation element inside the channel -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Separate deviation element inside the intake channel. E.g. a butterfly valve or an insert placed just before the valve seat.
[F02F 1/4257] with an intake liner -- The CPC code F02F 1/4257 covers cylinders for combustion engines that include an intake liner, a separate inner sleeve that forms the surface of the cylinder bore to enhance wear resistance, sealing and thermal management. It is a sub-category of mechanical engineering → combustion engines → cylinders, pistons or casings → cylinders.
[F02F 1/4264] of exhaust channels -- This subclass addresses the design and configuration of exhaust channels integrated with the cylinder, piston or casing of combustion engines, covering the pathways that convey combustion gases away from the cylinder.
[F02F 1/4271] with an exhaust liner -- F02F 1/4271 covers cylinders, pistons or casings for combustion engines that incorporate an exhaust liner, a component that forms a protective surface inside the cylinder to withstand hot exhaust gases. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and cylinder categories.
[F02F 1/4285] of both intake and exhaust channel -- The CPC subclass F02F 1/4285 covers cylinder designs that incorporate a combined intake and exhaust channel within the same passage. It pertains to combustion-engine cylinders where the flow path for fresh charge and expelled gases shares a common conduit, improving compactness and integration.
[F02F 1/4292] with liners  F02F1/4257 ,  F02F1/4271  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Liners in both intake and exhaust channel.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Liners in intake channels only -> F02F1/4257 - Liners in exhaust channels only -> F02F1/4271
[F02F 2001/006] having a ring at the inside of a liner or cylinder for preventing the deposit of carbon oil particles, e.g. oil scrapers -- The CPC subclass F02F 2001/006 covers devices that incorporate an inner ring within a liner or cylinder to stop carbon-oil particles from depositing on surfaces, commonly employed as oil scrapers in engines or compressors. This feature helps maintain cleanliness and efficiency of the moving parts by physically removing or preventing buildup of carbon residue.
[F02F 2001/008] Stress problems, especially related to thermal stress -- F02F 2001/008 covers inventions that address stress problems in structures, with a focus on stresses caused by temperature changes. It includes methods, devices, or control systems for monitoring, mitigating, or compensating thermal stress in buildings, machinery, or other constructions. The classification is used to categorize patents dealing with thermal expansion, contraction, and related deformation issues.
[F02F 2001/104] using an open deck, i.e. the water jacket is open at the block top face -- This subclass covers internal combustion engine blocks where the water jacket is not sealed at the top, allowing coolant to flow directly over the cylinder heads via an open deck arrangement. It addresses cooling methods where the deck surface is exposed.
[F02F 2001/106] using a closed deck, i.e. the water jacket is not open at the block top face -- The CPC subclass F02F 2001/106 covers heat-exchange devices that employ a closed-deck design, meaning the water jacket surrounding the block is sealed at the top face rather than being open. This configuration improves pressure containment and thermal efficiency in liquid-cooled systems. It applies to any apparatus where the cooling jacket is fully enclosed at the block's upper surface.
[F02F 2001/241] specially adapted to pent roof shape of the combustion chamber -- F02F 2001/241 classifies inventions in which a combustion chamber is specially adapted to a pent-roof shape, a geometry commonly used in gas-turbine and engine designs to enhance flame stability, mixing and heat transfer. The code is applied to patents that describe structural features or modifications built to fit this particular roof configuration.
[F02F 2001/244] Arrangement of valve stems in cylinder heads -- This CPC subgroup covers designs that specify the spatial positioning and orientation of valve stems within the cylinder head of an internal combustion engine, including their layout relative to each other and to the combustion chamber. It addresses how the arrangement affects valve operation, cooling, and engine performance.
[F02F 2001/245] the valve stems being orientated at an angle with the cylinder axis -- This subclass covers pumps whose valve stems are mounted at an angle to the cylinder axis instead of being parallel. The angled orientation can improve fluid flow, reduce wear and allow more compact designs. It is part of CPC group F02F for reciprocating pumps, cited in the 2001 edition.
[F02F 2001/246] and orientated radially from the combustion chamber surface -- The CPC subclass F02F 2001/246 concerns internal-combustion engines in which one or more components-such as injectors, valves or ignition devices-are positioned radially outward from the surface of the combustion chamber. This radial orientation is intended to improve fuel distribution, flame propagation or cooling within the chamber.
[F02F 2001/247] the valve stems being orientated in parallel with the cylinder axis -- The subgroup identifies internal-combustion engines whose valve stems are aligned parallel to the cylinder axis, so the valve motion follows a line that runs coaxially with the bore rather than being angled. This arrangement is common in overhead-cam or push-rod designs where the stem orientation remains straight relative to the cylinder.
[F02F 2001/248] Methods for avoiding thermal stress-induced cracks in the zone between valve seat openings -- F02F 2001/248 covers techniques for preventing cracks caused by thermal stress in the area between valve seat openings, including cooling methods, material selection and design modifications that reduce temperature gradients during operation.
[F02F 2001/249] with flame plate, e.g. insert in the cylinder head used as a thermal insulation between cylinder head and combustion chamber -- The CPC code F02F 2001/249 covers internal-combustion engines that incorporate a flame plate, typically an insert fitted in the cylinder head to provide thermal insulation between the head and the combustion chamber, reducing heat loss and protecting components.
[F02F 2001/4207] Arrangements with one conduit connected with two valves; Arrangements connecting one valve with two conduits -- The CPC group F02F 2001/4207 covers hydraulic or pneumatic arrangements in which a single conduit is connected to two separate valves, or a single valve is linked to two separate conduits, allowing the control of fluid flow through multiple paths within the same line.
[F02F 2001/4278] Exhaust collectors -- The CPC subclass F02F 2001/4278 pertains to exhaust collectors, which are components or systems that gather, direct, and control the flow of exhaust gases from combustion engines or other sources, often integrating filters, cooling or recirculation functions to improve efficiency and emissions control.
[F02F 2003/0007] Monolithic pistons; One piece constructions; Casting of pistons -- The CPC entry F02F 2003/0007 covers pistons that are formed as a single, solid piece, typically by casting methods. It includes the design and manufacture of monolithic pistons used in internal-combustion engines, where the component is produced without separate parts or assemblies. This classification is applied to technologies focusing on one-piece piston construction and related casting processes.
[F02F 2003/0038] by brazing -- The CPC code F02F 2003/0038 falls under the class for processes of joining metal parts, specifically within the subclass for brazing methods. It covers inventions that achieve a permanent joint by heating a filler metal above its melting point while the base materials remain solid, often using fluxes or protective atmospheres. This entry is used to classify patents that detail the preparation, execution, or equipment for brazing operations.
[F02F 2003/0046] by crimping -- F02F 2003/0046 covers pumps whose displacement is varied by crimping a flexible element such as a tube, diaphragm or membrane. The crimping reduces the internal volume of the pump chamber, thereby controlling flow rate or pressure. This method is used in variable-displacement positive-displacement pumps and related fluid-handling devices.
[F02F 2003/0053] by soldering -- This CPC group covers techniques for assembling or repairing electrical power equipment where the joining step is performed by soldering, including the preparation, heating and application of solder to create conductive bonds between metal parts. It is used for patents on methods and apparatus that employ soldering in the construction or maintenance of generators, transformers, or other power-generation components.
[F02F 2003/0061] by welding -- F02F 2003/0061 covers inventions in which parts of hydraulic, pneumatic or steam power plants are joined by welding. It includes methods, apparatuses or arrangements where welding is used to connect components such as pressure vessels, turbines, piping or structural members, and therefore relates to the manufacturing or assembly of power-plant elements by welding.
[F02F 2007/0041] Fixing Bolts -- The CPC code F02F 2007/0041 concerns inventions for securing bolts, covering methods and devices that tighten, lock or otherwise fix bolts in place on machinery, structures or components. It includes mechanisms such as nuts, washers, locking elements, torque control and related fastening techniques.
[F02F 2007/0056] using bearing beams, i.e. bearings interconnected by a beam or multiple beams -- The CPC subclass F02F 2007/0056 pertains to hydraulic turbine constructions in which individual bearing supports are joined together by a common beam or a series of beams, forming a unified bearing-beam structure. This arrangement distributes loads across the beam system, enhancing stiffness and alignment of the rotating parts.
[F02F 2007/0063] Head bolts; Arrangements of cylinder head bolts -- The CPC entry F02F 2007/0063 covers bolts that fasten a cylinder head to an engine block and the specific arrangements of those bolts. It includes designs for head-bolt types, positioning patterns, tensioning features and fastening methods used in internal combustion engines to secure the head and maintain sealing integrity.
[F02F 2007/0075] Front covers -- F02F 2007/0075 covers front covers for boilers, steam generators, and related pressure-vessel equipment. It includes protective panels or doors that close the front opening, providing safety, access for maintenance and insulation while maintaining the integrity of the pressure vessel. This subclass groups patents dealing with design, mounting, sealing and material aspects of such front-cover components.
[F02F 2007/0078] Covers for belt transmissions -- Covers for belt transmissions are protective enclosures or shields designed to encase power-transmission belts, preventing contamination, wear and accidental contact while maintaining alignment and tension. They are used in industrial and mechanical systems to extend belt life, improve safety and reduce noise.
[F02F 2007/009] Hypereutectic aluminum, e.g. aluminum alloys with high SI content -- The CPC entry F02F 2007/009 covers hypereutectic aluminium alloys, i.e., aluminium-silicon alloys whose silicon content exceeds the eutectic level, typically above about 12 % Si. It includes the composition, manufacture, treatment and applications of these high-silicon alloys, which are valued for improved wear resistance, low thermal expansion and good castability.
[F02F 2007/0092] Transparent materials -- F02F 2007/0092 covers transparent materials employed as glazing, windows, façades, skylights and other optically clear components in buildings and structures. The subclass addresses the composition, processing, surface treatment and performance of glass, plastics and similar substances used where light transmission and visibility are required.
[F02F 2007/0097] for large diesel engines -- CPC F02F 2007/0097 pertains to large diesel internal-combustion engines, usually defined by high displacement or power output, and used in marine propulsion, rail vehicles or stationary power generation. The entry addresses design aspects, fuel-injection systems and emission-control measures specific to these high-power diesel applications.
[F02F 2200/00] Indexing scheme relating to manufacturing -- The CPC entry F02F 2200/00 covers indexing schemes used in the manufacturing of cylinders, pistons or casings for combustion engines, detailing methods for locating, aligning or referencing positions during production processes such as machining, assembly or quality control. It includes systems that guide tool positioning, measurement fixtures or digital markers to ensure dimensional accuracy and repeatability.
[F02F 2200/02] Riveting -- F02F 2200/02 - Riveting refers to the manufacturing processes and equipment used to join cylinders, pistons or casings in combustion engines by means of rivets. It is classified under mechanical engineering, specifically the combustion-engine sub-category for components of engine housings.
[F02F 2200/04] Forging of engine parts -- F02F 2200/04 covers forging processes applied to engine components such as cylinders, pistons or casings. It is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the manufacturing of these parts. The code specifies the production technique rather than the design of the components.
[F02F 2200/06] Casting -- F02F 2200/06 designates casting methods applied to manufacture cylinders, pistons or casings for combustion engines. It resides under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for combustion-engine components and pertains specifically to the manufacturing stage. The code is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification used by the EPO and USPTO.
[F02F 2200/08] using a lost model, e.g. foam casting -- The CPC subclass F02F 2200/08 covers manufacturing of engine cylinders, pistons or casings by employing a lost-model technique, notably foam casting, where the model is removed after the casting material solidifies to produce the final component.
[F02F 2200/11] using wrought materials, e.g. wrought steels -- F02F 2200/11 is a CPC subclass within mechanical engineering for combustion engines, specifically cylinders, pistons or casings, that delineates manufacturing processes employing wrought materials such as wrought steel. The code guides patent classification of inventions that use these forged or rolled alloys in producing engine components.
[F02F 3/00] Pistons -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pistons for combustion engines:
[F02F 3/0015] Multi-part pistons -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a multi-part piston assembly comprising a piston head (60) having a cylindrical member (66) that is inserted into an upper cylindrical opening (68) of a piston body (40) and secured via a locking ring (54) to the piston body (40).
[F02F 3/0023] the parts being bolted or screwed together -- F02F 3/0023 classifies pistons for combustion-engine cylinders in which the component parts are joined by bolts or screws, rather than being integral or welded. It falls under the piston subgroup of mechanical-engineering combustion-engine technology.
[F02F 3/003] the parts being connected by casting, brazing, welding or clamping -- F02F 3/003 covers pistons whose parts are joined to other components by casting, brazing, welding or clamping. It falls under the mechanical engineering hierarchy for combustion-engine cylinders and pistons, specifying the method of attachment.
[F02F 3/0069] the crown and skirt being interconnected by the gudgeon pin -- The subclass covers piston designs where the crown and the skirt are linked together by the gudgeon (connecting) pin, forming a unitary structure. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, pistons or casings for combustion engines, specifically pistons.
[F02F 3/0076] the inside of the pistons being provided with ribs or fins -- This classification covers pistons for combustion engines whose interior surfaces are formed with ribs or fins to improve cooling, strengthen the structure, or reduce weight. It applies to piston designs where the inside has a ribbed-or-finned interior rather than a smooth one.
[F02F 3/0084] the pistons being constructed from specific materials -- F02F 3/0084 classifies pistons for combustion-engine cylinders according to the specific materials from which they are made, such as particular metal alloys, composite structures, ceramics or other specialized substances designed to enhance strength, wear resistance, thermal stability or sealing performance.
[F02F 3/0092] the material being steel-plate -- F02F 3/0092 designates pistons for combustion engines that are made from steel-plate, covering the material selection, manufacturing processes, and related features of such pistons. It is nested under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Cylinders, Pistons or Casings for Combustion Engines → Pistons.
[F02F 3/02] having means for accommodating or controlling heat expansion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structural elements in pistons for regulation of heat expansion, e.g. the shape of piston skirt walls or inserts located in the body of pistons.
[F02F 3/022] the pistons having an oval circumference or non-cylindrical shaped skirts, e.g. oval  F02F3/025 ,  F02F3/027  take precedence -- The F02F 3/022 subclass covers pistons whose outer shape is not circular, such as those with an oval circumference or skirts that are non-cylindrical. It includes designs where the piston's skirt deviates from a round profile, and it takes precedence over the related subclasses F02F 3/025 and F02F 3/027.
[F02F 3/025] having circumferentially slotted piston skirts, e.g. T-slots -- A piston design in which the skirt surrounding the piston body includes slots cut around its circumference, often shaped like a "T", to improve lubrication distribution, reduce friction and allow controlled thermal expansion during engine operation. This arrangement is used in combustion-engine cylinders to enhance sealing and durability.
[F02F 3/027] the skirt wall having cavities -- F02F 3/027 covers pistons whose skirt wall incorporates cavities, such as hollow or recessed sections, to improve lubrication, reduce weight, or manage thermal expansion in combustion-engine cylinders.
