[F02F 3/042] the inserts consisting of reinforcements in the skirt interconnecting separate wall parts, e.g. rods or strips -- This CPC subclass covers reinforcement inserts fitted into a piston skirt to interconnect separate wall parts, typically using rods, strips or similar members that increase rigidity and limit deformation of the piston during operation.
[F02F 3/045] the inserts being located in the crown -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.
[F02F 3/047] the inserts being located around the gudgeon pin bearings -- This CPC subclass concerns pistons for internal-combustion engines that incorporate inserts placed around the gudgeon-pin bearings. The inserts typically provide reinforcement, improved bearing support or lubrication to reduce wear and enhance durability of the piston assembly.
[F02F 3/06] the inserts having bimetallic effect -- The code F02F 3/06 classifies piston inserts that combine two different metals in a single component to exploit complementary properties, such as a hard wear-resistant surface bonded to a more ductile core, improving durability and thermal behavior in combustion-engine pistons.
[F02F 3/08] the inserts being ring-shaped -- F02F 3/08 designates pistons that incorporate ring-shaped inserts, typically used as reinforcement, cooling or sealing elements within the piston structure of combustion engines. The classification addresses the geometry and function of these annular components and their integration into the piston design.
[F02F 3/10] having surface coverings -- This subgroup covers pistons for combustion-engine cylinders that incorporate surface coverings such as coatings, claddings or other applied layers. The coverings are intended to enhance wear resistance, reduce friction, improve heat management or provide corrosion protection for the piston's working surfaces.
[F02F 3/105] the coverings forming a double skirt -- The subclass F02F 3/105 covers the coverings that form a double-skirt arrangement on pistons used in combustion-engine cylinders, detailing the design and function of the two concentric sealing or protective layers that encircle the piston skirt.
[F02F 3/12] on piston heads -- F02F 3/12 covers piston heads, the part of a piston that interfaces with the combustion chamber, transferring pressure to the connecting rod and often incorporating features such as sealing rings, cooling galleries, or special surface treatments to improve durability and performance. This subclass addresses design, material and functional aspects of the piston head in internal-combustion engines.
[F02F 3/14] within combustion chambers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.
[F02F 3/16] having cooling means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structural features, e.g. fins, apertures and arrangements for dissipating heat
[F02F 3/18] the means being a liquid or solid coolant, e.g. sodium, in a closed chamber in piston -- F02F 3/18 covers piston designs that incorporate a closed chamber containing a liquid or solid coolant, such as sodium, to absorb heat from the piston during operation. The coolant circulates within the piston to improve temperature control and reduce wear, enhancing engine efficiency and durability.
[F02F 3/20] the means being a fluid flowing through or along piston -- The code covers pistons that incorporate passages, channels or external flow paths allowing a fluid to flow through or along the piston for purposes such as cooling, lubrication, sealing or power transmission in combustion-engine cylinders. It includes designs where the fluid is directed internally through the piston body or along its surface to achieve the desired functional effect.
[F02F 3/22] the fluid being liquid -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Liquid cooling means:
[F02F 3/225] the liquid being directed into blind holes -- F02F 3/225 covers pistons for internal combustion engines in which a liquid such as oil or fuel is directed into blind holes drilled in the piston surface. The liquid is routed into these closed-end cavities to provide cooling, lubrication or improved sealing of the piston.
[F02F 3/24] having means for guiding gases in cylinders, e.g. for guiding scavenging charge in two-stroke engines -- F02F 3/24 covers pistons that incorporate features to direct gases within the cylinder, such as channels or ducts that guide the scavenging charge in two-stroke engines, improving the flow of fresh charge and the removal of exhaust gases.
[F02F 3/26] having combustion chamber in piston head -- This subgroup refers to piston designs where the combustion chamber is formed as part of the piston head rather than in the cylinder liner, allowing compact geometry and improved heat management. It includes features such as recesses, cavities or shaped crowns that contain the fuel-air mixture and flame front. Used in internal-combustion engines to reduce engine height and enhance combustion efficiency.
[F02F 3/28] Other pistons with specially-shaped head -- F02F 3/28 classifies pistons for combustion engines that have a specially shaped head differing from conventional flat or domed designs, intended to improve combustion, sealing or heat transfer. These pistons are used in the cylinders of internal-combustion engines.
[F02F 3/285] the head being provided with an insert located in or on the combustion-gas-swept surface -- This subgroup covers pistons whose heads incorporate an insert positioned in or on the combustion-gas-swept surface, typically to enhance heat transfer, wear resistance or sealing. It refines the classification of cylinders, pistons or casings for combustion engines within mechanical engineering.
[F02F 5/00] Piston rings, e.g. associated with piston crown -- Details of piston rings or related details of the pistons, piston crowns or cylinders are classified in group F16J9/00 .
[F02F 7/00] Casings, e.g. crankcases -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combustion engine casings, e.g. crankcases or frames, e.g. rigid supports for cylinders or crankcases.
[F02F 7/0002] Cylinder arrangements -- F02F 7/0002 covers the arrangement of cylinders within a combustion engine. It includes the spatial layout, number and configuration of cylinder banks as well as their relationship to the engine block or crankcase. The code is used to classify patents that address how cylinders are positioned to optimise performance, balance and packaging.
[F02F 7/0004] Crankcases of one-cylinder engines -- The code F02F 7/0004 classifies crankcases that are specifically designed for single-cylinder internal-combustion engines, providing the structural housing, support and sealing for the piston, cylinder and moving components of such engines.
[F02F 7/0007] Crankcases of engines with cylinders in line -- The CPC subclass F02F 7/0007 covers crankcases used in engines where the cylinders are arranged in a straight line. It pertains to the structural housing that encloses the crankshaft and related components of such linear-cylinder combustion engines.
[F02F 7/0009] Crankcases of opposed piston engines -- F02F 7/0009 refers to crankcases designed for opposed-piston engines, the housing that encloses the pistons, connecting rods and crankshaft in this type of combustion engine. It is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the subcategory of cylinders, pistons or casings.
[F02F 7/0012] Crankcases of V-engines -- F02F 7/0012 refers to crankcases designed for V-type internal combustion engines, covering the housing that supports the crankshaft and related components in that configuration. It is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the subgroup for cylinders, pistons or casings of combustion engines.
[F02F 7/0014] Crankcases of W-, deldic, or quadratic engines, or the like -- The CPC subclass F02F 7/0014 covers crankcase designs for W-type, deldic, quadratic or similar combustion-engine configurations, detailing structural features, materials and mounting methods specific to these non-conventional cylinder arrangements. It is positioned under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and cylinder/piston casings, focusing on the housing that encloses the crankshaft and related components in such engines.
[F02F 7/0017] Crankcases of radial engines -- F02F 7/0017 covers the design and construction of crankcases specifically for radial internal-combustion engines. It includes features such as the housing that supports and aligns the cylinders and the crankshaft in radial-engine configurations.
[F02F 7/0019] Cylinders and crankshaft not in one plane (deaxation) -- F02F 7/0019 covers engine designs in which the cylinders and the crankshaft are arranged out of a single plane, known as de-axial or deaxation configurations. This layout separates the cylinder line from the crankshaft axis, often to improve balance, reduce engine length or allow alternative packaging within the engine block.
[F02F 7/0021] Construction -- F02F 7/0021 covers the construction of engine casings, such as crankcases, used in combustion engines. It includes structural features, material choices, assembly methods and integration of accessories that form the outer shell surrounding the cylinders and pistons. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, cylinders-pistons-casings.
[F02F 7/0024] Casings for larger engines -- This subclass pertains to protective housings that enclose the main components of large internal-combustion engines, such as crankcases, oil-cavity covers and other structural shells. It covers designs and methods for manufacturing and mounting these casings to support bearing loads and isolate vibration.
[F02F 7/0026] Casings for horizontal engines -- This CPC subclass covers protective housings that are specifically designed for horizontally oriented internal-combustion engines, encompassing the external structure that encloses the crankcase and related components. It includes designs that provide support, sealing, mounting and vibration-damping for engine installations where the crankshaft lies in a horizontal plane.
[F02F 7/0029] Space-frames -- Space-frames are lightweight skeletal structures composed of interconnected struts that form a rigid yet low-mass casing, often used to support combustion-engine components or as a vehicle chassis. By distributing loads through the framework, they provide high strength while minimizing material use, which is valuable for engine casings such as crankcases. This classification covers designs where the engine enclosure itself functions as a space-frame.
[F02F 7/0031] Construction kit principle (modular engines) -- F02F 7/0031 covers engine designs that use a construction-kit principle, meaning the engine's major parts such as the crankcase and other casings are provided as modular units that can be assembled in various configurations. The classification applies to mechanical-engineering inventions related to combustion-engine cylinders, pistons or casings that are fabricated to enable interchangeable, interchangeable-component construction.
[F02F 7/0034] Built from sheet material and welded casings -- F02F 7/0034 covers engine casings, such as crankcases, that are fabricated by forming sheet metal and joining the pieces together by welding, rather than by casting or forging. The entry applies to any combustion-engine housing built from welded sheet components.
[F02F 7/0036] Casings for two-stroke engines with scavenging conduits -- This subgroup covers the design of housings that contain two-stroke internal combustion engines and incorporate passages for directing fresh charge and exhaust gases (scavenging conduits). It addresses crankcase or cylinder block structures that integrate these flow channels to improve scavenging efficiency.
[F02F 7/0039] Casings for small engines, especially with crankcase pumps -- F02F 7/0039 relates to casings specifically designed for small internal-combustion engines, especially those that incorporate crankcase pumps for lubrication. The classification covers the integrated crankcase-engine block structures used in lightweight or portable engines where the crankcase also functions as a pump housing.
[F02F 7/0043] Arrangements of mechanical drive elements -- F02F 7/0043 covers the layout and interconnection of mechanical drive elements-gears, shafts, belts, chains or similar devices-inside the casing of a combustion engine, such as a crankcase. It specifies how these power-transmitting components are arranged to transmit motion between the engine's moving parts. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the category of engine cylinders, pistons or casings.
[F02F 7/0046] Shape of casings adapted to facilitate fitting or dismantling of engine parts -- The CPC entry F02F 7/0046 covers casings, such as crankcases, whose shape is specifically designed to make the installation, removal or adjustment of engine components easier. It relates to geometric features that permit smoother fitting or dismantling of pistons, cylinders or other combustion-engine parts.
[F02F 7/0048] Tunnel-type frames -- Tunnel-type frames are structural enclosures that surround the crankcase of a combustion engine, forming a tunnel around the crankshaft and supporting bearings while improving rigidity and protecting moving parts. This design is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, cylinders, pistons or casings for combustion engines, specifically for casings such as crankcases.Tunnel-type frames are structural enclosures that surround the crankcase of a combustion engine, forming a tunnel around the crankshaft and supporting bearings while improving rigidity and protecting moving parts. This design is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, cylinders, pistons or casings for combustion engines, specifically for casings such as crankcases.
[F02F 7/0051] Crankcase pump engines -- Crankcase pump engines are internal combustion engines that use the pressure of the lubricating oil within the crankcase to pump oil directly to the working chambers, eliminating an external oil-pump system. This design is common in two-stroke engines and some four-stroke variants, integrating lubrication and scavenging functions in the engine's casing.
[F02F 7/0053] Crankshaft bearings fitted in the crankcase -- This subclass covers bearing assemblies that are mounted inside the crankcase to support the crankshaft of combustion engines. It includes various bearing types - plain, roller, or hydrodynamic - installed directly on the crankcase housing and their fixation methods.
[F02F 7/0058] Longitudinally or transversely separable crankcases -- This CPC subgroup covers crankcase designs for combustion engines that are built in sections that can be taken apart either along the length of the engine or across its width, facilitating assembly, inspection, or repair. Such separable casings are typically used to simplify manufacturing and service of engines with complex internal components.
[F02F 7/006] Camshaft or pushrod housings -- The CPC class F02F 7/006 covers camshaft or pushrod housings, the structural components that hold camshafts or pushrods inside internal combustion engines, typically integrated with the engine block or crankcase. It belongs to the hierarchy mechanical engineering - combustion engines - cylinders, pistons or casings for combustion engines.
[F02F 7/0065] Shape of casings for other machine parts and purposes, e.g. utilisation purposes, safety -- F02F 7/0065 pertains to the design of casings, such as crankcases, whose shape is tailored for machine parts other than the main combustion components, including forms that serve specific utilisation or safety functions.
[F02F 7/0068] Adaptations for other accessories -- F02F 7/0068 covers adaptations made to engine casings, such as crankcases, to mount or integrate additional accessories. It includes design features that enable the attachment of devices like pumps, generators, or cooling systems directly to the casing of a combustion-engine cylinder or piston assembly.
[F02F 7/007] Adaptations for cooling -- The entry covers features added to engine casings, such as crankcases, that provide cooling by dissipating heat. It includes designs like external fins, internal cooling passages, jackets, or other thermal-management structures integrated into the casing to keep the combustion engine components within safe temperature limits.
[F02F 7/0073] Adaptations for fitting the engine, e.g. front-plates or bell-housings -- F02F 7/0073 covers adaptations for fitting a combustion engine, such as front plates or bell housings, that attach the engine to other parts and protect its casing.
[F02F 7/008] Sound insulation -- The subclass F02F 7/008 of the Cooperative Patent Classification concerns sound-insulation measures for engine casings such as crankcases. It covers devices, materials and structural arrangements that reduce acoustic emission and vibration from combustion-engine cylinders, pistons or related housings.
[F02F 7/0082] Mounting of engine casings -- The entry covers arrangements and devices for attaching or supporting engine casings such as crankcases to a vehicle frame or other structure, including brackets, mounts, clamps and fastening methods used to secure the casing in place.
[F02F 7/0085] Materials for constructing engines or their parts -- F02F 7/0085 designates the types of materials employed in constructing engine casings and related components such as crankcases for combustion engines. It covers material choices that provide durability, heat-resistance and mechanical performance.
[F02F 7/0087] Ceramic materials -- F02F 7/0087 addresses ceramic materials used in the construction of engine casings such as crankcases, offering high temperature resistance, wear protection and lightweight strength for combustion engines.
[F02F 7/0095] Constructing engine casings  welded casings  F02F7/0034 -- The subclass F02F 7/0095 covers methods for constructing engine casings, especially welded casings used in combustion-engine crankcases. It includes techniques for assembling and joining the housing components of pistons, cylinders and related engine parts.
[F02G] HOT GAS OR COMBUSTION-PRODUCT POSITIVE-DISPLACEMENT ENGINE PLANTS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hot-gas or combustion product positive-displacement engine plants and use of waste heat of combustion engines, not otherwise provided for.
[F02G 1/00] Hot gas positive-displacement engine plants -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hot gas positive-displacement engine plants, e.g. Stirling engines.  **Glossary:** - Plant: An engine together with such additional apparatus as is necessary to run the engine. For example, a steam engine plant includes a steam engine and means for generating the steam.
[F02G 1/02] of open-cycle type -- F02G 1/02 refers to hot-gas positive-displacement engine plants of the open-cycle type, where high-temperature combustion gases expand directly to drive a piston or displacer without being recirculated or compressed for reuse. These engines convert the thermal energy of a single-pass flow of combustion products into mechanical work, typical of certain power-generation or propulsion systems that rely on a one-way gas stream.
[F02G 1/04] of closed-cycle type -- F02G 1/04 concerns hot-gas positive-displacement engine plants of the closed-cycle type, where the working gas is confined to a sealed system and recirculated for power generation without direct exhaust to the atmosphere. These engines typically heat a working fluid with combustion or external heat, expand it to produce mechanical work, and then return it to the heating source for repeated cycles.
[F02G 1/043] the engine being operated by expansion and contraction of a mass of working gas which is heated and cooled in one of a plurality of constantly communicating expansible chambers, e.g. Stirling cycle type engines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  "Stirling" type engines.  Stirling engines are divided in three types:  Pairs of sealed pistons in separate cylinders (no displacer).  a. with parallel pistons.  b. with cylinders opposed in line.  c. with cylinders opposed parallel.  Piston - displacer arrangement: piston and displacer in the same  cylinder. Cylinder has a hot end and a cool end:  Piston - displacer arrangement : piston and displacer in separate cylinders:
[F02G 1/0435] the engine being of the free piston type -- F02G 1/0435 covers free-piston engines which are positive-displacement machines that generate power without a conventional crankshaft, using the pistons' motion directly to drive a load or to compress gas.
[F02G 1/044] having at least two working members, e.g. pistons, delivering power output -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Stirling type engine having at least two working members, e.g. pistons, delivering power output. E.g. Vuilleumier cycle:
[F02G 1/0445] Engine plants with combined cycles, e.g. Vuilleumier -- Engine plants that employ combined thermodynamic cycles, such as the Vuilleumier system, integrate multiple heat-transfer processes to generate power or refrigeration from hot gas or combustion products. The design uses a positive-displacement mechanism to transfer energy between cycles, improving overall efficiency compared with single-cycle engines. This classification covers all such hybrid engine configurations within the hot-gas positive-displacement engine family.
[F02G 1/045] Controlling -- The F02G 1/045 "Controlling" subgroup covers methods and devices for regulating the operation of hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, such as controlling fuel or air flow, pressure, speed, temperature or load to achieve desired performance, efficiency, or safety. It includes control systems, sensors and actuators used in these engines.
[F02G 1/047] by varying the heating or cooling -- F02G 1/047 designates hot-gas positive-displacement engine plants in which the engine output is regulated by varying the heating or cooling of the working gas. The subclass includes any method that changes temperature to modify displacement volume, pressure or power output, covering both heating and cooling adjustments during operation.
[F02G 1/05] by varying the rate of flow or quantity of the working gas -- F02G 1/05 covers hot-gas positive-displacement engine plants that regulate power output by varying the flow rate or quantity of the working gas supplied to the engine. This includes devices that use throttling, variable inlet valves or adjustable chambers to change the amount of hot gas driving the piston or rotor, thereby controlling speed or torque.
[F02G 1/053] Component parts or details -- This subclass covers the design, arrangement and specific features of individual components and subassemblies used in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, such as pistons, valves, rotors and seals, as well as detailed modifications and refinements of those parts.
[F02G 1/0535] Seals or sealing arrangements -- This subgroup deals with sealing mechanisms employed in hot-gas positive-displacement engine plants, focusing on designs and materials that prevent gas leakage between moving parts. It covers seals for pistons, cylinders and other high-temperature interfaces.
[F02G 1/055] Heaters or coolers -- Heaters or coolers for hot-gas positive-displacement engines are devices that raise or lower the temperature of the working gas before, during, or after compression and expansion. Typical examples include pre-heaters, intercoolers, after-coolers and regenerative heat exchangers used to improve efficiency, control emissions and protect engine components.
[F02G 1/057] Regenerators -- Regenerators are heat-exchange components that temporarily store thermal energy from exhaust gases and release it back to the working fluid, thereby improving the efficiency of hot-gas positive-displacement engine plants such as Stirling or gas-turbine cycles. They typically consist of a porous matrix that alternately absorbs and gives off heat as the gas flows through, reducing fuel consumption and temperature gradients.
[F02G 1/06] Controlling -- F02G 1/06 pertains to the control of hot-gas positive-displacement engine plants, covering devices, circuits and strategies that regulate operating parameters such as pressure, flow, temperature and speed to ensure stable performance, efficiency and safety of combustion-engine systems.
[F02G 2242/00] Ericsson-type engines having open regenerative cycles controlled by valves -- The class F02G 2242/00 covers Ericsson-type positive-displacement engines that use an open regenerative cycle, where heat stored in a regenerator is transferred between the expansion and compression phases, and the cycle is regulated by the timing of inlet and exhaust valves.
[F02G 2242/02] Displacer-type engines -- F02G 2242/02 classifies displacer-type engines, a form of Ericsson-type hot-gas positive-displacement engines that operate with an open regenerative cycle controlled by valves.
[F02G 2242/04] having constant working volume -- F02G 2242/04 defines Ericsson-type hot-gas engines that use an open regenerative cycle controlled by valves and operate with a constant working volume throughout the power stroke, a feature of positive-displacement engine plants within the broader mechanical engineering and combustion-engine categories.
[F02G 2242/06] with external drive displacers -- The CPC subclass F02G 2242/06 covers Ericsson-type positive-displacement engines that operate on an open regenerative cycle controlled by valves and employ external-drive mechanisms to move the displacer elements. It applies to hot-gas or combustion-product engine plants where the power-transmitting motion is provided by a separate drive system rather than direct expansion of the working gas.
[F02G 2242/08] having gas actuated valves, e.g. "Bush engines" -- The subclass F02G 2242/08 refers to combustion-engine designs that use gas-actuated valves for controlling the engine cycle, such as the "Bush" or Ericsson-type engines with open regenerative cycles. These valves are driven by pressure differences in the working gas rather than mechanical linkages, allowing valve timing to be directly linked to combustion-product flow. This feature distinguishes them from conventional mechanically-operated valve systems.
[F02G 2242/10] having mechanically actuated valves, e.g. "Gifford" or "McMahon engines" -- F02G 2242/10 covers Ericsson-type engines that use mechanically actuated valves to control an open regenerative cycle, exemplified by Gifford and McMahon engines. The code classifies positive-displacement plants where valve timing is provided by mechanical linkage rather than fluid or electronic means.
[F02G 2242/30] having variable working volume -- The F02G 2242/30 entry concerns engine designs in which the working volume of the combustion chamber can be altered during operation. It applies to Ericsson-type positive-displacement engines that employ open regenerative cycles regulated by valves, allowing the space available for the working gas to vary to improve performance and efficiency.
[F02G 2242/32] Regenerative displacers with independent pistons -- The classification covers Ericsson-type positive-displacement engines that use an open regenerative cycle where the working gas passes through a regenerator, with the displacement mechanism provided by pistons that move independently of each other. It applies to designs where valve timing controls the flow and the pistons are not mechanically linked, allowing separate power strokes.
[F02G 2242/40] Piston-type engines -- F02G 2242/40 classifies piston-type engines that employ an Ericsson-type open regenerative cycle, where valves control the flow of hot gases or combustion products to achieve positive displacement. The category covers mechanical engineering designs that use regenerative heat exchange to improve efficiency in combustion-engine plants.
[F02G 2242/42] having a single piston regenerative displacer attached to the piston, e.g. "Gifford-McMahon" engines -- This CPC subclass covers Ericsson-type hot-gas engines that use an open regenerative cycle controlled by valves and feature a single piston regenerative displacer attached directly to the piston, exemplified by Gifford-McMahon engines.
[F02G 2242/44] having two pistons and reverse flow regenerators -- F02G 2242/44 relates to Ericsson-type positive-displacement engines that use two pistons and incorporate reverse-flow regenerators to recover heat from the combustion products. The configuration combines an open regenerative cycle with dual pistons to improve thermal efficiency in hot-gas engine plants.
[F02G 2243/00] Stirling type engines having closed regenerative thermodynamic cycles with flow controlled by volume changes -- F02G 2243/00 refers to Stirling-type engines that run on a closed regenerative thermodynamic cycle, where the flow of the working gas is produced by the engine's own volume changes rather than by external pumps, classifying them as positive-displacement combustion-engine plants.
[F02G 2243/02] having pistons and displacers in the same cylinder -- In the CPC, code F02G 2243/02 covers Stirling-type engines that run on a closed regenerative thermodynamic cycle with the piston and displacer occupying the same cylinder. The sub-class is nested under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants.
[F02G 2243/04] Crank-connecting-rod drives -- F02G 2243/04 refers to crank-connecting-rod drive arrangements that convert the linear motion of pistons into rotary motion in Stirling-type engines operating on closed regenerative thermodynamic cycles with flow controlled by volume changes. These mechanisms are part of the broader category of hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants within mechanical engineering.
[F02G 2243/06] Regenerative displacers -- The CPC entry F02G 2243/06 titled Regenerative displacers falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, specifically Stirling-type engines that operate with closed regenerative thermodynamic cycles. It covers components that convert volume changes into flow control, enabling heat-exchange regeneration within the engine.
[F02G 2243/08] External regenerators, e.g. "Rankine Napier" engines -- The entry covers external regenerative devices, such as those used in Rankine-Napier engines, that store and release heat outside the working-fluid circuit to improve efficiency. It applies to Stirling-type engines with closed regenerative cycles where fluid flow is controlled by volume changes.
[F02G 2243/20] each having a single free piston, e.g. "Beale engines" -- F02G 2243/20 is a CPC subclass under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants → Stirling-type engines with closed regenerative cycles whose flow is controlled by volume changes, and it specifically covers Stirling engines that employ a single free piston, for example the Beale engine.
[F02G 2243/202] resonant -- It covers Stirling-type engines that use resonant oscillations of the working gas within a closed regenerative cycle, employing volume-change-controlled flow to transfer heat and produce power.
[F02G 2243/204] non-resonant -- The F02G 2243/204 subclass covers Stirling-type engines that operate on a closed regenerative thermodynamic cycle where the working-fluid flow is managed by volume changes rather than acoustic resonance. It includes non-resonant designs of positive-displacement engine plants that convert heat from combustion or hot gas sources into mechanical work.
[F02G 2243/206] externally excited -- The subclass F02G 2243/206 pertains to Stirling-type engines that operate with external excitation, meaning the working fluid is heated by an external source rather than internal combustion. It covers closed regenerative thermodynamic cycles in which volume changes control the fluid flow within the engine plant.
[F02G 2243/22] with oscillating cylinders -- F02G 2243/22 covers Stirling engines that use oscillating cylinders as part of a closed regenerative cycle, where the cylinder motion controls the working-fluid flow by periodic volume changes. The oscillation replaces conventional piston motion to produce power from heat sources within hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants.
[F02G 2243/24] with free displacers -- F02G 2243/24 pertains to Stirling-type engines that run in a closed regenerative thermodynamic cycle and employ free displacers-components that move without a mechanical link to the piston-to control the flow of the working gas. The entry is situated under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, specifying engines where volume changes govern gas movement.
[F02G 2243/30] having their pistons and displacers each in separate cylinders -- F02G 2243/30 covers Stirling-type engines that use a closed regenerative cycle in which the piston and the displacer are mounted in separate cylinders. This arrangement keeps the working gas isolated between the two chambers while the pistons and displacers move independently to convert heat into mechanical work. It falls under hot-gas positive-displacement engine plants in mechanical engineering.
[F02G 2243/32] Regenerative displacers having parallel cylinder, e.g. "Lauberau" or "Schwartzkopff" engines -- The classification covers Stirling-type closed-cycle engines that use regenerative displacers arranged in parallel cylinders, exemplified by the Lauberau and Schwartzkopff designs, where the working gas flow is regulated by volume changes of the displacer. These engines belong to the hot-gas positive-displacement plant group within combustion-engine technology.
[F02G 2243/34] Regenerative displacers having their cylinders at right angle, e.g. "Robinson" engines -- A regenerative displacer engine in which the cylinder axes are positioned at right angles, as exemplified by Robinson-type Stirling engines. The design uses a closed regenerative thermodynamic cycle where the volume change of the displacer controls flow, allowing efficient conversion of heat from combustion or external sources into mechanical work.
[F02G 2243/36] with twin-expansion cylinders, e.g. "Rainbow" engines -- A Stirling-type hot-gas engine that uses a closed regenerative thermodynamic cycle with flow controlled by volume changes, featuring twin-expansion cylinders often marketed as "Rainbow" engines. This subcategory falls under mechanical engineering for combustion engines, specifically hot-gas positive-displacement engine plants.
[F02G 2243/38] External regenerators having parallel cylinders, e.g. "Heinrici" engines -- The code F02G 2243/38 designates external heat regenerators that employ parallel cylinders, as found in Stirling-type engines with closed regenerative cycles such as Heinrici engines. These components are used in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants to store and release thermal energy, improving efficiency by alternating heating and cooling of the gas within the parallel cylinder arrangement.
[F02G 2243/40] with free displacers -- The subclass F02G 2243/40 pertains to Stirling-type engines that use a closed regenerative thermodynamic cycle in which the flow of the working gas is governed by a free-moving displacer rather than a piston, allowing positive-displacement of combustion-product gases.
[F02G 2243/50] having resonance tubes -- This subclass relates to Stirling-type closed regenerative engines whose working-fluid flow is controlled by volume changes and that incorporate resonance tubes as part of the cycle. The resonance tubes create standing acoustic waves that improve heat transfer or pressure oscillations, thereby enhancing the engine's efficiency or power output.
[F02G 2243/52] acoustic -- The CPC subclass F02G 2243/52 pertains to acoustic aspects of Stirling-type engines that operate on a closed regenerative thermodynamic cycle with flow controlled by volume changes. It includes inventions dealing with sound generation, transmission, detection, or noise mitigation in such hot-gas positive-displacement engine plants.
[F02G 2243/54] thermo-acoustic -- The code F02G 2243/54 classifies Stirling-type engines that operate on a closed regenerative thermodynamic cycle in which fluid flow is managed by volume changes, specifically the thermo-acoustic variant that uses acoustic oscillations to drive heat exchange and power production. This falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants.
[F02G 2244/00] Machines having two pistons -- It covers machines in the combustion-engine field that employ a pair of pistons within each cylinder, such as opposed-piston or twin-piston designs, where the pistons move toward each other to compress and expand the working gas, improving scavenging and efficiency.
[F02G 2244/02] Single-acting two piston engines -- The CPC entry F02G 2244/02 covers single-acting engines that use two pistons, classed under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants → machines having two pistons. It specifically addresses designs where each piston performs work on only one side of the cylinder.
[F02G 2244/04] of rotary cylinder type, e.g. "Finkelstein" engines -- F02G 2244/04 covers two-piston engines of the rotary-cylinder type, known as "Finkelstein" engines, which belong to the positive-displacement engine group that uses hot gas or combustion-product pressure to drive motion. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and hot-gas-product engine plants.
[F02G 2244/06] of stationary cylinder type -- The CPC entry F02G 2244/06 covers stationary cylinder-type machines with two pistons that belong to hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, a class of combustion engines where the pistons move within a fixed cylinder to convert gas pressure into mechanical work.
[F02G 2244/08] having parallel cylinder, e.g. "Rider" engines -- This subclass covers two-piston engines in which the pistons move within parallel cylinders, a configuration typical of so-called Rider engines. The design falls under positive-displacement hot-gas engine plants and is used in combustion engine applications.
[F02G 2244/10] having cylinders in V-arrangement -- It covers positive-displacement engine plants that use two pistons arranged in a V-shaped cylinder configuration, a common layout for compact, high-power combustion engines within the broader mechanical-engineering and combustion-engine categories.
[F02G 2244/12] having opposed pistons -- The subgroup F02G 2244/12 covers engines in which two pistons are mounted opposite one another within a single cylinder and move toward and away from each other during operation, a layout used in positive-displacement hot-gas or combustion-product engine plants.
[F02G 2244/50] Double acting piston machines -- The entry classifies double-acting piston machines, in which two pistons share a cylinder and each performs work during opposite strokes as hot-gas or combustion-product pressure expands. These engines belong to the positive-displacement family of combustion engines used for mechanical power generation.
[F02G 2244/52] having interconnecting adjacent cylinders constituting a single system, e.g. "Rinia" engines -- F02G 2244/52 covers hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engines that use two pistons arranged in adjacent cylinders that are mechanically linked to operate as a single system, exemplified by "Rinia" engines. This subclass falls within the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and positive-displacement engine plants.
[F02G 2244/54] having two-cylinder twin systems, with compression in one cylinder and expansion in the other cylinder for each of the twin systems, e.g. "Finkelstein" engines -- The CPC subclass F02G 2244/54 pertains to hot-gas engine plants that employ a twin-cylinder arrangement in which each pair contains one cylinder dedicated to compression and the other to expansion, a principle exemplified by Finkelstein-type engines.
[F02G 2250/00] Special cycles or special engines -- The CPC entry F02G 2250/00 covers special cycles or special engines within hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants. It groups non-standard thermodynamic cycles, unconventional engine configurations and novel operating principles that differ from traditional internal-combustion or gas-turbine cycles. This subclass lies under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and focuses on innovative cycle designs and specialised engine types.
[F02G 2250/03] Brayton cycles -- F02G 2250/03 covers Brayton cycles, a thermodynamic process in which air is compressed, heated at constant pressure and then expanded to generate work, commonly used in gas-turbine and jet-engine systems. It is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, hot gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, and special cycles or special engines.
[F02G 2250/06] Beau de Rochas constant volume cycles -- The entry F02G 2250/06 identifies Beau de Rochas constant-volume cycles, a class of internal-combustion engine cycles in which heat is added to the working gas while its volume remains essentially fixed, a principle that underlies the Otto cycle. It is classified under hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, specifically special cycles or special engines.
[F02G 2250/09] Carnot cycles in general -- The entry F02G 2250/09 covers the theoretical study of Carnot cycles as applied to hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, addressing reversible heat-transfer processes, temperature limits and the ideal efficiency of these special-cycle engines.
[F02G 2250/12] Malone liquid thermal cycles -- The CPC subclass F02G 2250/12 covers Malone liquid thermal cycles, a type of closed-loop heat engine that uses a liquid working fluid undergoing phase change to convert heat into mechanical work, often applied in specialized power-generation or waste-heat recovery systems.
[F02G 2250/15] Sabathe mixed air cycles -- Sabathe mixed air cycles are a special class of positive-displacement engine plants that combine a mixed-air working fluid with a closed-cycle heat-exchange system. The cycle typically uses external heat sources and a regenerator to achieve high efficiency in compact combustion-engine designs.
[F02G 2250/18] Vuilleumier cycles -- F02G 2250/18 covers Vuilleumier cycles, a class of thermodynamic cycles that use temperature differences between heat sources to move a sealed piston and generate mechanical work or refrigeration without a conventional compressor. These cycles are employed in low-temperature heat-recovery and cryogenic cooling systems and fall under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and special-cycle engine plants.
[F02G 2250/21] Cooke Yarborough engines -- Cooke-Yarborough engines are a type of hot-gas, positive-displacement engine that employs a special combustion cycle distinct from conventional pistons or turbines. They belong to the subcategory of combustion-engine plants with unique cycle designs, used where compact size or specific power-delivery characteristics are required. The code F02G 2250/21 classifies these engines within mechanical engineering's combustion-engine hierarchy.
[F02G 2250/24] Ringbom engines, the displacement of the free displacer being obtained by expansion of the heated gas and the weight of the piston -- F02G 2250/24 designates Ringbom engines, a subset of hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants where the free displacer's motion is driven by the expansion of heated gas acting against the weight of the piston, classified under special cycles of combustion engines.
[F02G 2250/27] Martini Stirling engines
[F02G 2250/31] Nano- or microengines -- F02G 2250/31 covers nano- or microengines, which are extremely small positive-displacement combustion engines operating on special cycles. These devices generate mechanical power at micrometer to millimeter scales for applications such as micro-propulsion, sensing, or energy harvesting. They are classified under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, hot-gas engine plants.
[F02G 2253/00] Seals -- F02G 2253/00 covers seals employed in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, such as turbines and piston-type engines. These devices isolate high-temperature combustion gases from surrounding components, maintain pressure differentials, limit leakage and protect moving parts, and are commonly made from metal, ceramic or heat-resistant elastomers designed for extreme thermal and mechanical stresses.
[F02G 2253/01] Rotary piston seals -- Rotary piston seals are dynamic sealing elements that rotate with a piston in positive-displacement engines using hot gas or combustion-product cycles. They maintain a gas-tight interface between the moving piston and the surrounding cylinder, preventing leakage of combustion gases and retaining lubricating oil during operation.
[F02G 2253/02] Reciprocating piston seals -- F02G 2253/02 classifies seals specifically designed for reciprocating pistons in positive-displacement engines that operate with hot gases or combustion products. The entry covers designs that prevent gas, exhaust, or lubricant leakage between the piston and cylinder walls, improving engine efficiency and durability. It falls under the CPC hierarchy Mechanical Engineering - Combustion Engines - Hot gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants - Seals.
[F02G 2253/03] Stem seals -- F02G 2253/03 designates seals that are applied to stems, such as piston rods or valve stems, in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants. These stem seals prevent leakage of gases, liquids or combustion products while allowing the stem to move, and are used in engines where high-temperature, high-pressure conditions prevail.
[F02G 2253/04] Displacer seals -- F02G 2253/04 displacer seals are sealing components employed in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, such as hot-gas engines, to prevent leakage of working fluid while allowing the piston's or displacer's reciprocating motion. They are designed to withstand high temperatures and pressures typical of combustion-engine operation.
[F02G 2253/06] Bellow seals -- F02G 2253/06 covers bellow seals used in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants such as turbochargers and superchargers, where flexible metal or composite bellows provide a leak-tight connection while accommodating thermal expansion and vibration. The classification groups these seals with other combustion-engine sealing technologies that must endure high temperatures and cyclic pressures.
[F02G 2253/08] Stem with rolling membranes -- The CPC entry F02G 2253/08 covers stems equipped with rolling membrane seals, which provide a tight, low-friction barrier between moving parts in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine components. The rolling membrane, typically a flexible film that continuously contacts the cylinder wall, prevents leakage of gases or fluids while allowing axial movement of the stem. This design is used in engines where high-temperature and pressure sealing is critical.
[F02G 2253/10] Piston with rolling membranes -- The F02G 2253/10 subclass covers pistons that incorporate rolling membrane seals instead of conventional rings, providing a continuous flexible barrier against high-temperature gases in combustion-engine cylinders. Rolling membranes conform to piston movement, improve sealing reliability and reduce wear in hot-gas positive-displacement engines.
[F02G 2253/50] Liquid seals -- Liquid seals are sealing arrangements that employ a film of liquid to prevent leakage between moving components in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, such as combustion engines. They maintain pressure boundaries, minimise wear and often provide lubrication for the sealed parts.
[F02G 2253/60] Sealing of the lubrication circuit -- This subclass covers devices, arrangements and methods for sealing the lubrication circuit of hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, ensuring oil flow while preventing leaks and protecting the system from high-temperature gases and contaminants.
[F02G 2253/80] Sealing of the crankcase -- F02G 2253/80 concerns the design and implementation of sealing solutions for the crankcase of hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants. It includes seals, gaskets, O-rings and related methods that prevent leakage of oil, gases or combustion products from the crankcase.
[F02G 2254/00] Heat inputs -- F02G 2254/00 deals with the heat input to hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, describing the amount, rate and method of thermal energy supplied to these engines. It falls under mechanical engineering and combustion engines, focusing on how fuel or heat is introduced to drive the engine cycle.
[F02G 2254/05] by air -- F02G 2254/05 falls under the CPC section for hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants and specifically relates to the heat-input side of such engines. The code designates engines whose working fluid is heated by air, i.e., the combustion-product is pre-heated or ignited using air as the heat source.
[F02G 2254/10] by burners -- F02G 2254/10 classifies the method of supplying heat to hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants using burners. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, heat inputs, and covers burner-based energy delivery to such engines.
[F02G 2254/11] Catalytic burners -- This CPC code F02G 2254/11 covers catalytic burners, which are combustion devices that use a catalyst to facilitate oxidation of fuel at lower temperatures, improving efficiency and reducing emissions. They are employed in industrial heating, power generation and other processes requiring controlled heat input from combustion.
[F02G 2254/12] by ejectors -- F02G 2254/12 pertains to hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants that receive their heat input through ejectors. The subclass covers configurations where an ejector, using a high-pressure motive fluid, entrains and compresses secondary flow to provide the required combustion heat. It is situated within the mechanical-engineering branch of the CPC.
[F02G 2254/15] by exhaust gas -- F02G 2254/15 relates to positive-displacement engine plants that obtain their heat input from exhaust gas, outlining designs where the engine's own combustion products are recycled as the energy source for operation. This falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion engines and hot-gas engine technologies.
[F02G 2254/18] using deflectors, e.g. spirals -- The CPC subclass F02G 2254/18 covers methods that employ deflectors-such as spiral-shaped elements-to direct or shape the flow of hot gases or combustion products within positive-displacement engine plants, thereby influencing heat input and improving performance or efficiency. This belongs to the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants.
[F02G 2254/20] using heat transfer tubes -- This subclass concerns positive-displacement engine plants that employ heat-transfer tubes to convey heat from combustion gases or hot gases to a working fluid, thereby providing the heat input for the engine. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants → heat inputs.
[F02G 2254/30] using solar radiation -- F02G 2254/30 classifies engine systems that obtain their heat energy from solar radiation to drive hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engines, such as Stirling or internal-combustion engines adapted for solar heating. It covers designs, methods, and components that capture, concentrate, or otherwise use solar energy as the primary heat source for such engines.
[F02G 2254/40] using heat accumulators -- F02G 2254/40 covers the use of heat accumulators to store and release thermal energy in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants. The classification applies to systems that buffer heat between the combustion process and the engine to improve efficiency or control temperature.
[F02G 2254/45] by electric heating -- F02G 2254/45 concerns hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants in which the heat input is provided by electric heating. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for combustion engines, specifically addressing the method of supplying thermal energy to drive the engine cycle.
[F02G 2254/50] Dome arrangements for heat input -- F02G 2254/50 covers the design and placement of domes that direct heat into the combustion or hot-gas engine. It includes configurations of dome shape, orientation, and integration with the heat-input system to improve efficiency or control of the combustion process.
[F02G 2254/60] using air preheaters -- F02G 2254/60 covers technologies that utilize air preheaters to raise the temperature of combustion air before it enters a hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine, such as certain steam-turbine or gas-turbine plants. This sub-class falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the broader category of heat inputs.
[F02G 2254/70] by catalytic conversion, i.e. flameless oxydation -- F02G 2254/70 covers combustion-engine plants that obtain heat by catalytic conversion, commonly referred to as flameless oxidation, rather than by direct flame combustion. It classifies engines in which the fuel is oxidised over a catalyst, producing high-temperature gases for power generation without a visible flame.
[F02G 2254/90] by radioactivity -- The CPC subclass F02G 2254/90 covers hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants that obtain their heat input from radioactive sources, i.e., engines that are powered by the heat generated through radioactive decay rather than conventional fuel combustion.
[F02G 2255/00] Heater tubes -- Heater tubes are components installed in the combustion chamber or exhaust of positive-displacement engines to pre-heat incoming air or fuel, improving start-up and efficiency. In the CPC they fall under F02G 2255/00 within mechanical engineering, combustion engines, hot-gas engine plants.
[F02G 2255/10] dome shaped -- This subclass covers heater tubes that are formed with a dome-shaped cross-section, used in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants such as diesel or gas-turbine engines. The domed geometry is intended to enhance heat-transfer efficiency, resist pressure loads and improve durability of the heating element.
[F02G 2255/20] Heater fins -- The F02G 2255/20 subclass defines heater fins that are mounted on or integrated with heater tubes of hot-gas or combustion-product engine plants. These fins increase the surface area exposed to the gas flow, enhancing heat transfer from the combustion products to the surrounding coolant or fluid. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Positive-displacement engine plants → Heater tubes.
[F02G 2256/00] Coolers -- The CPC entry F02G 2256/00 titled Coolers falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, hot gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants. It covers devices that lower the temperature of gases or combustion products after they leave the engine, such as heat exchangers, radiators, and cooling loops integrated with positive-displacement engine systems.
[F02G 2256/02] Cooler fins -- The F02G 2256/02 subclass concerns cooler fins used in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants. It covers the design, geometry, material selection and mounting of finned surfaces that increase heat-transfer area on engine coolers, helping to dissipate combustion-generated heat and maintain operating temperatures.
[F02G 2256/04] Cooler tubes -- The CPC class F02G 2256/04 covers cooler tubes, the components that form the heat-exchange passages within coolers for hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants. These tubes are designed to remove heat from the working fluid before it enters or after it leaves the engine, improving efficiency and protecting downstream equipment.
[F02G 2256/50] with coolant circulation -- This CPC group pertains to cooling systems for hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants in which a fluid is actively circulated to absorb and remove heat from the engine or exhaust.
[F02G 2257/00] Regenerators -- A regenerator is a heat-exchange device that temporarily stores thermal energy from the hot exhaust gases of a positive-displacement combustion-engine plant and returns it to the incoming fresh charge, improving cycle efficiency and reducing fuel consumption. This CPC entry covers designs, configurations and control methods for such regenerators used in hot-gas engine systems.
[F02G 2257/02] rotating -- The CPC subclass F02G 2257/02 covers rotating regenerators, which are heat-exchange devices that rotate within the flow of hot gases from combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants. These rotating units continuously transfer heat from the exhaust stream to the incoming working fluid, enhancing the thermal efficiency of engines such as gas turbines and hot-gas engines.
[F02G 2258/00] Materials used -- The F02G 2258/00 class covers the selection and application of materials in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants. It includes metals, alloys, ceramics, composites and other substances used for components such as turbines, compressors, rotors and housings, focusing on properties that affect performance, durability and resistance to high temperature and pressure.
[F02G 2258/10] ceramic -- This CPC subclass (F02G 2258/10) covers the use of ceramic materials in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, such as turbines, compressors and internal-combustion engines. It includes components and structures where ceramics provide high temperature resistance, wear resistance or thermal insulation.
[F02G 2258/20] having heat insulating properties -- F02G 2258/20 designates materials used in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants that possess heat-insulating properties, helping to limit heat loss, protect components and improve overall engine efficiency. It falls under the CPC hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Hot Gas or Combustion-Product Positive-Displacement Engine Plants.
[F02G 2258/50] having frictional properties -- F02G 2258/50 designates engine components or materials selected for their frictional properties in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants. It falls under the combustion-engine branch of mechanical engineering and addresses how friction influences performance, wear, and efficiency of such engines.
[F02G 2258/80] having magnetic properties -- F02G 2258/80 covers hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants that incorporate components with magnetic properties, such as magnetic seals, sensors or actuators, to control fuel, air or exhaust flow within the engine.
[F02G 2258/90] Processing of materials -- The code covers methods and apparatus for treating, separating, recovering, conditioning or otherwise handling solid, liquid or gaseous materials within hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, such as exhaust-gas recirculation, after-burner treatment, waste-heat recovery, or material cleaning and conversion integrated into the engine system.
[F02G 2260/00] Recuperating heat from exhaust gases of combustion engines and heat from cooling circuits -- F02G 2260/00 covers technologies that recover waste heat from the exhaust gases of combustion engines and from their cooling systems, typically using heat exchangers or waste-heat recovery units to improve overall efficiency. This sub-class falls under hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants within the mechanical engineering field.
[F02G 2262/00] Recuperating heat from exhaust gases of combustion engines and heat from lubrication circuits -- F02G 2262/00 covers technologies that capture waste heat from the exhaust stream of internal-combustion engines and from their lubrication circuits, converting it into useful energy or reducing thermal losses. It includes heat-exchanger, recuperator and heat-recovery arrangements integrated with the engine or auxiliary components to improve overall efficiency.
[F02G 2270/00] Constructional features -- The CPC class F02G 2270/00 covers the structural design of positive-displacement engine plants that use hot gases or combustion products to produce power. It includes patents describing housings, casings, shaft arrangements, bearing supports, material choices and other constructional features of such engines.
[F02G 2270/005] Shells, e.g. a sealed or sealing shell for a Stirling engine -- The classification F02G 2270/005 covers protective shells for Stirling engines, such as sealed or sealing enclosures that retain the working gas and provide structural support. It applies to designs that form a continuous barrier around the hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plant to maintain pressure integrity and isolate the engine from its environment.
[F02G 2270/02] Pistons for reciprocating and rotating -- This CPC subclass, F02G 2270/02, covers pistons used in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, including both reciprocating and rotating types, and addresses their constructional features such as shape, materials, and sealing arrangements. It is nested under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, hot-gas engine plants, and constructional features.
[F02G 2270/04] Roller assemblies connecting opposed pistons -- This subclass covers mechanical arrangements where roller assemblies are employed to link pistons that move toward each other within a hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine. The focus is on the design and integration of roller bearings that transmit force between opposed pistons, improving alignment and reducing friction.
[F02G 2270/10] Rotary pistons -- F02G 2270/10 denotes the constructional features of rotary pistons used in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, such as rotary-type internal-combustion engines. The subclass covers design aspects, geometry, mounting and sealing of pistons that rotate rather than reciprocate, facilitating power conversion from expanding gases.
[F02G 2270/15] Rotating cylinders -- The entry relates to design aspects of positive-displacement engines that use rotating cylinders to compress and expand hot gases. It includes configurations where the cylinder block or individual cylinders rotate relative to the crankcase or housing, such as rotary cylinder banks or wobble-plate arrangements. The classification is used for patents concerning the constructional features of such rotating-cylinder mechanisms.
[F02G 2270/20] Plural piston swash plates -- F02G 2270/20 refers to plural piston swash plates, mechanical components that link multiple pistons to a common swash plate in positive-displacement hot-gas or combustion-product engine plants, converting the pistons' reciprocating motion into rotary motion for power transmission.
[F02G 2270/30] Displacer assemblies -- The subclass F02G 2270/30 covers the design and integration of displacer elements in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, such as pistons, rods or other mechanisms that shift the working fluid to generate power. It includes constructional features that position, mount and connect these moving components within the engine structure.
[F02G 2270/40] Piston assemblies -- F02G 2270/40 covers piston assemblies used in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, such as diesel or gasoline engines, detailing their constructional features and integration with the crankshaft, connecting rods and cylinder heads. The code falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and hot-gas engine plants.
[F02G 2270/42] Displacer drives -- Displacer drives are mechanisms that convert the pressure of hot gases or combustion products into linear or rotary motion to operate a positive-displacement engine. They typically use a moving plate or piston that alternately seals and releases chambers, allowing the high-pressure gas to push the displacer and generate power in engines such as hot-gas or steam cycles.
[F02G 2270/425] the displacer being driven by a four-bar mechanism, e.g. a rhombic mechanism -- This CPC entry covers the constructional feature of a hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plant in which the displacer is driven by a four-bar linkage, such as a rhombic mechanism. It is placed under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → hot-gas positive-displacement engine plants.
[F02G 2270/45] Piston rods -- F02G 2270/45 relates to piston rods, the structural elements that link pistons to connecting rods in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants. It addresses constructional features such as material choice, geometric design, mounting methods and dimensional characteristics that affect performance and durability.
[F02G 2270/50] Crosshead guiding pistons -- Crosshead guiding pistons are structural elements used in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, such as large diesel or marine engines, to guide the crosshead and keep the piston rod aligned with the cylinder axis. They reduce lateral wear, improve sealing and support the reciprocating assembly during high-pressure power strokes.
[F02G 2270/55] Cylinders -- The CPC entry F02G 2270/55 concerns the constructional features of cylinders used in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, i.e., the cylinder blocks, liners, cooling passages and related structural elements of combustion-engine cylinders. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion engines, and addresses the design, material selection, sealing and mounting aspects that affect engine performance and durability.
[F02G 2270/60] Counterweights for pistons -- The CPC subclass F02G 2270/60 covers counterweights attached to pistons in hot gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants. These counterweights balance reciprocating masses, reduce vibration and improve engine durability and performance. The subclass is nested under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and constructional features.
[F02G 2270/70] Liquid pistons -- Liquid pistons are constructional features of hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, used in engines where the working fluid directly drives the piston without a solid cylinder liner. They replace traditional metal pistons with a sealed liquid column that transmits force, improving sealing and reducing wear in high-temperature combustion environments.
[F02G 2270/80] Engines without crankshafts -- F02G 2270/80 covers engine designs that generate power without using a crankshaft to convert reciprocating motion to rotation. Such engines rely on alternative mechanisms like linear pistons, oscillating diaphragms or electromagnetic drives to produce useful work. The classification pertains to hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants.
[F02G 2270/85] Crankshafts -- F02G 2270/85 designates the crankshaft component of positive-displacement engines that convert the reciprocating motion of pistons into rotating output. It covers the design, material selection, geometry, balancing, mounting and related manufacturing aspects of crankshafts for hot-gas or combustion-product engine plants.
[F02G 2270/90] Valves -- F02G 2270/90 covers the constructional features of valves used in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, such as those found in turbo-charged or super-charged combustion engines. The classification addresses the design, mounting, and sealing elements that enable the valve to control the flow of high-temperature gases within the engine's power-train system.
[F02G 2270/95] Pressurised crankcases -- F02G 2270/95 covers constructional features of pressurised crankcases in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, a subclass of mechanical-engineering patents dealing with combustion-engine design. It pertains to crankcase designs that maintain internal pressure to improve sealing, lubrication or power transmission in such engines.
[F02G 2275/00] Controls -- The F02G 2275/00 entry covers control systems for hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, such as piston or rotary engines that are driven by high-temperature gases. It includes devices, methods and monitoring techniques used to regulate speed, power output, fuel feed and safety functions of these engines.
[F02G 2275/10] for vibration reduction -- F02G 2275/10 covers control measures designed to reduce vibration in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants such as turbines and compressors. The entry includes mechanisms, strategies or system adjustments that mitigate vibratory forces generated during operation, improving durability and performance.
[F02G 2275/20] for preventing piston over stroke -- The F02G 2275/20 subclass covers control devices and methods that prevent a piston from moving beyond its intended travel limit in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants. It includes mechanisms such as stroke-limiting cams, electronic overshoot detectors, and hydraulic or pneumatic stop systems that intervene when the piston approaches over-stroke conditions, thereby protecting engine components and maintaining safe operation.
[F02G 2275/30] for proper burning -- F02G 2275/30 relates to control mechanisms that ensure proper burning in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, covering systems such as fuel-air mixture regulation, ignition timing and combustion monitoring to achieve efficient and complete combustion.
[F02G 2275/40] for starting -- The code F02G 2275/40 covers control systems and devices used to start hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants. It includes starter mechanisms, ignition timing controls, and related circuitry that initiate engine operation. The classification is applied to inventions that manage the beginning of the engine's power cycle.
[F02G 2280/00] Output delivery -- The CPC subclass F02G 2280/00 covers the means by which a hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plant delivers its useful output-such as exhaust gases, heat, mechanical power or thrust-to the intended load or system. It includes ducts, nozzles, shafts, turbines, compressors and other transmission components that convey the energy produced by the engine to external devices.
[F02G 2280/005] Medical applications, e.g. for prosthesis or artificial hearts -- The F02G 2280/005 subclass, within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for combustion-engine output delivery, pertains to the use of engine-generated hot gases or combustion-product streams in medical devices. It covers applications such as powering prosthetic limbs, artificial hearts and other implantable or assistive technologies that exploit engine-derived flow or pressure.
[F02G 2280/10] Linear generators -- Linear generators are devices that convert the linear motion of pistons or other moving parts, driven by the expansion of hot gases in a positive-displacement engine, directly into electrical energy. The classification covers arrangements where the output delivery of a combustion-engine plant is achieved by a linear electromagnetic conversion system, such as free-piston or linear alternator configurations, rather than conventional rotary shafts.
[F02G 2280/20] Rotary generators -- Rotary generators are machines that convert the mechanical power produced by a hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine into electrical energy. They typically consist of a rotating shaft with attached magnets or windings that induce electricity in stationary coils, and are used for output delivery in turbine-type power plants. This class covers the design, integration and operation of such generators within combustion-engine systems.
[F02G 2280/50] Compressors or pumps -- F02G 2280/50 designates compressors or pumps used for output delivery in hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants. The subgroup is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, hot-gas engine plants and output delivery, and covers devices that raise the pressure of the working fluid produced by these engines.
[F02G 2280/60] Heat pumps -- F02G 2280/60 designates heat-pump systems that serve as the output delivery stage of hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants. It covers devices that transfer thermal energy from a low-temperature source to a higher-temperature sink using mechanical work, including refrigeration cycles, heat-recovery units and related control mechanisms.
[F02G 2280/70] Clutches -- The classification F02G 2280/70 covers clutches employed in the output-delivery stage of hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants, describing mechanisms that engage, disengage or transmit power from the engine to the driven load.
[F02G 2290/00] Engines characterised by the use of a particular power transfer medium, e.g. Helium -- A subclass of hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants that are defined by the specific power-transfer medium employed, such as helium, rather than by the engine's mechanical configuration. It covers engines where the working fluid itself serves as the means of transmitting power from the combustion process to the output shaft. This entry falls under the CPC hierarchy of mechanical engineering → combustion engines → hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants.
[F02G 3/00] Combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Positive-displacement engine plants characterised by the working gas being generated by combustion in the plant, e.g. positive displacement engine plants with external combustion:  **Glossary:** - Plant: An engine together with such additional apparatus as is necessary to run the engine. For example, a steam engine plant includes a steam engine and means for generating the steam.
[F02G 3/02] with reciprocating-piston engines -- F02G 3/02 covers hot-gas or combustion-product positive-displacement engine plants that employ reciprocating piston engines. It pertains to mechanical-engineering systems where the engine's working fluid is the combustion product itself, driving a piston to produce power, and is classified under combustion engines and hot-gas engine plants.
[F02G 5/00] Profiting from waste heat of combustion engines, not otherwise provided for -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Profiting from waste heat of combustion engines, not otherwise provided for, e.g. converting heat directly to electric power via a thermoelectric generator using the Seebeck effect or converting the waste heat to mechanical power using a Rankine cycle.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Vehicle heating derived from propulsion plant -> B60H1/02 - Exhaust or silencing apparatus combined or associated with devices profiting by exhaust heat -> F01N5/02
[F02G 5/02] Profiting from waste heat of exhaust gases -- F02G 5/02 covers technologies that capture waste heat from the exhaust gases of combustion engines and convert it into useful energy, such as power, heating or propulsion. It applies to positive-displacement engine plants where the recovered heat is not otherwise specified, facilitating improved overall efficiency.
[F02G 5/04] in combination with other waste heat from combustion engines -- The subclass F02G 5/04 covers technologies that recover and utilize waste heat generated by combustion engines together with other sources of waste heat, aiming to improve overall energy efficiency and create additional profit. It addresses combined heat-recovery schemes not otherwise specified in earlier subclasses.
[F02K] JET-PROPULSION PLANTS -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Features of jet-propulsion plants common to gas-turbine plants, air intakes or fuel supply control of air-breathing jet-propulsion plants -> F02C7/00 , F02C9/00  **Glossary:** - Jet-propulsion plants: means plants using combustion to produce a fluid stream from which a propulsive thrust on the plant is obtained on the reaction principle - Jet pipe: means the exhaust duct of a jet engine that carries the exhaust to the nozzle

This is used in jet-propulsion plants to improve performance across different regimes.
