[F02K 1/12] by means of pivoted flaps -- F02K 1/12 classifies jet-propulsion plants whose exhaust nozzle incorporates pivoted flaps to vary the flow direction or area. The flaps can be moved to adjust thrust or control the jet stream, often for thrust vectoring or performance optimisation.
[F02K 1/1207] of one series of flaps hinged at their upstream ends on a fixed structure  F02K1/1215  -  F02K1/1292  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Two or more series of flaps; One moveable series of flaps hinged to upstream axially moveable structure -> F02K1/1215 - F02K1/1292
[F02K 1/1215] of two series of flaps, the upstream series having its flaps hinged at their upstream ends on a fixed structure, and the downstream series having its flaps hinged at their downstream ends on a fixed structure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F02K 1/1223] of two series of flaps, the upstream series having its flaps hinged at their upstream ends on a fixed structure and the downstream series having its flaps hinged at their upstream ends on the downstream ends of the flaps of the upstream series -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F02K 1/123] of two series of flaps, both having their flaps hinged at their upstream ends on a fixed structure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F02K 1/1238] of two series of flaps, the upstream series having its flaps hinged at their upstream ends on a fixed structure and the downstream series having its flaps hinged at their upstream ends on a substantially axially movable structure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F02K 1/1246] of two series of flaps, the upstream series having its flaps hinged at their upstream ends on a fixed structure and the downstream series having its flaps hinged at their downstream ends on a substantially axially movable structure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F02K 1/1253] of one series of flaps hinged at their upstream ends on a fixed structure and of a substantially axially movable external member -- The classification covers jet-propulsion plants that employ a variable-geometry nozzle formed by a series of flaps hinged at their upstream ends to a fixed structure, together with an external member that can move substantially along the axis to adjust the nozzle shape and flow characteristics. This arrangement allows precise control of the exhaust stream for performance optimisation in combustion engines.
[F02K 1/1261] of one series of flaps hinged at their upstream ends on a substantially axially movable structure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F02K 1/1269] of three series of flaps, the upstream series having its flaps hinged at their upstream ends on a fixed structure and the internal downstream series having its flaps hinged at their downstream ends on the downstream ends of the flaps of the external downstream series hinged on a fixed structure at their upstream ends -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F02K 1/1276] of three series of flaps, the upstream series having its flaps hinged at their upstream ends on a substantially axially movable structure and the downstream or external series having its flaps hinged at their upstream ends on a fixed structure -- F02K 1/1276 refers to jet-propulsion plants that use three series of flaps, where the upstream series is hinged at its upstream ends to a structure that can move substantially along the axis, while the downstream or external series is hinged at its upstream ends to a fixed structure.
[F02K 1/1284] of three series of flaps, the upstream series having its flaps hinged at their upstream ends on a fixed structure and the internal downstream series having its flaps hinged at their downstream ends on the downstream ends of the flaps of the external downstream series hinged at their upstream ends on a substantially axially movable structure
[F02K 1/1292] of three series of flaps, the upstream series having its flaps hinged at their upstream ends on a fixed structure, the internal downstream series having its flaps hinged at their upstream ends on the downstream ends of the flaps of the upstream series and at their downstream ends on the downstream ends of the flaps of the external downstream series hinged at their upstream ends on a substantially axially movable structure -- F02K 1/1292 covers jet-propulsion plants having three series of flaps arranged sequentially: an upstream series hinged at its upstream ends to a fixed structure, a second series hinged at its upstream ends to the downstream ends of the upstream flaps, and a third external series hinged at its upstream ends to a substantially axially movable structure.
[F02K 1/15] Control or regulation -- F02K 1/15 covers the control or regulation of jet-propulsion plants, specifically devices and methods that manage fuel flow, ignition timing, thrust or nozzle operation in engines defined by the form or arrangement of their jet pipe or nozzle.
[F02K 1/16] conjointly with another control -- F02K 1/16 covers jet-propulsion plants whose jet pipe or nozzle is arranged together with another control device, such as thrust-vectoring mechanisms, variable-geometry extensions or secondary flow regulators, to modify the exhaust flow for performance or steering. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, jet-propulsion plants and concerns the form or arrangement of the jet pipe or nozzle.
[F02K 1/165] with air intake control -- The CPC subclass F02K 1/165 classifies jet-propulsion plants that incorporate control of the air intake, such as variable-geometry inlets, throttle mechanisms or other means to regulate the amount of air entering the combustion chamber. This subcategory falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and jet-propulsion plants defined by the form or arrangement of the jet pipe or nozzle.
[F02K 1/17] with control of fuel supply -- This subclass covers jet-propulsion plants where the fuel supply to the combustion chamber is actively controlled, including devices and methods for regulating fuel flow, pressure or timing.
[F02K 1/18] automatic -- F02K 1/18 relates to jet-propulsion plants whose jet pipe or nozzle configuration is controlled automatically, meaning the system adjusts flow, direction or thrust without manual operation. This subgroup falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and jet-propulsion plants characterised by the form or arrangement of the jet pipe or nozzle.
[F02K 1/28] using fluid jets to influence the jet flow -- This CPC subclass covers methods and devices that inject secondary fluid streams into a primary jet to modify its characteristics such as velocity, direction, or mixing. It applies to jet-propulsion plants where the nozzle or pipe geometry is designed for performance improvements, including flow control, thrust vectoring, and noise reduction.
[F02K 1/30] for varying effective area of jet pipe or nozzle -- F02K 1/30 relates to jet-propulsion plants in which the jet pipe or nozzle is designed to vary its effective flow area during operation, such as by adjustable geometry, movable flaps or interchangeable sections, allowing control of thrust and performance characteristics.
[F02K 1/32] for reversing thrust -- F02K 1/32 covers jet-propulsion plants that incorporate a mechanism for producing reversing thrust, allowing the aircraft or vehicle to decelerate or move backward by redirecting or modulating the jet flow. It applies to designs where the nozzle arrangement or control system enables thrust reversal.
[F02K 1/34] for attenuating noise -- This subclass deals with designs of jet-pipe or nozzle arrangements in jet-propulsion plants that incorporate features to reduce acoustic emissions, such as serrated edges, liners, or other noise-attenuation structures. The focus is on the geometric or configurational aspects that achieve sound damping.
[F02K 1/36] having an ejector -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F02K 1/38] Introducing air inside the jet -- F02K 1/38 covers jet-propulsion plants in which air is injected into the jet stream within the pipe or nozzle. The air may be mixed with combustion products to increase thrust, cool the jet, or reduce emissions, and the feature is defined by the way the air-introduction system is arranged in the jet conduit.
[F02K 1/383] with retractable elements -- F02K 1/383 covers jet-propulsion plants whose jet pipe or nozzle includes elements that can be retracted, such as movable vanes or adjustable exhaust sections, to modify thrust or reduce drag during operation. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, jet-propulsion plants, focusing on the form or arrangement of the nozzle.
[F02K 1/386] mixing devices in the jet pipe, e.g. for mixing primary and secondary flow -- F02K 1/386 relates to mixing devices installed in the jet pipe of a jet-propulsion plant that combine the primary exhaust flow with a secondary flow, such as air, fuel or gases, to improve combustion efficiency, control emissions or adjust thrust characteristics.
[F02K 1/40] Nozzles having means for dividing the jet into a plurality of partial jets or having an elongated cross-section outlet -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F02K 1/42] the means being movable into an inoperative position -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.

[F02K 1/46] Nozzles having means for adding air to the jet or for augmenting the mixing region between the jet and the ambient air, e.g. for silencing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Using fluid jets to influence the jet flow -> F02K1/28 - Having an ejector -> F02K1/36 - Introducing air inside the jet -> F02K1/38

[F02K 1/54] Nozzles having means for reversing jet thrust -- Nozzles equipped with devices that can redirect or reverse the jet stream, allowing the thrust to be turned back toward the aircraft or adjusted for maneuvering, such as thrust-reverse systems or vectoring mechanisms used in jet-propulsion plants.
[F02K 1/56] Reversing jet main flow -- The CPC subclass F02K 1/56 concerns jet-propulsion plants whose primary jet stream is redirected or reversed, such as systems that turn the main exhaust flow back toward the inlet for thrust reversal, flow control or after-burning purposes. It falls within the hierarchy of mechanical engineering → combustion engines → jet-propulsion plants → arrangements of the jet pipe or nozzle.

[F02K 1/64] Reversing fan flow -- F02K 1/64 covers jet-propulsion plant designs where the fan or compressor flow is reversed, typically to provide thrust reversal, assist start-up, or modulate airflow, involving a change in the direction of the working fluid within the jet pipe or nozzle.

[F02K 1/74] Reversing at least one flow in relation to at least one other flow in a plural- flow engine -- The entry F02K 1/74 covers jet-propulsion plants in which at least one fluid flow is reversed relative to another flow within a plural-flow engine, such as designs that use counter-flow or recirculating streams in the combustion or nozzle sections. It applies to engines where the arrangement of the jet pipe or nozzle creates this reversed-flow relationship.

[F02K 3/00] Plants including a gas turbine driving a compressor or a ducted fan -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mainly ducted gas turbine plants with ducted fans, whereby the main flow which is only slightly compressed by a fan or the like bypasses the core engine; Heating of the bypass flow; Afterburners.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F02K 3/02] in which part of the working fluid by-passes the turbine and combustion chamber -- This subclass covers jet-propulsion plants in which part of the working fluid is routed around the turbine and combustion chamber, typically as a bypass flow in turbofan or mixed-flow engines. The bypass stream provides thrust without undergoing combustion, enhancing overall efficiency and reducing fuel consumption.
[F02K 3/025] the by-pass flow being at least partly used to create an independent thrust component -- The entry covers jet-propulsion plants in which a portion of the bypass airflow is diverted or treated so that it generates an additional thrust vector separate from the main exhaust. This includes designs using auxiliary nozzles, thrust-vectoring devices, or secondary fans to create an independent thrust component from the bypass stream.
[F02K 3/04] the plant including ducted fans, i.e. fans with high volume, low pressure outputs, for augmenting the jet thrust, e.g. of double-flow type -- F02K 3/04 classifies jet-propulsion plants that incorporate ducted fans, which produce a large volume of low-pressure airflow to augment thrust, such as double-flow fan arrangements. The code lies within mechanical engineering, under combustion engines and jet-propulsion plants.
[F02K 3/06] with front fan -- The subclass F02K 3/06 covers jet-propulsion plants that incorporate a front fan into the gas-turbine system, describing arrangements where the fan is positioned at the inlet to provide airflow for thrust or to drive a compressor. It is used for mechanical-engineering classifications of combustion engines with front-fan configurations.
[F02K 3/062] with aft fan -- A jet-propulsion plant in which the gas turbine drives a fan mounted behind the turbine, i.e., an aft fan, typically used in turbofan engines for aircraft or industrial applications where the fan is downstream of the compressor-combustor assembly.
[F02K 3/065] with front and aft fans -- The subclass F02K 3/065 covers jet-propulsion plants that incorporate both a front-mounted fan and a rear-mounted fan, typically arranged to increase thrust and improve efficiency by accelerating airflow through two separate stages of compression and expansion. This configuration is used in certain gas-turbine engines where the front fan provides primary airflow and the aft fan contributes additional thrust, often in high-performance or thrust-vectoring applications.
[F02K 3/068] being characterised by a short axial length relative to the diameter -- This CPC subclass refers to jet-propulsion plants where the overall axial length of the gas-turbine-driven compressor or fan assembly is relatively short compared to its diameter, i.e. a compact layout. Such designs are used to reduce overall engine height while maintaining airflow, typical for certain aircraft or marine applications.
[F02K 3/072] with counter-rotating rotors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F02K 3/075] controlling flow ratio between flows -- The classification covers devices or control methods that adjust the proportion of two fluid streams in a jet-propulsion plant, such as regulating the bypass, bleed, or fuel-air flow in a gas-turbine system. It includes mechanisms that sense and vary the flow ratio to achieve desired performance, stability or efficiency. This applies to plants where a turbine drives a compressor or a ducted fan.
[F02K 3/077] the plant being of the multiple flow type, i.e. having three or more flows -- This subclass concerns jet-propulsion plants whose layout includes three or more fluid streams, such as a gas turbine driving a compressor together with additional flow paths for cooling, exhaust or secondary power extraction. It addresses multiple-flow configurations in gas-turbine-driven compressors or ducted-fan systems.
[F02K 3/08] with supplementary heating of the working fluid -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Heating of the working fluid, e.g. main working fluid or bypass flow, by heat exchangers or burners. These include after-burners.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Control of fuel supply therefor -> F02C9/26
[F02K 3/10] by after-burners -- F02K 3/10 relates to jet-propulsion plants that employ after-burners, which are additional combustion chambers located downstream of the main turbine. By injecting and igniting extra fuel in the exhaust stream, the after-burner raises exhaust temperature and velocity, providing a temporary thrust boost for high-speed aircraft or missiles.
[F02K 3/105] Heating the by-pass flow -- The F02K 3/105 entry concerns techniques for heating the bypass airflow in jet-propulsion plants that incorporate a gas turbine driving a compressor or ducted fan. It includes devices or arrangements that raise the temperature of the bypass stream, for example by using bleed air, electric heaters or heat exchangers, to improve engine performance, prevent icing or control exhaust characteristics.
[F02K 3/11] by means of burners or combustion chambers -- F02K 3/11 covers jet-propulsion plants that generate power by burning fuel in burners or combustion chambers, supplying hot gases to a gas turbine which drives a compressor or ducted fan. This subcategory falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and jet-propulsion plants.
[F02K 3/115] by means of indirect heat exchange -- The code F02K 3/115 covers jet-propulsion plants in which the energy transfer between the turbine and compressor, or fan, is performed through indirect heat exchange such as recuperators or heat-exchangers, rather than direct mixing of gases. This method recovers waste heat to raise the temperature of the incoming air, improving efficiency without direct contact between the hot and cold streams.
[F02K 3/12] characterised by having more than one gas turbine -- F02K 3/12 classifies jet-propulsion plants that are characterised by having more than one gas turbine to drive the compressor or ducted fan. It covers systems where multiple turbines share the load or operate in series or parallel.
[F02K 5/00] Plants including an engine, other than a gas turbine, driving a compressor or a ducted fan -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Plants including an engine, other than a gas turbine, driving a compressor or a ducted fan.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F02K 5/02] the engine being of the reciprocating-piston type -- This CPC subclass F02K 5/02 pertains to jet-propulsion plants that use a reciprocating-piston engine rather than a gas turbine to drive a compressor or ducted fan. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and jet-propulsion plants and covers plants where the power source is a piston engine.
[F02K 5/023] the compressor being of the reciprocating-piston type  F02K5/026  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, jet-propulsion plants, covering jet-propulsion plants that include an engine other than a gas turbine driving a compressor or ducted fan, specifically where the compressor is of the reciprocating-piston type; however, the more detailed subclass F02K5/026 supersedes it.
[F02K 5/026] free-piston engines -- F02K 5/026 covers free-piston engines, which are combustion engines that generate linear piston motion without a crankshaft and use that motion directly to drive a compressor or a ducted fan in jet-propulsion plants. These plants consist of an engine, other than a gas turbine, coupled to a compressor or fan, providing thrust or airflow without rotating machinery.
[F02K 7/00] Plants in which the working fluid is used in a jet only, i.e. the plants not having a turbine or other engine driving a compressor or a ducted fan -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Rocket- engine plants, i.e. plants carrying both fuel and oxidant therefor; Control thereof -> F02K9/00
[F02K 7/005] the engine comprising a rotor rotating under the actions of jets issuing from this rotor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Rotating rotors rotating due to the reaction force of the jets issued from the rotor.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F02K 7/02] the jet being intermittent, i.e. pulse-jet -- F02K 7/02 relates to jet-propulsion plants in which the jet is intermittent, commonly called pulse-jet operation. It covers devices that discharge the working fluid in a series of rapid bursts rather than a steady stream, and it applies to systems that do not employ a turbine, compressor or ducted fan to drive the jet.
[F02K 7/04] with resonant combustion chambers -- The F02K 7/04 class covers jet-propulsion plants that expel the working fluid directly as a jet and employ combustion chambers operating at acoustic resonance. Resonant chambers create pressure waves that enhance flame stability and thrust, a feature common in pulse-jet and resonant detonation engines.
[F02K 7/06] with combustion chambers having valves -- The CPC subclass F02K 7/06 covers jet-propulsion plants whose combustion chambers are equipped with valves to control the admission of fuel or air. It pertains to mechanical engineering designs where a valve-regulated combustion space generates thrust directly, without an intervening turbine or fan. This subclass refines the broader category of jet-propulsion plants by specifying the presence of valve-controlled chambers.
[F02K 7/067] having aerodynamic valves -- This CPC subclass F02K 7/067 covers jet-propulsion plants that use aerodynamic valves to control the flow of the working fluid, without a turbine or compressor. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and jet-propulsion plants.
[F02K 7/075] with multiple pulse-jet engines -- This subclass covers jet-propulsion plants that use two or more pulse-jet engines to produce thrust, with the working fluid expelled directly and no turbine, compressor or ducted-fan incorporated.
[F02K 7/08] the jet being continuous -- F02K 7/08 relates to jet-propulsion plants in which the working fluid is expelled continuously as a jet, without a turbine, compressor or ducted fan driving the flow. It covers continuous-jet operation in mechanical-engineering combustion engines.
[F02K 7/10] characterised by having ram-action compression, i.e. aero-thermo-dynamic-ducts or ram-jet engines -- F02K 7/10 designates jet-propulsion plants that obtain compression solely by ram-action, using aerodynamic ducts or ram-jet engines, and therefore lack turbines, compressors or ducted fans. The classification applies to systems where the working fluid is accelerated directly through a converging-diverging inlet to produce thrust.
[F02K 7/105] using a solid fuel -- F02K 7/105 relates to jet-propulsion plants that employ a solid fuel as the working fluid, generating thrust by a jet of combustion products without using a turbine, compressor or ducted fan. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and jet-propulsion plant categories.
[F02K 7/12] Injection-induction jet engines -- F02K 7/12 designates injection-induction jet engines, a type of jet-propulsion plant where the working fluid is introduced directly into the combustion zone and expelled as a high-velocity jet, producing thrust without a turbine, compressor or ducted fan driving a separate airflow.
[F02K 7/14] with external combustion, e.g. scram-jet engines -- F02K 7/14 relates to jet-propulsion plants that employ external combustion, such as scram-jet engines, where the working fluid is heated outside the flow and expands directly through a nozzle to generate thrust, without using a turbine, compressor or ducted fan. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → jet-propulsion plants.
[F02K 7/16] Composite ram-jet/turbo-jet engines -- F02K 7/16 covers engines that integrate ram-jet and turbo-jet principles, using a single flow path where the working fluid provides thrust solely through a jet without a turbine, compressor or ducted fan. The classification applies to composite propulsion units that combine the simplicity of ram-jets with the performance control of turbo-jets.
[F02K 7/18] Composite ram-jet/rocket engines -- F02K 7/18 covers composite ram-jet/rocket engines, which combine an air-breathing ram-jet stage with a rocket motor to provide thrust across a broad speed envelope. These propulsion plants operate without a turbine-driven compressor, using the working fluid solely in a high-velocity jet, and are classified as jet-propulsion plants that do not include separate driving turbines.
[F02K 7/20] Composite ram-jet/pulse-jet engines -- F02K 7/20 pertains to composite ram-jet and pulse-jet engines, a class of jet-propulsion plants that generate thrust solely by ejecting a working fluid through a jet and do not incorporate turbines, compressors or ducted fans. It is located under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Jet-Propulsion Plants.
[F02K 9/00] Rocket-engine plants, i.e. plants carrying both fuel and oxidant therefor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Rocket-engine plant related aspects such as solid and liquid propellant rocket plants; Constructional details of the charges; Supply and feeding of propellant; Starting and restarting.
[F02K 9/08] using solid propellants -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Solid propellant rocket plants.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Using semi-solid or pulverulent propellants -> F02K9/70 - Using liquid and solid propellants, i.e. hybrid rocket-engine plants -> F02K9/72
[F02K 9/10] Shape or structure of solid propellant charges -- The class F02K 9/10 concerns the design of the geometry and internal architecture of solid propellant grains used in rocket-engine plants. It includes the shape of the charge, grain segmentation, internal cavities and surface patterning that influence burn rate, thrust profile and mechanical stability, as well as the arrangement of the propellant within its casing.
[F02K 9/12] made of two or more portions burning at different rates -- F02K 9/12 covers rocket-engine plants in which the propellant charge is divided into two or more separate portions, each engineered to burn at a different rate, allowing staged thrust or controlled performance variations.
[F02K 9/14] made from sheet-like materials, e.g. of carpet-roll type, of layered structure -- This CPC subclass F02K 9/14 covers jet-propulsion plant components that are made from sheet-like materials, such as carpet-roll type, having a layered structure. It applies to rocket-engine plants where the combustion chamber or related parts are formed by winding or stacking sheets.
[F02K 9/16] of honeycomb structure -- The F02K 9/16 subclass relates to rocket-engine plants that incorporate honeycomb structures, such as liners, combustor walls or cooling passages formed from a regular array of cells. These honeycomb elements provide high strength-to-weight ratios, improved heat transfer and structural rigidity, and are used to support or protect components that experience intense thermal and mechanical loads.
[F02K 9/18] of the internal-burning type having a star or like shaped internal cavity -- F02K 9/18 covers rocket-engine plants of the internal-burning type in which the combustion chamber has a star-shaped or similarly contoured internal cavity, providing a specific geometry for fuel-oxidizer mixing and flame propagation. This subclass groups designs that use that distinctive cavity shape to influence performance and stability.
[F02K 9/20] of the external-burning type -- The F02K 9/20 class covers rocket-engine plants of the external-burning type, where the fuel and oxidizer are burned outside the thrust chamber and the hot gases are then expanded through a nozzle. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, jet-propulsion plants, and rocket-engine plants.
[F02K 9/22] of the front-burning type -- This CPC subclass F02K 9/22 covers rocket-engine plants of the front-burning type where combustion occurs at the front of the chamber and propellant flow is directed forward. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and jet-propulsion plants.
[F02K 9/24] Charging rocket engines with solid propellants -- F02K 9/24 relates to the charging of rocket engines with solid propellants, covering the storage, handling, feeding and ignition of solid-fuel rockets. It is a subcategory of jet-propulsion plants within mechanical engineering, focusing on the equipment and processes that supply solid propellant to the combustion chamber.
[F02K 9/26] Burning control -- The CPC subclass F02K 9/26 concerns the control of combustion in rocket-engine plants, covering systems and methods that regulate fuel-oxidizer mixing, ignition timing, flame-holding, and overall combustion stability to ensure safe and efficient operation of propulsion units.
[F02K 9/28] having two or more propellant charges with the propulsion gases exhausting through a common nozzle -- F02K 9/28 covers rocket-engine plants that use two or more separate propellant charges whose combustion gases are combined and expelled through a single common nozzle, allowing simultaneous or sequential firing of multiple charges for thrust generation. This subclass falls under jet-propulsion plants within mechanical engineering, specifically for systems that carry both fuel and oxidant and direct the combined exhaust through one nozzle.
[F02K 9/30] with the propulsion gases exhausting through a plurality of nozzles -- In rocket-engine plants, this subclass concerns designs where the combustion products are expelled through several nozzles rather than a single outlet, providing distributed thrust, enhanced control or redundancy. It includes arrangements for splitting the flow and shaping multiple exhaust passages to achieve the desired performance.
[F02K 9/32] Constructional parts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Solid propellant construction parts and details including: casings, liners, joints, seals, propellant supports, safety devices, and cooling arrangements.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F02K 9/34] Casings -- F02K 9/34 covers the casings that contain both fuel and oxidiser in rocket-engine plants, i.e., the structural housings that form the pressure-tight vessel for the propellant mixture in jet-propulsion systems. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and jet-propulsion plants.
[F02K 9/343] Joints, connections, seals therefor -- F02K 9/343 concerns the design and construction of joints, connections and sealing arrangements used in rocket-engine plants that handle both fuel and oxidizer. It addresses methods for securely linking components and preventing leaks under the high-pressure, high-temperature conditions typical of jet-propulsion systems. This subclass falls within mechanical engineering, combustion engines and jet-propulsion plant categories.
[F02K 9/346] Liners, e.g. inhibitors -- F02K 9/346 covers liners and related inhibitors used in rocket-engine plants, which are a subset of jet-propulsion combustion engines. The classification addresses components that line combustion chambers or ducts to protect them from heat, erosion, or chemical attack, and includes materials or treatments that inhibit degradation during operation. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for combustion engines, jet-propulsion plants, and rocket-engine systems.
[F02K 9/36] Propellant charge supports -- The classification F02K 9/36 covers devices that support and hold propellant charges in rocket-engine plants, ensuring correct positioning and structural integrity during combustion. It relates to mechanical engineering of jet-propulsion and combustion engines.
[F02K 9/38] Safety devices, e.g. to prevent accidental ignition -- F02K 9/38 covers safety devices for rocket-engine plants that handle both fuel and oxidant, such as mechanisms that prevent accidental ignition. The class includes systems designed to detect leaks, shut off flow, or otherwise intervene when ignition conditions arise, ensuring safe operation of jet-propulsion and combustion equipment.
[F02K 9/40] Cooling arrangements -- F02K 9/40 relates to the cooling arrangements used in rocket-engine plants, covering methods and structures that remove heat from combustion chambers and nozzles, such as regenerative, film, or ablative cooling, and the design of cooling channels, heat exchangers and thermal insulation to protect engine components.
[F02K 9/42] using liquid or gaseous propellants -- The subclass F02K 9/42 covers rocket-engine plants that are fed with liquid or gaseous propellants, meaning the fuel and oxidiser are stored separately and combined in the combustion chamber. It includes systems for handling, transporting, pressurising and injecting these propellants into the engine.
[F02K 9/425] Propellants -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Specific types of liquid or gaseous propellants, e.g. hypergolic propellants, methane, hydrogen peroxide.
[F02K 9/44] Feeding propellants -- F02K 9/44 covers the means of supplying propellants to rocket-engine plants, including storage, transfer lines, pumps and control devices that deliver fuel and oxidizer to the combustion chamber. It addresses the design of feed systems, pressure regulation and distribution hardware used in jet-propulsion plants.
[F02K 9/46] using pumps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feeding propellants by the use of pumps, e.g. turbo-pumps.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F02K 9/48] driven by a gas turbine fed by propellant combustion gases -- F02K 9/48 classifies rocket-engine plants in which a gas turbine, powered by the combustion gases of the propellants, provides the mechanical drive for the system. The turbine may operate pumps, compressors or other auxiliary units required for thrust generation.
[F02K 9/50] using pressurised fluid to pressurise the propellants -- This CPC subclass F02K 9/50 covers methods of pressurising propellants in rocket-engine or jet-propulsion plants by using a high-pressure fluid. It includes systems where a pressurised gas or liquid is introduced to raise the pressure of fuel or oxidiser before combustion, and it is situated under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, jet-propulsion plants.
[F02K 9/52] Injectors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Injectors for liquid or gaseous propellants.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F02K 9/54] Leakage detectors -- F02K 9/54 relates to leakage detectors used in rocket-engine plants, encompassing devices and systems that detect the unintended escape of fuel, oxidizer or other working fluids. It covers sensors, monitoring arrangements and methods such as pressure, acoustic, optical or chemical detection employed to identify and locate leaks in propulsion-system components.
[F02K 9/56] Control -- F02K 9/56 deals with the control of rocket-engine plants, which are propulsion systems that carry both fuel and oxidizer. The subclass covers mechanisms, methods and devices for regulating thrust, mixture ratio, start-up, shut-down and safety functions in these combustion-engine installations.
[F02K 9/563] of propellant feed pumps -- This subclass covers the design, construction and operation of pumps that deliver rocket propellants-fuel, oxidizer or their mixture-from storage to the combustion chamber, including pump types, drive mechanisms and integration with the overall feed system. It concerns centrifugal, positive-displacement and turbopumps used in rocket-engine plants.
[F02K 9/566] elements and safety devices, e.g. pressure relief valves -- F02K 9/566 covers components and safety mechanisms used in rocket-engine plants, such as pressure relief valves, that protect the system against over-pressure and other hazardous conditions.
[F02K 9/58] Propellant feed valves -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of rocket propellant feed valves.
[F02K 9/60] Constructional parts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Constructional parts or details of liquid or gaseous propellant rockets such as reservoirs, combustion chambers, decomposition chambers.
[F02K 9/605] Reservoirs -- F02K 9/605 concerns the design and use of storage vessels that hold propellant liquids or gases in rocket-engine plants. It includes tanks, bladder or sphere containers, feed-line connections, pressurisation and venting systems for both fuel and oxidiser, as well as methods of temperature control and insulation.
[F02K 9/62] Combustion or thrust chambers -- F02K 9/62 relates to the design, construction and operation of combustion or thrust chambers in rocket-engine plants, covering aspects such as chamber geometry, cooling methods, fuel-oxidizer injection and mixing, and the generation of high-velocity exhaust gases for propulsion.
[F02K 9/64] having cooling arrangements -- F02K 9/64 pertains to rocket-engine plants that incorporate cooling arrangements, such as regenerative, film or ablative systems, to protect combustion chambers, nozzles and related components from extreme temperatures. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and jet-propulsion plants.
[F02K 9/66] of the rotary type -- F02K 9/66 covers rocket-engine plants in which the propulsion system is of the rotary type, meaning the engine utilizes rotating components such as screw-type or centrifugal combustion chambers or turbopumps to feed and ignite the fuel-oxidizer mixture. These designs are applied to jet-propulsion units that carry both fuel and oxidant.
[F02K 9/68] Decomposition chambers -- F02K 9/68 covers decomposition chambers used in rocket-engine plants, where the propellant is broken down into hot gases before combustion or expulsion. These chambers are integral to thrust generation of jet-propulsion systems that carry both fuel and oxidizer.
[F02K 9/70] using semi- solid or pulverulent propellants -- This CPC subclass covers rocket-engine plants that employ propellants in a semi-solid or pulverulent (powder) form. It addresses the design, feeding, combustion and control methods specific to such formulations, including storage and handling within the propulsion system.
[F02K 9/72] using liquid and solid propellants, i.e. hybrid rocket-engine plants -- F02K 9/72 covers rocket-engine plants that employ a combination of liquid and solid propellants, known as hybrid rockets. It relates to jet-propulsion systems where fuel and oxidizer are stored in different phases, allowing mixed-propellant operation. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, jet-propulsion plants and rocket-engine plants.
[F02K 9/74] combined with another jet-propulsion plant -- F02K 9/74 covers rocket-engine plants that are integrated with, or operate together with, a second jet-propulsion system. The classification applies to configurations where a rocket motor shares facilities, fuel handling, or exhaust routing with another jet-propulsion plant, enabling combined thrust or dual-mode operation.
[F02K 9/76] with another rocket-engine plant -- F02K 9/76 covers rocket-engine plants that include an additional rocket-engine unit, i.e., configurations where two or more rocket engines are combined within a single plant for joint operation or staged performance. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → jet-propulsion plants and addresses the integration and interaction of multiple rocket-engine components.
[F02K 9/763] with solid propellant -- This subclass deals with rocket-engine plants that use a solid propellant, covering the design, combustion chamber, feed and ignition systems, and thrust-vector control specific to solid-fuel rockets. It includes plant components that store and deliver the combined fuel-oxidizer mixture in solid form for propulsion.
[F02K 9/766] with liquid propellant -- F02K 9/766 covers rocket-engine plants that store and deliver both fuel and oxidizer in liquid form to the combustion chamber. It addresses the design, feed systems and control mechanisms specific to liquid-propellant propulsion.
[F02K 9/78] with an air-breathing jet-propulsion plant -- This subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification (F02K 9/78) deals with rocket-engine plants that incorporate an air-breathing jet-propulsion system, meaning the vehicle obtains its oxidizer from atmospheric air rather than carrying it internally. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → jet-propulsion plants.
[F02K 9/80] characterised by thrust or thrust vector control -- The subclass F02K 9/80 pertains to rocket-engine plants in which the thrust magnitude or its direction is actively adjusted, using devices such as gimbaled or movable nozzles, secondary injection, thrust-reversal or other vectoring mechanisms. This control of thrust or thrust vector is employed to steer or maneuver missiles, launch vehicles and spacecraft during operation.
[F02K 9/805] servo-mechanisms or control devices therefor -- F02K 9/805 covers servo-mechanisms and control devices used in rocket-engine plants, such as thrust-vector, fuel-feed, and oxidizer-flow regulators that adjust performance during operation. The class sits in the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering > Combustion Engines > Jet-Propulsion Plants > Rocket-Engine Plants.
[F02K 9/82] by injection of a secondary fluid into the rocket exhaust gases -- The F02K 9/82 entry refers to rocket-engine plants that increase thrust or efficiency by injecting a secondary fluid into the exhaust stream. This technique, applied after combustion, can modify temperature, pressure or chemical composition of the gases, aiding performance optimisation in jet-propulsion systems.
[F02K 9/84] using movable nozzles -- F02K 9/84 classifies inventions that employ movable or adjustable nozzles in rocket-engine plants, where both fuel and oxidizer are stored on the vehicle. The provision allows thrust vector control or performance optimisation by shifting the nozzle position relative to the combustion chamber during operation. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for jet-propulsion and combustion-engine systems.
[F02K 9/86] using nozzle throats of adjustable cross- section -- F02K 9/86 covers techniques for varying the cross-sectional area of the throat in rocket-engine nozzles to control thrust, mixture ratio or performance. The adjustment may be mechanical, hydraulic or electromechanical and is applied in jet-propulsion plants that carry both fuel and oxidizer.
[F02K 9/88] using auxiliary rocket nozzles -- This subclass relates to rocket-engine plants that employ one or more auxiliary nozzles to modify or enhance thrust, such as secondary nozzles for thrust-vector control, thrust augmentation, or improved efficiency, and includes the design, arrangement and operation of those additional nozzles.
[F02K 9/90] using deflectors -- F02K 9/90 covers the use of deflectors in rocket-engine plants, where both fuel and oxidiser are stored on board. It relates to devices that divert or shape the exhaust or propellant flow to improve thrust direction, reduce erosive effects, or enhance combustion stability within the propulsion system.
[F02K 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- F02K 99/00 is a residual group within the jet-propulsion plants subclass of mechanical engineering. It covers any features, components or processes of jet-propulsion systems that are not specifically addressed by the other defined groups in the subclass. This catch-all entry ensures that novel or uncommon aspects can still be classified under the CPC system.
[F02K 9/92] incorporating means for reversing or terminating thrust -- F02K 9/92 covers rocket-engine plants that include devices to reverse or stop thrust, such as thrust-reversal nozzles, shutdown valves, or other mechanisms that can quickly reduce or cancel the propulsive force during operation. The entry is used for patents describing integrated systems that control or terminate the rocket's thrust output.
[F02K 9/94] Re-ignitable or restartable rocket- engine plants -- F02K 9/94 covers rocket-engine plants that can be re-ignited or restarted after shutdown, including systems that store both fuel and oxidizer and allow repeated ignition during a mission.
[F02K 9/95] characterised by starting or ignition means or arrangements -- This subgroup of the CPC deals with rocket-engine plants that are defined by the particular starting or ignition means and arrangements employed to initiate combustion of the combined fuel and oxidant. It includes various starter devices such as pyrotechnic, electrical, chemical or plasma igniters and their integration into the propulsion system.
[F02K 9/96] characterised by specially adapted arrangements for testing or measuring -- F02K 9/96 covers rocket-engine plants that include specially adapted arrangements for testing or measuring the engine's performance, parameters or behaviour. It classifies installations where built-in test rigs, sensors and measurement systems are integrated into the propulsion plant to monitor combustion, thrust, temperature or other operational data. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → jet-propulsion plants.
[F02K 9/97] Rocket nozzles -- F02K 9/97 concerns the design and operation of rocket nozzles, the divergent flow-controlling components that expand high-pressure combustion gases to produce thrust in rocket-engine plants. It addresses nozzle geometry, material selection, cooling methods and performance optimisation for both liquid and solid propellant rockets.
[F02K 9/972] Fluid cooling arrangements for nozzles  F02K9/64  takes precedence
[F02K 9/974] Nozzle- linings; Ablative coatings -- The F02K 9/974 subclass covers devices and materials used to line or coat rocket-engine nozzles. It includes ablative coatings that protect the nozzle surface by gradually eroding and absorbing heat during operation, thereby enhancing durability and performance of jet-propulsion plants. This subclass falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and rocket-engine plants.
[F02K 9/976] Deployable nozzles -- F02K 9/976 covers rocket-engine plants that use deployable nozzles, meaning nozzle sections that can be extended, retracted or otherwise altered in geometry after launch to optimise thrust or meet storage constraints. The code applies to designs where the nozzle shape changes during operation, such as unfolding or telescoping configurations.
[F02K 9/978] Closures for nozzles; Nozzles comprising ejectable or discardable elements -- The code F02K 9/978 covers devices that seal or close the outlet of a rocket or jet nozzle, as well as nozzle designs that incorporate parts which can be expelled or discarded during operation. Such closures and ejectable-or-discardable elements are used to control thrust or protect downstream components.
[F02M] SUPPLYING COMBUSTION ENGINES IN GENERAL WITH COMBUSTIBLE MIXTURES OR CONSTITUENTS THEREOF -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus for supplying fuel, air or non fuel substances to combustion engines, e.g. carburettors for liquid fuel; apparatus for feeding, or treating combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture; Fuel injection apparatus  **Glossary:** - Carburettors: Apparatus for mixing fuel with air, the fuel being brought into mixing contact with the air by lowering the air pressure, e.g. in a venturi - Fuel-injection apparatus: Apparatus for introducing fuel into a space, e.g. engine cylinder, by pressurising the fuel, e.g. by a pump acting behind the fuel, and thus includes the so-called "solid-fuel injection" in which liquid fuel is introduced without any admixture of gas - Low-pressure fuel injection: Fuel injection in which the fuel-air mixture containing fuel thus injected will be substantially compressed in the compression stroke of the engine - Pumping element: A single piston-cylinder unit in a reciprocating-piston fuel-injection pump or the equivalent unit in any other type of fuel-injection pump
[F02M 1/00] Carburettors for liquid fuels -- **Definition:** This place covers:  When the engine is cold, fuel vaporizes less readily and tends to condense on the walls of the intake manifold, starving the cylinders of fuel and making the engine difficult to start; thus, a richer mixture is required to start and run the engine until it warms up. To provide the extra fuel, a choke is typically used; this is a device that restricts the flow of air at the entrance to the carburettor, before the venturi. With this restriction in place, extra vacuum is developed in the carburettor venturi, which pulls extra fuel through the main metering system to supplement the fuel being pulled from the idle and off-idle circuits. This provides the rich mixture required to sustain operation at low engine temperatures. In addition, the choke can be connected to a cam or other device which prevents the throttle plate from closing fully while the choke is in operation. This causes the engine to idle at a higher speed. Fast idle serves as a way to help the engine warm up quickly, and give a more stable idle while cold by increasing airflow throughout the intake system which helps to better atomize the cold fuel. In many carburetted cars, the choke is controlled by a cable connected to a pull-knob on the dashboard operated by the driver. In some carburetted cars it is automatically controlled by a thermostat employing a bimetallic spring, which is exposed to engine heat, or to an electric heating element. This heat may be transferred to the choke thermostat via simple convection, via engine coolant, or via air heated by the exhaust. Some carburettors do not have a choke but instead use a mixture enrichment circuit. Typically used on small engines, enricheners work by opening a secondary fuel circuit below the throttle valves. This circuit works exactly like the idle circuit, and when engaged it simply supplies extra fuel when the throttle is closed.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Carburettors for gaseous fuel -> F02M21/00 - Carburettors combined with low pressure fuel injection -> F02M71/00  **Glossary:** - Idling: Engine running stationary - Priming: Pumping extra fuel - Float chamber: Fuel collection chamber controlled by float member
[F02M 1/005] Remote display or control for displaying the operational situation of the starter -- F02M 1/005 concerns a remote display or control system that presents the operational condition of the starter in carburettors for liquid-fuel engines. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and the supply of combustible mixtures, enabling operators to monitor starter status from a distance.
[F02M 1/02] the means to facilitate starting or idling being chokes for enriching fuel-air mixture -- F02M 1/02 covers carburettor features that aid engine start-up or idle by enriching the fuel-air mixture, typically through choke mechanisms. It is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and the supplying of engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 1/04] the means to facilitate starting or idling being auxiliary carburetting apparatus able to be put into, and out of, operation, e.g. having automatically-operated disc valves -- Auxiliary carburetting apparatus designed to aid starting or idling of combustion engines, such as automatically-operated disc-valve devices that can be engaged or disengaged from the main fuel-delivery system. This sub-class covers mechanisms that facilitate engine start-up or low-speed operation by providing supplemental fuel mixing.
[F02M 1/043] Auxiliary carburetting apparatus controlled by rotary sliding valves -- The entry covers auxiliary carburetting devices for liquid-fuel engines that supplement the main carburetor by using rotary sliding valves to meter fuel and air. The mechanism adjusts the mixture based on engine demand, improving response and efficiency in combustion engines.
[F02M 1/046] Auxiliary carburetting apparatus controlled by piston valves -- A device that assists a carburettor for liquid-fuel engines by using piston-operated valves to modulate the flow of fuel-air mixture, providing supplementary metering or enrichment under transient operating conditions.
[F02M 1/06] having axially-movable valves, e.g. piston-shaped -- A carburettor for liquid-fuel engines that uses an axially-movable valve, typically piston-shaped, to meter the fuel-air mixture supplied to a combustion engine. This arrangement belongs to the mechanical-engineering class for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 1/08] the means to facilitate starting or idling becoming operative or inoperative automatically -- F02M 1/08 covers automatic devices in liquid-fuel carburettors that control the engine's start-up and idle phases. It includes mechanisms such as automatic chokes, idle-speed regulators or enrichment systems that engage or disengage without driver intervention.
[F02M 1/10] dependent on engine temperature, e.g. having thermostat -- F02M 1/10 covers carburettors for liquid fuels that modify the supplied mixture according to the engine's temperature, typically using a thermostat or similar temperature-dependent control device. It is a sub-group of mechanisms that provide combustible mixtures to combustion engines.
[F02M 11/00] Multi-stage carburettors, Register-type carburettors, i.e. with slidable or rotatable throttling valves in which a plurality of fuel nozzles, other than only an idling nozzle and a main one, are sequentially exposed to air stream by throttling valve -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Carburettors with a plurality of means for adjusting the air to fuel mixing ratio according to the operational requirements, like acceleration, deceleration and idling of the engine.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Carburettors with means for influencing, e.g. enriching or keeping constant, fuel/air ratio of charge under varying conditions -> F02M7/00
[F02M 11/02] with throttling valve, e.g. of flap or butterfly type, in a later stage opening automatically -- F02M 11/02 covers multi-stage carburettors in which a throttling valve such as a flap or butterfly in a later stage is designed to open automatically, exposing additional fuel nozzles to the airstream.
[F02M 11/04] the later stage valves having damping means -- F02M 11/04 covers later-stage throttling valves in multi-stage or register-type carburettors that incorporate damping mechanisms to smooth valve motion. The damping means reduce shock and oscillation when successive fuel nozzles are exposed to the air stream, improving fuel metering stability.
[F02M 11/06] Other carburettors with throttling valve of flap or butterfly type -- F02M 11/06 classifies carburettors that employ a flap or butterfly throttling valve to control the air-fuel mixture, including multi-stage or register-type units where several fuel nozzles are sequentially exposed to the airstream as the valve moves. It falls under mechanical engineering for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 11/08] Register carburettors with throttling valve movable transversally to air passage
[F02M 11/10] Register carburettors with rotatable throttling valves
[F02M 11/105] Shape of the idling system -- The entry covers the geometric design of the idling system in register-type, multi-stage carburettors, including the shape of idle passages, throttle valves and nozzle arrangements that control how idle-only fuel jets are exposed to the airflow. It addresses any structural features that affect the flow and mixture formation at idle speed.
[F02M 1/12] with means for electrically heating thermostat -- The subclass F02M 1/12 covers carburettors for liquid fuels that include a thermostat equipped with an electrically heated element. The heating function maintains appropriate fuel temperature for reliable atomisation and engine operation, especially in cold start conditions.
[F02M 1/14] dependent on pressure in combustion-air- or fuel-air-mixture intake -- The F02M 1/14 subgroup covers carburettors for liquid fuels whose operation varies with the pressure in the combustion-air or fuel-air mixture intake. It is situated under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the supply of engines with combustible mixtures, and addresses pressure-dependent fuel-air mixing mechanisms.
[F02M 1/16] Other means for enriching fuel-air mixture during starting -- The subclass includes devices and methods that raise the fuel proportion in the intake charge when a liquid-fuel engine is started, covering enrichment valves, priming pumps, auxiliary injection or other supplemental means that are not the primary carburettor jets.
[F02M 1/165] Vaporizing light fractions from the fuel and condensing them for use during starting -- F02M 1/165 covers carburettor arrangements that vaporise the light-fraction components of a liquid fuel, separate them from the main charge and condense the vapour for use when the engine is started, improving ignition and reducing cold-start problems. It falls under the mechanical-engineering field for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures, specifically carburettors for liquid fuels.
[F02M 1/18] Enriching fuel-air mixture by depressing float to flood carburettor -- F02M 1/18 covers carburettors for liquid-fuel engines that enrich the fuel-air mixture by intentionally lowering the float, allowing excess fuel to flood the carburettor for improved starting or performance.
[F02M 1/185] Enriching the fuel-air mixture by altering the float chamber level by external means, e.g. by opening the input valve -- F02M 1/185 covers methods for enriching the fuel-air mixture in a carburettor by changing the level of fuel in the float chamber through external actions such as opening the inlet valve. The technique raises the amount of fuel drawn into the airstream to improve engine performance.
[F02M 13/00] Arrangements of two or more separate carburettors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The arrangement of two or more carburettors on the intake conduits of internal combustion engines and related constructional features.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Apparatus for adding small quantities of secondary fuel -> F02M25/00 - Re-atomising condensed fuel or homogenising fuel-air mixture -> F02M29/00
[F02M 13/02] Separate carburettors -- The F02M 13/02 entry defines arrangements that use two or more distinct carburettors to supply a combustion engine with fuel, allowing separate mixing or distribution of the combustible mixture. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering subclass dealing with general engine fueling systems.
[F02M 13/021] Particular constructional measures of the intake conduits between carburettors and cylinder -- F02M 13/021 relates to the specific design of intake conduits that connect two or more separate carburettors to the cylinders of a combustion engine. It covers particular constructional measures for the ducts between the carburettors and the engine, aiming to optimise fuel-air mixture delivery and overall engine performance.
[F02M 13/023] Special construction of the control rods -- F02M 13/023 covers special constructions of control rods in arrangements where two or more separate carburettors supply a combustion engine with fuel-air mixtures. It includes design features that modify the shape, positioning or operation of the rods to improve mixture distribution, engine response or overall performance.
[F02M 13/025] Equalizing pipes between the carburettors, e.g. between the float chambers -- This CPC subclass concerns arrangements where two or more separate carburettors are used and includes equalizing pipes that connect the carburettors, for example linking their float chambers to balance fuel levels. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 13/026] Common functional groups for several carburettors, e.g. common idling system -- This CPC subclass covers engine fuel-supply arrangements that use two or more separate carburettors linked by shared functional elements, for example a common idle control system, to provide coordinated delivery of the combustible mixture to the engine. It falls under supplying combustion engines in general with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 13/028] Tuning apparatus for multi-stage carburettors installations  other carburettor tuning apparatus  F02M19/01
[F02M 13/04] structurally united -- F02M 13/04 covers configurations where two or more separate carburettors are physically joined to form a single, structurally united assembly. The combined unit typically shares a common housing, inlet, or manifold to deliver the air-fuel mixture to a combustion engine, improving distribution uniformity or enabling distinct mixture settings within one integrated device.
[F02M 13/043] arranged in series, e.g. initial and main carburettor -- This subclass defines fuel-supply arrangements for combustion engines that employ two or more separate carburettors placed in series, for example an initial carburettor feeding a main carburettor to improve mixture preparation and delivery.
[F02M 13/046] arranged in parallel, e.g. initial and main carburettor -- The entry F02M 13/046 covers fuel-supply systems where two or more separate carburettors are installed in parallel, such as an initial (or pilot) carburettor together with a main carburettor, to provide a combined combustible mixture to a combustion engine.
[F02M 13/06] the carburettors using different fuels
[F02M 13/08] Carburettors adapted to use liquid and gaseous fuels, e.g. alternatively -- The entry F02M 13/08 covers carburettors designed to operate with both liquid and gaseous fuels, allowing the engine to run on either fuel type or a mixture. It includes dual-fuel arrangements where two or more separate carburettors are combined to supply the combustion chamber.
[F02M 15/00] Carburettors with heating, cooling or thermal insulating means for combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Carburettors with means for preventing freezing or overheating of the carburettor.
[F02M 15/02] with heating means, e.g. to combat ice-formation -- F02M 15/02 classifies carburettors equipped with heating devices intended to prevent ice formation in the combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture supplied to internal-combustion engines. The heating may be electric, exhaust-gas-derived or otherwise, and is used to keep the mixture above freezing temperatures during operation.
[F02M 15/022] near to manually operated throttle valve -- This CPC subclass covers carburettors that incorporate heating, cooling or thermal-insulating elements for the combustion air, fuel or their mixture, positioned close to a manually operated throttle valve. It pertains to devices that condition the charge just upstream of the valve to improve fuel metering and engine response.
[F02M 15/025] Fuel preheating -- F02M 15/025 covers devices that preheat fuel, air, or the fuel-air mixture in carburettors, including arrangements that provide heating, cooling or thermal insulation for these components to improve combustion and engine performance. It falls under supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures, specifically relating to carburettors with temperature-control means.
[F02M 15/027] Air or air-fuel mixture preheating -- F02M 15/027 covers devices that preheat the intake air or the air-fuel mixture before it enters a combustion engine, improving fuel vaporisation, combustion stability and efficiency. It includes heating elements, exhaust-gas recirculation or other thermal-insulating arrangements integrated in carburettors or manifold systems. The classification falls under supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures in the mechanical engineering hierarchy.
[F02M 15/04] the means being electrical -- This subgroup of the Cooperative Patent Classification addresses electrical devices that heat, cool or thermally insulate the combustion-air, fuel or air-fuel mixture in carburettors of combustion engines. It covers resistive heaters, electric thermostats, PTC elements and similar electrically powered temperature-control components used for starting, improving fuel atomisation or preventing icing.
[F02M 15/045] for the fuel system, e.g. built into the fuel conduits or nozzles -- This subclass covers carburettor features that are incorporated into the fuel system, such as heating, cooling or thermal-insulating elements built into fuel conduits or spray nozzles to control the temperature of the fuel or fuel-air mixture. It applies to mechanical-engineering provisions for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 15/06] Heat shieldings, e.g. from engine radiations -- F02M 15/06 covers heat shielding elements that protect carburettor parts or nearby components from thermal radiation generated by the engine, including shields, jackets or insulating barriers placed around the air-fuel mixture flow.
[F02M 17/00] Carburettors having pertinent characteristics not provided for in, or of interest apart from, the apparatus of preceding main groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Carburettors with a different type of configuration, like fuel bath carburettors or normal carburettors having special properties, like membrane carburettors.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Apparatus for treating combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixture by catalysts, electric means, magnetism, rays, sound waves or the like -> F02M27/00 - Combinations of carburettors and low-pressure fuel-injection apparatus -> F02M71/00
[F02M 17/02] Floatless carburettors -- F02M 17/02 covers floatless carburettors, which meter fuel to combustion engines without using a traditional float chamber. These devices typically employ diaphragms, pressure-compensating nozzles or other mechanisms to maintain a steady fuel flow regardless of vehicle orientation, improving reliability and performance in engine fueling systems.
[F02M 17/04] having fuel inlet valve controlled by diaphragm -- F02M 17/04 classifies carburettors in which the fuel-inlet valve is operated by a diaphragm, a feature not addressed in the preceding main groups. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the subcategory dealing with supplying engines with combustible mixtures, covering designs where diaphragm actuation controls fuel flow.
[F02M 17/06] having overflow chamber determining constant fuel level -- This subclass deals with carburettors that include an overflow chamber to maintain a constant fuel level, ensuring stable fuel supply despite fluctuations. The feature is distinct from the main groups covering general fuel-supply apparatus and is relevant for combustion engines that require a steady fuel head for proper mixture formation.
[F02M 17/08] Carburettors having one or more fuel passages opening in a valve-seat surrounding combustion-air passage, the valve being opened by passing air -- The subclass F02M 17/08 covers carburettors that have one or more fuel passages opening into a valve-seat surrounding the combustion-air passage, where the valve is opened by the incoming air flow. It pertains to devices for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures and falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and general engine fueling.
[F02M 17/09] the valve being of an eccentrically mounted butterfly type -- F02M 17/09 covers carburettors in which the throttle valve is an eccentrically mounted butterfly type, a design not addressed in the preceding main groups. This sub-category relates to supplying combustion engines with fuel-air mixtures using such specially configured valves.
[F02M 17/10] Carburettors having one or more fuel passages opening in valve-member of air throttle -- This CPC subclass covers carburettors in which one or more fuel passages open directly in the valve member that controls the air throttle, feeding fuel into the airstream at the throttle valve. Such configurations are treated as distinct from the more conventional carburettor arrangements described in earlier groups.
[F02M 17/12] the valve member being of butterfly type -- This subgroup covers carburettor designs where the flow-control valve is a butterfly-type disc that rotates about an axis transverse to the airflow. It is used to meter or shut off the combustible mixture within the carburettor, distinguishing it from other valve forms.
[F02M 17/14] Carburettors with fuel-supply parts opened and closed in synchronism with engine stroke -- The code F02M 17/14 designates carburettors whose fuel-supply components are opened and closed in synchronism with the piston strokes of a combustion engine. These devices control the timing of mixture delivery beyond the basic apparatus covered in broader groups, allowing precise coordination of intake and combustion phases.
[F02M 17/142] with the fuel exit nozzles in or near the valve seat or valve
[F02M 17/145] the valve being opened by the pressure of the passing fluid -- F02M 17/145 concerns carburettor designs in which the valve that admits the combustible mixture is opened by the pressure of the fluid flowing through it, rather than by separate mechanical actuation. This sub-group falls under supplying combustion engines with fuel-air mixtures and focuses on pressure-responsive valve mechanisms.
[F02M 17/147] Valve carburettor with simultaneous air and fuel control -- A valve carburettor that controls air and fuel flow simultaneously, falling under the sub-group for carburettors with features not addressed by earlier main groups, and classified within the supply of combustible mixtures to combustion engines (CPC F02M 17/147).
[F02M 17/16] Carburettors having continuously-rotating bodies, e.g. surface carburettors -- A carburettor that uses a continuously-rotating body-such as a surface-type carburettor-to meter and mix fuel with air for delivery to a combustion engine. The rotating element creates a dynamic surface that continuously renews the fuel film, improving mixture homogeneity and response during engine operation. This sub-group addresses designs not covered by earlier carburettor categories.
[F02M 17/18] Other surface carburettors -- F02M 17/18 designates "other surface carburettors", i.e., devices that feed fuel onto the surface of the intake charge for combustion engines and possess characteristics not covered by the more specific surface-carburettor subclasses. It falls under the broader group for supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 17/20] with fuel bath -- F02M 17/20 pertains to carburettors that employ a fuel-bath arrangement, where fuel is held in a pool and drawn into the airflow for mixing with combustible gases. This subclass captures designs that differ from standard carburettor types described in earlier groups, focusing on the bath-type fuel delivery mechanism.
[F02M 17/22] with air bubbling through bath -- F02M 17/22 covers carburettors that introduce air by bubbling it through a liquid fuel bath, a technique used to improve atomisation and mixing of the combustible mixture supplied to a combustion engine. It is a sub-group of provisions for supplying engines with fuel-air mixtures.
[F02M 17/24] with wicks -- F02M 17/24 covers carburettors that incorporate wicks to aid fuel delivery, a subcategory of devices for supplying combustible mixtures to combustion engines. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the broader group dealing with general engine fuel supply.
[F02M 17/26] with other wetted bodies -- F02M 17/26 covers carburettors that employ additional wetted surfaces beyond the standard devices of the main groups to assist fuel-air mixing. It relates to any auxiliary bodies or elements that become wet with fuel, enhancing atomisation or distribution of the combustible mixture supplied to combustion engines.
[F02M 17/28] fuel being drawn through a porous body -- F02M 17/28 pertains to carburettor arrangements in which the fuel is drawn through a porous body such as a wick, sintered metal or ceramic element, providing a means of fuel metering or mixing. The category includes designs whose operating principle or structural features are not covered by other carburettor groups.
[F02M 17/30] Carburettors with fire-protecting devices, e.g. combined with fire-extinguishing apparatus -- F02M 17/30 covers carburettors that incorporate fire-protecting or fire-extinguishing devices, such as integrated sprinklers or suppression systems, for use in combustion-engine applications. The subclass deals with safety-oriented features that are not part of standard carburettor designs and are intended to prevent or mitigate fires caused by fuel-air mixtures.
[F02M 17/32] automatically closing fuel conduits on outbreak of fire -- The entry covers carburettor designs that incorporate a fire-initiated shut-off system, automatically closing the fuel conduit when a fire is detected to stop fuel supply to a combustion engine. This safety feature is specific to carburettors and is not addressed in broader apparatus categories.
[F02M 17/34] Other carburettors combined or associated with other apparatus, e.g. air filters -- The subclass F02M 17/34 covers carburettors that are designed to be combined with or attached to other engine components, such as air-filter units, where the combination provides functions not addressed in the preceding main groups. It includes integrated or associated arrangements that impart specific characteristics beyond the basic carburettor apparatus.
[F02M 17/36] Carburettors having fitments facilitating their cleaning -- This CPC subclass F02M 17/36 covers carburettors equipped with fitments that facilitate cleaning, such as removable components, self-cleaning mechanisms or access openings. It lies within mechanical engineering → combustion engines → supplying engines with fuel mixtures, and addresses carburettor features not defined in preceding main groups.
[F02M 17/38] Controlling of carburettors, not otherwise provided for -- The F02M 17/38 subclass pertains to the control of carburettors in ways not specifically covered by other groups, encompassing novel mechanisms, timing methods, or adjustments that regulate the fuel-air mixture beyond standard designs. It includes any inventive features for managing carburettor operation that fall outside the previously defined apparatus categories.
[F02M 17/40] Selection of particular materials for carburettors, e.g. sheet metal, plastic, or translucent materials -- The CPC subclass F02M 17/40 relates to the selection of particular materials for carburetors, including sheet metal, plastics, or translucent components, emphasizing material choice rather than functional design. It sits within mechanical engineering, combustion engines, supplying engines with combustible mixtures, and carburetors with special characteristics not covered by earlier groups.
[F02M 17/42] Float-controlled carburettors not otherwise provided for -- The F02M 17/42 code refers to float-controlled carburettors. This classification is a subset of supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures. It covers carburettors with specific characteristics not found elsewhere.
[F02M 17/44] Carburettors characterised by draught direction and not otherwise provided for -- F02M 17/44 covers carburettors whose principal distinguishing feature is the direction of the draught through the device, such as upward, downward or transverse flow, when the design is not otherwise classified in earlier groups. It pertains to the supply of combustible mixtures to combustion engines and captures novel draught-direction configurations not covered by preceding entries.
[F02M 17/46] with down- draught -- F02M 17/46 classifies carburettors used to supply combustion engines with fuel-air mixtures when the mixture flows downward through the device, i.e., down-draught carburettors. It covers particular design features of these carburettors that are not addressed in the preceding main groups for engine supply.
[F02M 17/48] with up- draught -- The F02M 17/48 subclass covers carburettors that incorporate an up-draught arrangement for delivering the fuel-air mixture into a combustion engine, where the mixture flows upward into the intake manifold or cylinder. It applies to designs that are not covered by the preceding main groups and focuses on the specific geometry and flow direction of the draught system.
[F02M 17/50] Carburettors having means for combating ice-formation -- The subclass covers carburettors that incorporate anti-icing measures, such as heating elements, heated air or fuel passages, or chemical ice-prevention devices, to stop ice formation in the fuel-air mixture. It pertains to the design of carburettors beyond the basic apparatus, focusing on features that maintain reliable operation under cold-weather conditions.
[F02M 17/52] Use of cold, produced by carburettors, for other purposes -- F02M 17/52 relates to the exploitation of the cooling effect created by a carburettor beyond its primary function of mixing fuel and air. It covers devices or methods that use the cold air or temperature drop generated by the carburettor to cool other engine parts, auxiliary equipment or for environmental control.
[F02M 17/525] Use of the intake conduit vacuum -- It covers methods or arrangements that employ the vacuum created in the intake pipe of an internal combustion engine to perform functions such as fuel metering, air-fuel mixing or control of auxiliary devices, particularly in carburettor designs not covered by other main groups.
[F02M 19/00] Details, component parts, or accessories of carburettors, not provided for in, or of interest apart from, the apparatus of groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Residual group for details of carburettors which are not covered by main groups F02M1/00 – F02M17/00 . This group covers, e.g. metering orifices, spray nozzles, venturis, external control gear.  **Glossary:** - Dashpot: Type of damper
[F02M 19/01] Apparatus for testing, tuning, or synchronising carburettors, e.g. carburettor glow stands -- F02M 19/01 pertains to equipment used to examine, adjust or synchronise carburettors, including devices such as carburettor glow stands that heat and stabilise the unit before testing. The subclass covers apparatus for measuring performance, calibrating fuel-air mixtures and fine-tuning carburettor operation, independent of the main engine system.
[F02M 19/02] Metering-orifices, e.g. variable in diameter -- The CPC subclass F02M 19/02 covers metering orifices, including those whose diameter can be varied, that are used in carburettors for supplying combustible mixtures to combustion engines. It addresses details, component parts or accessories of carburettors that are not covered elsewhere in the classification.
[F02M 19/0203] the cross-sectional area being changed pneumatically, e.g. vacuum dependent -- This subcategory covers parts or accessories of carburetors that alter the cross-sectional area by pneumatic means, such as vacuum-dependent throttling devices used to regulate the fuel-air mixture supplied to combustion engines.
