It is located under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and the provision of combustible mixtures to those engines.
[F02M 19/0207] the cross-sectional area being changed electrically -- F02M 19/0207 covers parts of carburettors or related accessories that adjust the flow-channel cross-section by electrical means, enabling precise control of the fuel-air mixture supplied to combustion engines. This subclass falls under the broader group dealing with general fueling of engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 19/021] the cross-sectional area being changed mechanically -- F02M 19/021 covers carburettor components or accessories in which the cross-sectional flow area is altered by a mechanical means, such as adjustable throats or movable vanes, to regulate the combustible mixture supplied to a combustion engine.
[F02M 19/0214] Changing the nozzle cross-sectional area as a function of temperature -- The entry F02M 19/0214 covers inventions that vary the nozzle's cross-sectional area in accordance with temperature, thereby controlling the flow of fuel or air-fuel mixture supplied to a combustion engine. It pertains to carburettor components or accessories that adjust the nozzle opening dynamically to maintain optimal mixture properties under changing thermal conditions.
[F02M 19/0217] Movable mushroom-shaped spray nozzles -- F02M 19/0217 relates to movable mushroom-shaped spray nozzles that form part of carburettor assemblies used to supply combustible mixtures to combustion engines. The entry covers these specific nozzle designs when they are not addressed elsewhere in the carburettor sub-groups of the CPC.
[F02M 19/0221] with a roughened spray stimulating surface or the like, e.g. sieves near to the nozzle orifice -- This CPC subclass covers carburettor accessories that improve fuel atomisation by using a roughened spray-stimulating surface, such as sieves or similar structures placed close to the nozzle orifice. The textured element enhances the breakup of the combustible mixture before it enters the engine, aiding more uniform combustion.
[F02M 19/0225] Arrangement of nozzle in the suction passage  idling nozzles  F02M3/08 -- A sub-section of the Cooperative Patent Classification for mechanical engineering, specifically combustion-engine fuel-supply devices, covering the design of nozzles placed in the suction passage that provide idle-fueling. It pertains to carburettor accessories not otherwise covered, detailing the positioning and configuration of idling nozzles.
[F02M 19/0228] Ring nozzles -- F02M 19/0228 refers to ring-type nozzles employed in carburettors for supplying combustible mixtures to combustion engines. The subclass covers these specific nozzle components when they are not addressed by other carburettor categories.
[F02M 19/0232] Fuel nozzle with device for return flow of leaked fuel -- This subclass covers fuel nozzles for combustion engines that include a system to collect and return fuel that leaks past the main spray, reducing loss and emissions. It is located under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → supplying engines with combustible mixtures, specifically addressing carburettor accessories not otherwise classified.
[F02M 19/0235] Arrangements of several spray nozzles not provided for in  F02M3/00  or  F02M11/00 -- F02M 19/0235 relates to the design of fuel-spray systems for combustion engines that use more than one nozzle in configurations not covered by the main spray-nozzle groups F02M 3/00 or F02M 11/00. It addresses special arrangements of multiple nozzles for supplying combustible mixtures, typically as ancillary parts of carburettors.
[F02M 19/0239] in a fixed aerofoil profile -- F02M 19/0239 pertains to carburettor components or accessories that are incorporated into a fixed aerofoil shape, typically found in aircraft where the fuel-delivery system forms part of the wing profile. The entry covers design features that enable the supply of combustible mixtures to an engine through a permanently shaped aerodynamic surface.
[F02M 19/0242] with inserts of porous material -- F02M 19/0242 covers parts or accessories for carburettors that incorporate inserts made of porous material, intended to improve fuel-air mixing or vaporisation. It falls within the broader class for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures, focusing on specialized carburettor components not otherwise listed.
[F02M 19/0246] Nozzle cleaning -- F02M 19/0246 - Nozzle cleaning covers methods and devices for removing deposits from carburettor nozzles and related spray passages. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the supply of engines with combustible mixtures, specifically the accessories and component parts of carburettors that are not classified elsewhere.
[F02M 19/025] Metering orifices not variable in diameter -- F02M 19/025 covers fixed-diameter metering orifices used in carburettors that supply combustible mixtures to combustion engines. These orifices control fuel flow but cannot be adjusted in size during operation. The code falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and supplying engines with fuel mixtures.
[F02M 19/03] Fuel atomising nozzles -- F02M 19/03 relates to fuel-atomising nozzles employed in supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures, covering the detailed design, component parts and accessories of carburettors that are not otherwise classified. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for combustion-engine fuel supply.
[F02M 19/035] Mushroom-shaped atomising nozzles -- Mushroom-shaped atomising nozzles are components of carburettors used to supply combustion engines with fuel-air mixtures. Their characteristic rounded or flared outlet creates a fine spray by forcing fuel through a narrow passage, improving atomisation and mixing with air. The design is applied where precise fuel distribution and enhanced vapour formation are required, especially in engines that operate on varied combustible blends.
[F02M 19/04] Fuel-metering pins or needles -- This CPC subclass covers fuel-metering pins or needles employed in carburetors and related fuel-delivery devices to regulate the flow of combustible mixtures, focusing on their geometry, material and operating features. It pertains to components that meter fuel but are not otherwise classified in more specific carburetor groups.
[F02M 19/06] Other details of fuel conduits -- F02M 19/06 covers ancillary features of fuel-conduit systems for combustion engines, such as supplementary details, components or accessories of carburettors that are not otherwise classified. It includes parts that support or modify the fuel delivery path but are not integral to the primary carburettor apparatus.
[F02M 19/063] Built-in electric heaters -- F02M 19/063 designates built-in electric heaters that are incorporated into carburettor systems or related accessories used for supplying combustion engines with fuel mixtures. The code applies to heater components not covered elsewhere in the carburettor groups, covering their structure, integration and function within the fuel-delivery apparatus.
[F02M 19/066] Built-in cleaning elements, e.g. filters -- The F02M 19/066 entry covers built-in cleaning elements incorporated into carburettors, such as filters that remove particulates from the fuel-air mixture before it enters the engine. These components are considered part of the carburettor's internal structure rather than separate accessories, and they serve to maintain reliable combustion by preventing contamination of the mixture.
[F02M 19/08] Venturis -- Venturis are converging-diverging passages that generate a low-pressure zone to draw fuel into the airflow of a carburetor, facilitating the mixing of combustible mixture with the engine's intake. They are considered accessories of carburetors that are not covered by other specific carburetor groups.
[F02M 19/081] Shape of venturis or cross-section of mixture passages being adjustable -- The entry covers carburettor accessories that allow the venturi shape or the cross-section of the mixture passages to be adjusted, enabling variable airflow and fuel-air mixing. It falls under the F02M 19/08 group for details of components that supply combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 19/082] Venturi section being axially slidable in the mixture passages -- The CPC entry F02M 19/082 pertains to carburettor components in which the venturi section is designed to slide axially within the fuel-air mixture passages, allowing adjustment of flow geometry and mixture regulation during engine operation. It falls under the broader class for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 19/083] Venturi section consisting of a lamellae spring-like structure -- F02M 19/083 pertains to a venturi section that incorporates a lamellae spring-like structure, designed as an accessory for carburettors that supply combustion engines with fuel mixtures. The flexible lamellae grid enhances fuel-air mixing and stabilises pressure flow within the venturi.
[F02M 19/085] venturi section being made from elastic material, e.g. from rubber-like material -- The CPC subclass F02M 19/085 covers venturi sections of carburettors that are made from elastic or rubber-like materials. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the supply of combustible mixtures, addressing components not otherwise listed. This entry relates to flexible venturi designs used to shape airflow in fuel-air mixing.
[F02M 19/086] Venturi suction bypass systems -- The CPC class F02M 19/86 covers venturi suction bypass systems used with carburettors, including details, component parts and accessories that are not otherwise specified in the main carburettor groups. It relates to mechanisms that divert air-fuel mixture around the venturi to improve engine start-up, idle stability or fuel economy in combustion engines.
[F02M 19/087] Venturi throat consisting of automatically adjusting balls -- This CPC subclass covers carburettor venturi throats that employ one or more balls which move automatically to vary the throat cross-section and regulate the fuel-air mixture flow. The design lets the throat size adapt to engine speed or load without external control mechanisms. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and fuel-supply accessories.
[F02M 19/088] Whirl devices and other atomising means in or on the venturi walls -- F02M 19/088 relates to whirl devices and other atomising means that are placed in or mounted on the walls of a venturi, used to create a fine fuel-air mixture for supplying combustion engines. It covers components and accessories of carburettors that perform this atomisation function.
[F02M 19/10] in multiple arrangement -- This subclass concerns any carburettor parts, accessories or details that are not specified elsewhere, when they are provided in more than one arrangement for supplying combustible mixtures to combustion engines. It is situated under the broader group dealing with supplying combustion engines in general with combustible mixtures or constituents thereof.
[F02M 19/105] movable axially relative to each other -- F02M 19/105 covers components and accessories of carburettors that are not classified elsewhere, specifically those designed to move axially relative to one another. It falls under the supply of combustion engines with combustible mixtures, addressing mechanical engineering parts that adjust fuel-air flow by axial motion.
[F02M 19/12] External control gear, e.g. having dash-pots -- The CPC subclass F02M 19/12 covers external control devices for carburettor-type fuel-supply systems, such as dash-pots and other mechanisms that regulate the flow of combustible mixtures to combustion engines. It applies to accessories that are not part of the main carburettor apparatus but influence its operation, enabling precise mixture control in engines.
[F02M 19/122] Damping elements  pneumatic or hydraulic means for increasing idling speed  F02M3/062 -- F02M 19/122 classifies pneumatic or hydraulic damping elements that are employed to increase the idling speed of combustion engines, typically as accessories to carburettors. The entry covers components that modify the supply of combustible mixtures rather than the primary carburettor apparatus itself.
[F02M 19/124] Connecting rods between at least two throttle valves  F02M1/02  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass covers connecting rods that link two or more throttle valves in carburettor assemblies for combustion engines. It applies to details, components or accessories of carburettors not otherwise classified, focusing on the mechanical linkage that synchronizes valve movement.
[F02M 19/126] Connecting rods between at least a throttle valve and an accelerating pump  F02M7/08  takes precedence -- The code F02M 19/126 covers the connecting rods that link a throttle valve to an accelerating pump within carburettor accessories, a detail not addressed by the broader apparatus groups for supplying combustible mixtures to combustion engines.
[F02M 19/128] Reserve throttle idle return spring, e.g. for use upon failure of the main spring -- A reserve throttle idle-return spring is a backup spring fitted in carburettor throttle mechanisms to restore the valve to the idle position if the primary spring fails, ensuring continued engine control.
[F02M 2003/067] the valve for controlling the cross-section of the conduit being rotatable, but not being a screw-like valve -- This subclass of CPC F02M concerns valves whose flow-path area is varied by rotating the valve element rather than by a screw-type mechanism, allowing the conduit cross-section to be adjusted for fluid control.
[F02M 2003/105] Needle adjustment limiter caps -- Needle adjustment limiter caps are small components fitted on fuel-injection needle valves to restrict their travel and prevent over-adjustment, ensuring precise fuel metering in internal combustion engines.
[F02M 2023/008] by injecting compressed air directly into the combustion chamber -- The F02M 2023/008 subgroup of the Cooperative Patent Classification covers internal-combustion engines that introduce compressed air directly into the combustion chamber. This approach is used to increase the air-fuel mixture density, improve combustion efficiency and reduce emissions by enhancing the mixing of fuel and oxidizer.
[F02M 2025/0845] Electromagnetic valves -- F02M 2025/0845 covers electromagnetic valves, which use an electric coil or magnetic field to move a valve element and control the flow of liquids, gases, or vapors. The subgroup includes solenoid-operated and other electrically actuated valve designs used in industrial, automotive and hydraulic systems for on-off or proportional flow regulation.
[F02M 2025/0863] with means dealing with condensed fuel or water, e.g. having a liquid trap -- The subclass F02M 2025/0863 covers apparatus that incorporates features for managing condensate of fuel or water, such as a liquid-trap system that collects or separates the condensed fluid from the main flow. It is used for engines, burners or other combustion devices where condensate must be removed to maintain performance and prevent damage.
[F02M 2025/0881] with means to heat or cool the canister -- F02M 2025/0881 designates internal-combustion engines that incorporate a device for heating or cooling the fuel or air canister. The feature may be used to improve start-up, combustion efficiency, emissions control or protect the canister from extreme temperatures. It applies to any engine where temperature regulation of the canister is an integral part of the apparatus.
[F02M 2026/001] Arrangements; Control features; Details -- The code F02M 2026/001 refers to the specific arrangement and control features of machinery used in the production of paper, board or similar sheet materials, covering how sensors, actuators, feedback loops and other functional components are organized and integrated to regulate the process. It includes detailed designs of control circuitry, software logic and mechanical linkages that ensure proper operation of the equipment.
[F02M 2026/002] EGR valve being controlled by vacuum or overpressure -- F02M 2026/002 covers internal-combustion-engine exhaust-gas-recirculation systems in which the EGR valve is actuated by a vacuum source or by an over-pressure condition, using the pressure differential to modulate valve opening for emissions control.
[F02M 2026/0025] Intake vacuum or overpressure modulating valve -- The CPC subclass F02M 2026/0025 covers valves that regulate the pressure of the intake charge in reciprocating engines, either by creating a vacuum to assist filling or by relieving excess pressure to prevent overload. It includes mechanisms that adjust the opening degree in response to operating conditions, ensuring optimal air-fuel mixture flow and engine performance.
[F02M 2026/003] EGR valve controlled by air measuring device -- The code refers to an exhaust-gas recirculation (EGR) valve whose operation is regulated by a device that measures intake air quantity or flow. The classification covers systems that adjust EGR rates based on real-time air-mass or airflow data to optimise combustion and emissions control.
[F02M 2026/004] EGR valve controlled by a temperature signal or an air/fuel ratio (lambda) signal -- F02M 2026/004 covers exhaust-gas recirculation valves whose operation is regulated by a temperature sensor or by an air-fuel-ratio (lambda) signal, enabling the engine control system to adjust EGR flow based on combustion conditions and emissions targets.
[F02M 2026/005] EGR valve controlled by an engine speed signal -- Relates to exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) valves whose operation is regulated by a signal derived from engine speed, allowing the valve opening to be varied in proportion to rotational speed to control exhaust-gas flow for emissions and performance management.
[F02M 2026/0055] EGR valve controlled by inertia, e.g. having a pendulum controlling the EGR valve -- F02M 2026/0055 classifies exhaust-gas recirculation valves whose opening is governed by inertial forces, for example a pendulum that moves with engine vibrations or vehicle acceleration to adjust the valve position.
[F02M 2026/006] EGR specially adapted for intake systems having two or more fuel injectors per cylinder -- F02M 2026/006 covers exhaust-gas recirculation arrangements that are specifically designed for engines whose intake ports contain two or more fuel injectors per cylinder. The classification addresses the integration of EGR channels, valves or passages with multi-injector layouts to optimise mixture formation and emissions control.
[F02M 2026/007] EGR specially adapted for engines having two or more spark plugs per cylinder -- The CPC subclass F02M 2026/007 covers exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) systems that are specifically engineered for internal-combustion engines equipped with two or more spark plugs per cylinder, a configuration used to improve combustion stability and efficiency. It includes designs, control strategies and hardware adaptations that integrate EGR flow with the multi-spark-plug layout to optimise emissions and performance.
[F02M 2026/008] EGR specially adapted for engines having a combustion chamber divided by the piston at TDC into two or more sub-chambers -- This subgroup covers exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) systems that are specifically designed for engines whose piston at top-dead-center creates two or more separate combustion sub-chambers. It addresses the routing, control and integration of EGR in such divided-chamber engine architectures.
[F02M 2026/009] EGR combined with means to change air/fuel ratio, ignition timing, charge swirl in the cylinder -- This CPC subgroup relates to exhaust-gas recirculation devices that are combined with means for varying the air-fuel ratio, controlling ignition timing and modifying the swirl of the charge within the cylinder, typically for internal-combustion engines.
[F02M 2027/047] with a pulsating magnetic field -- The subclass covers devices that treat liquids by applying a magnetic field whose intensity or direction varies cyclically over time. A pulsating magnetic field is used to influence fluid motion, mixing, pumping or particle separation, providing dynamic control of the process rather than a constant field.
[F02M 2037/082] Details of the entry of the current supply lines into the pump housing, e.g. wire connectors, grommets, plugs or sockets -- This subclass covers the means by which electrical power is introduced into pump housings, including the use of wire connectors, grommets, plugs or sockets that provide sealed or protected entry points for supply lines.
[F02M 2037/085] Electric circuits therefor -- The CPC group F02M 2037/085 covers the design and arrangement of electric circuits that serve electric machines, including power, control and signal wiring, connectors, switches and protective devices that enable the machine's operation. It includes circuitry for power conversion, regulation and distribution within the machine and related auxiliary circuits.
[F02M 2037/087] Controlling fuel pressure valve -- F02M 2037/087 in the CPC covers devices that regulate the pressure of fuel delivered to an engine, specifically valves that control fuel pressure in fuel-injection or carburetion systems. The classification applies to designs that maintain a set pressure range to ensure proper combustion and engine performance.
[F02M 2041/1438] Arrangements or details pertaining to the devices classified in  F02M41/14  and subgroups -- The code groups information on the overall layout, mounting and auxiliary features of the devices defined in F02M41/14 and its sub-groups, such as hydraulic or pneumatic pumps, motors and valves. It covers how these elements are arranged, connected, sealed and integrated with control or supporting structures.
[F02M 2041/1444] Feed-pumps;  Arrangements or pressure regulation therefor -- F02M 2041/1444 relates to feed-pumps and the means for arranging or regulating their pressure. It covers devices that deliver fluid to a downstream process, including pump selection, mounting, speed control, pressure sensors and control systems that maintain desired feed rates.
[F02M 2041/145] Throttle valves for metering fuel to the pumping chamber -- In the Cooperative Patent Classification, code F02M 2041/145 pertains to throttle valves that meter fuel into the pumping chamber of an engine, controlling flow according to pressure, speed or demand to ensure proper combustion and performance.
[F02M 2041/1455] Shuttles  per se , or shuttles associated with throttle valve for metering fuel admitted to the pumping chamber -- The code F02M 2041/1455 relates to mechanical shuttles that are part of, or directly linked to, throttle valves used to meter fuel into the pumping chamber of internal-combustion engines. It covers designs where the shuttle moves to control the amount of fuel passing through the valve during each injection cycle.
[F02M 2041/1461] Axial displacement of rotor for varying piston stroke or for controlling fuel passages -- This CPC subgroup covers inventions where the rotor of a pump or engine is moved axially to change the stroke length of a piston or to adjust the flow paths for fuel. It includes mechanisms that shift the rotor position to vary displacement or control fuel delivery, typically in internal combustion engines or hydraulic pumps.
[F02M 2041/1466] Piston-stroke variation by other means than axial displacement of rotor -- This CPC subgroup covers rotary internal combustion engines in which the effective piston travel is altered by means other than axial movement of the rotor, for example by changing eccentricity, cam profiles, or chamber geometry to vary stroke length. It addresses variable-displacement designs that adjust stroke without shifting the rotor.
[F02M 2041/1472] Devices for limiting maximum delivery or for providing excess fuel for starting or for correcting advance at starting -- The CPC subclass F02M 2041/1472 belongs to the fuel-metering section for internal-combustion engines and covers devices that limit maximum fuel delivery, provide extra fuel for starting, or adjust ignition advance during start-up. Examples include chokes, start-enrichers and advance-correction mechanisms aimed at improving start reliability and preventing over-fueling.
[F02M 2041/1477] Releasing fuel pressure or adjusting quantity-time characteristics of fuel delivery, e.g. by conducting pressurised fuel to a variable volume space, an accumulator or a return conduit -- This CPC subclass concerns devices that control fuel pressure or modify the quantity-time profile of fuel delivery, for example by sending pressurised fuel to a variable-volume space, an accumulator or a return conduit, thereby adjusting the amount and timing of fuel supplied.
[F02M 2041/1483] Variably timed valves controlling fuel passages, e.g. sleeve-valves mounted on the rotor -- The CPC subclass F02M 2041/1483 covers engine designs in which the timing of fuel-delivery valves can be varied during operation, such as sleeve-type valves that are mounted on a rotary rotor and regulate the fuel passages. It pertains mainly to rotary-type internal combustion engines where adjustable valve timing is used to control intake or injection flow.
[F02M 2041/1488] Electric actuation of valves or other parts -- F02M 2041/1488 covers systems in which electrical energy is used to operate hydraulic or pneumatic valves, throttles, or other moving parts. It includes electric actuators, solenoids and related control circuitry that provide opening, closing or positioning functions within fluid power circuits. This subclass is applied to inventions that replace manual, pneumatic or hydraulic actuation with an electric drive for improved precision and automation.
[F02M 2041/1494] Details of cams, tappets, rotors, venting means, specially arranged valves, e.g. in the rotor
[F02M 21/00] Apparatus for supplying engines with non-liquid fuels, e.g. gaseous fuels stored in liquid form -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Systems like tanks, vaporizers, pumps and injectors for supplying engines with a non-liquid, mostly gaseous fuel. A gas engine differs from a petrol engine in many ways. The fuel and air are mixed differently. The combustion of a gas/ air mixture is different form a liquid fuel/ air mixture and requires adaptation of the combustion space. Also the energy contents of gas is much lower than that of a liquid fuel. Since (natural) gas is a clean, economical and readily available fuel, many industrial engines are either designed or modified to use gas, as distinguished from gasoline.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Engines characterised by operating on gaseous fuels; plants including such engines -> F02B43/00  **Glossary:** - Gaseous fuel: Fuel being a gas at atmospheric pressure and room temperature
[F02M 21/02] for gaseous fuels -- This CPC subclass covers devices that supply combustion engines with gaseous fuels, especially when the gas is stored in a liquid form. It includes apparatus for metering, vaporising, or injecting such fuels into engines, and lies under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 21/0203] characterised by the type of gaseous fuel -- The F02M 21/0203 subclass covers apparatus for feeding combustion engines with gaseous fuels, emphasizing the particular type of gas used. It includes devices that store the fuel in liquid form but supply it as a gas, such as systems for LPG, CNG or LNG, and related feeding mechanisms.
[F02M 21/0206] Non-hydrocarbon fuels, e.g. hydrogen, ammonia or carbon monoxide -- F02M 21/0206 classifies devices that feed internal combustion engines with non-hydrocarbon, non-liquid fuels such as hydrogen, ammonia or carbon monoxide, typically stored as gases or in liquefied form.
[F02M 21/0209] Hydrocarbon fuels, e.g. methane or acetylene -- This subclass covers apparatus that supplies internal combustion engines with non-liquid hydrocarbon fuels such as methane or acetylene, which are stored in a liquid or pressurised form. It includes devices for handling, vaporising, metering or injecting these gaseous fuels into the engine.
[F02M 21/0212] comprising at least 3 C-Atoms, e.g. liquefied petroleum gas [LPG], propane or butane -- This CPC subclass concerns devices that feed internal combustion engines with non-liquid fuels stored in liquid form and containing three or more carbon atoms, such as liquefied petroleum gas, propane or butane. It lies within mechanical engineering, under combustion engines and the supply of engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 21/0215] Mixtures of gaseous fuels; Natural gas; Biogas; Mine gas; Landfill gas -- This subclass concerns the use of gaseous fuel mixtures such as natural gas, biogas, mine gas or landfill gas for feeding combustion engines, especially when the gases are stored in liquid form. It includes apparatus and methods for supplying non-liquid fuels to engines.
[F02M 21/0218] Details on the gaseous fuel supply system, e.g. tanks, valves, pipes, pumps, rails, injectors or mixers -- This classification covers components and arrangements for delivering gaseous fuels stored in liquid form to combustion engines. It includes tanks, valves, piping, pumps, fuel rails, injectors and mixers designed for non-liquid fuel supply systems.
[F02M 21/0221] Fuel storage reservoirs, e.g. cryogenic tanks -- F02M 21/0221 covers apparatus that stores fuel for combustion engines in the form of reservoirs, such as cryogenic tanks, designed for non-liquid or liquefied gases used as engine fuel. The classification addresses the design, construction and integration of these storage units within engine supply systems.
[F02M 21/0224] Secondary gaseous fuel storages -- F02M 21/0224 covers apparatus that provides a secondary storage for gaseous fuels kept in a liquid state and supplied to combustion engines. The devices are intended to supplement the primary fuel system by delivering gas-phase fuel on demand, typically for engines running on non-liquid fuels.
[F02M 21/0227] Means to treat or clean gaseous fuels or fuel systems, e.g. removal of tar, cracking, reforming or enriching -- The subclass covers apparatuses and methods for treating or cleaning gaseous fuels or fuel-system components before they are supplied to an engine, including processes such as tar removal, cracking, reforming or enrichment of the gas. It is placed under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, supplying engines with non-liquid fuels.
[F02M 21/023] Valves; Pressure or flow regulators in the fuel supply or return system -- This CPC subclass addresses valves and pressure or flow regulators that form part of the fuel-supply or return system for engines using non-liquid fuels, such as gaseous fuels stored in liquid form. It is situated under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 21/0233] Details of actuators therefor -- F02M 21/0233 covers the specific design and operation of the actuators that control devices supplying combustion engines with non-liquid fuels such as gaseous fuels stored in liquid form. It details the mechanisms that regulate fuel flow, pressure and delivery timing to ensure reliable engine performance.
[F02M 21/0236] Multi-way valves; Multiple valves forming a multi-way valve system -- The F02M 21/0236 subclass covers valve assemblies that provide two or more flow paths by combining several individual valves into a single multi-way valve system, used to control the delivery of gaseous or otherwise non-liquid fuels to combustion engines. It includes designs where the valves are integrated to direct, switch, or mix fuel streams in engine-fuel-supply apparatus.
[F02M 21/0239] Pressure or flow regulators therefor -- The code F02M 21/0239 covers pressure-or-flow regulators used in devices that feed combustion engines with non-liquid fuels, such as gaseous fuels that are stored in a liquid state. These regulators control the delivery of the fuel to ensure proper engine operation.
[F02M 21/0242] Shut-off valves; Check valves; Safety valves; Pressure relief valves -- F02M 21/0242 is a subclass of mechanical-engineering patents dealing with the supply of combustion engines with non-liquid fuels such as gaseous fuels stored in liquid form. It specifically covers shut-off, check, safety and pressure-relief valves used in the fuel-delivery apparatus for engines.
[F02M 21/0245] High pressure fuel supply systems; Rails; Pumps; Arrangement of valves -- F02M 21/0245 covers high-pressure fuel supply systems for combustion engines that use non-liquid fuels, such as gases stored in liquid form. The class includes fuel rails, high-pressure pumps and the arrangement of valves that regulate the delivery of the gaseous fuel to the engine.
[F02M 21/0248] Injectors -- This subclass covers injectors that feed non-liquid fuels - typically gases that have been liquefied for storage, such as propane or methane - into combustion-engine cylinders. It relates to the apparatus that meters and delivers the gaseous fuel for engine operation.
[F02M 21/0251] Details of actuators therefor -- The subclass covers the design, construction, operation and performance characteristics of actuating devices that control the delivery of non-liquid fuels - such as gaseous fuels stored in liquid form - to combustion engines, including valves, pumps, regulators and related control mechanisms.
[F02M 21/0254] Electric actuators, e.g. solenoid or piezoelectric -- F02M 21/0254 covers electric actuators, such as solenoid or piezoelectric devices, that are employed in systems that feed non-liquid fuels-particularly gaseous fuels stored in liquid form-to combustion engines. The classification pertains to the mechanisms that control fuel flow or mixture preparation via electrically driven movement.
[F02M 21/0257] Details of the valve closing elements, e.g. valve seats, stems or arrangement of flow passages -- The CPC class F02M 21/0257 covers specific features of valve closing components in apparatus that supply non-liquid fuels-such as gaseous fuels stored as liquids-to combustion engines. It includes details of valve seats, stems, and the arrangement of flow passages that form part of the fuel-delivery system.
[F02M 21/026] Lift valves, i.e. stem operated valves -- Lift valves, also called stem-operated valves, are components that control the flow of gaseous or liquid-stored fuels in equipment that supplies non-liquid fuels to combustion engines. They work by moving a stem to open or close the valve passage, allowing precise regulation of fuel mixture delivery in systems such as gas-to-liquid storage units.
[F02M 21/0263] Inwardly opening single or multi nozzle valves, e.g. needle valves -- This subclass covers valve designs where the flow passage opens toward the interior of the valve body, typically using a needle or similar element, used in devices that feed gaseous or liquefied gases to engines. It includes single or multiple nozzle configurations that open inwardly to control fuel metering.
[F02M 21/0266] Hollow stem valves; Piston valves; Stems having a spherical tip -- The CPC subclass F02M 21/0266 pertains to valve components used in apparatus that feed non-liquid fuels to combustion engines. It covers hollow-stem valves, piston-type valves and stems terminating in a spherical tip, all employed in systems delivering gaseous fuels stored in liquid form.
[F02M 21/0269] Outwardly opening valves, e.g. poppet valves -- F02M 21/0269 relates to outwardly opening valves, chiefly poppet-type designs, employed in devices that deliver non-liquid fuels-such as gaseous fuels stored in liquid form-to combustion engines. The entry covers valve constructions that open away from their seats to permit controlled fuel flow into the engine.
[F02M 21/0272] Ball valves; Plate valves; Valves having deformable or flexible parts, e.g. membranes; Rotatable valves -- F02M 21/0272 covers ball, plate, and other rotatable valves, including those with deformable membranes, used in apparatus that supplies combustion engines with non-liquid fuels such as gaseous fuels stored in liquid form. The class is nested under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 21/0275] for in-cylinder direct injection, e.g. injector combined with spark plug -- This CPC subclass F02M 21/0275 covers devices that provide in-cylinder direct injection of a fuel, such as an injector integrated with a spark plug, for engines using non-liquid fuels. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, supplying engines with combustible mixtures, focusing on apparatus for delivering gaseous fuels stored as liquids.
[F02M 21/0278] Port fuel injectors for single or multipoint injection into the air intake system -- The subclass covers devices that introduce non-liquid fuels, such as gaseous fuels stored in liquid form, into an engine's air-intake system via port injection. It includes single-point and multi-point fuel injectors that deliver the fuel to the intake manifold or individual cylinder ports before the throttle valve.
[F02M 21/0281] Adapters, sockets or the like to mount injection valves onto engines; Fuel guiding passages between injectors and the air intake system or the combustion chamber -- The subclass covers devices that attach injection valves to an engine and the fuel-guiding passages that direct fuel from those injectors to the air-intake system or combustion chamber, particularly for non-liquid fuels such as gaseous fuels stored in liquid form.
[F02M 21/0284] Arrangement of multiple injectors or fuel-air mixers per combustion chamber -- The code F02M 21/0284 covers the layout of two or more injectors or fuel-air mixers within a single combustion chamber, typically used for supplying engines with non-liquid fuels such as gaseous fuels stored in liquid form. It addresses how these multiple devices are positioned and coordinated to ensure proper mixing and distribution of the combustible mixture to the chamber.
[F02M 21/0287] characterised by the transition from liquid to gaseous phase  F02M21/06  takes precedence ; Injection in liquid phase; Cooling and low temperature storage -- F02M 21/0287 covers apparatus that feeds combustion engines with non-liquid fuels by first storing the fuel in liquid form and then converting it to a gaseous phase before injection, typically involving low-temperature storage and cooling. It is a subcategory of F02M 21/06, which deals with injection in the liquid phase.
[F02M 21/029] Arrangement on engines or vehicle bodies; Conversion to gaseous fuel supply systems -- F02M 21/029 covers arrangements on engines or vehicle bodies that convert or adapt them to use gaseous fuels, including systems that store fuel in liquid form and supply it as gas to combustion engines. It relates to apparatus for supplying engines with non-liquid fuels such as pressurised or liquid-stored gaseous fuels.
[F02M 21/0293] Safety devices; Fail-safe measures -- This subclass covers safety devices and fail-safe mechanisms incorporated into systems that deliver non-liquid fuels such as compressed or liquefied gases to combustion engines. It includes devices that prevent accidental fuel release, ignition, or over-pressurisation, ensuring safe operation of the fueling apparatus.
[F02M 21/0296] Manufacturing or assembly; Materials, e.g. coatings -- The subclass concerns the manufacturing and assembly of apparatus that feed non-liquid fuels, especially gaseous fuels stored as liquids, focusing on the selection and application of materials such as protective or functional coatings. It includes processes for preparing, coating, and joining components to enhance corrosion resistance, thermal stability, and overall reliability of the fuel-supply system.
[F02M 21/04] Gas-air mixing apparatus -- F02M 21/04 covers apparatus that prepares a combustible gas-air mixture for feeding combustion engines, particularly when the fuel is a gaseous substance stored in a liquid state. The devices mix the gas with air in the correct proportion before delivery to the engine's intake system.
[F02M 21/042] Mixer comprising a plurality of bores or flow passages -- This subclass concerns mixers that contain several bores or flow passages for blending gaseous or cryogenic fuels before they enter a combustion engine. It falls within apparatus for supplying non-liquid fuels to engines, CPC code F02M 21/042.
[F02M 21/045] Vortex mixer -- A vortex mixer is a fuel-delivery device that generates a rotating flow to thoroughly blend gaseous or liquefied fuels with other combustible constituents before they are fed to a combustion engine. The induced vortex keeps the mixture uniform, preventing phase separation and improving combustion stability for engines using non-liquid fuels.
[F02M 21/047] Venturi mixer -- F02M 21/047 designates a venturi mixer incorporated in equipment that supplies combustion engines with non-liquid fuels, such as gases stored in liquid form. The mixer creates a pressure drop that draws and blends the fuel with air, providing a uniform combustible mixture for stable engine operation.
[F02M 21/06] Apparatus for de-liquefying, e.g. by heating -- This subclass relates to devices that turn liquefied fuels back into gas, usually by heating, so that engines can be fed with non-liquid fuels such as gases stored in liquid form. It is positioned under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and the supply of combustible mixtures to those engines.
[F02M 21/08] for non-gaseous fuels -- F02M 21/08 refers to apparatus that delivers non-gaseous fuels-such as solid fuels, powdered fuels or liquefied gases in a non-vapor form-to combustion engines. The subclass covers feed systems, injectors and conveyance devices that introduce these fuels directly into the combustion chamber or combustion air stream, distinguishing them from liquid-fuel supply mechanisms.
[F02M 21/10] for fuels with low melting point, e.g. apparatus having heating means -- F02M 21/10 relates to devices that feed engines with fuels that melt at low temperatures, such as gaseous fuels stored in liquid form, and includes heating arrangements to maintain the fuel above its melting point during supply.
[F02M 21/12] for fuels in pulverised state -- F02M 21/12 covers devices that feed combustion engines with fuels in pulverised form, such as powdered solid fuels or finely ground particles, enabling the engine to run on non-liquid combustible mixtures. The apparatus includes mechanisms for transporting, metering and injecting the powdered fuel from storage to the combustion chamber.

[F02M 2200/04] Fuel-injection apparatus having means for avoiding effect of cavitation, e.g. erosion -- A fuel-injection device designed for combustion engines that includes features to prevent cavitation-induced damage, such as erosion, during operation. The apparatus employs specific geometry, pressure-control or material-selection measures that mitigate bubble formation and collapse within the injection system.
[F02M 2200/05] Fuel-injection apparatus having means for preventing corrosion -- A fuel-injection system equipped with components or treatments that inhibit corrosion of the injector or associated passages, ensuring reliable fuel delivery and longevity of the engine. It includes any arrangement such as dampers, resonators, or control strategies intended to reduce injection-related noise, without falling under other more detailed injection-technology subclasses.

[F02M 2200/18] Fuel-injection apparatus having means for maintaining safety not otherwise provided for -- The CPC subclass F02M 2200/18 covers fuel-injection systems that include extra safety mechanisms not otherwise specified, such as fail-safe shutdown, over-pressure protection or leak prevention, used when supplying combustion engines in general. [F02M 2200/20] Fuel-injection apparatus with permanent magnets -- The subclass F02M 2200/20 covers fuel-injection devices for internal-combustion engines that incorporate permanent magnets as part of the injection mechanism. [F02M 2200/26] Fuel-injection apparatus with elastically deformable elements other than coil springs -- Fuel-injection apparatus that uses elastic, deformable components other than coil springs-such as diaphragms, membranes or elastomeric elements-to regulate the delivery of fuel to a combustion engine. [F02M 2200/29] Fuel-injection apparatus having rotating means -- This CPC subclass concerns fuel-injection devices for internal combustion engines that incorporate a rotating element such as a rotary pump, distributor or cam to meter and deliver fuel. [F02M 2200/30] Fuel-injection apparatus having mechanical parts, the movement of which is damped -- The CPC subclass F02M 2200/30 covers fuel-injection devices for combustion engines in which the mechanical parts that move during injection are equipped with damping means to control their motion, reduce shock and vibration, and improve injection accuracy and component durability. [F02M 2200/304] using hydraulic means -- The code refers to fuel-injection systems for combustion engines in which hydraulic pressure or fluid power is employed to control the delivery of fuel or its constituents, governing timing, quantity or atomisation of the injection. [F02M 2200/306] using mechanical means -- This CPC subclass deals with mechanical fuel-injection devices for supplying combustible mixtures to combustion engines, covering pumps, piston-type, gear, cam-driven or other purely mechanical means of metering and delivering fuel. [F02M 2200/31] Fuel-injection apparatus having hydraulic pressure fluctuations damping elements -- A fuel-injection system for combustion engines that incorporates elements to damp hydraulic pressure fluctuations, improving the stability of fuel delivery. [F02M 2200/40] Fuel-injection apparatus with fuel accumulators, e.g. a fuel injector having an integrated fuel accumulator -- F02M 2200/40 covers fuel-injection devices that incorporate a built-in fuel accumulator, such as injectors with an integrated reservoir that buffers pressure fluctuations, enhances fuel delivery stability and improves atomization. [F02M 2200/44] Valves, e.g. injectors, with valve bodies arranged side-by-side -- The subclass covers injectors or other fuel valves whose individual valve bodies are positioned adjacent to one another in a side-by-side arrangement. This configuration is used to deliver combustible mixtures to combustion engines, allowing multiple injection points within a single unit. It applies to designs where the bodies share a common housing or are mounted in parallel.
[F02M 2200/46] Valves, e.g. injectors, with concentric valve bodies -- F02M 2200/46 covers valves, especially fuel injectors, that consist of multiple concentric valve bodies arranged one inside another to control fuel flow in combustion engines. The design enables staged or sequential injection by using inner and outer passages.
[F02M 2200/50] Arrangements of springs for valves used in fuel injectors or fuel injection pumps -- The CPC subclass F02M 2200/50 covers the design and layout of spring mechanisms that actuate valves in fuel-injector or fuel-injection-pump systems. It addresses how springs are arranged to control valve opening and closing during the delivery of combustible mixtures to combustion engines. This subclass falls under the broader group dealing with supplying combustion engines with fuel mixtures.
[F02M 2200/502] Springs biasing the valve member to the open position -- This CPC entry covers fuel-injection devices that use a spring to hold a valve member in the open position, where the spring's bias ensures the valve stays open unless acted on by other forces. It applies to combustion-engine fuel systems that supply engines with combustible mixtures and are not otherwise classified.
[F02M 2200/505] Adjusting spring tension by sliding spring seats -- This CPC subclass (F02M 2200/505) relates to fuel-injection devices in which the tension of a spring is varied by sliding the spring seats relative to one another, thereby adjusting the spring preload that controls injection timing or pressure in combustion-engine fuel systems.
[F02M 2200/507] Adjusting spring tension by screwing spring seats -- This CPC subclass covers mechanisms that fine-tune the tension of a spring in a fuel-injection device by rotating or screwing the spring seat, providing precise control of injection pressure and flow. It is located under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures → details of fuel-injection apparatus not otherwise provided for.
[F02M 2200/60] Fuel-injection apparatus having means for facilitating the starting of engines, e.g. with valves or fuel passages for keeping residual pressure in common rails -- The CPC subclass F02M 2200/60 covers fuel-injection devices that incorporate features to aid engine start-up, such as valves or fuel passages designed to retain residual pressure in a common rail. It is applied to systems where the injector not only meters fuel but also maintains a ready-to-inject pressure, improving reliability of starting in combustion engines.
[F02M 2200/70] Linkage between actuator and actuated element, e.g. between piezoelectric actuator and needle valve or pump plunger -- F02M 2200/70 covers the mechanical linkage that transmits motion from an actuator to a fuel-injection element such as a needle valve or pump plunger, especially when the actuator is a piezoelectric device. It falls under the subclass dealing with details of fuel-injection apparatus for combustion engines.
[F02M 2200/701] mechanical -- This CPC subclass (F02M 2200/701) covers specific details of fuel-injection apparatus used to supply combustion engines with combustible mixtures when those design features are not captured by any more specific subclass. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → supplying engines with fuel mixtures, and addresses any injection-system aspects that are otherwise uncategorised.
[F02M 2200/702] with actuator and actuated element moving in different directions, e.g. in opposite directions -- A fuel-injection device for a combustion engine in which the actuator and the element it drives travel in different, often opposite, directions, allowing separate motion paths for pressurising the fuel and delivering it into the combustion chamber. This arrangement improves control of injection timing and spray pattern while reducing mechanical interference between moving parts.
[F02M 2200/703] hydraulic -- The CPC subclass F02M 2200/703 covers hydraulic fuel-injection devices used to supply combustion engines with combustible mixtures when the apparatus is not otherwise specified. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → supplying engines with fuel mixtures, focusing on hydraulic injection mechanisms.
[F02M 2200/704] with actuator and actuated element moving in different directions, e.g. in opposite directions -- This subclass covers fuel-injection devices in which the actuator and the element it drives move in opposite directions, for example a piston shifting one way while a cam pushes another part the reverse way, and is used for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 2200/705] with means for filling or emptying hydraulic chamber, e.g. for compensating clearance or thermal expansion -- The subclass deals with fuel-injection apparatus that include means for filling or emptying a hydraulic chamber, such as a device used to compensate clearance or thermal expansion in combustion engines. It is applied in the general supply of combustion engines with combustible mixtures or their constituents.
[F02M 2200/706] Valves for filling or emptying hydraulic chamber -- F02M 2200/706, within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for combustion engines, covers valves used to fill or empty hydraulic chambers in fuel-injection apparatus that are not otherwise specified. The entry addresses the design, structure and operation of such valves for supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 2200/707] with means for avoiding fuel contact with actuators, e.g. isolating actuators by using bellows or diaphragms -- The CPC subclass F02M 2200/707 covers fuel-injection devices that incorporate features to keep fuel from reaching any moving or control parts, such as actuators, by isolating them with bellows, diaphragms or similar protective elements. This detail falls under the broader group for supplying combustion engines with fuel mixtures and addresses a specific means of preventing fuel-actuator contact.
[F02M 2200/708] with hydraulic chambers formed by a movable sleeve -- This subclass covers fuel-injection devices for combustion engines in which the hydraulic chambers are created by a movable sleeve that defines the injection chamber. The design is used to meter and deliver combustible mixtures when the sleeve moves to vary chamber volume, enabling precise control of fuel delivery.
[F02M 2200/80] Fuel injection apparatus manufacture, repair or assembly -- The class covers technologies for making, repairing or assembling fuel-injection devices used to supply combustible mixtures to combustion engines, specifically those aspects that are not detailed in more specific subclasses. It applies to general or otherwise unspecified features of fuel-injection apparatus in mechanical engineering.
[F02M 2200/8007] Storing data on fuel injection apparatus, e.g. by printing, by using bar codes or EPROMs -- The subclass concerns techniques for recording information directly on fuel-injection hardware, such as printed symbols, bar-code markings or EPROM memory chips, to convey part specifications, calibration settings or traceability data. [F02M 2200/856] characterised by mounting injector to fuel or common rail, or -- In the Cooperative Patent Classification, code F02M 2200/856 falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Supplying engines with combustible mixtures, and covers fuel-injection apparatus not otherwise specified. [F02M 23/00] Engine-pertinent apparatus for feeding, or treating before their admission to engine, combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus for adding secondary air to the air/fuel mixture for varying or keeping constant the air/fuel ratio under varying engine operating circumstances, like acceleration, deceleration, cold start and high torque.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Engines characterised by air-storage chambers -> F02B21/00  **Glossary:** - Secondary air: air not entering the engine via the main air intake
[F02M 23/001] built into a flange -- F02M 23/001 covers apparatus that is incorporated into a flange for feeding or pre-treating combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixtures before they enter an engine, such as flanged connectors, manifolds or coupling devices that integrate mixing, heating, filtering or metering functions. This sub-class falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the supply of combustible mixtures to engines.
[F02M 23/003] Particular shape of air intake -- F02M 23/003 concerns the specific geometric configuration of an air-intake system for combustion engines, including devices that feed or treat combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixtures before entry into the engine. It addresses the shape of the inlet passage, ducts, or passages that influence airflow characteristics, pressure recovery, and mixing efficiency. This subclass falls under the broader category of apparatus for supplying combustible mixtures to engines.
[F02M 23/005] with a damping element in the secondary air control -- This subgroup deals with apparatus that controls secondary air entering a combustion engine and includes a damping element to smooth pressure or flow pulsations. The damping component-such as a valve, damper or similar device-helps stabilise combustion, reduce noise and improve emissions performance.
[F02M 23/006] Valves specially shaped for supplying secondary air -- Valves specially shaped for supplying secondary air are engine components whose geometry is optimized to introduce additional air into the combustion process, typically after the primary air-fuel mixture has entered the cylinder. Their design improves mixing, enhances combustion efficiency and helps reduce emissions in combustion-engine applications.
[F02M 23/02] with personal control, or with secondary-air valve controlled by main combustion-air throttle -- This subclass pertains to devices that deliver or condition the air-fuel mixture to a combustion engine and whose flow is regulated directly by the operator or by a secondary-air valve that follows the main throttle setting.
[F02M 23/025] Optional operation by means of a hand or foot switch -- F02M 23/025 covers engine-related devices that allow the feeding or pre-treatment of combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixtures to be started, stopped or otherwise controlled by an optional hand-operated or foot-operated switch, providing manual activation of the apparatus.
[F02M 23/03] the secondary air-valve controlled by main combustion-air throttle -- F02M 23/03 covers engine-related devices that regulate the secondary air valve in coordination with the primary combustion-air throttle, ensuring controlled admission of additional air to the combustion chamber for improved mixture management and engine performance. This falls under supplying combustion engines with air-fuel mixtures.
[F02M 23/04] with automatic control -- The subclass F02M 23/04 covers engine-related equipment that automatically controls the feeding or pre-treatment of combustion-air, fuel, or air-fuel mixtures before they enter the engine, using automatic regulators, sensors and feedback mechanisms to adjust flow, composition or temperature and optimise combustion.
[F02M 23/06] dependent on engine speed -- This subgroup covers devices that adjust fuel or air flow based on engine speed, such as speed-controlled injectors or throttle mechanisms that vary the mixture to match rpm. It is used to optimise performance, emissions, and fuel efficiency.
[F02M 23/062] Secondary air flow cut-off at low speed -- F02M 23/062 covers devices that stop or reduce the supply of secondary combustion-air to an engine when it is running at low speed, helping to optimise fuel use and lower emissions.
[F02M 23/065] Secondary air flow cut-off at high torque -- The CPC subclass F02M 23/065 deals with devices that cut off or interrupt the secondary air flow to a combustion engine when the engine is operating at high torque, typically to improve efficiency or control emissions during high-load conditions. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and fuel-supply systems.
[F02M 23/067] Secondary air admission flow at high speeds and with the main butterfly valve closed, e.g. during deceleration -- F02M 23/067 concerns devices that supply secondary combustion air when the main butterfly valve is closed, such as during vehicle deceleration at high speeds. The entry covers apparatus for admitting or treating air-fuel mixtures before they enter a combustion engine to maintain stable combustion or reduce emissions under these conditions.
[F02M 23/08] dependent on pressure in main combustion-air induction system -- The F02M 23/08 sub-group covers engine-related devices that regulate the supply or pre-treatment of combustion-air, fuel or their mixture by monitoring and responding to pressure conditions within the main air-induction system. Such apparatus adjust flow rates, timing or composition of the charge in proportion to the measured pressure, improving engine performance and efficiency.
[F02M 23/085] specially adapted for secondary air admission during braking or travelling down steep slopes -- This CPC subclass addresses devices that introduce secondary air into a combustion-engine intake specifically during braking or when travelling down steep slopes, aimed at stabilising combustion, reducing emissions or enhancing power under deceleration. It is part of the hierarchy for mechanical engineering, combustion engines and the supply of combustible mixtures.
[F02M 23/09] using valves directly opened by low pressure -- F02M 23/09 covers engine-related feeding or treatment devices in which the intake or fuel valves are actuated directly by a low-pressure source, such as the pressure of the incoming air-fuel mixture, rather than by mechanical or higher-pressure control mechanisms. This approach simplifies valve operation and can improve the responsiveness of combustion-engine supply systems.
[F02M 23/095] with balls which are not spring loaded -- This subclass covers engine-related devices that feed or condition combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixtures before they enter the engine and employ balls that are not spring loaded to regulate or meter the flow, such as ball valves, ball-type distributors or rotary ball chambers.
[F02M 23/10] dependent on temperature, e.g. engine temperature -- F02M 23/10 covers apparatus that modifies the supply of combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixture in response to temperature conditions, such as engine-temperature-controlled metering, heating or cooling of the mixture, or temperature-dependent regulation of flow to optimise performance.
[F02M 23/12] characterised by being combined with device for, or by secondary air effecting, re-atomising of condensed fuel -- F02M 23/12 covers engine-related equipment that feeds or treats combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixtures before they enter the engine and is distinguished by incorporating a secondary-air system that re-atomises condensed fuel. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and the supply of engines with combustible mixtures. This subclass specifies devices where secondary air is used to break up liquid fuel droplets for improved combustion.
[F02M 23/14] characterised by adding hot air -- This CPC subclass covers engine-related apparatus that feed or treat combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixtures by deliberately adding heated air to the stream. The inclusion of hot air as a treatment step is the defining technical feature that distinguishes this group from other feeding or conditioning systems.
[F02M 25/00] Engine-pertinent apparatus for adding non-fuel substances or small quantities of secondary fuel to combustion-air, main fuel or fuel-air mixture -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus for adding water, lubricant vapours or fuel vapours to the combustion mixture. These substances are added to improve the combustion properties of the fuel/air mixture and/or make the exhaust gases cleaner or prevent harmful gases from escaping to atmosphere.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Adding secondary air to fuel-air mixture -> F02M23/00 - Adding exhaust gases -> F02M26/00 - Fuel-injection apparatus operating simultaneously on two or more fuels or on a liquid fuel and another liquid -> F02M43/00
[F02M 25/022] Adding fuel and water emulsion, water or steam -- The CPC subclass F02M 25/022 covers apparatus that injects a fuel-water emulsion, pure water or steam into the air-fuel mixture or directly into the combustion chamber of an engine, typically to improve efficiency, reduce emissions or provide cooling. It includes systems that add small quantities of water or secondary fuel together with the main fuel or intake air.
[F02M 25/0221] Details of the water supply system, e.g. pumps or arrangement of valves -- The entry F02M 25/0221 covers the design and components of water-supply systems used in combustion-engine installations that introduce non-fuel additives or secondary fuels, including pumps, valve arrangements and related hardware that control water flow to the engine or its fuel-air mixture.
[F02M 25/0222] Water recovery or storage -- The F02M 25/0222 subclass covers devices used in combustion-engine fueling systems that incorporate mechanisms for recovering, collecting or storing water generated during the mixing or injection process, allowing the water to be retained for later use or disposal within the engine-related apparatus.
[F02M 25/0224] Water treatment or cleaning  F02M25/032  takes precedence
[F02M 25/0225] Water atomisers or mixers, e.g. using ultrasonic waves -- F02M 25/0225 covers devices that atomise or mix water-or other small-quantity secondary fuels-into the combustion-air or fuel-air mixture of an engine, including systems that use ultrasonic waves for fine droplet formation. These apparatuses are intended to improve combustion efficiency, reduce emissions, or provide cooling by introducing a controlled amount of non-fuel liquid into the intake or charge air stream.
[F02M 25/0227] Control aspects; Arrangement of sensors; Diagnostics; Actuators -- F02M 25/0227 covers the control aspects, sensor placement, diagnostic methods and actuator mechanisms for devices that introduce small amounts of secondary fuel, additives or non-fuel substances into the combustion-air, main fuel or fuel-air mixture of a combustion engine. The class is situated under supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures or constituents thereof.
[F02M 25/0228] Adding fuel and water emulsion -- The class includes apparatus that injects a fuel-water emulsion or other secondary fuel into the intake air or fuel-air mixture of a combustion engine, blending small amounts of water with the main fuel to modify combustion characteristics, improve efficiency or lower emissions.
[F02M 25/025] Adding water -- F02M 25/025 covers apparatus for introducing water, or small amounts of water, into the combustion-air, main fuel or fuel-air mixture of a combustion engine. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the supply of engines with combustible mixtures, addressing devices that add non-fuel liquids to improve performance or emissions.
[F02M 25/028] into the charge intakes -- The F02M 25/028 subclass concerns apparatus that introduces non-fuel substances or small amounts of secondary fuel into the charge-intake system of combustion engines, affecting the air-fuel mixture supplied to the cylinders. It covers devices for dosing additives, lubricants, water, or other secondary fuels in the intake manifold or intake ducts.
[F02M 25/03] into the cylinder -- F02M 25/03 covers apparatus that introduces non-fuel additives or small quantities of secondary fuel directly into the cylinder of a combustion engine, where they mix with the main fuel-air charge. The devices are used to modify combustion characteristics, emissions, or performance by delivering the substances at a precise point in the engine cycle.
[F02M 25/032] Producing and adding steam -- F02M 25/032 covers devices that generate steam and inject it into the combustion-air, main-fuel or fuel-air mixture of an engine. The steam serves as a secondary fluid to improve combustion, control temperature, or aid the introduction of small quantities of additives, functioning as part of systems that supply combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 25/035] into the charge intakes -- F02M 25/035 covers equipment that injects additives or trace secondary fuels into the intake manifold or charge intake of an internal-combustion engine. Such devices deliver small quantities of non-fuel substances or alternative fuels together with the main fuel to influence combustion characteristics.
[F02M 25/038] into the cylinder -- The F02M 25/038 class covers apparatus that injects non-fuel substances or small quantities of secondary fuel directly into a combustion-engine cylinder. It includes devices for adding additives, lubricants, water, or other constituents to the fuel-air mixture during the intake or compression stroke.
[F02M 25/06] adding lubricant vapours -- The subclass F02M 25/06 covers apparatus or methods that introduce vaporised lubricants into the intake air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture of a combustion engine, typically in small quantities to reduce friction, wear or emissions. It includes devices that inject, evaporate or otherwise dispense oil or other lubricating fluids as part of the engine's supply system.
[F02M 25/08] adding fuel vapours drawn from engine fuel reservoir -- F02M 25/08 concerns devices that draw fuel vapour from an engine's fuel reservoir and introduce it into the combustion-air or fuel-air mixture, often to enhance atomisation, performance or emissions control. It is a sub-category of mechanical-engineering combustion-engine supplies for adding non-fuel substances or small amounts of secondary fuel.
[F02M 25/0809] Judging failure of purge control system -- F02M 25/0809 relates to detecting or judging a malfunction of the purge-control system in combustion-engine installations that introduce non-fuel substances or small amounts of secondary fuel into the air, main fuel or fuel-air mixture. It covers apparatus and methods used to monitor purge operation and signal failure to maintain proper mixture preparation.
[F02M 25/0818] having means for pressurising the evaporative emission space -- F02M 25/0818 covers engine-related devices that introduce non-fuel additives or trace secondary fuels into the combustion-air, main-fuel or fuel-air mixture, and that include a mechanism for pressurising the evaporative-emission space to aid delivery. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the broader category of supplying engines with combustible mixtures or constituents.
[F02M 25/0827] by monitoring engine running conditions -- F02M 25/0827 relates to devices or methods that monitor an engine's operating parameters in order to regulate the addition of additives, trace fuels or small quantities of secondary fuel to the intake air, main fuel or the fuel-air mixture, thereby optimizing combustion performance.
[F02M 25/0836] Arrangement of valves controlling the admission of fuel vapour to an engine, e.g. valve being disposed between fuel tank or absorption canister and intake manifold -- F02M 25/0836 pertains to valve arrangements that control the admission of fuel vapour into an internal-combustion engine, for example a valve located between the fuel tank or an absorption canister and the intake manifold. The classification covers apparatus that introduces small quantities of secondary fuel or additives into the air-fuel mixture to modify combustion.
[F02M 25/0854] Details of the absorption canister -- F02M 25/0854 covers the design and construction details of absorption canisters that inject small quantities of secondary fuels, additives or non-fuel substances into the combustion-air, main-fuel or fuel-air mixture of a combustion engine, enabling precise control of mixture composition. This subcategory falls under supplying combustion engines in general with combustible mixtures or constituents thereof.
[F02M 25/0872] Details of the fuel vapour pipes or conduits -- This CPC subclass pertains to the design, arrangement, and specifications of piping or conduits that transport fuel vapour within an engine or its fuel delivery system, including dimensions, materials, connections, and routing. It covers components used to introduce small quantities of secondary fuel or additives into the air-fuel mixture for combustion control.
[F02M 25/089] Layout of the fuel vapour installation -- F02M 25/089 covers the arrangement of equipment that introduces fuel vapour into a combustion engine, including the layout of vapour generation, storage and delivery components that add small amounts of secondary fuel or additives to the air-fuel mixture.
[F02M 25/10] adding acetylene, non-waterborne hydrogen, non-airborne oxygen, or ozone -- F02M 25/10 classifies engine-related devices that introduce limited quantities of acetylene, hydrogen not dissolved in water, oxygen not supplied as air, or ozone into the combustion-air, main fuel, or fuel-air mixture. The purpose is to modify ignition or combustion characteristics, often to enhance efficiency, power output, or emissions control.
[F02M 25/12] the apparatus having means for generating such gases -- It covers engine-related apparatus that incorporates means for generating gases which are introduced as additives to the combustion-air, main-fuel or fuel-air mixture, such as secondary fuels or non-fuel substances.
[F02M 25/14] adding anti-knock agents, not provided for in subgroups -- This CPC subclass covers devices that introduce anti-knock additives into the intake air, main fuel, or fuel-air mixture of combustion engines, where the additive is not covered by more specific subclasses. It relates to apparatus that injects small quantities of secondary fuel or non-fuel substances to improve octane rating and reduce knocking.
[F02M 2547/00] Special features for fuel-injection valves actuated by fluid pressure -- The classification F02M 2547/00 covers special design elements of fuel-injection valves that are operated by fluid pressure, such as pressure-responsive pistons, springs or diaphragms that modify spray pattern, timing or quantity of fuel delivered to a combustion engine. These features are intended to improve the control of combustible mixtures supplied to the engine.
[F02M 2547/001] Control chambers formed by movable sleeves -- This subclass covers combustion-engine fuel-injection valves in which a fluid-pressure actuation creates a control chamber bounded by a sleeve that can slide or rotate to adjust the chamber volume. The movable sleeve forms part of the valve's hydraulic circuit, allowing precise regulation of fuel delivery to the engine.
[F02M 2547/003] Valve inserts containing control chamber and valve piston -- This subclass covers valve inserts that incorporate a control chamber and a piston for fuel-injection valves driven by fluid pressure, used in supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 2547/005] Fuel injectors without fuel return, i.e. the pressure in the control chamber is released into the combustion chamber with fluid flow only in one direction -- This subclass deals with fuel-injection valves whose control-chamber pressure is vented straight into the combustion chamber, eliminating any fuel-return line so that fuel moves only in one direction. It covers designs that use fluid-pressure actuation without recirculating fuel.
[F02M 2547/006] Springs assisting hydraulic closing force -- The subclass F02M 2547/006 covers fuel-injection valves for combustion engines that use a spring to augment the hydraulic pressure that closes the valve. It pertains to systems where the spring assists the fluid-pressure actuation, improving valve sealing and response during fuel supply. This feature is classified under supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 2547/008] Means for influencing the flow rate out of or into a control chamber, e.g. depending on the position of the needle -- This CPC subclass concerns devices that adjust the flow rate of fluid entering or leaving a control chamber of a fuel-injection valve, typically by linking the flow to the needle position. It is used in combustion engines for controlling fuel supply and falls under F02M 2547/008.
[F02M 26/00] Engine-pertinent apparatus for adding exhaust gases to combustion-air, main fuel or fuel-air mixture, e.g. by exhaust gas recirculation [EGR] systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus for adding exhaust gases to the combustion mixture. The exhaust gas is added to improve the combustion properties of the fuel/air mixture and/or make the exhaust gases cleaner or prevent harmful gases from escaping to atmosphere.
[F02M 26/01] Internal exhaust gas recirculation, i.e. wherein the residual exhaust gases are trapped in the cylinder or pushed back from the intake or the exhaust manifold into the combustion chamber without the use of additional passages -- F02M 26/01 covers internal exhaust-gas recirculation methods in which residual exhaust gases are retained in the cylinder or redirected from the intake or exhaust manifold back into the combustion chamber without employing separate external passages or dedicated EGR ducts. This approach mixes the exhaust gases directly with fresh charge to control combustion temperature and emissions. It is used in combustion-engine designs that integrate EGR functionality within the existing engine architecture.
[F02M 26/02] EGR systems specially adapted for supercharged engines -- This subclass relates to exhaust-gas-recirculation devices that are specially designed for use in supercharged internal-combustion engines, where the recycled exhaust is introduced into the intake-air or fuel-air mixture to control emissions and improve performance.
[F02M 26/03] with a single mechanically or electrically driven intake charge compressor -- F02M 26/03 covers engine-related devices that incorporate a single mechanically or electrically driven compressor to pressurize the intake charge. The compressor is used in systems that introduce exhaust gases into the combustion-air or fuel-air mixture, such as exhaust-gas recirculation arrangements for internal-combustion engines.
[F02M 26/04] with a single turbocharger -- This subclass covers engine-related apparatus that introduce exhaust gases into the combustion-air or fuel-air mixture by means of a single turbocharger, typically as part of exhaust-gas recirculation (EGR) systems for internal combustion engines.
[F02M 26/05] High pressure loops, i.e. wherein recirculated exhaust gas is taken out from the exhaust system upstream of the turbine and reintroduced into the intake system downstream of the compressor -- It covers high-pressure loops in combustion-engine exhaust-gas recirculation where exhaust gas is taken from the exhaust system upstream of the turbine and re-introduced into the intake downstream of the compressor, forming a closed high-pressure EGR path.
[F02M 26/06] Low pressure loops, i.e. wherein recirculated exhaust gas is taken out from the exhaust downstream of the turbocharger turbine and reintroduced into the intake system upstream of the compressor -- F02M 26/06 covers low-pressure exhaust-gas recirculation loops in which exhaust gases are tapped downstream of the turbo-charger turbine and fed back into the intake upstream of the compressor, enabling EGR operation without high-pressure routing and improving engine efficiency and emissions control.
[F02M 26/07] Mixed pressure loops, i.e. wherein recirculated exhaust gas is either taken out upstream of the turbine and reintroduced upstream of the compressor, or is taken out downstream of the turbine and reintroduced downstream of the compressor
[F02M 26/08] for engines having two or more intake charge compressors or exhaust gas turbines, e.g. a turbocharger combined with an additional compressor -- This subclass relates to engines that employ two or more intake-charge compressors or exhaust-gas turbines, such as a turbocharger combined with an additional compressor, to raise the pressure of the air-fuel mixture. It covers configurations where multiple compression stages or turbine-driven devices work together to enhance engine performance or efficiency.
[F02M 26/09] Constructional details, e.g. structural combinations of EGR systems and supercharger systems -- The F02M 26/09 subclass concerns the structural design of systems that combine exhaust-gas recirculation (EGR) with supercharging in combustion engines. It covers how EGR components are integrated with superchargers, including mounting arrangements, housing configurations and other constructional details.
