[F02M 26/10] having means to increase the pressure difference between the exhaust and intake system, e.g. venturis, variable geometry turbines, check valves using pressure pulsations or throttles in the air intake or exhaust system -- This subclass covers engine-related devices that deliberately raise the pressure difference between the exhaust and intake streams, for example by using venturi tubes, variable-geometry turbines, check valves that exploit pressure pulsations, or throttles placed in the air-intake or exhaust passages.
[F02M 26/11] Manufacture or assembly of EGR systems -- The F02M 26/11 class covers the manufacture or assembly of exhaust-gas recirculation (EGR) systems, which are apparatuses that feed a portion of engine exhaust back into the combustion-air, main-fuel or fuel-air mixture to control emissions and improve efficiency.
[F02M 26/12] characterised by means for attaching parts of an EGR system to each other or to engine parts -- The class includes engine-related devices that provide a means for connecting elements of an exhaust-gas recirculation system to one another or to engine components. It covers attachment structures such as brackets, clamps, welding, fastening or adhesive techniques used to secure EGR pipes, valves, coolers and related parts.
[F02M 26/13] Arrangement or layout of EGR passages, e.g. in relation to specific engine parts or for incorporation of accessories -- It covers the design and positioning of exhaust-gas-recirculation channels within an internal combustion engine, including their routing relative to components such as intake manifolds, valves or other engine parts, and the integration of accessories like filters, coolers or sensors. The classification addresses the overall layout of EGR passages to achieve desired mixing, temperature control or emission reduction.
[F02M 26/14] in relation to the exhaust system -- The code F02M 26/14 covers devices that integrate exhaust gas recirculation functions into the engine's exhaust system, such as valves, passages or control units that route exhaust gases back into the intake or fuel mixture. It falls under supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures, specifically apparatus that adds exhaust gases to the air-fuel charge.
[F02M 26/15] in relation to engine exhaust purifying apparatus -- F02M 26/15 classifies devices that integrate exhaust-gas recirculation with engine exhaust-purifying systems, covering arrangements that feed a portion of the exhaust back into the combustion-air or fuel-air mixture to reduce emissions. The code is applied to apparatus that combines EGR functions with catalytic converters, filters or other treatment elements intended to clean the engine's exhaust before it is released.
[F02M 26/16] with EGR valves located at or near the connection to the exhaust system -- F02M 26/16 pertains to engine-related devices that incorporate an exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) valve positioned at or close to the junction with the exhaust system. The valve routes exhaust gases back into the intake air or fuel-air mixture to control combustion characteristics, emissions, and efficiency in combustion engines supplied with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 26/17] in relation to the intake system -- It pertains to intake system aspects of engine-pertinent apparatus that introduce exhaust gas into the combustion-air or fuel-air mixture, such as exhaust-gas recirculation arrangements. The classification covers ducts, valves, mixers or control means incorporated in the intake to manage recycled exhaust flow.
[F02M 26/18] Thermal insulation or heat protection -- F02M 26/18 covers thermal insulation and heat-protective measures for apparatus that introduce exhaust gases into the combustion-air or fuel-air mixture, such as exhaust-gas recirculation (EGR) systems. The classification includes heat shields, insulating liners, cooling jackets and other structures designed to limit temperature rise and protect components from thermal stress.
[F02M 26/19] Means for improving the mixing of air and recirculated exhaust gases, e.g. venturis or multiple openings to the intake system -- F02M 26/19 classifies components that enhance the mixing of intake air with recirculated exhaust gases in combustion engines, such as venturi-shaped passages or multiple openings in the intake manifold, typically employed in exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) systems to improve combustion efficiency and emissions control.
[F02M 26/20] Feeding recirculated exhaust gases directly into the combustion chambers or into the intake runners -- The class covers devices that introduce exhaust gases that have been recirculated directly into an engine's combustion chambers or intake passages. It includes exhaust-gas-recirculation systems that feed the reclaimed gases to the fuel-air mixture to improve combustion efficiency, reduce emissions, or control temperature.
[F02M 26/21] with EGR valves located at or near the connection to the intake system -- F02M 26/21 covers apparatus for supplying combustion engines with air-fuel mixtures that include exhaust-gas recirculation, specifically where the EGR valve is installed at or close to the point where exhaust gases join the intake system. This sub-group addresses the design and placement of such valves to control recirculated exhaust flow in engines.
[F02M 26/22] with coolers in the recirculation passage -- F02M 26/22 covers exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) arrangements in which the recirculated exhaust stream is cooled within the recirculation passage, typically by a heat-exchanger or cooler integrated in the pipe. The cooling improves combustion stability, reduces NOx formation and lowers engine inlet charge temperature.
[F02M 26/23] Layout, e.g. schematics -- The F02M 26/23 subclass covers the graphical representation of exhaust-gas-recirculation apparatus for combustion engines, including schematic, plan and wiring layouts that illustrate how exhaust gases are introduced into the intake, fuel or fuel-air mixture. It focuses on the overall arrangement of components rather than individual element details.
[F02M 26/24] with two or more coolers -- F02M 26/24 covers engine-related devices that introduce exhaust gases into the combustion-air, main-fuel or fuel-air mixture, such as exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) systems, where the recirculated exhaust is passed through two or more coolers before mixing. This sub-class is part of the broader category for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures or constituents.
[F02M 26/25] with coolers having bypasses -- F02M 26/25 covers engine-related devices that introduce exhaust gases into the intake air or fuel-air mixture and incorporate a cooler equipped with one or more bypass paths, allowing a portion of the gas flow to circumvent the cooling element. The bypass arrangement is used to regulate gas temperature and EGR effectiveness.
[F02M 26/26] characterised by details of the bypass valve -- This subclass covers engine-related devices that introduce exhaust gas into the intake air or fuel stream, such as exhaust gas recirculation systems, where the invention is defined by the specific construction, operation or control of the bypass valve that regulates the recirculated flow.
[F02M 26/27] with air-cooled heat exchangers -- F02M 26/27 covers engine-related devices that introduce exhaust gases into the combustion-air, main fuel or fuel-air mixture and incorporate air-cooled heat exchangers for recovering heat from the exhaust stream. It applies to systems such as exhaust-gas recirculation (EGR) where cooling is achieved with air-cooled exchangers.
[F02M 26/28] with liquid-cooled heat exchangers -- This subclass covers devices that introduce exhaust gases into the engine's air-fuel mixture by means of liquid-cooled heat exchangers, such as exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) systems that chill the recirculated gases before they re-enter the combustion chamber. It is used to control intake temperature, reduce emissions and improve engine efficiency.
[F02M 26/29] Constructional details of the coolers, e.g. pipes, plates, ribs, insulation or materials -- F02M 26/29 covers the structural aspects of coolers used in exhaust-gas recirculation and similar engine systems, including the design of pipes, plates, ribs, insulation and the selection of materials. The subclass details how these components are shaped, arranged and fabricated to achieve efficient cooling of exhaust gases before they re-enter the combustion process.
[F02M 26/30] Connections of coolers to other devices, e.g. to valves, heaters, compressors or filters -- This subclass covers the way coolers are linked to other components such as valves, heaters, compressors or filters in systems that supply combustion engines with fuel mixtures. It addresses the piping, fittings and coupling arrangements used to integrate cooling devices into exhaust-gas recirculation or other engine-related circuits. The focus is on the mechanical connections that facilitate heat exchange and flow control.
[F02M 26/31] Air-cooled heat exchangers -- F02M 26/31 designates air-cooled heat exchangers used in combustion-engine systems, particularly those that add exhaust gases to the intake air-fuel mixture in exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) arrangements. The class falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and the supply of engines with combustible mixtures. It covers apparatus that cools exhaust gases by exposing them to ambient air before they are mixed with fresh intake charge.
[F02M 26/32] Liquid-cooled heat exchangers -- F02M 26/32 pertains to liquid-cooled heat exchangers employed in apparatus that adds exhaust gases to the combustion-air, main fuel or fuel-air mixture, such as EGR coolers that lower exhaust temperature before recirculation.
[F02M 26/33] controlling the temperature of the recirculated gases -- F02M 26/33 relates to devices and methods that control the temperature of gases recirculated in combustion-engine systems, such as exhaust-gas-recirculation arrangements. It covers heating, cooling or thermostatic regulation of the recirculated stream to optimise combustion performance and emissions.
[F02M 26/34] with compressors, turbines or the like in the recirculation passage -- F02M 26/34 covers engine-related systems that introduce exhaust gases into the combustion-air or fuel mixture using a recirculation line that contains a compressor, turbine or similar device to boost pressure or recover energy. Such arrangements are employed in exhaust gas recirculation (EGR) circuits to improve engine efficiency and emissions control.
[F02M 26/35] with means for cleaning or treating the recirculated gases, e.g. catalysts, condensate traps, particle filters or heaters -- F02M 26/35 covers apparatus that adds exhaust gases to the combustion-air or fuel-air mixture and includes devices for cleaning or treating the recirculated gases, such as catalytic converters, condensate traps, particle filters, or heating elements, used in general combustion-engine supply systems.
[F02M 26/36] with means for adding fluids other than exhaust gas to the recirculation passage -- This subclass covers engine-related equipment that introduces fluids other than exhaust gas-such as water, steam, or other additives-into the recirculation passage of an exhaust-gas-recirculation system, modifying the composition of the recirculated charge to affect performance, emissions or cooling.
[F02M 26/37] with temporary storage of recirculated exhaust gas -- F02M 26/37 covers engine systems that temporarily hold recirculated exhaust gases before re-injecting them into the intake or fuel-air mixture, such as exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) devices with a storage chamber that smooths flow, reduces emissions and improves combustion control.
[F02M 26/38] with two or more EGR valves disposed in parallel -- F02M 26/38 covers engine-related devices that return exhaust gas to the intake-air or fuel-air mixture using two or more exhaust-gas-recirculation valves arranged in parallel, providing separate flow paths for improved control of emissions, combustion stability or engine efficiency.
[F02M 26/39] with two or more EGR valves disposed in series -- F02M 26/39 relates to engine-pertinent devices that introduce exhaust gases into the combustion-air or fuel-air mixture by employing two or more exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) valves arranged in series, a configuration used to control emissions and improve combustion efficiency.
[F02M 26/40] with timing means in the recirculation passage, e.g. cyclically operating valves or regenerators -- This subclass deals with engine-related devices that introduce exhaust gases into the combustion-air or fuel-air stream using timing mechanisms in the recirculation passage, such as cyclically operating valves or regenerators.
[F02M 26/41] characterised by the arrangement of the recirculation passage in relation to the engine, e.g. to cylinder heads, liners, spark plugs or manifolds -- The subclass F02M 26/41 covers engine designs where the exhaust-gas recirculation passage is positioned with respect to specific engine parts such as cylinder heads, liners, spark plugs or intake/exhaust manifolds. It focuses on the geometric relationship of the recirculation route to these components, influencing how exhaust gases are mixed with the intake charge.
[F02M 26/42] having two or more EGR passages -- The subclass F02M 26/42 covers engine devices that incorporate two or more separate exhaust-gas recirculation (EGR) passages, allowing parallel or staged blending of exhaust gases with intake air or fuel-air mixture to improve combustion control and emissions reduction. It is a sub-group of apparatus for adding exhaust gases to the combustion charge.
[F02M 26/43] in which exhaust from only one cylinder or only a group of cylinders is directed to the intake of the engine -- The code F02M 26/43 covers engine designs that route the exhaust gases from a single cylinder or a selected group of cylinders back into the engine's intake system. This cylinder-specific exhaust gas recirculation is used to influence combustion, control emissions, or improve efficiency.
[F02M 26/44] in which a main EGR passage is branched into multiple passages -- It falls under mechanical-engineering and combustion-engine technology for supplying combustible mixtures. The class specifically addresses apparatus that divides a main exhaust-gas recirculation passage into multiple secondary passages.
[F02M 26/45] Sensors specially adapted for EGR systems -- Sensors specially adapted for EGR systems are devices that detect flow rate, temperature, pressure or composition of recirculated exhaust gases to enable precise control of exhaust-gas recirculation in combustion engines.
[F02M 26/46] for determining the characteristics of gases, e.g. composition -- The subclass F02M 26/46 covers apparatus and methods for analysing gas characteristics, especially determining composition, temperature, pressure or flow, as used in systems that introduce exhaust gases into the intake of combustion engines such as exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) arrangements.
[F02M 26/47] the characteristics being temperatures, pressures or flow rates -- The CPC subclass F02M 26/47 covers apparatus and control methods that measure or regulate the temperature, pressure or flow-rate characteristics of systems adding exhaust gases to the combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixture, such as exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) devices for combustion engines. It focuses on the physical parameters that define the performance and operation of these supply mechanisms.
[F02M 26/48] EGR valve position sensors -- The entry F02M 26/48 covers sensors that detect the position of exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) valves in internal-combustion engines, enabling precise control of how much exhaust gas is mixed with intake air or fuel. These devices are part of engine-pertinent apparatus that adds exhaust gases to the combustion mixture.
[F02M 26/49] Detecting, diagnosing or indicating an abnormal function of the EGR system -- The CPC subclass F02M 26/49 covers devices and methods for detecting, diagnosing or indicating abnormal operation of exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) systems in combustion engines. It includes sensors, diagnostic circuits and indicator displays that monitor EGR flow or performance and alert the user to malfunctions.
[F02M 26/50] Arrangements or methods for preventing or reducing deposits, corrosion or wear caused by impurities -- F02M 26/50 covers devices or techniques that limit or eliminate deposits, corrosion or wear produced by impurities in engines where exhaust gases are mixed with combustion air or fuel, such as exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) arrangements. It addresses protective measures for components exposed to contaminated gases.
[F02M 26/51] EGR valves combined with other devices, e.g. with intake valves or compressors -- The CPC class F02M 26/51 covers integrated exhaust-gas recirculation (EGR) valves that are combined with other components such as intake valves or compressors in engines. These devices provide a means to route exhaust gases back into the intake or fuel-air mixture, improving emissions control and performance. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and systems for supplying combustible mixtures.
[F02M 26/52] Systems for actuating EGR valves -- F02M 26/52 relates to systems that operate exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) valves, controlling the quantity of exhaust gases mixed with fresh intake air or fuel in combustion engines. These actuating mechanisms are part of equipment for supplying engines with combustible mixtures that include recirculated exhaust.
[F02M 26/53] using electric actuators, e.g. solenoids -- This subclass concerns combustion-engine systems where exhaust gases are introduced into the intake or fuel mixture, and the flow-control devices are operated by electric actuators such as solenoids. It includes valve or pump mechanisms driven electrically to perform exhaust-gas recirculation or similar functions.
[F02M 26/54] Rotary actuators, e.g. step motors -- The code F02M 26/54 covers rotary actuators such as step-motors employed in engine-related devices that add exhaust gases to the combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixture, for example in exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) systems. It resides in the hierarchy under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 26/55] using vacuum actuators -- F02M 26/55 pertains to engine-related devices that introduce exhaust gases into the combustion-air or fuel-air mixture, such as exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) systems, and specifically addresses the use of vacuum actuators to control the addition of those gases.
[F02M 26/56] having pressure modulation valves -- F02M 26/56 classifies engine equipment that uses pressure-modulation valves to control the introduction of exhaust gases into the intake or fuel mixture, such as in exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) systems. The valves adjust pressure to achieve the desired proportion of recirculated gases, improving combustion efficiency and emissions control.
[F02M 26/57] using electronic means, e.g. electromagnetic valves -- F02M 26/57 covers engine-related apparatus that introduces exhaust gases into the intake or fuel stream by electronic control, typically using electromagnetic valves. The subclass is part of the supply of combustible mixtures to combustion engines and addresses electronically actuated EGR systems.
[F02M 26/58] Constructional details of the actuator -- F02M 26/58 pertains to the constructional details of actuators that control the introduction of exhaust gases into the intake air-fuel mixture of combustion engines, such as components of exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) systems. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the supply of engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 26/59] using positive pressure actuators -- F02M 26/59 covers engine-related devices that employ positive-pressure actuators to introduce exhaust gases into the combustion-air, main-fuel or fuel-air mixture, such as those used in exhaust-gas recirculation (EGR) systems for combustion engines.
[F02M 26/60] in response to air intake pressure -- F02M 26/60 covers engine devices that adjust the amount of exhaust gas recirculated into the intake air or fuel-air mixture based on the measured pressure of the incoming air. The control responds to intake-pressure variations to optimise combustion and emissions.
[F02M 26/61] in response to exhaust pressure -- This subclass covers devices that adjust the amount of exhaust gas recirculated into the intake based on measured exhaust pressure, typically using pressure sensors and control valves to modulate the EGR flow for emissions or performance control.
[F02M 26/615] the exhaust back pressure -- The F02M 26/615 class covers features of exhaust back pressure control in internal-combustion engines, especially devices that adjust pressure to influence fuel-air mixing or emissions, such as exhaust-gas-recirculation valves and pressure-regulating passages.
[F02M 26/62] in response to fuel pressure
[F02M 26/63] the EGR valve being directly controlled by an operator -- It covers exhaust-gas recirculation valves that are manually or directly operated by the driver or another operator instead of being automatically controlled. The entry relates to apparatus for adding exhaust gases to the intake mixture of combustion engines, focusing on operator-controlled EGR valves.
[F02M 26/64] the EGR valve being operated together with an intake air throttle -- The F02M 26/64 class covers systems in which the exhaust-gas-recirculation valve is controlled in conjunction with the intake-air throttle. It pertains to engine hardware that coordinates EGR flow with throttle position to regulate combustion-air mixture and emissions in gasoline or diesel engines.
[F02M 26/65] Constructional details of EGR valves -- The subclass deals with the structural features of exhaust-gas recirculation (EGR) valves that add exhaust gases to the intake mixture of combustion engines. It includes designs of valve bodies, seats, actuators, sealing arrangements and how the valve is mounted or integrated with engine manifolds. Relevant for patents describing the mechanical construction of EGR valve components.
[F02M 26/66] Lift valves, e.g. poppet valves -- F02M 26/66 covers lift valves, notably poppet valves, employed in systems that introduce exhaust gases into the combustion-air or fuel-air mixture, such as exhaust-gas recirculation arrangements. The class addresses the valve designs that regulate the flow of gases or mixture in combustion engines.
[F02M 26/67] Pintles -- F02M 26/67 covers pintles which are pivot or hinge pins that support and guide moving parts in engine systems such as EGR valves, fuel injection devices or other components that introduce exhaust gases into the combustion mixture. They provide a secure, low-friction pivot point for the moving element.
[F02M 26/68] Closing members -- Closing members are components that seal, regulate or modulate a conduit through which exhaust gases are recirculated into the combustion-air or fuel-air mixture, such as valves, flaps or shutters used in exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) systems. They control the amount of exhaust gas returned to the engine to influence combustion and emissions.
[F02M 26/69] having two or more valve-closing members -- F02M 26/69 pertains to apparatus that delivers exhaust gases to the air-fuel mixture of a combustion engine, such as exhaust-gas-recirculation systems, and that uses two or more valve-closing members to regulate the flow of gases into the engine.
[F02M 26/70] Flap valves -- Flap valves are movable plates or shutters that open and close to regulate the amount of exhaust gas recirculated into the combustion-air or fuel-air mixture, helping control emissions and improve engine efficiency. They are typically actuated hydraulically, pneumatically or electrically and are installed in EGR lines of internal combustion engines.
[F02M 26/71] Multi-way valves -- F02M 26/71 covers multi-way valves used in apparatus that inject exhaust gases into the combustion-air, main fuel or fuel-air mixture of combustion engines. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the supply of combustible mixtures, and it applies to systems such as exhaust-gas recirculation (EGR) that blend exhaust streams with fresh intake for improved engine performance and emissions control.
[F02M 26/72] Housings -- The CPC subclass F02M 26/72 refers to housings used in devices that introduce exhaust gases into the combustion-air or fuel-air mixture, such as components of exhaust-gas-recirculation (EGR) systems for combustion engines. It covers the structural enclosures that contain and support the flow-control elements.
[F02M 26/73] with means for heating or cooling the EGR valve -- It covers engine-related apparatus that adds exhaust gases to the combustion-air or fuel-air mixture, specifically exhaust-gas-recirculation systems in which the EGR valve is equipped with heating or cooling means to regulate valve temperature and flow.
[F02M 26/74] Protection from damage, e.g. shielding means -- F02M 26/74 classifies protective arrangements for engine components that receive exhaust-derived gases, such as shields, barriers or housings that guard against heat, particulates or chemical attack in EGR or similar fuel-air mixing systems.
[F02M 27/00] Apparatus for treating combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture, by catalysts, electric means, magnetism, rays, sound waves, or the like -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus for treating combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixture by catalysts, electric means, magnetism, (radioactive) rays or sound waves in order to improve the combustibility of the fuel-air-mixture.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Engine-pertinent apparatus for adding non-fuel substances or small quantities of secondary fuel to combustion-air, main fuel or fuel-air mixture -> F02M25/00 - Apparatus for thermally treating combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixture -> F02M31/00  **Glossary:** - Sonic waves: Sound waves
[F02M 2700/00] Supplying, feeding or preparing air, fuel, fuel air mixtures or auxiliary fluids for a combustion engine -- F02M 2700/00 in the Cooperative Patent Classification pertains to methods and devices for supplying, feeding, or preparing air, fuel, fuel-air mixtures, or auxiliary fluids to combustion engines. It falls under Mechanical Engineering - Combustion Engines - Supplying combustion engines in general with combustible mixtures or constituents thereof. The class covers systems that manage intake air, fuel injection, mixture formation, and related fluid handling for engine operation.
[F02M 2700/05] Miscellaneous constructional elements -- F02M 2700/05 covers various structural components used in systems that supply air, fuel or auxiliary fluids to combustion engines. It groups miscellaneous constructional parts that do not fit into more specific categories, such as brackets, housings, connectors or other fittings involved in fuel-air handling. This subclass sits under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the supplying of combustible mixtures.
[F02M 2700/055] Fuel distribution among injection nozzles -- This subclass addresses systems and methods for allocating fuel among the injection nozzles of a combustion engine, ensuring balanced delivery and proper mixture formation across cylinders. It is situated under supplying combustible mixtures to engines in the broader mechanical-engineering hierarchy.
[F02M 2700/07] Nozzles and injectors with controllable fuel supply -- The entry F02M 2700/07 in CPC covers nozzles and injectors that provide adjustable fuel flow for combustion engines, falling under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, supplying engines with combustible mixtures. It deals with devices that control the quantity or timing of fuel, air, or auxiliary fluids introduced to the engine.
[F02M 2700/071] Injectors having valves -- The subclass F02M 2700/071 pertains to injector devices that incorporate one or more valves to control the delivery of fuel, air-fuel mixtures, or auxiliary fluids to a combustion-engine cylinder. It covers designs where a valve element regulates flow for precise metering, timing or pressure control during the supplying or feeding process.
[F02M 2700/072] Injection valve actuated by engine for supply of pressurised fuel -- This subclass covers injection valves that are actuated by the engine itself to deliver pressurised fuel into a combustion engine. It includes mechanisms that use engine motion or power to control injection timing and quantity as part of fuel-air mixture supply.
[F02M 2700/074] Injection valve actuated by fuel pressure for pressurised fuel supply -- The subclass covers injection valves that are operated directly by the pressure of fuel within a pressurised fuel-supply system, providing metering and delivery of fuel to a combustion engine without separate mechanical or hydraulic actuators. It includes designs where fuel pressure alone opens or moves the valve.
[F02M 2700/075] Injection valve actuated by cylinder pressure or other air pressure for pressurised fuel supply -- The F02M 2700/075 subclass covers injection valves that are triggered by cylinder pressure or other air pressures to deliver pressurised fuel to a combustion engine. It pertains to systems that use the engine's own pressure or auxiliary air sources to control fuel injection, enabling precise metering of fuel-air mixtures for efficient engine operation.
[F02M 2700/077] Injectors having cooling or heating means -- Injectors having cooling or heating means are fuel or air injection devices for combustion engines that incorporate passages, jackets or integrated heating elements to control the temperature of the injected mixture. Temperature regulation improves atomization, reduces deposit formation and enhances engine efficiency.
[F02M 2700/078] Injectors combined with fuel injection pump -- The F02M 2700/078 subclass covers devices in which the fuel injector is integrated with the fuel-injection pump, forming a single unit that both pressurises the fuel and injects it into the combustion chamber. This combined arrangement streamlines the fuel-supply system for internal combustion engines, enabling coordinated control of pressure and injection timing.
[F02M 2700/12] Devices or methods for making a gas mixture for a combustion engine -- This subclass covers apparatus and processes that prepare or supply air-fuel mixtures for internal combustion engines, including mixers, injectors and control systems that ensure the correct composition is delivered. It also includes devices that generate, regulate or blend gases before they enter the engine cylinder.
[F02M 2700/123] Fuel supply devices -- F02M 2700/123 refers to fuel supply devices for combustion engines, encompassing apparatuses that deliver, mix, or prepare fuel, air-fuel mixtures, or auxiliary fluids to the engine. It is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the general supply of combustible mixtures to engines.
[F02M 2700/126] Devices for the supply or mixing of air and gas -- This CPC subclass covers apparatus that provide or blend air with gases before they enter a combustion engine. It includes mixers, injectors and other devices that prepare the air-gas charge for fuel-burning cycles, falling under supplying combustion engines in general with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 2700/13] Special devices for making an explosive mixture -- The CPC subclass F02M 2700/13 pertains to special devices that create an explosive or combustible mixture for combustion engines, covering apparatus that mix fuel, air or other oxidisers in a controlled way before delivery to the engine, such as mixers, injectors and preparation systems.
[F02M 2700/1305] Auxiliary air supply devices for carburettors -- Auxiliary air supply devices for carburettors are components that provide additional airflow to a carburetor in order to optimise the air-fuel mixture for a combustion engine. They are used to improve engine performance, emissions control and fuel efficiency by enriching or leaning the mixture as required.
[F02M 2700/1311] Devices for controlling register carburettors or for carburettors disposed in parallel -- F02M 2700/1311 covers mechanisms that regulate the operation of register carburettors or of several carburettors mounted in parallel, adjusting the air-fuel mixture supplied to a combustion engine. These devices typically vary valve positions or fuel flow rates to achieve the desired engine performance.
[F02M 2700/1317] Fuel pumpo for internal combustion engines -- F02M 2700/1317 pertains to fuel pumps designed to deliver fuel to internal combustion engines, including devices that feed or prepare fuel or fuel-air mixtures for engine operation. It falls within the mechanical engineering group for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures or their constituents.
[F02M 2700/1323] Controlled diaphragm type fuel pump -- A controlled diaphragm type fuel pump delivers fuel to a combustion engine by moving a flexible diaphragm that creates suction and pressure, allowing precise metering of fuel or air-fuel mixtures. The pump's operation is regulated by a control valve or electronic signal, ensuring consistent supply under varying engine loads.
[F02M 2700/1329] Controlled rotary fuel pump with parallel pistons or with a single piston in the extension of the driving shaft -- A controlled rotary fuel pump featuring parallel pistons or a single piston aligned with the driving shaft, designed to accurately meter and deliver fuel or fuel-air mixtures to combustion engines. The device regulates flow to match engine demand, improving efficiency and emissions control.
[F02M 2700/1335] Fuel pump combined with the fuel injector -- The CPC subgroup F02M 2700/1335 covers integrated units that combine a fuel-pumping mechanism with a fuel-injector, providing both pressurisation and atomisation of fuel for combustion engines. These devices feed, mix or prepare fuel-air mixtures in a single component, improving efficiency and compactness in engine fuel-delivery systems.
[F02M 2700/1341] Fuel pump driven by the differential pressure of a gas -- A fuel pump that operates using the pressure difference of a gas to move fuel, typically employed to supply air, fuel or fuel-air mixtures to combustion engines as part of the system that prepares combustible mixtures.
[F02M 2700/1347] Fuel pump acting on a carburetoor -- The CPC subclass F02M 2700/1347 covers a fuel-pump that directly drives a carburetor to provide the required air-fuel mixture for a combustion engine. It is used in systems that supply, feed or prepare the combustible mixture for engines in general.
[F02M 2700/1352] Fuel pump with a constant stroke piston without control means -- A fuel pump employing a piston that moves a fixed distance each cycle and lacks any active control devices, designed to deliver fuel or fuel-air mixtures to a combustion engine in a simple, mechanically regulated manner.
[F02M 2700/1358] Fuel pump with control of fuel inlet to the pumping chamber -- A fuel pump designed to regulate the flow of fuel into its pumping chamber, providing precise control of the amount and timing of fuel delivered to a combustion engine. This allows optimized mixing with air or other fluids, improving engine performance and efficiency in systems that supply combustible mixtures.
[F02M 2700/1364] Fuel pump controlled by means of a fuel return valve -- The classification covers a fuel-pump system whose operation is regulated by a fuel-return valve that redirects surplus fuel back to the source, thereby controlling flow rate and pressure for a combustion engine. This arrangement helps maintain a proper fuel supply and pressure in the air-fuel mixture.
[F02M 2700/137] Fuel pump with control of fuel outlet of pumping chamber to delivery pipe -- F02M 2700/137 covers a fuel pump that includes a means to control the outlet of its pumping chamber toward the delivery pipe, allowing precise regulation of fuel flow to a combustion engine. It belongs to the group concerned with supplying, feeding or preparing air, fuel or fuel-air mixtures for combustion engines.
[F02M 2700/1376] Fuel pump with control of the pump piston stroke -- F02M 2700/1376 covers fuel pumps that incorporate a mechanism to vary the stroke of the pump piston, allowing control of fuel delivery to a combustion engine. The classification applies to devices that adjust pump displacement to regulate flow rate or pressure, often for engine performance or emissions management.
[F02M 2700/1382] Fuel pump with control of the cylinder relative to non-rotary piston -- The entry F02M 2700/1382 covers a fuel-pump system that controls the position of a cylinder in relation to a non-rotary piston, enabling precise regulation of fuel or fuel-air mixture delivery to a combustion engine. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion-engine supplying, and deals with fueling and auxiliary-fluid preparation.
[F02M 2700/1388] Fuel pump with control of the piston relative to a fixed cylinder -- This CPC subclass covers fuel-pump arrangements in which the piston's stroke is regulated relative to a fixed cylinder, enabling precise delivery of air, fuel or fuel-air mixtures to a combustion engine. It is situated under mechanical engineering, combustion-engine supply systems.
[F02M 2700/1394] Knock sensors -- The F02M 2700/1394 entry covers knock sensors, which are devices installed in the intake or combustion system of an engine to detect abnormal pressure spikes caused by uncontrolled combustion (knocking). By monitoring these events, the sensor enables control systems to adjust fuel-air mixture or ignition timing, improving engine performance and preventing damage. It falls under the hierarchy of supplying combustible mixtures to combustion engines.
[F02M 2700/31] Use of exhaust gas of combustion engines -- This subgroup concerns methods that utilize the exhaust gases produced by combustion engines, for example in recycling, energy recovery, or emission treatment, within systems that supply air, fuel, or auxiliary fluids to the engine.
[F02M 2700/33] Compressors for piston combustion engines -- F02M 2700/33 covers compressors designed for piston-type combustion engines, used to raise the pressure of intake air, fuel or air-fuel mixtures before they enter the cylinder. These units may be integral to the engine's charging system or installed as separate feeding equipment, improving volumetric efficiency and power output.
[F02M 2700/331] Charging and scavenging compressors -- The classification F02M 2700/331 pertains to charging and scavenging compressors, which pressurise the air-fuel mixture for a combustion engine and remove exhaust gases to prepare the cylinder for the next cycle. These devices are part of the broader supply of combustible mixtures to engines.
[F02M 2700/333] Drive thereof -- The subclass F02M 2700/333 covers the drive mechanisms that power equipment for supplying air, fuel, fuel-air mixtures or auxiliary fluids to a combustion engine, including pumps, compressors and other feed-system actuators. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the general supply of combustible mixtures.
[F02M 2700/335] Control therefor -- This subclass covers methods and devices for regulating the supply, feeding or preparation of air, fuel, air-fuel mixtures or auxiliary fluids to a combustion engine. It includes control strategies, sensors, actuators and feedback loops that adjust flow rates, pressures, composition or timing to achieve desired engine performance.
[F02M 2700/336] Arrangements thereof on the engine -- F02M 2700/336 concerns the specific design and positioning of components that supply or prepare air, fuel, fuel-air mixtures, or auxiliary fluids directly on a combustion engine. It includes arrangements such as manifolds, injectors, carburetors or other distribution systems integrated with the engine structure.
[F02M 2700/338] Injection air compressors -- The CPC subclass F02M 2700/338, titled "Injection air compressors," covers devices that compress and deliver air into a combustion-engine intake to boost the air charge and improve fuel-air mixing. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Supplying combustion engines in general with combustible mixtures or constituents thereof, specifically addressing the preparation and feeding of air, fuel or auxiliary fluids for engine operation.
[F02M 2700/34] Measures, also constructive measures, for avoiding the generation of nixious products such as CO in the exhaust gases -- This subclass covers measures, including constructive arrangements, that aim to prevent the formation of harmful exhaust products such as carbon monoxide in combustion engines. It includes techniques for supplying, feeding or preparing air, fuel or fuel-air mixtures, as well as auxiliary fluids, that reduce CO generation.
[F02M 2700/43] Arrangements for supplying air, fuel or auxiliary fluids to a combustion space of mixture compressing engines working with liquid fuel -- The subclass F02M 2700/43 covers designs for delivering air, liquid fuel or auxiliary fluids into the combustion chamber of mixture-compressing engines, such as diesel or direct-injection engines. It includes manifolds, injectors, pumps and control devices that prepare and feed the charge, and falls under the CPC hierarchy for supplying combustible mixtures to combustion engines.
[F02M 2700/4302] whereby air and fuel are sucked into the mixture conduit -- This subclass covers arrangements in which air and fuel are drawn by suction into the mixture conduit for supply to a combustion engine, rather than being pumped or injected mechanically. It pertains to feeding, preparing or delivering the combustible mixture to the engine.
[F02M 2700/4304] working only with one fuel -- This subclass addresses the provision, preparation or feeding of air, fuel or fuel-air mixtures to a combustion engine when the engine operates with a single fuel only, rather than multiple fuels or blended mixtures. It covers devices, methods or control systems that handle the sole-fuel supply for engines in the broader context of mechanical engineering and combustion-engine fuel delivery.
[F02M 2700/4307] without mixing chambers disposed in parallel -- The code F02M 2700/4307 covers systems for supplying air, fuel or fuel-air mixtures to combustion engines in which the mixing chambers are not arranged in parallel. It addresses designs that avoid parallel mixing units, focusing on sequential or single-path delivery of the combustible constituents to the engine.
[F02M 2700/4309] specially adapted for motorcycles -- The CPC subclass F02M 2700/4309 falls under mechanical engineering and combustion engines and concerns the provision of air, fuel or auxiliary fluid mixtures to an engine. This group covers systems that are specially adapted for motorcycles, such as carburetors, fuel-injection or air-mixing devices designed for two-wheel vehicles.
[F02M 2700/4311] with mixing chambers disposed in parallel -- The CPC subgroup F02M 2700/4311 covers methods and devices for supplying, feeding or preparing air, fuel or air-fuel mixtures for combustion engines in which the mixing chambers are arranged in parallel. It lies within mechanical engineering, under combustion engines and the broader category of supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 2700/4314] with mixing chambers disposed in parallel -- The F02M 2700/4314 subclass covers methods for supplying a combustion engine with air, fuel, or air-fuel mixtures in which the mixing chambers are arranged in parallel. It pertains to the provision, feeding, or preparation of these mixtures or auxiliary fluids for the engine's operation.
[F02M 2700/4316] without mixing chambers disposed in parallel -- This CPC subgroup covers supplying, feeding or preparing air, fuel or fuel-air mixtures for combustion engines where the system does not employ mixing chambers arranged in parallel. It pertains to the broader area of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the provision of combustible mixtures or constituents without parallel mixing chambers.
[F02M 2700/4319] with mixing chambers disposed in parallel -- This CPC subclass deals with methods and apparatus for supplying air, fuel or fuel-air mixtures to a combustion engine where the mixing chambers are arranged in parallel. It covers parallel mixing chamber designs that prepare the combustible mixture before it enters the engine, including auxiliary fluid handling. The code F02M 2700/4319 falls under the broader category of supplying combustion engines in general with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 2700/4321] working with fuel and admission of auxiliary fluids such as water, anti-knock agents, hydrogen, ozone or the like -- The CPC subclass F02M 2700/4321 covers techniques for handling fuel and introducing auxiliary fluids such as water, anti-knock agents, hydrogen or ozone into a combustion engine's intake or combustion chamber. It pertains to the supply and preparation of air-fuel mixtures and other substances that improve performance, emissions or stability.
[F02M 2700/4323] Throttling devices (not control systems thereof) -- The F02M 2700/4323 class pertains to mechanical devices that restrict or regulate the flow of air, fuel, or fuel-air mixtures to a combustion engine, focusing on throttling components themselves rather than the electronic or hydraulic control systems that drive them.
[F02M 2700/4326] Means for preventing back-fire -- This subclass covers devices built into the fuel or air supply system of a combustion engine that stop the reverse propagation of flame, known as back-fire, while the engine runs. Such means can include check valves, flame arrestors or flow-control mechanisms that regulate the mixture to prevent ignition in the supply line.
[F02M 2700/4328] Reservoirs -- Reservoirs are containers or tanks that store air, fuel, fuel-air mixtures or auxiliary fluids for supplying a combustion engine. The entry covers their design, material selection, integration with feeding or preparation systems, and any features that facilitate the controlled delivery of these substances to the engine.
[F02M 2700/433] without limitation of the liquid level -- This subclass covers methods and devices for supplying or preparing air, fuel, or fuel-air mixtures to a combustion engine where the control does not depend on the liquid level in the system. It includes arrangements for feeding auxiliary fluids and handling the mixture regardless of the amount of liquid present.
[F02M 2700/4333] with limitation of the liquid level -- This subclass covers methods or devices that control the amount of liquid - such as fuel or coolant - by limiting its level when supplying air, fuel, or auxiliary fluids to a combustion engine.
[F02M 2700/4335] Transport devices -- The code F02M 2700/4335 designates transport devices that move air, fuel, fuel-air mixtures or auxiliary fluids to a combustion engine, such as pumps, compressors, blowers and related conveying mechanisms. It is part of the hierarchy covering mechanical engineering, combustion engines and the supply of engines with combustible mixtures, focusing on the equipment that physically transfers these fluids from storage or preparation stages to the engine.
[F02M 2700/4338] Acceleration pumps -- F02M 2700/4338 Acceleration pumps covers devices that raise the pressure or flow of fuel, air or fuel-air mixtures before they are supplied to a combustion engine, aiding start-up, throttle response and overall performance. It belongs to the CPC subclass for supplying combustible mixtures to combustion engines within mechanical engineering.
[F02M 2700/434] Heating or cooling devices -- This subclass covers heating or cooling devices used in the supply system for air, fuel, or mixed fluids in combustion engines, such as preheaters, intercoolers, or temperature control apparatus that condition the charge before it enters the engine.
[F02M 2700/4342] Heating devices -- F02M 2700/4342 covers heating devices that warm air, fuel, fuel-air mixtures or auxiliary fluids before they are supplied to a combustion engine. These devices are employed to improve engine start-up, efficiency or emissions by raising the temperature of the supplied media.
[F02M 2700/4345] by means of exhaust gases -- This subclass concerns supplying, feeding or preparing air, fuel or auxiliary fluids for a combustion engine by recirculating or otherwise using the engine's own exhaust gases. It includes techniques that employ exhaust-gas energy or composition to improve mixture formation or engine performance.
[F02M 2700/4347] by means of water -- The F02M 2700/4347 subclass covers methods of supplying water-alone or mixed with other fluids-to a combustion engine's intake system, such as injecting water into the air-fuel mixture or delivering it as an auxiliary fluid for cooling or performance enhancement.
[F02M 2700/435] by means of electricity -- This CPC subclass F02M 2700/435 covers methods of supplying, feeding or preparing air, fuel or fuel-air mixtures for combustion engines using electrical energy. It includes electric pumps, heaters, valves or control systems that deliver the required fluids to the engine. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the broader group for supplying combustion engines in general with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 2700/4352] by means of hot air -- This subclass covers supplying a combustion engine with air, fuel or mixtures by means of hot air. It includes methods that pre-heat the intake air to improve fuel vaporisation, combustion stability or start-up performance.
[F02M 2700/4354] by means of heat radiated from the engine -- The CPC subclass F02M 2700/4354 covers methods of supplying air, fuel, or fuel-air mixtures to a combustion engine by utilizing heat that is radiated from the engine itself, such that the engine's own thermal output assists in preparing or delivering the combustible mixture. This falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Supplying Combustion Engines in General with Combustible Mixtures or Constituents Thereof.
[F02M 2700/4357] by other means -- F02M 2700/4357 covers methods of supplying air, fuel, fuel-air mixtures or auxiliary fluids to a combustion engine that are not described by the more specific feeding techniques defined elsewhere. It belongs to the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering - Combustion Engines - Supplying combustion engines in general with combustible mixtures or constituents thereof. The subclass is applied when the supply is achieved by other means.
[F02M 2700/4359] Cooling devices -- F02M 2700/4359 covers cooling devices used in the supply system of combustion engines, including air-intake coolers, fuel pre-coolers and intercoolers that lower the temperature of gases or liquids before they enter the engine.
[F02M 2700/4361] Mixing chambers -- Mixing chambers are components within a fuel or air-fuel delivery system that combine gaseous or liquid constituents before they enter a combustion engine. They may be integral to carburetors, fuel injectors or manifold designs and are used to ensure a homogeneous mixture for efficient combustion.
[F02M 2700/4364] with fuel atomization -- The CPC subclass F02M 2700/4364 covers methods and devices for supplying, feeding or preparing air-fuel mixtures for combustion engines where the fuel is atomised. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the broader group dealing with supplying engines with combustible mixtures or their constituents. This entry addresses the atomisation step that creates a fine spray of fuel before it enters the engine.
[F02M 2700/4366] with fuel atomization by a valve -- This subclass covers systems that supply air, fuel or fuel-air mixtures to a combustion engine where the fuel is atomized by means of a valve. It includes valve-based atomizers, injectors and related feeding arrangements for internal-combustion engines.
[F02M 2700/4369] with fuel atomization from an open fuel surface -- The subclass F02M 2700/4369 covers methods and devices that supply a combustion engine by atomising fuel from an open surface, such as a pool, spill or open reservoir, using jets, nozzles, impinging streams or other means to create a fine spray that can be mixed with air for combustion. It falls under the broader CPC group for feeding air, fuel or auxiliary fluids to engines.
[F02M 2700/4371] with fuel atomization from a fuel film dispersed over a surface -- The subclass F02M 2700/4371 covers supplying or preparing fuel for a combustion engine by atomising the fuel from a thin film that is spread over a surface, typically using the film to create a fine spray before mixing with air.
[F02M 2700/4373] Mixture improving devices -- F02M 2700/4373 covers devices that improve the quality or homogeneity of the air-fuel mixture supplied to a combustion engine. This includes systems such as fuel injectors, air-mass meters, mixers and other components that optimise combustion efficiency, reduce emissions, or enhance engine performance.
[F02M 2700/4376] Mechanical devices -- F02M 2700/4376 designates mechanical devices that supply, feed or prepare air, fuel or fuel-air mixtures and auxiliary fluids for combustion engines. It falls under the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering, Combustion Engines, Supplying Combustion Engines in General with Combustible Mixtures or Constituents Thereof.
[F02M 2700/4378] Other devices -- F02M 2700/4378 Other devices covers any apparatus for supplying, feeding or preparing air, fuel, fuel-air mixtures or auxiliary fluids for a combustion engine that does not fit into the more specific CPC subclasses, encompassing miscellaneous or unconventional feeding mechanisms.
[F02M 2700/438] Supply of liquid to a carburettor reservoir with limitation of the liquid level -- F02M 2700/438 pertains to the provision of liquid to a carburettor reservoir in a combustion engine while controlling the amount of liquid present. It falls under the broader class for supplying air, fuel, or fuel-air mixtures, and addresses methods or devices that limit the reservoir's level to ensure proper engine operation.
[F02M 2700/4383] with fuel displacement by gas pressure working on the fuel -- The code F02M 2700/4383 relates to supplying or preparing fuel for a combustion engine by using gas pressure to displace the fuel. It covers systems where a pressurised gas acts on the fuel to move it into the engine's injection or delivery system.
[F02M 2700/4385] the pressure being an overpressure -- The CPC subclass F02M 2700/4385 relates to the supply, feeding or preparation of air, fuel, or fuel-air mixtures for combustion engines when the delivery pressure is higher than the surrounding pressure, i.e., an over-pressure condition. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the broader category of general supplying of combustible mixtures.
[F02M 2700/4388] with fuel displacement by a pump -- This CPC subclass refers to the supply of fuel, air-fuel mixtures or auxiliary fluids to a combustion engine using a pump that displaces the fuel. It includes arrangements and methods for feeding, preparing or delivering the combustible mixture to the engine and is nested under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, supplying combustion engines in general with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 2700/439] the pump being a membrane pump -- This subclass (F02M 2700/439) concerns membrane-type pumps employed to deliver air, fuel, fuel-air mixtures or auxiliary fluids to combustion engines. It falls under supplying combustion engines in general with combustible mixtures, within the broader mechanical engineering and combustion engine categories.
[F02M 2700/4392] Conduits, manifolds, as far as heating and cooling if not concerned -- This CPC subgroup covers conduits and manifolds used to heat or cool air, fuel, fuel-air mixtures or auxiliary fluids supplied to a combustion engine, without addressing the mixing or distribution functions themselves.
[F02M 2700/4395] Other details -- F02M 2700/4395 addresses additional aspects of supplying, feeding or preparing air, fuel, fuel-air mixtures or auxiliary fluids for combustion engines that are not covered by more specific categories. It is applied to describe other details of the fueling or fluid-supply system. This entry resides under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and the broader group for supplying combustion engines.
[F02M 2700/4397] whereby air or fuel are admitted in the mixture conduit by means other than vacuum or an acceleration pump -- The entry covers methods of introducing air, fuel, or their mixture into a combustion-engine's mixture conduit that do not rely on a vacuum system or an acceleration pump, describing alternative admission techniques for supplying the combustible charge.
[F02M 27/02] by catalysts -- This subclass covers devices that condition the combustion-air, fuel or their mixture by employing a catalyst to promote chemical reactions before delivery to the engine. Typical applications include catalytic reformers, oxidizers or reducers that alter fuel composition, remove pollutants, or enhance ignition. The focus is on catalyst-based treatment rather than electrical, magnetic or acoustic methods.
[F02M 27/04] by electric means, or magnetism -- F02M 27/04 classifies devices that condition the combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture for engines by applying electric fields, currents or magnetic fields. Such apparatuses use electrical discharge, plasma generation or magnetic induction to alter mixture properties and improve ignition, combustion efficiency or emissions control.
[F02M 27/042] by plasma -- F02M 27/042 pertains to apparatus that treats the combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture by means of plasma. The plasma is used to alter the mixture's chemical or physical properties to improve ignition, efficiency or emissions control.
[F02M 27/045] by permanent magnets -- F02M 27/045 covers devices that influence the combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture by means of permanent magnets. The treatment may involve altering flow, distribution, or atomisation to improve combustion efficiency or emissions. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 27/06] by rays -- This sub-group of F02M 27 covers apparatus that treat combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixtures by applying electromagnetic rays such as ultraviolet, infrared, laser or other radiation to promote ignition, enhance mixing or reduce emissions, without using catalysts, electric arcs or sound. It includes devices that direct the rays onto the mixture through windows or directly into the flow.
[F02M 27/065] Radioactive radiation -- F02M 27/065 designates devices that employ ionising radioactive radiation to treat the air, fuel or fuel-air mixture supplied to combustion engines, with the aim of influencing ignition, flame stability, efficiency or emissions. It is a subcategory of apparatus that use catalysts, electric, magnetic, optical or acoustic means to modify combustion mixtures, situated under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 27/08] by sonic or ultrasonic waves -- This subclass covers devices that use sound or ultrasonic energy to modify the properties of the intake air, fuel, or their mixture before combustion, aiming to improve mixing, ignition or emissions. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, supplying engines with combustible mixtures, and the broader group of treatment apparatus by catalysts, electricity, magnetism, rays, or sound waves.
[F02M 29/00] Apparatus for re-atomising condensed fuel or homogenising fuel-air mixture -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Especially when the air is cold the process of vaporisation of fuel into the air is more difficult. Apparatus for re-atomising condensed fuel, e.g. fuel droplets on the inner side of the air intake tube, or homogenising fuel-air mixture, e.g. distribution of fuel and air, help to improve this process.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Combined with secondary-air supply -> F02M23/12 - Apparatus for thermally treating combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture -> F02M31/00 - Apparatus for collecting and returning condensed fuel -> F02M33/02  **Glossary:** - Secondary air: Air entering the intake via an extra opening
[F02M 29/02] having rotary parts -- The CPC subclass F02M 29/02 covers apparatus for re-atomising condensed fuel or homogenising a fuel-air mixture that incorporates rotary components. It includes devices such as rotating jets, turbines or impellers that break up liquid fuel droplets and aid uniform mixing before combustion.
[F02M 29/04] having screens, gratings, baffles or the like -- F02M 29/04 covers devices used in the supply of combustion engines that re-atomise condensed fuel or homogenise the fuel-air mixture by employing screens, gratings, baffles or similar elements. These components improve fuel distribution and prevent clogging, enhancing engine performance and emissions control. The class falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the general supply of combustible mixtures.
[F02M 29/06] generating whirling motion of mixture -- F02M 29/06 refers to devices that impart a whirling motion to a fuel-air mixture to promote re-atomisation of condensed fuel or to homogenise the mixture, commonly employed in the supply systems of combustion engines. This subclass falls under the broader group for apparatus that re-atomise fuel or mix fuel and air.
[F02M 29/08] having spirally-wound wires -- F02M 29/08 pertains to fuel-supply devices for combustion engines that re-atomise condensed fuel or homogenise a fuel-air mixture using spirally-wound wires. The wire coil mechanically disrupts droplets and enhances mixing, improving combustion efficiency in engines that receive fuel as liquids or condensates.
[F02M 29/10] adjustable -- The CPC subclass F02M 29/10 covers adjustable apparatus for re-atomising condensed fuel or homogenising a fuel-air mixture in combustion engines. It includes devices whose adjustment mechanisms change atomisation or mixing characteristics to optimise engine performance.
[F02M 29/12] having homogenising valves held open by mixture current -- F02M 29/12 pertains to devices that re-atomise condensed fuel or homogenise a fuel-air mixture, specifically those where the homogenising valves are kept open by the current of the mixture itself. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 29/14] re-atomising or homogenising being effected by unevenness of internal surfaces of mixture intake -- This group covers devices that improve fuel atomisation or air-fuel mixing by employing rough or irregular surfaces inside the intake passage, causing turbulence that breaks up liquid fuel or evens the mixture. It applies to engines where condensed fuel is supplied and a non-uniform wall geometry is used for re-atomising or homogenising.
[F02M 3/00] Idling devices for carburettors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for delivering the correct amount of fuel and air under idling i.e. slow running conditions of the engine.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Carburettors with means for facilitating engine's starting or its idling below operational temperatures -> F02M1/00
[F02M 3/005] Idling fuel enrichment with motor driven instead of driving; Switching the fuel supply from the main to idling jet system -- The entry covers devices that enrich the fuel mixture during engine idling by using a motor-driven mechanism to shift the fuel flow from the main jet to an idling jet system. It pertains to carburettor arrangements that automatically adjust fuel delivery for smoother idle operation.
[F02M 3/02] Preventing flow of idling fuel -- The CPC subclass F02M 3/02 covers devices that stop or restrict the flow of fuel to a carburettor when the engine is idling, preventing excess fuel consumption and emissions. It includes mechanical, pneumatic or electronic mechanisms that intervene in the idle-fuel supply line to maintain a lean mixture during low-speed operation.
[F02M 3/04] under conditions where engine is driven instead of driving, e.g. driven by vehicle running down hill -- F02M 3/04 covers devices that control or assist the idling of a carburettor when the engine is being driven rather than providing propulsion, such as when a vehicle coasts downhill. The classification applies to mechanisms that adjust the combustible-mixture supply under these driven-engine conditions to prevent stalling or excessive fuel consumption.
[F02M 3/041] Removal of the fuel from the main jet system, e.g. by means of a pump -- This subclass deals with devices or methods that extract fuel from the main jet of a carburettor, usually by means of a pump, to halt fuel flow during shutdown or idle control. It aims to improve engine stopping, reduce emissions and prevent flooding when the engine is idling.
[F02M 3/042] Fuel cut-off by altering the pressure in the float chamber; Arrangement of pneumatic accumulators for pressure equalization -- F02M 3/042 covers idling devices for carburettors that achieve fuel cut-off by changing the pressure in the float chamber, and includes the design of pneumatic accumulators used to equalise that pressure. The classification applies to methods that control the fuel supply to combustion engines during idle conditions.
[F02M 3/043] Devices as described in  F02M3/005 ,  F02M3/041 ,  F02M3/042 ,  F02M3/045 ,  F02M3/05  and  F02M3/055  and also equipped with additional air -- The CPC subclass F02M 3/043 covers idling devices for carburettors that incorporate an auxiliary air supply. These devices adjust the air-fuel mixture at idle to stabilize engine speed and lower emissions. They fall within the broader category of systems for supplying combustible mixtures to combustion engines.
[F02M 3/045] Control of valves situated in the idling nozzle system, or the passage system, by electrical means or by a combination of electrical means with fluidic or mechanical means
[F02M 3/05] Pneumatic or mechanical control, e.g. with speed regulation -- F02M 3/05 relates to pneumatic or mechanical control, such as speed regulation, of idling devices that supply combustible mixtures to combustion engines through carburettors. It addresses mechanisms that adjust idle speed and mixture flow.
[F02M 3/055] Fuel flow cut-off by introducing air, e.g. brake air, into the idling fuel system -- The F02M 3/055 entry covers devices that stop fuel flow to a carburetor by admitting air-such as brake-air pressure-into the idle fuel circuit, thereby cutting off fuel during idling. It falls under the mechanical-engineering subclass for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures, specifically idling devices for carburettors.
[F02M 3/06] Increasing idling speed -- F02M 3/06 pertains to features that raise the idle speed of internal-combustion engines, especially through adjustments to carburettor operation or fuel-air supply. It includes mechanisms that increase the minimum revolutions per minute when the engine is idling, such as altered throttling, jet sizing or control circuits.
[F02M 3/062] by altering as a function of motor r.p.m. the throttle valve stop or the fuel conduit cross-section by means of pneumatic or hydraulic means  external damping elements  F02M19/122 -- F02M 3/062 covers idling devices for carburettors that adjust the throttle valve stop or the fuel-conduit cross-section in proportion to engine speed using pneumatic or hydraulic actuation, often with external damping elements. The adjustment is made to maintain stable idle as rpm varies.
[F02M 3/065] by randomly altering the throttle valve stop -- F02M 3/065 pertains to idling devices for carburettors that maintain a steady idle speed by randomly varying the throttle-valve stop position. The approach intermittently changes the stop setting to counteract fluctuations in fuel-air mixture or engine load, thereby smoothing engine operation while idling.
[F02M 3/07] by positioning the throttle flap stop, or by changing the fuel flow cross-sectional area, by electrical, electromechanical or electropneumatic means, according to engine speed -- The entry covers methods for regulating the throttle in combustion-engine carburettors by positioning the throttle-flap stop or altering the fuel-flow cross-sectional area with electrical, electromechanical or electropneumatic devices that adjust according to engine speed. It pertains to fuel-mixture supply control in engines using speed-dependent actuation.
[F02M 3/075] the valve altering the fuel conduit cross-section being a slidable valve -- F02M 3/075 pertains to idling devices for carburettors in which the valve that modifies the fuel-conduit cross-section is a slidable valve, used to regulate the amount of fuel supplied during engine idle. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the broader category of supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 3/08] Other details of idling devices -- F02M 3/08 covers auxiliary aspects of idle-control devices for carburettors, including supplemental adjustments, secondary mechanisms, and specific design details that fine-tune engine speed when the throttle is closed.
[F02M 3/09] Valves responsive to engine conditions, e.g. manifold vacuum -- This subclass deals with valves that automatically adapt their position or operation in response to engine variables such as manifold vacuum to control fuel or air flow in carburettor idle systems. It applies to devices that modulate mixture supply based on real-time engine conditions for more stable idling.
[F02M 3/10] Fuel metering pins -- Fuel metering pins are small, precision-drilled components inserted into a carburettor to regulate the amount of fuel delivered during idle operation. By creating a controlled restriction, they ensure a stable air-fuel mixture when the engine runs at low speed, improving idle smoothness and reducing emissions. This device falls under CPC code F02M 3/10 for idling devices in combustion-engine fuel supply.
[F02M 31/00] Apparatus for thermally treating combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus for thermally treating combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixture for improving the combustion properties of the fuel-air mixture.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Carburettors with heating, cooling or thermal insulating means for combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture -> F02M15/00 - Apparatus for de-liquefying non-liquid fuels by heating -> F02M21/06 - Apparatus having heating means for non-gaseous fuels with low melting point -> F02M21/10 - Apparatus characterised by adding hot secondary air to fuel-air mixture -> F02M23/14 - Fuel-injection apparatus characterised by having heating, cooling or thermally insulating means -> F02M53/00
[F02M 31/005] using a heat-pipe  heat-pipe  per se   F28D
[F02M 31/02] for heating -- F02M 31/02 covers devices that heat the combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture before it enters a combustion engine, aiding start-up, efficiency or emissions control. It is part of the hierarchy mechanical engineering → combustion engines → supplying combustion engines in general with combustible mixtures or constituents thereof.
[F02M 31/04] combustion-air or fuel-air mixture -- F02M 31/04 relates to equipment that thermally conditions the air, fuel, or their mixture before it enters a combustion engine. Typical examples include pre-heaters, inter-coolers and heat-exchange devices that raise or lower temperature to improve ignition, efficiency or emissions.
[F02M 31/042] Combustion air -- This CPC subclass (F02M 31/042) covers apparatus that thermally treats the air fed to a combustion engine, such as heaters, heat exchangers or burners placed in the intake system to raise the temperature of combustion air before it mixes with fuel. The aim is to improve ignition, increase efficiency or reduce emissions.
[F02M 31/045] Fuel-air mixture -- This subclass concerns devices that condition the fuel-air charge before it enters a combustion engine, such as burners, pre-heaters, vaporizers or mixers that heat, vaporise or otherwise treat the mixture to improve ignition, efficiency or emissions. It falls under supplying engines with combustible mixtures, specifically the thermal treatment of combustion-air, fuel, or their mixture.
[F02M 31/047] for fuel enriched partial mixture flow path -- The CPC subgroup F02M 31/047 covers devices that thermally treat the flow path of a fuel-enriched partial mixture in combustion-engine systems. It applies to apparatuses that preheat, pre-mix or otherwise condition fuel-air blends before they enter the cylinder or combustion chamber, improving ignition and efficiency.
[F02M 31/06] by hot gases, e.g. by mixing cold and hot air
[F02M 31/062] with thermostat and pneumatic actuator both working on the air mixture control valve -- The CPC subclass F02M 31/062 covers devices that thermally treat the combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixture and use both a thermostat and a pneumatic actuator to operate the air-mixture control valve. It applies to systems that regulate temperature while supplying engines with the proper combustible mixture.
[F02M 31/064] pneumatically controlled  F02M31/062  takes precedence
[F02M 31/066] operated manually, e.g. by means of valves on the air filter -- This subclass concerns apparatus for thermally treating the combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixture in which the heating or cooling action is controlled manually, typically by hand-operated valves mounted on the air filter.
[F02M 31/068] particular constructional aspects of the switching devices, e.g. connecting linkage between two control valves -- F02M 31/068 covers particular constructional aspects of switching devices such as the connecting linkage between two control valves in apparatus that thermally treat combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixtures. The code falls under supply of combustion engines with combustible mixtures, focusing on mechanical arrangements that coordinate valve operation to control flow and temperature.
[F02M 31/07] Temperature-responsive control, e.g. using thermostatically-controlled valves -- F02M 31/07 pertains to devices that regulate the temperature of combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixtures by employing temperature-responsive mechanisms such as thermostatically controlled valves, adjusting flow or heating to achieve desired thermal conditions.
[F02M 31/08] the gases being exhaust gases -- F02M 31/08 concerns devices that condition or treat the exhaust gases of combustion engines, including cooling, reheating, filtering, cleaning or otherwise modifying the temperature, composition or pollutant content of the gases after combustion. It is situated under supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures and relates to apparatus for thermally treating combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixtures.
[F02M 31/0805] Pneumatic control of the amount of exhaust gas or combustion air directed to the heat exchange surfaces, e.g. as a function of the pressure in the air intake passage -- The entry concerns devices that regulate the quantity of exhaust gas or combustion air directed to heat-exchange surfaces by means of pneumatic control, often using pressure signals from the intake passage. By adjusting the gas flow in response to pressure variations, the system optimises thermal treatment of the air-fuel mixture and engine performance.
[F02M 31/081] Manual switching of the fluids directed to the heat exchange surfaces -- This subclass covers devices that allow an operator to manually select which fluid-combustion air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture-is directed over heat-exchange surfaces for pre-heating or cooling, using valves, levers or similar selectors to change the flow path in combustion-engine related equipment.
[F02M 31/0815] Heat exchange surfaces arranged inside a flange -- The entry concerns heat-exchange surfaces that are integrated inside a flange of a device used to thermally treat combustion-air, fuel, or a fuel-air mixture before it is supplied to a combustion engine. Such internal flange-mounted exchangers improve temperature control and mixture preparation for efficient engine operation.
[F02M 31/082] Particular shape of air input passage near to the branch -- The subclass covers the specific contour or cross-section of an air-supply conduit immediately upstream of a branching point in a combustion-engine fuel-air system. It includes features that modify the shape of the inlet passage to influence flow distribution, turbulence or pressure recovery before the mixture is divided among cylinders or burners.
[F02M 31/0825] Particular constructional characteristics of the heat exchange surfaces, e.g. finned pipes, coiled pipes or the like -- F02M 31/0825 covers the specific design features of heat-exchange surfaces in devices that thermally treat combustion air, fuel, or fuel-air mixtures, such as finned or coiled pipes and similar configurations that enhance heat transfer.
[F02M 31/083] Temperature-responsive control of the amount of exhaust gas or combustion air directed to the heat exchange surface -- This CPC subgroup covers devices that adjust the flow of exhaust gases or combustion air to a heat-exchange surface in response to temperature changes, thereby controlling the amount of heat transferred during thermal treatment of combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixtures.
[F02M 31/087] Heat-exchange arrangements between the air intake and exhaust gas passages, e.g. by means of contact between the passages -- The code F02M 31/087 relates to devices that create heat-exchange links between the air-intake passages and the exhaust-gas passages of a combustion engine, allowing heating of the incoming air or fuel-air mixture by direct contact with hot exhaust gases. Such arrangements improve fuel vaporisation, cold-start performance and overall engine efficiency.
[F02M 31/093] Air intake passage surrounding the exhaust gas passage -- This subclass covers designs where the duct that delivers fresh air to a combustion engine is arranged to encircle the exhaust-gas duct, allowing heat exchange between hot exhaust and incoming charge. The configuration can preheat the intake air, improve fuel vaporisation, and may include insulation or cooling features.
[F02M 31/10] by hot liquids, e.g. lubricants -- This subclass covers devices that heat the combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture by circulating hot liquids such as lubricating oils, improving atomisation, combustion stability or reducing emissions in internal-combustion engines.
[F02M 31/102] Particular constructional characteristics of the shape of the heat exchange surfaces -- F02M 31/102 covers the specific geometric design of heat-exchange surfaces in devices that thermally treat combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixtures for supplying combustion engines. It addresses how the shape of the surfaces is constructed to optimise heat transfer, flow distribution or safety, and includes features such as corrugations, ribs, fins or other shaping elements. This subclass falls under the broader category of apparatus for treating combustible mixtures in mechanical engineering.
