[F02M 31/105] Particular constructional characteristics of the switching apparatus -- F02M 31/105 classifies the specific structural features of switching devices used in equipment that thermally treats combustion air, fuel, or fuel-air mixtures. It covers the design of components that route, ignite or regulate the heated mixture within systems supplying engines with combustible mixtures. The entry falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the provision of combustible mixtures to engines.
[F02M 31/107] Controlled or manual switching -- This subclass concerns apparatus that provide a user-operated or automatically controlled means to select among different thermal-treatment functions for the combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixture, such as switching on/off preheating, cooling, humidifying or other conditioning steps. It includes manual selectors, electromechanical relays, control valves or electronic controllers that change the treatment path.

[F02M 31/125] Fuel -- F02M 31/125 relates to equipment that thermally treats fuel, combustion air, or the fuel-air mixture before it enters a combustion engine, such as preheaters, vaporizers, steam injectors or devices that cool or change the phase of the fuel to improve ignition and overall engine performance.
[F02M 31/13] Combustion air -- F02M 31/13 covers apparatus used to treat the air supplied to a combustion engine before combustion takes place. This includes devices for pre-heating, cooling, humidifying, filtering or otherwise conditioning the combustion-air stream, as well as systems that combine the air with steam or other additives to improve engine performance.
[F02M 31/135] Fuel-air mixture -- This subclass deals with devices that prepare or condition the fuel-air mixture for internal combustion engines, including pre-heating, humidifying, atomizing or otherwise treating the air, fuel or combined mixture before it enters the cylinder. It covers apparatus for thermal treatment of combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture to improve combustion efficiency.
[F02M 31/14] by using heat from working cylinders or cylinder heads -- This subclass covers apparatus that uses heat recovered from the working cylinders or cylinder heads of an internal combustion engine to thermally treat the intake air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture, improving combustion efficiency. It is placed under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 31/145] with particular constructional means -- F02M 31/145 classifies devices that thermally treat the combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixture for engines and are distinguished by specific structural features such as particular arrangements of chambers, tubes, coils or other constructional elements that implement heating, cooling or conditioning functions.
[F02M 31/16] Other apparatus for heating fuel -- F02M 31/16 covers devices that heat fuel or fuel-air mixtures before they enter a combustion engine, but which do not fall into more specific heating-apparatus subclasses. It includes any ancillary or unconventional equipment used to raise the temperature of fuel for improved combustion performance.
[F02M 31/163] Preheating by burning an auxiliary mixture -- The subclass concerns devices that raise the temperature of combustion-air, fuel or a fuel-air mixture by burning a small auxiliary mixture in a separate burner. The heat generated preheats the main charge before it enters the engine, aiding start-up, improving efficiency or lowering emissions.
[F02M 31/166] with mechanical generation of heat, e.g. by surface friction -- The F02M 31/166 subclass concerns devices that heat the combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture by mechanically generated heat, typically produced through surface friction. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering and combustion engines, specifically focusing on apparatus for thermally treating these mixtures before they enter the engine.
[F02M 31/18] to vaporise fuel -- F02M 31/18 classifies apparatus that converts fuel into a vapor before it is mixed with combustion-air, such as heaters, atomisers or pressurising devices used to prepare liquid or solid fuels for engine intake. The technology improves fuel-air mixing and combustion efficiency in combustion-engine fueling systems.
[F02M 31/183] Control -- F02M 31/183 covers control mechanisms for devices that thermally treat the combustion-air, fuel, or their mixture in engines. It includes sensors, regulators and feedback loops that adjust heating, cooling or mixing to maintain desired temperature, composition or pressure, thereby optimizing engine performance and emissions.
[F02M 31/186] with simultaneous mixing of secondary air -- F02M 31/186 covers devices that thermally treat the combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture while simultaneously blending secondary air into the stream. This subclass refines the broader category of supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures, focusing on apparatus that combine heating or cooling actions with secondary-air injection to improve combustion stability, efficiency and emissions control.
[F02M 3/12] Passageway systems -- The subclass F02M 3/12 covers passageway systems that convey air-fuel mixture to the idle circuit of a carburettor, enabling controlled engine idling. It includes manifolds, ducts, and other routing components that connect the idle jet with the intake of the combustion engine.
[F02M 31/20] for cooling -- F02M 31/20 refers to apparatus that cools the combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture before it is supplied to a combustion engine. Cooling devices in this subclass are intended to improve engine efficiency, reduce emissions, and support general supply of combustible mixtures to engines. This entry is nested under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the broader category of supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 31/205] Control -- F02M 31/205 - Control pertains to devices that regulate the thermal treatment of combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixtures supplied to combustion engines. The classification includes mechanisms that adjust heating, cooling, or conditioning of these streams to optimise engine performance, emissions, or start-up reliability.
[F02M 3/14] Location of idling system outlet relative to throttle valve -- The F02M 3/14 subclass deals with how the outlet of an idling system in a carburettor is positioned in relation to the throttle valve. It covers designs that specify whether the idle air passage discharges upstream, downstream or alongside the throttle plate, influencing idle speed and mixture control.
[F02M 33/00] Other apparatus for treating combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixture -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Other apparatus for treating combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixture, like collectors for condensed fuel, coatings of intake passages.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Combustion-air cleaners -> F02M35/00 - Arrangements for purifying liquid fuel -> F02M37/22
[F02M 33/02] for collecting and returning condensed fuel -- F02M 33/02 covers devices that capture fuel that has condensed during operation and recycle it back into the combustion system, improving fuel efficiency and reducing emissions. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and apparatus for treating combustion-air or fuel-air mixtures.
[F02M 33/025] Means not otherwise provided for -- This CPC subgroup covers devices for treating combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixtures where the means employed are not specifically defined in other subclasses. It includes any auxiliary or supplementary mechanisms for mixing, heating, humidifying or otherwise conditioning the charge that lack a dedicated classification.
[F02M 33/04] returning to the intake passage -- F02M 33/04 covers apparatus that redirects gases, such as exhaust or recirculated combustion products, back into the intake passage of a combustion engine. It includes systems for mixing returned gases with fresh intake air or fuel to improve efficiency, reduce emissions, or control combustion temperature.
[F02M 33/043] Coating of the intake passage with a porous material -- F02M 33/043 covers methods and devices that apply a porous coating to the intake passage of a combustion engine. The porous layer is intended to modify the airflow or fuel-air mixture, providing filtration, fuel injection enhancement, heat exchange or combustion improvement.
[F02M 33/046] Coating of the intake passage with material preventing the formation of condensation -- This subclass covers the application of a coating to the intake passage of a combustion engine that prevents condensation of moisture in the air-fuel mixture, thereby improving engine efficiency, reducing corrosion, and stabilizing fuel-air preparation.
[F02M 33/06] with simultaneous heat supply -- F02M 33/06 covers apparatus that supplies a combustion engine with fuel or fuel-air mixtures while simultaneously providing heat to the mixture or its components, such as pre-heating systems integrated with the delivery unit. It is used for devices that combine heating and fuel supply functions to improve engine efficiency or emissions.
[F02M 33/08] returning to the fuel tank -- The F02M 33/08 subclass covers devices that return fuel from a combustion-engine system back to the fuel tank, such as recirculation or recovery mechanisms for unburned or excess fuel. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the supply of engines with combustible mixtures, specifically addressing apparatus for treating the fuel-air mixture.
[F02M 35/00] Combustion-air cleaners, air intakes, intake silencers, or induction systems specially adapted for, or arranged on, internal-combustion engines -- The class covers devices that condition the air entering internal-combustion engines, such as combustion-air cleaners, air-intake ducts, intake silencers and other specially adapted induction systems designed to improve fuel-air mixing, reduce noise or protect the engine from contaminants.
[F02M 35/02] Air cleaners -- The F02M 35/02 class covers air-cleaning devices installed in the intake system of internal-combustion engines, such as filters, air-cleaner housings, intake silencers and related components that remove dust, particles or contaminants from the combustion air before it reaches the cylinders. It applies to systems specially adapted or positioned on engines to improve air quality and engine performance.
[F02M 35/0201] Housings; Casings; Frame constructions; Lids; Manufacturing or assembling thereof -- The entry addresses the design, manufacture and assembly of housings, casings, frame structures and lids that form part of air-intake, air-cleaning or induction systems for internal-combustion engines, focusing on components such as intake silencers and air-intake ducts.
[F02M 35/0202] Manufacturing or assembling; Materials for air cleaner housings -- F02M 35/0202 covers the manufacturing and assembly processes, as well as the selection of materials, used to produce housings for air cleaners that are fitted to internal-combustion engines, including intake silencers and related induction components.
[F02M 35/0203] by using clamps, catches, locks or the like, e.g. for disposable plug-in filter cartridges -- This CPC subclass covers intake or air-cleaning devices for internal-combustion engines where the filter element is a disposable cartridge that is secured by clamps, catches, locks or similar fastening means. The arrangement is used to attach or detach the cartridge quickly in the induction system.
[F02M 35/0204] for connecting or joining to other devices, e.g. pipes -- The F02M 35/0204 subgroup covers accessories for intake or cleaning systems of internal-combustion engines that provide means of connecting or joining those components to other devices such as pipes, ducts or other parts of the fuel-air supply network. It specifies coupling, fitting or attachment features designed for these engine-related air-handling elements.
[F02M 35/0205] Details, e.g. sensors or measuring devices -- The subclass F02M 35/0205 pertains to components that provide detailed monitoring or control of intake-air cleaning and induction systems on internal-combustion engines, such as sensors, measuring devices, and related accessories that detect airflow, pressure, temperature or composition.
[F02M 35/0207] on the clean air side -- The F02M 35/0207 entry covers components that treat the air before it enters an internal-combustion engine, such as combustion-air cleaners, intake silencers and specially adapted induction systems. These devices are designed to remove contaminants, reduce noise and optimise the mixture supplied to the engine.
[F02M 35/0208] with sensing means on both, the air feeding side and the clean air side -- The F02M 35/0208 entry pertains to intake-air cleaning or induction systems for internal-combustion engines that incorporate sensing devices on both the upstream (air-feeding) side and the downstream (clean-air) side, enabling monitoring of conditions such as pressure, flow or contamination across the filter.
[F02M 35/0209] comprising flexible, resilient, movable or rotatable elements, e.g. with vibrating or contracting movements; Springs; Valves; Flaps  F02M35/06 ,  F02M35/08  take precedence -- This subclass covers intake components for internal combustion engines that incorporate flexible or movable parts such as springs, valves, flaps that can vibrate, contract, or rotate to condition the incoming air-fuel mixture. It falls under the broader group dealing with combustion-air cleaners and intake systems and is subordinated to the more specific groups F02M35/06 and F02M35/08.
[F02M 35/021] Arrangements of air flow meters in or on air cleaner housings -- This subclass covers the design and positioning of airflow measuring devices integrated into or mounted on the housings of air cleaners for internal combustion engines. It includes configurations where the meter is built into the cleaner body, attached to its external surface, or linked to the intake duct to monitor the volume of air entering the engine.
[F02M 35/0212] Multiple cleaners -- F02M 35/0212 relates to intake-air cleaning systems for internal-combustion engines that employ two or more cleaners in series or parallel to remove contaminants from the supplied mixture. It includes arrangements such as cascade filters, combined filter-separator-dryers, or other multi-stage devices that treat the combustion air before it enters the engine.
[F02M 35/0214] arranged concentrically or coaxially -- F02M 35/0214 covers intake, air-cleaning or silencing devices for internal-combustion engines that are mounted concentrically or coaxially with another engine component, such as around a crankshaft or exhaust outlet, to supply the engine with a combustible mixture.
[F02M 35/0215] arranged in parallel -- F02M 35/0215 covers internal-combustion engine induction systems in which two or more air-cleaning or intake components are positioned side by side so that the airflow is split and recombined, i.e., arranged in parallel. This parallel layout is used to balance flow, reduce pressure loss or provide redundancy in the supply of combustible mixtures.
[F02M 35/0216] arranged in series, e.g. pre- and main filter in series -- The CPC code F02M 35/0216 covers intake-air cleaning arrangements in which two or more filters are placed in series, such as a pre-filter followed by a main filter, to purify the combustible mixture supplied to an internal-combustion engine. This sub-class falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the supply of combustion engines with air-intake or induction systems.
[F02M 35/0217] acting by electric discharge; Electrostatic precipitators therefor -- The entry F02M 35/0217 covers electrostatic precipitator systems that clean the intake air of internal-combustion engines by using an electric discharge to charge and collect airborne particles. It relates to air-intake filters or silencers that employ high-voltage ionisation for removing soot, dust or other contaminants before the mixture enters the combustion chamber.
[F02M 35/0218] acting by absorption or adsorption; trapping or removing vapours or liquids, e.g. originating from fuel -- This subclass covers intake-system devices or processes that remove fuel-derived vapours or liquids by absorption or adsorption before they enter the combustion chamber. It includes air cleaners, traps, filters or other components that capture or retain fuel vapour in the intake or induction system of internal-combustion engines.
[F02M 35/022] acting by gravity, by centrifugal, or by other inertial forces, e.g. with moistened walls -- The subgroup F02M 35/022 covers intake-air cleaning or supply arrangements that rely on gravity, centrifugal action, or other inertial forces, such as devices with moistened walls that pull out particles or droplets as the air passes through the system. It includes air-intake silencers, cleaners and induction components specially adapted for internal-combustion engines that use these physical forces instead of active filtration.
[F02M 35/0223] by centrifugal forces, e.g. cyclones -- The F02M 35/0223 subgroup classifies intake-air cleaning or induction systems for internal-combustion engines that separate particles by centrifugal action, such as cyclone separators. It covers devices that generate a swirling airflow to throw contaminants outward, allowing cleaner air to enter the engine while retaining dust and debris in a collection chamber. This approach is used in air intakes, intake silencers and related engine-supply components.
[F02M 35/0226] by gravity or by mass inertia, e.g. labyrinths, deflectors -- F02M 35/0226 covers intake or induction devices for internal-combustion engines that rely on gravity or mass-inertia to move the charge, such as labyrinths, deflectors and similar elements that direct airflow or fuel without active pumping. This falls under the broader class of combustion-engine supply systems.
[F02M 35/024] using filters, e.g. moistened -- This subclass covers methods of cleaning the intake air for internal-combustion engines by passing it through filters, optionally moistened, to remove contaminants before combustion. It includes designs of air-intake, intake-silencer or induction systems that incorporate such filtered or moistened pathways.
[F02M 35/02408] Manufacturing filter elements -- The code F02M 35/02408 covers the production of filter elements used in intake-air cleaning, intake silencers or other induction-system components for internal-combustion engines. It falls under the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the supply of engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 35/02416] Fixing, mounting, supporting or arranging filter elements; Filter element cartridges -- This CPC subclass F02M 35/02416 pertains to ways of fixing, mounting, supporting or arranging filter elements and filter-element cartridges in combustion-engine intake or air-cleaning systems. It includes devices such as air cleaners, intake silencers and other arrangements that secure filters on internal-combustion engines.
[F02M 35/02425] Support structures increasing the stability or stiffness of the filter element -- This CPC subclass deals with support structures that enhance the stability or stiffness of filter elements used in combustion-air cleaners, intake silencers or induction systems for internal-combustion engines. It is situated under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, supplying engines with combustible mixtures, focusing on air-intake cleaning components.
[F02M 35/02433] Special alignment with respect to the air intake flow, e.g. angled or in longitudinal flow direction -- F02M 35/02433 covers intake-system components that are positioned with a specific orientation relative to the incoming air flow, such as angling or aligning them in the longitudinal direction of the airflow, to improve mixing, flow stability or noise reduction in internal-combustion engines.
[F02M 35/02441] Materials or structure of filter elements, e.g. foams -- This subclass concerns the composition and design of filter elements for air-cleaning or intake-silencer systems of internal-combustion engines, including porous foams, meshes and other structures. It addresses the material choices, geometric features and manufacturing techniques that give the filter its filtering efficiency and mechanical strength.
[F02M 35/0245] Pleated, folded, corrugated filter elements, e.g. made of paper -- The entry covers pleated, folded or corrugated filter elements-often made of paper-used as air-cleaning components in the intake or induction systems of internal-combustion engines. Their pleated design provides a large filtering surface in a compact form, effectively removing dust and particles from the fuel-air mixture before it enters the engine.
[F02M 35/02458] consisting of multiple layers, e.g. coarse and fine filters; Coatings; Impregnations; Wet or moistened filter elements -- The CPC entry F02M 35/02458 covers intake and induction system filters for internal-combustion engines that employ multi-layer constructions, such as coarse and fine filter layers, and may include surface coatings, impregnations, or be wet or moistened. These features are intended to improve the removal of contaminants from the combustible mixture before it enters the engine.
[F02M 35/02466] Meshes; Grids; Perforated plates -- F02M 35/02466 pertains to meshes, grids and perforated plates used in intake or cleaning systems of internal combustion engines. These components are designed to filter, direct or silence airflow in air intakes, cleaners or induction systems that supply combustible mixtures to the engine.
[F02M 35/02475] characterised by the shape of the filter element -- F02M 35/02475 covers combustion-air cleaners, intake silencers or induction systems for internal combustion engines that are defined by the geometric shape of the filter element. The classification is used when the filter's configuration, such as cylindrical, conical or pleated forms, is the distinguishing technical feature.
[F02M 35/02483] Cylindrical, conical, oval, spherical or the like filter elements; wounded filter elements -- The code refers to filter elements of assorted shapes-cylindrical, conical, oval, spherical or similar-and to wound or pleated filter constructions that are employed in air-cleaning, intake, silencer or induction systems for internal-combustion engines.
[F02M 35/02491] Flat filter elements, e.g. rectangular -- The CPC subclass F02M 35/02491 covers flat filter elements, such as rectangular filters, that are incorporated in combustion-air cleaners, intake manifolds, intake silencers or other induction systems designed for internal-combustion engines. It is part of the hierarchy for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures, focusing on air-flow cleaning components.
[F02M 35/026] acting by guiding the air over or through an oil or other liquid bath, e.g. combined with filters -- The entry F02M 35/026 refers to intake or air-cleaning arrangements for internal-combustion engines that direct the incoming air over or through an oil or other liquid bath, often in combination with conventional filters. This method helps remove contaminants and moisture before the air enters the combustion chamber.
[F02M 35/04] specially arranged with respect to engine -- The CPC subclass F02M 35/04 covers combustion-air cleaners, air intakes, intake silencers and other induction-system components that are specially arranged with respect to an internal-combustion engine. It falls under mechanical engineering > combustion engines > supplying engines with combustible mixtures, focusing on how these intake devices are positioned or integrated on the engine.
[F02M 35/042] combined with other devices, e.g. heaters  F02M35/021 ,  F02M35/06 ,  F02M35/14  take precedence ; for use other than engine air intake cleaning, e.g. air intake filters arranged in the fuel vapour recovery system -- This subclass covers arrangements where intake-air cleaning devices are integrated with auxiliary components such as heaters, after-treatment units or other equipment, and where the air filter is positioned outside the conventional engine inlet, for example in fuel-vapour recovery circuits. It applies when those additional devices take precedence over the basic intake-cleaning features.
[F02M 35/044] Special arrangements of cleaners in or with respect to the air intake system, e.g. in the intake plenum, in ducts or with respect to carburettors -- This CPC subclass deals with special arrangements of air cleaners integrated into the intake system of internal combustion engines, including placement in the intake plenum, ducts, or near carburetors. It covers designs that combine cleaning functions with the air supply path.
[F02M 35/046] Inline cleaners, i.e. the cleaner being arranged along the length of a wall of a pipe or manifold -- F02M 35/046 covers inline air cleaners that are installed along the interior wall of a pipe or manifold in a combustion-engine intake system. These devices clean the airflow continuously as it passes through the length of the conduit, improving engine performance and reducing deposits.
[F02M 35/048] Arranging or mounting on or with respect to engines or vehicle bodies -- This CPC subclass (F02M 35/048) covers the way components such as combustion-air cleaners, intake silencers or other induction-system parts are arranged or mounted on internal-combustion engines or on the vehicle body that houses them. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and the supply of engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 35/06] combined or associated with engine's cooling blower or fan, or with flywheel -- The subclass includes intake or induction devices that are built together with, or linked to, an engine's cooling blower or fan, or mounted on the flywheel, such as combined air-intake/cooling-fan assemblies or flywheel-attached induction systems.
[F02M 35/08] with means for removing dust, from cleaners -- This subclass pertains to intake or air-cleaning systems for internal-combustion engines that include means for removing dust, such as filters, cyclones or electrostatic separators, to condition the combustible mixture before combustion.
[F02M 35/082] By-pass means -- F02M 35/082 covers bypass means incorporated in intake-air cleaning, intake, or silencing devices for internal-combustion engines, allowing the air stream to be diverted around or through a filter, valve, or other element to regulate flow, reduce pressure loss, or enable alternative operating modes. These mechanisms are part of the broader category of systems that supply combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 35/084] Dust collection chambers or discharge sockets, e.g. chambers fed by gravity or closed by a valve -- Dust collection chambers or discharge sockets are components attached to the intake or exhaust of internal-combustion engines to capture particulate matter from the air-fuel mixture; they may be gravity-fed chambers or sealed by a valve to control discharge. This arrangement helps keep the engine's air supply clean, improving performance and reducing wear.
[F02M 35/086] Dust removal by flushing, blasting, pulsating or aspirating flow, washing or the like; Mechanical dust removal, e.g. by using scrapers -- F02M 35/86 covers methods and devices that clean dust from intake or combustion-air systems of internal-combustion engines by using flushing, blasting, pulsating, aspirating, washing or similar mechanical actions, including scrapers that continuously remove deposits to maintain airflow and engine performance.
[F02M 35/088] Water, snow or ice proofing; Separation or drainage of water, snow or ice -- F02M 35/088 covers designs that protect an engine's intake system from water, snow or ice and methods for removing or draining these substances. It includes water-proofing seals, drainage channels and snow-clearing devices integrated into air cleaners, intakes or intake silencers.
[F02M 35/09] Clogging indicators -- F02M 35/09 covers devices that signal when intake or combustion-air cleaners, air-intakes, intake silencers or other induction components on internal-combustion engines become clogged. Clogging indicators detect restricted airflow or filter blockage and provide a warning or control signal to the engine system. This class falls within the supply of combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 35/10] Air intakes -- F02M 35/10 pertains to air-intake components for internal-combustion engines, encompassing intake ducts, filters, silencers and related induction systems that condition and deliver the air-fuel mixture. These devices are engineered to enhance airflow, reduce noise and prevent contaminants from entering the combustion chamber.
[F02M 35/10006] characterised by the position of elements of the air intake system in direction of the air intake flow, i.e. between ambient air inlet and supply to the combustion chamber -- The code refers to air-intake arrangements for internal-combustion engines where one or more components such as filters, silencers or pre-chambers are located sequentially along the flow path from the outside inlet to the point where air is delivered to the combustion chamber. It classifies designs that specify the positional relationship of these elements relative to the direction of the intake stream.
[F02M 35/10013] Means upstream of the air filter; Connection to the ambient air -- This subgroup covers devices placed before the engine air-filter that provide the pathway for outside air to reach the intake, such as ducting, snorkels, pre-filters or intake manifolds that connect the ambient atmosphere to the combustion chamber.
[F02M 35/10019] Means upstream of the fuel injection system, carburettor or plenum chamber  F02M35/10013  takes precedence -- The code classifies devices positioned before the fuel-injection system, carburettor or plenum chamber that clean, condition or silence the intake air for internal-combustion engines, such as air filters, intake silencers and specially arranged intake passages. It falls under the broader group for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures and is subordinate to the more specific subclass F02M 35/10013.
[F02M 35/10026] Plenum chambers -- F02M 35/10026 covers plenum chambers that are integral to the intake or air-cleaning system of internal-combustion engines, including manifolds, intake silencers and other pressure-equalising chambers that distribute the combustible mixture to the cylinders.
[F02M 35/10032] specially shaped or arranged connecting duct between carburettor or air inlet duct and the plenum chamber; specially positioned carburettors or throttle bodies with respect to the plenum chamber -- This subclass deals with specially shaped or arranged ducts linking a carburetor or air-inlet to the engine's intake plenum, as well as carburetors or throttle bodies that are positioned in a particular way relative to the plenum. It addresses design features that improve airflow uniformity, turbulence control, or packaging in internal-combustion engine induction systems.
[F02M 35/10039] Intake ducts situated partly within or on the plenum chamber housing -- Intake ducts that are positioned partially inside or on the surface of the plenum chamber housing of an internal-combustion engine, forming a portion of the air-or fuel-air mixture passage and often integrated with the intake manifold to improve flow distribution and compactness.
[F02M 35/10045] Multiple plenum chambers; Plenum chambers having inner separation walls  for V-engines  F02M35/116 ; for resonance charging  F02B27/02 -- F02M 35/10045 covers intake or induction systems for internal-combustion engines that employ multiple plenum chambers, including designs with internal separation walls for V-engine configurations and for resonance charging. The classification addresses the geometry and arrangement of these chambers to improve air flow and mixture preparation.
[F02M 35/10052] special shapes or arrangements of plenum chambers; Constructional details -- F02M 35/10052 relates to the structural design of intake plenum chambers for internal-combustion engines, covering special shapes, configurations and constructional details that influence the flow of the air-fuel mixture. It includes features such as customised chamber geometry, manifold arrangements and integration with intake silencers or air cleaners.
[F02M 35/10059] Swirl chamber upstream of the plenum chamber -- The F02M 35/10059 subclass covers intake devices for internal-combustion engines that incorporate a swirl-inducing chamber positioned before the plenum chamber. This arrangement is designed to create a rotating flow of the air-fuel mixture, improving mixing and charge homogenization prior to entry into the combustion cylinders.
[F02M 35/10065] Valves arranged in the plenum chamber -- The CPC entry F02M 35/10065 concerns valves that are placed in the plenum chamber of an engine's intake or induction system. These valves are used to control or direct the flow of air, fuel-air mixture, or cleaning media within the combustion-air cleaner, air intake or silencer of internal-combustion engines.
[F02M 35/10072] Intake runners -- This code covers the design and arrangement of intake runners - air-flow passages that connect the engine's air-filter or intake manifold to the cylinder head in internal-combustion engines. It includes features that shape, lengthen or silence the runner to optimise mixture delivery and engine performance.
[F02M 35/10078] Connections of intake systems to the engine -- The subclass F02M 35/10078 covers the ways in which intake components such as air cleaners, intake manifolds, silencers or other induction devices are connected to an internal combustion engine. It pertains to the design of the junctions, fittings, couplings and sealing methods that join the intake system to the engine cylinder head or inlet ports, ensuring proper airflow and mixture delivery.
[F02M 35/10085] having a connecting piece, e.g. a flange, between the engine and the air intake being foreseen with a throttle valve, fuel injector, mixture ducts or the like -- This CPC subclass F02M 35/10085 covers intake systems for combustion engines that include a connecting piece such as a flange between the engine and the air intake, designed to accommodate a throttle valve, fuel injector, mixture ducts or similar components. It pertains to air cleaners, intake silencers or induction arrangements specifically adapted for internal-combustion engines.
[F02M 35/10091] characterised by details of intake ducts: shapes; connections; arrangements  ducts within or on the plenum chamber  F02M35/10039 -- F02M 35/10091 pertains to internal-combustion engine intake systems and classifies inventions that specify the shape, connection method, and spatial arrangement of intake ducts, particularly those located within or mounted on the plenum chamber. The code is used for patents describing detailed duct designs that affect airflow cleaning, silencing, or distribution.
[F02M 35/10098] Straight ducts -- F02M 35/10098 covers straight ducts that are incorporated in intake or induction systems of internal-combustion engines. These ducts form part of combustion-air cleaners, air intakes or intake silencers that deliver the combustible mixture to the engine.
[F02M 35/10104] Substantially vertically arranged ducts -- The subclass F02M 35/10104 covers intake ducts that are positioned substantially vertically within combustion-air cleaning or induction systems for internal-combustion engines. It includes designs where the duct orientation is mainly upright to aid fuel-air mixing, flow control, or space-saving in the engine's intake manifold.
[F02M 35/10111] Substantially V-, C- or U-shaped ducts in direction of the flow path -- The code F02M 35/10111 covers intake ducts for internal-combustion engines that are formed in substantially V-, C- or U-shaped sections following the direction of the airflow. Such curved ducts are used in air-intake, air-cleaner or induction systems to guide the fuel-air mixture efficiently into the engine.
[F02M 35/10118] with variable cross-sections of intake ducts along their length; Venturis; Diffusers -- The CPC subclass F02M 35/10118 covers intake ducts for internal-combustion engines whose cross-section changes along the flow path, employing venturi or diffuser geometries. Such variable-section ducts are used in air-cleaners, intakes, silencers, or other induction systems to optimise pressure recovery and airflow distribution.
[F02M 35/10124] Ducts with special cross-sections, e.g. non-circular cross-section -- A duct for an internal-combustion engine intake that has a non-circular or otherwise special cross-section, designed to influence airflow, reduce noise, or improve mixing of fuel and air. This includes intake manifolds, air cleaners and silencers whose shape deviates from a simple round profile to meet specific performance or packaging requirements.
[F02M 35/10131] Ducts situated in more than one plane; Ducts of one plane crossing ducts of another plane -- This subclass covers intake or exhaust ductwork installed in more than one geometric plane, for example a single-plane duct that intersects ducts lying in another plane, enabling compact routing of air or fuel in engine induction or cleaning systems. It applies to internal-combustion engine air-intake, air-cleaner or silencer arrangements where ducts cross or are stacked across different planes.
[F02M 35/10137] Flexible ducts, e.g. bellows or hoses -- This subclass covers flexible ducts such as bellows or hoses that form part of air-cleaning, intake or silencing systems for internal-combustion engines. It relates to components that convey the combustible mixture from the intake to the engine and may be shaped to accommodate movement or vibration.
[F02M 35/10144] Connections of intake ducts to each other or to another device -- This subclass covers the ways intake ducts are linked together or attached to other components within an engine's air-supply system. It includes the design of connections for air cleaners, intake manifolds, silencers and other induction devices that feed combustible mixtures to internal-combustion engines.
[F02M 35/1015] characterised by the engine type  engine intake manifolds  F02M35/104
[F02M 35/10157] Supercharged engines -- F02M 35/10157 covers supercharged internal-combustion engines, focusing on intake-system adaptations such as air cleaners, intakes, intake silencers and induction arrangements that are specially designed for or mounted on the engine. The classification addresses how forced induction interacts with the supply of combustible mixtures to improve engine performance.
[F02M 35/10163] having air intakes specially adapted to selectively deliver naturally aspirated fluid or supercharged fluid -- The classification covers air-intake configurations for internal-combustion engines that are designed to selectively feed either naturally aspirated or supercharged air-fuel mixtures. It includes intake manifolds, filters and silencers that can be adjusted or switched to optimise flow depending on operating mode.
[F02M 35/1017] Small engines, e.g. for handheld tools, or model engines; Single cylinder engines -- F02M 35/1017 covers small single-cylinder internal-combustion engines, such as those used in handheld power tools or model applications. It concerns design features for supplying the engine with combustible mixtures, including specialised air-intake, cleaner or silencer arrangements adapted to these compact engines.
[F02M 35/10177] Engines having multiple fuel injectors or carburettors per cylinder -- This CPC subclass concerns internal-combustion engines that employ more than one fuel injector or carburettor for each cylinder, aiming to improve mixture distribution, combustion efficiency or emissions control. It is nested under the broader group dealing with supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures and related intake components.
[F02M 35/10183] Engines having intake ducts fed from a separate carburettor or injector, the idling system being considered as a separate carburettor -- This classification covers internal combustion engines where the intake ducts are supplied by a separate carburetor or injector, with the idle system treated as a distinct carburetor. It relates to intake-duct designs and idling fuel delivery.
[F02M 35/1019] Two-stroke engines; Reverse-flow scavenged or cross scavenged engines -- This CPC subclass covers two-stroke internal-combustion engines in which the fresh charge is introduced by reverse-flow or cross-scavenging, meaning the intake and exhaust ports are arranged so that the fresh mixture flows opposite to or across the exhaust flow. It includes designs of air cleaners, intake silencers, and induction systems specifically configured for such scavenging arrangements.
[F02M 35/10196] Carburetted engines -- The F02M 35/10196 entry covers internal-combustion engines that are supplied with a combustible mixture by a carburetor, including the design of air-intake, air-cleaner and induction components adapted for carburetted operation. It focuses on the mechanisms that mix fuel and air before entry into the cylinder, addressing the specific features of carburetted engine supply systems.
[F02M 35/10203] Rotary, e.g. "Wankel", engines; Engines with cylinders in star arrangement; Radial piston engines; W-engines  F02M35/112  and  F02M35/116  take precedence -- F02M 35/10203 covers combustion-air intake and cleaning arrangements for rotary (Wankel) engines, engines with star-configured cylinders, radial piston engines and W-type engines, focusing on specially adapted intake silencers, air intakes and induction systems. This group takes precedence over the more detailed sub-groups F02M35/112 and F02M35/116.
[F02M 35/10209] Fluid connections to the air intake system; their arrangement of pipes, valves or the like -- The code covers the design and arrangement of pipework, valves and other fluid-conducting components that feed or regulate fluids within an internal-combustion engine's air-intake system. It includes connections for fuel, lubrication, cleaning or pressure-control lines integrated with intake manifolds or ducts.
[F02M 35/10216] Fuel injectors; Fuel pipes or rails; Fuel pumps or pressure regulators -- F02M 35/10216 covers components that deliver fuel to an internal-combustion engine, specifically fuel injectors, the pipes or rails that channel fuel, and the pumps or pressure regulators that control fuel pressure, as part of intake-air or combustion-air cleaning and induction systems for engines.
[F02M 35/10222] Exhaust gas recirculation [EGR]; Positive crankcase ventilation [PCV]; Additional air admission, lubricant or fuel vapour admission -- This subclass concerns systems that recirculate exhaust gases, provide positive crankcase ventilation, and admit extra air, lubricant or fuel vapours into the intake of an internal-combustion engine to control emissions, improve combustion stability and reduce oil consumption.
[F02M 35/10229] the intake system acting as a vacuum or overpressure source for auxiliary devices, e.g. brake systems; Vacuum chambers  air storage chamber  F02B21/00 -- This subclass covers intake manifolds or related components of internal-combustion engines that are designed to generate a vacuum or positive pressure to operate auxiliary systems such as brakes, using built-in vacuum chambers or air-storage chambers. It includes arrangements where the engine's air-intake serves both fueling and ancillary-device actuation functions.
[F02M 35/10236] Overpressure or vacuum relief means; Burst protection
[F02M 35/10242] Devices or means connected to or integrated into air intakes; Air intakes combined with other engine or vehicle parts  filters  F02M35/02 ; silencers  F02M35/12  and  F02M35/14 ; air coolers  F02B29/04 ; heaters  F02M31/00 ; air storage tanks  F02B21/00 ; compressors  F02B33/00 ; sensors  F02M35/10373
[F02M 35/10249] Electrical or electronic devices fixed to the intake system; Electric wiring  electric heaters  F02M31/12 ; sensors  F02M35/10373 -- This subclass groups electrical and electronic items that are permanently mounted on an internal-combustion engine's intake system, including the wiring, electric heaters and sensors used to condition, monitor or control the incoming air mixture. It is part of the CPC hierarchy for combustion-engine air-cleaner and intake-system adaptations.
[F02M 35/10255] Arrangements of valves; Multi-way valves  F02M35/10032  takes precedence; valves in the plenum chamber  F02M35/10065 ; check valves  F02M35/10275 -- This CPC subclass covers the design and configuration of valves used in intake and air-cleaning systems of internal-combustion engines, including multi-way and check valves as well as valves located in the plenum chamber. It addresses how these valve arrangements control the flow of combustible mixtures into the engine.
[F02M 35/10262] Flow guides, obstructions, deflectors or the like  for generating a charge motion in the cylinder  F02B31/00 ; for re-atomising condensed fuel or homogenising fuel-air mixture  F02M29/00 -- It covers devices such as flow guides, obstacles, or deflectors placed in the combustion chamber of internal-combustion engines to create desired charge motion, improve fuel atomisation, or homogenise the fuel-air mixture. The invention relates to intake or induction components that manipulate airflow for more efficient combustion.
[F02M 35/10268] Heating, cooling or thermal insulating means  air coolers  F02B29/04 ; thermal treatment of combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixture  F02M31/00 ; details of the throttle valve housing  F02D9/1035 -- The CPC subclass F02M 35/10268 pertains to components that heat, cool or thermally insulate the intake air of internal-combustion engines, covering air-cooler devices, thermal treatment of the combustion-air or fuel-air mixture, and specific details of the throttle-valve housing used to regulate that airflow.
[F02M 35/10275] Means to avoid a change in direction of incoming fluid, e.g. all intake ducts diverging from plenum chamber at acute angles; Check valves; Flame arrestors for backfire prevention -- The subclass covers intake-air arrangements for internal-combustion engines that prevent the incoming fuel-air mixture from changing direction, such as ducts that branch from a plenum at acute angles, as well as devices like check valves and flame arrestors that stop back-fire and reverse flow.
[F02M 35/10281] Means to remove, re-atomise or redistribute condensed fuel; Means to avoid fuel particles from separating from the mixture  apparatus for re-atomising condensed fuel or homogenising fuel-air mixture  F02M29/00 ; other apparatus for treating combustion-air, fuel or fuel-air mixture  F02M33/00
[F02M 35/10288] Air intakes combined with another engine part, e.g. cylinder head cover or being cast in one piece with the exhaust manifold, cylinder head or engine block -- The entry F02M 35/10288 covers air-intake structures that are permanently joined to another engine component, such as being cast as a single piece with the cylinder-head cover, exhaust manifold, cylinder head or engine block, providing an integrated intake system for internal-combustion engines.
[F02M 35/10295] Damping means, e.g. tranquillising chamber to dampen air oscillations  intake silencers  F02M35/12 -- F02M 35/10295 covers devices that damp air pulsations in intake systems of internal-combustion engines, such as tranquillising chambers or other damping means incorporated in intake silencers. The element is used to smooth airflow and reduce noise in engine air-supply passages.
[F02M 35/10301] Flexible, resilient, pivotally or movable parts; Membranes  F02M35/10255  and  F02M35/10032  take precedence -- F02M 35/10301 classifies flexible, resilient, pivotally or otherwise movable components such as membranes used in combustion-air cleaners, air-intake ducts, intake silencers or induction systems that are specially adapted for or mounted on internal-combustion engines. The entry falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → supplying combustion engines in general with combustible mixtures or constituents, specifically the intake and cleaning subsystems. It incorporates the earlier sections on flexible or movable parts (F02M 35/10255 and F02M 35/10032).
[F02M 35/10308] Equalizing conduits, e.g. between intake ducts or between plenum chambers -- The CPC entry F02M 35/10308 covers equalizing conduits that connect intake ducts or plenum chambers in internal-combustion engines, helping to balance air flow and pressure across the intake system. These passages are used in combustion-air cleaners, air intakes, intake silencers or other intake-air arrangements to improve engine performance and reduce uneven distribution of the combustible mixture.
[F02M 35/10314] Materials for intake systems  for sound damping  F02M35/12 ; for air cleaners  F02M35/02 -- This CPC subclass relates to the materials used in intake system components of internal combustion engines, focusing on sound-damping elements such as intake silencers and on parts of air-cleaning devices. It covers the selection, design and application of such materials for improving acoustic performance and filtering efficiency in engine air-intake arrangements.
[F02M 35/10321] Plastics; Composites; Rubbers -- The code F02M 35/10321 covers the use of plastics, composites and rubbers in components such as combustion-air cleaners, air-intake ducts, intake silencers and other induction-system parts specially adapted for internal-combustion engines. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines and the general supply of engines with combustible mixtures. This classification addresses material choices that improve durability, noise reduction and airflow management.

It covers material selections and configurations that improve filtration, noise reduction or flow conditioning within these engine subsystems.
[F02M 35/1034] Manufacturing and assembling intake systems -- F02M 35/1034 covers the processes and methods used to manufacture and assemble intake systems for internal-combustion engines, including air cleaners, intake silencers and other components that condition the combustion-air mixture. The classification focuses on the production steps, tooling and assembly techniques that ensure proper fit, flow and durability of these engine-feeding parts. It falls within the broader category of supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
