[F02M 35/10367] Machining, e.g. milling, grinding, punching, sanding; Bending; Surface treatments -- The code F02M 35/10367 covers machining operations such as milling, grinding, punching, sanding and bending, as well as surface-treatment methods applied to combustion-air cleaners, air intakes, intake silencers or other induction components of internal-combustion engines.
[F02M 35/10373] Sensors for intake systems  throttle position sensors  F02D9/105 -- The F02M 35/10373 subclass covers sensors built into intake systems of internal-combustion engines that monitor throttle position, enabling precise control of air-fuel mixture delivery. These devices are used in combustion-air cleaners, air intakes, intake silencers or other induction components that supply engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 35/1038] for temperature or pressure -- F02M 35/1038 covers intake-system elements such as air cleaners, intakes or silencers that are specially designed to manage the temperature or pressure of the air-fuel mixture supplied to internal-combustion engines.
[F02M 35/10386] for flow rate  air flow meters in air cleaners  F02M35/021 ; circuit arrangements for generating control signals by measuring intake air flow  F02D41/18 -- The entry F02M 35/10386 covers devices for measuring air-flow rate in intake air cleaners of internal-combustion engines and the associated circuit arrangements that generate control signals from those measurements, enabling precise regulation of the engine's air supply.
[F02M 35/10393] for characterising a multi-component mixture, e.g. for the composition such as humidity, density or viscosity -- The code F02M 35/10393 relates to methods or devices that characterise the properties of multi-component mixtures supplied to internal-combustion engines, such as humidity, density or viscosity, to support the operation of specialised intake, air-cleaner or silencer systems.
[F02M 35/104] Intake manifolds -- F02M 35/104 covers intake manifolds for internal-combustion engines, the components that collect and distribute the air-fuel mixture to the cylinders. It includes designs for air cleaners, intake silencers and other induction elements that are specially adapted to or mounted on the engine.
[F02M 35/1042] characterised by provisions to avoid mixture or air supply from one plenum chamber to two successively firing cylinders -- F02M 35/1042 covers intake or air-supply arrangements for internal-combustion engines that include measures to prevent a combustible mixture or air from a common plenum from being delivered to two cylinders that fire in succession. Such provisions may involve separate passages, valves or timing devices that isolate the flow to each cylinder, ensuring distinct charging cycles and improving performance or emissions control.
[F02M 35/1045] characterised by the charge distribution between the cylinders/combustion chambers or its homogenisation -- F02M 35/1045 covers intake components such as air cleaners, intake silencers or induction ducts specially designed for internal-combustion engines, where the invention focuses on how the fuel-air charge is distributed or homogenised among the cylinders or combustion chambers. This sub-class deals with features that ensure uniform charge delivery to improve engine performance and emissions control.
[F02M 35/1047] characterised by some cylinders being fed from one side of engine block and the other cylinders being fed from the other side of engine block -- The CPC entry F02M 35/1047 pertains to intake or air-cleaning systems for internal-combustion engines in which the air-fuel mixture is delivered to cylinders from opposite sides of the engine block. Such split-side feeding arrangements are used to balance flow, optimise combustion and reduce noise in multi-cylinder engines.
[F02M 35/108] with primary and secondary intake passages -- F02M 35/108 covers intake arrangements for internal-combustion engines that incorporate both a primary passage for the main airflow and one or more secondary passages for auxiliary functions such as pre-mixing, scavenging or charging. The design is intended to improve fuel-air distribution, reduce turbulence and enhance engine performance.
[F02M 35/1085] the combustion chamber having multiple intake valves  modifying induction systems for imparting a rotation to the charge in the cylinder and having multiple air inlets  F02B31/08 ; shape or arrangement of intake or exhaust channels in cylinder heads  F02F1/42 -- This class covers internal-combustion engine designs where the combustion chamber uses several intake valves and multiple air inlets to create a rotational motion of the charge, improving mixture distribution. It also includes the shaping or routing of intake and exhaust passages in the cylinder head to achieve the swirl effect.
[F02M 35/112] for engines with cylinders all in one line -- F02M 35/112 covers air-cleaning, intake-silencing and induction devices specially adapted for internal-combustion engines whose cylinders are arranged in a single line, forming part of the broader category of supplying combustible mixtures to engines. It addresses intake systems, air filters and silencers designed for inline-cylinder configurations.
[F02M 35/116] for engines with cylinders in V-arrangement or arranged oppositely relative to the main shaft -- The code F02M 35/116 covers intake and air-cleaning apparatus specially adapted for internal-combustion engines whose cylinders are arranged in a V-configuration or opposite each other on the crankshaft, including air intakes, intake silencers and induction systems designed to supply a combustible mixture to such engines.
[F02M 35/1165] Boxer or pancake engines -- F02M 35/1165 covers boxer or pancake internal-combustion engines, a horizontally opposed layout where cylinders are arranged in two opposing banks on a common crankshaft, giving a low centre of gravity and a compact lateral profile.
[F02M 35/12] Intake silencers -- The classification F02M 35/12 covers intake silencers, devices that attenuate noise generated by the air-flow path of internal-combustion engines. These components are fitted to or integrated with the engine's air-intake system-such as the intake manifold or filter housing-to reduce turbulent-flow sound while still permitting sufficient airflow for combustion. They fall under the broader group of combustion-air cleaners and induction systems for supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 35/1205] Flow throttling or guiding -- The F02M 35/1205 entry covers devices and arrangements that throttle or guide the flow of air-fuel mixtures into internal-combustion engines, such as throttle valves, intake guide vanes, or flow-directing ducts, used to regulate engine speed, power output, and emissions.
[F02M 35/1211] by using inserts in the air intake flow path, e.g. baffles, throttles or orifices; Flow guides  F02M35/1244  takes precedence -- F02M 35/1211 relates to air-intake systems for internal-combustion engines that employ inserts such as baffles, throttles or orifices to guide the flow of incoming air. The flow guides are used to improve mixture formation, control air-fuel ratio or reduce intake noise.
[F02M 35/1216] by using a plurality of holes, slits, protrusions, perforations, ribs or the like; Surface structures; Turbulence generators -- F02M 35/1216 covers intake or air-cleaning components for internal-combustion engines that create turbulence by employing surface structures such as holes, slits, protrusions, perforations or ribs. These features are designed to improve fuel-air mixing, reduce flow resistance or suppress noise in the induction system. The code falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the supply of engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 35/1222] by using adjustable or movable elements, e.g. valves, membranes, bellows, expanding or shrinking elements -- F02M 35/1222 covers intake or air-cleaner arrangements for internal-combustion engines that incorporate adjustable or movable components such as valves, membranes, bellows or other expanding and shrinking elements to regulate airflow or filtration.
[F02M 35/1227] by using multiple air intake flow paths, e.g. bypass, honeycomb or pipes opening into an expansion chamber
[F02M 35/1233] by using expansion chambers in the air intake flow path -- This subclass defines intake or induction devices for internal-combustion engines that employ expansion chambers in the air-flow path to clean, smooth, or silence the incoming combustible mixture.
[F02M 35/1238] by using secondary connections to the ambient, e.g. covered by a membrane or a porous member -- F02M 35/1238 covers intake or air-cleaning devices for internal combustion engines that incorporate secondary connections to the surrounding atmosphere, such as membranes or porous members, to allow ambient air exchange.
[F02M 35/1244] using interference; Masking or reflecting sound -- The CPC entry F02M 35/1244 covers intake-air components for internal-combustion engines that employ interference techniques, such as masking or reflecting sound, to reduce noise. It relates to air cleaners, intakes, silencers or induction systems specially adapted or arranged on the engine for acoustic management.
[F02M 35/125] by using active elements, e.g. speakers -- F02M 35/125 covers intake-air devices for internal-combustion engines that incorporate active components such as speakers to modify or control the airflow, typically to improve combustion or reduce noise. This sub-class falls under supplying engines with combustible mixtures, specifically addressing air-cleaners, intakes, silencers and other induction systems that are specially adapted or arranged on the engine.
[F02M 35/1255] using resonance -- The F02M 35/1255 entry covers techniques that employ acoustic resonance in combustion-air cleaners, air-intakes, intake silencers or other induction systems for internal-combustion engines, aiming to enhance airflow, filtering or noise-reduction performance.
[F02M 35/1261] Helmholtz resonators -- Helmholtz resonators are acoustic cavities tuned to absorb specific sound frequencies, often incorporated into intake or exhaust ducts of internal-combustion engines to reduce noise and improve airflow stability. In the context of F02M 35/1261 they are classified as air-intake or induction-system components that clean or condition the combustion-air mixture supplied to the engine.
[F02M 35/1266] comprising multiple chambers or compartments -- F02M 35/1266 covers combustion-air cleaners, intake silencers or other induction systems for internal-combustion engines that are built with two or more chambers or compartments, allowing staged filtering, flow control or noise reduction within the air-intake path.
[F02M 35/1272] using absorbing, damping, insulating or reflecting materials, e.g. porous foams, fibres, rubbers, fabrics, coatings or membranes
[F02M 35/1277] Reinforcement of walls, e.g. with ribs or laminates; Walls having air gaps or additional sound damping layers -- The F02M 35/1277 class covers structural reinforcement of intake-system walls for internal-combustion engines, such as adding ribs, laminates or other stiffening features. It also includes designs where the walls contain air gaps or extra layers that provide sound-damping or acoustic insulation, improving durability and reducing noise in air-cleaner, intake or induction components.
[F02M 35/1283] Manufacturing or assembly; Connectors; Fixations -- F02M 35/1283 covers the manufacturing or assembly aspects of connectors and fixation elements used in intake-air or combustion-air cleaning systems for internal-combustion engines, such as air-intake silencers, filters, or related duct components. It includes the design, attachment methods and securing devices that integrate these parts into the engine's air-supply arrangement.
[F02M 35/1288] combined with or integrated into other devices  F02M35/14  takes precedence ; Plurality of air intake silencers  F02M35/1266  takes precedence
[F02M 35/1294] Amplifying, modulating, tuning or transmitting sound, e.g. directing sound to the passenger cabin; Sound modulation -- This subclass deals with devices that amplify, modulate, tune or transmit sound within the intake or air-cleaning system of a combustion engine, such as directing engine noise or music into the passenger cabin. It includes acoustic treatments, silencers, resonators and other components designed to control or convey sound from the engine's air-flow path.
[F02M 35/14] Combined air cleaners and silencers -- F02M 35/14 concerns components that combine an air filter with a silencer for the intake system of internal-combustion engines. The integrated device simultaneously cleans incoming air and reduces intake noise, providing a compact solution for engine supply and emission control.
[F02M 35/16] characterised by use in vehicles -- The subgroup F02M 35/16 pertains to combustion-air cleaners, air intakes, intake silencers or induction systems that are specially adapted for, or mounted on, internal-combustion engines when used in vehicles. It covers designs that integrate these components into the vehicle's fuel-air supply to improve engine performance, reduce noise or filter contaminants.
[F02M 35/161] Arrangement of the air intake system in the engine compartment, e.g. with respect to the bonnet or the vehicle front face -- The entry covers designs that position the air-intake system within the engine compartment relative to the vehicle's bonnet or front face, focusing on how the intake is mounted, routed and integrated to optimise airflow, reduce drag and protect the intake from debris while maintaining efficient engine performance.
[F02M 35/162] Motorcycles; All-terrain vehicles, e.g. quads, snowmobiles; Small vehicles, e.g. forklifts
[F02M 35/164] Heavy duty vehicles, e.g. trucks, trains, agricultural or construction machines
[F02M 35/165] Marine vessels; Ships; Boats -- F02M 35/165 pertains to air-intake and cleaning arrangements used to supply combustible mixtures to internal-combustion engines installed in marine vessels such as ships and boats. The code includes specially adapted intake silencers, combustion-air filters and induction systems designed for maritime applications.
[F02M 35/167] having outboard engines; Jet-skis -- This subdivision concerns air-intake and cleaning arrangements that are specially designed for outboard propulsion units such as jet-ski engines. It includes intake silencers, air-cleaners and induction ducts integrated with or mounted on the outboard motor, providing the necessary air supply and filtration for these small marine combustion engines.
[F02M 35/168] with means, e.g. valves, to prevent water entry -- This CPC subgroup covers air-cleaning, intake and induction components for internal-combustion engines that incorporate devices such as valves to stop water from entering the engine.
[F02M 37/00] Apparatus or systems for feeding liquid fuel from storage containers to carburettors or fuel-injection apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus or systems for feeding liquid fuel from storage containers to carburettors or fuel-injection apparatus, like pumps, fuel conduits and venting means, including fuel return lines.
[F02M 37/0011] Constructional details; Manufacturing or assembly of elements of fuel systems; Materials therefor -- This subclass deals with the design, manufacturing and assembly aspects of fuel-system components for combustion engines, such as fuel lines, pumps, filters, injectors and carburettor feed hardware, and the materials used to make these parts. It covers structural features, fabrication methods and material choices for apparatus that transports liquid fuel from tanks to the engine.
[F02M 37/0017] related to fuel pipes or their connections, e.g. joints or sealings  F02M55/004  takes precedence -- The code F02M 37/0017 covers devices that transport liquid fuel from storage tanks to carburetors or fuel-injection systems, focusing on fuel pipes, their connections, joints and sealing arrangements. It belongs to the class for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures and is superseded by the more specific subclass F02M55/004.
[F02M 37/0023] Valves in the fuel supply and return system -- The classification F02M 37/0023 covers valves used in the fuel-supply and return circuit of combustion-engine fuel systems, including those that control the flow of liquid fuel from storage tanks to carburettors or injection devices and back to the tank.
[F02M 37/0029] Pressure regulator in the low pressure fuel system  pressure regulator in low-pressure injection apparatus  F02M69/54 -- This CPC subclass (F02M 37/0029) deals with pressure regulators used in low-pressure fuel lines that feed liquid fuel from storage containers to carburettors or injection apparatus in combustion engines. The devices control and maintain the fuel pressure required for proper engine operation within the broader category of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and fuel-supplying systems.
[F02M 37/0035] Thermo sensitive valves -- Thermo-sensitive valves are temperature-responsive devices that control the flow of liquid fuel in feeding lines from storage tanks to carburettors or fuel-injection systems. By opening or closing according to fuel temperature, they help maintain proper mixture characteristics and protect engine components. This sub-class falls under mechanical engineering for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 37/0041] Means for damping pressure pulsations  equalisation of pulses in positive displacement pumps  F04B1/00 ; devices for damping fluid pulsations in pipes  F16L55/04 -- The code F02M 37/0041 covers devices that reduce or equalise pressure pulsations in liquid-fuel supply systems for combustion engines, such as dampers, accumulators or other pulsation-absorbing elements fitted to positive-displacement pumps or fuel-line piping, to provide a steadier flow to carburettors or fuel-injection apparatus.
[F02M 37/0047] Layout or arrangement of systems for feeding fuel  fuel injection apparatus characterised by their conduits and venting means  F02M55/00 ; fuel injection apparatus having a common rail  F02M63/0225 ; arrangement of fuel conduits of low pressure fuel injection apparatus  F02M69/462 -- This CPC subclass covers the design and spatial arrangement of fuel-delivery systems that transport liquid fuel from storage to carburetors or fuel-injection units, focusing on the configuration of pipelines, venting channels and related components. It includes variants such as common-rail injectors, low-pressure conduit layouts and specific venting schemes identified in the referenced sub-groups.
[F02M 37/0052] Details on the fuel return circuit; Arrangement of pressure regulators -- The code covers devices that manage the return of unused liquid fuel from carburettors or injectors back to the fuel tank and the associated pressure-regulator configurations. It details how the return circuit is designed to maintain proper fuel pressure and prevent flooding. These features belong to the broader class of fuel-feeding systems for combustion engines.
[F02M 37/0058] Returnless fuel systems, i.e. the fuel return lines are not entering the fuel tank -- This subclass describes fuel-supply arrangements that omit the conventional return line to the tank, routing excess fuel directly back to the engine or an external reservoir. By eliminating the return to the fuel tank, the system improves efficiency, reduces heat soak in the tank and is applied to liquid-fuel feeding of carburettors or injection apparatus.
[F02M 37/0064] for engines being fed with multiple fuels or fuels having special properties, e.g. bio-fuels; varying the fuel composition  controlling engines working with pluralities of fuels  F02D19/06 -- This subclass covers devices that deliver liquid fuel from one or more storage tanks to carburettors or fuel-injection systems of engines that operate on several fuels or on fuels with special characteristics such as bio-fuels, allowing the fuel mixture to be varied or controlled during operation.
[F02M 37/007] characterised by its use in vehicles, in stationary plants or in small engines, e.g. hand held tools -- F02M 37/007 relates to fuel-supply devices that move liquid fuel from storage containers to carburettors or fuel-injection systems and are intended for use in vehicles, stationary installations or small engines such as handheld tools.
[F02M 37/0076] Details of the fuel feeding system related to the fuel tank  vehicle fuel tanks  B60K15/03 -- F02M 37/0076 covers the design and configuration of fuel-feeding components that connect a vehicle's fuel tank to carburettors or fuel-injection systems, including pumps, lines, valves and control devices. The subclass details how the tank geometry, venting, pressure regulation and safety features are integrated to ensure reliable fuel delivery to the engine.
[F02M 37/0082] Devices inside the fuel tank other than fuel pumps or filters  electrical pumps submerged in fuel tanks  F02M37/10 , jet pumps  F02M37/025 -- The F02M 37/0082 entry covers devices located inside a fuel tank that are not fuel pumps or filters, such as electric pumps that operate while submerged and jet-type pumps, intended to transfer liquid fuel from the tank to carburettors or fuel-injection systems.
[F02M 37/0088] Multiple separate fuel tanks or tanks being at least partially partitioned -- The classification covers designs that use two or more distinct fuel reservoirs or a single tank divided into separate compartments to supply liquid fuel to carburettors or fuel-injection systems. Such arrangements enable the use of different fuel types, staged delivery, or redundancy in feeding the engine, improving control over mixture composition or safety.
[F02M 37/0094] Saddle tanks; Tanks having partition walls -- The code F02M 37/0094 covers fuel-storage tanks that are shaped like a saddle or contain internal partition walls and are used to supply liquid fuel from a storage container to a carburettor or fuel-injection system, typically mounted on a vehicle or engine frame. It relates to apparatus that manages the flow of combustible liquids to combustion engines.
[F02M 37/02] Feeding by means of suction apparatus, e.g. by air flow through carburettors -- F02M 37/02 classifies apparatus or systems that deliver liquid fuel to carburettors or fuel-injection units by creating a suction effect, typically using airflow through the carburettor to draw the fuel from storage containers. This includes designs that rely on pressure differentials rather than positive-displacement pumps to supply combustible mixtures to combustion engines.
[F02M 37/025] Feeding by means of a liquid fuel-driven jet pump  jet pumps  per se   F04F -- The classification F02M 37/025 covers apparatus that moves liquid fuel from a storage container to a carburettor or fuel-injection system by means of a jet pump driven directly by the fuel itself. It includes the design and operation of such fuel-driven jet pumps used for supplying combustion engines with the required liquid mixture.
[F02M 37/04] Feeding by means of driven pumps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Feeding liquid fuel from storage containers to carburettors or fuel-injection apparatus by means of driven pumps.
[F02M 37/041] Arrangements for driving gear-type pumps -- This subclass covers mechanisms that provide the driving force for gear-type fuel pumps used to transfer liquid fuel from storage tanks to carburettors or injection systems in internal-combustion engines. It includes arrangements such as engine-driven shafts, cams, electric motors or other linkages that actuate the gear pump.
[F02M 37/043] Arrangements for driving reciprocating piston-type pumps -- F02M 37/043 classifies systems that use a reciprocating piston-type pump, driven by a mechanical arrangement, to transfer liquid fuel from a storage container to a carburettor or fuel-injection unit in combustion engines. It falls under the broader category of supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 37/045] Arrangements for driving rotary positive-displacement pumps -- F02M 37/045 covers the means of powering rotary positive-displacement pumps that feed liquid fuel to carburettors or injection systems. It includes mechanical, hydraulic or electric drive arrangements that transmit rotational motion from a source such as an engine, motor or crankshaft to the pump.
[F02M 37/046] Arrangements for driving diaphragm-type pumps -- F02M 37/046 covers arrangements that power diaphragm-type fuel pumps used to deliver liquid fuel from tanks to carburettors or injection systems in combustion engines. The classification includes mechanical drive mechanisms such as cam shafts, gears or electric actuators that convert engine or auxiliary motion into the reciprocating action of the diaphragm pump. It is a sub-category of supplying combustible mixtures to engines.
[F02M 37/048] Arrangements for driving regenerative pumps, i.e. side-channel pumps -- The code F02M 37/048 covers designs that power regenerative (side-channel) fuel pumps used to transfer liquid fuel from storage tanks to carburettors or fuel-injection systems. It includes mechanical arrangements such as geared drives, cam mechanisms or motor couplings that ensure continuous pump operation in combustion-engine fuel supply systems.
[F02M 37/06] mechanically driven -- F02M 37/06 designates mechanically driven devices that transfer liquid fuel from a storage tank to a carburettor or fuel-injection system in combustion engines. It falls under the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 37/08] electrically driven -- F02M 37/08 covers electrically driven devices that transfer liquid fuel from a tank to carburettors or fuel-injection systems in combustion engines. The entry includes pumps, motors and control units powered by electricity for supplying combustible mixtures to the engine.
[F02M 37/10] submerged in fuel, e.g. in reservoir -- F02M 37/10 covers devices that deliver liquid fuel while the delivery component is immersed in the fuel, such as pumps or feeders placed directly in a reservoir. These submerged systems are used to supply carburettors or fuel-injection apparatuses in combustion engines.
[F02M 37/103] Mounting pumps on fuel tanks -- The subclass deals with the arrangement of fuel pumps that are attached directly to fuel tanks, describing configurations that integrate the pump with the tank structure to deliver liquid fuel to carburettors or injection systems in combustion engines. It falls under mechanical engineering for supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 37/106] the pump being installed in a sub-tank -- The code F02M 37/106 covers devices that transfer liquid fuel from a storage container to a carburettor or fuel-injection system, where the pump is mounted inside a subsidiary tank rather than in the main fuel reservoir. This sub-tank arrangement is used to feed combustible mixtures to combustion engines.
[F02M 37/12] fluid-driven, e.g. by compressed combustion-air -- A fluid-driven supply system that uses the pressure of compressed combustion-air to transfer liquid fuel from storage containers to carburettors or fuel-injection devices, eliminating the need for dedicated pumps. It belongs to the subclass for feeding liquid fuel in combustion-engine applications.
[F02M 37/14] the pumps being combined with other apparatus -- F02M 37/14 covers fuel-delivery systems in which the fuel pump is integrated with additional components, such as filters, regulators or injectors, forming a combined unit that supplies liquid fuel from a storage tank to carburettors or injection apparatus in combustion engines. This subclass falls under the broader category of mechanical engineering for supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 37/16] characterised by provision of personally-, e.g. manually-, operated pumps -- F02M 37/16 covers devices for supplying liquid fuel from a tank to carburettors or fuel-injection systems that are operated manually, such as hand-pumped or foot-operated fuel pumps. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion engines and the provision of fuel to them, and is used for systems where the operator directly actuates the pump to move fuel from storage to the engine.
[F02M 37/18] characterised by provision of main and auxiliary pumps -- This subclass covers devices that feed liquid fuel from storage containers to carburettors or fuel-injection systems and incorporate both a main pump and one or more auxiliary pumps, typically for use in combustion engines.
[F02M 37/20] characterised by means for preventing vapour lock -- F02M 37/20 covers fuel-delivery apparatus that transports liquid from a tank to a carburettor or fuel-injection system and includes features specifically designed to prevent vapor lock, such as heated conduits, pressure-maintaining devices or other means that keep the fuel in liquid form during supply.
[F02M 37/22] Arrangements for purifying liquid fuel specially adapted for, or arranged on, internal-combustion engines, e.g. arrangements in the feeding system -- The subclass F02M 37/22 covers devices incorporated in the fuel-feeding system of internal-combustion engines that clean or treat liquid fuel, such as filters, separators or other purification arrangements placed on or adapted for carburettors or injection apparatus. It falls under mechanical engineering for supplying combustion engines with fuel.
[F02M 37/24] characterised by water separating means -- F02M 37/24 covers devices that feed liquid fuel from a storage tank to carburetors or fuel-injection systems and incorporate means for separating water from the fuel before delivery. The water-separating means may be a filter, coalescer or separator to protect the engine.
[F02M 37/26] with water detection means -- This CPC subclass covers devices that transfer liquid fuel from a storage tank to carburettors or fuel-injection systems and include means for detecting water in the fuel. The water-detection feature is integrated into the feeding apparatus to prevent engine damage. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and fuel supply.
[F02M 37/28] with means activated by the presence of water, e.g. alarms or means for automatic drainage -- This CPC sub-class (F02M 37/28) relates to liquid-fuel feeding apparatus for carburettors or fuel-injection systems that incorporate water-sensing features, such as alarms or automatic drainage, to detect and remove moisture from the fuel supply. It is used in mechanical-engineering applications for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 37/30] characterised by heating means -- The subclass concerns fuel-supply devices that incorporate a heating element to warm liquid fuel as it moves from storage tanks to carburettors or fuel-injection systems, helping prevent icing and improve fuel flow. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and general fuel feeding arrangements.
[F02M 37/32] characterised by filters or filter arrangements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements for purifying liquid fuel specially adapted for, or arranged on, internal-combustion engines, e.g. arrangements in the feeding system that are characterised by filters or filter arrangements.
[F02M 37/34] by the filter structure, e.g. honeycomb, mesh or fibrous -- The subclass F02M 37/34 deals with apparatus or systems that deliver liquid fuel from a storage container to a carburettor or fuel-injection device using a filter structure such as a honeycomb, mesh or fibrous element to condition the fuel before it enters the engine.
[F02M 37/36] with bypass means -- This subclass pertains to fuel-delivery apparatus that incorporates a bypass arrangement, enabling part of the liquid fuel from a storage tank to be diverted around the main line feeding a carburettor or fuel-injection system, typically for priming, pressure control or start-up functions.
[F02M 37/38] with regeneration means -- F02M 37/38 covers liquid-fuel supply apparatus for internal-combustion engines that include regeneration means, such as filters, vapor-recovery or cleaning mechanisms that restore the fuel line or storage tank to maintain performance.
[F02M 37/40] with means for detection of clogging -- This CPC subclass covers apparatus or systems that feed liquid fuel from a storage container to a carburetor or fuel-injection unit and incorporate a mechanism for detecting clogging, such as sensors or indicators that alert to blockages in the fuel line or filter. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the supply of combustible mixtures to engines.
[F02M 37/42] Installation or removal of filters -- The class covers procedures and equipment for mounting or extracting fuel filters in liquid-fuel feed systems that supply carburettors or fuel-injection units of combustion engines. It includes mechanisms that enable insertion, removal, replacement or servicing of filtration elements within the fuel line.
[F02M 37/44] Filters structurally associated with pumps -- F02M 37/44 covers filters that are built into or directly attached to fuel pumps in liquid-fuel supply systems for combustion engines. The filters remove particles and impurities from the fuel before it reaches carburettors or injection units, ensuring clean fuel delivery from storage containers.
[F02M 37/46] Filters structurally associated with pressure regulators -- The F02M 37/46 subclass deals with filters that are integrally combined with pressure regulators in liquid-fuel feeding systems for combustion engines, removing contaminants before the fuel reaches carburettors or injection units.
[F02M 37/48] Filters structurally associated with fuel valves -- This class covers filters that are built into or directly attached to fuel valves in liquid-fuel feed systems for combustion engines, such as those supplying fuel to carburettors or injection devices. The filters remove contaminants before the fuel reaches the engine.
[F02M 37/50] Filters arranged in or on fuel tanks -- Filters incorporated in or mounted on fuel tanks remove solid particles, water and other contaminants from liquid fuel before it reaches carburettors or injection systems, ensuring a clean supply for combustion engines. The devices may be screens, mesh elements or coalescers positioned inside the tank or attached to its exterior, and are designed to protect downstream fuel-delivery components and improve engine reliability.
[F02M 37/52] using magnetic means -- This CPC subclass deals with fuel-delivery equipment that moves liquid fuel from a storage tank to a carburettor or fuel-injection system by magnetic means. It covers magnetic pumps, separators or actuators that use magnetic fields to control flow, pressure or metering of the fuel.
[F02M 37/54] characterised by air purging means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements for purifying liquid fuel specially adapted for, or arranged on, internal-combustion engines characterised by air purging means.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Having priming pumps -> F02M37/16
[F02M 39/00] Fuel-injection apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The physical or geometrical relation of fuel-injection apparatus to an engine or the drive arrangement of a fuel pump.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Fuel-injection apparatus in which injection pumps are driven, or injectors are actuated, by the pressure in engine working cylinders, or by impact of engine working piston -> F02M49/00 - Arrangements of injectors with respect to engines -> F02M61/14
[F02M 39/005] Arrangements of fuel feed-pumps with respect to fuel injection apparatus  F02M37/00  takes precedence -- This subclass deals with the relative positioning and mounting of fuel feed-pumps in relation to the fuel-injection system of a combustion engine. It includes designs that integrate the pump and injector, specify the layout on a common housing, or define the geometric relationship to optimize fuel delivery. The subclass follows the broader class for fuel-injection apparatus and is subordinate to F02M 37/00.
[F02M 39/02] Arrangements of fuel-injection apparatus to facilitate the driving of pumps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements of fuel-injection pumps including mounting, i.e. fitting or attaching the pumps to the engines.
[F02M 41/00] Fuel-injection apparatus with two or more injectors fed from a common pressure-source sequentially by means of a distributor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fuel injection apparatus having a driven sequential distributor, combined with or separated from a pump or common pressure source.  Injection pumps for metering and distributing fuel.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Common rail or accumulator injection systems -> F02M47/02 , F02M63/0003 , F02M63/0225
[F02M 41/02] the distributor being spaced from pumping elements -- The code F02M 41/02 covers fuel-injection systems that use a common high-pressure source to supply two or more injectors, where the distributor that allocates pressure to each injector is positioned at a distance from the pumping elements. This arrangement separates the pressure-distribution component from the pump for improved layout or maintenance.
[F02M 41/04] the distributor reciprocating -- F02M 41/04 covers fuel-injection systems in which a single high-pressure source supplies two or more injectors through a reciprocating distributor that sequentially routes the fuel to each injector, enabling precise timing and metering of combustible mixtures for combustion engines.
[F02M 41/042] by means of mechanical drive -- This subgroup covers fuel-injection systems for combustion engines in which two or more injectors receive fuel from a common pressure source and are actuated sequentially by a mechanical drive such as a cam, lever or gear. The classification applies to distributor mechanisms that control injection timing without hydraulic or electronic actuation.
[F02M 41/045] by means of hydraulic or pneumatic drive -- This CPC subclass (F02M 41/045) covers fuel-injection devices for combustion engines that use a hydraulic or pneumatic drive to sequentially supply two or more injectors from a common pressure source through a distributor, enabling controlled delivery of the combustible mixture.
[F02M 41/047] by means of electric drive -- The subclass F02M 41/047 covers fuel-injection devices for combustion engines in which two or more injectors draw from a shared pressure source and are fed sequentially by an electrically driven distributor, governing the timing and control of the combustible mixture supply.
[F02M 41/06] the distributor rotating -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Radial pistons carried by the distributor -> F02M41/1405
[F02M 41/063] the distributor and rotary valve controlling fuel passages to pumping elements being combined -- F02M 41/063 covers fuel-injection systems in which a single high-pressure source supplies two or more injectors and the fuel is sequentially routed by a distributor that incorporates a rotary valve. The subclass specifically addresses designs where the distributor and rotary valve that control the fuel passages to the pumping elements are combined into a single unit.
[F02M 41/066] Arrangements for adjusting the rotary valve-distributor -- F02M 41/066 covers mechanisms that adjust a rotary valve-distributor in fuel-injection systems that supply two or more injectors from a shared pressure source. The classification addresses the geometry, control or synchronization features that vary the timing or amount of fuel delivered to each injector.
[F02M 41/08] the distributor and pumping elements being combined -- The subclass defines fuel-injection systems in which the distributor that allocates high-pressure fuel to several injectors is merged with the pumping element, creating a single combined distributor-pump unit for sequential injection from a common pressure source.
[F02M 41/10] pump pistons acting as the distributor -- F02M 41/10 covers fuel-injection systems in which the pump pistons themselves serve as the distributor, delivering fuel from a single pressure source to two or more injectors in a sequential manner. It is used for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures by integrating the distribution function directly into the pump pistons.
[F02M 41/12] the pistons rotating to act as the distributor -- F02M 41/12 covers fuel-injection systems in which one or more rotating pistons serve as the distributor, sequentially channeling high-pressure fuel from a common source to multiple injectors. The rotating piston mechanism replaces a separate distribution valve, providing timing and metering for each injector.
[F02M 41/121] with piston arranged axially to driving shaft  F02M41/123  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Characterised by means for varying fuel delivery or injection timing -> F02M41/123
[F02M 41/122] with piston arranged radially to driving shaft  F02M41/123  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Characterised by means for varying fuel delivery or injection timing -> F02M41/123
[F02M 41/123] characterised by means for varying fuel delivery or injection timing -- The CPC subclass F02M 41/123 pertains to fuel-injection devices for combustion engines that employ two or more injectors drawing from a single pressure source and are sequentially operated through a distributor. It specifically covers configurations that include mechanisms for adjusting the amount of fuel delivered or the exact timing of each injection event, enabling variable fuel metering and timing control.
[F02M 41/124] Throttling of fuel passages to or from the pumping chamber -- This CPC subclass deals with the throttling of fuel passages that lead to or from the pumping chamber in fuel-injection systems that use two or more injectors supplied from a common pressure source, describing methods or devices for controlling flow or pressure in those fuel lines.
[F02M 41/125] Variably-timed valves controlling fuel passages -- This CPC subclass deals with fuel-injection systems where two or more injectors share a common high-pressure source and are actuated sequentially by a distributor, featuring valves whose timing can be varied to control the fuel flow.
[F02M 41/126] valves being mechanically or electrically adjustable sleeves slidably mounted on rotary piston -- F02M 41/126 covers fuel-injection devices in which the valve sleeves that control injection are mounted so they can slide on a rotary piston and can be set either mechanically or electrically. The arrangement allows sequential feeding of two or more injectors from a single pressure source, providing precise control of the combustible mixture supplied to a combustion engine.
[F02M 41/127] valves being fluid-actuated slide-valves, e.g. differential rotary-piston pump -- F02M 41/127 pertains to fluid-actuated slide valves, such as those used in differential rotary-piston pumps, which control the timing and quantity of fuel delivered by a common-pressure injection system with multiple injectors.
[F02M 41/128] Varying injection timing by angular adjustment of the face-cam or the rollers support -- The classification F02M 41/128 relates to fuel-injection systems that use two or more injectors fed from a common pressure source and operated sequentially by a distributor, focusing on mechanisms that alter injection timing. It covers angular adjustment of the face-cam or roller support to vary the timing of fuel delivery in combustion engines.
[F02M 41/14] rotary distributor supporting pump pistons -- The F02M 41/14 subclass defines rotary distributors that are integral to fuel-injection systems with two or more injectors supplied from a common pressure source and operated sequentially. It covers designs where the distributor is mounted on, or supports, the pump pistons that generate the injection pressure.
[F02M 41/1405] pistons being disposed radially with respect to rotation axis -- F02M 41/1405 relates to fuel-injection systems that draw from a single pressure source and feed two or more injectors sequentially through a distributor, with the pistons arranged radially around the rotation axis. The entry is located under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and the supply of combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 41/1411] characterised by means for varying fuel delivery or injection timing -- The F02M 41/1411 sub-group covers fuel-injection systems that use two or more injectors supplied from a single pressure source and operated sequentially by a distributor, where the apparatus includes features for adjusting either the amount of fuel delivered or the timing of each injection.
[F02M 41/1416] Devices specially adapted for angular adjustment of annular cam -- F02M 41/1416 covers devices designed to rotate or set the angular position of an annular cam that controls the timing of multiple fuel injectors fed from a common pressure source. The subclass deals with mechanisms that allow precise angular adjustment of the cam to vary injection sequencing in combustion-engine fuel-injection systems.
[F02M 41/1422] Injection being effected by means of a free-piston displaced by the pressure of fuel -- The entry F02M 41/1422 covers fuel-injection devices in which a free piston is driven by the pressure of the fuel itself, using that motion to meter and inject the mixture into a combustion engine. It applies to systems with multiple injectors fed from a common pressure source and coordinated by a distributor.
[F02M 41/1427] Arrangements for metering fuel admitted to pumping chambers, e.g. by shuttles or by throttle-valves -- The CPC subclass F02M 41/1427 covers devices that meter fuel into the pumping chambers of injection systems, using mechanisms such as shuttles or throttle-valves to control the amount of fuel supplied from a common pressure source. This falls under mechanical engineering for combustion-engine fuel supply.
[F02M 41/1433] pistons being parallel to rotation axis -- The code F02M 41/1433 relates to fuel-injection apparatus for combustion engines in which two or more injectors are fed from a common pressure source and are sequentially operated by a distributor, with the pistons positioned parallel to the rotation axis. It falls under supplying combustion engines in general with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 41/16] characterised by the distributor being fed from a constant pressure source, e.g. accumulator -- This subclass covers fuel-injection systems in which two or more injectors are supplied sequentially from a common pressure source through a distributor, and the distributor itself is fed by a constant-pressure reservoir such as an accumulator.
[F02M 43/00] Fuel-injection apparatus operating simultaneously on two or more fuels, or on a liquid fuel and another liquid, e.g. the other liquid being an anti-knock additive -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fuel injection apparatuses operating simultaneously on two or more fuels or on a liquid fuel and another liquid, the other liquid can e.g. be an antiknock additive, e.g. water.  One of the fuels may be gaseous.  This group does not contain documents describing gaseous fuel injection system. It is limited to constructional aspects of pumps or injectors.  However fuel injection apparatuses operating simultaneously on two or more fuels, one of them being gaseous, also fall under F02M43/00 .  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Injection valves for gaseous fuels -> F02M21/0257 - Engine-pertinent apparatus for adding non-fuel substances or small quantities of secondary fuel to combustion-air, main fuel, or fuel-air mixture -> F02M25/00 - Engines operating on gaseous fuel -> F02B43/00 - Engines operating on other non-liquid fuels -> F02B45/00
[F02M 43/02] Pumps peculiar thereto
[F02M 43/04] Injectors peculiar thereto -- This subclass deals with injector designs that can introduce two or more liquids - such as a primary fuel together with an additive or a second fuel - into a combustion engine at the same time. The focus is on the specific geometry, control and feeding mechanisms that enable simultaneous delivery, often to improve combustion efficiency or reduce knock.
[F02M 45/00] Fuel-injection apparatus characterised by having a cyclic delivery of specific time/pressure or time/quantity relationship -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Both functional and constructional aspects of injection apparatus. When a pump or injector is specially designed for such injection, then they are classified in these groups.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Pumps having such delivery by means of delivery valves -> F02M59/462
[F02M 45/02] with each cyclic delivery being separated into two or more parts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus with each cyclic delivery being separated into two or more parts or if there is "a continuous cyclic delivery with variable pressure", or  in case "each cyclic delivery" is not "separated into two or more parts", i.e. in case injector doesn't close between parts.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Two or more closing springs acting on injection-valve, and "each cyclic delivery" is NOT "separated into two or more parts" -> F02M45/083
[F02M 45/04] with a small initial part -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Functional and constructional aspects of injection apparatus.
[F02M 45/06] Pumps peculiar thereto
[F02M 45/063] Delivery stroke of piston being divided into two or more parts, e.g. by using specially shaped cams -- The F02M 45/063 subclass concerns fuel-injection devices where the piston's delivery stroke is split into two or more segments, often achieved with specially shaped cams. This partitioning enables a cyclic delivery of fuel that follows defined time-pressure or time-quantity relationships, improving control of the combustible mixture supplied to combustion engines.
[F02M 45/066] Having specially arranged spill port and spill contour on the piston  F02M45/063  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Delivery stroke of piston being divided into two or more parts, e.g. by using specially shaped cams -> F02M45/063
[F02M 45/08] Injectors peculiar thereto
[F02M 45/083] Having two or more closing springs acting on injection-valve -- The classification F02M 45/083 covers fuel-injection devices for combustion engines where the injection valve is returned to its closed position by two or more closing springs, allowing cyclic control of fuel delivery based on specific time-pressure or time-quantity relationships.
[F02M 45/086] Having more than one injection-valve controlling discharge orifices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus having more than one injection-valve controlling discharge orifices, even if "each cyclic delivery" is not "separated into two or more parts". Also used if there is "a continuous cyclic delivery with variable pressure".
[F02M 45/10] Other injectors with multiple-part delivery, e.g. with vibrating valves -- F02M 45/10 covers fuel-injection devices that deliver fuel in a cyclic pattern defined by precise time-pressure or time-quantity relationships, typically using multiple-part delivery mechanisms such as vibrating valves. These injectors are intended for general supply of combustion engines with fuel or other combustible mixtures. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the broader category of supplying combustion engines.
[F02M 45/12] providing a continuous delivery with variable pressure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus providing a continuous cyclic delivery with variable pressure. "Variable pressure" is understood to include "variable flow rate".  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - If "2 or more closing springs are acting on injection-valve", even if, as here, "each cyclic delivery" is not "separated into two or more parts", but is "a continuous cyclic delivery with variable pressure" -> F02M45/083 - If there is "more than one injection valve controlling discharge orifices", even if, as here, "each cyclic delivery" is not "separated into two or more parts", but is "a continuous cyclic delivery with variable pressure" -> F02M45/086
[F02M 47/00] Fuel-injection apparatus operated cyclically with fuel-injection valves actuated by fluid pressure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Injectors only. The needle valves of these injectors are closed by a fluid pressure, with the exception of injectors classified in F02M47/046  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Fuel-injectors actuated by the pressure in engine working cylinders -> F02M49/00
[F02M 47/02] of accumulator-injector type, i.e. having fuel pressure of accumulator tending to open, and fuel pressure in other chamber tending to close, injection valves and having means for periodically releasing that closing pressure -- F02M 47/02 covers accumulator-injector fuel-injection systems in which the pressure of a fuel-filled accumulator tends to open the injection valve while pressure in a separate chamber tends to close it, and the design includes a mechanism that periodically releases the closing pressure. This cyclic, fluid-pressure-actuated arrangement supplies combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 47/022] Mechanically actuated valves draining the chamber to release the closing pressure -- The CPC subclass F02M 47/022 covers mechanically actuated fuel-injection valves that drain the injection chamber to release the closing pressure, enabling the valve to shut during each injection cycle. It applies to cyclic fuel-injection systems for combustion engines where fluid-pressure-actuated valves are reset by venting the chamber, improving control of fuel delivery.
[F02M 47/025] Hydraulically actuated valves draining the chamber to release the closing pressure -- This CPC subclass F02M 47/025 covers fuel-injection systems in which the injection valves are hydraulically actuated and include a mechanism to drain the valve chamber, thereby releasing the pressure that keeps the valve closed. The drainage allows the valve to open cyclically under fluid pressure, improving control of fuel delivery in combustion engines.
[F02M 47/027] Electrically actuated valves draining the chamber to release the closing pressure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F02M 47/04] using fluid, other than fuel, for injection-valve actuation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Using fluid, other than the fuel taking part of the injection, for injection-valve actuation. Thus it also covers the case where the actuation fluid is actually fuel that is not taking part of the injection or stems from a separate fuel line, i.e. shown in this example:
[F02M 47/043] Fluid pressure acting on injection-valve in the period of non-injection to keep it closed -- The code F02M 47/043 covers fuel-injection systems where fluid pressure is applied to the injection valve during the non-injection interval to hold the valve closed. This technique is used in cyclic injection devices for combustion engines to improve control of fuel delivery.
[F02M 47/046] Fluid pressure acting on injection-valve in the period of injection to open it -- This subclass covers fuel-injection devices in which the injection valve is opened by fluid pressure applied during the injection stroke. The pressure, typically from the fuel itself or a separate hydraulic source, directly forces the valve open to admit the charge into the cylinder, allowing cyclic control of fuel delivery in combustion engines.
[F02M 47/06] Other fuel injectors peculiar thereto -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fuel injectors not covered by F02M47/02 and F02M47/04 .
[F02M 49/00] Fuel-injection apparatus in which injection pumps are driven or injectors are actuated, by the pressure in engine working cylinders, or by impact of engine working piston -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F02M 49/02] using the cylinder pressure, e.g. compression end pressure -- F02M 49/02 covers fuel-injection devices that exploit the pressure generated in an engine cylinder, such as compression end pressure, to drive injection pumps or actuate injectors. The concept is used for supplying combustion engines with fuel mixtures by harnessing cylinder pressure rather than external power sources. It falls within the broader class of mechanical engineering and combustion-engine fuel supply systems.
[F02M 49/04] using the piston impact -- F02M 49/04 covers fuel-injection devices for combustion engines in which the driving force for the injection pump or injector comes from the pressure generated in the engine's working cylinders or directly from the impact of the piston during its stroke. This approach uses the piston's kinetic energy to power fuel delivery.
[F02M 5/00] Float-controlled apparatus for maintaining a constant fuel level -- **Definition:** This place covers:  To ensure a ready mixture, the carburettor has a float chamber that contains a quantity of fuel at near-atmospheric pressure, ready for use. This reservoir is constantly replenished with fuel supplied by a fuel pump. The correct fuel level in the bowl is maintained by means of a float controlling an inlet valve When fuel is used up, the float drops, opening the inlet valve and admitting fuel. As the fuel level rises, the float rises and closes the inlet valve. The level of fuel maintained in the float bowl can usually be adjusted by a setscrew.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Floatless carburettors -> F02M17/02  **Glossary:** - Float: Hollow body floating on fuel level and acting on fuel inlet valve
[F02M 5/02] with provisions to meet variations in carburettor position, e.g. upside-down position in aircraft -- A subclass of F02M covering fuel-supply apparatus that incorporates measures to compensate for changes in carburettor orientation, such as the upside-down positions used in aircraft. It includes float-controlled devices that adjust fuel flow to maintain a constant level despite the carburettor's varied position.
[F02M 5/04] with pivotally or rotatably mounted float chambers -- The subclass F02M 5/04 covers float-controlled devices that keep a fuel reservoir at a steady level by using float chambers that are mounted so they can pivot or rotate. These mechanisms are employed in systems that supply combustible mixtures to combustion engines.
[F02M 5/06] having adjustable float mechanism, e.g. to meet dissimilarities in specific gravity of different fuels -- The subclass F02M 5/06 covers fuel-level control devices for combustion-engine supply systems that incorporate an adjustable float mechanism. The adjustment compensates for differences in fuel specific gravity, allowing accurate level maintenance across various fuel types. This feature is applied in float-controlled apparatus that keeps a constant fuel level despite changes in fuel density.
[F02M 5/08] having means for venting float chambers -- F02M 5/08 relates to fuel-supply systems for combustion engines that incorporate a vent for the float chamber. The venting means relieve pressure or vapour buildup, ensuring the float can move freely and keep the fuel level constant. It is used in float-controlled fuel-level regulators.
[F02M 5/085] consisting of an overflow from the float chamber -- F02M 5/085 refers to fuel-level control devices that maintain a constant fuel supply by using an overflow from the float chamber. The overflow path regulates the amount of fuel entering the engine, ensuring a steady combustible mixture for reliable combustion.
[F02M 5/10] having means for preventing vapour lock, e.g. insulated float chambers or forced fuel circulation through float chamber with engine stopped -- The subclass F02M 5/10 covers fuel-supply arrangements that prevent vapor lock, for example by insulating the float chamber or by forcing fuel circulation through the chamber when the engine is stopped, ensuring a constant fuel level and reliable engine start.
[F02M 51/00] Fuel-injection apparatus characterised by being operated electrically -- **Definition:** This place covers:  E.g.: electrical wirings, pumps with electric plunger drive and injectors with electric needle drive (solenoid, piezoelectric etc.)  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Injectors with electric control valves -> F02M47/027 , F02M63/0007 - Pumps with electric control valves -> F02M59/366
[F02M 51/005] Arrangement of electrical wires and connections, e.g. wire harness, sockets, plugs; Arrangement of electronic control circuits in or on fuel injection apparatus -- The F02M 51/005 subclass concerns the internal wiring and connector layout of electrically operated fuel-injection systems, including wire-harness designs, sockets and plugs. It also covers the placement and routing of electronic control circuits within or on the injection apparatus, focusing on how these electrical components are organized to manage fuel delivery.

[F02M 51/08] specially for low-pressure fuel-injection -- F02M 51/08 covers electrically operated fuel-injection devices designed for low-pressure injection in combustion engines. [F02M 5/12] Other details, e.g. floats, valves, setting devices or tools -- A float-controlled device for keeping the fuel level constant in engines, including the associated floats, valves, and setting tools that regulate fuel flow or quantity in tanks, carburetors or similar supply systems. This subclass covers mechanisms that automatically adjust fuel supply to maintain a steady level during engine operation.
[F02M 5/125] Shape of the jet needle -- The subclass concerns the geometric configuration of the jet needle in fuel-injection devices used in combustion engines, covering the shape of the needle tip, its taper, cross-section and other dimensions that influence the fuel jet stream and level control. It falls under supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures, specifically float-controlled apparatus for constant fuel level.
[F02M 5/14] Float chambers, e.g. adjustable in position  float chamber with a built-in intermediate reservoir  F02M7/06 -- F02M 5/14 pertains to float-controlled fuel-supply devices for internal combustion engines, specifically float chambers that can be positioned or adjusted and incorporate an internal reservoir for fuel. These chambers use a buoyant float to regulate the fuel level, ensuring a steady supply to the engine and preventing overflow or starvation.
[F02M 5/16] Floats -- F02M 5/16 classifies floats employed in fuel-supply systems for combustion engines, such as float-controlled valves or regulators that sense fuel level and adjust flow to keep the level constant. These devices are used in carburetors, fuel tanks and other apparatus that maintain a steady supply of combustible mixture.
[F02M 53/00] Fuel-injection apparatus characterised by having heating, cooling or thermally-insulating means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fuel injection apparatus having heating, cooling or thermally-insulating means, including devices to influence the fuel temperature in injectors or common rails  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Carburettors with heating, cooling or thermally insulating means -> F02M15/00 - Apparatus for de-liquefying gaseous fuels by heating -> F02M21/06 - Apparatus for heating fuels with low melting point -> F02M21/10 - Adding hot secondary air to fuel-air mixture -> F02M23/14 - Apparatus for thermally treating combustion-air, fuel, or fuel-air mixture -> F02M31/00

[F02M 53/046] with thermally-insulating means -- This CPC subclass covers fuel-injection devices for combustion engines that incorporate thermal-insulating components to reduce heat loss, improve temperature control, and protect surrounding parts. It is nested under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and supplying engines with combustible mixtures, specifically addressing injection apparatus with thermal-insulating means.
