[F02M 53/08] with air cooling -- This subclass defines fuel-injection devices for combustion engines that incorporate air-cooling mechanisms to control the temperature of the injector or fuel delivery components, falling under the broader category of supplying engines with combustible mixtures and of heating, cooling or thermal-insulating means.
[F02M 55/00] Fuel-injection apparatus characterised by their fuel conduits or their venting means; -- **Definition:** This place covers:  E.g. fuel conduits between pump and injector, their coupling, constructional aspects of accumulators or common rails.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Fuel feed and filtering systems -> F02M37/00 - Arrangements of conduits between fuel tank and pump and apparatus or systems for feeding liquid fuel from storage containers to carburettors or fuel-injection apparatus -> F02M37/00 - General layout of a common rail system with fuel circuit and valving for control of fuel pressure -> F02M63/0225 - Low pressure fuel rails and fuel circuits -> F02M69/462 , F02M69/465 - Venting in general -> B01D19/00 - Pipes in general -> F16L
[F02M 55/001] Pumps with means for preventing erosion on fuel discharge -- F02M 55/001 covers fuel-injection pumps that include erosion-prevention measures on the fuel discharge side, such as specially shaped conduits, protective liners or venting devices designed to reduce wear caused by high-velocity fuel jets.
[F02M 55/002] Arrangement of leakage or drain conduits in or from injectors -- This subclass concerns the design and routing of leak-or drain passages that discharge excess fuel or gases from fuel-injection nozzles, including how those conduits are positioned within the injector or led to external drainage. It applies to fuel-injection apparatus where the fuel conduits or venting means are the defining characteristic.
[F02M 55/004] Joints; Sealings -- This subgroup covers the design and features of joints and sealing arrangements in fuel-injection systems, focusing on how fuel lines or venting passages are connected and sealed to prevent leaks. It includes various sealing geometries, gaskets, welding, brazing, and other methods used to join components within the injection apparatus.
[F02M 55/005] for high pressure conduits, e.g. connected to pump outlet or to injector inlet -- F02M 55/005 classifies fuel-injection devices that are distinguished by the design of their high-pressure fuel conduits, such as lines linking the pump outlet to the injector inlet. The code falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion engines and the broader category of supplying engines with combustible mixtures. It covers apparatus where conduit geometry or venting features are the defining characteristics.
[F02M 55/007] Venting means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Venting means, i.e. for venting gases.
[F02M 55/008] Arrangement of fuel passages inside of injectors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Specific arrangements or details of fuel passages inside injectors. E.g.
[F02M 55/02] Conduits between injection pumps and injectors -- The code F02M 55/02 covers fuel-injection systems in which the conduit linking the injection pump to the injector, or its venting arrangement, is the defining feature. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the supply of engines with combustible mixtures. The classification addresses the design of the fuel lines and related vent means that connect pump and injector.
[F02M 55/025] Common rails -- F02M 55/025 pertains to fuel-injection systems for combustion engines that use a common rail-a high-pressure accumulator feeding multiple injectors-and includes the design of the fuel conduits or venting arrangements. It covers configurations that distribute fuel from the rail to the injectors and manage pressure relief or purging through dedicated channels.
[F02M 55/04] Means for damping vibrations in injection pump inlets -- This subclass covers devices that reduce vibration in the inlet of fuel-injection pumps, using specially shaped conduits, buffers or venting means to smooth pressure pulsations and protect the pump. It pertains to supplying combustible mixtures to combustion engines.
[F02M 57/00] Fuel-injectors combined or associated with other devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fuel injectors combined or associate with other devices, e.g. injectors combined with fuel pumps, injectors combined with sensors, injectors combined with engine valves, and injectors combined with plugs.
[F02M 57/005] the devices being sensors -- F02M 57/005 covers devices that combine or associate sensors with fuel-injector systems used to supply combustible mixtures to combustion engines. The classification pertains to integrated sensing elements that monitor or control fuel delivery, mixture composition or injection timing as part of the engine's fueling apparatus.
[F02M 57/02] Injectors structurally combined with fuel-injection pumps -- This subclass covers fuel injectors that are integrally built with the fuel-injection pump, forming a single structural unit for delivering combustible mixtures to combustion engines. It includes designs where the injector nozzle and pump housing are combined, improving compactness, reducing piping and simplifying assembly.
[F02M 57/021] the injector being of valveless type, e.g. the pump piston co-operating with a conical seat of an injection nozzle at the end of the pumping stroke -- This subclass addresses valveless fuel injectors where the pump piston directly engages a conical seat of the injection nozzle at the end of the pumping stroke, delivering combustible mixture to a combustion engine. It is part of the hierarchy for mechanical engineering, combustion engines, supplying engines with fuel mixtures, and fuel-injector assemblies combined with other devices.
[F02M 57/022] characterised by the pump drive -- F02M 57/022 refers to fuel-injector assemblies that are combined with or associated with other components and are defined by the way the pump is driven. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering for combustion engines, specifically the supply of engines with combustible mixtures, and covers designs where the pump drive mechanism is an integral distinguishing feature.
[F02M 57/023] mechanical -- The CPC class F02M 57/023 covers fuel-injector systems that are combined with, or integrated into, other engine components for delivering combustible mixtures to combustion engines. It applies to configurations where the injector is physically attached to or functionally linked with additional devices such as pumps, valves or control units.
[F02M 57/024] with hydraulic link for varying the piston stroke -- The code F02M 57/024 covers fuel-injector assemblies that incorporate a hydraulic link to adjust the piston stroke, enabling variable delivery of combustible mixtures to a combustion engine. This subclass pertains to devices that combine the injector with other components for precise control of fuel quantity and injection timing.
[F02M 57/025] hydraulic, e.g. with pressure amplification -- A hydraulic fuel-injector arrangement that employs pressure amplification, such as a pump or accumulator, to deliver combustible mixtures into a combustion engine. It is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the supply of engines with fuel-injectors combined with other devices. This subclass covers designs where hydraulic pressure is increased to improve injection performance.
[F02M 57/026] Construction details of pressure amplifiers, e.g. fuel passages or check valves arranged in the intensifier piston or head, particular diameter relationships, stop members, arrangement of ports or conduits -- The entry covers the construction of pressure-amplifying components in fuel-injector systems, such as the design of fuel passages, check valves, and the specific diameter relationships of those passages. It also includes stop members and the arrangement of ports or conduits within the intensifier piston or head.
[F02M 57/027] electric
[F02M 57/028] pneumatic  using engine cylinder pressure  F02M49/02
[F02M 57/04] the devices being combustion-air intake or exhaust valves -- F02M 57/04 designates devices that act as combustion-air intake or exhaust valves, frequently integrated with fuel-injectors or other components, for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 57/06] the devices being sparking plugs -- F02M 57/06 pertains to spark plugs, the devices that generate the electrical spark required to ignite the combustible mixture in a combustion engine. It includes designs where the spark plug is combined with, or closely associated to, fuel-injector components or other engine-feeding mechanisms.
[F02M 59/00] Pumps specially adapted for fuel-injection and not provided for in groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pumps specially adapted for fuel-injection and not provided for in groups F02M39/00 - F02M57/00 . For further subject-matter covered, see the section "Special rules of classification".  The structure of this group comprises:
[F02M 59/02] of reciprocating-piston type -- F02M 59/02 covers pumps of the reciprocating-piston type that are specially adapted for delivering fuel or fuel-air mixtures to combustion engines and are not listed in any other CPC group. These pumps are used for general fueling of engines with combustible constituents.
[F02M 59/022] having an accumulator storing pressurised fuel during pumping stroke of the piston for subsequent delivery to the injector
[F02M 59/025] characterised by a single piston -- This subgroup covers fuel-injection pumps that are specially designed with a single piston to pressurise and deliver fuel, where such a configuration is not covered in other pump groups. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 59/027] Unit-pumps, i.e. single piston and cylinder pump-units, e.g. for cooperating with a camshaft -- F02M 59/027 covers unit-pumps that consist of a single piston and cylinder forming a self-contained pump-unit, typically used to deliver fuel to combustion engines. These pumps are specially adapted for fuel-injection tasks, often driven by a camshaft, and are not classified elsewhere in the CPC fuel-injection groups. The entry falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the supply of combustible mixtures.
[F02M 59/04] characterised by special arrangement of cylinders with respect to piston-driving shaft, e.g. arranged parallel to that shaft -- F02M 59/04 covers combustion-engine supply systems in which the cylinders are positioned in a distinctive layout relative to the piston-driving shaft, such as cylinders placed parallel to that shaft. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion engines and fuel-injection pump arrangements not covered elsewhere.
[F02M 59/06] with cylinders arranged radially to driving shaft, e.g. in V or star arrangement -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Reciprocating piston type pumps with cylinders arranged radially to driving shaft, e.g. in V or star arrangement. "arranged radially" is interpreted to be understood as excluding in-line pumps.
[F02M 59/08] characterised by two or more pumping elements with conjoint outlet -- F02M 59/08 classifies fuel-injection pumps that contain two or more pumping elements whose discharge streams merge into a single outlet. The code falls under mechanical engineering for combustion engines, specifically pumps specially adapted for supplying combustible mixtures that are not covered by other groups. This entry is used to identify multi-element injection systems that share a common discharge path.
[F02M 59/10] characterised by the piston-drive -- This CPC subclass concerns fuel-injection pumps that are distinguished by employing a piston-driven mechanism to pressurise or meter the fuel supplied to combustion engines. The definition excludes pumps covered in other groups and applies to systems where the piston motion is the primary means of creating the injection pressure.
[F02M 59/102] Mechanical drive, e.g. tappets or cams  F02M45/063  takes precedence -- F02M 59/102 covers pumps specially adapted for fuel-injection that are not listed in other groups, focusing on the mechanical drive that operates the pump such as tappets or cams. The class deals with the means by which rotation or reciprocation is transferred to the pump elements, enabling precise delivery of combustible mixtures to combustion engines. It is subordinate to the broader category of supplying combustion engines with fuel mixtures.
[F02M 59/105] hydraulic drive  F02M59/32  takes precedence -- F02M 59/105 covers hydraulic-drive arrangements for fuel-injection pumps that are not classified elsewhere. It includes pumps in which hydraulic pressure generated by a separate motor or pump is used to pressurize the fuel for injection into combustion engines.
[F02M 59/107] pneumatic drive, e.g. crankcase pressure drive  F02M49/00  takes precedence -- F02M 59/107 covers pneumatic drive systems for fuel-injection pumps, notably crankcase-pressure drives that use compressed gas to operate the pump rather than conventional mechanical linkages. It is situated under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures → pumps specially adapted for fuel-injection, and is subordinate to the broader F02M 49/00 group which takes precedence.
[F02M 59/12] having other positive-displacement pumping elements, e.g. rotary -- F02M 59/12 covers fuel-injection pumps that incorporate positive-displacement elements other than the usual pistons, such as rotary or screw mechanisms, specifically adapted for supplying combustible mixtures to combustion engines. These pumps are not listed in the standard fuel-injection groups and are used where alternative displacement types improve flow control, pressure delivery, or packaging. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering for combustion-engine fuel supply.
[F02M 59/14] of elastic-wall type -- F02M 59/14 covers fuel-injection pumps of the elastic-wall type, such as diaphragm or bellows pumps, in which a flexible wall expands and contracts to move the fuel. These pumps are designed for supplying combustible mixtures to combustion engines and are not classified elsewhere in the fuel-injection pump groups. Their construction allows precise metering of fuel under varying pressure conditions.
[F02M 59/16] characterised by having multi-stage compression of fuel -- F02M 59/16 refers to fuel-injection pumps specially adapted for supplying combustion engines that achieve fuel pressurisation through multiple compression stages, enhancing pressure buildup and fuel atomisation for improved engine performance. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and the broader group for supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 59/18] characterised by the pumping action being achieved through release of pre-compressed springs -- The F02M 59/18 subclass covers fuel-injection pumps whose pumping motion is produced by the release of pre-compressed springs, rather than by cam-driven or hydraulic means. These spring-actuated pumps are specifically intended for supplying combustible mixtures to combustion engines and are not classified in other fuel-injection pump groups.
[F02M 59/20] Varying fuel delivery in quantity or timing -- This CPC subclass F02M 59/20 covers pumps specially adapted for fuel-injection systems that can vary the quantity or timing of fuel delivery to combustion engines, enabling adjustable fueling for performance, efficiency or emissions control.
[F02M 59/205] Quantity of fuel admitted to pumping elements being metered by an auxiliary metering device -- The CPC entry F02M 59/205 covers fuel-injection pumps that have an auxiliary device for metering the amount of fuel delivered to the pumping elements, a feature not addressed in other groups. It relates to supplying combustion engines with precisely measured quantities of combustible mixtures.
[F02M 59/22] Varying quantity by adjusting cylinder-head space -- This CPC subclass describes pumps specially adapted for fuel-injection that vary the quantity of fuel supplied to a combustion engine by adjusting the clearance volume in the cylinder head, providing fine control of the fuel mixture. It is used for engines where the amount of fuel is regulated through changes in cylinder-head space and is not covered by other groups.
[F02M 59/24] with constant-length-stroke pistons having variable effective portion of stroke -- A subclass of fuel-injection pumps that are not listed elsewhere, characterized by pistons whose total travel remains constant while the effective portion of the stroke varies to modulate fuel delivery. This design permits adjustable injection quantities without changing the overall piston length, supporting precise fueling in combustion-engine applications.
[F02M 59/243] caused by movement of cylinders relative to their pistons -- This subclass concerns issues that arise from the relative motion of a cylinder and its piston in engines, such as leakage, pressure fluctuations or fuel-delivery disturbances caused by that movement. It applies to fuel-injection pumps specially adapted for supplying combustion engines that are not covered in other groups.
[F02M 59/246] Mechanisms therefor -- The CPC entry F02M 59/246 pertains to mechanisms for pumps specifically adapted for fuel-injection that are not covered elsewhere. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures, and addresses specialized fuel-delivery components used in the overall fuel-supply system.
[F02M 59/26] caused by movements of pistons relative to their cylinders -- This subgroup covers fuel-injection pumps whose performance is influenced by the relative motion of pistons within their cylinders, addressing problems such as wear, leakage, timing errors or other effects caused by piston-cylinder interaction.
[F02M 59/265] characterised by the arrangement or form of spill port of spill contour on the piston  F02M45/066  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass F02M 59/265 covers pumps specially adapted for fuel-injection that are not listed elsewhere, focusing on the design of the spill-port contour on the piston. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, supplying engines with fuel mixtures, and is overridden by the more specific F02M 45/066 regarding piston spill-port form.
[F02M 59/28] Mechanisms therefor -- The CPC code F02M 59/28 covers mechanisms that drive or control fuel-injection pumps specially adapted for supplying combustion engines with fuel mixtures, where such mechanisms are not classified elsewhere in the CPC. It includes devices such as cam-actuated linkages, gear trains, hydraulic actuation systems and other specialized arrangements that generate the precise timing and pressure required for fuel injection. This subclass falls under the broader hierarchy of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and general engine fuelling.
[F02M 59/30] with variable-length-stroke pistons -- F02M 59/30 covers pumps specially adapted for fuel-injection that incorporate variable-length-stroke pistons, used to supply combustion engines with combustible mixtures or their constituents. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and general methods of supplying engines with fuel, distinguishing these pumps from those listed in other groups.
[F02M 59/32] fuel delivery being controlled by means of fuel-displaced auxiliary pistons, which effect injection -- **Glossary:** - Auxiliary pistons: Pistons that do not provide pressure intensification but act as metering pistons effecting injection.
[F02M 59/34] by throttling of passages to pumping elements or of overflow passages -- F02M 59/34 relates to fuel-injection pumps whose flow is controlled by narrowing the ducts that lead to the pumping elements or the overflow channels, thereby throttling the amount of fuel supplied. It is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and general supply of combustion engines with combustible mixtures, specifically for pumps that are not covered by other groups.
[F02M 59/36] by variably-timed valves controlling fuel passages -- **Glossary:** - Overflow passages: any draining passage wherever it is situated, even after (e.g. downstream of) the pump or the delivery valve. Generally, it is the pumping chamber that is drained.
[F02M 59/361] Valves being actuated mechanically -- This CPC subclass covers valves that are actuated by mechanical means in fuel-injection pumps for combustion engines, addressing designs where valve motion is driven by linkages, cams or levers rather than hydraulic or electronic control. It is situated in group F02M 59/361 under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and the supply of combustible mixtures.
[F02M 59/362] Rotary valves -- A rotary valve is a rotating member that intermittently opens and closes passages to meter and direct fuel or combustible mixtures into an engine's combustion chamber, commonly used in specialized fuel-injection pumps not covered by other groups. It belongs to the subclass F02M 59/362 for pumps specially adapted for fuel-injection.
[F02M 59/363] arrangements for adjusting the rotary valve -- The entry F02M 59/363 covers mechanisms that adjust the position, timing or clearance of a rotary valve in fuel-injection pumps that are not covered elsewhere in the CPC. It focuses on designs that allow precise control of valve opening and closing to regulate fuel flow. These arrangements are used in combustion-engine fuel-delivery systems.
[F02M 59/365] valves being actuated by the fluid pressure produced in an auxiliary pump, e.g. pumps with differential pistons; Regulated pressure of supply pump actuating a metering valve, e.g. a sleeve surrounding the pump piston -- The entry covers valves that are driven by the fluid pressure generated in an auxiliary pump, such as those used in fuel-injection systems with differential pistons, and includes arrangements where the supply-pump pressure is regulated to operate a metering valve, for example a sleeve surrounding the pump piston.
[F02M 59/366] Valves being actuated electrically -- This CPC subgroup concerns valves in fuel-injection pumps for combustion engines that are driven by electrical actuation rather than hydraulic or mechanical means. It applies to electric-operated valve mechanisms used to control the supply of combustible mixtures to the engine, covering their design and integration in injection pump systems.
[F02M 59/367] Pump inlet valves of the check valve type being open when actuated -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F02M 59/368] Pump inlet valves being closed when actuated -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F02M 59/38] Pumps characterised by adaptations to special uses or conditions -- The code F02M 59/38 covers pumps that are specially adapted for particular uses or operating conditions, notably those designed for fuel-injection in combustion engines and not listed in other groups. These pumps may incorporate unique features to handle specific fuel types, pressures, or injection strategies required for engine performance.
[F02M 59/40] for reversible engines -- F02M 59/40 designates pumps that are specially adapted for fuel-injection in reversible internal-combustion engines, i.e., engines capable of operating in both forward and reverse directions. The subgroup falls under supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures and addresses fuel-injection devices not covered by other pump groups.
[F02M 59/42] for starting of engines -- F02M 59/42 refers to pumps specially adapted for fuel-injection that are used to start internal combustion engines, a niche not covered in other groups. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and the supply of engines with combustible mixtures, focusing on starting-system components.
[F02M 59/44] Details, components parts, or accessories not provided for in, or of interest apart from, the apparatus of groups -- F02M 59/44 covers pumps specially adapted for fuel-injection that are not listed in other CPC groups, together with any ancillary details, components, parts or accessories that are separate from or only indirectly related to the main fuel-injection apparatus. The class falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and the supply of engines with combustible mixtures, and is intended for inventions dealing with unique pump configurations or supporting elements not otherwise classified.
[F02M 59/442] means preventing fuel leakage around pump plunger, e.g. fluid barriers -- F02M 59/442 covers devices that stop fuel from escaping around the pump plunger in fuel-injection systems, usually by employing fluid barriers or other sealing arrangements. These measures are applied to pumps specially adapted for fuel injection and are not listed in other groups.
[F02M 59/445] Selection of particular materials -- F02M 59/445 covers the selection of particular materials for pumps that are specially adapted to inject fuel or combustible mixtures into combustion engines, where those pump designs are not listed in any other group. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the supply of engines with fuel-injection systems.
[F02M 59/447] means specially adapted to limit fuel delivery or to supply excess of fuel temporarily, e.g. for starting of the engine  combined with fuel pump regulating devices  F02D -- This subclass concerns devices attached to fuel pumps that can temporarily restrict or increase the amount of fuel delivered to a combustion engine, for example to aid starting, and are used together with pump-regulating mechanisms. It falls under mechanical engineering - combustion engines - general fuel supply - specially adapted pumps.
[F02M 59/46] Valves -- F02M 59/46 covers valves incorporated in fuel-injection pumps that are specially adapted for delivering combustible mixtures to combustion engines and are not classified elsewhere. These valves control the timing and quantity of fuel entering the pump chambers, ensuring precise injection for efficient engine operation. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and the supply of fuel mixtures to such engines.
[F02M 59/462] Delivery valves -- F02M 59/462 covers delivery valves employed in fuel-injection pumps that are specially adapted for supplying combustible mixtures to combustion engines. The class addresses valve designs that regulate the timing and quantity of fuel discharged from the pump to the engine, focusing on features such as flow control, sealing, and response to engine operating conditions. It falls under the broader sections on mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and general fuel supply.
[F02M 59/464] Inlet valves of the check valve type -- F02M 59/464 covers inlet valves that function as check valves in fuel-injection pumps, allowing fuel to flow into the injection system while preventing backflow. These valves are specifically designed for supplying combustible mixtures to combustion engines and are not listed in other fuel-injection pump groups.
[F02M 59/466] Electrically operated valves, e.g. using electromagnetic or piezoelectric operating means -- The CPC subclass F02M 59/466 covers electrically actuated valves, such as those driven by electromagnetic or piezoelectric mechanisms, that are used in fuel-injection pumps not otherwise listed in the main groups. These valves control the delivery of combustible mixtures to combustion engines.
[F02M 59/468] using piezoelectric operating means -- F02M 59/468 covers the use of piezoelectric operating means to drive or control fuel-injection pumps that are specially adapted and not otherwise classified. It belongs to the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 59/48] Assembling -- This CPC subclass covers processes and devices for assembling fuel-injection pumps that are specially adapted for delivering combustible mixtures to combustion engines, where the assembly steps are not covered by other more specific subclasses. It includes the integration of pump components, bonding and fitting techniques used in manufacturing such pumps.
[F02M 59/485] Means for fixing delivery valve casing and barrel to each other or to pump casing -- F02M 59/485 relates to devices that secure the delivery-valve casing and its barrel together or to the pump housing in fuel-injection pumps not covered by other groups. It falls under mechanical engineering for combustion-engine fuel supply, describing a mounting arrangement that ensures proper alignment and structural integrity of the valve assembly within the pump.
[F02M 61/00] Fuel-injectors not provided for in groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Constructional parts ( F02M61/02 - F02M61/12 ) and a general, details part ( F02M61/16 - F02M61/205 ).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Mounting of injectors with respect to engines -> F02M61/14
[F02M 61/02] of valveless type -- F02M 61/02 designates valveless-type fuel-injectors used to supply combustion engines with combustible mixtures, a subcategory of supplying engines in general with such mixtures and distinct from groups that include valve-based injectors. It sits under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → supplying combustion engines in general with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 61/04] having valves -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fuel-injectors not provided for in groups F02M39/00 - F02M57/00 or F02M67/00 having valves, e.g. having a plurality of valves in series.
[F02M 61/042] The valves being provided with fuel passages -- The subclass covers fuel-injector valves that incorporate one or more internal fuel passages for delivering combustible mixture to a combustion-engine cylinder. It applies to designs where the valve itself is provided with the fuel-flow channels, distinguishing them from injector groups lacking such integrated passages.
[F02M 61/045] The valves being provided with fuel discharge orifices -- F02M 61/045 classifies valves that incorporate fuel discharge orifices used in fuel-injector systems for supplying combustible mixtures to combustion engines. The code covers designs where the valve itself forms the fuel-flow passage rather than a separate component, and it falls under the broader group for fuel injectors not listed elsewhere.
[F02M 61/047] the valves being formed by deformable nozzle parts, e.g. flexible plates or discs with fuel discharge orifices -- F02M 61/047 covers fuel-injector valves whose nozzle elements are made of deformable parts such as flexible plates or discs that contain the fuel discharge orifices. The design lets the valve change shape to control fuel flow and is classified under supplying combustion engines in general with combustible mixtures, outside the groups specifically listed for fuel-injectors.
[F02M 61/06] the valves being furnished at seated ends with pintle or plug shaped extensions -- This CPC subclass relates to fuel-injector valves that are installed at seated ends and equipped with pintle or plug-shaped extensions, used for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, supplying engines with fuel mixtures, and the fuel-injectors not provided for in other groups.
[F02M 61/08] the valves opening in direction of fuel flow -- F02M 61/08 relates to fuel-supply systems for combustion engines and defines valve arrangements that open in the same direction as the fuel flow, a feature not classified in the other fuel-injector groups. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → supplying combustion engines in general with combustible mixtures, specifically for fuel-injectors not provided for in the listed groups.
[F02M 61/10] Other injectors with elongated valve bodies, i.e. of needle-valve type -- This CPC subclass deals with fuel-injector designs that employ a needle-type valve mounted in an elongated body, distinct from the injector groups specifically enumerated elsewhere. It covers injector arrangements for delivering fuel or other combustible mixtures to internal-combustion engines. It is used when the needle-valve geometry does not fit into the more specific CPC groups.
[F02M 61/12] characterised by the provision of guiding or centring means for valve bodies -- The CPC class F02M 61/12 covers fuel-injection arrangements for combustion engines that incorporate guiding or centring mechanisms for valve bodies, ensuring precise alignment of the valves during operation. These features are not covered by any other specific fuel-injector groups within the larger subclass for supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 61/14] Arrangements of injectors with respect to engines -- The CPC subclass F02M 61/14 concerns the spatial arrangement and configuration of fuel injectors in relation to combustion engines, specifically addressing injector positioning that is not covered by other groups, within the broader field of supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 61/145] the injection nozzle opening into the air intake conduit -- This subclass covers fuel-injector designs in which the injection nozzle opens directly into the air-intake conduit of a combustion engine, delivering fuel-air mixture upstream of the throttle or manifold. It applies to injector arrangements not listed in other groups and relates to general engine fueling systems.
[F02M 61/16] Details not provided for in, or of interest apart from, the apparatus of groups -- F02M 61/16 is a cooperative patent subclass for fuel-injection arrangements that fall outside the specific apparatus groups defined for supplying combustible mixtures to combustion engines. It covers auxiliary, control or monitoring features, alternative configurations or any fuel-supply aspects not otherwise classified in the detailed fuel-injector groups. This subclass is used for inventions dealing with supplementary or non-standard elements of engine fuel delivery.
[F02M 61/161] Means for adjusting injection-valve lift -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for adjusting injection-valve lift during operation of the valve, in order to adjust it to the operating conditions and requirements of the moment.
[F02M 61/162] Means to impart a whirling motion to fuel upstream or near discharging orifices -- This subclass covers devices that create a rotational or swirling motion in fuel immediately before it reaches the injector's discharge orifice, using features such as helical passages, vanes or rotating elements to enhance atomisation and mixing. It applies to fuel-delivery systems for combustion engines where the injector type is not specified in other groups.
[F02M 61/163] Means being injection-valves with helically or spirally shaped grooves -- The subclass covers injection-valves that incorporate helically or spirally shaped grooves to create swirl and improve fuel atomisation when supplying combustible mixtures to combustion engines, applicable where other fuel-injector groups are not specified.
[F02M 61/165] Filtering elements specially adapted in fuel inlets to injector -- F02M 61/165 denotes filtering elements that are specially designed for placement in the fuel inlet of an injector, used to clean the fuel before it reaches the injector and thereby protect the injector and engine from contaminants. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the supply of combustible mixtures, and is distinct from other fuel-injector groups not covered elsewhere.
[F02M 61/166] Selection of particular materials -- F02M 61/166 concerns the selection of particular materials for fuel-injectors that are not covered by other CPC groups, focusing on the components used when supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → supplying engines in general with fuel mixtures.
[F02M 61/167] Means for compensating clearance or thermal expansion -- This CPC subgroup covers devices that adjust or accommodate clearance changes and thermal expansion in fuel-injector systems used to supply combustion engines with fuel mixtures. It includes mechanisms such as spring-loaded pistons, compliant seals, or temperature-responsive components that maintain proper injection pressure and alignment despite dimensional variations.
[F02M 61/168] Assembling; Disassembling; Manufacturing; Adjusting -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Assembling; Disassembling; Manufacturing and Adjusting in the sense of calibrating during manufacture.
[F02M 61/18] Injection nozzles, e.g. having valve seats; -- A classification for injection nozzles, such as those incorporating valve seats, used to deliver combustible mixtures or fuel constituents to combustion engines. It applies to nozzle designs that are not covered by other more specific groups within the supply of fuel-mixes to engines. This entry falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the general supply of combustible mixtures.
[F02M 61/1806] characterised by the arrangement of discharge orifices, e.g. orientation or size -- F02M 61/1806 covers fuel-injector designs for supplying combustion engines in which the defining feature is the specific arrangement of the discharge orifices, such as their orientation, size or pattern. The classification applies to injectors not listed in other groups where these orifice characteristics are essential to the injector's operation.
[F02M 61/1813] Discharge orifices having different orientations with respect to valve member direction of movement, e.g. orientations being such that fuel jets emerging from discharge orifices collide with each other -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Discharge orifices arranged so that the jets are flowing therethrough at an angle to the normal axis of the injector.
[F02M 61/182] Discharge orifices being situated in different transversal planes with respect to valve member direction of movement -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F02M 61/1826] Discharge orifices having different sizes -- This subclass addresses fuel-injection discharge orifices that are provided with different sizes to create varied spray or flow characteristics, enabling more precise delivery of combustible mixtures to combustion engines that are not covered by other fuel-injector groups.
[F02M 61/1833] Discharge orifices having changing cross sections, e.g. being divergent -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Discharge orifices having variable cross section, e.g. divergent, convergent or stepwise changing cross section. E.g.:
[F02M 61/184] Discharge orifices having non circular sections -- F02M 61/184 covers discharge orifices in fuel-injector systems that have shapes other than a simple circle, such as rectangular, elliptical or custom profiles, designed to influence spray pattern, flow rate or atomisation in combustion-engine fuel delivery. These non-circular sections are used when conventional circular orifices are insufficient for the required performance or emissions control.
[F02M 61/1846] Dimensional characteristics of discharge orifices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  E.g. the exact size, diameter is given, or a special relationship between a diameter and a length is given.
[F02M 61/1853] Orifice plates -- F02M 61/1853 classifies orifice plates employed in fuel-injector systems that supply combustible mixtures to combustion engines, covering designs not captured by other fuel-injector groups. The entry falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the subcategory for supplying combustion engines with fuel mixtures, and details the structure and function of these flow-restricting components.
[F02M 61/186] Multi-layered orifice plates -- F02M 61/186 covers multi-layered orifice plates, which are assemblies of two or more stacked plates each containing calibrated holes to meter fuel or air-fuel mixtures in engines. By varying the number and size of the layers the flow rate can be finely adjusted, providing precise delivery in fuel-injection systems that are not classified elsewhere.
[F02M 61/1866] Valve seats or member ends having multiple cones -- The CPC entry F02M 61/1866 covers valve seats or the ends of components that incorporate multiple conical surfaces. These multi-cone designs are used in fuel-injector assemblies that supply combustible mixtures to combustion engines, improving flow control and atomisation.
[F02M 61/1873] Valve seats or member ends having circumferential grooves or ridges, e.g. toroidal -- This CPC subclass pertains to valve seats or the ends of components in fuel-injection systems that are provided with circumferential grooves or ridges, such as toroidal shapes, to enhance fuel flow, atomisation or sealing. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and the supply of engines with combustible mixtures, specifically for fuel injectors not covered in other groups.
[F02M 61/188] Spherical or partly spherical shaped valve member ends -- The code F02M 61/188 designates valve-member ends that are spherical or partly spherical in shape, which are used in fuel-injector systems for supplying combustion engines with fuel mixtures. It falls under the mechanical-engineering field, specifically the subclass handling engine supply components not covered by other groups.
[F02M 61/1886] Details of valve seats not covered by groups  F02M61/1866  -  F02M61/188
[F02M 61/1893] Details of valve member ends not covered by groups  F02M61/1866  -  F02M61/188 -- The F02M 61/1893 subclass addresses the design and construction features of the ends of valve members in fuel-injector systems for combustion engines that are not covered by the more specific groups F02M 61/1866 to F02M 61/188. It concerns auxiliary or unconventional details of valve ends, such as sealing, attachment or shaping aspects, which fall outside the standard injector-valve classifications.
[F02M 61/20] Closing valves mechanically, e.g. arrangements of springs or weights -- F02M 61/20 covers mechanical closing devices for fuel-injector valves in combustion engines, such as spring-loaded or weight-biased mechanisms that shut the valve without electronic control. It applies to arrangements not classified in other fuel-injector groups and relates to the general supply of combustible mixtures to engines.
[F02M 61/205] Means specially adapted for varying the spring tension or assisting the spring force to close the injection-valve, e.g. with damping of valve lift -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Fuel injectors of the accumulator type having a spring whose force assists the hydraulic closing of the injection valve -> F02M47/02 , F02M2547/006
[F02M 63/00] Other fuel-injection apparatus having pertinent characteristics not provided for in groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Most active subgroup in this group is F02M63/0225 dealing with the common rails. The groups F02M63/0003 - F02M63/0008 include injection system having a fuel pressure source and an on/off valve in each fuel conduit to injector. These systems are different from accumulator type fuel injectors classified in F02M47/02 . Also these valves have different function than the control valves in the fuel injection pumps defined by the groups F02M59/36 - F02M59/366 .
[F02M 63/0001] Fuel-injection apparatus with specially arranged lubricating system, e.g. by fuel oil  lubrication of engines  F01M -- This CPC subclass (F02M 63/0001) covers fuel-injection devices that incorporate a specially arranged lubricating system, for example using the injected fuel itself to lubricate engine components. It applies to combustion engines where the lubrication function is integrated with the fuel-injection apparatus and is not covered by any more specific groups in the fuel-injection category.
[F02M 63/0003] Fuel-injection apparatus having a cyclically-operated valve for connecting a pressure source, e.g. constant pressure pump or accumulator, to an injection valve held closed mechanically, e.g. by springs, and automatically opened by fuel pressure  having a distributor  F02M41/16 ; low pressure fuel injection  F02M69/14 -- This subclass deals with fuel-injection devices that use a cyclically operated valve to connect a constant-pressure source such as a pump or accumulator to an injection valve that is mechanically held closed, typically by springs, and that opens automatically when fuel pressure reaches a set level, often incorporating a distributor and low-pressure injection features for supplying combustion engines with fuel mixtures not otherwise classified.
[F02M 63/0005] using valves actuated by fluid pressure -- F02M 63/0005 covers the use of fluid-pressure-actuated valves in fuel-injection devices for combustion engines. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering subclass dealing with supplying engines with combustible mixtures and captures valve designs that rely on hydraulic or pneumatic pressure rather than mechanical actuation.
[F02M 63/0007] using electrically actuated valves  injection valves  F02M51/06
[F02M 63/0008] using mechanically actuated valves -- F02M 63/0008 classifies fuel-injection apparatus that employ mechanically actuated valves to meter or deliver combustible mixtures to combustion engines, where the valve operation is not covered by other specific groups. It falls under the broader category of supplying combustion engines with fuel mixtures in mechanical engineering.
[F02M 63/001] Fuel-injection apparatus having injection valves held closed mechanically, e.g. by springs, and opened by a cyclically-operated mechanism for a time  F02M67/12  takes precedence; operated by fluid pressure  F02M47/00 ; operated electrically  F02M51/06 ; opened by fuel pressure  F02M61/00 -- This subclass covers fuel-injection devices whose injection valves are mechanically retained in the closed position, typically by springs, and are opened for a preset interval by a cyclically-driven mechanism, excluding designs that use fluid pressure, electric actuation, or fuel-pressure opening.
[F02M 63/0012] Valves  for fuel metering  see  the relevant groups, e.g.  F02M59/34 ; inlet or outlet check valves for fuel injection pumps  F02M59/46 ; for fuel injectors  see  the relevant groups, e.g.  F02M61/00 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Valves other than fuel injection valves, i.e. fuel injectors, whenever relevant.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Metering fuel by means of throttling valves in fuel-injection pumps -> F02M41/124 , F02M41/1427 , F02M59/34 - Metering fuel by means of valves in fuel-injection pumps -> F02M41/125 , F02M41/1427 , F02M59/36 - Valves used in fuel-injection pumps only and not provided for in F02M39/00 - F02M57/00 , e.g. inlet or outlet check valves for fuel-injection pumps -> F02M59/46 - Valves in general -> F16K
[F02M 63/0014] characterised by the valve actuating means -- A subclass of the CPC for mechanical engineering, combustion engines and supplying combustible mixtures, this entry covers fuel-injection apparatus not otherwise specified, distinguished by the particular means used to actuate the valve. It groups designs where the valve-actuating mechanism is the defining technical feature.
[F02M 63/0015] electrical, e.g. using solenoid -- F02M 63/0015 relates to fuel-injection devices that are powered electrically, typically using solenoids, for delivering combustible mixtures to combustion engines. [F02M 63/0019] characterised by the arrangement of electromagnets or fixed armatures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The fixed armature is the magnetic core that does not move.
[F02M 63/0021] characterised by the arrangement of mobile armatures -- The code F02M 63/0021 designates fuel-injection devices for combustion engines that are distinguished by the specific layout of their movable armatures, such as needles or plungers, within the injector. The arrangement influences injection timing, pressure or spray formation and is not covered by other more specific sub-groups.
[F02M 63/0022] the armature and the valve being allowed to move relatively to each other -- A subcategory of fuel-injection systems for combustion engines where the armature and the valve are designed to move relative to one another, allowing coordinated adjustment of fuel delivery. It falls under supplying engines with combustible mixtures and covers arrangements not listed in other specific groups.
[F02M 63/0024] in combination with permanent magnet -- F02M 63/0024 classifies fuel-injection devices that incorporate a permanent magnet as part of their operation, falling under the broader group for supplying combustion engines with fuel mixtures where other specific apparatus is not defined. The entry relates to general combustion-engine fueling systems that use magnetic elements to influence fuel flow or atomisation. It is a sub-category of Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Supplying Engines with Combustible Mixtures.
[F02M 63/0026] using piezoelectric or magnetostrictive actuators -- It covers fuel-injection devices that use piezoelectric or magnetostrictive actuators to meter and deliver combustible mixtures to combustion engines, and is placed in the "other fuel-injection apparatus" subgroup not otherwise specified in the classification. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the supply of combustion engines in general with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 63/0028] hydraulic -- F02M 63/0028 designates hydraulic fuel-injection systems used for supplying combustible mixtures to combustion engines when the apparatus does not fit any of the more specific groups. It includes hydraulic pumps, actuators or distribution units that provide fuel under pressure but are not covered elsewhere in the CPC hierarchy.
[F02M 63/0029] using a pilot valve controlling a hydraulic chamber -- This subclass covers fuel-injection devices for combustion engines that employ a pilot valve to regulate a hydraulic chamber, thereby controlling the delivery of fuel or mixture to the engine. It is applicable to arrangements not otherwise specified in the surrounding groups of the F02M 63/00 series.
[F02M 63/0031] characterized by the type of valves, e.g. special valve member details, valve seat details, valve housing details -- This subclass covers fuel-injection devices for internal combustion engines where the invention is defined primarily by the design of valve components such as the valve member, seat, or housing rather than by the injection principle itself. It gathers solutions that differ from those in other groups by focusing on special valve geometry or construction.

[F02M 63/0038] rotary -- The CPC subclass F02M 63/0038 covers rotary fuel-injection devices that do not fall within any of the more specific groups defined in the CPC. It includes rotating pumps, distributors or injectors used to supply combustible mixtures to internal-combustion engines, where the rotary mechanism possesses characteristics not otherwise listed. These devices are typically employed for precise metering and atomisation of fuel in a wide range of engine types.
[F02M 63/004] Sliding valves, e.g. spool valves, i.e. whereby the closing member has a sliding movement along a seat for opening and closing -- This CPC subclass relates to sliding valves such as spool valves used in fuel-injection systems for combustion engines, where the closing member moves linearly along a seat to control flow of fuel or mixture. It falls under the mechanical engineering category, specifically the supply of combustion engines with combustible mixtures, covering other fuel-injection apparatus not listed elsewhere.
[F02M 63/0042] combined with valve seats of the lift valve type -- F02M 63/0042 covers fuel-injection devices that incorporate lift-type valve seats, meaning the valve and its seat move together as a unit to control fuel delivery into the combustion chamber. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion-engine supply, and specifically addresses other fuel-injection apparatuses not detailed in earlier groups.
[F02M 63/0043] Two-way valves -- The entry F02M 63/0043 covers two-way valves employed in fuel-injection apparatus that supply combustible mixtures to combustion engines, where the valve design does not fall within the specified groups of the CPC. These valves control the flow direction of fuel or air-fuel mixtures in systems that are not otherwise classified.
[F02M 63/0045] Three-way valves -- F02M 63/0045 covers three-way valves employed in fuel-injection apparatus for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures. The subclass relates to valves that provide three distinct flow paths, enabling selective routing of fuel, air or exhaust streams to control mixture delivery, pressure or shut-off functions in engine fuel systems.
[F02M 63/0047] Four-way valves or valves with more than four ways -- F02M 63/0047 designates four-way valves or valves with more than four ways that are used in fuel-injection apparatus not covered by other groups. These multi-port valves provide several separate flow paths for delivering combustible mixtures to combustion engines, allowing complex control of fuel distribution. They fall under the subclass for supplying combustion engines in general with combustible mixtures.

[F02M 63/0052] with means for adjusting the opening pressure, e.g. electrically controlled -- F02M 63/0052 covers fuel-injection devices for combustion engines that incorporate a means to vary the opening pressure of the injector, for example by an electrically controlled actuator. The adjustment allows precise control of fuel-air mixture delivery during engine operation.
