[F02M 63/0056] Throttling valves, e.g. having variable opening positions throttling the flow -- This CPC subclass, F02M 63/0056, falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → supplying combustion engines in general with combustible mixtures, and covers fuel-injection apparatus not provided for in other groups. It specifically pertains to throttling valves that can be set to variable opening positions to regulate the flow of fuel or mixture. The classification is used for devices such as variable-position throttling valves in fuel-injection systems.
[F02M 63/0057] Means for avoiding fuel contact with valve actuator, e.g. isolating actuators by using bellows or diaphragms -- F02M 63/0057 covers arrangements that prevent fuel from reaching the valve actuator in a combustion-engine fuel-injection system, typically by separating the actuator with a bellows, diaphragm or similar flexible barrier, thereby protecting the actuator from fuel exposure.
[F02M 63/0059] Arrangements of valve actuators -- The CPC entry F02M 63/0059 covers the design and layout of valve-actuator systems used in fuel-injection devices for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures. It includes any mechanical or hydraulic arrangements that control valve timing or movement and are not specifically listed in other groups of the F02M subclass.
[F02M 63/0061] Single actuator acting on two or more valve bodies -- F02M 63/0061 covers fuel-injection devices in which a single actuator is designed to operate two or more valve bodies simultaneously. The provision applies to combustion-engine supply systems where a common control element drives multiple valves that regulate the delivery of combustible mixtures, and it falls under the "other fuel-injection apparatus" subgroup.
[F02M 63/0063] Two or more actuators acting on a single valve body -- F02M 63/0063 relates to fuel-injection devices for combustion engines where a single valve body is operated by two or more actuators, such as multiple pumps, pistons or electronic drives, working together to control the flow of combustible mixture. This subclass covers arrangements that are not specifically listed in other groups of the supply-of-combustion-engine-fuel-injection category.
[F02M 63/0064] Two or more actuators acting on two or more valve bodies -- The CPC subclass F02M 63/0064 concerns fuel-injection systems in which at least two actuators simultaneously operate two or more valve bodies. It applies to mechanisms that coordinate multiple moving parts to control fuel or mixture delivery in combustion engines, beyond the arrangements defined in other groups.
[F02M 63/0066] Combination of electromagnetic and piezoelectric or magnetostrictive actuators -- The CPC entry F02M 63/0066 relates to fuel-injection devices for combustion engines in which an electromagnetic actuator works together with a piezoelectric or magnetostrictive actuator to control the injection of combustible mixtures. The combined actuation aims to improve precision, response speed, or energy efficiency over single-type systems.
[F02M 63/0068] Actuators specially adapted for partial and full opening of the valves -- F02M 63/0068 covers actuators specially adapted to control valve movement in combustion-engine fuel-injection systems, allowing both partial and full opening of the valves. These devices are used for supplying engines with combustible mixtures and are distinguished from other actuator groups by their tailored opening characteristics.
[F02M 63/007] Details not provided for in, or of interest apart from, the apparatus of the groups  F02M63/0014  -  F02M63/0059 -- This CPC subclass covers fuel-injection devices for combustion engines that have features not specifically listed in the earlier groups 0014 to 0059, such as unconventional injector designs, control mechanisms or auxiliary components used to supply combustible mixtures to the engine.
[F02M 63/0071] characterised by guiding or centering means in valves including the absence of any guiding means, e.g. "flying arrangements" -- F02M 63/0071 covers fuel-injection apparatus for combustion engines that incorporate guiding or centering features for the injection valve, or deliberately lack such guides. It includes so-called flying-type arrangements where the valve is self-aligned without separate guiding elements.
[F02M 63/0073] Pressure balanced valves -- F02M 63/0073 covers pressure balanced valves used in fuel-injection systems for combustion engines, where the valve design equalizes pressures on both sides to reduce leakage and improve control. These valves are not covered by other specific groups in the fuel-injection apparatus subclass.
[F02M 63/0075] Stop members in valves, e.g. plates or disks limiting the movement of armature, valve or spring
[F02M 63/0077] Valve seat details -- The CPC subgroup F02M 63/0077 concerns the detailed design of valve seats in fuel-injection systems used to supply combustion engines. It covers specific features such as seat geometry, materials, cooling provisions, sealing elements and adjustment mechanisms that are not addressed in other related groups.
[F02M 63/0078] Valve member details, e.g. special shape, hollow or fuel passages in the valve member -- The CPC subclass F02M 63/0078 covers specific design features of valve members used in fuel-injection systems for combustion engines, including special shapes, internal hollows and dedicated fuel passages integrated within the valve. It pertains to downstream components that control the delivery of fuel or combustible mixtures into the combustion chamber.
[F02M 63/008] Hollow valve members, e.g. members internally guided -- This CPC subclass covers hollow valve elements, such as internally guided members, used in fuel-injection devices that supply combustible mixtures to combustion engines. It includes tubular or hollow valve parts that direct fuel flow and applies to designs not covered by other more specific groups in the F02M 63 series.
[F02M 63/02] Fuel-injection apparatus having several injectors fed by a common pumping element, or having several pumping elements feeding a common injector -- The CPC subclass F02M 63/02 pertains to fuel-injection devices in combustion engines where several injectors are supplied by a common pumping element, or conversely, where multiple pumping elements feed a single injector. It covers arrangements that differ from the standard single-injector-single-pump configurations and is used to classify innovations in shared-pump or shared-injector fuel delivery systems.
[F02M 63/0205] for cutting-out pumps or injectors in case of abnormal operation of the engine or the injection apparatus, e.g. over-speed, break-down of fuel pumps or injectors  safety devices acting on engine fuel system on lubricant pressure failure  F01M1/24 ; for cutting-out pumps for stopping the engine -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Safety devices acting on engine fuel system upon lubricant pressure failure -> F01M1/24
[F02M 63/021] by locking pump pistons -- F02M 63/021 covers fuel-injection devices that secure the pump pistons in a locked position, preventing their movement during operation. This feature is applied to other fuel-injection apparatus not classified elsewhere in the group, improving control of fuel delivery in combustion-engine supply systems.
[F02M 63/0215] by draining or closing fuel conduits -- F02M 63/0215 pertains to fuel-injection devices that supply combustion engines by draining or closing fuel conduits, a feature used when the engine is stopped or when fuel flow must be halted. It falls under other fuel-injection apparatus not covered in the preceding groups.
[F02M 63/022] by acting on fuel control mechanism -- F02M 63/022 covers fuel-injection apparatus that influence the engine's fuel supply by acting on the fuel-control mechanism rather than delivering fuel directly. It includes systems that adjust timing, quantity or pressure of the mixture through control valves, pistons, electronics or other elements that modify the control circuit, and is used for engines supplied with combustible mixtures not otherwise classified.
[F02M 63/0225] Fuel-injection apparatus having a common rail feeding several injectors  F02M63/0003  takes precedence ; Means for varying pressure in common rails; Pumps feeding common rails -- This CPC subclass covers fuel-injection systems that use a common rail to supply multiple injectors, including mechanisms for adjusting rail pressure and pumps that feed the rail. It falls under the broader group of other fuel-injection apparatus not otherwise classified.
[F02M 63/023] Means for varying pressure in common rails  pressure control  F02D41/3845
[F02M 63/0235] by bleeding fuel pressure -- This subgroup covers fuel-injection apparatus that use a bleed or pressure-relief method to lower fuel pressure as part of the injection control, applicable to general combustion-engine fueling systems.
[F02M 63/024] between the low pressure pump and the high pressure pump -- F02M 63/024 covers arrangements positioned between a low-pressure fuel pump and a high-pressure pump in a fuel-injection system for combustion engines. The subclass pertains to devices that control or modify the fuel flow as it moves from the low-pressure stage to the high-pressure stage, without falling into other specific groups.
[F02M 63/0245] between the high pressure pump and the common rail -- F02M 63/0245 designates the portion of a fuel-injection system that lies between the high-pressure pump and the common-rail accumulator, covering ducts, valves, or control devices that manage pressure or flow in this segment. It is used for diesel or other internal-combustion engines where the pump delivers fuel to a shared high-pressure rail before distribution to injectors.
[F02M 63/025] from the common rail -- F02M 63/025 covers fuel-injection devices for combustion engines that receive fuel from a common-rail system, focusing on designs and features of the injector that are not specifically addressed in other groups within the same subclass.
[F02M 63/026] Means for reducing the pressure in common rails at power off  pressure control  F02D41/3845 -- F02M 63/026 classifies devices that depressurise common-rail fuel lines when the engine is switched off, such as pressure-relief valves or control circuits that vent excess fuel to prevent residual pressure. This subclass belongs to mechanical engineering, combustion engines, supplying engines with combustible mixtures, and other fuel-injection apparatus not covered in other groups.
[F02M 63/0265] Pumps feeding common rails -- This CPC subclass covers pumps that supply fuel to common-rail systems in combustion engines, used for delivering combustible mixtures to injectors. It applies to pump designs and features not covered by other groups within the supplying-engines-with-fuel category.
[F02M 63/027] More than one high pressure pump feeding a single common rail -- A fuel-injection system for combustion engines that employs two or more high-pressure pumps whose outputs are combined into a single common-rail distributor, allowing the rail to be fed simultaneously from multiple sources for improved pressure stability and flow control.
[F02M 63/0275] Arrangement of common rails -- The CPC code F02M 63/0275 covers the layout and integration of common-rail systems that distribute fuel to several injectors in combustion engines. It addresses how the shared fuel rail is positioned, connected, and organized within the engine's fuel-injection architecture, focusing on designs not covered by other specific groups.
[F02M 63/028] Returnless common rail system -- The classification F02M 63/028 covers return-less common rail fuel-injection systems for combustion engines, where fuel is supplied to the rail without a return line and pressure is stabilized within the rail itself, integrating delivery and regulation in a single unit distinct from other fuel-injection apparatus.
[F02M 63/0285] having more than one common rail -- This subclass groups fuel-injection devices that employ two or more common-rail reservoirs to supply combustible mixtures to an engine, allowing separate pressure control or staged delivery that is not covered by other CPC groups.
[F02M 63/029] per cylinder bank, e.g. storing different fuels or fuels at different pressure levels per cylinder bank -- The code covers fuel-injection systems that allocate separate fuel supplies to each cylinder bank, allowing different fuel types or pressure levels to be stored and delivered per bank in a combustion engine.
[F02M 63/0295] for V- or star- or boxer-engines -- F02M 63/0295 pertains to fuel-injection devices specially designed for V-, star- or boxer-engine layouts, covering arrangements and features that are not covered by any other subgroup in the supply-of-combustible-mixture section of the classification. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the broader category of supplying engines with fuel mixtures, addressing apparatuses with characteristics unique to these multi-cylinder configurations.
[F02M 63/04] Fuel-injection apparatus having injection valves held closed by a cyclically-operated mechanism for a time and automatically opened by fuel pressure, e.g. constant-pressure pump or accumulator, when that mechanism releases the valve -- The CPC subgroup F02M 63/04 classifies fuel-injection systems in which the injection valve is kept shut by a cyclically-operated latch or cam and is released automatically by fuel pressure, such as in constant-pressure pump or accumulator arrangements. The valve opens only when the timing mechanism releases it, providing controlled injection timing.
[F02M 63/06] Use of pressure wave generated by fuel inertia to open injection valves -- This CPC subclass deals with mechanisms that exploit the pressure wave created by the inertia of fuel during injection to actuate the injection valve, allowing fuel to be introduced into a combustion engine without separate actuating devices. It falls under the broader category of supplying combustion engines with fuel mixtures and covers fuel-injection systems not otherwise classified.
[F02M 65/00] Testing fuel-injection apparatus, e.g. testing injection timing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Testing of fuel injection apparatus.  Testing injection timing.  Measuring injector spray angle  Quality check of fuel-injection apparatus.  Monitoring the function of the fuel injection apparatus in situ, e.g. for monitoring injection timing or injection quantity, or malfunction detection.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Testing of ignition -> F02P17/00 - Measuring fuel consumption -> G01F9/00
[F02M 65/001] Measuring fuel delivery of a fuel injector -- F02M 65/001 covers methods for measuring the quantity of fuel supplied by a fuel injector, typically as part of testing the timing and performance of injection systems in internal combustion engines. It is used to assess injector delivery characteristics and ensure compliance with engine fuel-supply specifications.
[F02M 65/002] Measuring fuel delivery of multi-cylinder injection pumps -- F02M 65/002 covers methods and devices for measuring the fuel delivery of multi-cylinder injection pumps, often by testing injection timing and quantity. The entry is part of the mechanical engineering hierarchy for combustion engines and the supply of engines with combustible mixtures, focusing on testing fuel-injection apparatus.
[F02M 65/003] Measuring variation of fuel pressure in high pressure line -- The code F02M 65/003 covers methods for measuring pressure fluctuations in the high-pressure fuel line of a combustion engine, typically applied when testing fuel-injection equipment such as injection timing. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the supply of engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 65/005] Measuring or detecting injection-valve lift, e.g. to determine injection timing -- F02M 65/005 relates to methods and devices for measuring or detecting the lift of injection valves in combustion engines, chiefly to determine fuel-injection timing. The subclass is applied during testing of fuel-injection apparatus to assess valve travel and verify proper timing for optimal engine performance.
[F02M 65/006] Measuring or detecting fuel leakage of fuel injection apparatus -- F02M 65/006 relates to methods and devices for measuring or detecting fuel leakage in fuel-injection apparatus of combustion engines. It includes testing techniques that monitor fuel escape during operation to assess the integrity and performance of the injection system.
[F02M 65/007] Cleaning -- The F02M 65/007 subgroup covers methods and devices for cleaning fuel-injection equipment used in combustion engines, including removal of deposits from injectors, lines and related components to restore proper spray characteristics. It is part of the broader class dealing with supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 65/008] of injectors only -- F02M 65/008 covers methods and devices for testing fuel-injector performance, specifically the timing of injection events, applied to injectors alone without the full engine system. It falls under the CPC hierarchy for mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 67/00] Fuel-injection by high-pressure gas carrying the fuel into engine working cylinders -- This CPC subclass covers high-pressure gas fuel-injection systems that deliver fuel directly into the working cylinders of combustion engines, typically diesel or direct-injection gasoline engines. It encompasses methods and apparatus for transporting fuel as a pressurised gas and introducing it into the cylinder for combustion.
[F02M 67/005] fuel-gas mixture being compressed in a pump for subsequent injection into the engine -- The code F02M 67/005 covers systems where a combustible gas-fuel mixture is first compressed in a high-pressure pump and then injected directly into the working cylinders of a combustion engine. It relates to supplying engines with fuel-air mixtures prepared and pressurised by the pump before injection.
[F02M 67/02] the gas being compressed air, e.g. compressed in pumps -- F02M 67/02 refers to fuel-injection methods for combustion engines in which compressed air acts as the carrier gas delivering fuel into the working cylinders. The air is typically compressed by pumps and mixed with the fuel, enabling high-pressure injection of the combustible mixture.
[F02M 67/04] the air being extracted from working cylinders of the engine -- F02M 67/04 pertains to the removal of air from the working cylinders of combustion engines, a step commonly associated with high-pressure gas fuel-injection systems that supply the engine with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 67/06] the gas being other than air, e.g. steam, combustion gas -- This subclass deals with fuel injection that uses a carrier gas other than air-such as steam or combustion gas-to transport fuel into the working cylinders of a combustion engine under high pressure, covering methods and apparatus where the carrier medium differs from atmospheric air.
[F02M 67/08] the gas being generated by combustion of part of fuel other than in engine working cylinders -- This subclass deals with fuel-injection systems that employ gas produced by partially burning fuel outside the engine's working cylinders. The generated gas, created by combusting a portion of the fuel supply, is used as a high-pressure carrier to deliver the remaining fuel into the cylinders. It applies to methods and apparatuses where this auxiliary combustion gas powers the injection process.
[F02M 67/10] Injectors peculiar thereto, e.g. valve less type -- This subclass covers specialised fuel-injection devices that deliver fuel to the working cylinders by a high-pressure gas stream, including designs that omit conventional valves such as valve-less injectors. It falls under the broader group for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 67/12] having valves -- The classification F02M 67/12 pertains to fuel-injection systems that employ valves to convey high-pressure gas carrying fuel into the working cylinders of combustion engines, facilitating the supply of combustible mixtures to the engine.
[F02M 67/14] characterised by provisions for injecting different fuels, e.g. main fuel and readily self-igniting starting fuel -- F02M 67/14 covers fuel-injection systems that provide separate provisions for delivering multiple fuels, such as a primary fuel together with a readily self-igniting starter fuel, into the working cylinders of a combustion engine. The classification pertains to the supply of engines with combustible mixtures using high-pressure gas to transport the fuels.
[F02M 69/00] Low-pressure fuel-injection apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Low pressure fuel injection apparatuses including such apparatus with both continuous and intermittent injection, and apparatus injecting different types of fuel. Low pressure fuel injection normally means gasoline fuel injection. However in the recent years with common rail technology development the gap between diesel fuel injection and gasoline fuel injection has become very narrow. Therefore, it is more practical to classify the details of gasoline fuel injection apparatus just in the groups F02M41/00 - F02M63/00 . In specific cases and the injection of fuel in the air inlet manifold, the inventions are classified in the subgroups of F02M69/00 .  **Glossary:** - Low pressure fuel injection: fuel injection in which the fuel-air mixture containing fuel thus injected will be substantially compressed in the compression stroke of the engine. Traditionally, the concept of "low-pressure" fuel-injection is associated with spark ignition engines, where the mixture is compressed. - High-pressure fuel-injection: fuel-injection being associated with compression ignition engines, where fuel is injected after compression of the air.
[F02M 69/002] characterised by means for intermittently metering the portion of fuel injected  F02M69/12 ,  F02M69/14  take precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Low-pressure fuel-injection apparatus comprising a fuel-displaced free-piston for intermittently metering and supplying fuel to injection nozzles -> F02M69/12 - Low-pressure fuel-injection apparatus having cyclically-operated valves connecting injection nozzles to a source of fuel -> F02M69/14
[F02M 69/005] characterised by control of air admission to the engine according to the fuel injected -- The subclass F02M 69/005 covers low-pressure fuel-injection systems that regulate the amount of air entering the engine in proportion to the injected fuel, thereby controlling the air-fuel mixture for combustion. It is a subcategory of supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 69/007] by means of devices using fuel pressure deviated from main fuel circuit acting on air throttle valve -- F02M 69/007 relates to low-pressure fuel-injection devices that draw fuel from the main circuit and use the diverted pressure to operate the air-throttle valve, thereby regulating the fuel-air mixture supplied to a combustion engine.
[F02M 69/02] Pumps peculiar thereto -- F02M 69/02 pertains to pumps that are specially adapted for low-pressure fuel-injection systems used to supply combustible mixtures to combustion engines. These pumps are designed to handle the precise flow and pressure requirements of such engines in general-purpose fueling arrangements.
[F02M 69/04] Injectors peculiar thereto -- F02M 69/04 designates low-pressure fuel-injection apparatus for combustion engines, specifically injectors specially adapted to supply the engine with combustible mixtures or their constituents. This subclass covers devices that differ from standard high-pressure injectors and are tailored to the particular requirements of the engine's fuel delivery system.
[F02M 69/041] having vibrating means for atomizing the fuel, e.g. with sonic or ultrasonic vibrations -- This subclass covers low-pressure fuel-injection devices that employ vibrating elements such as sonic or ultrasonic transducers to atomize the fuel before it enters a combustion engine. The vibration aids in breaking the fuel into fine droplets, improving mixing with air.
[F02M 69/042] Positioning of injectors with respect to engine, e.g. in the air intake conduit  mounting of injectors  F02M61/14 -- The subclass deals with how low-pressure fuel injectors are situated in relation to the engine, particularly when placed in the air-intake conduit or similar passages. It covers mounting arrangements, positioning relative to the airflow and securing methods that influence mixture formation and combustion performance.
[F02M 69/043] for injecting into the intake conduit upstream of an air throttle valve -- F02M 69/043 covers low-pressure fuel-injection devices that introduce fuel into the intake conduit upstream of an air-throttle valve, enabling the fuel-air mixture to be formed before it passes through the throttle.
[F02M 69/044] for injecting into the intake conduit downstream of an air throttle valve -- F02M 69/044 covers low-pressure fuel-injection devices that deliver fuel into the intake manifold after the air throttle valve, mixing the fuel with incoming air before it reaches the engine cylinders. The classification applies to systems that inject combustible mixtures downstream of the throttle to control engine fueling and improve combustion efficiency.
[F02M 69/045] for injecting into the combustion chamber  F02M69/046  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The injection nozzle opening into the engine cylinder for direct injection, i.e. in-cylinder injection.
[F02M 69/046] for injecting into both the combustion chamber and the intake conduit -- **Definition:** This place covers:  One injector injecting fuel in the intake plus one injector injecting directly into the combustion chamber.
[F02M 69/047] injectors with air chambers, e.g. communicating with atmosphere for aerating the nozzles  F02M69/325  takes precedence -- F02M 69/047 covers low-pressure fuel-injection devices that incorporate an air chamber or atmospheric vent which introduces air into the nozzle to aerate the fuel stream, enhancing atomisation for combustion-engine supply. It is a subdivision of supplying engines with combustible mixtures and is superseded by the more specific F02M 69/325.
[F02M 69/048] having variable fuel outlets, e.g. controlled by a valve actuated by operator -- This CPC entry F02M 69/048 concerns low-pressure fuel-injection devices for combustion engines that feature multiple fuel outlets whose flow can be varied, typically by an operator-controlled valve. The arrangement allows the operator to adjust the mixture supplied to the engine, enhancing flexibility in fueling.
[F02M 69/06] characterised by the pressurisation of the fuel being caused by centrifugal force acting on the fuel -- The code F02M 69/06 designates low-pressure fuel-injection devices in which the fuel is pressurised by centrifugal force generated by a rotating element. Such systems use the spin of a distributor, rotor or similar component to raise fuel pressure before it is metered into the engine's combustion chamber.
[F02M 69/08] characterised by the fuel being carried by compressed air into main stream of combustion-air -- F02M 69/08 classifies low-pressure fuel-injection devices in which the fuel is conveyed by compressed air and injected into the main stream of combustion-air, covering systems that mix fuel and air prior to ignition in combustion engines.
[F02M 69/10] peculiar to scavenged two-stroke engines, e.g. injecting into crankcase-pump chamber -- F02M 69/10 covers low-pressure fuel injection systems that are specially adapted for scavenged two-stroke engines, where fuel is injected into the crankcase-pump chamber rather than directly into the combustion cylinder. The classification addresses designs that supply a combustible mixture to the engine via the crankcase, typical of small marine or outboard power units.
[F02M 69/12] comprising a fuel-displaced free-piston for intermittently metering and supplying fuel to injection nozzles -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - High-pressure fuel-injection with fuel-displaced auxiliary pistons -> F02M59/32
[F02M 69/125] Means for varying the stroke of the free-piston -- The classification covers devices that can change the travel length of a free-piston in a combustion engine, allowing the engine's output power or efficiency to be adjusted by varying the piston stroke. Such mechanisms may use adjustable stops, hydraulic cushions or electronic controls to modify the piston's range of motion.
[F02M 69/14] having cyclically-operated valves connecting injection nozzles to a source of fuel under pressure during the injection period -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - High-pressure fuel injection apparatus -> F02M63/0003
[F02M 69/142] the valves being operated by fluid impulses, e.g. using bistable fluid operated valves -- F02M 69/142 covers low-pressure fuel-injection systems where the injection valves are actuated by fluid pressure pulses instead of mechanical or electrical actuation, including bistable fluid-operated valves that stay in one position until a fluid impulse switches them.
[F02M 69/145] the valves being actuated electrically  electrically-operated injectors  F02M51/06 -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Electrically-operated injectors -> F02M51/06
[F02M 69/147] the valves being actuated mechanically, e.g. rotating -- F02M 69/147 covers low-pressure fuel-injection systems in which the fuel-delivery valves are driven by mechanical means, such as rotating actuation. The classification is used for devices that supply combustible mixtures to combustion engines by physically moving the valve rather than using hydraulic or electronic control. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and general supplying of engines with fuel mixtures.
[F02M 69/16] characterised by means for metering continuous fuel flow to injectors or means for varying fuel pressure upstream of injectors -- F02M 69/16 covers low-pressure fuel-injection systems for supplying combustion engines that incorporate devices for continuously metering fuel to the injectors or for adjusting the fuel pressure before the injectors, enabling precise control of the fuel supply.
[F02M 69/18] the means being metering valves throttling fuel passages to injectors or by-pass valves throttling overflow passages, the metering valves being actuated by a device responsive to the engine working parameters, e.g. engine load, speed, temperature or quantity of air -- F02M 69/18 covers systems that control fuel flow to combustion-engine injectors by using metering or by-pass throttling valves whose opening is modulated by a device that senses engine parameters such as load, speed, temperature or intake-air amount, thereby optimizing fuel delivery.
[F02M 69/20] the device being a servo-motor, e.g. using engine intake air pressure or vacuum -- F02M 69/20 relates to low-pressure fuel-injection systems where the injection device is driven as a servo-motor, typically powered by engine intake-air pressure or a vacuum source, to control fuel delivery to the combustion chamber.
[F02M 69/22] the device comprising a member movably mounted in the air intake conduit and displaced according to the quantity of air admitted to the engine -- A low-pressure fuel-injection apparatus for combustion engines that includes a member mounted so it can move within the air-intake conduit, shifting position in response to the amount of air entering the engine to help regulate the fuel-air mixture.
[F02M 69/24] the device comprising a member for transmitting the movement of the air throttle valve actuated by the operator to the valves controlling fuel passages -- The F02M 69/24 entry covers a low-pressure fuel-injection system in which a component mechanically links the operator-controlled air throttle valve to the fuel-passage control valves, transmitting the throttle's motion to regulate fuel flow in combustion engines. This arrangement ensures that changes in throttle position directly adjust the opening of fuel valves, providing coordinated air-fuel delivery.
[F02M 69/26] the means varying fuel pressure in a fuel by-pass passage, the pressure acting on a throttle valve against the action of metered or throttled fuel pressure for variably throttling fuel flow to injection nozzles, e.g. to keep constant the pressure differential at the metering valve -- F02M 69/26 covers low-pressure fuel-injection systems that include a mechanism for varying fuel pressure in a bypass passage. The pressure acts on a throttle valve opposite the metered fuel pressure, allowing variable throttling of flow to the injection nozzles, for example to maintain a constant pressure differential across the metering valve.
[F02M 69/28] characterised by means for cutting-out the fuel supply to the engine or to main injectors during certain operating periods, e.g. deceleration -- F02M 69/28 covers low-pressure fuel-injection devices that include a means to cut off the fuel flow to the engine or its main injectors during specific operating intervals, such as when the vehicle is decelerating, to improve control and efficiency.
[F02M 69/30] characterised by means for facilitating the starting-up or idling of engines or by means for enriching fuel charge, e.g. below operational temperatures or upon high power demand of engines -- F02M 69/30 relates to low-pressure fuel-injection devices that include means for helping start-up or idling of engines, or for enriching the fuel charge when the engine is below operational temperature or under high power demand. It is situated under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 69/32] with an air by-pass around the air throttle valve or with an auxiliary air passage, e.g. with a variably controlled valve therein -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Constructional details of a throttle valve housing having a fluid by-pass -> F02D9/1055
[F02M 69/325] with an auxiliary injection nozzle therein  F02M69/34  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass (F02M 69/325) relates to low-pressure fuel-injection apparatus for combustion engines that includes an auxiliary injection nozzle inside the main injector, providing additional fuel-spray staging or improved atomisation. It is situated under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → supplying combustion engines with fuel mixtures.
[F02M 69/34] with an auxiliary fuel circuit supplying fuel to the engine, e.g. with the fuel pump outlet being directly connected to injection nozzles -- F02M 69/34 covers low-pressure fuel-injection devices that incorporate an auxiliary fuel circuit delivering fuel directly from the pump outlet to the injection nozzles. The feature is used in systems that supply combustion engines with combustible mixtures, simplifying the feed path and enhancing control of fuel delivery.
[F02M 69/36] having an enrichment mechanism modifying fuel flow to injectors, e.g. by acting on the fuel metering device or on the valves throttling fuel passages to injection nozzles or overflow passages -- This CPC subgroup concerns low-pressure fuel-injection apparatus that incorporates an enrichment mechanism to alter the amount of fuel delivered to the injectors. The mechanism may act on the fuel-metering device or on throttling valves that regulate fuel passages to injection nozzles or overflow passages.
[F02M 69/38] using fuel pressure, e.g. by varying fuel pressure in the control chambers of the fuel metering device -- F02M 69/38 defines methods that regulate low-pressure fuel-injection systems by varying the fuel pressure in the control chambers of the metering device, thereby influencing the amount of combustible mixture supplied to a combustion engine. It is used for precise control of fuel delivery in engines.
[F02M 69/383] the fuel passing through different passages to injectors or to a drain, the pressure of fuel acting on valves to close or open selectively these passages -- F02M 69/383 covers low-pressure fuel-injection systems in which fuel moves through a network of passages leading to injectors or a drain, and where fuel pressure is used to actuate valves that selectively open or close those passages. This subclass falls under supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 69/386] variably controlling the pressure of the fuel by-passing the metering valves, e.g. by valves responsive to signals of temperature or oxygen sensors -- This classification covers low-pressure fuel-injection systems that vary fuel pressure by routing flow around the metering valves, using control signals such as temperature or oxygen sensor outputs to adjust the pressure for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 69/40] using variably controlled air pressure, e.g. by modifying the intake air vacuum signal acting on the fuel metering device -- F02M 69/40 covers low-pressure fuel-injection systems for combustion engines in which the fuel metering is influenced by a variably controlled air-pressure signal, such as adjusting the intake-air vacuum to modulate the amount of fuel delivered. The classification addresses techniques that link changes in intake-air conditions directly to fuel-supply regulation.
[F02M 69/42] using other means than variable fluid pressure, e.g. acting on the fuel metering device mechanically or electrically -- F02M 69/42 covers low-pressure fuel-injection systems for combustion engines that meter fuel without varying fluid pressure, using mechanical linkages or electrical actuators to control the metering device. It belongs to the class for supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 69/44] characterised by means for supplying extra fuel to the engine on sudden air throttle opening, e.g. at acceleration -- F02M 69/44 covers low-pressure fuel-injection devices that automatically add extra fuel when the airflow throttle opens suddenly, for example during acceleration. The system detects rapid throttle movement and supplies additional combustible mixture to maintain engine performance and reduce hesitation, fitting within the broader class of mechanisms for supplying fuel to combustion engines.
[F02M 69/46] Details, component parts or accessories not provided for in, or of interest apart from, the apparatus covered by groups -- F02M 69/46 classifies ancillary details, component parts or accessories for low-pressure fuel-injection apparatus that are not otherwise covered in the main groups. It is used for peripheral items related to the supply of combustion engines with fuel mixtures, falling under mechanical engineering and combustion-engine categories.
[F02M 69/462] Arrangement of fuel conduits, e.g. with valves for maintaining pressure in the pipes after the engine being shut-down -- This CPC subclass deals with low-pressure fuel-injection systems for combustion engines, specifically the layout of fuel lines and any valves used to retain pressure in the conduits after the engine is stopped. It covers arrangements that prevent pressure loss and ensure ready restart.
[F02M 69/465] of fuel rails -- F02M 69/465 designates fuel rails within low-pressure fuel-injection systems that supply combustible mixture to combustion engines. The classification includes the design, arrangement and features of the rail that distributes fuel from the tank to the injectors, typically operating at pressures below the high-pressure injection stage.
[F02M 69/467] Devices using intake air for generating a control signal acting on fuel delivery  F02M69/125 ,  F02M69/20 ,  F02M69/40  take precedence -- The CPC subclass F02M 69/467 covers low-pressure fuel-injection systems that derive a control signal from intake-air parameters to regulate fuel delivery to combustion engines. It includes devices that sense air flow, pressure or temperature and use that information to modulate the injector's timing or quantity, superseding the broader subclasses F02M 69/125, 69/20 and 69/40.
[F02M 69/48] Arrangement of air sensors -- F02M 69/48 covers the positioning and integration of air-flow or oxygen sensors within low-pressure fuel-injection systems that supply combustion engines with fuel mixtures. The classification addresses how sensors are arranged to monitor inlet air characteristics for optimal mixture preparation and engine performance.
[F02M 69/50] Arrangement of fuel distributors -- F02M 69/50 covers the arrangement of fuel distributors in low-pressure fuel-injection systems that supply combustible mixtures to combustion engines. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy, specifically the branch dealing with general fuel supply to engines. The code addresses the spatial and functional layout of distributor components.
[F02M 69/52] Arrangement of fuel metering devices -- F02M 69/52 relates to the spatial arrangement and integration of fuel-metering devices in low-pressure fuel-injection systems for combustion engines, covering how injectors, pumps, regulators and associated passages are positioned and connected to optimise fuel delivery and engine performance.
[F02M 69/54] Arrangement of fuel pressure regulators -- F02M 69/54 covers the layout and interconnection of fuel pressure regulators in low-pressure fuel-injection systems for combustion engines. It addresses how regulators are positioned and linked to supply the engine with the proper fuel mixture.
[F02M 7/00] Carburettors with means for influencing, e.g. enriching or keeping constant, fuel/air ratio of charge under varying conditions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Under all engine operating conditions, the carburettor must measure the airflow of the engine, deliver the correct amount of fuel to keep the air/fuel mixture in the proper range and distribute the two finely and evenly. This job would be simple if air and gasoline were ideal fluids; in practice, however, their deviations from ideal behaviour due to viscosity, fluid drag, inertia, etc. require a great deal of complexity to compensate for exceptionally high or low engine speeds. A carburettor must provide the proper air/fuel mixture across a wide range of ambient temperatures, atmospheric pressures, engine speeds and loads and centrifugal forces, like cold start, hot start, idling, acceleration, high speed at full throttle (high load), slow speed at part throttle (light load).  To function correctly under all these conditions, carburettors contain a complex set of mechanisms to support several different operating modes.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Carburettors with means for facilitating engine's starting or its idling below operational temperatures -> F02M1/00 - Idling devices -> F02M3/00  **Glossary:** - Aerated fuel spray nozzles: Nozzle with air and fuel dispersion - Equaliser jets: Jets for equalising the air/fuel ratio
[F02M 7/02] Carburettors having aerated fuel spray nozzles -- F02M 7/02 relates to carburettors that incorporate aerated fuel-spray nozzles, mixing fuel with air before it reaches the engine to improve atomisation and allow the fuel-air ratio to be adjusted under varying operating conditions. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the supply of such engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 7/025] Fuel cut-off by introducing brake air in the conduit system leading to the main jet  fuel cut-off by introducing brake air into the idling fuel system  F02M3/055 -- F02M 7/025 covers carburettor arrangements that achieve fuel cut-off by injecting brake air into the conduit feeding the main jet, particularly in the idling fuel system. The air stream reduces or interrupts fuel delivery, allowing the engine to maintain a stable fuel/air ratio under varying conditions such as deceleration or shutdown. This technique is used to control combustion and improve idle stability.
[F02M 7/04] Means for enriching charge at high combustion-air flow -- F02M 7/04 pertains to carburettor devices that add fuel to the air-fuel mixture when the engine is supplied with a high volume of combustion air, using enrichment valves, jets or other control mechanisms to keep the mixture appropriate for combustion.
[F02M 7/045] Mechanical adjustment of the spray nozzle cross-section in connection with the choke -- F02M 7/045 covers carburettors that mechanically adjust the spray-nozzle cross-section in conjunction with a choke to regulate the fuel-air mixture supplied to combustion engines.
[F02M 7/06] Means for enriching charge on sudden throttle opening, i.e. at acceleration, e.g. storage means in passage way system -- F02M 7/06 relates to devices that enrich the fuel-air charge during a rapid throttle opening, such as acceleration, by providing temporary fuel storage or injection means in the carburettor passage to boost the mixture when conditions demand a richer charge.
[F02M 7/08] using pumps -- This subclass covers carburettors that incorporate pump devices to adjust the fuel-air mixture, enabling enrichment or a constant ratio despite changes in engine load or speed. It is situated in the hierarchy under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and the supply of combustible mixtures to engines.
[F02M 7/083] a pump sucking fuel from the conduit system leading to the spray nozzle downstream of the metering cross-section during deceleration -- F02M 7/083 covers a carburettor arrangement in which a pump draws fuel from the conduit that feeds the spray nozzle, positioned downstream of the metering cross-section, to supply fuel during engine deceleration and help maintain a stable air-fuel ratio.
[F02M 7/087] changing output according to temperature in engine -- F02M 7/087 covers carburettor systems that adjust the fuel-air mixture to compensate for temperature changes, thereby modifying engine output to maintain performance. The classification applies to devices that enrich or stabilize the charge under varying thermal conditions, ensuring consistent operation of combustion engines.
[F02M 7/093] changing output according to intake vacuum -- The F02M 7/093 subclass covers carburettors that modify the fuel-air mixture or engine output in response to changes in intake vacuum. It includes devices that enrich, lean or otherwise keep the mixture constant when operating conditions vary, allowing the engine's power to follow the vacuum level.
[F02M 7/10] Other installations, without moving parts, for influencing fuel/air ratio, e.g. electrical means -- This CPC subclass F02M 7/10 covers stationary installations that adjust the fuel-air mixture in carburettors, such as electronic control devices, without any moving parts. It includes devices that enrich or keep constant the mixture under varying operating conditions.
[F02M 71/00] Combinations of carburettors and low-pressure fuel-injection apparatus -- The CPC subclass F02M 71/00 covers arrangements that integrate a carburettor with a low-pressure fuel-injection system, enabling mixed fuel delivery to internal combustion engines. Such combinations aim to improve fuel atomisation, throttle response and emissions control.
[F02M 71/02] with fuel-air mixture being produced by the carburettor and being compressed by a pump for subsequent injection into main combustion-air -- This subclass covers systems in which the carburetor produces the fuel-air mixture, which is then compressed by a pump before being injected into the main combustion-air stream, typical of combined carburetor and low-pressure fuel-injection arrangements.
[F02M 7/103] with self-acting equaliser jets -- This subclass covers carburettors that include self-acting equaliser jets which automatically adjust the fuel-air mixture to maintain a constant ratio despite changes in operating conditions. The equaliser jets respond to pressure or flow variations to enrich or lean the charge without manual intervention.
[F02M 71/04] with carburettor being used at starting or idling only and injection apparatus being used during normal operation of engine -- The code F02M 71/04 covers combustion-engine fuel-supply systems that combine a carburetor and a low-pressure injection device. It specifies that the carburetor is used only for starting or idling, while the injection apparatus provides fuel during normal engine operation.
[F02M 7/106] Fluid amplifier as a device for influencing the fuel-air mixture -- The CPC entry F02M 7/106 covers fluid-amplifier devices employed in carburettors to modify the fuel-air mixture, enabling enrichment or stabilization of the charge as operating conditions change. These amplifiers use a fluid-dynamic principle to vary the ratio of fuel to air supplied to combustion engines, fitting within the broader category of supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 7/11] Altering float-chamber pressure -- F02M 7/11 covers devices that adjust the pressure in the carburettor's float-chamber to control the fuel-air mixture, typically by varying venturi size, valve settings or pressure-regulating mechanisms, enabling the engine to maintain optimal performance under changing operating conditions.
[F02M 7/12] Other installations, with moving parts, for influencing fuel/air ratio, e.g. having valves -- This subclass covers installations with moving parts that adjust the fuel-air mixture in combustion engines, such as valves that enrich or keep the ratio constant under varying conditions. It includes carburettors and similar devices designed to control the charge composition.
[F02M 7/127] Altering the float-chamber pressure -- This subclass covers carburetor designs that modify the float-chamber pressure to control the fuel-air mixture, enabling enrichment or maintaining a constant ratio under varying operating conditions. It is classified under CPC code F02M 7/127 within mechanical engineering, combustion engines and supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 7/133] Auxiliary jets, i.e. operating only under certain conditions, e.g. full power -- F02M 7/133 covers auxiliary jets in carburettors that are only activated under specific operating conditions, such as full-power demand, to modify the fuel-air mixture. These jets provide a supplemental flow of fuel or air, enriching or stabilising the charge when the engine load or speed changes, thereby improving performance and emissions control.
[F02M 7/14] with means for controlling cross-sectional area of fuel spray nozzle -- This subclass concerns carburettors that incorporate devices for adjusting the cross-sectional area of the fuel spray nozzle, allowing the fuel-air mixture to be regulated under varying operating conditions.
[F02M 7/16] operated automatically, e.g. dependent on exhaust-gas analysis -- This subclass covers carburettors equipped with mechanisms that automatically adjust the fuel-air ratio of the charge, for example by responding to exhaust-gas analysis, to maintain optimal combustion under varying operating conditions. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, supplying engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 7/17] by a pneumatically adjustable piston-like element, e.g. constant depression carburettors -- This CPC subclass covers carburettors that regulate the fuel-air mixture using a piston-like element whose position is varied by pneumatic pressure, such as constant-depression carburettors. The design enables enrichment or stabilization of the mixture under changing operating conditions.
[F02M 7/18] with means for controlling cross-sectional area of fuel-metering orifice -- The entry F02M 7/18 covers carburettors that incorporate mechanisms to vary the cross-sectional area of the fuel-metering orifice, thereby adjusting the fuel-air mixture in response to operating conditions. It is classified under mechanical engineering, specifically the supply of combustion engines with combustible mixtures, and pertains to devices that can enrich or stabilize the charge by controlling the orifice size.
[F02M 7/20] operated automatically, e.g. dependent on altitude -- This CPC subclass concerns carburettors that automatically adjust the fuel-air mixture in response to operating conditions such as altitude, using mechanisms that vary the ratio to keep engine performance optimal without manual control. It falls under the supply of combustion engines with combustible mixtures and includes devices that maintain a constant or enriched mixture as required.
[F02M 7/22] fuel flow cross-sectional area being controlled dependent on air-throttle-valve position -- This CPC subclass F02M 7/22 covers carburettors in which the fuel flow cross-sectional area is adjusted depending on the position of the air-throttle valve, enabling the fuel-air mixture to be enriched or kept constant under varying operating conditions.
[F02M 7/225] The fuel orifice opening is controlled by a manually actuatable throttle valve so as to vary the cross-sectional area of the orifice -- A carburettor in which the fuel-orifice opening is varied by a manually operated throttle valve that changes the cross-sectional area of the orifice, allowing the fuel-air mixture to be enriched or kept constant under different operating conditions.
[F02M 7/23] Fuel aerating devices -- F02M 7/23 covers fuel-aerating devices used in carburettors to modify the fuel-air mixture, such as systems that enrich or stabilise the charge when operating conditions change. The classification applies to mechanisms that intentionally introduce air or control fuel flow to maintain a constant or optimal mixture for combustion engines.
[F02M 7/24] Controlling flow of aerating air -- This subclass covers devices and methods that regulate the amount of aerating air delivered to a carburetor to maintain or adjust the fuel-air ratio as operating conditions change. It includes valves, passages, sensors or control mechanisms that modulate airflow for stable combustion in gasoline or similar engines.
[F02M 7/245] Accessories, e.g. sieves, accelerating wheels, whirl generating devices and the like, for the intimate mixing of emulsifying air with fuel -- F02M 7/245 covers accessories such as sieves, accelerating wheels and whirl-generating devices that promote intimate mixing of air and fuel in carburettors, helping to control the fuel-air ratio under varying operating conditions.
[F02M 7/26] dependent on position of optionally operable throttle means -- F02M 7/26 is a CPC subclass for carburettors that include a throttle device whose position can be optionally operated to adjust or maintain the fuel-air mixture. The classification covers designs where the throttle's position directly influences the charge under varying operating conditions, situated within the broader group for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 7/28] dependent on temperature or pressure -- This subclass covers carburettors that adjust the fuel-air mixture in response to changes in temperature or pressure, ensuring a constant ratio under varying operating conditions. It falls under supplying combustion engines in general with combustible mixtures within mechanical engineering.
[F02M 9/00] Carburettors having air or fuel-air mixture passage throttling valves other than of butterfly type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  A carburettor basically consists of an open pipe through which the air passes into the inlet manifold of the engine. The pipe is in the form of a venture, which narrows in section and then widens again, causing the airflow to increase in speed in the narrowest part. Below the venturi is a valve called the throttle valve. This valve controls the flow of air through the carburettor throat and thus the quantity of air/fuel mixture the system will deliver, thereby regulating engine power and speed. The throttle valve is connected through a mechanical linkage of rods and joints to the accelerator pedal.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Register-type carburettors -> F02M11/00  **Glossary:** - Iris diaphragms: Series of metal plates that can fold in over an opening on each other
[F02M 9/02] having throttling valves, e.g. of piston shape, slidably arranged transversely to the passage
[F02M 9/023] General constructional elements -- This CPC subclass covers the overall structural components of carburettors that employ throttling valves other than the usual butterfly type for controlling the air-fuel mixture. It relates to the design features common to such carburettors used in supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 9/026] with plate-like throttle valve -- The CPC subclass F02M 9/026 covers carburettors in which the air-fuel mixture flow is controlled by a flat, plate-shaped throttle valve rather than a rotating butterfly, describing the throttle mechanism used for regulating supply to combustion engines.
[F02M 9/04] with throttling valves sliding in a plane inclined to the passage -- The CPC subclass F02M 9/04 covers carburettors in which the throttling valve moves along a plane that is inclined relative to the air- or fuel-air passage, rather than rotating about a shaft as in a butterfly valve. This arrangement allows the valve to open or close by sliding, providing a different flow-control geometry for regulating the combustible mixture supplied to the engine.
[F02M 9/06] with means for varying cross-sectional area of fuel spray nozzle dependent on throttle position -- This CPC subclass concerns carburettors that use throttling devices other than butterfly valves, where the fuel-spray nozzle's cross-sectional area is adjusted in proportion to the throttle setting. The mechanism links throttle position to nozzle geometry to control the air-fuel mixture delivered to combustion engines.
[F02M 9/065] Automatically and not automatically controlled throttle valves operating mutually -- This CPC subclass deals with carburettor throttle valves other than butterfly types that can be coordinated, either by automatic control or by manual adjustment, to regulate the air-fuel mixture supplied to a combustion engine. It falls under supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures, focusing on mutually operating throttling mechanisms.
[F02M 9/08] having throttling valves rotatably mounted in the passage -- Carburettors that control the air-fuel mixture by using a throttling valve that is rotatably mounted within the passage, rather than a conventional butterfly valve. The rotating valve adjusts flow cross-section to vary mixture composition during engine operation. This arrangement is classified under CPC code F02M 9/08 for supply systems of combustion engines.
[F02M 9/085] Fuel spray nozzles in the throttling valves -- F02M 9/085 covers fuel-spray nozzles that are integrated into throttling valves of carburettors, excluding butterfly-type valves, for regulating the air-fuel mixture supplied to combustion engines. The classification relates to devices that atomise fuel within the valve passage to control mixture flow.
[F02M 9/10] having valves, or like controls, of elastic-wall type for controlling the passage, or for varying cross-sectional area, of fuel-air mixing chambers -- F02M 9/10 covers carburettors that employ elastic-wall or similarly flexible throttling elements to regulate flow or vary the cross-sectional area of the fuel-air mixing chamber, instead of using rigid butterfly-type valves.
[F02M 9/103] Mechanical control -- F02M 9/103 covers mechanical control mechanisms for carburettors that use air-or fuel-air mixture throttling valves other than the conventional butterfly type, enabling precise regulation of the mixture supplied to combustion engines. This subclass falls under the broader category of supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures and deals specifically with non-butterfly throttle designs.
[F02M 9/106] Pneumatic or hydraulic control -- The subclass F02M 9/106 covers pneumatic or hydraulic control systems for carburettors that regulate the air-fuel mixture through throttling valves other than the conventional butterfly type, fitting within the broader group for supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 9/12] having other specific means for controlling the passage, or for varying cross-sectional area, of fuel-air mixing chambers
[F02M 9/121] Iris diaphragms -- F02M 9/121 covers iris-type diaphragms used as throttling valves in carburettors, where a flexible, radially expanding membrane opens or closes the air- or fuel-air passage to control mixture flow. These devices replace conventional butterfly valves in supplying combustion engines with the correct combustible mixture.
[F02M 9/122] consisting of lamellae or wires, e.g. a hyperboloid formed by twisting a wire cylinder -- F02M 9/122 relates to carburettor throttling valves that are built from lamellae or wire structures, for example a hyperboloid created by twisting a wire cylinder, and are used to control the passage of air or fuel-air mixture into a combustion engine. These devices fall under the broader category of supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures, excluding butterfly-type valves.
[F02M 9/123] Spiral springs -- F02M 9/123 covers the use of spiral-shaped springs as the biasing element in non-butterfly throttling valves of carburettors that feed combustion engines with air-fuel mixtures. The helical spring provides a compact, progressive restoring force to close or position the valve when fuel-delivery pressure drops.
[F02M 9/124] Throttle valves with an action corresponding to those in apparatus for re-atomising condensed fuel or homogenising fuel-air mixture  shape of throttle valves  F02M3/14 -- F02M 9/124 covers carburettor throttle valves that are not of the butterfly type, specifically those whose shape or action provides re-atomisation of condensed fuel or homogenisation of the fuel-air mixture. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the supply of engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 9/125] specially shaped throttle valves not otherwise covered in groups  F02M9/121  -  F02M9/124 -- A carburettor throttle valve that has a non-standard shape, distinct from the butterfly valves covered in earlier groups, is classified under F02M 9/125. This group captures specially contoured air or fuel-air mixture throttling devices used in supplying combustion engines with combustible mixtures. It applies to designs not covered by the more general groups F02M 9/121 through F02M 9/124.
[F02M 9/127] Axially movable throttle valves concentric with the axis of the mixture passage -- Axially movable throttle valves that are aligned with the axis of the air-fuel passage in a carburettor, used for regulating the mixture flow without employing a butterfly-type valve. This sub-category falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion engines and the supply of engines with combustible mixtures, focusing on non-butterfly throttling mechanisms.
[F02M 9/1275] Venturi-nozzle shaped type, e.g. a venturi nozzle being moved relative to a fixed mushroom-shaped body -- The CPC subclass F02M 9/1275 covers carburettors in which the throttling element is a venturi-shaped nozzle that moves relative to a fixed mushroom-shaped body, providing air-fuel mixture control without a butterfly valve. This falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the supply of engines with combustible mixtures.
[F02M 9/133] the throttle valves having mushroom-shaped bodies -- This subclass covers throttle valves used in carburettors that have a mushroom-shaped body rather than a butterfly disc. The valves control the passage of air or fuel-air mixture to the engine and are characterized by a circular disk with a raised central hub resembling a mushroom. They fall under CPC F02M 9/133 for non-butterfly throttling devices.
[F02M 9/14] having venturi and nozzle relatively displaceable essentially along the venture axis -- The F02M 9/14 subclass covers carburettors whose venturi and nozzle are mounted so they can be shifted relative to one another along the venturi axis, allowing the throttle valve to be positioned by moving the nozzle rather than using a conventional butterfly valve. This arrangement provides variable airflow or fuel-air mixture control by axial displacement of the venturi-nozzle assembly.
[F02M 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- F02M 99/00 includes any features related to the supply of combustible mixtures to combustion engines that are not captured by the specific groups of this subclass. It covers unconventional fuel delivery arrangements, auxiliary feeding systems, or novel mixing techniques that fall outside the defined categories.

[F02N 11/00] Starting of engines by means of electric motors -- F02N 11/00 covers methods and devices for initiating the operation of combustion engines using electric motors, including the design, integration and control of starter motor systems that engage the engine's crankshaft to bring it to running speed before fuel ignition. It addresses electric starter configurations, coupling mechanisms, and related power-supply arrangements specific to internal-combustion engine start-up.

[F02N 11/02] the motors having longitudinally-shiftable rotors -- F02N 11/02 covers electric starter motors for combustion engines in which the rotor can be shifted along its axis during operation, enabling a more compact design or variable gearing during start-up. The classification pertains to starters that use longitudinally-movable rotors to engage the engine crankshaft. This technology is used to improve starting efficiency and reduce space requirements in various internal-combustion engine applications.

[F02N 15/00] Other power-operated starting apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All remaining aspects of starting devices, especially the connection between the starting apparatus and the engine.

[F02N 15/006] Assembling or mounting of starting devices -- This subclass covers methods and arrangements for installing or attaching power-operated starters to combustion engines, including the positioning, fastening, and integration of the starter components with the engine. It pertains to mechanical engineering topics related to starting of combustion engines.
