[F02N 15/026] of the centrifugal type -- F02N 15/026 concerns power-operated starting devices for combustion engines that use a centrifugal mechanism. The subclass includes starters in which a motor accelerates a rotating mass that is then released to spin the engine, such as gear-type and inertia starters, and falls under the broader group of other power-operated starting apparatus.
[F02N 15/027] of the pawl type -- F02N 15/027 classifies pawl-type mechanisms employed in power-operated starting apparatus for combustion engines. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the subcategory of other power-operated starting devices used to engage and disengage the engine's drive train during start-up.
[F02N 15/028] of the jaw type -- F02N 15/028 classifies jaw-type power-operated starting devices used to initiate the operation of combustion engines. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the subgroup for other power-operated starting apparatus within the broader category of engine starting mechanisms.
[F02N 15/04] the gearing including disengaging toothed gears -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Disengaged gearing between engine and starting means, e.g. starter.
[F02N 15/043] the gearing including a speed reducer -- This subclass covers gear arrangements that incorporate a speed-reducer within power-operated starters for combustion engines. It relates to gear trains that lower the motor speed to provide the higher torque needed to turn the engine during starting.
[F02N 15/046] of the planetary type -- This class covers planetary-type power-operated starters used to start combustion engines. It includes devices that employ a planetary gear train to transmit motor torque to the crankshaft, falling under other power-operated starting apparatus in mechanical engineering.
[F02N 15/06] the toothed gears being moved by axial displacement -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  Figure 1a illustrates a control lever (37) pushing pinion (31) and moving it axially outwards.  1b.  Figure 1b illustrates the pinion (31) of figure 1a with gear teeth (42).
[F02N 15/062] Starter drives -- F02N 15/062 relates to power-operated starter drives that transmit torque from a starter motor to a combustion engine, encompassing gear, belt, hydraulic or pneumatic mechanisms that engage the engine for initial rotation. The subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Starting of Combustion Engines → Other power-operated starting apparatus.
[F02N 15/063] with resilient shock absorbers -- A starting device for a combustion engine that incorporates resilient shock-absorbing components to dampen the impact and torque when the engine is engaged. The shock absorbers protect the starter mechanism and reduce stress on the engine during the initiation of operation. This falls under other power-operated starting apparatus in the mechanical-engineering hierarchy.
[F02N 15/065] with blocking means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Starter drives with blocking means which prevent the retracting movement of the starter drive back into the disengaged position or which maintain the pinion in the engaged position. The blocking means may not necessarily include an electro-magnetic relay.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a starter assembly comprising a regulation member (510, 511) used to move a pinion gear (210) of a pinion assembly (200) into engagement with a ring gear (100) used to start an engine, while blocking backward movement of the pinion assembly (200) via the regulation member (510, 511).
[F02N 15/066] the starter being of the coaxial type -- The code F02N 15/066 relates to power-operated starters for combustion engines in which the motor and driven gear are mounted on a common axis, i.e., a coaxial starter. Such devices are integrated with the engine's crankshaft or flywheel and belong to the "other power-operated starting apparatus" subgroup.
[F02N 15/067] the starter comprising an electro-magnetically actuated lever -- F02N 15/067 defines a starter for combustion engines where an electromagnet drives a lever to engage the engine, providing a power-operated starting device that uses magnetic actuation instead of purely mechanical or hydraulic means.
[F02N 15/068] starter drive being actuated by muscular force -- A starter drive for a combustion engine that is operated by human muscular effort, typically a hand- or foot-crank mechanism that directly engages the engine's flywheel or gear train to initiate rotation. It is used where electric or pneumatic starters are unavailable or unsuitable.
[F02N 15/08] the gearing being of friction type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Friction-type connections between starter and combustion engine, e.g. starters with pulley-belt connection (for instance belt-integrated starter-generators).  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[F02N 15/10] Safety devices not otherwise provided for -- This subgroup includes safety mechanisms integrated in power-operated starters that are not classified elsewhere, such as overload protection, anti-reverse lockout, or emergency-stop devices specific to engine start systems. They are intended to prevent injury or damage during the starting process.
[F02N 19/00] Starting aids for combustion engines, not otherwise provided for -- The CPC subclass F02N 19/00 covers auxiliary devices and methods that help initiate the operation of combustion engines but are not covered by more specific starting-aid categories. It includes systems such as electric, pneumatic or hydraulic starters and related control mechanisms that provide the initial torque or fuel-air mixture needed to fire the engine. This entry falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Starting of Combustion Engines.
[F02N 19/001] Arrangements thereof -- F02N 19/001 classifies the structural and functional arrangements of starting aids for combustion engines that are not specifically covered by other subclasses. This includes the layout, mounting and integration of devices such as starters, ignition systems or auxiliary mechanisms used to initiate engine operation.
[F02N 19/004] Aiding engine start by using decompression means or variable valve actuation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for reduction of the necessary torque for starting using decompression valves or variable intake or exhaust valve actuation
[F02N 19/005] Aiding engine start by starting from a predetermined position, e.g. pre-positioning or reverse rotation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for reducing torque needed to start the engine taking into account engine crank angle, e.g. by moving the engine to a certain position the necessary torque can be reduced.
[F02N 19/02] Aiding engine start by thermal means, e.g. using lighted wicks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Heating the engine or its components prior to engine start.
[F02N 19/04] by heating of fluids used in engines -- F02N 19/04 covers starting aids for combustion engines that work by heating the fluids used in the engine. It includes devices such as electric heaters, glow plugs, or external heaters that raise the temperature of fuel, oil or coolant to improve ignition and reduce cranking effort.
[F02N 19/06] by heating of combustion-air by flame generating means, e.g. flame glow-plugs -- F02N 19/06 covers starting aids for combustion engines that heat the incoming air with a flame, such as flame glow-plugs. The method raises the air temperature to improve ignition during engine start, and is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, starting of combustion engines, and starting aids not otherwise provided.
[F02N 19/08] Arrangement thereof -- F02N 19/08 concerns the arrangement of starting aids for combustion engines that are not otherwise specified. It addresses how devices such as starters, batteries, or auxiliary power units are positioned and integrated within the engine system to facilitate ignition.
[F02N 19/10] by heating of engine coolants -- F02N 19/10 covers devices that assist in starting combustion engines by heating the engine coolant to raise the temperature of the cylinder and improve ignition. This includes systems that use electric heaters, heat exchangers or exhaust-derived heat to pre-warm the coolant before start-up. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and starting aids not otherwise provided for.
[F02N 2011/0874] characterised by said switch being an electronic switch -- The CPC subgroup F02N 2011/0874 relates to control means for starting or stopping a turbine in which the switch employed is an electronic switch rather than a mechanical or electromechanical one. It defines turbine control arrangements that use solid-state or other electronic switching technology for initiating or terminating turbine operation.
[F02N 2011/0877] said switch being used as a series-parallel switch, e.g. to switch circuit elements from series to parallel connection -- This CPC group pertains to switches incorporated in rotating electrical machines that are used as series-parallel switches, enabling circuit elements such as windings or auxiliary circuits to be reconfigured from a series to a parallel connection, often for starting, speed control or efficiency optimization.
[F02N 2011/0881] Components of the circuit not provided for by previous groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[F02N 2011/0885] Capacitors, e.g. for additional power supply -- F02N 2011/0885 covers devices that store electrical energy in capacitors and are intended to provide auxiliary or backup power to an electrical system. The classification includes electrostatic, electrolytic and super-capacitor types used for supplemental supply, voltage smoothing or short-term energy delivery.
[F02N 2011/0888] DC/DC converters -- F02N 2011/0888 refers to DC/DC converters, which are static power-electronic devices that transform a direct-current input voltage to a different direct-current output voltage. The group includes buck, boost, buck-boost and isolated converters used in automotive, renewable-energy and portable-electronics applications.
[F02N 2011/0892] Two coils being used in the starting circuit, e.g. in two windings in the starting relay or two field windings in the starter -- This subclass covers motor or generator starter designs that employ a pair of coils in the initiating circuit, such as two windings within a starting relay or two field windings in the starter, to supply the required starting current or magnetic field.
[F02N 2011/0896] Inverters for electric machines, e.g. starter-generators -- The CPC subclass F02N 2011/0896 relates to power-electronic inverters that supply electric machines, particularly those used as starter-generators. It covers designs and control methods for converting DC to AC to drive the machine, including integrated circuitry, modulation techniques and protection functions for automotive and other applications.
[F02N 2015/061] said axial displacement being limited, e.g. by using a stopper -- This CPC entry covers pump constructions in which the axial movement of a component-such as a piston, plunger or shaft-is deliberately restrained, for example by a stopper, to limit travel, maintain clearances or prevent over-extension during operation.
[F02N 2019/002] Aiding engine start by acting on fuel -- The CPC subclass F02N 2019/002 covers inventions that facilitate the starting of internal combustion engines by modifying or treating the fuel prior to or during start-up. It includes methods or devices that enrich, heat, inject or otherwise condition fuel to improve ignition and reduce cranking effort.
[F02N 2019/007] using inertial reverse rotation -- The CPC subclass F02N 2019/007 concerns the use of an inertial reverse rotation principle in rotating machines. It covers systems in which the inertia of a rotating mass is employed to drive a reverse rotation without external power, such as in energy-recovery or startup assists for turbines, compressors and pumps. Applications include mechanisms that store kinetic energy during forward rotation and release it to generate a controlled reverse motion.
[F02N 2019/008] the engine being stopped in a particular position -- F02N 2019/008 relates to internal-combustion engine control techniques that keep the engine locked in a defined crankshaft or piston position after it has been stopped, allowing precise positioning for rapid restart, valve timing maintenance or safety checks. It covers mechanical, hydraulic or electronic means that hold the engine at that particular angular position during shutdown.
[F02N 2200/00] Indexing codes relating to engine starting associated with subclass -- F02N 2200/00 is a Cooperative Patent Classification code that groups indexing codes pertaining to the starting of combustion engines. It is used to classify patent literature describing mechanisms, control strategies, or auxiliary systems employed to initiate engine operation.
[F02N 2200/02] said parameters being related to the engine -- F02N 2200/02 concerns features in which parameters such as temperature, pressure, speed or other engine-related values are directly linked to the combustion engine during its starting phase, typically for control, monitoring or optimisation of the start-up process. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → starting of combustion engines.
[F02N 2200/021] Engine crank angle -- The F02N 2200/021 subclass covers aspects of engine crank-angle determination and control related to starting combustion engines. It includes devices, methods and sensors that measure, monitor or adjust the crank-shaft position during engine start-up, facilitating timing and synchronization of ignition and fuel injection.
[F02N 2200/022] Engine speed -- F02N 2200/022 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to engine speed, specifically the rotational speed of a combustion engine during its start-up phase. It covers methods, devices and control systems for measuring, regulating or optimizing the engine's rpm while the engine is being initiated. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Starting of Combustion Engines.
[F02N 2200/023] Engine temperature -- F02N 2200/023 is a CPC subclass under mechanical engineering, dealing with combustion engines and specifically the starting phase. It covers inventions that control or monitor engine temperature to facilitate or improve engine start-up, such as pre-heating or temperature-based control systems.
[F02N 2200/024] Engine oil temperature -- The subclass relates to systems that monitor or regulate the temperature of oil in the starting apparatus of combustion engines, encompassing sensors, heating or cooling devices, and associated control methods to ensure reliable engine start. It resides in the CPC hierarchy under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Starting of Combustion Engines.
[F02N 2200/025] Engine oil pressure -- F02N 2200/025 relates to engine oil pressure in the starting of internal combustion engines, covering monitoring, regulation and control of oil pressure during start-up to ensure proper lubrication before ignition.
[F02N 2200/026] Catalyst temperature -- The subclass F02N 2200/026 covers specifications for the temperature of catalytic devices used during the start-up of combustion engines. It includes methods for monitoring, controlling or adjusting catalyst temperature to ensure reliable ignition and reduce emissions in the initial power-up phase. This entry falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the starting of combustion engines.

It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Combustion Engines → Starting of Combustion Engines and covers technical parameters that are specifically related to the starter motor used to initiate engine operation.

It includes specifications, control methods, and performance criteria for starter rotational velocity during engine start-up. This entry falls under the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering > Combustion Engines > Starting of Combustion Engines.
[F02N 2200/042] Starter torque -- The entry F02N 2200/042 defines the torque that a starter must deliver to initiate the rotation of a combustion engine. It encompasses the specification, measurement and limits of starter torque for various engine types and operating conditions, linking the starting process to performance requirements within the broader category of mechanical engineering and combustion-engine starting.
[F02N 2200/043] Starter voltage -- The subclass F02N 2200/043, titled Starter voltage, covers the electrical voltage characteristics and requirements for the starter system of internal combustion engines. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the starting of combustion engines, and is used to index patents that describe voltage levels, supply sources, and control methods for engine starters.
[F02N 2200/044] Starter current -- F02N 2200/044 covers the electrical current used by starter systems to crank combustion engines. It is applied in the context of mechanical engineering, specifically for starting of combustion engines, and includes indexing codes that relate to the power requirements of engine starters.
[F02N 2200/045] Starter temperature or parameters related to it -- The entry F02N 2200/045 covers technical information on the temperature of an engine starter and any related parameters, such as heat generation, thermal limits, cooling or heating methods, and their effect on starter performance during the initiation of combustion engines.
[F02N 2200/046] Energy or power necessary for starting -- F02N 2200/046 concerns the energy or power required to start combustion engines. It includes technical solutions, calculations and sources of the starting energy, focusing on how much power is needed to initiate engine operation within the broader class of mechanical engineering and engine-starting methods.
[F02N 2200/047] Information about pinion position -- F02N 2200/047 covers information concerning the position of a pinion in systems used to start combustion engines. It relates to the detection, monitoring or signaling of pinion location during the engine-starting process, aiding control and safety functions.
[F02N 2200/048] Information about pinion speed, both translational or rotational speed -- The subclass F02N 2200/048 covers technical information concerning the speed of a pinion used in engine-starting systems, including both its translational and rotational velocities. It is part of the broader category for starting of combustion engines within mechanical engineering.
[F02N 2200/06] said parameters being related to the power supply or driving circuits for the starter -- F02N 2200/06 covers parameters that relate to the electrical power supply or driving circuits for a starter motor in combustion engines. It is situated in the CPC hierarchy Mechanical Engineering, Combustion Engines, Starting of Combustion Engines and is used to index patents concerning starter power and control electronics.
[F02N 2200/061] Battery state of charge [SOC] -- F02N 2200/061 designates technology for determining and managing the battery's state of charge in systems that start combustion engines, ensuring the battery has sufficient energy to operate the starter motor. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and engine-starting methods.
[F02N 2200/062] Battery current -- F02N 2200/062 covers the electrical current supplied by a battery for starting internal-combustion engines, including the magnitude, duration, and control of the current used to initiate engine ignition, as well as related battery performance characteristics.
[F02N 2200/063] Battery voltage -- The code F02N 2200/063 covers specifications of battery voltage used for starting combustion engines. It pertains to the electrical parameters of the starter battery that affect engine ignition performance, including nominal voltage ranges, voltage drop during cranking, and related control and monitoring features.
[F02N 2200/064] Battery temperature -- F02N 2200/064 covers provisions for measuring, controlling or conditioning the temperature of the battery used in starting a combustion engine. It addresses heating, cooling or thermal management methods that ensure the battery delivers adequate power for reliable engine start, especially in extreme ambient conditions.

[F02N 2200/0806] Air condition state -- This class covers inventions that monitor or control the condition of the intake air when starting a combustion engine, such as temperature, pressure, humidity, or mixture quality, to improve start reliability. [F02N 2200/104] Driver's intention to turn, e.g. by evaluating direction indicators -- The code F02N 2200/104 relates to systems that detect the driver's intention to turn, for example by monitoring the direction-indicator control, and use that information during the starting of a combustion engine. [F02N 2200/106] Driver presence, e.g. detected by door lock, seat sensor or belt sensor -- The CPC entry F02N 2200/106 covers methods and devices for confirming the presence of a driver before a combustion engine can be started, such as detecting whether a door is locked, a seat is occupied, or a seat-belt is fastened. This subclass is part of the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and engine starting, and relates to safety or control features that prevent engine start without a recognized driver.

[F02N 2200/122] Atmospheric temperature -- The entry F02N 2200/122 in the Cooperative Patent Classification addresses aspects of engine starting that are influenced by ambient atmospheric temperature. It covers devices, methods or control strategies that compensate for or exploit temperature conditions to improve the reliability or performance of starting a combustion engine.
[F02N 2200/123] Information about vehicle position, e.g. from navigation systems or GPS signals -- F02N 2200/123 covers the provision of vehicle-position data, such as from navigation or GPS systems, to the engine-starting control unit. [F02N 2200/14] said parameter being related to wear of starter or other components, e.g. based on total number of starts or age -- The code F02N 2200/14 covers parameters that relate to wear of a starter or other engine-starting components, such as the total number of starts or the age of the unit. [F02N 2250/00] Problems related to engine starting or engine's starting apparatus -- This subclass deals with technical problems affecting the initiation of combustion engines and the devices used to start them. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, starting of combustion engines.
[F02N 2250/04] Reverse rotation of the engine -- The code covers issues concerning an engine that runs in the opposite direction to its intended rotation, including methods to prevent, detect, or correct reverse rotation during or after starting. It addresses design features, control strategies, and failure modes that cause the crankshaft or other rotating components to turn backwards, which can impair performance or cause damage.
[F02N 2250/06] Engine stall and related control features, e.g. for automatic restart -- The F02N 2250/06 entry covers problems that arise when a combustion engine stalls during start-up and the control measures used to detect, prevent or automatically restart the engine. It includes systems such as automatic restart devices, stall detection circuits and related control strategies for ensuring reliable engine initiation.
[F02N 2250/08] Lubrication of starters -- The CPC entry F02N 2250/08 covers lubrication of engine starters, addressing methods, substances and designs that reduce friction, wear and heat in starters and related components. It falls within the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, combustion engines and the specific problems of starting those engines.
[F02N 2300/00] Control related aspects of engine starting -- F02N 2300/00 covers technologies and methods for controlling the starting of combustion engines, including systems that manage ignition timing, fuel delivery, and starter motor operation to ensure reliable engine start. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the subcategory of engine starting.
[F02N 2300/10] characterised by the control output, i.e. means or parameters used as a control output or target -- This subclass pertains to engine-starting devices whose control strategy is defined by the output signal or parameter used as the control target, such as a voltage, pressure or speed command that directs the starting operation.
[F02N 2300/102] Control of the starter motor speed -- The entry F02N 2300/102 concerns technologies that regulate the speed of a starter motor during the start-up of combustion engines, covering control strategies, feedback mechanisms and actuators that ensure appropriate torque and prevent overspeed while initiating engine operation.
[F02N 2300/104] Control of the starter motor torque -- The CPC class F02N 2300/104 covers methods and systems for controlling the torque delivered by a starter motor during the startup of combustion engines. It addresses control strategies, sensors and actuation techniques that adjust starter torque to improve starting performance and protect the engine.
[F02N 2300/106] Control of starter current -- The code F02N 2300/106 covers technologies that regulate the electrical current delivered to a combustion-engine starter. It includes methods and devices that monitor, limit or adapt starter power to ensure reliable engine ignition while protecting the battery and starter motor from overload.
[F02N 2300/108] Duty cycle control or pulse width modulation [PWM] -- F02N 2300/108 covers control of the duty cycle or pulse width modulation in starting systems for combustion engines, focusing on how the on/off timing of the starter is regulated to improve performance and reliability. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and the sub-category of engine starting.
[F02N 2300/20] characterised by the control method -- The F02N 2300/20 subclass covers combustion-engine starting systems where the control method itself defines the invention. [F02N 2300/2002] using different starting modes, methods, or actuators depending on circumstances, e.g. engine temperature or component wear -- The F02N 2300/2002 entry covers control strategies for starting combustion engines that select different start-up modes, methods or actuators based on operating conditions such as engine temperature, component wear or other situational factors. It pertains to the management of engine-starting processes within the broader mechanical engineering and combustion-engine classification.
[F02N 2300/2004] using adaptive control -- F02N 2300/2004 is a CPC subgroup in the mechanical-engineering field that deals with combustion-engine starting and the control aspects of that process, focusing on the use of adaptive control techniques to modify starter parameters in real time for reliable ignition.
[F02N 2300/2006] using prediction of future conditions -- This classification covers methods that predict upcoming operating conditions to manage the starting of combustion engines, enabling control systems to adjust parameters such as fuel delivery, ignition timing or starter torque in advance. It applies to technologies that use forecasting models or sensor data to improve starter performance and reliability.
[F02N 2300/2008] using a model -- F02N 2300/2008 covers the use of mathematical or physical models to control the starting process of combustion engines. The entry addresses model-based techniques for managing ignition, fuel delivery, and starter-motor operation.
[F02N 2300/2011] Control involving a delay -- The entry F02N 2300/2011 covers control functions for starting combustion engines that deliberately introduce a time delay between a command and the actuation of the starter system. It includes timed ignition control, delayed engagement of the starter motor or auxiliary devices, and any logic that schedules the start sequence to improve reliability or safety.

[F02N 9/02] the pressure fluid being generated directly by combustion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Starting of engines by generating pressure fluid directly by combustion, e.g. in an external combustion chamber, and supplying said pressure fluid to the working chambers of the engines.
