[F02N 9/04] the pressure fluid being generated otherwise, e.g. by compressing air -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[F02N 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in the other groups of this subclass -- F02N 99/00 is a residual group within the subclass for starting of combustion engines, covering any subject matter related to engine start-up that is not specifically addressed in the other groups of the subclass. It serves as a catch-all category for novel or unconventional starting-system features that fall outside the defined codes.
[F02N 99/002] Starting combustion engines by ignition means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  So-called direct start by injecting fuel into one engine cylinder and igniting it, can be without or combined with starter actuation.
[F02N 99/004] Generation of the ignition spark -- The subclass covers devices and methods that generate the spark used to ignite the air-fuel mixture in internal combustion engines during starting. It includes ignition coils, spark plugs and related control circuitry that are not classified elsewhere.
[F02N 99/006] Providing a combustible mixture inside the cylinder -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Starting whereby the fuel is injected directly into the combustion chamber and then ignited to achieve engine start.
[F02N 99/008] Providing a combustible mixture outside the cylinder -- F02N 99/008 covers methods of delivering a combustible fuel-air mixture to a combustion engine at a point external to the cylinder, such as pre-chambers, intake manifolds, or injection ports, used primarily for engine start-up. It includes any arrangement not classified elsewhere in the starting-engine subclass.
[F02P] IGNITION, OTHER THAN COMPRESSION IGNITION, FOR INTERNAL-COMBUSTION ENGINES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Systems and arrangements for causing ignition in internal combustion engines.  Details of electrically actuated spark ignition systems, this includes the generation and supply of the ignition energy to the spark plugs.  Testing of and feedback about the ignition.  Control of the engine by using parameters of the ignition, e.g. the ignition timing or the ignition strength.
[F02P 1/00] Electric spark ignition installations characterised by the type of ignition power generation or storage -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Installations wherein spark ignition is generated by a spark plug fed by a coil responsive to the field changes of a magnet fixed on a flywheel and rotating with it. This is used in most of small engines without battery, as well as on small piston airplanes.
[F02P 1/005] Construction and fastening of elements of magnetos other than the magnetic circuit and the windings  F02P1/02  -  F02P1/08  take precedence -- This subclass covers the design and fastening methods for magneto components other than the magnetic circuit and coil windings, such as housings, brackets and covers, in electric spark-ignition systems for internal-combustion engines. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines and non-compression-ignition systems, and is superseded by the more specific sub-groups F02P 1/02 to F02P 1/08.
[F02P 1/02] the generator rotor being characterised by forming part of the engine flywheel -- F02P 1/02 refers to electric spark-ignition installations in which the generator rotor is combined with the engine flywheel, so the rotating mass itself generates and stores ignition energy. This integration links the ignition power source directly to the flywheel component of the combustion engine.
[F02P 1/04] the generator being specially adapted for use with specific engine types, e.g. engines with V arrangement of cylinders -- Electric spark ignition installations in which the generator is specifically adapted to match particular engine configurations, such as V-type cylinder arrangements, fall under CPC code F02P 1/04. This subclass addresses power-generation or storage units designed for the unique spatial and performance requirements of those engines.
[F02P 1/06] Generator drives, e.g. having snap couplings -- The F02P 1/06 subgroup covers ignition systems in which a mechanically driven generator, often linked to the engine by snap-type couplings, produces the electrical energy for spark creation. It includes arrangements where the generator's drive is derived directly from engine motion rather than from batteries or external power sources.
[F02P 1/08] Layout of circuits -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Circuits specially adapted to ignition circuits without batteries, e.g. the arrangement of capacitors, coils, resistors, semiconductors. coils.
[F02P 1/083] for generating sparks by opening or closing a coil circuit -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Circuits wherein the current which has been generated after a change of magnetic fields is transformed in (high) voltage by opening (here mechanically) the coil-plug circuit.
[F02P 1/086] for generating sparks by discharging a capacitor into a coil circuit -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Circuits with switching capacitors to increase the voltage created by opening the coil-plug circuit.
[F02P 11/00] Safety means for electric spark ignition, not otherwise provided for -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for protecting the engine or associated parts using ignition elements.
[F02P 11/02] Preventing damage to engines or engine-driven gearing -- The F02P 11/02 subclass covers safety devices related to electric-spark ignition systems that are not otherwise specified, specifically those intended to protect an internal-combustion engine or its driven gearing from damage caused by ignition-related failures. It includes protective measures such as over-voltage suppression, spark-energy limiting, and fault-detecting switches.
[F02P 11/025] Shortening the ignition when the engine is stopped  to prevent damage to the coil  F02P3/0556 -- The code F02P 11/025 classifies safety measures for electric-spark ignition systems that are not otherwise covered, specifically methods that shorten or suppress the spark when the engine is stopped to avoid damage to the ignition coil. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and non-compression-ignition ignition safety.
[F02P 11/04] Preventing unauthorised use of engines -- F02P 11/04 covers safety measures that stop a spark-ignition engine from being started or running without proper authorization. It includes immobilisers, key-controlled shut-off circuits, electronic locks and similar devices that prevent theft or misuse of internal-combustion engines.
[F02P 11/06] Indicating unsafe conditions -- The F02P 11/06 subclass covers devices that detect and signal unsafe conditions in electric spark-ignition systems of internal-combustion engines, such as alarms, warning lights, or indicators that alert the operator to faults or hazardous states not otherwise addressed by the ignition hardware. It is used for safety means that warn of abnormal ignition performance or potential fire risk.
[F02P 13/00] Sparking plugs structurally combined with other parts of internal-combustion engines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combination of spark plugs with other elements of the engine, e.g. combination of a spark plug with a sensor.
[F02P 15/00] Electric spark ignition having characteristics not provided for in, or of interest apart from, groups -- F02P 15/00 designates electric spark-ignition arrangements for internal-combustion engines that do not fall within any of the more specific groups defined elsewhere in the CPC. It covers spark-ignition devices whose particular characteristics, such as novel electrode shapes, timing methods or energy-delivery schemes, are not otherwise classified.
[F02P 15/001] Ignition installations adapted to specific engine types  ignition of jet propulsion plants  F02K9/95 ; for rotary piston engines  F02B53/12 -- F02P 15/001 covers ignition installations that are specially designed for particular engine configurations, such as jet-propulsion plants (see F02K 9/95) or rotary piston engines (see F02B 53/12). The entry groups electric spark ignition systems whose features differ from those defined in the standard groups, addressing unique requirements of those engine types.
[F02P 15/003] Layout of ignition circuits for gas turbine plants  ignition of gas turbine plants  per se   F02C7/26 -- The entry covers the arrangement of electric-spark ignition circuits used in gas-turbine power plants, addressing designs and features that are not covered by other groups. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → non-compression ignition for internal-combustion engines, focusing on the specific layout of the ignition system for gas turbines.
[F02P 15/005] Layout of ignition circuits for rotary- or oscillating piston engines  ignition of those engines  per se   F02B53/12 -- This CPC subclass F02P 15/005 covers the arrangement of electric spark-ignition circuits specifically for rotary or oscillating piston internal-combustion engines. It deals with the physical layout of the wiring, distributor, coils and related components used to fire the engine, distinct from general ignition concepts in broader classes. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering - combustion engines - ignition other than compression ignition.
[F02P 15/006] Ignition installations combined with other systems, e.g. fuel injection  to advance or to retard the ignition spark  F02P5/045 -- F02P 15/006 refers to ignition installations for internal-combustion engines that are integrated with other systems, such as fuel-injection units, to control the timing of the electric spark. It covers arrangements that advance or retard the spark in coordination with these ancillary functions, offering features not covered by the standard ignition groups.
[F02P 15/008] Reserve ignition systems; Redundancy of some ignition devices -- F02P 15/008 classifies ignition arrangements that provide a backup or redundant spark source for internal-combustion engines, such as secondary spark plugs, parallel circuits or duplicate ignition modules, intended to ensure reliable starts or continued operation when the primary system fails. It covers designs where multiple ignition devices are incorporated for safety, performance or fail-safe purposes.
[F02P 15/02] Arrangements having two or more sparking plugs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Two or more spark plugs in one cylinder.
[F02P 15/04] one of the spark electrodes being mounted on the engine working piston -- This subclass covers internal combustion engines where a spark electrode is attached directly to the moving piston rather than being fixed in the cylinder head. It falls under electric spark ignition for non-compression-ignition engines and addresses designs in which the electrode moves with the working piston during operation.
[F02P 15/06] the electric spark triggered by engine working cylinder compression -- This subclass deals with systems where an electric spark is generated as a result of the compression of the working cylinder, rather than by a separate ignition coil, focusing on ignition methods that rely on compression-induced charges. It covers designs where the spark occurs due to pressure-induced phenomena within the cylinder, typically for spark-ignition engines that use compression-triggered discharge.
[F02P 15/08] having multiple-spark ignition, i.e. ignition occurring simultaneously at different places in one engine cylinder or in two or more separate engine cylinders -- F02P 15/08 covers internal combustion engines that use an electric spark ignition system producing multiple sparks simultaneously, either at different locations within a single cylinder or across two or more cylinders. This type of ignition is not covered by the standard groups and is of interest for its distinct timing and distribution characteristics.
[F02P 15/10] having continuous electric sparks -- A subclass of mechanical engineering for combustion-engine ignition, F02P 15/10 covers electric spark-ignition systems that generate a continuous spark rather than the intermittent sparks typical of conventional spark plugs. It applies to designs whose spark characteristics are not defined in existing groups or are of separate interest, such as continuous-spark coils or plasma-arc devices used to ignite the fuel-air mixture.
[F02P 15/12] having means for strengthening spark during starting -- F02P 15/12 covers electric spark-ignition systems for internal-combustion engines that incorporate features to boost spark intensity during engine start-up, such as additional voltage or energy-enhancing devices, distinguishing them from standard ignition arrangements.
[F02P 17/00] Testing of ignition installations, e.g. in combination with adjusting -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Testing and diagnosis of ignition installations in engines, e.g. for adjusting the ignition control or setup.
[F02P 17/02] Checking or adjusting ignition timing -- The class covers methods and devices for testing ignition installations on internal-combustion engines, specifically procedures for checking or adjusting the timing of the spark or other ignition event.
[F02P 17/04] dynamically
[F02P 17/06] using a stroboscopic lamp -- The classification F02P 17/06 covers methods that employ a stroboscopic lamp to observe and test ignition installations in non-compression-ignition internal-combustion engines. It is used to evaluate spark timing and performance during engine operation. This falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and ignition systems.
[F02P 17/08] using a cathode-ray oscilloscope -- The code designates techniques for testing ignition systems of non-compression-ignition engines by employing a cathode-ray oscilloscope to record and analyse spark voltage, timing and waveform characteristics. This allows precise adjustment and diagnostics of ignition installations.
[F02P 17/10] Measuring dwell or antidwell time -- F02P 17/10 covers methods and devices for measuring the dwell or antidwell time of ignition systems in non-compression-ignition engines, such as the period a spark plug is energized or the interval between spark events, typically used during testing or adjustment of ignition installations.
[F02P 17/12] Testing characteristics of the spark, ignition voltage or current -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Testing of ignition by evaluating the quality of the ignition using ionic current measurements or primary or secondary current evaluation.
[F02P 19/00] Other ignition -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ignition systems providing an incandescent heat source to induce or support ignition.
[F02P 19/02] electric, e.g. layout of circuits of apparatus having glowing plugs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Circuits and control means for glow plug, e.g. supply of current to the glow plugs, control of activation and deactivation and monitoring of the glow plugs.
[F02P 19/021] characterised by power delivery controls -- F02P 19/021 classifies ignition systems for internal-combustion engines that are not compression-ignition types and that incorporate controls for regulating the engine's power output, covering devices and methods that modulate spark timing, duration or energy to manage delivered power.
[F02P 19/022] using intermittent current supply -- F02P 19/022 relates to spark-ignition systems for internal-combustion engines and covers methods that use an intermittent (pulsed or timed) electric-current supply to generate the ignition energy. The classification includes any arrangement where the current is switched on and off to produce a spark or comparable ignition event for engines that do not employ compression ignition.
[F02P 19/023] Individual control of the glow plugs -- The classification F02P 19/023 covers technologies that allow each glow plug in a combustion engine to be controlled separately, enabling individual heating of cylinders for improved cold-start performance, fuel efficiency and emissions control. It includes electronic or mechanical systems that activate, modulate or deactivate glow plugs on a per-cylinder basis rather than using a single common control.
[F02P 19/025] with means for determining glow plug temperature or glow plug resistance -- This subclass covers ignition arrangements for internal-combustion engines that incorporate a device for measuring the temperature or electrical resistance of a glow plug, enabling real-time monitoring and control of the plug's heating state. It applies to systems where the diagnostic means are integral to the ignition circuit, such as diesel or other engines using glow-plug start-up.
[F02P 19/026] Glow plug actuation during engine operation -- F02P 19/026 relates to systems that activate a glow plug while the engine is running, providing controlled heating of the combustion chamber in non-compression-ignition engines to assist cold starts or enhance combustion stability. It is classified under other ignition methods within the broader area of mechanical engineering for combustion-engine ignition.
[F02P 19/027] Safety devices, e.g. for diagnosing the glow plugs or the related circuits -- The F02P 19/027 subclass covers safety and diagnostic devices that monitor or test glow-plug units and their control circuits in non-compression-ignition engines, detecting faults, verifying operation and protecting the ignition system from malfunctions. It includes built-in or external tools that assess plug heating, circuit integrity and related parameters to ensure reliable engine start-up.
[F02P 19/028] the glow plug being combined with or used as a sensor -- F02P 19/028 falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and other ignition for internal combustion engines. It pertains to glow plugs that are integrated with or function as sensors, providing feedback on temperature, combustion conditions or engine performance.
[F02P 19/04] non-electric, e.g. heating incandescent spots by burners -- This subclass covers ignition systems for internal-combustion engines that use non-electric means such as heating incandescent spots with flame burners, rather than spark plugs or compression ignition. It includes methods where a burner supplies thermal energy to ignite the fuel-air mixture.
[F02P 2017/003] using an inductive sensor, e.g. trigger tongs -- The entry covers the use of inductive sensors, exemplified by trigger tongs, to detect the presence or position of metallic objects without contact. It is classified under CPC F02P, which pertains to devices that employ inductive principles for measurement, control or safety functions in industrial and mechanical applications.
[F02P 2017/006] using a capacitive sensor -- F02P 2017/006 relates to the use of capacitive sensors in fluid-handling equipment such as pumps, compressors or ventilation systems for detecting level, position or presence of a material and providing feedback for control functions. The classification is applied when the sensor is employed to monitor or regulate the operation of the device.
[F02P 2017/121] by measuring spark voltage -- This sub-group of CPC F02P relates to internal combustion engines where the ignition system is regulated by detecting the voltage of the spark. It includes devices and methods that monitor spark voltage to adjust timing, fuel amount, or other parameters for optimal performance and emissions.
[F02P 2017/123] Generating additional sparks for diagnostics -- F02P 2017/123 covers methods that create extra electrical sparks during well-bore diagnostics to improve signal strength or detection of formation characteristics, often applied in hydraulic fracturing or monitoring operations. The techniques generate controlled additional discharges to enhance the reliability of measurement data.
[F02P 2017/125] Measuring ionisation of combustion gas, e.g. by using ignition circuits
[F02P 2017/126] for burners -- F02P 2017/126 is a CPC subgroup within the "Burners" class that deals with the design, construction, and operation of devices that mix fuel and combustion air and deliver the mixture to a flame-holding chamber. It covers various burner types, their control mechanisms, fuel-feed systems and adaptations for different fuels or applications, ranging from industrial furnaces to household heating.
[F02P 2017/128] for knock detection -- F02P 2017/128 covers technologies for detecting knock in internal-combustion engines, such as sensors, signal-processing methods, or control systems that identify abnormal combustion events and enable corrective engine management. The classification is used for patents focusing on knock-sensing and knock-mitigation techniques.
[F02P 21/00] Direct use of flames or burners for ignition -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ignition system that directly uses flames or burners to cause ignition in internal combustion engines, e.g. ignition using the glowing gaseous part of a fire.
[F02P 21/02] the flames being kept burning essentially external to engine working chambers -- F02P 21/02 covers systems where a flame or burner is maintained continuously outside the engine's working chambers to provide ignition for non-compression-ignition internal-combustion engines. The external flame serves as a ready source of spark or heat, eliminating the need for internal ignition devices.
[F02P 21/04] Burning-cartridges or like inserts being arranged in engine working chambers -- This subclass covers the use of combustible cartridges or similar inserts that are placed directly in the working chambers of internal-combustion engines to provide ignition. The devices burn to create a flame that initiates the fuel-air mixture rather than using spark or compression ignition.
[F02P 23/00] Other ignition -- F02P 23/00 - Other ignition covers ignition arrangements for internal-combustion engines that are not compression-ignition types, such as spark-plug, laser, plasma or other alternative ignition methods not classified elsewhere. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and non-compression ignition.
[F02P 23/02] Friction, pyrophoric, or catalytic ignition -- F02P 23/02 covers ignition methods for internal-combustion engines that rely on friction, pyrophoric materials, or catalytic reactions rather than compression. It includes devices or processes that generate a spark or flame by mechanical friction, spontaneously ignitable substances, or catalyst-induced combustion.
[F02P 23/04] Other physical ignition means, e.g. using laser rays -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ignition systems using coherent light generator or high frequency electromagnetic waves causing plasma ignition or corona discharge ignition.
[F02P 23/045] using electromagnetic microwaves -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ignition systems generating high frequency waves in the microwave range for causing ignition.
[F02P 3/00] Other installations -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ignition installations comprising a battery, a switching circuit and an ignition transformer.
[F02P 3/005] having inductive-capacitance energy storage  capacitive storage installations using an intermediate charging inductance  F02P3/0876 -- This CPC subclass covers ignition systems for internal-combustion engines that employ an inductive-capacitance energy-storage arrangement, using an intermediate charging inductance between the power source and a capacitive storage unit. The configuration delivers spark energy through the inductance, improving charging efficiency and control, and is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, non-compression ignition.
[F02P 3/01] Electric spark ignition installations without subsequent energy storage, i.e. energy supplied by an electrical oscillator -- The F02P 3/01 subclass covers electric spark-ignition systems used in internal-combustion engines where the ignition energy is delivered directly by an electrical oscillator and no subsequent energy-storage device, such as a capacitor, is employed; it applies to other installations beyond standard automotive engines.
[F02P 3/02] having inductive energy storage, e.g. arrangements of induction coils -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Circuits wherein a current is drawn into a coil storing magnetic energy and wherein the current is subsequently interrupted and the magnetic energy is discharged between the electrodes of a spark plug. In this group are mostly found:
[F02P 3/04] Layout of circuits -- F02P 3/04 concerns the physical arrangement and routing of the electrical circuits that deliver spark to non-compression-ignition internal-combustion engines, covering the placement of wires, connectors, harnesses and related components within the engine compartment to ensure reliable ignition performance.
[F02P 3/0407] Opening or closing the primary coil circuit with electronic switching means  F02P3/045  -  F02P3/055  take precedence -- The entry concerns electronic switching of the primary coil circuit in ignition systems for spark-ignition internal-combustion engines. It covers methods and devices that open or close the coil circuit by means such as transistors, SCRs or other solid-state switches, rather than mechanical contacts. This falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and non-compression ignition, and supersedes the broader F02P 3/045-F02P 3/055 groups.
[F02P 3/0414] using digital techniques  F02P3/0428 ,  F02P3/0442  take precedence
[F02P 3/0421] with electronic tubes -- F02P 3/0421 covers ignition systems for internal-combustion engines that employ electronic tubes as part of the installation. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and non-compression ignition. This subclass addresses configurations where electronic tubes are used to generate or control the spark.
[F02P 3/0428] using digital techniques -- F02P 3/0428 covers the use of digital methods to control or monitor ignition systems for spark-ignition internal-combustion engines, excluding compression-ignition types. It includes electronic controllers, digital signal processing, and software-based timing strategies for generating and managing the spark event.
[F02P 3/0435] with semiconductor devices  F02P3/0453 ,  F02P3/051 ,  F02P3/0552  take precedence -- F02P 3/0435 denotes ignition installations for internal-combustion engines that use semiconductor devices, such as electronic controllers, spark-generating diodes or transistors, to create or manage the spark. This subclass supersedes the more specific groups F02P 3/0453, 3/051 and 3/0552 and falls under other installations within the broader non-compression-ignition engine ignition category.
[F02P 3/0442] using digital techniques  F02P3/0456 ,  F02P3/053 ,  F02P3/0554 ,  F02P3/0558  take precedence
[F02P 3/045] for control of the dwell or anti dwell time -- This subclass covers devices or methods that regulate the dwell period of an ignition event, or provide anti-dwell functions to prevent excessive spark or arc duration, in internal-combustion engines that use non-compression ignition. It pertains to control systems that adjust the timing of ignition to improve performance or safety.
[F02P 3/0453] Opening or closing the primary coil circuit with semiconductor devices
[F02P 3/0456] using digital techniques -- F02P 3/0456 relates to ignition systems for spark-ignition internal-combustion engines in which digital methods are employed. It covers electronic control, software algorithms, signal processing or communication techniques used to manage timing, monitoring, diagnostics or adaptive operation of ancillary ignition installations rather than the primary spark-producing device.
[F02P 3/05] for control of the magnitude of the current in the ignition coil -- The subclass F02P 3/05 covers devices or methods that regulate the magnitude of the electric current supplied to an ignition coil in spark-ignition internal-combustion engines, including control circuits, regulators and related components that adjust coil current for reliable spark generation and engine performance.
[F02P 3/051] Opening or closing the primary coil circuit with semiconductor devices -- The subclass covers ignition systems for non-compression-ignition internal-combustion engines in which the primary coil circuit is opened or closed using semiconductor switching devices such as transistors, MOSFETs or IGBTs.
[F02P 3/053] using digital techniques -- F02P 3/053 covers the use of digital technologies to control ignition processes in spark-ignition internal-combustion engines. It includes electronic ignition modules, micro-controller-based timing control, and software-driven strategies for spark generation and distribution. These digital techniques replace or augment traditional mechanical or analog ignition systems.
[F02P 3/055] with protective means to prevent damage to the circuit, or the ignition coil -- A sub-class of internal-combustion-engine ignition systems, this group covers installations that incorporate protective devices or structures intended to shield the ignition circuit or ignition coil from damage, such as overload, voltage spikes, moisture, or mechanical impact. It includes various shielding, surge-suppression, and enclosure arrangements.
[F02P 3/0552] Opening or closing the primary coil circuit with semiconductor devices -- The entry covers ignition systems for internal-combustion engines in which the primary coil circuit is opened or closed by semiconductor switches such as transistors, MOSFETs or IGBTs. It specifies electronic control of the coil's power flow rather than mechanical or electromechanical means.
[F02P 3/0554] using digital techniques  F02P3/0558  takes precedence -- This subgroup deals with applying digital technologies to auxiliary systems that control spark ignition in gasoline or other non-compression ignition engines. It includes electronic control units, software algorithms and digital signal processing used to manage timing, fuel mixture and engine start-stop functions.
[F02P 3/0556] Protecting the coil when the engine is stopped -- The entry concerns means for safeguarding the ignition coil of a non-compression-spark engine when the engine is stopped. It includes devices or circuits that disconnect, limit voltage or otherwise isolate the coil to prevent overheating, discharge or wear during shutdown.
[F02P 3/0558] using digital techniques -- F02P 3/0558 relates to the use of digital techniques for controlling ignition systems that are not compression-ignition types, applied in auxiliary or other installations of internal-combustion engines. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and ignition for non-compression-ignition engines.
[F02P 3/06] having capacitive energy storage -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Circuits for capacitive discharge ignition (CDI) wherein a discharge current from a capacitor is used to generate the spark, usually by discharging the output to an ignition coil.
[F02P 3/08] Layout of circuits -- F02P 3/08 covers the arrangement and interconnection of electrical or electronic components that control spark-type ignition systems in internal-combustion engines, excluding compression-ignition designs. It includes the design of wiring diagrams, circuit topology, placement of igniters, sensors and control units, and the overall layout of the ignition circuitry within the engine installation.
[F02P 3/0807] Closing the discharge circuit of the storage capacitor with electronic switching means  F02P3/0853 ,  F02P3/0876 ,  F02P3/09  take precedence -- The entry covers systems that close the discharge circuit of a storage capacitor using electronic switching devices in non-compression-ignition engines. It pertains to auxiliary ignition installations where the capacitor is discharged to generate a spark or high-energy pulse, improving timing precision and reducing wear on mechanical contacts. This technology is classified under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and other ignition installations.
[F02P 3/0815] using digital techniques  F02P3/083 ,  F02P3/0846  take precedence -- The classification pertains to the application of digital technologies such as software algorithms, electronic control units and digital signal processing to control or improve ignition systems in non-compression-ignition internal combustion engines. It includes digital timing, spark-plug monitoring and electronic ignition management.
[F02P 3/0823] with electronic tubes -- This CPC subgroup covers ignition arrangements for spark-ignition internal-combustion engines that incorporate electronic tubes such as vacuum tubes, thyratrons or other gaseous discharge devices as part of the ignition circuit.
[F02P 3/083] using digital techniques -- This subclass covers the use of digital techniques-such as electronic control units, software algorithms, sensors, and data processing-to manage and optimise spark or other non-compression ignition systems in internal-combustion engines, including digital timing, adaptive control and fault-diagnosis functions.
[F02P 3/0838] with semiconductor devices  F02P3/0861 ,  F02P3/0884 ,  F02P3/093  take precedence
[F02P 3/0846] using digital techniques  F02P3/0869 ,  F02P3/0892 ,  F02P3/096  take precedence
[F02P 3/0853] for control of the dwell or anti-dwell time -- This subclass covers devices or methods that adjust the duration of the spark, known as dwell, or inhibit it (anti-dwell) in spark-ignition engines. It is used to optimise ignition timing, improve efficiency, and prevent misfires in non-compression-ignition internal-combustion engines.
[F02P 3/0861] Closing the discharge circuit of the storage capacitor with semiconductor devices -- F02P 3/0861 covers the use of semiconductor devices to close the discharge circuit of a storage capacitor in ignition systems for spark-ignition internal-combustion engines. It relates to electronic control of spark generation, replacing mechanical switches with semiconductor switches.
[F02P 3/0869] using digital techniques -- F02P 3/0869 pertains to the application of digital techniques in the ignition systems of spark-ignition internal-combustion engines, covering electronic control units, software algorithms, digital signal processing and other computer-based methods for timing, spark generation and management of ignition devices.
[F02P 3/0876] the storage capacitor being charged by means of an energy converter (DC-DC converter) or of an intermediate storage inductance -- F02P 3/0876 relates to ignition systems for internal-combustion engines that include a storage capacitor charged via an energy-converter such as a DC-DC converter or an intermediate inductive storage element, providing the needed ignition energy.
[F02P 3/0884] Closing the discharge circuit of the storage capacitor with semiconductor devices -- The code refers to electronic ignition arrangements for spark-ignition engines where a storage capacitor is discharged through semiconductor switches rather than mechanical contacts. It covers circuits that use devices such as thyristors, MOSFETs or IGBTs to close the discharge path, improving timing precision and reliability of spark generation. This falls under other installations of non-compression ignition systems in combustion engines.
[F02P 3/0892] using digital techniques -- F02P 3/0892 classifies the use of digital techniques in ignition systems that are not compression-ignition, such as spark-plug based engines. It covers electronic control, software algorithms, digital signal processing and sensor-based timing methods applied to the ignition installation of internal-combustion engines.
[F02P 3/09] for control of the charging current in the capacitor -- F02P 3/09 relates to ignition systems for spark-ignition internal-combustion engines and covers arrangements that control the charging current supplied to the ignition capacitor, such as current-limiting or charging-rate regulation circuits. It pertains to auxiliary installations that ensure the capacitor is charged safely and reliably before discharge for spark generation.
[F02P 3/093] Closing the discharge circuit of the storage capacitor with semiconductor devices -- This subclass covers ignition installations in which the storage capacitor is discharged through a circuit that is closed by semiconductor devices such as thyristors, MOSFETs or IGBTs. It applies to non-compression-ignition engines and describes the electronic means of controlling the capacitor's discharge for spark generation.
[F02P 3/096] using digital techniques -- F02P 3/096 relates to the use of digital methods to control, monitor or optimise ignition devices in internal-combustion engines that are not compression-ignition types, focusing on auxiliary or non-standard installations within the broader field of mechanical engineering and combustion engines.
[F02P 3/10] Low-tension installation, e.g. using surface-discharge sparking plugs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ignition installations having plugs with a high resistive, e.g. carbon, surface where a spark slowly propagates.
[F02P 3/12] Piezoelectric ignition -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ignition installation using a shock on a piezoelectrical crystal to trigger a spark.
[F02P 5/00] Advancing or retarding electric ignition spark -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of the ignition timing and arrangements therefore.
[F02P 5/005] with combination of automatic and non- automatic means -- The F02P 5/005 subclass covers electric ignition systems for spark-ignition internal-combustion engines where the timing of the spark can be advanced or retarded using a mix of automatic (electronic or sensor-based) and non-automatic (mechanical or manual) control methods. This falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and ignition types other than compression ignition.
[F02P 5/02] non-automatically -- It pertains to ignition systems for internal-combustion engines where the electric spark is advanced or retarded manually or by a fixed schedule rather than by an automatic control. The subclass addresses non-automatic means of spark timing adjustment.
[F02P 5/04] automatically, as a function of the working conditions of the engine or vehicle or of the atmospheric conditions -- F02P 5/04 covers systems that automatically advance or retard an electric ignition spark in non-compression-ignition engines based on real-time engine or vehicle operating conditions, such as load, speed, temperature, or ambient atmospheric parameters, to optimise performance and emissions.
[F02P 5/045] combined with electronic control of other engine functions, e.g. fuel injection  in general  F02D37/02 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Conjoint control of ignition timing and other engine functions like control of fuel injection, e.g. control of fuel amount or air control, e.g. by throttle control.
[F02P 5/05] using mechanical means -- F02P 5/05 covers mechanical arrangements that advance or retard the electric ignition spark in non-compression-ignition internal-combustion engines. It includes devices such as cams, levers, gears or other purely mechanical linkages that adjust spark timing without electronic control.
[F02P 5/06] dependent on engine speed -- The subclass F02P 5/06 covers electric ignition systems for spark-ignition internal-combustion engines in which the timing of the ignition spark is varied-advanced or retarded-according to the engine's speed. It is a refinement of the broader category dealing with non-compression ignition spark control.
[F02P 5/07] Centrifugal timing mechanisms -- Centrifugal timing mechanisms are devices that use rotational speed to advance or retard the electric ignition spark in spark-ignition internal-combustion engines, controlling engine timing without reliance on compression ignition. This technology falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion engines and non-compression ignition systems.
[F02P 5/075] Centrifugal devices combined with other specific conditions -- The CPC subclass F02P 5/075 deals with centrifugal devices used in electric ignition systems for internal-combustion engines, specifically when such devices are combined with other defined conditions that affect spark timing, such as advancing or retarding the spark. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and non-compression ignition spark control.
[F02P 5/10] dependent on fluid pressure in engine, e.g. combustion-air pressure -- The F02P 5/10 subclass includes ignition systems for spark-ignited internal-combustion engines whose timing is adjusted in response to fluid pressure within the engine, such as variations in combustion-air or intake-air pressure. It covers methods of advancing or retarding the spark based on these pressure measurements, falling under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and non-compression-ignition categories.
[F02P 5/103] dependent on the combustion-air pressure in engine -- F02P 5/103 covers ignition devices for spark-ignition internal-combustion engines whose timing is varied in response to the pressure of the intake or combustion air. The system advances or retards the electric spark based on measured air-pressure conditions to optimise ignition timing and improve engine performance under different loads. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and non-compression ignition categories.
[F02P 5/106] Combustion-air pressure devices combined with other specific conditions  with centrifugal devices  F02P5/075 -- F02P 5/106 covers devices that combine a combustion-air pressure control mechanism with a centrifugal element for spark-ignition internal-combustion engines. The classification applies to systems where the air-pressure regulator is linked to a rotor, flyweight or similar centrifugal device to modify intake pressure under specific operating conditions, thereby influencing ignition timing or mixture preparation. It is a sub-group of F02P 5/075 within the broader category of non-compression ignition engine technologies.
[F02P 5/12] dependent a specific pressure other than that of combustion-air, e.g. of exhaust, cooling fluid, lubricant -- This subclass covers ignition systems for non-compression-ignition internal-combustion engines in which the timing of the electric spark is adjusted based on a pressure other than the combustion-air pressure, such as exhaust-gas, coolant or lubricant pressure. It defines methods and devices that sense these secondary pressures and use them to advance or retard the spark for improved engine performance or emissions control.
[F02P 5/14] dependent on specific conditions other than engine speed or engine fluid pressure, e.g. temperature -- The F02P 5/14 subclass covers electric ignition systems for spark-ignition internal-combustion engines where the timing of spark advance or retard is varied based on specific conditions other than engine speed or fuel-fluid pressure, such as temperature or other environmental factors. This allows the ignition timing to be optimized for performance, emissions, or efficiency under varying operating circumstances.
[F02P 5/142] dependent on a combination of several specific conditions  F02P5/075 ,  F02P5/106  takes precedence -- F02P 5/142 covers control of the timing of an electric ignition spark for non-compression-ignition engines where the advance or retard is determined by a combination of several specific operating conditions, such that this combined-condition rule takes precedence over individual condition rules such as those in F02P 5/075 and F02P 5/106.
[F02P 5/145] using electrical means -- F02P 5/145 covers the use of electrical means to control the timing of an ignition spark in non-compression-ignition internal-combustion engines, enabling either advancement or retardation of the spark for improved engine performance or emissions control. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and ignition (other than compression ignition).
[F02P 5/1455] by using a second control of the closed loop type  dependent on pinking  F02P5/152 -- The subclass F02P 5/1455 concerns spark-ignition engines that use a secondary closed-loop controller to modify spark timing based on a pinking signal, such as knock detection. This additional control adjusts the advance or retard of the electric ignition spark to optimize combustion performance.
[F02P 5/15] Digital data processing -- F02P 5/15 covers digital data-processing techniques used to control the advance or retard of an electric ignition spark in non-compression-ignition internal-combustion engines. It includes software, algorithms, and electronic systems that calculate timing parameters and adjust spark timing to optimize engine performance and emissions. This falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and ignition systems.
[F02P 5/1502] using one central computing unit -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ignition installations with an electronic control unit which is the main application of modern ignition control.
[F02P 5/1504] with particular means during a transient phase, e.g. acceleration, deceleration, gear change  during starting  F02P5/1506
[F02P 5/1506] with particular means during starting -- A device for an internal-combustion engine that varies the timing of the electric ignition spark when the engine is being started, using particular means to advance or retard the spark. It pertains to non-compression ignition systems that control spark timing during start-up.
[F02P 5/1508] with particular means during idling -- This subclass covers arrangements that modify the timing of an electric ignition spark when the engine is idling, using particular means to advance or retard the spark. It includes mechanisms that detect idle conditions and adjust spark timing to improve stability, performance or emissions.
[F02P 5/151] with means for compensating the variation of the characteristics of the engine or of a sensor, e.g. by ageing -- F02P 5/151 covers electric-spark ignition systems that include means to compensate for variations in engine or sensor characteristics, such as changes that occur with ageing. It relates to adjusting spark timing in internal-combustion engines where the ignition is not compression-based, and is placed under mechanical engineering, combustion engines, and non-compression ignition categories.
[F02P 5/1512] with particular means concerning an individual cylinder -- F02P 5/1512 relates to electric-spark ignition systems for internal-combustion engines that incorporate means specifically addressing a single cylinder, such as individual coil or spark-plug control devices that advance or retard the spark timing for that cylinder.
[F02P 5/1514] with means for optimising the use of registers or of memories, e.g. interpolation -- This subclass relates to electric ignition systems for spark-ignition engines that employ optimized handling of registers or memories-such as interpolation-to control the timing of spark advance or retard.
[F02P 5/1516] with means relating to exhaust gas recirculation, e.g. turbo
[F02P 5/1518] using two or more central computing units, e.g. interpolation -- F02P 5/1518 relates to electric ignition systems for spark-ignition engines in which two or more central computing units cooperate, for example by interpolating data from sensors to adjust the timing of the spark. The classification covers designs that use multiple processors to advance or retard ignition events, improving engine performance and emissions control.
[F02P 5/152] dependent on pinking -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ignition control of pinking/knocking i.e. undesired too early ignition which results in a shock against all the mechanical pieces (pistons, valves, rods, crankshaft) resulting in a very characteristic noise. This phenomenon arises mostly in acceleration with a warm engine. When knock is detected by a microphone or high frequency vibrations in pressure, ionic or light sensor, the ignition should be directly retarded and afterwards, slowly set back to where it belongs to be.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Detection of knocking without engine control -> G01L23/22
[F02P 5/1521] with particular means during a transient phase, e.g. starting, acceleration, deceleration, gear change -- This subclass covers electric ignition systems that advance or retard the spark timing using specific means during transient engine phases such as starting, acceleration, deceleration or gear changes. It belongs to the hierarchy for combustion engines and focuses on non-compression-ignition control of internal-combustion engines.
[F02P 5/1522] with particular means concerning an individual cylinder -- The CPC subclass F02P 5/1522 deals with systems that advance or retard the electric ignition spark for a particular cylinder of a spark-ignition internal-combustion engine. It specifies individual-cylinder control mechanisms, such as separate timing modules or channel-specific sensors, to optimise combustion on a per-cylinder basis.
[F02P 5/1523] with particular laws of return to advance, e.g. step by step, differing from the laws of retard -- F02P 5/1523 covers electric ignition systems for internal-combustion engines that advance the spark timing by applying specific, step-by-step return laws rather than the conventional retardation laws. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → ignition other than compression ignition, focusing on precise control of spark advance.
[F02P 5/1525] with means for compensating the variation of the characteristics of the pinking sensor or of the electrical means, e.g. by ageing  when variation of characteristics results only from incorrect functioning  F02P5/1526 -- The subclass addresses ignition systems that incorporate devices or circuits to offset changes in the characteristics of a pinking sensor or related electrical components, such as drift caused by aging or faulty operation, thereby maintaining consistent spark performance. It is situated within mechanical engineering for combustion engines, specifically non-compression internal-combustion ignition.
[F02P 5/1526] with means for taking into account incorrect functioning of the pinking sensor or of the electrical means -- A device that monitors the operation of the ignition-timing sensor or its electrical circuitry and, when a fault is detected, modifies the spark advance or retard to compensate for the error, ensuring proper engine performance despite sensor or wiring malfunctions.
[F02P 5/1527] with means allowing burning of two or more fuels, e.g. super or normal, premium or regular -- The subclass covers electric-spark ignition systems for internal-combustion engines that incorporate means to burn two or more fuel types-such as super, normal, premium or regular gasoline-allowing the engine to select or blend fuels for improved performance, efficiency or emissions control.
[F02P 5/1528] for turbocompressed engine -- F02P 5/1528 relates to electric ignition systems that advance or retard the spark timing in turbo-charged internal-combustion engines. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically combustion engines, and more narrowly ignition other than compression ignition. It covers technologies for controlling spark timing in turbocompressed engines.
[F02P 5/153] dependent on combustion pressure -- This subgroup covers ignition systems for non-compression-ignition engines in which the timing of the electric spark is varied according to the pressure developing during combustion, allowing the spark to be advanced or retarded based on measured combustion pressure to optimise performance, efficiency or emissions.
[F02P 5/155] Analogue data processing -- F02P 5/155 covers analogue-based methods and circuits for advancing or retarding the electric ignition spark in internal-combustion engines. The classification applies to devices that process continuous electrical signals to control spark timing, rather than digital or software approaches, within the broader area of ignition systems that are not compression-ignition types.
[F02P 5/1551] by determination of elapsed time with reference to a particular point on the motor axle, dependent on specific conditions -- A method for controlling the advance or retard of an electric spark ignition in a non-compression-ignition internal-combustion engine by measuring the elapsed time from a reference point on the motor axle and adjusting the spark based on specific operating conditions. This timing technique is used to optimise combustion performance and emissions.
[F02P 5/1553] by determination of elapsed angle with reference to a particular point on the motor axle, dependent on specific conditions -- This subclass covers methods of adjusting the timing of an ignition spark in non-compression-ignition engines by measuring the angular displacement of the crankshaft from a reference point, with the timing varied according to operating conditions such as load, speed, or temperature.
[F02P 5/1555] using a continuous control, dependent on speed -- The classification pertains to internal-combustion engines that use an electric ignition system whose spark timing is continuously adjusted based on engine speed, rather than fixed intervals. This continuous speed-dependent control can advance or retard the spark to optimise performance across the operating range.
[F02P 5/1556] using a stepped control, dependent on speed -- F02P 5/1556 relates to methods for advancing or retarding the electric ignition spark in spark-ignition internal-combustion engines by employing a stepped control whose settings vary with engine speed.
[F02P 5/1558] with special measures for starting -- This subgroup relates to ignition systems that modify the timing of the electric spark when the engine is being started, using devices such as pre-spark, boost, enrichment or auxiliary ignition to ensure reliable start-up. It covers methods that advance or retard the spark specifically for starting conditions.
[F02P 5/16] characterised by the mechanical transmission between sensing elements or personal controls and final actuating elements -- This subclass deals with ignition systems for spark-ignition internal-combustion engines where the signal from sensors or driver controls is conveyed to the spark-producing element by a mechanical linkage rather than electrically.
[F02P 7/00] Arrangements of distributors, circuit-makers or -breakers, or pick-up devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Distributors, i.e. mechanical arrangements for distributing ignition signals as well as circuit makers and breakers, i.e. mechanical means for opening and closing ignition circuits. Furthermore it covers pickup-devices, i.e. means for detecting the current working phase or crank angle.
[F02P 7/02] of distributors -- F02P 7/02 covers the arrangement of distributors, circuit-makers or circuit-breakers, and pick-up devices used in spark-ignition (non-compression-ignition) internal-combustion engines, detailing how these components are positioned and interconnected within the ignition system.
[F02P 7/021] Mechanical distributors -- F02P 7/021 classifies mechanical distributors employed in spark-ignition internal-combustion engines, covering their arrangement along with associated circuit-making or breaking components and pick-up devices. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → ignition other than compression ignition.
[F02P 7/022] Details of the distributor rotor or electrode -- F02P 7/022 relates to the design of the rotor and electrode in a distributor or comparable ignition device for spark-ignition internal-combustion engines. It includes the geometry, material choice, surface treatment and mounting features that influence the timing and distribution of electrical sparks to the engine cylinders.
[F02P 7/023] with magnetically controlled mechanical contacts -- This subclass covers ignition systems for spark-ignition engines that use distributors, circuit-making or breaking devices, or pickup components whose operation is governed by magnetic forces moving mechanical contacts. The magnetic actuation provides precise timing of spark generation without direct electrical control of the contacts.
[F02P 7/025] with noise suppression means specially adapted for the distributor -- The F02P 7/025 group covers arrangements of distributors, circuit-makers or pick-up devices for spark-ignition internal-combustion engines that incorporate special noise-suppression means, such as dampers or acoustic liners, to reduce electrical and mechanical noise generated by the distributor operation.
[F02P 7/026] Distributors combined with other ignition devices, e.g. coils, fuel-injectors -- F02P 7/026 relates to non-compression ignition systems for internal-combustion engines where a distributor is integrated with additional ignition components such as ignition coils or fuel-injectors, forming combined devices that control spark timing and fuel delivery.
[F02P 7/027] combined with centrifugal advance devices -- The F02P 7/027 subclass covers ignition system arrangements-such as distributors, circuit-makers, breakers or pick-up devices-that are integrated with centrifugal advance mechanisms, allowing timing to be varied automatically with engine speed. It pertains to non-compression-ignition internal-combustion engines and specifies how the advance device is combined with the basic ignition hardware.
[F02P 7/028] combined with circuit-makers or -breakers  and with centrifugal advance devices  F02P7/027
[F02P 7/03] with electrical means -- This subclass covers ignition systems for spark-ignition internal-combustion engines that employ electrical means, specifically the arrangement of distributors, circuit-makers, circuit-breakers or pick-up devices to generate and time the spark.
[F02P 7/035] without mechanical switching means -- The entry covers ignition arrangements for internal-combustion engines in which the distributor, circuit-maker, breaker or pick-up device operates without any moving contacts, relying on solid-state or electronic switching instead of mechanical switches. It pertains to non-mechanical switching means used in spark-ignition systems.
[F02P 7/04] having distributors with air-tight casing -- The CPC subclass F02P 7/04 covers ignition-system arrangements for non-compression-ignition engines in which the distributor is enclosed in an air-tight casing. This sealed design protects the distributor's contacts and moving parts from dust, moisture and contaminants, enhancing reliability and preventing malfunction of the spark-generation circuit.
[F02P 7/06] of circuit-makers or -breakers, or pick-up devices adapted to sense particular points of the timing cycle -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pick-up devices which provide position information to the ignition timing control unit;Circuit-makers or -breakers.
[F02P 7/061] pick-up devices without mechanical contacts  F02P7/067  -  F02P7/077  take precedence -- This subclass covers electronic pick-up devices used in ignition systems for spark-ignition engines that operate without mechanical contacts, such as solid-state or magnetic sensors that detect rotor position. It falls under arrangements of distributors, circuit-makers or breakers and supersedes the more specific groups F02P7/067 to F02P7/077.
[F02P 7/063] Mechanical pick-up devices, circuit-makers or -breakers, e.g. contact-breakers -- The classification covers mechanical devices that pick up the ignition signal and segment or open the circuit in non-compression-ignition engines, such as contact-breakers, circuit-makers and circuit-breakers, including their arrangement with distributors or similar components.
[F02P 7/0631] Constructional details of contacts -- The code addresses the design and construction features of electrical contacts within distributors, circuit-makers, breakers or pickup devices used in spark-ignition internal-combustion engines. It covers aspects such as contact geometry, material selection, mounting arrangements and connection methods that affect the reliability and performance of the ignition system.
[F02P 7/0632] with rotary contacts -- This subclass covers the design of distributor or circuit-maker arrangements that use rotary contacts for spark-ignition internal-combustion engines. It includes pick-up devices and breaker mechanisms where the contact surfaces rotate to open or close the ignition circuit.
[F02P 7/0634] Details of cams or cam-followers -- F02P 7/0634 deals with the specific design of cams or cam-followers employed in distributor, circuit-maker, circuit-breaker or pick-up device arrangements for ignition systems that are not compression-ignition in internal-combustion engines. It falls under mechanical engineering → combustion engines → ignition other than compression ignition.
[F02P 7/0635] with means to set the breaker gap -- The subclass F02P 7/0635 covers ignition arrangements for internal-combustion engines that include a mechanism for setting the breaker (spark-gap) distance. It applies to distributors, circuit-makers or breakers equipped with adjustable gap-setting devices, whether manually or automatically operated.
[F02P 7/0637] with several circuit-makers or -breakers actuated by the same cam -- The code F02P 7/0637 covers ignition arrangements for spark-ignition internal-combustion engines in which a single cam operates several circuit-makers or circuit-breakers. It pertains to distributor or pick-up device configurations that drive multiple ignition contacts simultaneously.
[F02P 7/0638] with noise suppression means specially adapted for the breakers -- The subclass F02P 7/0638 covers designs of distributors, circuit-makers or breakers, and pick-up devices that incorporate noise-suppression means specially adapted for the breaker component. It is situated within ignition arrangements for spark-ignition internal-combustion engines.
[F02P 7/067] Electromagnetic pick-up devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pick-up devices for detecting the engine position or cycle with electromagnetic means.
[F02P 7/0672] using Wiegand effect -- The entry covers designs for distributor, circuit-maker, circuit-breaker or pick-up arrangements in spark-ignition engines that employ the Wiegand effect to generate or sense magnetic pulses for timing or signal transmission. It relates to non-compression ignition internal-combustion engines and specifies how the Wiegand phenomenon is integrated into ignition circuitry.
[F02P 7/0675] with variable reluctance, e.g. depending on the shape of a tooth -- The entry covers ignition devices for internal-combustion engines that use a magnetic circuit whose reluctance changes, typically by varying the shape of a tooth, to control the operation of distributors, circuit-makers, circuit-breakers or pick-up units. It relates to the design of variable-reluctance components that influence spark timing or ignition voltage.
[F02P 7/0677] Mechanical arrangements -- The entry F02P 7/0677 covers the mechanical layout of components such as distributors, circuit-makers, circuit-breakers and pick-up devices used in non-compression-ignition systems for internal-combustion engines. It concerns how these parts are arranged within the ignition apparatus. This classification falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and ignition systems.
[F02P 7/07] Hall-effect pick-up devices -- F02P 7/07 defines Hall-effect pickup devices used in spark-ignition engines to sense the rotational position of the distributor or camshaft and generate timing signals for the ignition system. These solid-state sensors replace traditional magnetic or mechanical pickups, providing reliable detection of the ignition timing without compression-ignition. The code applies to arrangements of distributors, circuit-makers or breakers that incorporate Hall-effect technology.
[F02P 7/073] Optical pick-up devices -- The classification F02P 7/073 covers optical pickup devices used in ignition systems for internal-combustion engines, specifically arrangements of distributors, circuit-makers or circuit-breakers, and related pick-up components that employ light-based sensing to trigger ignition events.
[F02P 7/077] Circuits therefor, e.g. pulse generators -- F02P 7/077 deals with the electrical circuits that operate non-compression-ignition spark systems in internal-combustion engines, covering pulse-generating devices and related circuit components used to drive distributors, circuit-makers or pick-up devices.
[F02P 7/0775] Electronical verniers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for generating higher frequency signals to increase the precision of crank (or cam) angle detection for ignition control.
[F02P 7/08] having air-tight casings -- The subclass F02P 7/08 covers ignition system arrangements-such as distributors, circuit-makers, circuit-breakers or pick-up devices-used in non-compression-ignition internal-combustion engines that are housed in air-tight casings. It falls under mechanical engineering, combustion engines and the broader category of ignition devices.
[F02P 7/10] Drives of distributors or of circuit-makers or -breakers -- This classification covers mechanisms that drive distributors, circuit-makers or circuit-breakers in non-compression-ignition internal-combustion engines, specifying how these devices are arranged to control the timing of ignition events.
[F02P 9/00] Electric spark ignition control, not otherwise provided for -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Electric control of sparks not provided for in previous groups, e.g. for controlling the intensity or length of the luminous discharge between two conductors.
[F02P 9/002] Control of spark intensity, intensifying, lengthening, suppression  by means of current control in the storage devices  F02P3/05 ,  F02P3/09 , during starting  F02P15/12 -- The entry F02P 9/002 covers methods for controlling electric spark intensity in internal-combustion engines by regulating the current in the energy-storage devices, including techniques to intensify, lengthen or suppress the spark, with particular emphasis on applications during engine start-up.
[F02P 9/005] by weakening or suppression of sparks to limit the engine speed -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Systems for weakening or suppressing the spark for maximum speed control. Contains also engine speed limitation via ignition advance.
[F02P 9/007] by supplementary electrical discharge in the pre-ionised electrode interspace of the sparking plug, e.g. plasma jet ignition -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Plasma jet ignition plugs or similar arrangments including a massive ignition kernel and the liberation of high energy photons..
[F03] MACHINES OR ENGINES FOR LIQUIDS -- F03 encompasses machines or engines that move, process, or otherwise handle liquids, such as pumps, compressors, hydraulic devices and fluid-power systems. It is classified within mechanical engineering and addresses equipment that generates or transmits fluid power for industrial, automotive and related applications.
[F03B] MACHINES OR ENGINES FOR LIQUIDS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines driven by liquids and machines for liquids, other than of positive-displacement type.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Machines for liquids and gases -> F01 - Positive-displacement engines for liquid -> F03C - Ocean thermal energy conversion (OTEC) -> F03G7/05 - Positive-displacement machines for liquids -> F04 - Rotary fluid gearing of the hydrokinetic type -> F16H41/00
[F03B 1/00] Non-positive-displacement machines or engines characterised by specified type, e.g. water turbines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines of impulse type, i.e. turbines with jets of high-velocity liquid impinging on blades or like rotors, e.g. Pelton wheels; Parts or details peculiar thereto.  In these engines the energy - usually potential - of the liquid is entirely transformed into kinetic energy (or momentum) in the nozzle system, before the liquid reaches the runner or rotor.  Another way of defining these engines is to say that in an impulse turbine the runner or rotor is not enclosed by a pressurised space or casing, but placed within a space which is normally at atmospheric pressure.  DE202004019537U
[F03B 1/02] Buckets -- F03B 1/02 designates buckets employed in non-positive-displacement machines for liquids, such as water-turbine buckets that capture and direct fluid flow. It falls under mechanical engineering, machines or engines for liquids, specifically those characterized by a specified type.
[F03B 1/04] Nozzles -- F03B 1/04 classifies nozzles used in machines or engines for liquids, especially non-positive-displacement types such as water turbines, where the device shapes or accelerates a fluid stream for propulsion or energy conversion.
[F03B 11/00] Parts or details not provided for in, or of interest apart from, the preceding groups , -- F03B 11/00 pertains to components or features of liquid-handling machines that are not specifically listed in earlier subclasses. It includes any ancillary or special parts of pumps, compressors, or similar hydraulic equipment that fall outside the defined groups. The class is used to capture unique or peripheral elements of such devices.
[F03B 11/002] Injecting air or other fluid  F03D80/40 ,  F03B11/04 ,  F03B15/00  take precedence -- F03B 11/002 concerns devices that inject air or other fluids into machines or engines handling liquids, covering parts or details not otherwise classified in the preceding groups. It sits within mechanical engineering for liquid-handling machinery and is subordinated to the more specific subclasses F03D80/40, F03B11/04 and F03B15/00.
[F03B 11/004] Valve arrangements  F03B3/10  takes precedence; adjustable wicket gates  F03B3/183 ; valves in general  F16K -- F03B 11/004 relates to valve arrangements for machines or engines processing liquids, covering components or details not specifically addressed in earlier subclasses. It includes general valve configurations and adjustable wicket gates, with the broader subclass F03B 3/10 taking precedence, and overlaps with the general valve class F16K.
[F03B 11/006] Sealing arrangements  F03B3/14 ,  F03B3/183 ,  F03B13/083  takes precedence; sealings in general  F16J
[F03B 11/008] Measuring or testing arrangements  in general  G01 -- F03B 11/008 classifies general measuring or testing arrangements associated with machines or engines for liquids, such as pumps or compressors, when the measuring device is not otherwise specified in the CPC. It covers instrumentation that monitors flow, pressure, temperature or performance of liquid-handling equipment and is linked to the broader G01 measurement field.
[F03B 11/02] Casings -- F03B 11/02 covers the casings that enclose machines or engines for liquids, such as pumps, compressors and hydraulic devices. It includes protective housings, shells and structural enclosures that are not otherwise specified in the preceding groups of the mechanical-engineering hierarchy.
[F03B 11/025] Covers
[F03B 11/04] for diminishing cavitation or vibration, e.g. balancing -- F03B 11/04 pertains to components or arrangements in liquid-handling machines that are designed to lessen cavitation or vibration, for example by providing balancing features. It is classified under mechanical engineering, machines or engines for liquids, in the subgroup for parts not otherwise specified.
[F03B 11/06] Bearing arrangements -- F03B 11/06 covers the design, selection and configuration of bearings used in liquid-handling machines or engines, including lubrication systems, bearing housings, seals and mounting methods that support rotating shafts and reduce friction.
[F03B 11/063] Arrangements for balancing axial thrust -- The subclass F03B 11/063 covers arrangements that balance axial thrust in liquid-handling machines such as pumps or compressors, including thrust-bearing systems, counter-rotating components or hydraulic compensation schemes designed to neutralise the longitudinal forces generated during operation.
[F03B 11/066] in vertical axis machines -- F03B 11/066 covers machines or engines for liquids whose axis of rotation is vertical, such as vertical-axis pumps, turbines or wind-energy devices where the rotor is mounted on a vertical shaft and interacts with the fluid flow in a defined manner. The classification is used for designs that are not covered by more specific vertical-axis subgroups and focuses on the overall arrangement of the rotating element relative to the vertical axis.
[F03B 11/08] for removing foreign matter, e.g. mud -- F03B 11/08 covers devices or methods in liquid-handling machines that are intended to separate or extract unwanted substances such as mud, debris or other foreign matter from the fluid. Typical applications include pumps, filters, or cleaning attachments used in water-treatment, drilling, irrigation and similar systems.
[F03B 13/00] Adaptations of machines or engines for special use -- F03B 13/00 relates to machines or engines for liquids that have been altered for particular purposes, such as operation under unique temperatures, pressures, viscosities or environments. It covers modifications for specialised tasks like firefighting, chemical processing, marine use or other specific applications.
[F03B 13/02] Adaptations for drilling wells -- F03B 13/02 covers modifications of liquid-pumping machines or engines so they can be used for well drilling. It includes adaptations that enable the equipment to handle high pressures, abrasive fluids, cuttings removal and the specific suction or thrust requirements of drilling operations.
[F03B 13/04] Adaptations for use in dentistry -- F03B 13/04 covers adaptations of machines or engines that handle liquids, specifically modified for dental applications. It includes alterations to pumps, syringes, or other fluid-handling equipment to meet the precision, hygiene, and ergonomic requirements of dental procedures. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering for liquid-based machinery.

[F03B 13/08] Machine or engine aggregates in dams or the like -- Machines or engine aggregates installed in dams, reservoirs or similar hydraulic structures, including turbines, pumps, generators and related drive units that harness or control water flow for power generation, water regulation, flood control or irrigation. These assemblies are adapted to the specific conditions and constraints of large-scale water-holding facilities.
[F03B 13/083] The generator rotor being mounted as turbine rotor rim -- The CPC subclass F03B 13/083 covers adaptations of liquid-driven machines in which the rotor of an electrical generator is employed as the rim of a turbine, integrating the turbine's rotating outer periphery with the generator's armature. This design is used in hydraulic or water-powered power plants to combine energy extraction and conversion in a single rotating assembly.
[F03B 13/086] Plants characterised by the use of siphons; their regulation  siphon weirs  E02B7/18 ; siphons in general  F04F10/00
[F03B 13/10] Submerged units incorporating electric generators or motors -- F03B 13/10 classifies submerged machines or engines that incorporate an electric generator or motor within the same unit, such as turbine-driven generators used in underwater power stations or marine propulsion systems where the drive motor is housed in the submerged portion of the equipment. This sub-class falls under adaptations of liquid-working machines for special uses.
[F03B 13/105] Bulb groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  An installation where the turbine (usually propeller type), the gearbox (if any) and the electrical generator are all enclosed in a watertight enclosure (the "bulb") which is submerged and completely surrounded by the fluid from which the group extracts energy.
