[F03B 15/00] Controlling -- F03B 15/00 covers devices, mechanisms and methods for controlling the operation of pumps, compressors, turbines or fans handling liquids, including speed regulation, flow or pressure control, automatic start-stop systems and other supervisory functions that adjust performance to meet desired operating conditions.
[F03B 15/005] Starting, also of pump-turbines -- The code F03B 15/005 covers methods and devices for initiating the operation of liquid-handling machines such as pump-turbines. It includes start-up mechanisms, control systems and auxiliary equipment that bring the pump-turbine into service. This falls under the controlling sub-category of machines or engines for liquids in the mechanical engineering classification.
[F03B 15/02] by varying liquid flow -- F03B 15/02 covers control methods for liquid-driven machines and engines that achieve regulation by changing the liquid's flow rate, such as variable-speed pumps, hydraulic drives, or any system where flow modulation adjusts speed, power output, or operating characteristics. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering topics dealing with machines or engines for liquids and their control mechanisms.
[F03B 15/04] of turbines
[F03B 15/06] Regulating, i.e. acting automatically -- The CPC subclass F03B 15/06 covers automatic regulation of hydraulic or fluid-driven machines, such as pumps or engines for liquids. It includes devices that sense operating conditions and adjust performance without manual intervention, encompassing feedback-controlled valves, speed regulators and other self-acting control mechanisms.
[F03B 15/08] by speed, e.g. by measuring electric frequency or liquid flow -- F03B 15/08 relates to controlling machines or engines for liquids by monitoring their speed, such as using electric frequency measurements or direct liquid-flow measurement. It applies to regulation of pumps, turbines and comparable hydraulic devices based on speed-derived signals.
[F03B 15/10] without retroactive action -- This group concerns control schemes for liquid-driven machines such as pumps or compressors in which the control action is applied only to the present or future operating state and does not affect a portion of the cycle that has already occurred, for example valve timing based solely on current pressure or flow without retroactive correction.
[F03B 15/12] with retroactive action -- F03B 15/12 relates to control devices for liquid-driven machines that work with retroactive action, i.e., systems that can alter or influence a process after it has occurred to achieve a desired effect, such as adjusting pressures, flow rates or valve positions based on feedback from earlier operation.
[F03B 15/14] by or of water level -- F03B 15/14 relates to mechanisms for measuring, indicating or regulating the level of water in tanks, reservoirs, pipelines or other containers, often by means of floats, pressure sensors, electronic transducers or similar devices that keep the liquid at a desired height. This subclass falls under machines or engines for liquids that control fluid flow.
[F03B 15/16] by power output -- F03B 15/16 classifies control arrangements for liquid-driven machines and engines according to the power output of the system. It is a subcategory of mechanical engineering concerned with machines or engines for liquids and falls under the controlling group.
[F03B 15/18] for safety purposes, e.g. preventing overspeed -- F03B 15/18 covers safety control mechanisms for liquid-driven machines, especially systems that detect and prevent excessive rotational speed. The class includes overspeed governors, speed limiters and related protective devices designed to avoid damage or hazardous operation of pumps, turbines or other fluid-power equipment.
[F03B 15/20] specially adapted for turbines with jets of high-velocity liquid impinging on bladed or like rotors -- A turbine design in which streams of high-velocity liquid are directed to strike the blades or similar rotors, with specialized features to optimise the impingement and energy transfer. This falls within the mechanical-engineering class for machines or engines handling liquids, specifically those controlling fluid flow to power rotary devices.
[F03B 15/22] for safety purposes -- The F03B 15/22 subgroup covers safety-related control measures for machines or engines that move liquids, such as pumps, compressors and similar equipment. It includes devices and methods that detect abnormal conditions, limit pressure, prevent leaks or over-run, and otherwise protect the installation and its environment from hazardous situations.
[F03B 17/00] Other machines or engines -- F03B 17/00 designates machines or engines that handle liquids but are not classified elsewhere in the mechanical-engineering hierarchy; it groups together diverse equipment such as unconventional pumps, mixers, agitators, and other liquid-processing devices that do not fit the more narrowly defined subclasses.
[F03B 17/005] Installations wherein the liquid circulates in a closed loop  F03B13/06  takes precedence ; Alleged  perpetua mobilia  of this or similar kind  perpetua mobilia  using hydrostatic thrust or buoyancy  F03B17/04 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Turbines driven by fluid with (height) potential energy, which drives a generator to produce electricity, and a pump which returns the fluid to the original height.  WO2004094816
[F03B 17/02] using hydrostatic thrust -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Turbines in which the conversion of energy is made thanks to the hydrostatic vertical thrust of objects lighter than water experienced when submerged. Often uses additional compressed air in which case the device is a motor.  DE10200903
[F03B 17/025] and reciprocating motion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Floats which usually follow alternative water levels. Also containers (floats) which are alternatively filled and emptied within a water basin. All these devices are motors.  WO201106632
[F03B 17/04] Alleged -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Perpetua mobilia of alleged kind, i.e. devices where the hydrostatic thrust effect is used to supposedly drive a device continuously, without input of energy, or of additional energy after the start, even when the concept perpetual is not explicitly mentioned.  US2010223922
[F03B 17/06] using liquid flow , e.g. of swinging-flap type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines or engines using liquid flow with predominantly kinetic energy conversion, e.g. of swinging-flap type, "run-of-river", "ultra-low head".  The term "liquid flow" is meant here to refer to fluid flows with minimal gradient, little more than that necessary for maintaining the flow, like for instance in calm rivers. The energy being converted is thus the kinetic energy of the flow, rather than its potential (height) energy, as in older, conventional hydro(electric) schemes involving large height differences, or even medium to small height ("head") differences, used in water wheel type devices.  **Glossary:** - Rotation axis: Rotational axis of the device converting the kinetic energy.
[F03B 17/061] with rotation axis substantially in flow direction -- F03B 17/061 designates liquid-handling machines whose rotating shaft is aligned substantially with the direction of fluid flow, such as axial-flow pumps, propellers or turbines where the rotor spins parallel to the stream.
[F03B 17/062] with rotation axis substantially at right angle to flow direction -- This CPC subgroup concerns liquid-handling machines whose driving shaft lies essentially perpendicular to the direction of fluid flow. It includes pumps, turbines or mixers where the rotation axis is at a right angle to the flow, distinguishing them from axial-flow or radial-flow designs.
[F03B 17/063] the flow engaging parts having no movement relative to the rotor during its rotation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines or engines in which the flow engaging parts, i.e. the elements in contact with the flow, transferring its kinetic energy to the rest of the converting device, are permanently and rigidly fixed to the rest of the structure of the converter.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[F03B 17/064] and a rotor of the endless-chain type -- This subclass covers liquid-handling machines that use a rotor formed from an endless chain. The chain acts as the moving element within a pump or similar engine, transmitting power to the fluid by rotating inside a housing. It is applied to chain-type pump rotors and related mechanisms.
[F03B 17/065] the flow engaging parts having a cyclic movement relative to the rotor during its rotation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines or engines in which the concept "cyclic movement" refers to the relative movement of the flow engaging parts with respect to the rest of the converter structure, where the parts adopt different positions depending on the rotational position of the whole converter structure as it rotates about its axis, returning to the original position after each revolution. When coupled to the movement of rotation then F03B17/067 .  WO03029646
[F03B 17/066] and a rotor of the endless-chain type -- F03B 17/066 covers machines or engines for liquids that employ a rotor of the endless-chain type, where a continuous chain forms the rotating element to convey or pressurise fluid. This design is used in pumps, mixers or similar liquid-handling equipment and is classified under other machines or engines within the mechanical engineering hierarchy.
[F03B 17/067] the cyclic relative movement being positively coupled to the movement of rotation -- This subclass covers machines for liquids where a cyclic relative motion - such as reciprocating, oscillating or wobbling movement - is directly and positively linked to a rotational motion, ensuring the two motions are synchronized. Typical examples include pump mechanisms that convert rotary input into a back-and-forth movement of a piston or diaphragm.
[F03B 17/068] and a rotor of the endless-chain type -- F03B 17/068 refers to pumps in which the rotating element is an endless-chain type rotor that moves the fluid as the chain circulates. The classification covers machines that use a continuous loop of linked elements as the rotor to impart energy to liquids, typically seen in special-purpose or low-speed pumping designs.
[F03B 3/00] Machines or engines of reaction type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines or engines of reaction type; Parts or details peculiar thereto.  A reaction type machine or engine in which the potential energy of the liquid is transformed into mechanical energy partly in the stator and partly in the rotor, in other words the fluid is forced to accelerate by a decrease in the available cross reaction, in both sections. Both the stator and rotor spaces are completely filled with the liquid whilst in operation, at a pressure higher than atmospheric.  A special case is when all the energy is transformed in the rotor, the so called Hero's turbine.  FR935827
[F03B 3/02] with radial flow at high-pressure side and axial flow at low-pressure side of rotors, e.g. Francis turbines -- F03B 3/02 classifies reaction-type liquid machines in which the working fluid moves radially outward on the high-pressure side of the rotor and axially inward on the low-pressure side, a flow arrangement characteristic of Francis turbines. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically machines or engines for liquids.
[F03B 3/04] with substantially axial flow throughout rotors, e.g. propeller turbines -- F03B 3/04 covers reaction-type liquid machines such as propeller turbines that maintain substantially axial flow throughout the rotor assembly. The classification is applied to devices where the fluid moves mainly parallel to the axis of rotation, distinguishing them from radial-flow or mixed-flow designs.
[F03B 3/06] with adjustable blades, e.g. Kaplan turbines -- This subclass covers reaction-type hydraulic machines whose runner blades can be adjusted in pitch or angle, enabling the device to maintain efficiency over varying flow conditions, as in Kaplan turbines used for low-head water power.
[F03B 3/08] with pressure-velocity transformation exclusively in rotors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Turbines in which all the potential energy of the liquid is transformed by acceleration in the rotor itself, causing the reaction which drives the turbine. The typical example is the Greek Hero's turbine - although that one was driven by steam. A rotating garden sprinkler uses this effect.  WO9612872
[F03B 3/10] characterised by having means for functioning alternatively as pumps or turbines -- F03B 3/10 covers machines that can work both as pumps and as turbines, using the same rotating element to either add energy to a fluid or extract energy from it. Such reversible devices are common in hydro-electric installations, pumped-storage plants and other systems where fluid flow direction or power mode may change.
[F03B 3/103] the same wheel acting as turbine wheel and as pump wheel -- The entry covers machines in which a single wheel functions both as a turbine wheel and as a pump wheel, typically in reaction-type liquid-handling equipment such as hydraulic or hydrostatic systems where the same rotary element extracts energy from a fluid stream and simultaneously imparts energy to move another fluid.
[F03B 3/106] the turbine wheel and the pumps wheel being mounted in adjacent positions on the same shaft in a single casing -- The CPC subclass F03B 3/106 covers reaction-type liquid-handling machines where a turbine wheel and a pump wheel are mounted side-by-side on the same shaft within a single housing. This arrangement integrates power generation and fluid movement in one unit, typical of combined turbine-pump assemblies.
[F03B 3/12] Blades -- F03B 3/12 covers the blades that form the rotating parts of reaction-type liquid machines such as turbines, propellers and impellers. The code addresses the geometry, attachment, and aerodynamic or hydrodynamic shape of these blades, which extract or impart energy to a fluid flow. It is applied to designs where the blade motion is driven by reaction forces from the fluid.
[F03B 3/121] Blades, their form or construction -- F03B 3/121 covers the design, shape and manufacturing aspects of blades used in reaction-type liquid machines or engines such as turbines and pumps. It addresses blade geometry, airfoil profiles, materials and construction methods that affect fluid flow and performance.
[F03B 3/123] specially designed as adjustable blades, e.g. for Kaplan-type turbines -- F03B 3/123 covers machines with adjustable-pitch blades, such as those used in Kaplan-type hydraulic turbines, where the blade angle can be varied to optimise performance over changing flow conditions. The classification applies to designs that allow blade adjustment during operation to improve efficiency and control in reaction-type liquid engines.
[F03B 3/125] Rotors for radial flow at high-pressure side and axial flow at low-pressure side, e.g. for Francis-type turbines -- The CPC entry F03B 3/125 covers rotors used in reaction-type liquid machines where the fluid moves radially on the high-pressure side and axially on the low-pressure side, a configuration typical of Francis-type turbines. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically machines or engines for liquids.
[F03B 3/126] Rotors for essentially axial flow, e.g. for propeller turbines  with adjustable blades  F03B3/14
[F03B 3/128] Mounting, demounting -- F03B 3/128 covers the mounting and demounting of reaction-type liquid-engine machines, such as pumps or turbines, including the fixtures, supports, and procedures used to install, detach, or replace these units. The code applies to designs that facilitate secure attachment and easy removal while maintaining alignment and operational integrity.
[F03B 3/14] Rotors having adjustable blades -- Rotors with adjustable blades are components of reaction-type fluid machines such as turbines, where the blade pitch can be varied during operation to control flow, power output or efficiency.
[F03B 3/145] Mechanisms for adjusting the blades  if the regulation aspect is preponderant,  see   F03B15/00  and subgroups -- F03B 3/145 pertains to blade-adjusting mechanisms in reaction-type machines or engines for liquids where the control or regulation function is dominant; it directs users to the related regulatory group F03B15/00 for further detail.
[F03B 3/16] Stators -- This subclass concerns the stationary component of reaction-type liquid machines, such as pumps, turbines and other fluid engines. It covers the design, construction, mounting and material aspects of stators that interact with the rotating rotor to transfer energy.
[F03B 3/18] Stator blades -- The CPC subclass F03B 3/18 covers stator blades, the stationary air or fluid-flow guiding components in reaction-type machines for liquids such as pumps, turbines, or water-jet engines. It pertains to the design, shape, mounting and function of these fixed blades that shape the fluid path and generate thrust or pressure.
[F03B 3/183] Adjustable vanes, e.g. wicket gates -- Adjustable vanes, also known as wicket gates, are movable flow-directing elements in reaction-type liquid machines such as water turbines. By rotating to vary the opening, they regulate the fluid volume and angle entering the runner, allowing control of speed, power output and efficiency across operating conditions.
[F03B 3/186] Spiral or volute casings -- F03B 3/186 concerns the design of spiral or volute casings for reaction-type liquid machines such as pumps, turbines or mixers, where the casing's helical shape directs the fluid flow and enhances hydraulic efficiency. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically machines or engines for liquids, and addresses the geometry and construction of these fluid-handling housings.
[F03B 5/00] Machines or engines characterised by non-bladed rotors, e.g. serrated, using friction -- F03B 5/00 covers machines or engines that generate power using non-bladed rotors such as serrated surfaces, where mechanical work is produced by friction rather than blades. These devices are classified under mechanical engineering for liquid-handling machines.
[F03B 7/00] Water wheels -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Water wheels which convert the potential energy of the liquid due to height differences, in this case up to a few meters.  The channels conveying the fluid to the water wheel are open to atmosphere.  The traditional distinction between overshot and undershot water wheels refers to whether the point of introduction of the fluid onto the wheel is above or below the axis of rotation of the wheel. This distinction is not reflected (yet) in the classification.  Undershot water wheels use small gradients, but greater that the minimum incline which would still cause flow. They often have no buckets but blades.  An Archimedes screw is also a water wheel in this sense when it extracts power from the potential difference on the fluid.  Examples:  Overshot  Undershot  Archimedes screw
[F03B 7/003] with buckets receiving the liquid -- F03B 7/003 covers water-wheel machines whose buckets are designed to receive the liquid directly as the wheel turns, such as overshoot or breast-wheel configurations where water is fed into the buckets rather than onto a blade or rim. This arrangement allows the weight of the water in the buckets to generate torque for power transmission or pumping. It is a subcategory of mechanical engineering devices for moving liquids.
[F03B 7/006] of the endless-chain type -- This subclass covers water wheels whose driving element is an endless chain that carries the buckets or paddles around the wheel, allowing continuous power transmission from flowing water. The design differs from conventional rim-driven or shaft-driven wheels by using a chain loop to transmit motion to a shaft or other machinery.
[F03B 9/00] Endless-chain machines or engines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines where the collecting and transmitting the energy of a fluid is done with the help of a flexible continuous member closing on itself, which is maintained in place whilst moving with the help of rotating elements.  The flexible continuous member can be made of hinged elements ( as a bicycle chain) or consist of an elongated member also closing on itself, like a rope loop or a belt.  This flexible continuous member closing on itself ( "sans-fin" in French, also internationally known) is held in place whilst it travels by usually two, sometimes more than two, rotating guiding elements, like pulleys or toothed wheels.  Usually the elements directly interacting with the fluid flow (collecting its energy), in the form of buckets or paddles, are attached to the flexible continuous member at regular intervals along its length.  Any device using these elements in F03B is either a water wheel (see F03B7/006 and subdivisions), or a machine using only the kinetic energy of a liquid flow (see F03B17/06 and subdivisions).The group F03B9/00 is therefore not used anymore for classifying new documents. Instead classify in the corresponding water wheel or liquid flow machine groups; that is, any hydraulic motor involving an endless chain arrangement should be classified in either water wheels F03B7/006 or in machines using liquid flow F03B17/06 .
[F03B 9/005] with buckets receiving the liquid -- F03B 9/005 designates endless-chain pumps that incorporate buckets mounted on the chain to receive and transport liquid. The classification applies to machines where the bucket-type receptacles collect fluid as the chain moves, a common arrangement in water-lifting and oil-transfer equipment. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically machines or engines for liquids.
[F03C] POSITIVE-DISPLACEMENT ENGINES DRIVEN BY LIQUIDS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines with reciprocating pistons or rotary or oscillating pistons wherein the working fluid is a liquid.  **Glossary:** - Pump: Device for continuously raising, forcing, compressing, or exhausting fluid by mechanical means - Machine: Device that could equally be both an engine and a pump and not a device which is restricted to an engine or one which is restricted to a pump - Positive displacement pumps: Pumps using pistons or other mechanical members to displace a working fluid in a working chamber, the dynamic effect on the fluid being of minor importance - Positive displacement engines: The energy of a working fluid is transformed into mechanical energy, in which variations of volume created by the working fluid in a working chamber produce equivalent movement of mechanical members, e.g. pistons transmitting the energy, the dynamic effect of the fluid being of minor importance - Oscillating piston machine: Positive-displacement machine in which a fluid-engaging, work-transmitting member oscillates, e.g. a vane piston swinging back and forth about a fixed axis - Reciprocating piston: Fluid-engaging, work-transmitting member of an reciprocating-piston type machine or pump that slides alternately back and forth usually along a straight line or path - Rotary piston: Fluid engaging, work-transmitting member of a rotary-piston machine or pump that can completely rotate about a fixed axis or about an axis moving along a circular or similar orbit when operating, e.g. rotor having vanes or teeth - Free-piston machine: A linear , "crankless" reciprocating piston machine in which the piston motion is not controlled by a crankshaft but determined by the interaction forces from the fluid pressure in the working chamber, a rebound device (e.g. a piston in a closed cylinder) and a load device (e.g. a gas compressor or a linear alternator) - Rotary piston machine: Positive-displacement machine in which a liquid-engaging, work-transmitting member rotates about a fixed axis or about an axis moving along a circular or similar orbit, e.g. machine with a rotor having vanes or teeth - Cooperating members: The "oscillating piston" or "rotary piston" and another member, e.g., the working-chamber wall, which assists in the pumping action or machine's action - Movement of the cooperating members: To be interpreted as relative, i.e. one of the "cooperating members" may be stationary, even though reference may be made to its rotational axis, or both may move - Teeth or tooth equivalents: Includes lobes, projections or abutments - Internal axis type: The rotational axes of the inner and outer co-operating members remain at all times within the outer member, e.g., in a similar manner to that of a pinion meshing with the internal teeth of a ring gear - Working fluid: The driven fluid in a pump or driving or driven liquid in a machine. The working fluid can be in a compressible, gaseous state, e.g. steam, called elastic fluid, a liquid state, or a state where there is coexistence of elastic fluid and liquid state
[F03C 1/00] Reciprocating-piston liquid engines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hydraulic motors wherein an axial or radial piston reciprocates inside a working chamber.
[F03C 1/001] the movement in two directions being obtained by two or more double-acting piston liquid motors -- This subclass covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines where two or more double-acting pistons generate motion in both directions of the crankshaft, providing bidirectional power output. It relates to positive-displacement engines driven by liquids such as hydraulic motors, with pistons doing work during both forward and return strokes.
[F03C 1/002] details; components parts -- It covers the structural details and individual parts of reciprocating piston engines that are driven by liquids, including pistons, cylinders, valves, crank mechanisms and auxiliary components, and their specific construction or arrangement.
[F03C 1/003] controlling -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods and means for controlling the reciprocating piston movement, such as e.g. piston speed-control or piston stroke-control.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Controlling specifically related to multiple-cylinder hydraulic motors having cylinders in tsar or fan arrangement -> F03C1/0447 - Controlling specifically related to multiple-cylinder hydraulic motors having cylinders with axes generally coaxial with, or parallel or inclined to, main shaft axis -> F03C1/0678
[F03C 1/004] speed-control -- The subclass F03C 1/004 covers speed-control arrangements for reciprocating-piston liquid engines, such as throttles, governors and other devices that vary fuel or steam flow to regulate engine speed. It includes mechanisms for adjusting speed in response to load changes or operator commands.
[F03C 1/005] motor piston stroke control -- The F03C 1/005 entry pertains to devices that regulate the stroke length of pistons in reciprocating-piston liquid engines, such as diesel or other positive-displacement engines driven by fluid power. It covers mechanisms that adjust, limit or vary piston travel to control engine performance, efficiency or output.
[F03C 1/007] with single cylinder, double-acting piston -- F03C 1/007 covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines that have a single cylinder in which the piston performs work on both strokes, i.e., a double-acting piston. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, machines or engines for liquids, and positive-displacement engines driven by liquids.
[F03C 1/0073] one side of the double-acting piston being always under the influence of the liquid under pressure -- F03C 1/0073 covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines in which a double-acting piston has one side continuously subjected to pressurised liquid, ensuring constant force on that side throughout the cycle. This design is used in positive-displacement engines such as hydraulic or steam pistons where the fluid pressure drives the piston both forward and backward.
[F03C 1/0076] the liquid under pressure being continuously delivered to one cylinder chamber through a valve in the piston for actuating the return stroke -- This CPC subclass covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines in which pressurised fluid is continuously fed to a single cylinder chamber through a piston-integrated valve to power the return stroke. It falls under mechanical engineering, machines or engines for liquids, positive-displacement engines driven by liquids.
[F03C 1/013] with single cylinder, single-acting piston -- This subclass covers liquid-fuel reciprocating engines that have only one cylinder and use a single-acting piston, meaning the piston receives pressure on only one side during each power stroke. It falls under positive-displacement engines driven by liquids.
[F03C 1/0135] with actuation of the return stroke by gravity -- The subclass F03C 1/0135 pertains to reciprocating-piston liquid engines in which the return stroke of the pistons is driven solely by gravity, rather than by hydraulic or mechanical means. It falls under positive-displacement engines powered by liquids, a subcategory of machines or engines for liquids within mechanical engineering.
[F03C 1/02] with multiple-cylinders, characterised by the number or arrangement of cylinders -- The F03C 1/02 classification concerns reciprocating-piston liquid engines that employ more than one cylinder, focusing on how the cylinders are numbered or arranged. It includes designs such as inline, V-type, opposed-piston and other multi-cylinder configurations where the spatial layout or cylinder count is a defining feature.
[F03C 1/03] with movement in two directions being obtained by two single-acting piston liquid engines, each acting in one direction -- The code F03C 1/03 covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines that achieve bidirectional motion by using two single-acting pistons, each driving the mechanism in one direction. It falls under positive-displacement engines driven by liquids, specifically machines or engines for liquids that move in two opposite directions.
[F03C 1/035] one single-acting piston being always under the influence of the liquid under pressure -- This subclass pertains to reciprocating-piston liquid engines in which a single-acting piston is continuously subjected to the pressure of the fluid throughout its motion, typical of positive-displacement hydraulic engines. It is used to index machines where the working piston receives power from liquid pressure on only one side during each stroke.
[F03C 1/04] with cylinders in star or fan arrangement -- F03C 1/04 covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines in which the cylinders are positioned in a star or fan layout, a subcategory of positive-displacement engines driven by liquids within the broader class of machines or engines for liquids in mechanical engineering.
[F03C 1/0403] Details, component parts specially adapted of such engines -- F03C 1/0403 covers the engineering details and specially adapted component parts of liquid-driven reciprocating-piston engines, such as uniquely shaped pistons, cylinders, valve gear, fuel-injection or lubrication systems and other elements designed to enhance performance, sealing or durability.
[F03C 1/0406] Pistons -- The entry F03C 1/0406 covers pistons used in reciprocating-piston liquid engines, such as diesel or hot-oil engines, where the pistons convert pressure from a working fluid into mechanical motion. It addresses piston design, construction, and features that affect sealing, cooling, and durability within these positive-displacement engines.
[F03C 1/0409] Cams -- F03C 1/0409 designates cams employed in reciprocating-piston liquid engines, a subgroup of positive-displacement engines driven by liquids within mechanical engineering. The code addresses cam shapes, arrangements and mechanisms that convert rotary motion into the reciprocating motion required for piston operation in liquid-fuel engines.
[F03C 1/0412] consisting of several cylindrical elements, e.g. rollers -- The CPC entry F03C 1/0412 covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines whose cylinder is formed by a number of cylindrical elements such as rollers, acting as the moving surface for the piston. This design replaces a solid barrel with a series of rollers that rotate as the piston reciprocates, providing reduced friction and easier sealing.
[F03C 1/0415] Cylinders -- This CPC subclass designates the cylindrical components forming the working chambers of reciprocating-piston engines that are powered by liquids such as hydraulic or steam power. It includes the design, construction, cooling, sealing and arrangement of cylinder barrels, liners and related features in positive-displacement liquid engines.
[F03C 1/0419] Arrangements for pressing or connecting the pistons against the actuated cam -- The code F03C 1/0419 covers designs that press or link the pistons of a reciprocating-piston liquid engine to an actuated cam, ensuring the pistons follow the cam's motion. It includes mechanisms such as cams, followers, levers or connectors that transmit the cam's movement to the pistons for synchronized operation.
[F03C 1/0422] hydraulically -- F03C 1/0422 covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines that are driven directly by hydraulic fluid. The classification includes engines in which pressurised liquid is supplied to a cylinder and acts on the piston to generate power, without intermediate combustion or turbine stages. It is a narrow subclass of positive-displacement engines powered by liquids.
[F03C 1/0425] Disconnecting the pistons from the actuated cam  in general  F01B31/24
[F03C 1/0428] Supporting and guiding means for the pistons -- The entry covers components that keep pistons aligned and stable within reciprocating-piston liquid engines, such as bearings, guides, piston rods and related support structures. These means prevent lateral movement, reduce friction and transmit forces between the piston and the cylinder or crankshaft, ensuring reliable operation of positive-displacement engines driven by liquids.
[F03C 1/0431] Draining of the engine housing; arrangements dealing with leakage fluid -- F03C 1/0431 covers features for removing fluids that collect inside the housing of reciprocating-piston liquid engines, including systems that drain the crankcase or other compartments and arrangements that manage leakage of the working fluid. It addresses how the engine is kept free of excess liquid to maintain performance and reliability.
[F03C 1/0435] Particularities relating to the distribution members  F03C1/0472 ,  F03C1/0531 , and  F03C1/0538  take precedence -- The CPC subclass F03C 1/0435, within mechanical engineering-machines or engines for liquids-positive-displacement engines driven by liquids-reciprocating-piston liquid engines, deals with special features of the distribution members that deliver fuel, air or combustion products. Sub-groups F03C 1/0472, 1/0531 and 1/0538 are given precedence over this broader group.
[F03C 1/0438] to cylindrical distribution members -- A F03C 1/0438 classification covers cylindrical distribution members used in reciprocating-piston liquid engines, such as camshafts, distributor shafts or other round parts that convey fuel, oil or coolant to the engine's cylinders. The element's shape and function are defined by its role in directing fluid flow within the positive-displacement engine.
[F03C 1/0441] to conical distribution members -- The F03C 1/0441 subclass pertains to reciprocating-piston liquid engines and specifies the use of conical distribution members, which are components that direct the working liquid into the engine's cylinders in a cone-shaped pattern. This feature is part of the positive-displacement engine group within the broader category of machines or engines for liquids.
[F03C 1/0444] to plate-like distribution members -- F03C 1/0444 covers plate-like distribution members used in positive-displacement liquid engines, such as the reciprocating-piston pumps and compressors classified under mechanical engineering → machines or engines for liquids → positive-displacement engines driven by liquids. These components are flat or thin plates that direct, spread or separate fluid streams within the engine, for example valve plates, distribution plates or suction plates that ensure even flow to pistons or chambers.
[F03C 1/0447] Controlling -- F03C 1/0447 covers control arrangements for reciprocating-piston liquid engines, such as mechanisms for regulating speed, power output, and valve timing. It includes governors, electronic or hydraulic regulators, and other devices that adjust engine operation in response to load changes or desired performance criteria.
[F03C 1/045] by using a valve in a system with several pump or motor chambers, wherein the flow path through the chambers can be changed, e.g. series-parallel -- F03C 1/045 covers hydraulic or pneumatic positive-displacement engines that use a valve to re-configure the flow among multiple pump or motor chambers, allowing the chambers to be connected in series, parallel or other combinations to vary output characteristics.
[F03C 1/0454] by changing the effective cross sectional piston working surface -- This subclass covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines whose power output is varied by changing the effective cross-sectional area of the piston during operation, for example with telescoping or variable-radius pistons. It is part of the positive-displacement engines driven by liquids.
[F03C 1/0457] by changing the effective piston stroke -- F03C 1/0457 covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines that achieve variable displacement by altering the effective piston stroke, such as mechanisms that shorten or lengthen the stroke during operation to adjust power output, efficiency, or speed. This subclass falls under positive-displacement engines driven by liquids within mechanical engineering.
[F03C 1/046] by changing the excentricity of one element relative to another element
[F03C 1/0463] by changing the phase relationship between two actuated cams -- A positive-displacement liquid engine in which two cams that drive the piston or valve mechanism are phased relative to each other, and the phase relationship can be altered during operation. Varying the cam timing changes the piston motion or valve opening sequence, allowing control of displacement, compression ratio, or power output. This concept is applied to reciprocating-piston engines powered by hydraulic or pneumatic fluid.
[F03C 1/0466] by changing the phase relationship between the actuated cam and the distributing means -- This subclass covers reciprocating piston engines where the timing of fuel or fluid distribution is varied by altering the phase relationship between an actuated cam and the distributing means. The approach adjusts the cam's position relative to the valve or injector to control injection or valve-opening sequences, improving performance or emissions.
[F03C 1/047] the pistons co-operating with an actuated element at the outer ends of the cylinders -- F03C 1/047 relates to reciprocating-piston liquid engines in which the pistons are linked to an actuated element positioned at the outer ends of the cylinders, such as a cam, lever or hydraulic device. The arrangement is intended to control piston motion, timing or sealing by external actuation rather than by the piston's own crankshaft connection.
[F03C 1/0472] with cam-actuated distribution members -- A subclass of positive-displacement piston engines powered by liquid fuel in which the valve or distribution system is operated by cam mechanisms, providing timed opening and closing of intake and exhaust ports.
[F03C 1/0474] with two or more radial piston/cylinder units in series -- The F03C 1/0474 subclass covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines that employ two or more radial piston-cylinder assemblies arranged in series, so that the output of one unit drives the next. This design increases pressure or flow capacity while maintaining the compact radial configuration typical of liquid-driven engines.
[F03C 1/0476] directly located side by side -- This subgroup covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines whose cylinders or pistons are arranged directly adjacent to one another in a side by side layout, typically sharing a common crankshaft or bearing housing. The configuration is used to achieve compactness or balanced forces in positive-displacement engines driven by liquids.
[F03C 1/0478] having several cylinder barrels coupled together -- This group defines reciprocating-piston liquid engines in which the cylinders are arranged as several barrels that are mechanically coupled together. The configuration enables larger displacement, smoother operation, or modular construction by linking multiple cylinder blocks within a single engine.
[F03C 1/053] the pistons co-operating with an actuated element at the inner ends of the cylinders -- This subclass covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines in which the pistons work together with an actuated component located at the inner ends of the cylinders, such as synchronising rods or valves that control piston motion during each power stroke.
[F03C 1/0531] with cam-actuated distribution members -- The subclass F03C 1/0531 pertains to reciprocating-piston liquid engines that employ cam-actuated distribution members for controlling the admission and exhaust of the working fluid. It falls under positive-displacement engines driven by liquids within the mechanical engineering hierarchy.
[F03C 1/0533] each piston being provided with channels coacting with the cylinder and being used as a distribution member for another cylinder -- This subclass concerns reciprocating-piston liquid engines in which each piston is formed with channels that cooperate with the cylinder and act as a distribution member for another cylinder, enabling fluid routing between cylinders. It falls under positive-displacement engines driven by liquids.
[F03C 1/0535] with two or more radial piston/cylinder units in series -- F03C 1/0535 covers positive-displacement liquid engines that use two or more radial piston-cylinder assemblies arranged in series, so the output of one unit feeds the next. This configuration provides staged compression or expansion within a single reciprocating-piston engine.
[F03C 1/0536] directly located side by side -- The F03C 1/0536 sub-group covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines whose cylinders are positioned directly side by side, sharing a common crankshaft or bearing arrangement without intervening structures, allowing compact layout and balanced operation.
[F03C 1/0538] the piston-driven cams being provided with inlets or outlets -- This CPC subclass relates to piston-driven cam mechanisms that incorporate inlet or outlet ports for fluid flow in reciprocating-piston liquid engines. It covers designs where the cam is part of the piston assembly and provides charging, exhaust or cooling passages within positive-displacement engines driven by liquids.
[F03C 1/06] with cylinder axes generally coaxial with, or parallel or inclined to, main shaft axis -- This subclass covers liquid-fuel positive-displacement engines whose cylinder axes are aligned with, parallel to, or inclined relative to the main shaft axis, such as in marine diesel or other reciprocating piston engines where the cylinders run alongside the crankshaft.
[F03C 1/0602] Component parts, details -- F03C 1/0602 concerns the individual component parts and detailed sub-assemblies of reciprocating-piston liquid engines, such as pistons, connecting rods, valves, crankshafts and related mechanisms, focusing on their design, construction and functional role within positive-displacement engines driven by liquids.
[F03C 1/0605] Adaptations of pistons  pump pistons  F04B1/124 ,  F04B53/14 -- F03C 1/0605 covers design changes to the pistons of reciprocating-piston liquid engines, including pump pistons, such as alterations to shape, sealing arrangements, cooling features or motion-transfer mechanisms that improve performance or durability. It cross-references related piston adaptations in the F04B series for fluid-driven pumps.
[F03C 1/0607] Driven means -- F03C 1/0607 covers the means that provide the driving power to reciprocating-piston liquid engines, including crankshaft and cam mechanisms as well as hydraulic or pneumatic drives that convert liquid pressure into piston motion.
[F03C 1/061] having stationary cylinders -- A reciprocating-piston liquid engine in which the cylinders remain fixed while the pistons move within them, typical of conventional internal-combustion engines. The class covers designs that employ stationary cylinders as part of a positive-displacement system driven by liquids.
[F03C 1/0613] having two or more sets of cylinders or pistons -- F03C 1/0613 classifies reciprocating-piston liquid engines that use two or more cylinder or piston groups, such as twin- or multi-cylinder configurations, to increase power output or balance forces within the engine. This falls under positive-displacement engines driven by liquids.
[F03C 1/0615] distributing members -- Distributing members are components in reciprocating-piston liquid engines that allocate the working fluid-such as steam, water or oil-to the individual cylinders or chambers in a controlled sequence, ensuring proper timing and pressure distribution for efficient engine operation.
[F03C 1/0618] cylindrical distribution members -- Cylindrical distribution members are tube-like parts that channel liquid between cylinders or between the crankcase and cylinders in reciprocating-piston liquid engines. They may include distribution sleeves, manifolds or ports that are shaped as cylinders and serve to equalise pressure or supply fuel or lubricating oil.
[F03C 1/0621] conical distribution members -- F03C 1/0621 covers conical distribution members used in reciprocating-piston liquid engines. These are conical-shaped parts that direct or spread the working liquid from the cylinder to intake or exhaust passages, improving flow uniformity and reducing pressure losses. The code falls under mechanical engineering, machines or engines for liquids, positive-displacement engines driven by liquids.
[F03C 1/0623] Details, component parts -- The subclass F03C 1/0623 catalogs the detailed features and individual component parts of liquid-fuel reciprocating-piston engines, such as pistons, cylinders, valves, crankshafts, and related fittings, focusing on their design, construction and functional aspects within positive-displacement engines driven by liquids.
[F03C 1/0626] Cylinders -- The code F03C 1/0626 covers the cylinders of reciprocating-piston liquid engines, such as piston-type steam or hydraulic machines. It pertains to the design, construction, sealing and arrangement of the cylinder chambers where the pistons move to convert fluid pressure into motion.
[F03C 1/0628] Casings, housings -- F03C 1/0628 relates to the design and construction of casings and housings that enclose reciprocating-piston engines powered by liquids. The classification covers structural elements that contain the moving pistons, support bearings, and provide sealing, cooling and mechanical strength for the engine assembly.
[F03C 1/0631] Wobbler or actuated element -- The CPC subclass F03C 1/0631 covers wobblers or actuated elements used in reciprocating-piston liquid engines. These are devices that create a periodic rocking or tilting motion of a piston, valve or other moving part to influence fluid flow or combustion within the engine.
[F03C 1/0634] Actuated element bearing means or driven axis bearing means -- The subclass F03C 1/0634 concerns bearing arrangements for the moving components of reciprocating-piston liquid engines, specifically the supports that locate, guide or carry loads on the actuated element or the driven shaft axis. It addresses how these bearings are used within positive-displacement engines driven by liquids.
[F03C 1/0636] having rotary cylinder block -- The code F03C 1/0636 denotes reciprocating-piston liquid engines that employ a rotary cylinder block, meaning the whole cylinder assembly rotates around a stationary crankshaft. This design is found mainly in certain marine diesel engines and similar power units where a rotating engine housing provides balanced forces and a compact layout.
[F03C 1/0639] having two or more sets of cylinders or pistons -- This subclass of the CPC covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines that incorporate two or more cylinder or piston groups, allowing multiple displacement stages or parallel operation. Such configurations can improve power output, balance forces, or enable variable stroke arrangements.
[F03C 1/0642] inclined on main shaft axis -- The CPC entry F03C 1/0642 covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines in which the cylinder or piston assembly is inclined relative to the main shaft axis, rather than being parallel. This configuration is used to adapt the engine layout, improve balance or accommodate space constraints within the overall machine design.
[F03C 1/0644] Component parts -- The F03C 1/0644 entry covers individual component parts used in reciprocating-piston engines that are driven by liquids, such as pistons, cylinders, valves, crankshafts and connecting rods. It classifies the separate mechanical elements that make up positive-displacement liquid-driven engines within the broader machine-engineering hierarchy.
[F03C 1/0647] Particularities in the contacting area between cylinder barrel and valve plate -- The CPC code F03C 1/0647 covers design details of the contact region between the cylinder barrel and the valve plate in reciprocating-piston liquid engines, such as pumps or hydraulic motors. It addresses specific geometries, surface treatments, sealing arrangements or machining features intended to improve sealing, wear resistance or fluid flow at that interface.
[F03C 1/0649] Bearing means -- The classification F03C 1/0649 relates to the bearing means used in reciprocating-piston liquid engines, which are positive-displacement engines driven by liquids such as water or hydraulic fluid. It covers the design, arrangement and supporting structures that locate and guide the moving pistons, crankshaft or other rotating parts within the engine housing.
[F03C 1/0652] Cylinders -- This subclass defines the structure, materials and features of cylinders used in positive-displacement liquid engines, such as piston-driven machines, covering aspects like shape, cooling, sealing and mounting. It falls within mechanical engineering of machines or engines for liquids.
[F03C 1/0655] Valve means -- F03C 1/0655 defines the valve arrangements used in reciprocating-piston liquid engines, covering the design and placement of intake, exhaust, and control valves that regulate the flow of working fluid within positive-displacement engines driven by liquids. It includes valve stems, seats, guides and associated sealing or timing mechanisms.
[F03C 1/0657] Cylindrical valve means -- A cylindrical valve means is a valve with a cylindrical body that slides or rotates to open or close passages, used in reciprocating-piston liquid engines to control the admission and exhaust of fluid. It is classified under F03C 1/0657, a subgroup of positive-displacement engines driven by liquids within the broader mechanical-engineering hierarchy.
[F03C 1/066] Conical valve means -- Conical valve means are tapered valve bodies that seat against a complementary cone-shaped seat to control the flow of liquid in a reciprocating-piston engine. Their shape provides a self-centering seal and reduces wear, allowing efficient intake or exhaust timing in positive-displacement hydraulic or steam engines.
[F03C 1/0663] Casings, housings -- F03C 1/0663 relates to the design and construction of casings and housings for reciprocating-piston liquid engines, which are positive-displacement machines powered by fluid pressure. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically machines or engines for liquids, and addresses structural components that contain and support the pistons, cylinders and associated fluid passages.
[F03C 1/0665] Cylinder barrel bearing means -- The F03C 1/0665 entry deals with bearing arrangements that support and locate the cylinder barrel in reciprocating-piston engines powered by liquids. It includes plain, roller, or bushing bearings and related mounting structures that transmit forces between the barrel and the engine block while minimizing wear.
[F03C 1/0668] Swash or actuated plate -- F03C 1/0668 covers swash or actuated plates used in reciprocating-piston liquid engines. The plate is an angled or movable element that translates the linear motion of pistons into rotary motion, or vice versa, and may also control valve timing in hydraulic or marine engines. It is a key component of positive-displacement engines driven by liquids.
[F03C 1/0671] Swash or actuated plate bearing means or driven axis bearing means -- The F03C 1/0671 entry covers bearing arrangements for the driven shaft or axis of reciprocating-piston liquid engines that use a swash plate, an actuated plate, or similar bearing structures to support and guide the moving components. These designs provide controlled motion and load distribution within positive-displacement engines powered by liquids.
[F03C 1/0673] Connection between rotating cylinder and rotating inclined swash plate -- The subclass F03C 1/0673 covers mechanisms that link a rotating cylinder to a rotating inclined swash plate in reciprocating-piston liquid engines. It details the design of shafts, gears, or coupling arrangements that transmit motion between the two rotating components, enabling conversion of fluid power into piston motion.
[F03C 1/0676] Arrangement for pressing the cylinder barrel against the valve plate -- An arrangement that forces the cylinder barrel firmly against the valve plate, improving the seal and preventing fluid leakage in reciprocating-piston liquid engines. The pressing action is typically achieved by springs, hydraulic pressure or mechanical levers to maintain tight contact during operation.
[F03C 1/0678] Control -- The CPC subclass F03C 1/0678 pertains to control devices and methods for liquid-driven reciprocating-piston engines, covering systems that regulate fuel or working-fluid delivery, valve timing, speed, and other operating parameters to optimise performance, efficiency, and safety. It falls under mechanical engineering, machines or engines for liquids, positive-displacement engines driven by liquids.
[F03C 1/0681] using a valve in a system with several motor chambers, wherein the flow path through the chambers can be changed -- This subclass relates to liquid-driven positive-displacement engines with multiple motor chambers that use a valve to alter the fluid flow path among the chambers, allowing the engine to switch between different power-distribution or operating modes by rerouting the working liquid.
[F03C 1/0684] using a by-pass valve -- This subclass covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines that incorporate a bypass valve to divert fluid flow, typically to control pressure, improve starting, or protect the pump. The valve may be used for priming, reducing load, or emergency shut-off.
[F03C 1/0686] by changing the inclination of the swash plate -- A subclass of the CPC covering reciprocating-piston liquid engines in which the engine's swash plate is tilted to vary its inclination, thereby modifying piston stroke or timing. It resides under mechanical engineering → machines or engines for liquids → positive-displacement engines driven by liquids.
[F03C 1/0689] using wedges -- F03C 1/0689 covers reciprocating-piston engines driven by liquids, such as steam, in which wedges are incorporated into the piston, valve or crank mechanisms to improve sealing, transmit motion or reduce wear.
[F03C 1/0692] by changing the phase relationship between the actuated element and the distribution means, e.g. turning the valve plate; turning the swash plate -- It covers liquid-driven reciprocating-piston engines in which the phase relationship between the moving piston (or other actuated element) and the fluid-distribution mechanism is varied, for example by rotating a valve plate or a swash plate, to adjust timing, power output or efficiency.
[F03C 1/0694] by changing the inclination of the axis of the cylinder barrel in relation to the axis of the actuated element -- The subclass F03C 1/0694 refers to reciprocating-piston liquid engines whose cylinder barrel is inclined relative to the axis of the actuated element, thereby changing the geometry of the working space. This design feature is used to influence valve timing, combustion, or power transmission in positive-displacement engines driven by liquids.
[F03C 1/0697] responsive to the speed -- The CPC entry F03C 1/0697 covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines that incorporate control means responding to the engine's speed, such as governors, speed sensors or variable-timing devices. It pertains to adjustments of fuel delivery, valve timing or other operating parameters based on measured rotational speed.
[F03C 1/08] Distributing valve-gear peculiar thereto -- The class covers the valve mechanisms that allocate fluid to and from the cylinders of reciprocating-piston liquid engines, detailing the specific distributing gear-such as cams, levers or eccentric drives-used to control inlet and outlet timing in these positive-displacement engines.
[F03C 1/10] actuated by piston or piston-rod -- This subclass covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines in which the motion is produced directly by the piston or a piston-rod rather than by external mechanisms. It includes designs where the piston's linear movement drives the engine's output, typical of hydraulic or steam piston engines.
[F03C 1/12] mechanically -- F03C 1/12 pertains to reciprocating-piston liquid engines whose piston motion is generated mechanically, for example by a crankshaft, cam, or gear train, rather than by hydraulic or pneumatic means. The sub-class addresses the mechanical power-transmission arrangements used in positive-displacement engines that operate on liquid fuel.
[F03C 1/14] by driving liquid of engine -- A subclass of reciprocating-piston liquid engines that are powered directly by the liquid used to drive the engine, such as oil, water or other working fluids, rather than by combustion gases or steam. It covers designs where the driving liquid provides the pressure that moves the pistons to produce mechanical work. This classification groups engines that employ the liquid itself as the motive medium.
[F03C 1/16] Speed controlling, equalising or cushioning -- F03C 1/16 covers devices that regulate, balance or dampen the speed of reciprocating-piston liquid engines, such as hydraulic or pneumatic pistons. The classification includes governors, speed equalisers and cushioning mechanisms that control rotation rates, prevent overspeed and smooth out fluctuations during operation. It is applied to engines that convert liquid pressure into reciprocal motion.
[F03C 1/20] specially adapted for engines generating vibration only -- F03C 1/20 classifies reciprocating-piston liquid engines that are specially adapted for operation in which vibration is the only output, covering design features that manage, exploit or tolerate such vibration. It lies under mechanical-engineering categories for machines or engines for liquids, specifically positive-displacement engines driven by liquids.
[F03C 1/22] with movable cylinders -- F03C 1/22 classifies reciprocating-piston liquid engines in which the cylinders themselves move during operation, such as engines with sliding, rocking or otherwise mobile cylinders that convert fluid power to mechanical motion by shifting the cylinder block or individual cylinders relative to a fixed crankshaft or other parts.
[F03C 1/223] having cylinders in star or fan arrangement, the connection of the pistons with an actuated element being at the inner ends of the cylinders -- The code F03C 1/223 covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines in which the cylinders are arranged in a star or fan pattern and the pistons are linked to an actuated element at the inner ends of the cylinders.
[F03C 1/226] with cam actuated distribution members -- This subclass covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines whose inlet, exhaust or fuel-distribution elements are driven by cams. It includes designs where cam-actuated valves, ports or injection devices control the timing and flow of the working liquid within positive-displacement engines.
[F03C 1/24] in which the liquid exclusively displaces one or more pistons reciprocating in rotary cylinders -- F03C 1/24 covers positive-displacement liquid engines in which the working fluid acts solely to push one or more pistons that move reciprocally within rotating (rotary) cylinders. The fluid's pressure directly drives the pistons without intermediate gear or crank mechanisms, typical of hydraulic motors and certain pump-type engines.
[F03C 1/2407] having cylinders in star or fan arrangement, the connection of the pistons with an actuated element being at the outer ends of the cylinders -- F03C 1/2407 pertains to reciprocating-piston liquid engines that feature a star- or fan-shaped cylinder layout, with the pistons connected to an actuated element at the outer ends of the cylinders. This arrangement is classified under mechanical engineering for machines or engines for liquids, specifically positive-displacement engines driven by liquids.
[F03C 1/2415] cylinder block and actuated cam both rotating  F03C1/2431  and  F03C1/2446  take precedence -- This subclass covers positive-displacement liquid engines with reciprocating pistons in which the cylinder block is designed to rotate together with an actuated cam, rather than remaining stationary. The rotating arrangement is defined for configurations that fall under the more specific groups F03C 1/2431 and F03C 1/2446, which have priority over this entry.
[F03C 1/2423] with two or more series radial piston-cylinder units -- F03C 1/2423 covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines that use two or more series of radial piston-cylinder units, meaning the pistons are arranged around a central crankshaft in several concentric rows. This configuration is used to increase power output while keeping engine size compact.
[F03C 1/2431] cylinder block and actuated cam both rotating  F03C1/2446  takes precedence
[F03C 1/2438] directly located side by side -- F03C 1/2438 covers positive-displacement liquid-driven engines where two or more reciprocating pistons are placed directly side by side, sharing a common crankshaft and often a common combustion chamber.
[F03C 1/2446] cylinder block and actuated cam both rotating -- This CPC sub-group covers cylinder blocks in which the cam is actuated and rotates together with the block, used in reciprocating-piston liquid engines. It pertains to positive-displacement engines driven by liquids where the cam and cylinder block are integrated to rotate as a unit.
[F03C 1/2454] having cylinders in star or fan arrangement, the connection of the pistons with an actuated element being at the inner ends of the cylinders -- The subclass F03C 1/2454 covers reciprocating-piston liquid engines whose cylinders are grouped in a star or fan configuration, with the pistons connected to an actuated element at the inner ends of the cylinders. This arrangement falls under positive-displacement engines driven by liquids within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy.
[F03C 1/2462] the rotary cylinder being provided with only one piston reciprocating within this cylinder -- The subclass F03C 1/2462 covers liquid-driven positive-displacement engines in which a single piston reciprocates inside a rotary cylinder, rather than using multiple pistons. It relates to reciprocating-piston engines where the cylinder rotates while the lone piston moves back and forth.
[F03C 1/247] with cylinders in star- or fan-arrangement -- F03C 1/247 classifies reciprocating-piston liquid engines in which the cylinders are arranged in a star- or fan-shaped pattern, a layout that shortens the engine's overall length and helps balance reciprocating forces. This falls under mechanical engineering → machines or engines for liquids → positive-displacement engines driven by liquids.
[F03C 1/26] adapted for special use or combined with apparatus driven thereby -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hydraulic motors which are primarily defined by their use, or their specific combination, with an other apparatus, e.g. for driving said apparatus.
[F03C 2/00] Rotary-piston engines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hydraulic motors wherein the rotary-piston moves in a single direction inside the working chamber including "cat and mouse" type hydraulic motors.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Rotary-piston engines in which the liquid exclusively displaces one or more piston reciprocating in rotary cylinders -> F03C1/24
[F03C 2/02] of arcuate-engagement type, i.e. with circular translatory movement of co-operating members, each member having the same number of teeth or tooth-equivalents -- F03C 2/02 pertains to rotary-piston engines of the arcuate-engagement type, in which cooperating members execute a circular translatory motion and each member bears the same number of teeth or tooth-equivalent features.
[F03C 2/08] of intermeshing-engagement type, i.e. with engagement of co- operating members similar to that of toothed gearing -- This subclass covers rotary-piston engines in which the moving parts engage each other by intermeshing, much like the teeth of gears, to transmit power generated by a liquid drive.
[F03C 2/22] of internal-axis type with equidirectional movement of co-operating members at the points of engagement, or with one of the co-operating members being stationary, the inner member having more teeth or tooth- equivalents than the outer member -- This subclass defines internal-axis rotary-piston engines where the cooperating gear-like members move in the same direction at their points of engagement, or where one member remains fixed, and the inner member possesses more teeth or tooth-equivalents than the outer member. It narrows the broader category of positive-displacement engines driven by liquids to a specific tooth-ratio arrangement in the rotating elements.
[F03C 2/24] of counter-engagement type, i.e. the movement of co-operating members at the points of engagement being in opposite directions -- In the CPC, code F03C 2/24 designates rotary-piston engines of the counter-engagement type, where the interacting components move in opposite directions at their points of engagement, a variant within positive-displacement liquid-driven engines.
[F03C 2/30] having the characteristics covered by two or more of groups -- F03C 2/30 classifies rotary-piston positive-displacement engines powered by liquids that incorporate features defined in two or more of the adjoining subgroups. It is used when an invention combines characteristics of multiple rotary-piston engine categories rather than fitting a single, more specific group.
[F03C 2/302] having both the movements defined in sub-groups  F03C2/02  and relative reciprocation between members -- F03C 2/302 covers rotary-piston engines that combine the motions defined in sub-group F03C2/02 with a relative back-and-forth reciprocation between the engine members.
[F03C 2/304] having both the movements defined in sub-group  F03C2/08  or  F03C2/22  and relative reciprocation between members
[F03C 2/306] having both the movements defined in sub-groups  F03C2/22  and  F03C2/24 -- F03C 2/306 relates to rotary-piston engines that combine the two characteristic motions defined in sub-groups F03C 2/22 and F03C 2/24, meaning the piston executes both an axial or reciprocating displacement and an orbital or eccentric rotation within the same device, allowing liquid-driven power to be converted into rotary motion.
[F03C 2/308] having the movement defined in  F03C2/08  and having a hinged member -- F03C 2/308 relates to rotary-piston positive-displacement engines driven by liquids in which the piston motion is defined by a hinged member, such as a crank or cam, as specified in the broader group F03C2/08. This subclass captures designs where the hinge controls the rotary-to-reciprocating conversion.
[F03C 4/00] Oscillating-piston engines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hydraulic motors in which the rotary-piston moves back and forth inside the working chamber.
[F03C 7/00] Engines of flexible-wall type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hydraulic motors having flexible working members, such as membranes, diaphragm, or flexible tubes, e.g. peristaltic motors.
[F03C 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hydraulic motors having working principles not provided for in other groups, and components or parts specifically adapted for hydraulic motors.
[F03C 99/005] Free-piston type engines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Linear fluid engines, in which the piston motion is determined only by the interaction of fluid forces within a cylinder, rebound means and/or a load means, but without mechanical connection between the piston and a crankshaft.
[F03D] WIND MOTORS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mechanisms for converting the energy of wind into useful mechanical power.  In particular:  Wind motors with rotation axis substantially parallel to the flow of air entering the machine.  Wind motors with rotation axis substantially at right angle to the flow of air entering the machine.  Other wind motors.  Controlling wind motors.  Adaptations of wind motors for special use.  Combinations of wind motors with apparatus driven thereby.  Wind motors specially adapted for installation in particular locations.  Assembly, mounting or commission of wind motors.  Arrangements specially adapted for transporting wind motor components.  Transmission of mechanical power.  Monitoring or testing of wind motors.  Other details, component parts, or accessories of wind motors.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Electric power networks; Circuit arrangements or systems for supplying or distributing electric power; Systems for storing electric energy -> H02J - Control or regulation of electric generators, or dynamo-electric converters -> H02P  **Glossary:** - Motor: An apparatus for producing mechanical motion from any other form of energy; the motion may be continuous or in separate strokes - Rotor: The wind-engaging parts of the wind motor and the rotary member carrying them - Rotation axis: The axis of rotation of the rotor
[F03D 1/00] Wind motors with rotation axis substantially parallel to the air flow entering the rotor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Wind motors having their rotation axis substantially parallel to the flow of air entering the machine. When the flow has been guided into a direction other that the original wind direction, the final flow into the wind motor should be considered for classification.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Controlling of wind motors -> F03D7/02
[F03D 1/02] having a plurality of rotors -- The subgroup F03D 1/02 pertains to wind-motor devices whose rotation axis runs substantially parallel to the incoming airflow and that incorporate two or more rotors, such as twin-rotor or multi-rotor wind turbines, to boost aerodynamic capture and torque output.
[F03D 1/025] coaxially arranged -- The F03D 1/025 subclass covers wind-driven motors whose rotor axis is essentially parallel to the incoming airflow and that are arranged coaxially, meaning the rotor and any accompanying components share the same central axis. This configuration is used for streamlined, efficient wind energy conversion.
[F03D 1/04] having stationary wind-guiding means, e.g. with shrouds or channels -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Wind motors having stationary wind-guiding means. The nacelle can be the wind guiding means, tip-shrouds are excluded.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Wind motors specially adapted for installation within towers -> F03D9/35

[F03D 1/0601] using the Magnus effect -- F03D 1/0601 concerns wind motors whose rotation axis is essentially parallel to the incoming airflow and that generate power by exploiting the Magnus effect, where spinning surfaces create lift perpendicular to the wind. These designs use rotating cylinders or specially shaped blades to convert wind energy into mechanical rotation.

[F03D 1/0679] Load carrying structures, e.g. beams -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Wind turbine blades having an outer structure, e.g. shells, skins, which via its shape defines the aerodynamic property of the blade and an inner structure that carries the structural loads and provides stiffness. The inner structure may include beams, spar caps and shear webs as illustrated below.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this group:
[F03D 1/0681] Spar caps -- F03D 1/0681 concerns spar caps, the longitudinal structural members that reinforce the blades of wind-turbine rotors whose axis is essentially parallel to the incoming air stream. The classification places these components within mechanical engineering, specifically machines or engines for liquids and wind motors, highlighting their role in bearing loads and maintaining blade rigidity.
[F03D 1/0682] incorporated into the shell structure -- This class covers wind motors whose rotor axis runs substantially parallel to the incoming air flow and that are built into the surrounding shell or housing of a device. The integration may involve embedding the turbine within the outer skin of a vehicle, building façade, or other structural enclosure, so that the wind-driven rotor forms part of the shell rather than being a separate external component.
[F03D 1/0684] Shear webs -- Shear webs are internal reinforcing plates or web structures incorporated in wind-motor rotors whose axis runs substantially parallel to the incoming airflow. They transmit shear forces between blades and the hub, stiffening the rotor and improving load distribution while permitting a lightweight design.

[F03D 1/0691] of the hub -- This subclass (F03D 1/0691) deals with the hub of wind-driven machines whose rotation axis is substantially parallel to the incoming air flow. It covers hub design, attachment and structural features specific to such wind motors within the broader category of mechanical engineering, machines or engines for liquids, and wind motors.

[F03D 1/125] Access thereto, e.g. doors or platforms -- F03D 1/125 covers provisions for accessing wind motors whose rotor axis is substantially parallel to the incoming airflow, including doors, platforms, ladders and other service openings that enable inspection, maintenance or repair.
[F03D 1/141] Supporting structures, i.e. load carrying structures -- This subclass covers the structural components that support wind motor rotors whose shaft is parallel to the incoming airflow, including frames, brackets and mounts that bear mechanical loads. It addresses designs that transmit forces from the rotor to foundations or other support elements. The classification helps identify patents dealing with load-bearing arrangements for such wind motors.
[F03D 1/165] Fairings, i.e. elements defining or supporting the outer surface -- F03D 1/165 covers fairings - components that define or support the outer surface of wind motors whose rotation axis runs substantially parallel to the incoming airflow. These elements shape the rotor's exterior to reduce aerodynamic drag, protect internal parts and improve overall efficiency of horizontal-axis wind turbines.

The classification organises patents related to the set-up of wind motors within the broader mechanical-engineering category of machines or engines for liquids.

[F03D 13/30] Commissioning, e.g. inspection, testing or final adjustment before releasing for production -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Inspection, testing or adjustment of the wind motor before operating the wind motor to produce power.
[F03D 13/35] Balancing static or dynamic imbalances -- F03D 13/35 covers methods and devices for correcting static or dynamic imbalances in wind turbines during assembly, mounting or commissioning. It includes balancing techniques, mass adjustment, and vibration monitoring to ensure smooth operation.
[F03D 13/40] Arrangements or methods specially adapted for transporting wind motor components -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Transporting or storing of wind motor components.

[F03D 15/00] Transmission of mechanical power -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Transmission of mechanical power, i.e. mechanisms by which the wind-derived mechanical power is conveyed to its point of use
[F03D 15/05] using hollow exhausting blades -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Air flow through the blades, e.g. windmills creating a flow of air through the blade by their rotation.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[F03D 15/10] using gearing not limited to rotary motion, e.g. with oscillating or reciprocating members -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Wind motors transmitting mechanical power to their power generating device with a gear or gearbox. The gear is of any type, including rotary, as well as a rack and pinion gearing.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[F03D 15/101] of the epicyclic or planetary type -- Group F03D15/101 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups F03D15/00 , F03D15/10 and F05B2260/40311 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 15/15] Changing or adjusting stroke -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Wind motors having the stroke length of a pump cylinder adjusted. The length of stroke is typically adjusted relative to the speed of the wind.
[F03D 15/20] Gearless transmission, i.e. direct-drive -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearless transmission arrangements in which the rotor shaft is directly coupled to the generator without intermediate gearing or other drive elements.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  **Glossary:** - Direct-drive: Gearless transmission arrangement in which the rotor shaft is attached directly to the generator without intermediate gearing.
