[F03D 17/00] Monitoring or testing of wind motors, e.g. diagnostics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Monitoring or testing of wind motors, e.g. diagnostics or inspection.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Testing during commissioning of wind motors -> F03D13/30  **Glossary:** - Monitoring or testing: Overseeing or analysing the operating conditions of the wind motor mechanism, typically to identify problematic conditions. This may involve reference to wind motor sensor signals indicating, e.g. wind speed and direction, pitch angle, vibration, rotary torque, blade load and strain.
[F03D 17/001] Inspection -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Inspection of the wind motor at the installation site. Inspection is performed on-demand or on a scheduled basis rather than a continuous monitoring.  The use of occupied vehicles, e.g. boats, helicopters for personnel to reach wind motor inspection sites.
[F03D 17/002] by testing or performing a safety check -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Inspecting or checking the operation of the wind motor when implemented with a controlled input to verify the wind motor is performing to its specifications.
[F03D 17/003] characterised by using optical devices, e.g. lidar or cameras -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The use of optical devices during the inspection process.
[F03D 17/004] by using remote inspection vehicles, e.g. robots or drones -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The inspection process being performed or assisted by the use of remote vehicles, e.g. remotely controlled robots or unmanned aerial vehicles.
[F03D 17/005] using computation methods, e.g. neural networks -- Groups F03D17/005 , F03D17/006 and F03D17/0065 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups F03D17/00 , F05B2260/80 , F05B2260/82 , F05B2260/821 , F05B2260/8211 and F05B2260/84 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 17/006] Estimation methods -- F03D 17/006 covers estimation methods applied to wind motors, focusing on techniques that predict performance metrics, efficiency, load or fault likelihood during monitoring or testing activities. These methods support diagnostics by providing calculated values for parameters such as power output, blade stress or aerodynamic behavior.
[F03D 17/0065] for diagnostics -- This CPC subclass covers methods and devices used to monitor, test or diagnose the performance and condition of wind-driven rotary machines. It includes diagnostic systems that detect faults, assess efficiency and provide real-time condition monitoring of wind motors. The subclass falls under the broader group for machines or engines for liquids, specifically wind motors.
[F03D 17/007] Wind farm monitoring -- Group F03D17/007 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group F03D17/00 . Groups F03D17/00 and F03D17/007 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 17/008] through wake analysis -- Group F03D17/008 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups F03D17/00 and F05B2270/204 . Groups F03D17/00 , F05B2270/204 and F03D17/008 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 17/009] characterised by the purpose -- Groups F03D17/009 and F03D17/014 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups F03D17/00 and F05B2260/80 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 17/011] for monitoring mechanical loads or assessing fatigue; for monitoring structural integrity -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The use of sensors to measure variables such as stress and strain which represent forces exposed on components of the wind motor.  Diagnosing the remaining useful life of a wind motor component.  Monitoring the wind motor with respect to the initiation and propagation of cracks due to cyclic loading.
[F03D 17/012] for monitoring wear or clearance -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Monitoring the wind motor with respect to wear or clearance. Excessive wear can cause for clearances between parts to be outside of acceptable ranges.
[F03D 17/013] for detecting abnormalities or damage -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Detection of an abnormality or damage in the wind motor. Faults or failures are considered an extreme case of abnormalities and damage, i.e. the wind motor is still operable with the detection of an abnormality or damage, whereas a fault or failure detection indicates the inoperability of the wind motor. Detecting faults or failures are covered by subgroup F03D17/014 .
[F03D 17/014] indicative of a fault or failure -- F03D 17/014 covers methods and devices for monitoring or testing wind turbines that give an indication of a fault or failure. It includes diagnostic sensors, condition-monitoring systems and data-analysis techniques used to detect abnormal performance in wind-motor drive trains.
[F03D 17/015] for monitoring vibrations -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Monitoring wind motors with respect to a measured vibration.
[F03D 17/016] indicative of rotor imbalance -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The vibration value is representative of the rotor imbalance.
[F03D 17/017] Natural frequencies or oscillations -- Group F03D17/017 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups F03D7/0296 and F03D17/00 . Groups F03D7/0296 , F03D17/00 and F03D17/017 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 17/018] for monitoring temperature -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Monitoring temperature of wind motor elements.
[F03D 17/021] for monitoring power or current -- Group F03D17/021 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group F03D17/00 . Groups F03D17/00 and F03D17/021 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 17/022] for monitoring displacement -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sensors or methods for determining and monitoring variables such as length or distance between components of the wind motor.
[F03D 17/024] for monitoring sound or acoustic signals -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sensors or methods for determining and monitoring noises emitted by the wind motor.
[F03D 17/025] for calibrating -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Monitoring or diagnosing an error or offset of the wind motor, such as the yaw error, and implementing a correction to the measured error.
[F03D 17/026] for assessing power production capabilities -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Estimating or predicting the power producing capabilities of the wind motor, e.g. based on predicted wind speed or on wind motor operability. Analysis of operating the wind motor above the power curve or above its rated power for a length of time.
[F03D 17/027] characterised by the component being monitored or tested -- Group F03D17/027 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups F03D17/00 and F05B2260/80 . Groups F03D17/00 , F05B2260/80 and F03D17/027 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 17/028] Blades -- Group F03D17/028 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups F03D17/00 , F05B2260/80 , F05B2270/17 , F05B2270/33 , F05B2270/332 , F05B2270/333 and F05B2270/334 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 17/0285] Surfaces thereof -- Group F03D17/0285 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups F03D17/00 , F05B2270/332 , F05B2270/333 and F05B2270/334 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 17/029] Blade pitch or yaw drive systems, e.g. pitch or yaw angle -- Group F03D17/029 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups F03D7/0224 , F03D17/00 , F05B2270/328 , F05B2270/329 , F05B2270/332 , F05B2270/333 and F05B2270/334 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 17/031] Shafts -- Group F03D17/031 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups F03D17/00 , F05B2270/332 , F05B2270/333 and F05B2270/334 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 17/032] Bearings -- Group F03D17/032 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups F03D17/00 , F05B2260/80 , F05B2270/332 , F05B2270/333 and F05B2270/334 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 17/033] Gearboxes -- Group F03D17/033 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups F03D17/00 , F05B2270/332 , F05B2270/333 and F05B2270/334 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 17/034] Towers -- Group F03D17/034 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups F03D17/00 , F05B2260/80 , F05B2270/17 , F05B2270/33 , F05B2270/332 , F05B2270/333 and F05B2270/334 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 17/036] Generators -- Group F03D17/036 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups F03D17/00 , F05B2260/80 , F05B2270/3032 , F05B2270/332 , F05B2270/333 and F05B2270/334 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 3/00] Wind motors with rotation axis substantially perpendicular to the air flow entering the rotor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Wind motors having their rotation axis substantially perpendicular to the flow of air entering the machine. When the flow has been guided into a direction other that the original wind direction, the final flow into the wind motor should be considered for classification.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Controlling of wind motors -> F03D7/06
[F03D 3/002] the axis being horizontal -- The subclass F03D 3/002 covers wind-driven machines whose rotor axis is essentially horizontal, meaning it is substantially perpendicular to the incoming airflow. It applies to wind motors that use this orientation to convert wind energy into mechanical power or electricity.
[F03D 3/005] the axis being vertical -- Group F03D3/005 is impacted by reclassification into groups F03D3/009 and F03D3/011 . Groups F03D3/005 , F03D3/009 and F03D3/011 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 3/007] using the Magnus effect -- F03D 3/007 pertains to wind motors whose rotor axis is essentially perpendicular to the incoming air stream and that generate thrust by exploiting the Magnus effect, where a rotating body creates lift perpendicular to the flow. It falls under mechanical engineering, machines or engines for liquids, and the wind-motor subclass.
[F03D 3/009] of the drag type, e.g. Savonius -- Group F03D3/009 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups F03D3/005 , F05B2240/213 , F05B2240/215 and F05B2240/216 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 3/011] of the lift type, e.g. Darrieus or Musgrove -- Group F03D3/011 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups F03D3/005 , F05B2240/212 and F05B2240/214 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 3/02] having a plurality of rotors -- The F03D 3/02 subclass covers wind-motor designs in which the rotor axis is substantially perpendicular to the incoming air flow and the machine incorporates multiple rotors. It is used for classifying wind-turbine configurations that employ a plurality of rotors to capture energy from the wind.
[F03D 3/04] having stationary wind-guiding means, e.g. with shrouds or channels -- **Definition:** This place covers:  A wind-guiding means positioned upstream, downstream or around a wind motor. The wind-guiding means directs wind to the rotor, or blocks wind from reaching a part of the rotor. The wind-guiding means may have a movement which is independent from the rotation of the rotor.
[F03D 3/0409] surrounding the rotor  F03D3/0427  takes precedence
[F03D 3/0418] comprising controllable elements -- This class covers wind turbines whose rotor axis is essentially perpendicular to the incoming air flow and that incorporate controllable elements such as pitch-adjustable blades, yaw mechanisms or variable-speed controls to regulate power output and operating characteristics.
[F03D 3/0427] with converging inlets, i.e. the guiding means intercepting an area greater than the effective rotor area  F03D3/0463 ,  F03D3/049  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Wind motors characterised by the "effective area" of the rotor which is the cross section area of the rotor which contributes positively, i.e. causes a torque in/ to the desired rotation. The "augmenting" guiding means are those guiding means which intercept an area of flow greater than this "effective rotor area", and direct the flow into the rotor. The guiding means which fulfil this intercepting role are bounded on all sides from inlet to outlet into rotor, and therefore also accelerate or "concentrate" the flow into the rotor.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Guiding means forming also a shield means on one side of the rotor -> F03D3/0463 , F03D3/049
[F03D 3/0436] for shielding one side of the rotor -- The F03D 3/0436 subclass covers wind-motor designs in which the rotation axis is essentially perpendicular to the incoming air flow and includes provisions for shielding only one side of the rotor. This shielding may serve aerodynamic, structural or noise-reduction purposes while leaving the opposite side exposed.
[F03D 3/0445] the shield being fixed with respect to the wind motor -- The subgroup classifies wind motors whose protective shield is permanently attached to the turbine rather than rotating with the rotor. In these machines the shield is fixed to the stator, tower or housing and does not move relative to the rotor, serving to guide airflow or provide safety.
[F03D 3/0454] and only with concentrating action, i.e. only increasing the airflow speed into the rotor, e.g. divergent outlets  F03D3/0463  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Wind motors with a concentrating means, i.e. the guiding means only increase the flow speed into the rotor without intercepting an area of flow greater than the effective rotor area (see definition of the effective area in F03D3/0427 ).  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  See channel 12  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - With augmenting action, i.e. the shield means intercepting an area greater than the effective rotor area -> F03D3/0463
[F03D 3/0463] with converging inlets, i.e. the shield intercepting an area greater than the effective rotor area -- This subclass covers wind-driven machines whose rotor spins on an axis essentially perpendicular to the incoming air stream and that employ a converging inlet or shroud that captures a flow area larger than the rotor's swept area, directing the air onto the blades.
[F03D 3/0472] the shield orientation being adaptable to the wind motor -- F03D 3/0472 covers wind-motor designs in which the protective shield or shroud can be adjusted relative to the rotor, allowing the orientation of the shield to be varied for optimal performance. It falls under mechanical engineering, machines or engines for liquids, wind motors with an axis essentially perpendicular to the incoming air flow.
[F03D 3/0481] and only with concentrating action, i.e. only increasing the airflow speed into the rotor, e.g. divergent outlets  F03D3/049  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass (F03D 3/0481) concerns wind motors whose rotation axis is essentially perpendicular to the incoming airflow and that employ only a concentrating action to raise the air-speed into the rotor, such as divergent outlet designs. It is a narrower facet of the broader F03D 3/049 category, which takes precedence.
[F03D 3/049] with converging inlets, i.e. the shield intercepting an area greater than the effective rotor area -- The subclass F03D 3/049 covers wind motors whose rotation axis is essentially perpendicular to the incoming air flow and that use a converging inlet or shroud whose opening area exceeds the effective rotor area, channeling a larger air volume onto the rotor to improve aerodynamic performance.
[F03D 3/06] Rotors -- F03D 3/06 covers wind-motor rotors in which the rotation axis is substantially perpendicular to the incoming air flow, defining devices that harness wind energy with a transverse-axis orientation. It falls under mechanical engineering, machines or engines for liquids, and specifically wind motors.
[F03D 3/061] characterised by their aerodynamic shape, e.g. aerofoil profiles -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Rotors characterised by their shape or form which is defining the aerodynamic property of the rotor.
[F03D 3/062] characterised by their construction elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Constructional aspects of the rotors.
[F03D 3/063] Telescoping or folding wind engaging parts -- Group F03D3/063 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups F03D3/062 and F05B2240/2021 . Groups F03D3/062 , F05B2240/2021 and F03D3/063 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 3/064] Fixing wind engaging parts to rest of rotor -- F03D 3/064 covers the means for attaching the wind-engaging components, such as blades or rotor disks, to the remainder of a wind-motor rotor whose axis of rotation is essentially perpendicular to the incoming air flow. The code applies to mechanical joining methods, fastening devices, or integral designs used in wind turbines and other perpendicular-axis wind motors.
[F03D 3/066] the wind engaging parts being movable relative to the rotor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Wind motors where the wind engaging parts can be moved relative to the rotor, e.g. arrangements allowing pitching the wind engaging parts independently from the mechanical rotation of the rotor.
[F03D 3/067] Cyclic movements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Wind motors wherein the wind engaging parts, i.e. the blades, vanes, paddles or cups, undergo a change of orientation within each cycle of rotation of the wind rotor, coming back to their original position after each revolution. This change of orientation happens of itself, passively, the direct result of the wind acting on the wind engaging parts as the relative wind direction changes with the rotation.  Example:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[F03D 3/068] mechanically controlled by the rotor structure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Wind motors whereby the cyclic movement of the wind engaging parts during each revolution of the rotor is mechanically related to the rotor rotation, and optionally the wind force or direction.  Examples:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:

[F03D 5/005] Wind motors having a single vane which axis generate a conus or like surface -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[F03D 5/015] of the tethered aircraft type, e.g. kites, with traction and retraction of the tether  controlling thereof  F03D7/051 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Tethered aircraft, e.g. kite or wings, having a particular flying pattern and getting energy from traction and retraction movement of the tether.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Controlling thereof -> F03D7/051

[F03D 7/00] Controlling wind motors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of wind motors and related control arrangements.  F03D7/02 and F03D7/06 covers essentially the two main types of wind motors according to groups F03D1/00 and F03D3/00 . F03D7/051 covers the control of wind motors according to groups F03D5/015 and F03D9/322 . The head group F03D7/00 covers all other types of wind motors.  Subgroups F03D7/0204 - F03D7/0296 cover the purpose of the control or the type of control actuators. Subgroups under F03D7/04 cover the type of controllers and/or the control methods.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Electric power networks; Circuit arrangements or systems for supplying or distributing electric power; Systems for storing electric energy -> H02J - Controlling electric generators -> H02P
[F03D 7/02] the wind motors having rotation axis substantially parallel to the air flow entering the rotor -- Group F03D7/02 is impacted by reclassification into group F03D7/0202 . Groups F03D7/02 and F03D7/0202 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 7/0202] controlling floating wind motors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling wind motors, e.g. pitch, yaw, power output, while taking under consideration the behaviour of the floating support structure, e.g. ensuring wind motor stability or taking into account wave induced movements of the supporting platform.
[F03D 7/0204] for orientation in relation to wind direction -- Group F03D7/0204 is impacted by reclassification into groups F03D7/0202 and F03D1/181 . Groups F03D7/0204 , F03D7/0202 and F03D1/181 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 7/0208] Orientating out of wind -- The F03D 7/0208 entry covers methods and devices for turning wind-driven machines away from the wind direction, such as yaw-control systems that rotate the rotor or nacelle to keep it out of the airflow. It includes mechanisms that detect wind direction and automatically adjust the orientation of wind motors for safety, maintenance or shut-down purposes.
[F03D 7/0212] the rotating axis remaining horizontal -- The classification covers wind-motor designs in which the rotor shaft is kept horizontal during operation, often by using a fixed-axis arrangement, a yaw-control system that maintains level orientation, or mechanical linkages that prevent tilting as wind speed varies. This ensures the rotating axis remains parallel to the ground while the turbine extracts energy from the wind.
[F03D 7/0216] the rotating axis changing to vertical position -- The subclass deals with devices that re-orient the shaft of a wind-motor from a horizontal to a vertical alignment, enabling control of blade pitch or turbine yaw. It covers mechanical or hydraulic arrangements that shift the rotating axis to a vertical position for performance or safety adjustments.
[F03D 7/022] Adjusting aerodynamic properties of the blades -- F03D 7/022 covers techniques for adjusting the aerodynamic characteristics of wind-motor blades, including methods, devices and control systems that modify blade pitch, twist, shape or surface features to optimise performance, power output or load response.
[F03D 7/0224] Adjusting blade pitch -- Group F03D7/0224 is impacted by reclassification into groups F03D1/0664 , F03D1/0667 , F03D1/0669 , F03D1/0671 , F03D7/0202 , F03D17/029 and F03D80/701 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 7/0228] of the blade tips only -- This classification covers devices or methods that adjust or regulate wind-motor performance by acting solely on the blade tips. It includes tip-based pitch systems, tip brakes or other tip-located actuators that change angle or aerodynamic load without affecting the rest of the blade.
[F03D 7/0232] with flaps or slats  with aerodynamic drag devices on the blades for braking  F03D7/0252 -- Group F03D7/0232 is impacted by reclassification into groups F03D1/06495 and F03D1/0685 . Groups F03D7/0232 , F03D1/06495 and F03D1/0685 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 7/0236] by changing the active surface of the wind engaging parts, e.g. reefing or furling -- Group F03D7/0236 is impacted by reclassification into groups F03D7/0202 , F03D7/0237 and F03D7/0239 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.

[F03D 7/024] of individual blades -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling each blade individually or independently, e.g. modulating the pitch angle based on the rotational angle of the blade to compensate for gravity or vertical wind shear.
[F03D 7/0244] for braking -- The code F03D 7/0244 covers braking systems for wind motors, detailing devices and methods that slow or halt the rotation of wind-driven turbines, typically integrated into the control mechanisms of these machines.
[F03D 7/0248] by mechanical means acting on the power train -- The F03D 7/0248 subcategory includes wind-motor control arrangements that operate the power-train through mechanical elements such as gears, clutches, brakes or other linkages. It pertains to mechanical engineering of machines or engines for liquids, specifically the control of wind motors by direct mechanical action on their transmission components.
[F03D 7/0252] with aerodynamic drag devices on the blades -- F03D 7/0252 relates to wind turbines that incorporate aerodynamic drag devices on the blades for controlling rotor speed, braking or pitch regulation, thereby adjusting the turbine's power output and protecting it from overspeed conditions.
[F03D 7/0256] Stall control  adjusting the blades in stall position  F03D7/0224
[F03D 7/026] for starting-up -- F03D 7/026 covers devices or methods that provide the initial power to start a wind motor, enabling the turbine to begin rotation before wind energy can be harvested. It falls under mechanical engineering, machines or engines for liquids, wind motors, and specifically the control of wind motors.
[F03D 7/0264] for stopping; controlling in emergency situations  orientating out of wind  F03D7/0208 -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Orientating out of wind -> F03D7/0208
[F03D 7/0268] Parking or storm protection -- The subclass F03D 7/0268 covers arrangements and control strategies that secure wind-turbine generators when they are not operating or during severe weather, including parking positions, braking, blade pitch or furling systems designed to minimise damage from storms.
[F03D 7/0272] by measures acting on the electrical generator -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of the generator to control the rotor, e.g. for regulating the rotor output torque or its speed. This can be done in various ways, e.g. control of the converter or of the generator speed.
[F03D 7/0276] controlling rotor speed, e.g. variable speed -- F03D 7/0276 concerns methods and devices for controlling the speed of a wind-turbine rotor, particularly variable-speed operation. It includes systems that adjust blade pitch, generator load or power-electronics to maintain a desired rotational speed, and falls under mechanical engineering → machines or engines for liquids → wind motors → controlling wind motors.
[F03D 7/028] controlling wind motor output power -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling the output power of the wind rotor when acting on the rotor itself, i.e. by actuating some rotor or generator elements.
[F03D 7/0284] in relation to the state of the electric grid -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of the wind motor based on grid demand.
[F03D 7/0288] in relation to clearance between the blade and the tower, i.e. preventing tower strike -- Group F03D7/0288 is impacted by reclassification into group F03D7/028 . Groups F03D7/0288 and F03D7/028 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 7/0292] to reduce fatigue -- F03D 7/0292 covers inventions that use control techniques or device features to lessen fatigue in wind-motor components such as blades, shafts, gearboxes or other rotating parts, by monitoring loads and adjusting operation to mitigate stress cycles. This subclass lies within mechanical engineering, specifically machines for liquids and wind motors, and focuses on fatigue-reduction measures in wind-turbine control systems.
[F03D 7/0296] to prevent, counteract or reduce noise emissions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling wind motors to prevent, counteract or reduce noise, e.g. modifying the control strategy and reducing rotor speed during night time.
[F03D 7/0298] to prevent, counteract or reduce vibrations -- Groups F03D7/0298 and F03D7/0302 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups F03D7/0296 and F05B2270/334 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03D 7/0302] of the tower -- The F03D 7/0302 subclass covers inventions that relate to the tower of a wind-motor system. It includes features such as tower height, structural design, positioning or adjustment methods that influence how the turbine is supported and controlled. This falls under the broader field of mechanical engineering for machines or engines that handle liquids, specifically the control of wind motors.
[F03D 7/04] Automatic control -- F03D 7/04 pertains to automatic control of wind motors, covering systems that regulate turbine speed, orientation or power output without manual input. It includes sensor-based feedback and actuator mechanisms that continuously adjust operation to optimize performance.
[F03D 7/041] by means of a mechanical governor -- F03D 7/041 covers wind motors that are regulated by a mechanical governor, where the device adjusts blade pitch or speed to maintain constant output despite wind variations. The classification applies to machines using mechanical linkage or centrifugal weight systems for speed control.
[F03D 7/042] by means of an electrical or electronic controller -- F03D 7/042 covers wind-motor systems in which the turbine's performance is regulated by an electrical or electronic controller. This includes variable-speed drives, pitch-control mechanisms, power-converter units and other electronic devices that adjust rotor speed, blade angle or generated power to match wind conditions or grid requirements.
[F03D 7/043] characterised by the type of control logic -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control methods specially adapted to wind motors.
[F03D 7/044] with PID control -- F03D 7/044 falls under Mechanical Engineering > Machines or Engines for Liquids > Wind Motors and covers wind-motor systems that use proportional-integral-derivative (PID) control to regulate speed, torque or power output, ensuring stable operation despite variable wind conditions. This subclass is used to classify patents focusing on the implementation of PID algorithms in wind-energy conversion devices.
[F03D 7/045] with model-based controls -- F03D 7/045 pertains to wind motors equipped with model-based control systems, where a mathematical model of the turbine is used to predict and adjust its operation for improved efficiency and response. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, machines or engines for liquids, wind motors, and addresses advanced control strategies for regulating wind-driven machinery.
[F03D 7/046] with learning or adaptive control, e.g. self-tuning, fuzzy logic or neural network
[F03D 7/047] characterised by the controller architecture, e.g. multiple processors or data communications -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control hardware or remote computing specially adapted to wind motors, e.g. redundant controllers, multi-processors architecture, decentralized cloud computing.  Data communication between SCADA system, turbine controllers or actuators.
[F03D 7/048] controlling wind farms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Conjoint control of two or more wind motors located in relatively close proximity.

[F03D 9/00] Adaptations of wind motors for special use -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Adaptations of wind motors for special use;  Combinations of wind motors with means for converting solar radiation into useful energy;  Combinations of wind motors with water energy converters, e.g. water turbines;  Combinations of wind motors with an apparatus storing energy;  Wind motors being characterised by the driven apparatus, e.g. heat pump, hydraulic pump, compressor, or electric generator;  Wind motors specially adapted for installation in particular locations, e.g. moving objects, stationary objects, infrastructure, or using landscape.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Hybrid wind-photovoltaic energy systems for the generation of electric power -> H02S10/12
[F03D 9/007] the wind motor being combined with means for converting solar radiation into useful energy -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Solar panels built in or on the blades or tower.
[F03D 9/008] the wind motor being combined with water energy converters, e.g. a water turbine -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[F03D 9/10] Combinations of wind motors with apparatus storing energy -- The subclass F03D 9/10 covers wind-motor systems that are combined with devices for storing the generated energy, such as batteries, flywheels, or hydraulic accumulators. It includes arrangements where the wind turbine is integrated with the storage apparatus to provide a continuous or buffered power output for special applications.
[F03D 9/11] storing electrical energy -- F03D 9/11 covers wind-motor designs that incorporate means for storing the electrical energy they generate, such as integrated batteries, super-capacitors or flywheels. It includes adaptations where the generated power is retained on-board for later use rather than being fed directly to an external grid.
[F03D 9/12] storing kinetic energy, e.g. using flywheels -- F03D 9/12 covers devices and methods for storing the kinetic energy produced by wind motors, chiefly by means of rotating flywheels. It includes systems that capture excess rotational power and release it when needed, enabling smoother output or backup supply for wind-driven machinery.
[F03D 9/13] storing gravitational potential energy -- This subclass covers wind-driven systems that convert kinetic energy into stored gravitational potential, such as mechanisms that lift weights, raise water, or move masses for later release. The designs are adaptations of wind motors intended for special uses like energy-storage, load-balancing or backup power. It includes any arrangement where the wind turbine directly powers a device that stores energy by raising an object against gravity.
[F03D 9/14] using liquids -- F03D 9/14 classifies adaptations of wind-driven machines that employ liquids, such as hydraulic drives, fluid couplings or water-based lubrication systems, for special purposes. It covers modifications where the kinetic energy of wind is transferred to or managed by liquid media to operate or improve the performance of wind motors.
[F03D 9/16] using weights -- This CPC subclass covers wind motors that employ weight systems to modify rotor speed, torque or orientation for special applications such as load-regulation, pitch control or stability enhancement. It includes designs where movable masses are used to adapt the turbine's operation under varying wind conditions.
[F03D 9/17] storing energy in pressurised fluids -- The CPC subclass F03D 9/17 covers methods and devices for storing energy in pressurised fluids, typically in systems that convert wind-driven mechanical power into compressed air or other fluids for later use. It includes adaptations of wind motors designed for this purpose, such as compressed-air storage tanks and associated control mechanisms.
[F03D 9/18] storing heat -- F03D 9/18 covers wind-driven machines that incorporate thermal-energy storage devices, allowing excess mechanical power to be converted into heat and retained for later use. This enables wind motors to operate continuously or supply heat to auxiliary processes when wind is insufficient, and includes various storage media such as water, phase-change materials, or rocks integrated into the turbine structure.
[F03D 9/19] storing chemical energy, e.g. using electrolysis -- F03D 9/19 covers wind-motor installations that are adapted to store the generated power as chemical energy, for example by using the turbine's electricity to electrolyse water and produce hydrogen for later use. It is a special-use adaptation of wind motors within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy.
[F03D 9/20] Wind motors characterised by the driven apparatus -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Combinations of wind motors with apparatus storing energy -> F03D9/10
[F03D 9/22] the apparatus producing heat -- This subclass covers wind-driven devices modified to generate heat, such as wind turbines integrated with heating elements or heat exchangers, used for space heating, water warming, or industrial processes. It includes adaptations that convert the kinetic energy of wind into thermal energy.

[F03D 9/34] on stationary objects or on stationary man-made structures -- F03D 9/34 covers wind-driven machines that are mounted on fixed installations rather than on mobile platforms. It includes turbines installed on buildings, towers, bridges, offshore platforms, or any other stationary man-made structures, detailing adaptations for such permanent mounting.

[F03D 9/43] using infrastructure primarily used for other purposes, e.g. masts for overhead railway power lines -- This subclass covers wind-motor adaptations that use existing structures, such as masts for overhead railway power lines, to mount the turbine on infrastructure primarily intended for other purposes. It addresses special-use configurations where the wind-driven machine is integrated with non-dedicated supports to reduce space requirements and installation costs.
[F03D 9/45] Building formations -- F03D 9/45 covers adaptations of wind motors for special use, specifically building formations. It classifies inventions that use wind-driven engines to generate forces or motions that assist in constructing, supporting or shaping structures such as towers, roofs or movable façades. The code is nested under mechanical engineering, machines or engines for liquids, wind motors.
[F03D 9/46] Tunnels or streets -- F03D 9/46 pertains to wind-motor adaptations that are incorporated into tunnels or streets, typically for purposes such as ventilation, air-flow control or on-site power generation within underground passages, roadways or similar urban conduits. It covers the specific design features needed to install and operate wind turbines in these confined or street-level environments.
[F03D 9/48] using landscape topography, e.g. valleys -- This subclass covers wind-motor designs that exploit natural terrain features such as valleys or ridges to enhance airflow and power generation. It includes arrangements that position turbines within depressions or along slopes to capture channelled wind.
[F03G] SPRING, WEIGHT, INERTIA OR LIKE MOTORS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Spring, gravity, inertia or like motors. Devices or mechanisms for producing power, e.g. mechanical or electric power, from muscle energy, or solar energy, or not otherwise provided for, or using energy sources not otherwise provided for.  **Glossary:** - Motor: mechanism for producing mechanical power from potential energy of solid bodies

[F03G 3/00] Other motors, e.g. gravity or inertia motors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Motors driven by gravity or inertia force, e.g. driven by solid falling bodies like bowls, or by solid falling materials like sand, or by out of balance bodies like pendulums, or by the change of the orientation of the rotation axis of a rotating body.
[F03G 3/02] using wheels with circumferentially-arranged compartments co-operating with solid falling bodies -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Wheels cooperating with solid falling bodies coming from outside of the wheel and causing an imbalance on and a rotation of the wheel. [F03G 3/087] Gravity or weight motors  using solid falling bodies  F03G3/02 ; using sand or the like  F03G3/04 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Motors using the reduction of potential energy of a weight for generating a rotational movement.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Using solid falling bodies -> F03G3/02 - Using sand or the like -> F03G3/04
[F03G 3/091] using unbalanced wheels -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The wheel is unbalanced and thus the wheel rotation is generated by moving the gravity point of one or several masses within the wheel between a position close to the wheel rotation axis and a position far from the wheel rotational axis. For the movement of the mass or masses, a specific input of energy is required.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a device for converting mechanical energy into electrical energy, in which a weight (23-26) is arranged so as to be periodically displaceable by mechanical, hydraulic or pneumatic means against the direction of gravity, in which further at least one duct (11-14) extending in a diametral direction and arranged in a circular frame rotatable about its middle transverse axis is provided. In this arrangement the frame (10) is drive connected at the circumference to at least one electrical generator (21, 22).  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a rotary device of the unbalanced wheel type with weights actuated by hydraulic motors.
[F03G 3/094] specially adapted for potential energy power storage stations; combinations of gravity or weight motors with electric motors or generators -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The potential energy previously stored in a weight is converted into rotational energy, in particular for driving a generator, e.g. during high demand of electricity, by moving down the weight. The process is reversed, e.g. during low demand of electricity and the generator is used as an electric motor that rotates a shaft and shifts up the respective weight.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[F03G 3/096] adapted for pumping or conveying fluids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Weight or inertia motors specifically adapted for pumping or conveying fluids.
[F03G 3/097] Motors specially adapted for creating a reciprocating movement, e.g. by rotating eccentric masses -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  The following illustrations show the purpose: To permit a propulsion plate to be transferred to an arbitrary position on a plane by installing each pair of vibration generating apparatuses opposed in the perpendicular directions on a same propulsion plate and adjusting the number of revolution of each eccentric motor.
[F03G 4/00] Devices for producing mechanical power from geothermal energy -- **Glossary:** - Steam: Water in the gas phase - Vapour: Substance, not being steam, in the gas phase - Working fluid: The fluid that converts heat into mechanical work in a turbine - Source fluid: The fluid in thermal contact with the geothermal well. This fluid is either in direct fluid and thermal contact with the well or only in thermal contact with the well. - Rankine Cycle: During conversion of heat into mechanical work, a phase change of the working fluid is involved. - Brayton cycle: Open or closed thermodynamic cycle of a constant pressure heat engine, e.g. of the gas turbine - Stirling Cycle: Closed, reversible and regenerative thermodynamic cycle with a gaseous working fluid
[F03G 4/001] Binary cycle plants where the source fluid from the geothermal collector heats the working fluid via a heat exchanger -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Binary cycle plants whereby the source fluid is in contact with the geothermal well and whereby the working fluid forms separate circuits that are thermally connected to each other via a heat exchanger.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[F03G 4/02] with direct fluid contact -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Single-cycle plants where the source fluid is the working fluid.  In such a single cycle power plant there is only one fluid cycle. This implies that the source fluid extracting the heat from the geothermal well and the working fluid typically driving a steam turbine are the same.  In this place, it is not relevant if the source fluid as such is in direct contact with the geothermal well rock or not. In this place, the source fluid can be either in direct contact with the rock or could flow through a piping that is only in thermal exchange with the rock.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  2.
[F03G 4/023] characterised by the geothermal collectors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The geothermal collector with the source fluid is in contact with the geothermal well.
[F03G 4/026] open loop geothermal collectors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The source fluid in the geothermal collector is in fluid contact with the geothermal well.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[F03G 4/029] closed loop geothermal collectors, i.e. the fluid is pumped through a closed loop in heat exchange with the geothermal source, e.g. via a heat exchanger -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Closed-loop geothermal collectors. The source fluid flows through a piping that is in thermal exchange with the rock. There is no direct contact between the source fluid and (dry) rock in the geothermal well.  In this place, both closed-loop geothermal collectors being part of a binary-cycle-type ( F03G4/001 ) as well as of a single-cycle-type geothermal power plant ( F03G4/02 ) are covered.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1. Closed-loop geothermal collector of the binary-cycle type  2. Closed-loop geothermal collector of the single-cycle type
[F03G 4/031] using waste heat from a primary power cycle to produce mechanical power in a second cycle -- **Definition:** This place covers:  There are at least two working fluid circuits, which are fluidically separated but thermally connected via a heat exchanger.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[F03G 4/033] having a Rankine cycle -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The working fluid being subject to a phase change from liquid to vapour, e.g. water turning to steam.
[F03G 4/035] of the Organic Rankine Cycle [ORC] type or the Kalina Cycle type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  An Organic Rankine Cycle uses an organic fluid, like CFC, HFC, HC, PFC, with a boiling point lower than the water boiling point.  In a Kalina cycle, the conversion of heat into mechanical power is realized by using a solution of two fluids with different boiling points, the most widely used combination being water and ammonia.
[F03G 4/037] having other power cycles, e.g. Stirling, transcritical or supercritical cycles; combined with other power sources, e.g. wind, gas or nuclear -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this group:
[F03G 4/04] with deep-well turbo pump -- The subclass F03G 4/04 covers geothermal power installations that employ a deep-well turbo pump to raise hot fluids from subterranean sources. It includes arrangements where the pump is integrated with the turbine or generator to convert the geothermal energy directly into mechanical power.
[F03G 4/045] with heat pumps -- F03G 4/045 covers geothermal power systems that incorporate heat-pump technology to convert low-grade thermal energy into mechanical work. The classification includes devices that use a heat pump cycle to raise the temperature of geothermal fluid, driving a turbine or other power-producing machine. These systems are used for electricity generation and cogeneration applications.
[F03G 4/06] with fluid flashing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Causing evaporation by lowering a fluid's pressure below its vapour pressure.
[F03G 4/063] with energy storage devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Geothermal power plants with thermal storage devices.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[F03G 4/066] of the non-thermal type, e.g. springs or batteries -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Geothermal power plants with non-thermal storage devices.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[F03G 4/069] characterised by the brine or scale treatment, e.g. brine desalination, scale deposit prevention or corrosion-proofing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Treatment of accumulating brine (salt-water) or scale (chalky deposit caused by water evaporation).  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  The following example shows a method for producing geothermal power using geothermal brines while producing a reduced silica and iron brine having improved injectivity. The resulting compositions include a composition with reduced silica, iron, and lithium having reduced quantity of total suspended solids.  Method for inhibiting scale and geothermal power generating device
[F03G 4/072] Controlling or monitoring -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control or monitoring of geothermal power plants.
[F03G 4/074] Safety arrangements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Safety arrangements of geothermal power plants.
[F03G 5/00] Devices for producing mechanical power from muscle energy -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for producing mechanical power from human or animal muscle energy and being of the endless-walk type, e.g. treadmills, or other than of the endless-walk type, e.g. pedal generators.

[F03G 5/045] Security devices for horsemills -- Security devices for horsemills are mechanisms that protect against accidental start-up, overload, or misuse of horse-driven power units, typically incorporating brakes, lock-outs, and overload-release elements to ensure safe operation while the animal provides the mechanical energy.
[F03G 5/047] Transmissions or couplings for horsemills -- This subclass covers mechanical devices that transmit power from a horse-driven mill to other machinery, such as gear trains, belts, chains or coupling mechanisms linking the horse-mill shaft to auxiliary equipment. They are used to adapt the rotational speed or torque of the horse-mill for varied applications.

[F03G 5/066] of the motor-generator type, e.g. the generator is also usable as an electric motor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for producing mechanical power from muscle energy other than of endless-walk type driven by humans and having a combined motor-generator.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  Illustrated is an electric bike with capability of self-charging. The electric bike is fixedly supported by supporting legs so as to be operated in a stationary point. A user steps on a petal assembly of the electric bike to drive a wheel to rotate. The electric bike further includes a power-generating device, a controller, a battery and a damper. The controller is used to receive a power source outputted by the power-generating device and convert the power source into a plurality of DC power sources to charge the battery. The damper consumes a portion of electricity generated by the power-generating device. With the above arrangement, the electric bike can be charged by itself while the user is exercising.

[F03G 6/00] Devices for producing mechanical power from solar energy -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for producing mechanical power from solar energy, e.g. with photovoltaic cells, or solar collectors with or without concentration of solar rays, the devices using a single-state working fluid, e.g. Stirling cycle, or involving a phase change of the working fluid (Rankine cycle), e.g. a steam-water cycle.  **Glossary:** - Steam: Water in the gas phase. - Vapour: Substance, not being steam, in the gas phase - Working fluid: The fluid that converts heat into mechanical work in a turbine. - Heating fluid: The fluid in thermal contact with the solar collectors or concentrators. - Rankine Cycle: During conversion of heat into mechanical work a phase change of the working fluid is involved. - Brayton cycle: Open or closed thermodynamic cycle of a constant pressure heat engine, e.g. of the gas turbine. - Stirling Cycle: Closed, reversible and regenerative thermodynamic cycle with a gaseous working fluid.
[F03G 6/001] having photovoltaic cells -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Solar thermal power plant and with photovoltaic cells.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[F03G 6/002] with expansion and contraction elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Solar power plants with expansion and contraction elements, no matter if there are means for concentrating the solar rays or not.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  The figure illustrated above shows a solar-powered device (500) that includes a central hub (520); plural curved blades (400) attached to the central hub (520); and plural weights (310) connected to the plural curved blades (400). A curved blade (400i) of the plural curved blades (400) has a proximal end attached to the central hub (520) and a distal end attached to a corresponding weight (310), and wherein the curved blade (400i) is made of a material that changes its geometry when exposed to solar energy.
[F03G 6/003] having a Rankine cycle  F03G6/065  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for producing mechanical power from solar energy having a Rankine cycle and without means for concentrating the solar rays, e.g. solar collectors. There is only one fluid circuit for the collectors and the turbine.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Devices for producing mechanical power from solar energy with means for concentrating solar rays and having a Rankine cycle -> F03G6/065
[F03G 6/004] of the Organic Rankine Cycle [ORC] type or the Kalina Cycle type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for producing mechanical power from solar energy having an Organic Rankine cycle or a Kalina cycle and without means for concentrating the solar rays, e.g. solar collectors. There is only one fluid circuit for the collectors and the turbine.  An Organic Rankine cycle uses an organic fluid, like CFC, HFC, HC, PFC, with a boiling point lower than the water boiling point.  In a Kalina cycle, the conversion of heat into mechanical power is realized by using a solution of two fluids with different boiling points, the most widely used combination being water and ammonia.
[F03G 6/005] Binary cycle plants where the fluid from the solar collector heats the working fluid via a heat exchanger -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for producing mechanical power from solar energy having a Rankine cycle and without means for concentrating the solar rays, e.g. with solar collectors. There are at least two fluid cycles involved (i.e. binary cycle). The heating fluid, sometimes also called the intermediate fluid, e.g. water or a molten salt, is the fluid transporting the heat from the solar field to at least one heat exchanger, e.g. a preheater or vaporizer, the heat exchanger transferring the heat to the separate working fluid cycle. The working mechanical/electrical power. The Rankine cycle refers to the working fluid circuit.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[F03G 6/0055] having other power cycles, e.g. Stirling or transcritical, supercritical cycles; combined with other power sources, e.g. wind, gas or nuclear -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for producing mechanical power from solar energy without solar concentration and having non-Rankine power cycles.
[F03G 6/02] using a single state working fluid -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for producing mechanical power from solar energy using a single state working fluid, e.g. air or ammonia gas, with or without means for concentrating solar rays and with or without an intermediate fluid for heat transfer.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  The following illustration showing an apparatus for generating electricity by using solar energy is provided. The main structure includes a circulation pipeline 1 and a hydroelectric generator 4. The circulation line 1 is filled with water or other convective water. The circulation line 1, partially submerged below the sea surface 10, is partially exposed above the sea surface 10. A portion of the circulation pipeline 1 submerged below the sea surface 10 forms a cooling water tank 3. A portion of the circulation pipeline 1 exposed above the sea surface 10 forms a heat-absorbing water tank 2 whose outer circumference is covered with a heat-absorbing layer. A water outlet is formed above the heat absorbing water tank 2, and a water inlet is formed below. A water turbine generator 4 is provided near the water outlet of the heat-absorbing water tank 2.

[F03G 6/066] of the Organic Rankine Cycle [ORC] type or the Kalina Cycle type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for producing mechanical power from solar energy having an Organic Rankine cycle or a Kalina cycle and with means for concentrating the solar rays, e.g. parabolic linear or point concentrators. There is commonly only one fluid circuit between the collectors and the turbine.  An Organic Rankine cycle uses an organic fluid, like CFC, HFC, HC, PFC, with a boiling point lower than the water boiling point.  In a Kalina cycle the conversion of heat into mechanical power is realized by using a solution of two fluids with different boiling points, the most widely used combination being water and ammonia.
[F03G 6/067] Binary cycle plants where the fluid from the solar collector heats the working fluid via a heat exchanger -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for producing mechanical power from solar energy having a Rankine cycle and with means for concentrating the solar rays, e.g. with parabolic linear or point concentrators. There are at least two fluid cycles involved (i.e. binary cycle). The heating fluid, sometimes also called the intermediate fluid, e.g. water or a molten salt, is the fluid transporting the heat from the solar field to at least one heat exchanger, e.g. a preheater or vaporizer, the heat exchanger transferring the heat to the separate working fluid cycle. The working fluid is the fluid used in a working machine, e.g. a steam turbine, to generate the mechanical/electrical power. The Rankine cycle refers to the working fluid circuit. [F03G 6/074] of the non-thermal type, e.g. springs or batteries -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  Illustrated is a combined thermal and compressed-gas energy conversion system comprising a compressed-gas energy conversion system configured for substantially isothermal storage and recovery of energy, a source of recovered thermal energy: and a heat-exchange subsystem in fluid communication with the compressed-gas energy conversion system and the source of recovered thermal energy. Optionally the source of recovered thermal energy comprises at least one of a fossil fuel power plant, a heat engine power plant, a solar thermal source, a geothermal source, an industrial process with waste heat, a heat pump, a heat source, a heat sink, or a source of environmentally chilled water.
[F03G 6/083] with solar ponds -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for producing mechanical power from solar energy with solar ponds for collecting the solar energy.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  Waste salts and brines are disposed of by constructing salt water solar pond power plants, concentrating the waste salts and brines, and then introducing them into the heat storage layers of the solar ponds of the power plants. The basins in which such solar ponds are constructed are made deeper than needed to sustain operation of the power plant at its designed output, and after a deep basin is filled, the concentrated brine from the heat storage layer is available to construct another solar pond which is then available for storing additional waste salts. In concentrating the waste salts, sodium chloride will precipitate, and this material can be deposited at the bottom of the solar ponds to seal the same against leakage.  Method and apparatus for producing power from solar ponds

[F03G 6/127] Over-night operation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Features specifically related to the over-night operation of solar power plants.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified:  The figure above illustrates a solar chimney, comprising an enclosure defining a path for flow of a fluid to be heated, the enclosure containing a heat exchanger, a solar collector located outside the enclosure, a reservoir for storing a heat transfer fluid, and means for directing heat transfer fluid between the reservoir, the solar collector, and the heat exchanger, wherein, in a first position of the directing means, appropriate for use during daytime, heat transfer fluid can flow from the reservoir, then into the solar collector, then into the heat exchanger, and then back to the reservoir, and wherein, in a second position of the directing means, appropriate for use during nighttime, heat transfer fluid can flow from the reservoir, into the heat exchanger, and then back to the reservoir.  The figure above illustrates a thermal pump system energized by a daytime heating phase and a night time cooling phase associated with a naturally occurring environmental heating and cooling cycle that includes a hydraulic fluid source, a thermal fluid expansion chamber having a fixed internal volume, and a hydraulic accumulator at least partially filled by a compressible gas. A first unidirectional flow valve connected to a first duct permits flow only out of the hydraulic fluid source during the night time cooling phase. A second unidirectional flow valve connected to a second duct permits flow only out of the thermal fluid expansion chamber upon expansion of the hydraulic fluid trapped in the thermal fluid expansion chamber due to heating during the daytime heating phase. The thermal fluid expansion chamber has a thermally conductive wall communicating thermal energy from the naturally occurring heating and cooling cycle to the hydraulic fluid trapped in the fixed internal volume.
[F03G 6/129] Safety arrangements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Features specifically related to safety arrangements of solar power plants.
[F03G 7/00] Mechanical-power-producing mechanisms, not otherwise provided for or using energy sources not otherwise provided for -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices or mechanisms for producing mechanical power from vibrational energy, e.g. of a fluid or a machine, or from an electro-chemical process, or by using pressure differences or thermal differences occurring in nature, or from expansion or contraction of bodies.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Flexible endoscopes using shape memory elements -> A61B1/0058 - Electrothermal relays making use of shape memory materials -> H01H61/0107
[F03G 7/002] using the energy of vibration of fluid columns -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mechanical-power-producing mechanisms not otherwise provided for using the energy of vibration of fluid columns.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified:  The illustration above shows a thermoacoustic transducer for converting between thermal energy and mechanical energy.
[F03G 7/003] Motors driven by thermophoresis or thermodiffusion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified:  The figure above illustrates a device that generates an oriented flux of gas molecules and utilises said oriented flux for obtaining electric energy. The device comprises a housing (2) containing gas, a membrane (3) dividing the housing into compartments, wherein the membrane (3) is provided with a plurality of funnel-like through holes (6), a bypass (7) that links the said compartments, a turbine machine (8) with an electric current generator (9), arranged within the bypass (7), and a heat exchanger (10) supplying heat to the first compartment (4). Owing to the configuration of the holes in the membrane, the number of molecules that penetrate from the first compartment (4) to the last compartment (5) exceeds the number of molecules that return from the last compartment (5) to the first compartment (4). Due to the difference in pressures in the compartments, the gas flux flows through the bypass (7) and generates electricity by means of turbine machine (8) and the generator.  **Glossary:** - Thermophoresis or Thermodiffusion: Different particles in a mixture of mobile particles exhibit different responses to the force of a temperature gradient.
[F03G 7/004] Thermo-magnetic motors, e.g. using the Curie effect -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Thermo-magnetic motors make use of the reduction of a magnetic force under thermal influence (Curie effect).  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  The illustration above shows a thermodynamic device based on a magnetic field and curie effect.
[F03G 7/005] Electro-chemical actuators; Actuators having a material for absorbing or desorbing gas, e.g. a metal hydride; Actuators using the difference in osmotic pressure between fluids; Actuators with elements stretchable when contacted with liquid rich in ions, with UV light, with a salt solution -- Group F03G7/005 is no longer used for the classification of documents as of 2021-08-01 . The content of this group is being reclassified into groups F03G7/009 , F03G7/011 , F03G7/012 , F03G7/0121 , F03G7/015 , F03G7/016 and F03G7/017 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03G 7/008] characterised by the actuating element  actuating elements for mechanical power producing mechanism using expansion or contraction of bodies due to heating, cooling, moistening, drying or the like  F03G7/061 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  An actuation apparatus includes at least one magnetic shape memory (MSM) element containing a material configured to expand and/or contract in response to exposure to a magnetic field. Among other things, the MSM element may be configured to pump fluid through a micropump by expanding and/or contracting in response to the magnetic field. The magnetic field may rotate about an axis of rotation and exhibit a distribution having a component substantially perpendicular to the axis of rotation. Further, the magnetic field distribution may include at least two components substantially orthogonal to one another lying in one or more planes perpendicular to the axis of rotation. The at least one MSM element may contain nickel, manganese, and gallium. A polymerase chain reaction (PCR) may be enhanced by contacting a PCR reagent and DNA material with the MSM element.

[F03G 7/011] Actuators having a material for absorbing or desorbing a gas, e.g. with a fuel cell reaction or a metal hydride -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  The illustrative example above shows an actuator incorporating an electrolytic gas generator-evacuator is disclosed. A membrane of material selectively permeable to a drive gas is inserted in a combined inlet-outlet of an expandable chamber. The membrane forms a portion of an electrolytic cell by being immersed in a body of electrolyte capable of transporting an ionic species of the drive gas between the membrane as one electrode and a counter electrode. By connecting the combined inlet-outlet of the chamber to the outside surface of the membrane and cyclically driving the electrolytic cell in a drive gas generation mode and a drive gas evacuation mode, the chamber will cyclically expand and contract.  The illustrative example above shows a metal hydride actuator.
[F03G 7/012] Electro-chemical actuators -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  Illustrated is a device involving electrochemical actuation. The devices may comprise at least one component capable of exhibiting a volumetric or dimensional change upon application of a voltage. The volumetric or dimensional change may produce mechanical work. The devices may be capable of exhibiting high actuation rates and supporting high amounts of stress (e.g., in tensile and/or compressive loading). The device may provide morphing structures that may be useful in applications such as airfoils.  Illustrated is a protein array for converting chemical energy into mechanical energy.

[F03G 7/027] Control or monitoring -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.  Control or monitoring related features of mechanical-power-producing devices not otherwise provided for.  An electroactive material actuator device is provided having a controllable stiffness profile. An actuator member of the device comprises an electroactive polymer material having light absorbing filler elements embedded or contained therein. The light absorbing filler elements are adapted to absorb and convert incident light energy to heat energy to therefore heat surrounding sections of the electroactive material. By selectively controlling an intensity level or spectral composition of a light source directed at the actuator member, a specific degree and spread of heating can be achieved across the member and, as a result, a specific desired stiffness or flexibility profile realised across the body of the actuator member.  Illustrated is an actuator, and more particularly, the configuration of an actuator which can detect a direction of displacement or an amount of displacement.

[F03G 7/045] Environmental heat plants or OTEC plants using an Organic Rankine Cycle [ORC] or a Kalina Cycle -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Environmental heat plants using pressure or thermal differences occurring in nature or ocean thermal energy conversion (OTEC) plants using an Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) or a Kalina cycle.  An Organic Rankine cycle uses an organic fluid, like CFC, HFC, HC, PFC, with a boiling point lower than the water boiling point.  In a Kalina cycle the conversion of heat into mechanical power is realized by using a solution of two fluids with different boiling points, the most widely used combination being water and ammonia.
[F03G 7/047] Environmental heat plants or OTEC plants using heat pumps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Environmental heat plants using pressure or thermal differences occurring in nature or ocean thermal energy conversion (OTEC) plants using heat pumps.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  The figure above illustrates a cooling electric energy generator with Stirling engine (46) integrated in it that absorbs heat energy from environment through external heat collector (30), and it concentrates heat energy on heat side of Stirling engine (46) by internal heat pump device (8). Internal heat pump device (8) cools cold side of Stirling engine (46) as well as it absorbs heat energy from cool side and it concentrates heat energy on heat side of Stirling engine (46). Internal heat pump device (8) is initially started with electric energy from external generator. Stirling engine (46) starts rotation in electric generator (48), which further supplies internal heat pump devices (8), through programmed switcher. Heat energy from environment and from cold side of Stirling engine (46) is four to nine time higher quantity than electric energy necessary for functioning internal heat pump device (8).

[F03G 7/06112] using the thermal expansion or contraction of enclosed fluids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  This invention relates to motors of the kind in which motion is produced by transmitting to a piston the expansion of a liquid due to the heating thereof in a temporarily closed space surrounded by a continuously heated body, said space having an inlet and outlet closable by valves, and wherein the expanded heated liquid is displaced through said outlet by the introduction of cold liquid through said inlet, the displaced liquid being led through a cooling device prior to being utilised again in the heated working space.  An actuator is composed of a plurality of three-dimensionally arranged containers each of which is constructed into deformable configuration and each of which is set to accommodate therein an amount of expandable fluid in response to the increase of the temperature, a heat supply device for supplying the heat to the fluid in each of the containers, a cooling device for cooling the fluid in each of the containers and a control device for controlling the heat supply device and the cooling device.  The invention relates to fluid pressure responsive devices and, in particular, to devices employing a member which is movable by a fluid pressure and volume change, such as fluid expansion and contraction in response to changes in temperature.
[F03G 7/06113] the fluids subjected to phase change -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The enclosed fluid changes its phase when heated/cooled, e.g. wax actuators, liquid crystal actuators, low melting point alloy actuators, melting polymer actuators.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  A piston (14) is slidably received in a chamber (10). The chamber is filled with an expandable medium (16), such as wax that expands and contracts significantly as it undergoes a phase change from a solid to a liquid state. A Peltier effect chip (20) selectively adds and removes heat energy from the chamber, hence the medium, to cause the medium to change phase. A temperature control (26) maintains the temperature of the medium substantially at the phase change temperature such that the addition of heat causes an isothermal melting (hence, expansion) and the removal of heat causes an isothermal solidification (hence, contraction). The chamber defines a plurality of thin branches such that the heat is transferred more effectively.  A wax actuator and a method of actuating by means of a wax actuator
[F03G 7/06114] using the thermal expansion or contraction of solid materials -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  The illustration above shows a ratcheting micromotor with a ratchet bar defining ratchet teeth along an edge thereof, and first and second ends in opposition to one another. The first end is adapted to be coupled to an object to be moved. A spring is coupled on one end thereof to the second end of the ratchet bar and adapted to be fixed at another end thereof to a base. A bidirectional actuator configured for linear movement in one of a first direction and a second direction, and when energized has a common yoke coupled to a central portion thereof for movement in correspondence therewith.  The common yoke includes teeth formed along an edge and positioned such that its teeth engage a portion of the ratchet teeth. A control system energizes the bidirectional actuator to move, alternately, in the first direction and the second direction.  The illustration above shows a motor comprising a rotor supported to rotate freely about its longitudinal axis while the axis is confined to an elastically bowed configuration by internal or external means. Heat, cold or other stress inducing means applied to the side of the rotor causes local internal stresses that cause the rotor to rotate in the direction that allows relaxation of the induced stress.
[F03G 7/06115] using materials changing their chemical composition -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  Illustrated above is a morphing cellular structure, comprising a group of unit cells with each unit cell having a cellular geometry, the group of unit cells formed of a first active material, where the first active material has a first state and a second state, and where each unit cell has a configuration where the configuration of each unit cell, while the first active material is in the first state, is different from the configuration of each unit cell while the first active material is in the second state, and where the first active material is responsive to a first actuation signal such that when the first actuation signal is actuated, the active material is deformed from the first state to the second state, thereby changing the configuration of each unit cell affected by the actuation of the first actuation signal and morphing the cellular structure, wherein the first actuation signal is a signal selected from a group consisting of a temperature change, redox reaction, pH change, electromagnetic field, chemical reaction, electric current, electric field, and magnetic field; and wherein the active material is deformable from both a first state to a second state and from a second state to a first state such that the deployment is a re-settable deployment where the active material is re-settable through an external reset signal, allowing for at least one additional deployment, the re-settable deformation being selected from a group consisting of deforming from the first state to the second state upon receiving the first actuation signal and deforming from the second state to the first state when the first actuation signal is removed, and deforming from the first state to the second state when the first actuation signal is removed and deforming from the second state to the first state when the first actuation signal is received.
