[F03G 7/0613] using layers of different materials joined together, e.g. bimetals -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  Illustrated is an electrothermal actuator comprising at least two operating portions electrically connected with each other to define at least one conductive path and at least two electrodes spaced from each other and configured to introduce a current to the at least one conductive path, wherein each of the at least two operating portions comprises a flexible polymer layer and a carbon nanotube paper stacked with each other, the carbon nanotube paper is at least partly embedded into the flexible polymer layer; a thickness ratio of the carbon nanotube paper and the flexible polymer layer is in a range from about 1:7 to about 1:10, a density of the carbon nanotube paper is greater than or equal to 0.5 g/cm 3 , a thermal expansion coefficient of the carbon nanotube paper is greater than or equal to ten times that of the flexible polymer layer; a conductivity of the carbon nanotube paper of each of the at least two operating portions along a current direction of the at least two operating portions is in a range from about 1000 S/m to about 6000 S/m.
[F03G 7/0614] using shape memory elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  The actuator comprises a strand of shape memory material having a length that is variable between a first length corresponding to an undeformed state and a second length corresponding to a state of lengthwise deformation. The strand is arranged to return to the undeformed state when it is heated to a predetermined temperature. The strand comprises a fixed end and a mobile end arranged to move between a first and a second position when the variable length of the SMM strand changes. The actuator further comprises a heating wire coiled around the strand for heating the strand, the coiled heating wire forming a circular helix, the uniform slope of which is selected so as to avoid any substantial change of the arc length of the circular helix caused by the strand changing its length.
[F03G 7/06143] Wires -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Shape memory elements using wires.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  An SMA (scalable multi-element shape memory alloy) actuator is arranged to tilt the mirror in a zoom camera to effect optical image stabilization. The actuator may comprise four angled-V SMA wires. The actuator may alternatively comprise a gimbal flexure and SMA wires. In this case, there may be four SMA wires or two SMA wires and two springs. The SMA wires may be straight wires or wires hooked in a V shape.
[F03G 7/06145] Springs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Shape memory elements using springs.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  Actuators are known for operating a control element, with a control element (14) which is fastened to a force-transmitting member (20), an opening spring (26) made of a shape memory alloy which acts on the force-transmitting member (20) in a first direction, the spring force being dependent upon the temperature of the opening spring (26), and a restoring spring (22) which acts on the force-transmitting member(20) in a direction opposite to the first direction. In order to prevent unwanted opening at ambient temperatures above the structure conversion temperature, according to the invention, a closing spring (24) made of a shape memory alloy acts on the force-transmitting member (20) in a direction opposite to the first direction.  The present disclosure relates to an actuator and an actuator set that stretch or contract in accordance with a change in temperature.

A ratcheting gear transmission couples the plurality of SMA tubes in parallel to a common output. A controller provides power to a plurality of heaters associated with each of the plurality of SMA tubes, sequentially heating selected heaters to provide continuous rotational motion of the common output.

The content of this group is being reclassified into groups F03G7/0614 , F03G7/06143 , F03G7/06145 , F03G7/06146 , F03G7/06147 and F03G7/0615 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F03G 7/066] Actuator control or monitoring -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control or monitoring related features of mechanical-power-producing devices or actuators not otherwise provided for and using expansion or contraction of bodies due to heating, cooling, moistening, drying or the like.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  Illustrated is a pump comprising a reservoir having a maximum volume, an actuator comprising a shape memory alloy that moves a plunger, the plunger displacing liquid from the reservoir and a control system that utilizes linear feedback to sense the position of the actuator and to manipulate the actuator to provide doses of variable volumes less than the maximum volume.  The control system further comprises a microprocessor and means for position detection.
[F03G 7/0665] controlled displacement, e.g. by using a lens positioning actuator -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[F03G 7/067] Safety arrangements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Safety arrangements related features of mechanical-power-producing devices or actuators not otherwise provided for and using expansion or contraction of bodies due to heating, cooling, moistening, drying or the like.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  The illustrative example relates to systems for protecting actuators and the assemblies they compose from overload conditions, and more particularly, to a system for providing overload protection to same, which employs magnetorheological fluid mechanisms connected in series with or parallel to the load acting upon the actuator, so as to effect tunable protection.
[F03G 7/08] recovering energy derived from swinging, rolling, pitching or like movements, e.g. from the vibrations of a machine -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  The illustration above shows a portable electronic devices requiring electrical power for their operation and specifically to those powered by harvesting kinetic energy.
[F03G 7/081] recovering energy from moving road or rail vehicles, e.g. collecting vehicle vibrations in the vehicle tyres or shock absorbers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  Wheel device for use as e.g. alternator, in car, has energy conversion element to convert mechanical energy into electrical energy in operating condition, where conversion element includes smart material designed as electroactive polymer.
[F03G 7/083] using devices on streets or on rails -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  Illustrated is a kinetic energy collector mechanism for generating electric power from passing vehicles, using a main axle connected to a flywheel, wherein the main axle is supported on two bearings and/or rollers, said main axle having a half-cylindrical support section for the vehicle wheels, and having attached on each end at least one flywheel that is mounted so as to transmit load in only one direction while it can rotate freely in the opposite direction, said main axle rotating in an angle ranging between 0° and 120°, preferably 45°, equivalent to 1/8th of a turn as the vehicle wheels pass over the half-cylindrical section of the main axle, thus driving the attached flywheel; wherein the main axle is returned to its original resting position by means of springs, then repeating the rotational motion every time the main axle is driven by passing vehicle wheels; and wherein, the flywheel has enough driving force to rotate and drive a generator and its corresponding regulator by mechanical means that transmit the load to said generator with its corresponding regulator, which in turn generates electric power that is either delivered to a power network or charged in batteries.
[F03G 7/085] hydraulic or pneumatic devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[F03G 7/087] magnetic or electromagnetic devices, e.g. linear electric generators -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  Illustrated is an apparatus to provide a means of generating electrical energy in an "environmentally clean" and efficient manner by recovering vehicular generated energy on a roadway surface comprising Modular Prefabricated Structural Sandwich Composite Panels comprised of a plurality of "spring-less" linear electromagnetic generators and a tri-axial differentially deformable composite tread comprised of crumb rubber and aggregate, an insert rod for increased "Y" axis stiffness and a foil layer to disperse heat generated by the plurality of Linear Electromagnetic Generators within the Modular Prefabricated Structural Sandwich Composite Panels.
[F03G 7/092] Motors following new scientific theories not otherwise provide for, e.g. using quantum field effects like zero-point energy, Casimir effect -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  In the illustration above, a force cell provides propellant-less propulsion for linear thrust applications and fuel-less torque for rotary applications. Linear thrust applications include propulsion for aircraft, spacecraft, flying cars, construction equipment for use in low and zero gravity environments, stabilization for ultra-high buildings and realization of ultra-long unsupported spans. Rotary torque applications include engines to drive electric generators of all sizes-from mobile phone size to power station size. Force cells use radiation pressure originating from the zero-point fields in the vacuum of space-the force in the Casimir effect, to produce a macroscopic external force through use of a multiplicity of microscopic Casimir cavities consisting of wedge shaped non-charged conducting plates attached to a matrix of non-conducting material. Force cells arranged in balanced pairs can produce modulated external thrust. Force cells arranged circularly can produce modulated torque.  The illustration above shows two conducting plates are joined at an angle to produce a Vee or wedge shape of nanometer scale. It is claimed that this can use zero-point energy to produce a propulsive (Casimir) force or drive a machine such as a generator. The device may comprise a plurality of wedges in a zigzag shape, and may have a smooth plate ^ attached to one side, which is claimed to give rise to an asymmetric force. It is also claimed that the device, when positioned wholly or partly surrounding an object, can reduce the mass of the object.  The illustration above shows methods, microstructures and devices for generating a transverse Casimir force by using an array of non-parallel conducting plates and an intervening dielectric or semiconductor material to provide propulsion in manned or unmanned space vehicles.
[F03G 7/10] Alleged -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mechanical perpetua mobilia.
[F03G 7/104] continuously converting gravity into usable power -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  Device for producing renewable energy by pulley forces with guide rollers has reservoir and force of gravity for obtaining energy, where block and reservoir are suspended from pulley.
[F03G 7/107] using an imbalance for increasing torque or saving energy -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  An out-of-balance drive has multiple rollers running on a common axis and multiple balls running through an effective short path via a bridge path, where balls are push-started by a starting spring.
[F03G 7/111] using magnets, e.g. gravo-magnetic motors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[F03G 7/115] harvesting energy from inertia forces -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  The illustration above shows an electrical generator for generating electrical energy from the kinetic energy associated with a rotating body, designed in the form of a heavy wheel. The generator includes a vertical cylindrical container housing a rotor float supported by a central attachment and a quantity of liquid in the space between the container and the rotor float. The rotor float includes a cover from which bars made from metal or another heavy metal extend, the ends of which bars support a circular ring bearing a non-slip band acted on by electric propulsion motors. In addition, a drive device, such as a ring gear, is associated with the cover and a toothed wheel is meshed therewith, said toothed wheel being associated with the rotation axle of an electrical generator.  Illustrated above is an apparatus for producing energy comprising a flywheel assembly arranged for rotation, drive means operatively coupled to the flywheel assembly, the drive means including biasing means connected to an actuator arranged to bias the biasing means providing stored energy in the biasing means, transmission means coupled between the flywheel assembly and the biasing means where release of the stored energy in the basing means provides a driving force which drives the transmission means to effect rotation of the flywheel assembly which gains momentum, extraction means operatively coupled to the flywheel assembly for rapid extraction of the momentum of the flywheel assembly as well as an energy generator associated with the extraction means for generating energy from the rapidly extracted momentum of the flywheel assembly.
[F03G 7/119] amplifying power, torque or energy -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  Illustrated is a torque multiplier device applicable in any machine for achieving a higher torque and for longer than currently known. For this purpose, it employs discs or plates whose diameters vary according to the number of revolutions per minutes required.
[F03G 7/122] of closed energy loops -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  This method covers the utilization of alternators for recharging batteries and electric car mileage range improvement. It involves the usage of the rotors to charge and to extend range for an electric car, while the car is in motion. There are two methods that will be explained; these methods are called the Jent methods. The first method will be connecting the alternator to the rear axles. The second method is having an alternator rotor hybrid so that the car can still charge and have the ability to stop and apply brakes with full function.
[F03G 7/125] creating a thrust by violating the principle of momentum conservation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  Propulsion mechanism of the dual-inertial-impulse
[F03G 7/129] Thermodynamic processes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  Method for converting low-grade energy and a fuelless jet engine for carrying out said method
[F03G 7/135] following unproven scientific theories; Theories about perpetual motion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:  System for quantum energy storage can include a quantum information engine including topological insulator having at least one edge. A coherence capacitor can include nuclei of atoms within the topological insulator, and each nucleus can have a spin direction. An energy source can be electrically connected to the topological insulator and configured to supply a current along at least one edge of the topological insulator. The current can interact with at least one nucleus of the nuclei to flip a spin direction of the at least one nucleus.
[F03H] PRODUCING A REACTIVE PROPULSIVE THRUST, NOT OTHERWISE PROVIDED FOR -- In this subclass non-limiting references (in the sense of paragraph 39 of the Guide to the IPC) may still be displayed in the scheme.
[F03H 1/00] Using plasma to produce a reactive propulsive thrust -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines exhausting ions or a plasma (ions and electrons) to produce a reactive propulsive thrust.  Details thereof.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Adaptations of ion or plasma engines for fitting in or to, cosmonautic vehicles -> B64G1/413 - Ion sources for plasma processing or for ion beams -> H01J37/08 - Apparatus for generating ions to be introduced into non-enclosed gases -> H01T23/00 - Plasma accelerators -> H05H1/54
[F03H 1/0006] Details applicable to different types of plasma thrusters  arrangements specially adapted for fitting plasma engines in or to cosmonautic vehicles  B64G1/413 -- This CPC subclass covers design and integration aspects of plasma propulsion systems intended for spacecraft, including mounting, feeding, cooling, and control arrangements that enable plasma thrusters to be installed on or within vehicles for spaceflight. It falls under mechanical engineering, machines or engines for liquids producing reactive propulsive thrust, focusing on plasma-based thrust generation.
[F03H 1/0012] Means for supplying the propellant -- The subclass F03H 1/0012 covers components that deliver propellant to a plasma-based thrust system, such as pumps, valves, feed lines or storage devices that feed liquid or gaseous propellant into the reactive engine. It is situated within mechanical engineering for machines that generate thrust by plasma reactions.
[F03H 1/0018] Arrangements or adaptations of power supply systems  for cosmonautic vehicles  B64G1/42
[F03H 1/0025] Neutralisers, i.e. means for keeping electrical neutrality -- F03H 1/0025 covers neutralisers, devices that maintain electrical neutrality in plasma-based propulsion systems that generate reactive thrust. These components balance charge by emitting electrons or ions, preventing buildup that could disrupt plasma flow and thrust efficiency.
[F03H 1/0031] Thermal management, heating or cooling parts of the thruster  temperature control for cosmonautic vehicles  B64G1/50
[F03H 1/0037] Electrostatic ion thrusters -- F03H 1/0037 covers electrostatic ion thrusters, which generate thrust by accelerating ions using an electric field. These devices belong to the class of machines that produce reactive propulsive thrust for liquids, employing plasma to create the thrust. The technology is used in spacecraft propulsion for fine attitude control and deep space missions.
[F03H 1/0043] characterised by the acceleration grid  extraction optics for ion sources  H01J27/024 -- F03H 1/0043 covers propulsion units that generate thrust by ionising a plasma and accelerating the ions through an extraction grid or other optics. The entry specifies the use of an acceleration grid to shape and extract the ion stream from the source, a key feature of plasma-based reactive thrusters not classified elsewhere.
[F03H 1/005] using field emission, e.g. Field Emission Electric Propulsion [FEEP] -- This subclass covers propulsion systems that generate thrust by field-emission of charged particles, such as Field Emission Electric Propulsion (FEEP). The method uses an electric field to emit ions or electrons from a liquid propellant, producing a plasma jet that provides reactive thrust. It is applied in fine-control satellite attitude and station-keeping.
[F03H 1/0056] with an acceleration grid and an applied magnetic field -- The F03H 1/0056 subclass covers plasma-based propulsion systems that generate reactive thrust by ionising a liquid-derived propellant and accelerating it through an acceleration grid while an external magnetic field is applied. It is part of the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for machines that produce thrust using plasma processes.
[F03H 1/0062] grid-less with an applied magnetic field -- A plasma propulsion system that generates thrust without using physical grids, employing an applied magnetic field to accelerate and direct the ionized gas. The magnetic field replaces conventional grid electrodes, improving efficiency and reducing erosion. This configuration falls under mechanical engineering for machines producing reactive propulsive thrust.
[F03H 1/0068] with a central channel, e.g. end-Hall type -- The entry F03H 1/0068 covers plasma-based thrust generators that create a reactive propulsive force by accelerating plasma through a central channel, typified by end-Hall thrusters. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically machines or engines for liquids producing a reactive propulsive thrust not otherwise provided for. This classification pertains to designs where the plasma discharge is confined and expelled along an axial passage.
[F03H 1/0075] with an annular channel; Hall-effect thrusters with closed electron drift -- This subclass covers Hall-effect plasma thrusters that employ an annular flow channel and a closed-electron-drift configuration to generate reactive thrust. It falls under mechanical engineering for liquid-fuel engines producing propulsive thrust by plasma acceleration.
[F03H 1/0081] Electromagnetic plasma thrusters -- Electromagnetic plasma thrusters generate reactive thrust by ionising a propellant and accelerating the resulting plasma with electric or magnetic fields, producing a jet that propels a vehicle without combustion. The class includes designs where the plasma itself provides the thrust, covering space-craft propulsion systems that differ from conventional chemical rockets.
[F03H 1/0087] Electro-dynamic thrusters, e.g. pulsed plasma thrusters -- The class F03H 1/0087 covers electro-dynamic thrusters, such as pulsed plasma thrusters, which generate reactive thrust by accelerating ionized gas with electromagnetic fields. These devices belong to the broader group of liquid-based machines that produce a propulsive thrust not otherwise provided for and are used primarily for spacecraft propulsion.
[F03H 1/0093] Electro-thermal plasma thrusters, i.e. thrusters heating the particles in a plasma  resistojets  per se   B64G1/415 -- An electro-thermal plasma thruster heats a propellant by creating and energising a plasma, then expels the hot particles to produce reactive thrust. The class includes resistojets and similar devices that generate thrust by electrically heating a gas or vapor to form a plasma jet, covering the physics, design and operation of these plasma-based propulsion systems.
[F03H 3/00] Use of photons to produce a reactive propulsive thrust -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines exhausting photons to produce a reactive propulsive thrust .  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Vehicle lighting -> B60Q - Arrangements or adaptations of signal or lighting devices for fitting in or to aircraft -> B64D47/02 - Adaptations of propulsion systems for fitting in or to, cosmonautic vehicles -> B64G1/40 - Lighting in general -> F21 - Devices using stimulated emission, e.g. lasers -> H01S - Electric lighting -> H05B
[F03H 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Engines producing a reactive propulsive thrust without exhausting a fluid, a plasma or photons .  Engines allegedly producing a reactive propulsive thrust without exhausting anything, in violation of the Newtonian law of action and reaction.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Using plasma to produce a reactive propulsive thrust -> F03H1/00 - Using photons to produce a reactive propulsive thrust -> F03H3/00 - Jet-propulsion plants -> F02K
[F04] POSITIVE - DISPLACEMENT MACHINES FOR LIQUIDS -- F04 classifies positive-displacement machines that move liquids by trapping a fixed volume and forcing it through the system. It includes gear, screw, piston, vane and other pump types that deliver a constant flow regardless of pressure variations. The category falls under mechanical engineering.
[F04B] POSITIVE-DISPLACEMENT MACHINES FOR LIQUIDS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pumps or compressors which cause a fluid to move by trapping a fixed amount of said fluid, and then forcing or displacing the trapped fluid into discharge means.  Reciprocating pumps or compressors causing the fluid to move using one or more oscillating pistons, plungers or flexible working members, i.e. membranes, diaphragms or tubes.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Machines for liquids, or pumps, of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston type -> F04C - Non-positive-displacement pumps -> F04D - Pumping of fluid by direct contact of another fluid or by using inertia of fluid to be pumped -> F04F  **Glossary:** - Pump: Device for continuously raising, forcing, compressing, or exhausting fluid by mechanical means - Machine: Device that could equally be both an engine and a pump and not a device which is restricted to an engine or one which is restricted to a pump - Positive displacement pumps: Pumps using pistons or other mechanical members to displace a working fluid in a working chamber, the dynamic effect on the fluid being of minor importance - Positive displacement engines: The energy of a working fluid is transformed into mechanical energy, in which variations of volume created by the working fluid in a working chamber produce equivalent movement of mechanical members, e.g. pistons transmitting the energy, the dynamic effect of the fluid being of minor importance - Oscillating piston machine: Positive-displacement machine in which a fluid-engaging, work-transmitting member oscillates, e.g. a vane piston swinging back and forth about a fixed axis - Reciprocating piston: A fluid-engaging, work-transmitting member of an reciprocating-piston type machine or pump that slides alternately back and forth usually along a straight line or path - Rotary piston: A fluid engaging, work-transmitting member of a rotary-piston machine or pump that can completely rotate about a fixed axis or about an axis moving along a circular or similar orbit when operating, e.g. rotor having vanes or teeth - Free-piston machine: A linear , "crankless" reciprocating piston machine in which the piston motion is not controlled by a crankshaft but determined by the interaction forces from the fluid pressure in the working chamber, a rebound device (e.g. a piston in a closed cylinder) and a load device (e.g. a gas compressor or a linear alternator) - Rotary piston machine: Positive-displacement machine in which a liquid-engaging, work-transmitting member rotates about a fixed axis or about an axis moving along a circular or similar orbit, e.g. machine with a rotor having vanes or teeth - Cooperating members: The "oscillating piston" or "rotary piston" and another member, e.g., the working-chamber wall, which assists in the pumping action or machine's action - Movement of the cooperating members: To be interpreted as relative, so that one of the "cooperating members" may be stationary, even though reference may be made to its rotational axis, or both may move - Teeth or tooth equivalents: Includes lobes, projections or abutments - Internal axis type: The rotational axes of the inner and outer co-operating members remain at all times within the outer member, e.g., in a similar manner to that of a pinion meshing with the internal teeth of a ring gear - Working fluid: Driven fluid in a pump or driving or driven liquid in a machine. The working fluid can be in a compressible, gaseous state, e.g. steam, called elastic fluid, a liquid state, or a state where there is coexistence of elastic fluid and liquid state
[F04B 1/00] Pumps for liquids or for liquid and elastic fluids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pumps for liquids defined by the number of cylinders (e.g. having two or more cylinders) or by the arrangement of the cylinders (e.g. radial piston pumps with cylinders in star or fan arrangement, or axial piston pumps with cylinders parallel or inclined with respect to a main shaft).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Machines or pumps with pistons coacting within one cylinder -> F04B3/00
[F04B 1/005] Pumps with cylinder axis arranged substantially tangentially to a circle centred on main shaft axis -- The code F04B 1/005 designates positive-displacement pumps whose cylinders are arranged essentially tangentially to an imaginary circle whose centre coincides with the main shaft axis. This geometry causes the cylinder axes to follow a circular path around the shaft, typical of certain rotary or eccentric piston pumps.
[F04B 1/02] having two cylinders -- This subclass covers pumps that use two cylinders to displace liquid, typically with the cylinders operating alternately or together in a reciprocating motion. It includes double-acting and twin-cylinder designs for moving liquids or liquid-elastic fluid mixtures.
[F04B 1/03] with cylinder axis arranged substantially tangentially to a circle centred on main shaft axis -- This subcategory covers positive-displacement pumps where the cylinder axis is arranged substantially tangential to a circle centered on the main shaft, a layout found in axial-piston and some rotary pumps used for moving liquids or liquid-elastic fluids.
[F04B 1/04] having cylinders in star- or fan-arrangement -- This subclass concerns positive-displacement pumps whose working cylinders are positioned in a star- or fan-like configuration radiating from a central drive shaft. The arrangement provides compact axial dimensions and even load distribution among the pistons.
[F04B 1/0404] Details or component parts -- This classification subcategory F04B 1/0404 relates to the specific details or component parts of positive-displacement pumps used for moving liquids or liquid-elastic fluid mixtures. It includes design features such as chambers, rotors, gears, pistons, seals, and related mechanical elements. It falls under the broader mechanical engineering category for positive-displacement liquid machines.
[F04B 1/0408] Pistons -- F04B 1/0408 covers pistons used in positive-displacement pumps that move liquids or liquid-elastic fluids. The pistons form the moving chamber that isolates a fixed volume of fluid and forces it through the pump cycle. This sub-class belongs to the mechanical engineering group for positive-displacement machines.
[F04B 1/0413] Cams -- Cams are rotating or sliding components that transform rotary motion into reciprocating or oscillating movement, enabling precise timing of valve opening and closing in positive-displacement pumps for liquids. They are integral to controlling fluid flow and pump stroke characteristics.
[F04B 1/0417] consisting of two or more cylindrical elements, e.g. rollers -- This subclass covers positive-displacement liquid pumps that employ two or more cylindrical components, such as rollers, to move fluid. The design uses the rolling action of the cylinders to create displacement, providing a steady flow in pumps for liquids or liquid-elastic mixtures. It falls under mechanical engineering within the CPC hierarchy.
[F04B 1/0421] Cylinders -- F04B 1/0421 designates cylinders employed in positive-displacement machines that move liquids or liquid-elastic fluids. The subclass covers the construction, operation and control of hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders that generate the reciprocating motion needed for pump action. It falls under mechanical engineering, positive-displacement machines for liquids.
[F04B 1/0426] Arrangements for pressing the pistons against the actuated cam -- A device that uses a cam to press the pistons of a positive-displacement pump against each other, ensuring precise piston positioning and reliable sealing during each pumping cycle. This arrangement improves pump efficiency and reduces leakage by controlling piston motion through the cam's profile.
[F04B 1/043] Hydraulic arrangements -- F04B 1/043 covers hydraulic arrangements used in positive-displacement pumps that handle liquids or liquid-elastic fluid mixtures, defining the way fluid power is generated, transmitted or regulated within the pump mechanism. It focuses on the internal hydraulic circuits and components that control flow and pressure.
[F04B 1/0435] Arrangements for disconnecting the pistons from the actuated cam -- F04B 1/0435 covers designs that enable the pistons of a cam-driven positive-displacement pump to be disengaged from the cam. Such arrangements allow the piston motion to be halted, reversed or serviced without stopping the whole pump, improving control and maintenance of liquid-handling machinery.
[F04B 1/0439] Supporting or guiding means for the pistons -- F04B 1/0439 covers the structural parts that support or guide pistons inside positive-displacement pumps for liquids, ensuring proper alignment, reducing side loads and wear, and improving sealing and efficiency of the pumping action.
[F04B 1/0443] Draining of the housing -- The subclass F04B 1/0443 deals with features for draining the housing of positive-displacement liquid pumps. It covers arrangements, passages, valves or procedures that remove liquid or residual fluid from the pump casing after operation, preventing accumulation and facilitating maintenance or shutdown.
[F04B 1/0448] Sealing means, e.g. for shafts or housings -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Sealing means for piston -> F04B1/0408 - Stoffing boxes -> F04B53/164
[F04B 1/0452] Distribution members, e.g. valves -- The CPC class F04B 1/0452 pertains to distribution members such as valves used in positive-displacement pumps for liquids or liquid-elastic fluids. These components regulate the flow of fluid within the pump, directing it to different passages or outlets. The class falls under mechanical engineering, positive-displacement machines for liquids, and specifically pumps.
[F04B 1/0456] Cylindrical -- F04B 1/0456 classifies cylindrical positive-displacement pumps in which a cylindrical working chamber is created by a moving piston, rotor or similar element that operates coaxially to force liquids or liquid-elastic mixtures through the pump, delivering flow by reciprocating or rotating motion.
[F04B 1/0461] Conical -- The CPC entry F04B 1/0461 defines pumps that employ a conical displacement element, such as a conical rotor or screw, to move liquids or liquid-elastic fluids. These positive-displacement machines use the geometry of the cone to create chambers that vary in volume, producing a steady flow. The classification covers design, operation and related components of conical-type pumps within the broader category of mechanical engineering positive-displacement pumps.
[F04B 1/0465] plate-like -- F04B 1/0465 classifies positive-displacement pumps that use a series of flat, plate-like elements to move liquid or liquid-elastic mixtures. The plates form chambers whose volume changes as they slide or rotate, delivering a steady flow with minimal pulsation. This design is typical for hygienic or low-shear applications such as food processing and medical devices.
[F04B 1/047] with actuating or actuated elements at the outer ends of the cylinders -- F04B 1/047 covers pumps in which the actuating or actuated components, such as pistons, rods or diaphragms, are positioned at the outer ends of the pump cylinders rather than centrally. This arrangement is used in positive-displacement machines for moving liquids or liquid-elastic fluids.
[F04B 1/0472] with cam-actuated distribution members -- F04B 1/0472 covers positive-displacement pumps that use cam-actuated distribution elements to control the flow of liquids or liquid-elastic fluids. These machines employ cam mechanisms to move valves, ports or pistons for precise dosing or metering during pumping. The classification falls within the mechanical engineering hierarchy for pumps for liquids or liquid-elastic fluids.
[F04B 1/0474] with two or more serially arranged radial piston-cylinder units -- This CPC subclass covers pumps for liquids that use two or more radial piston-cylinder units placed in series, each piston moving radially within its cylinder to displace fluid. The serial arrangement provides staged pressure increase and is typical of high-pressure or multi-stage pump designs.
[F04B 1/0476] located side-by-side -- F04B 1/0476 refers to positive-displacement pumps in which the working elements are arranged side-by-side, such as adjacent pistons, rollers or chambers that operate in parallel rather than in series or stacked configurations. This layout is used to balance forces, reduce vibration and allow compact, high-flow designs in liquid and elastic-fluid pumping applications.
[F04B 1/0478] Coupling of two or more cylinder-barrels -- F04B 1/0478 relates to positive-displacement pumps in which two or more cylinder-barrels are mechanically coupled, allowing them to operate together either in series or parallel to transmit power or increase flow. The classification covers the design and arrangement of such coupled cylinder-barrel pump assemblies.
[F04B 1/053] with actuating or actuated elements at the inner ends of the cylinders -- This subclass covers positive-displacement pumps in which the moving or driven parts are located at the inner ends of the pump cylinders, such as pistons, plungers or diaphragms that are actuated from within the chamber. It includes designs where the actuation mechanism is integrated at the cylinder interior rather than external.
[F04B 1/0531] with cam-actuated distribution members -- It refers to positive-displacement liquid pumps that use a cam to control the movement of distribution components such as valves or ports. The cam drives the timing and sequencing of fluid flow within the pump.
[F04B 1/0533] each machine piston having channels that coact with the cylinder and serve as distribution members for another piston-cylinder unit -- A positive-displacement pump in which each piston contains internal channels that cooperate with its cylinder and function as distribution passages for a second piston-cylinder unit, allowing fluid to be routed between the two working elements. This arrangement integrates the distribution function within the moving piston itself.
[F04B 1/0535] the piston-driving cams being provided with inlets and outlets -- This subclass covers piston-driving cams that incorporate both inlet and outlet passages, enabling the admission and discharge of fluid directly through the cam mechanism. The cams are used in positive-displacement pumps for liquids or liquid-elastic fluids, providing a compact means of moving the pistons that generate flow.
[F04B 1/0536] with two or more serially arranged radial piston-cylinder units -- F04B 1/0536 designates positive-displacement pumps that employ two or more radial piston-cylinder assemblies placed in series. The arrangement allows the pistons to successively compress the liquid, providing higher pressure or flow than a single unit. This subclass is part of the broader category of mechanical engineering pumps for liquids.
[F04B 1/0538] located side-by-side -- F04B 1/0538 covers positive-displacement pumps in which the drive mechanism and the pumping chamber are arranged side-by-side rather than coaxially. This layout is used for piston, diaphragm or gear types where the cylinders or chambers are positioned adjacent to each other, enabling compact construction and easy access for maintenance.
[F04B 1/06] Control -- F04B 1/06 covers devices that regulate the operation of positive-displacement pumps for liquids or liquid-elastic fluid mixtures. It includes mechanisms that adjust flow rate, pressure, speed, or direction by means such as throttles, variable-stroke cylinders, electronic or hydraulic controllers. The subclass is used to classify pump designs where control features are integral to the pump itself rather than external auxiliary equipment.
[F04B 1/063] by using a valve in a system with several pumping chambers wherein the flow-path through the chambers can be changed, e.g. between series and parallel flow -- F04B 1/063 covers pumps that incorporate a valve to reconfigure the fluid path among multiple pumping chambers, allowing the chambers to be connected in series or in parallel. The arrangement enables a single pump system to adapt its displacement characteristics by changing the flow configuration.
[F04B 1/066] by changing the phase relationship between the actuating cam and the distributing means -- F04B 1/066 classifies positive-displacement pumps whose operation is varied by altering the phase relationship between the actuating cam and the distributing or valve mechanism, thereby changing the timing of fluid intake and discharge. This includes designs that adjust cam-valve timing to control volume per stroke, pressure, or flow characteristics.
[F04B 1/07] by varying the relative eccentricity between two members, e.g. a cam and a drive shaft -- F04B 1/07 covers positive-displacement pumps whose output is modulated by altering the relative eccentricity of two components, such as a cam and a drive shaft. By varying this offset the machine changes its displacement per cycle, allowing variable flow.
[F04B 1/08] regulated by delivery pressure -- The sub-class covers positive-displacement pumps whose flow is controlled by maintaining a prescribed delivery pressure. It includes designs that use pressure-regulating mechanisms, such as relief valves or feedback controls, to adjust displacement and keep the outlet pressure within set limits. This applies to pumps handling liquids or liquid-elastic fluids where pressure, rather than speed, dictates the output.
[F04B 1/10] the cylinders being movable, e.g. rotary -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Machines or pumps with piston coacting with rotating cylinder block -> F04B3/006
[F04B 11/00] Equalisation of pulses, e.g. by use of air vessels -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pumps for liquids comprising means for preventing problems occurring with pulsation, vibration and/or cavitation.
[F04B 11/0008] using accumulators -- The classification covers the use of accumulators, such as air or hydraulic vessels, to smooth out pressure fluctuations in positive-displacement liquid pumps or compressors. It includes methods that store energy during high-pressure phases and release it during low-pressure phases to maintain a steady flow.
[F04B 11/0016] with a fluid spring -- F04B 11/0016 covers positive-displacement liquid machines that employ a fluid spring, for example air-filled vessels, to smooth out pressure pulses during operation. This subclass falls under the broader category of pulse-equalising devices in positive-displacement pumps and compressors.
[F04B 11/0025] the spring fluid being in direct contact with the pumped fluid -- This CPC subclass F04B 11/0025 relates to positive-displacement machines for liquids where the spring fluid is in direct contact with the fluid being pumped, for example systems that use air vessels to equalise pressure pulses.
[F04B 11/0033] with a mechanical spring -- The F04B 11/0033 subgroup concerns positive-displacement liquid machines that use a mechanical spring to equalise pressure pulses, for example by incorporating a spring-loaded air vessel that smooths the pulsating flow generated by the pump.
[F04B 11/0041] by piston speed control  F04B11/0058  takes precedence
[F04B 11/005] using two or more pumping pistons -- F04B 11/005 relates to positive-displacement liquid pumps that use two or more pistons in combination, typically to smooth out pressure pulses, such as by employing air-vessel equalisation.
[F04B 11/0058] with piston speed control -- A positive-displacement liquid machine that uses piston-speed control to smooth pressure fluctuations, often by means of air-filled vessels that equalise pulses in reciprocating pumps or compressors. This technique improves flow steadiness and reduces vibration in hydraulic systems.
[F04B 11/0066] with special shape of the actuating element -- The CPC subclass F04B 11/0066 covers displacement machines for liquids that incorporate an actuating element having a special shape. It relates to devices that equalise pressure pulses, such as those using air vessels, where the geometry of the piston, diaphragm or other moving part is designed to improve pulse damping. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering positive-displacement machines for liquids.
[F04B 11/0075] connected in series -- This CPC subclass addresses positive-displacement machines for liquids that are connected in series to equalise pressure pulses, typically employing air vessels or similar devices to smooth out pulsations in the fluid flow.
[F04B 11/0083] the pistons having different cross-sections -- This subclass concerns positive-displacement liquid machines that use pistons whose cross-section changes along the stroke. Varying piston geometry is employed to modify flow characteristics and to help equalise pressure pulses, for example when air vessels are used for pulse smoothing.
[F04B 11/0091] using a special shape of fluid pass, e.g. throttles, ducts -- The entry covers the use of specially shaped fluid passages such as throttles or ducts within positive-displacement machines for liquids to regulate flow and equalise pulsing pressures, often employing air vessels or similar devices for pulse smoothing.
[F04B 1/107] with actuating or actuated elements at the outer ends of the cylinders -- A sub-category of positive-displacement pumps in which the drive or driven components-such as pistons, diaphragms, or other actuators-are placed at the outer ends of the pump cylinders, defining the motion and fluid transfer from those end positions.
[F04B 1/1071] with rotary cylinder blocks -- A pump of the positive-displacement type that moves liquid by means of one or more rotary cylinder blocks, in which the blocks rotate to create sealed chambers that draw in fluid and then discharge it as the cylinders turn. This design is used for handling liquids and elastic fluids such as oils, fuels or hydraulic media, providing a steady, non-pulsating flow.
[F04B 1/1072] with cylinder blocks and actuating cams rotating together -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Control with cylinder blocks and actuating cams rotating together -> F04B1/1075
[F04B 1/1074] with two or more serially arranged radial piston-cylinder units -- This subclass of positive-displacement pumps specifies devices that contain two or more radial piston-cylinder units placed in series, allowing the liquid to be transferred through successive stages for higher pressure or flow in a single pump assembly. It falls under mechanical engineering, positive-displacement machines for liquids, and is used for pumping liquids or liquid-elastic fluids.
[F04B 1/1075] with cylinder blocks and actuating cams rotating together -- F04B 1/1075 covers positive-displacement pumps in which the cylinder block and the cam that drives it rotate as a single unit. The design integrates the actuating cam with the pump cylinders, providing synchronized motion for pumping liquids or liquid-elastic fluids. This subclass lies under Mechanical Engineering → Positive-Displacement Machines for Liquids → Pumps for liquids or for liquid and elastic fluids.
[F04B 1/1077] located side-by-side -- F04B 1/1077 designates pumps in which the driving mechanism and the pumping elements are arranged side-by-side, rather than in a coaxial or stacked configuration. This layout is typical for many positive-displacement machines for liquids, allowing compact installation and straightforward mechanical coupling.
[F04B 1/1078] with cylinder blocks and actuating cams rotating together -- This CPC subclass covers positive-displacement pumps in which the cylinder block and the cam that drives it are mounted on a common rotating shaft, so the cam and the cylinders turn together as a unit. Such arrangement synchronizes piston movement and is used in liquid or liquid-elastic-fluid pumping applications.
[F04B 1/113] with actuating or actuated elements at the inner ends of the cylinders -- This subclass covers positive-displacement pumps in which the actuating or actuated components are positioned at the inner ends of the pump cylinders, such as pistons, diaphragms or scroll elements directly within the cylinder bore.
[F04B 1/1133] with rotary cylinder blocks -- F04B 1/1133 covers positive-displacement liquid pumps that employ rotary cylinder blocks as the moving elements. These pumps use a set of rotating cylinders to trap and transfer fluid, typical of rotary block or gear-type designs, and fall within the broader category of mechanical engineering positive-displacement machines for liquids.
[F04B 1/1136] with a rotary cylinder with a single piston reciprocating within the cylinder -- F04B 1/1136 covers positive-displacement pumps that use a rotary cylinder in which a single piston reciprocates inside the cylinder to displace liquid. The design converts the cylinder's rotation into a back-and-forth piston motion, producing a continuous fluid flow.
[F04B 1/12] having cylinder axes coaxial with, or parallel or inclined to, main shaft axis -- This subgroup of positive-displacement pumps includes designs where the pump cylinders are aligned on the same line as the drive shaft, set parallel to it, or inclined at an angle, defining the relationship of the cylinder axes to the main shaft. It applies to pumps handling liquids or liquid-elastic fluids in mechanical engineering.
[F04B 1/122] Details or component parts, e.g. valves, sealings or lubrication means -- F04B 1/122 pertains to the sub-components of liquid positive-displacement pumps such as valves, seals and lubrication devices. It addresses the design, structure and function of these elements within pumps that move liquids or liquid-elastic fluid mixtures. The code is used in the CPC system for classifying mechanical-engineering inventions related to pump internals.
[F04B 1/124] Pistons -- F04B 1/124 refers to pistons used in positive-displacement pumps for liquids and elastic fluids, where a reciprocating piston inside a cylinder creates pressure differentials that drive fluid flow. The code falls under mechanical engineering classifications for pump designs that move fluid by direct displacement.
[F04B 1/126] Piston shoe retaining means -- F04B 1/126 covers devices that secure the piston shoe within positive-displacement pumps for liquids, ensuring proper alignment and preventing loss of the shoe during operation. The retaining means may include sleeves, retaining rings, or other locking features designed to hold the shoe in the pump cylinder.
[F04B 1/128] Driving means -- F04B 1/128 - Driving means covers the devices that supply power to positive-displacement pumps for liquids, converting energy into the motion required to move fluid. It includes electric, hydraulic, pneumatic or mechanical drives such as motors, gear trains, cams, pistons and other mechanisms that directly operate the pump's moving parts.
[F04B 1/14] having stationary cylinders -- F04B 1/14 classifies positive-displacement pumps in which the pumping cylinders remain stationary while a moving component-such as a rotor or crankshaft-drives pistons within them. This arrangement is typical of reciprocating and rotary-piston pumps that trap and transfer liquid volumes with each cycle.
[F04B 1/141] Details or component parts -- The CPC subclass F04B 1/141 deals with the detailed design and individual component parts of positive-displacement pumps for liquids or liquid-elastic fluid mixtures, such as shafts, impellers, valves, seals, housings and related accessories that influence pump operation, performance or servicing.
[F04B 1/143] Cylinders -- A cylinder is a pump component in which a moving piston or sliding element displaces liquid or liquid-elastic fluid to generate pressure or flow, forming the core of positive-displacement pump designs. It typically operates within a sealed barrel and converts mechanical motion into fluid movement.
[F04B 1/145] Housings -- F04B 1/145 covers the housings that enclose the working parts of positive-displacement pumps used for liquids or liquid-elastic fluid mixtures. It includes the design, construction and material aspects of the pump casing that contains the moving elements and provides a sealed environment for fluid transfer.
[F04B 1/146] Swash plates -- Swash plates are inclined disks that transmit rotational motion into reciprocating movement of pistons in axial-piston pumps or compressors. By varying the plate angle they regulate the displacement of liquids or liquid-elastic fluid mixtures in positive-displacement pump designs. In CPC class F04B 1/146 the term specifically covers these swash-plate components.
[F04B 1/148] Bearings therefor -- F04B 1/148 covers the bearings used in positive-displacement pumps for liquids or liquid-elastic fluids. It includes bearing types that support rotating shafts or rotors of these pumps, such as journal, thrust and hydrostatic bearings, and addresses their mounting, lubrication and sealing features.
[F04B 1/16] having two or more sets of cylinders or pistons -- F04B 1/16 designates positive-displacement pumps that use two or more sets of cylinders or pistons, allowing staged or parallel operation to increase flow or pressure. These pumps handle liquids alone or liquid-elastic fluid mixtures and belong to the mechanical-engineering class for liquid displacement machines.
[F04B 1/18] having self-acting distribution members, i.e. actuated by working fluid -- The F04B 1/18 subclass defines positive-displacement pumps that contain distribution members, such as valves or ports, which move automatically under the pressure of the working fluid itself, eliminating the need for separate actuation mechanisms.
[F04B 1/182] Check valves -- F04B 1/182 covers check valves, which are non-return devices that permit fluid to flow in only one direction and automatically close to block reverse flow. They are used in positive-displacement machines for liquids to protect pumps and pipelines from back-pressure.
[F04B 1/184] Cylindrical distribution members -- F04B 1/184 pertains to cylindrical distribution members employed in positive-displacement pumps for liquids, such as rods, tubes or shafts that convey fluid between pump chambers. These cylindrical components distribute liquid or elastic fluids within the pump to maintain flow and pressure.
[F04B 1/186] Conical distribution members -- Conical distribution members are components of positive-displacement pumps that guide the liquid or elastic fluid from the pumping chamber into a cone-shaped flow, using diffusers or guide vanes to achieve uniform distribution and reduce turbulence.
[F04B 1/188] Plate-like distribution members -- Plate-like distribution members are flat, plate-shaped elements used in positive-displacement pumps to separate and direct the flow of liquid or elastic fluids between chambers, ensuring even distribution and minimizing leakage. They typically slide or rotate against matching counterparts, creating sealed pockets that move the fluid through the pump.
[F04B 1/20] having rotary cylinder block -- F04B 1/20 relates to pumps that employ a rotary cylinder block to transfer liquids. The block consists of multiple cylinders that rotate within a housing, forming chambers that draw fluid in on one side of the rotor and expel it on the opposite side. This principle is used in rotary-vane and axial-piston pump designs.
[F04B 1/2007] Arrangements for pressing the cylinder barrel against the valve plate, e.g. by fluid pressure -- This CPC subclass covers arrangements that press the cylinder barrel against the valve plate, using fluid pressure, in positive displacement pumps for liquids.
[F04B 1/2014] Details or component parts -- F04B 1/2014 classifies the description of individual components, sub-assemblies and construction details of positive-displacement pumps for liquids or liquid-elastic fluids, covering elements such as rotors, pistons, valves, sealing arrangements and housings that form part of the overall pump mechanism.
[F04B 1/2021] characterised by the contact area between cylinder barrel and valve plate -- The F04B 1/2021 subgroup covers positive-displacement pumps whose design is defined by the contact area between the cylinder barrel and the valve plate. This characteristic affects sealing quality, wear patterns and overall hydraulic efficiency of liquid or liquid-elastic fluid pumps.
[F04B 1/2028] Bearings -- The entry defines bearing arrangements for positive-displacement pumps, covering the design, placement and support of rotating or reciprocating shafts and other moving parts, together with associated lubrication and sealing features. It addresses bearing components used in pumps that handle liquids or liquid-elastic fluids.
[F04B 1/2035] Cylinder barrels -- Cylinder barrels are the stationary housing components that contain the moving pistons or plungers within positive-displacement pumps for liquids and elastic fluids. They provide the sealed cylindrical chamber in which the pistons reciprocate, defining the pump's displacement volume and withstanding the hydraulic pressures generated during operation.
[F04B 1/2042] Valves -- F04B 1/2042 - Valves used in positive-displacement pumps for liquids or liquid-elastic fluid mixtures, covering devices that control flow, pressure or direction within the pump assembly. The class includes valve types, their placement and operation as part of pump systems in mechanical engineering.
[F04B 1/205] Cylindrical -- F04B 1/205 Cylindrical covers positive-displacement pumps whose working chamber or moving element is cylindrical, such as piston or diaphragm pumps that use a cylindrical volume to move liquid. The subclass is applied when the cylindrical shape of the pump's active part is a defining characteristic of its operation.
[F04B 1/2057] Conical -- The subclass includes pumps that employ a conical geometry in their moving or stationary components, such as conical screws, rotors or chambers, to positively displace liquids or liquid-elastic mixtures.
[F04B 1/2064] Housings -- The CPC subclass F04B 1/2064, titled Housings, covers the structural enclosures of positive-displacement pumps for liquids and elastic fluids. It includes casings, shells, bodies and other containers that retain the working parts, sustain pressure, protect the mechanism and define the flow path within the pump.
[F04B 1/2071] Bearings for cylinder barrels -- Bearings for cylinder barrels are components that support and guide the rotating barrel in positive-displacement liquid pumps, reducing friction and wear while maintaining precise alignment of moving parts. They may be made of metal, polymer or composite materials and are selected to withstand the pressures, speeds and fluid-compatibility requirements of the pump's operation.
[F04B 1/2078] Swash plates -- Swash plates are angled disks used in positive-displacement pumps to convert rotary motion of the drive shaft into the reciprocating motion of pistons or diaphragms, enabling precise fluid displacement. They are characteristic of axial-piston pump designs and similar hydraulic machines where the plate's tilt determines the pump's displacement and pressure output.
[F04B 1/2085] Bearings for swash plates or driving axles -- This CPC subclass concerns bearings that support the swash plate or driving axle in positive-displacement liquid pumps. It includes any journal, thrust or rolling-element bearing used to guide or locate these components, reducing friction and wear in mechanisms that convert rotary motion to reciprocating motion.
[F04B 1/2092] Means for connecting rotating cylinder barrels and rotating inclined swash plates -- The entry covers devices that connect rotating cylinder barrels to inclined swash plates in positive-displacement liquid pumps, providing a mechanical link that transmits torque and synchronises axial movement while maintaining sealing and alignment.
[F04B 1/22] having two or more sets of cylinders or pistons -- This subclass defines positive-displacement pumps that employ two or more sets of cylinders or pistons to transfer liquids or liquid-elastic fluids. Such designs include multi-stage piston or rotary-cylinder pumps where the multiple cylinder groups operate in sequence or parallel to increase pressure or flow.
[F04B 1/24] inclined to the main shaft axis -- F04B 1/24 covers pumps in which the pumping elements, such as impellers or rotors, are arranged at an angle to the main shaft axis rather than being parallel. This inclination is used to achieve specific flow or pressure characteristics in positive-displacement machines for liquids. It falls under the broader group of pumps for liquids within mechanical engineering.
[F04B 1/26] Control -- F04B 1/26 covers control arrangements for positive-displacement pumps used to move liquids or liquid-elastic mixtures. It includes devices and methods that regulate pump speed, flow rate, direction, or start-stop operation, such as throttling valves, speed governors, and electronic controllers. The class pertains to mechanical engineering applications of pump control systems.
[F04B 1/28] of machines or pumps with stationary cylinders -- F04B 1/28 covers positive-displacement pumps or machines in which the cylinders stay fixed while a moving piston or rotor generates fluid flow, used for liquids or liquid-elastic mixtures. These devices belong to the broader mechanical-engineering category of pumps with stationary cylinders.
[F04B 1/29] by varying the relative positions of a swash plate and a cylinder block -- This entry covers positive-displacement pumps that vary the relative positions of a swash plate and a cylinder block to move fluid, typical of axial-piston pump designs. The adjustment of the swash-plate angle changes the stroke of the pistons, thereby controlling the flow rate.
[F04B 1/295] by changing the inclination of the swash plate -- F04B 1/295 describes positive-displacement pumps that vary their output by altering the inclination of a swash plate. By changing the plate's angle the pump adjusts the volume of liquid moved per cycle, providing controllable flow in liquid and liquid-elastic-fluid applications.
[F04B 1/30] of machines or pumps with rotary cylinder blocks -- F04B 1/30 pertains to positive-displacement pumps that employ a rotary cylinder block to form pumping chambers. The rotating block creates and collapses cavities, moving liquid or elastic fluids in devices such as rotary-vane, gear or lobe pumps.
[F04B 13/00] Pumps specially modified to deliver fixed or variable measured quantities -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pumps for liquids specially adapted for dosing purposes or for mixing two or more fluids.
[F04B 13/02] of two or more fluids at the same time -- This subclass covers pumps designed to simultaneously handle two or more fluids, delivering them in fixed or adjustable measured amounts for processes where fluids must be combined or metered concurrently.
[F04B 1/303] by turning the valve plate -- F04B 1/303 covers positive-displacement pumps that operate by rotating a valve plate to regulate the admission and discharge of liquid or elastic fluid. The turning plate alternately opens and closes ports, directing flow through fixed chambers and providing a steady output pressure.
[F04B 1/306] by turning the swash plate, e.g. with fixed inclination -- The code F04B 1/306 refers to positive-displacement pumps that generate flow by rotating a swash plate that has a fixed tilt angle. The rotating plate causes pistons or gears to move in and out, delivering liquids or liquid-elastic mixtures. This classification falls under mechanical engineering, positive-displacement machines for liquids.
[F04B 1/32] by varying the relative positions of a swash plate and a cylinder block -- This subclass covers pumps that achieve fluid displacement by varying the relative positions of a swash plate and a cylinder block, as in axial-piston pump designs where the tilt of the swash plate changes piston stroke and thus flow rate.
[F04B 1/322] by moving the swash plate in a direction perpendicular to the axis of rotation of the cylinder barrel -- F04B 1/322 covers positive-displacement liquid pumps that obtain fluid movement by shifting a swash plate sideways, i.e., in a direction perpendicular to the cylinder barrel's axis of rotation. The transverse motion changes the pistons' stroke, allowing the pump to vary displacement while maintaining a rotary drive.
[F04B 1/324] by changing the inclination of the swash plate -- F04B 1/324 designates pumps that vary their displacement by altering the tilt angle of a swash plate. The change in inclination adjusts the stroke of the pistons, allowing the pump to deliver a controllable flow rate of liquids or elastic fluids. This principle is used in variable-displacement axial-piston pump designs.
[F04B 1/326] using wedges -- F04B 1/326 covers positive-displacement pumps that employ wedges as the primary moving element to transfer liquids or liquid-elastic mixtures. The wedge mechanism creates a variable-volume chamber that draws fluid in and forces it out as the wedge slides or rotates, enabling precise flow control in applications such as hydraulic systems, fuel delivery and metering devices. This subclass falls under the broader category of mechanical engineering pumps for liquids.
[F04B 1/328] by changing the inclination of the axis of the cylinder barrel relative to the swash plate -- F04B 1/328 designates positive-displacement pumps that achieve variable flow by altering the angle between the cylinder barrel's axis and the swash plate. By changing this inclination the pump modifies the piston stroke, allowing continuous adjustment of output volume for liquids or liquid-elastic mixtures.
[F04B 1/34] Control not provided for in groups -- F04B 1/34 covers positive-displacement pumps for liquids or liquid-elastic fluid mixtures where the method of control is not specified in any other subgroup. It includes pump designs that lack dedicated flow, pressure or speed regulation mechanisms, or where such controls are considered generic and not delineated elsewhere in the classification.
[F04B 15/00] Pumps adapted to handle specific fluids, e.g. by selection of specific materials for pumps or pump parts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pumps specially adapted for pumping e.g. viscous fluids, fluids at high temperatures, corrosive fluids or cryogenic fluids.  Various types of pumps for liquids and for elastic fluids not provided for in other groups, e.g. pumps for corrosive fluids or for cryogenic fluids.
[F04B 15/02] the fluids being viscous or non-homogeneous -- F04B 15/02 covers pumps specially adapted for handling fluids that are highly viscous or non-homogeneous, such as sludges, pastes, emulsions or suspensions. It includes features like material choices, clearance adjustments, heating, agitation or other modifications that enable the pump to move these difficult liquids efficiently and without damage.
[F04B 15/023] supply of fluid to the pump by gravity through a hopper, e.g. without intake valve -- This CPC group relates to positive-displacement liquid pumps that receive their feed by gravity from a hopper, eliminating the need for an intake valve. The arrangement is used when the pump is designed for particular fluids, often with material choices tailored to the handled liquid.
[F04B 15/04] the fluids being hot or corrosive -- F04B 15/04 covers pumps that are specially adapted for fluids that are hot or corrosive, requiring the use of particular materials or component designs to resist temperature and chemical attack. This subclass pertains to positive-displacement liquid-moving machines engineered for aggressive operating conditions.
[F04B 15/06] for liquids near their boiling point, e.g. under subnormal pressure -- This subclass covers pumps designed to move liquids that are close to their boiling point, typically operating under reduced or sub-atmospheric pressure to prevent vaporisation. Such pumps may incorporate special materials or construction to handle the thermal and cavitation stresses associated with near-boiling fluids.
[F04B 15/08] the liquids having low boiling points
[F04B 17/00] Pumps characterised by combination with, or adaptation to, specific driving engines or motors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pumps for liquids driven by specific engines or other driving means, such as e.g. electric motors, piezoelectric means, solar means, wind energy, or combustion engines. In this group are also classified mobile pumping systems, e.g. pumps on vehicles.
[F04B 17/003] driven by piezoelectric means  F04B43/046  and  F04B43/095  take precedence -- F04B 17/003 covers positive-displacement pumps for liquids that are driven by piezoelectric means, meaning the pumping action is produced by the deformation of piezoelectric elements rather than traditional mechanical drives. This subgroup falls under pumps adapted to specific engines or motors, and more detailed subclasses F04B43/046 and F04B43/095 take precedence over it.
[F04B 17/006] Solar operated -- F04B 17/006 covers positive-displacement liquid pumps that are driven by solar energy, meaning the pump's power source is a solar-powered engine or motor integrated with the pump mechanism. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically pumps adapted to particular driving engines.
[F04B 17/02] driven by wind motors -- F04B 17/02 covers positive-displacement pumps that are powered by wind-driven engines or motors. The class includes pumps specifically adapted or combined with wind turbines to move liquids.
[F04B 17/03] driven by electric motors -- F04B 17/03 denotes pumps of the positive-displacement type that are driven by electric motors, covering designs where the pump is combined with or adapted to an electric motor as the prime mover. This subclass falls under the mechanical-engineering section of the CPC, within positive-displacement machines for liquids.
[F04B 17/04] using solenoids -- F04B 17/04 covers pumps that are driven by solenoid actuators, where the solenoid directly provides the mechanical energy for displacement of fluid. This subclass falls under positive-displacement pumps adapted to specific driving engines or motors within mechanical engineering.
[F04B 17/042] the solenoid motor being separated from the fluid flow -- This CPC subclass covers pumps where the solenoid actuator is physically separated from the fluid stream, meaning the solenoid motor operates outside the flow path and drives the pump via a linkage or other means. It applies to positive-displacement liquid pumps combined with specific drive units where the drive is not immersed in the fluid.
[F04B 17/044] using solenoids directly actuating the piston -- F04B 17/044 covers positive-displacement liquid pumps in which the piston is driven directly by a solenoid actuator rather than by a conventional mechanical linkage or engine. The classification applies to designs that integrate the solenoid as the primary driving element, enabling precise, rapid control of piston motion for accurate flow regulation.
[F04B 17/046] the fluid flowing through the moving part of the motor -- F04B 17/046 pertains to positive-displacement pumps that are combined with or adapted to specific driving engines or motors, where the fluid to be pumped flows through the moving parts of the motor itself. This subclass addresses integrated pump-motor arrangements in which the working fluid passes inside the rotor or stator, enabling compact designs and direct energy transfer.
[F04B 17/048] the fluid flowing around the moving part of the motor -- F04B 17/048 covers pumps in which the fluid circulates around the moving part of the driving motor, detailing how the working fluid interacts with the motor's rotor or moving components as part of a combined pump-motor system. This subclass falls under positive-displacement machines for liquids that are adapted to specific engines or motors.
[F04B 17/05] driven by internal-combustion engines -- F04B 17/05 covers positive-displacement pumps that are powered directly by internal-combustion engines, including adaptations that integrate the engine with the pump for compact or self-contained systems. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering and relates to pumps combined with specific driving engines.
[F04B 17/06] Mobile combinations -- F04B 17/06 covers pump systems that are combined with or adapted to mobile power sources, such as engines or motors mounted on vehicles, trailers, or other transportable units, enabling the pump to be moved and operated as a unit.
[F04B 19/00] Machines or pumps having pertinent characteristics not provided for in, or of interest apart from, groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  In this group are classified various types of pumps not provided for in other groups, such as e.g. free-piston pumps, micropumps, pumps with movable cylinders, pumps for storage containers, scoop devices adhesion type pumps, or pumps using the effect of heat expansion.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Micropumps having plate-like flexible members. -> F04B43/043
[F04B 19/003] free-piston type pumps -- Free-piston type pumps are displacement machines in which a piston moves back-and-forth within a cylinder without a connecting rod or crankshaft, directly driving fluid flow. Their reciprocating motion creates pressure spikes that can be used for high-pressure hydraulic or fuel-injection applications, often offering compact design and reduced mechanical wear. This class covers such pumps that do not fit into other listed positive-displacement groups.
[F04B 19/006] Micropumps  F04B43/043  and  F04B43/095  take precedence -- Micropumps are small positive-displacement machines that move liquids by forcing a confined volume through a tiny pump chamber, used where low flow rates and compact size are required. The CPC group F04B 19/006 covers these devices, and it is subordinated to the more specific groups F04B43/043 and F04B43/095, which take precedence.
[F04B 19/02] having movable cylinders -- This group covers positive-displacement pumps whose working volume is varied by cylinders that can move relative to each other, such as reciprocating, plunger or piston pumps. It applies to machines whose design is defined by movable cylinder components used to displace liquid.
[F04B 19/022] reciprocating cylinders -- Reciprocating cylinders are pistons or chambers that move back and forth within a pump housing to displace liquid volumes in a controlled manner, typically used in piston, diaphragm, or plunger pumps. The motion creates alternating suction and pressure phases that draw fluid into the cylinder and then expel it, enabling precise flow rates and pressures.
[F04B 19/025] cylinders rotating around their own axis -- F04B 19/025 designates positive-displacement liquid pumps that use a cylinder rotating about its own axis as the moving chamber. The rotating cylinder directly displaces fluid, as found in rotary-cylinder or screw-type pumps, and is classified when the device's characteristics are not covered by more specific groups.
[F04B 19/027] cylinders oscillating around an axis perpendicular to their own axis -- F04B 19/027 covers machines in which cylindrical parts are made to oscillate about an axis that is perpendicular to the cylinder's own longitudinal axis, a design used in pumps and hydraulic devices to create reciprocating motion or control fluid flow.
[F04B 19/04] Pumps for special use -- F04B 19/04 covers pumps designed for special or atypical applications where the machine's characteristics differ from those listed in the standard groups, including devices tailored for unique operating conditions, substances, or installation requirements. This subclass groups such specialised displacement machines for liquids under the broader mechanical-engineering category of positive-displacement pumps.
[F04B 19/06] Pumps for delivery of both liquid and elastic fluids at the same time -- The code F04B 19/06 covers pumps that simultaneously convey a liquid and an elastic fluid, such as oil mixed with gas or slurry, in a single device. These are positive-displacement machines designed to handle two-phase or mixed media during operation.
[F04B 19/08] Scoop devices -- F04B 19/08 covers scoop devices used in positive-displacement pumps for liquids, where a bucket-like element draws fluid into a chamber and then forces it out during each pump cycle. These devices are classified separately from other pump types because the scooping action is the primary means of fluid movement.
[F04B 19/10] of wheel type -- F04B 19/10 covers positive-displacement pumps that use a wheel-type mechanism to move liquid, where rotating wheels or drums create sealed compartments that draw in fluid and deliver it at a constant flow. Examples include gear, lobe and barrel-type pumps that rely on the rotary motion of intermeshing wheels.
[F04B 19/12] of helical or screw-type -- F04B 19/12 covers positive-displacement machines for liquids that use a helical or screw mechanism and possess features not specifically listed in other groups. It includes pumps and similar devices whose operation relies on a rotating screw or helical screw to move fluid, addressing niche designs outside the main classifications.
[F04B 19/14] of endless-chain type, e.g. with the chains carrying pistons co-operating with open-ended cylinders -- F04B 19/14 covers positive-displacement pumps that use an endless chain as the moving element, where the chain carries pistons that slide within open-ended cylinders to displace fluid. This sub-group is part of the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for displacement machines for liquids, focusing on chain-driven piston pump designs not covered by other categories.
[F04B 19/16] Adhesion-type liquid-lifting devices -- F04B 19/16 covers machines that lift liquids by adhesion rather than pressure or suction. These adhesion-type devices rely on surface tension or capillary action to draw fluid upward, such as wicks, porous media, or adhesive rollers, and are classified when their principle differs from conventional pumps.
[F04B 19/18] Adhesion members therefor -- The F04B 19/18 subclass covers the various adhesion members used in positive-displacement liquid pumps, such as seals, clamping devices, shoes, and other fastening elements that attach or secure pump components to one another or to the machine housing. It pertains to parts that are not otherwise classified in the main pump groups.
[F04B 19/20] Other positive-displacement pumps -- F04B 19/20 covers positive-displacement pumps that are not allocated to any more specific subgroup within the class. It includes miscellaneous pump types whose operating principle is positive displacement, such as unique gear, diaphragm, rotary-vane or other designs that do not fit existing categories. The entry is used for machines of interest apart from the defined groups.
[F04B 19/22] of reciprocating-piston type -- F04B 19/22 covers positive-displacement machines for liquids that operate with a reciprocating-piston mechanism and are not classified elsewhere in the F04B series.
