[F04B 53/108] Valves characterised by the material -- F04B 53/108 covers valves employed in positive-displacement liquid machines whose defining feature is the material from which they are made, such as metal, polymer, ceramic or composite. The subclass groups any valve type-check, relief, shut-off, etc.-when the material itself is the primary criterion for classification, separate from the valve's function or design details.
[F04B 53/1082] magnetic -- F04B 53/1082 covers magnetic elements used in positive-displacement fluid machines, such as magnetic couplings, bearings, seals or actuators that provide drive, positioning or sensing functions. The subclass includes magnetic accessories not otherwise specified in the main groups for pumps or compressors.
[F04B 53/1085] having means for limiting the opening height  F04B53/101  and  F04B53/1035  take precedence
[F04B 53/1087] Valve seats -- F04B 53/1087 designates valve seats, the components that provide a sealing surface for the closing of a valve in positive displacement liquid machines, ensuring proper flow control and preventing leakage.
[F04B 53/109] inlet and outlet valve forming one unit -- F04B 53/109 covers a combined inlet-outlet valve that functions as a single unit in positive-displacement liquid machines such as pumps. The valve integrates both supply and discharge passages, simplifying construction and reducing the number of separate parts, and is classified as a component part not otherwise grouped.
[F04B 53/1092] and one single element forming both the inlet and outlet closure member -- F04B 53/1092 covers a single component that functions simultaneously as the inlet and outlet closure in positive-displacement liquid pumps, combining the two valve functions in one element to regulate fluid flow entering and leaving the pump chamber. This type of part is used where a unified sealing or gating piece simplifies the pump's design and operation.
[F04B 53/1095] Valves linked to another valve of another pumping chamber -- F04B 53/1095 classifies valves that are mechanically linked to a valve in another pumping chamber of a positive-displacement liquid pump. The connection synchronizes the opening or closing of the two valves, enabling coordinated flow control between chambers. It applies to component parts and accessories of multi-chamber pumps where valve linkage is a functional feature.
[F04B 53/1097] with means for lifting the closure member for pump cleaning purposes -- A component of a positive-displacement liquid pump that includes a mechanism for raising the pump's closure or cover to facilitate cleaning of the pump interior. This feature enables easy removal or lifting of the sealing member during maintenance, improving access to the pump's working parts. It pertains to accessories or details not covered by other groups within the mechanical-engineering subclass.
[F04B 53/12] arranged in or on pistons -- F04B 53/12 covers positive-displacement machines for liquids where components, details or accessories are arranged in or on the pistons, such as piston rings, seals, coatings or mounting elements.
[F04B 53/121] the valve being an annular ring surrounding the piston, e.g. an O-ring -- F04B 53/121 covers piston-driven positive-displacement liquid pumps that incorporate a valve formed as an annular ring around the piston, such as an O-ring, which controls fluid flow during the pumping cycle. This subclass focuses on the valve's shape and placement rather than other pump components.
[F04B 53/122] the piston being free-floating, e.g. the valve being formed between the actuating rod and the piston -- F04B 53/122 covers component parts of positive-displacement liquid machines in which the piston is not fixed but free-floating, allowing a valve to be formed in the gap between the actuating rod and the piston. The classification pertains to designs where the piston moves independently of the drive mechanism, enabling simplified sealing or flow control.
[F04B 53/123] Flexible valves -- F04B 53/123 designates flexible valves used as component parts, details or accessories for positive-displacement machines that move liquids. These valves provide a pliable flow-control element within the pump or hydraulic system, separate from the main valve groups. They are classified under mechanical engineering.
[F04B 53/124] Oscillating valves -- F04B 53/124 deals with oscillating valves used in positive-displacement machines for liquids. The subclass covers component parts, details or accessories of such valves that are not otherwise specified in the classification.
[F04B 53/125] Reciprocating valves -- F04B 53/125 covers valves that move back and forth to control fluid flow in reciprocating positive-displacement pumps and similar machines. The classification includes valve designs, actuation mechanisms and accessories that are not otherwise classified in the main groups, focusing on component parts specific to reciprocating pump operation.
[F04B 53/126] Ball valves -- Ball valves are rotary-type shutoff devices that use a perforated spherical disc to control fluid flow in positive-displacement liquid machines. This subclass covers the design, sealing, actuation and material details of the valve body, stem, seats and accessories that are not classified elsewhere. It is used for describing components that regulate or stop flow within pumps, compressors or other liquid-displacement equipment.
[F04B 53/127] Disc valves -- Disc valves are rotating or reciprocating disks that open and close to control fluid flow in positive-displacement pumps and compressors. They are typically mounted on a shaft and pivot or slide to allow intake and discharge passages to be alternately exposed, providing precise regulation of liquid movement. This type of valve is used as a component part in machines that move fluids by trapping and transferring fixed volumes.
[F04B 53/128] Annular disc valves -- F04B 53/128 pertains to annular disc valves, a valve type used in positive-displacement machines for liquids where a disc-shaped element arranged in an annular fashion controls the flow by opening, closing or throttling the passage. It falls under component parts, details or accessories of such machines.
[F04B 53/129] Poppet valves -- Poppet valves are disc- or mushroom-shaped closure members that seat against a matching surface to regulate the flow of liquid in positive-displacement machines such as pumps and compressors. They are mounted on a stem and can be opened or closed manually or by a spring or actuator, providing a simple, reliable means of starting, stopping or throttling liquid passage.
[F04B 53/14] Pistons, piston-rods or piston-rod connections -- The CPC subclass F04B 53/14 covers pistons, the rods that link pistons to a crankshaft, and the various connections between pistons and rods in positive-displacement liquid machines such as pumps and compressors. It includes design features, materials, sealing arrangements and auxiliary components that are not classified elsewhere in the piston-related groups.
[F04B 53/141] Intermediate liquid piston between the driving piston and the pumped liquid  F04B43/06  and  F04B43/10  take precedence -- An intermediate liquid piston placed between the driving piston and the pumped liquid is a component part used in positive-displacement machines for liquids. It acts as a hydraulic buffer that transmits motion while isolating the driving piston from the pumped fluid, helping to smooth flow, reduce wear and improve sealing. This element is classified in the F04B 53/141 group, which covers accessories not otherwise specified for such machines.
[F04B 53/142] Intermediate liquid-piston between a driving piston and a driven piston  F04B9/10 ,  F04B43/06 ,  F04B43/10  and  F04B53/141  take precedence -- F04B 53/142 covers component parts for positive-displacement liquid machines, specifically an intermediate liquid piston positioned between a driving piston and a driven piston. The liquid column functions as a movable barrier that transmits force from the driver to the driven piston, as seen in hydraulic pumps and motors, and takes precedence over the related sub-groups F04B 9/10, F04B 43/06, F04B 43/10 and F04B 53/141.
[F04B 53/143] Sealing provided on the piston -- This subclass covers the provision of sealing elements directly on pistons used in positive-displacement machines for liquids, including seals, gaskets, or packing designed to prevent fluid leakage between the piston and cylinder. It relates to component parts or accessories that are not otherwise classified elsewhere in the piston group.
[F04B 53/144] Adaptation of piston-rods -- The CPC class F04B 53/144 covers modifications or adaptations of piston-rods used in positive-displacement machines that move liquids, such as pumps or compressors. It concerns design changes, auxiliary features or improvements to the rod itself rather than the surrounding mechanism.
[F04B 53/145] Rod shock absorber -- The rod shock absorber is a damping component used in positive-displacement liquid machines such as hydraulic cylinders, where a rod moves within a sealed cylinder and the absorber cushions the motion by forcing fluid through restricted passages or valves, thereby converting kinetic energy into heat and reducing shock and vibration.
[F04B 53/146] Piston-rod guiding arrangements -- This subclass covers the guiding arrangements for piston rods in positive-displacement liquid machines such as pumps or compressors, including bearings, sleeves, guides and related support structures that maintain rod alignment and reduce wear, and is listed under component parts not otherwise specified.
[F04B 53/147] Mounting or detaching of piston rod -- F04B 53/147 covers methods and mechanisms for mounting or detaching the piston rod in positive-displacement liquid pumps. It addresses the components, details and accessories used to secure or release the rod, facilitating assembly, maintenance or repair.
[F04B 53/148] the piston being provided with channels which are coacting with the cylinder and are used as a distribution member for another piston-cylinder unit -- This subclass covers positive-displacement liquid pumps in which the piston is machined with internal channels that cooperate with the cylinder to route fluid to a second piston-cylinder unit, serving as a distribution member for the additional stage.
[F04B 53/16] Casings -- F04B 53/16 pertains to the casings of positive-displacement liquid machines, covering housings, shells, covers and protective enclosures that contain or support the moving parts but are not otherwise classified as principal components. It includes any accessory or detail related to the outer structure of pumps, compressors or similar devices when the specific casing is of interest apart from the core functional groups.
[F04B 53/162] Adaptations of cylinders -- F04B 53/162 relates to adaptations of cylinders in positive-displacement liquid machines, covering structural or material modifications, sealing improvements, cooling arrangements, auxiliary attachments and other alterations that enhance performance, efficiency, durability or integration of the cylinder with the overall device.
[F04B 53/164] Stoffing boxes -- Stoffing boxes are sealing assemblies fitted around a rotating shaft in positive-displacement liquid machines such as pumps, allowing the shaft to pass through the housing while preventing fluid leakage. They typically contain packing material or replaceable liners and may include adjustment mechanisms to maintain a tight seal as wear occurs.
[F04B 53/166] Cylinder liners -- The CPC code F04B 53/166 designates cylinder liners, the internal sleeves fitted inside the cylinders of positive-displacement machines for liquids such as reciprocating pumps. They serve as wear-resistant, sealing and heat-dissipating components and are classified as separate parts or accessories apart from the main pump assembly.
[F04B 53/168] Mounting of cylinder liners in cylinders -- This subclass covers methods and devices for installing and securing cylinder liners within the bores of hydraulic or fluid-powered pistons, including clamps, sleeves, sealing arrangements, and related components that ensure proper alignment and sealing of the liner. It falls under the mechanical engineering field for positive-displacement liquid machines.
[F04B 53/18] Lubricating -- The CPC subclass F04B 53/18 covers lubricating devices or systems that are used with positive-displacement liquid machines, such as pumps or compressors, but are not integral to the main functional groups of the machine. It includes accessories, parts or details whose sole purpose is to supply, distribute or manage lubricants for reducing wear, friction or heat in the moving components.
[F04B 53/20] Filtering -- F04B 53/20 designates the filtering elements, assemblies and accessories employed in positive-displacement machines for liquids such as pumps and compressors. The class covers any filter unit that is part of or added to the machine for removing contaminants from the fluid stream, irrespective of the filter's specific construction.
[F04B 53/22] Arrangements for enabling ready assembly or disassembly -- F04B 53/22 relates to design features that make it easy to put together or take apart parts of positive-displacement liquid machines, such as quick-connect fittings, modular housings or detachable casings, helping simplify maintenance, replacement or reconfiguration of the equipment.
[F04B 7/00] Piston machines or pumps characterised by having positively-driven valving -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Valves and distribution members being mounted in or adapted for pumps, wherein the valves are mechanically driven, actuated by electro-magnetic means, fluid actuated, or by reciprocating pistons.
[F04B 7/0003] the distribution member forming both the inlet and discharge distributor for one single pumping chamber  F04B7/0208  takes precedence -- This subclass covers piston or positive-displacement pumps in which a single distribution element serves both as the inlet and the discharge conduit for one pumping chamber, combining the two flow paths in one component. It applies to designs where the distribution member forms the inlet and outlet for a single chamber.
[F04B 7/0007] and having a rotating movement -- F04B 7/0007 covers piston pumps in which the valve operation is driven positively and the pistons execute a rotating motion rather than reciprocating straight-line movement. This subcategory falls under positive-displacement machines for liquids within mechanical engineering.
[F04B 7/0011] and having an oscillating movement -- F04B 7/0011 covers piston-type positive-displacement machines or pumps that use positively driven valves and incorporate an oscillating motion as part of their operation, a subset of mechanical-engineering devices for moving liquids.
[F04B 7/0015] and having a slidable movement -- The subclass F04B 7/0015 covers piston-type machines or pumps that use positively driven valving and incorporate a slidable movement in the valve or piston mechanism, a feature of positive-displacement liquid handling equipment.
[F04B 7/0019] a common distribution member forming a single discharge distributor for a plurality of pumping chambers  F04B7/0233  takes precedence -- F04B 7/0019 classifies piston or pump designs in which a single distribution member serves as a common discharge distributor for several pumping chambers. It falls within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under positive-displacement machines for liquids, specifically piston machines with positively-driven valving.
[F04B 7/0023] and having a rotating movement -- F04B 7/0023 covers positive-displacement piston pumps that use a rotating movement, such as a cam, crank or rotor, to drive the valve mechanism, opening and closing the valve as the machine turns.
[F04B 7/0026] and having an oscillating movement -- F04B 7/0026 covers piston-type positive-displacement machines or pumps that incorporate an oscillating movement as part of their operation, with the valve action driven positively rather than by fluid pressure.
[F04B 7/003] and having a slidable movement -- This subclass covers piston-type positive-displacement pumps whose valves are driven positively and move by a sliding action, rather than rotating or swinging. It applies to liquid-handling machines where the valve's motion is linear and directly actuated by the piston mechanism.
[F04B 7/0034] and having an orbital movement, e.g. elbow-pipe type members -- This CPC subclass covers piston machines or pumps with positively-driven valving that employ an orbital movement, for example elbow-pipe type members. It falls under the positive-displacement machines for liquids category within mechanical engineering.

[F04B 7/0042] with specific kinematics of the distribution member  F04B7/0003 ,  F04B7/0019  take precedence
[F04B 7/0046] for rotating distribution members -- F04B 7/0046 covers piston machines or pumps that use positively-driven valving and employ rotating components such as rotors or impellers to distribute the fluid within the cylinder, enabling precise flow control.
[F04B 7/0049] for oscillating distribution members -- This subclass covers piston-type positive-displacement pumps that use oscillating distribution elements to control fluid flow, such as swinging or reciprocating valve plates, flappers or lobes that alternately open and close ports during the piston's travel. The classification applies to designs where the distribution members move back and forth rather than rotate continuously.
[F04B 7/0053] for reciprocating distribution members -- The code refers to piston-type positive-displacement pumps in which the valve action is mechanically linked to the reciprocating piston, directing the liquid to multiple outlets or chambers. It covers designs where the reciprocating distribution members-such as slide or spool valves-are driven directly by the piston's motion to control flow paths.
[F04B 7/0057] Mechanical driving means therefor, e.g. cams -- The subclass F04B 7/0057 covers the mechanical devices that provide the driving motion for positively-driven valve mechanisms in displacement pumps, such as cam shafts, cam levers or other cam-type actuators that translate rotary motion into the valve's timed opening and closing.
[F04B 7/0061] for a rotating member -- The subclass F04B 7/0061 covers piston-type pumps or positive-displacement machines in which the valve that controls liquid flow is driven by a rotating member such as a shaft or rotor, providing actuation through the rotation. It lies within the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, positive-displacement machines for liquids, specifically piston machines with positively-driven valving.
[F04B 7/0065] being mounted on the main shaft -- F04B 7/0065 covers piston-type positive-displacement pumps where the valve mechanism is directly mounted on the main shaft of the machine, integrating the valving with the drive shaft and eliminating the need for separate gearing or couplings. This configuration simplifies construction and transmits motion directly from the shaft to the valves.
[F04B 7/0069] for a sliding member -- F04B 7/0069 pertains to piston or pump designs in which the valving is actuated by a sliding component such as a sliding valve or plate that moves relative to the pump housing to control fluid flow. This subclass falls within positive-displacement machines for liquids where the valve operation is directly driven by the piston motion.
[F04B 7/0073] the member being of the lost-motion type, e.g. friction-actuated members, or having means for pushing it against or pulling it from its seat -- F04B 7/0073 classifies piston pumps or other positive-displacement machines whose valve member works with lost-motion action, such as friction-actuated components, or includes mechanisms that push the member against its seat or pull it away, enabling controlled opening and closing of the valve.
[F04B 7/0076] the members being actuated by electro-magnetic means -- Subclass F04B 7/0076 covers piston or pump devices in which the moving parts that control fluid flow-such as valves, ports or valve members-are driven by electromagnetic means. This includes solenoid-operated valves, electromagnetic actuators or magnetic cams that open, close or shift the members, providing electrically controlled positive-displacement operation.
[F04B 7/008] the distribution being realised by moving the cylinder itself, e.g. by sliding or swinging  F04B7/0291  takes precedence -- F04B 7/008 classifies positive-displacement liquid machines in which the distribution of fluid is accomplished by moving the cylinder itself, for example by sliding or swinging the cylinder, as a means of delivering the working medium.
[F04B 7/0084] Component parts or details specially adapted therefor -- F04B 7/0084 covers specially adapted components and detailed parts of piston-type positive-displacement liquid pumps, such as custom valves, seals, housings or moving elements designed for the specific operation of these machines.
[F04B 7/0088] Sealing arrangements between the distribution members and the housing -- F04B 7/0088 covers sealing arrangements that isolate the distribution elements of positively-driven liquid displacement machines-such as valves, rotors or distributors-from the surrounding housing. It includes the design, materials and mounting of static or dynamic seals intended to prevent leakage and maintain pressurised flow.
[F04B 7/0092] for oscillating distribution members -- This subclass covers piston machines or pumps with positively-driven valving in which the distribution members move back and forth rather than rotate, providing an oscillating action to direct liquid flow. It includes designs that use oscillating pistons, swash-plates or similar components to achieve alternating displacement of the fluid.
[F04B 7/0096] for pipe-type distribution members -- F04B 7/0096 covers piston-driven pumps or positive-displacement machines that incorporate pipe-type distribution members, meaning components that direct the pumped fluid through a network of pipes rather than through individual outlets. It applies to systems where the valving is integrated with the pipe distribution network for fluids such as water, oil or hydraulic media.
[F04B 7/02] the valving being fluid-actuated -- Piston pumps or other positive-displacement machines in which the valve that controls fluid flow is operated by the pressure of the fluid itself rather than by mechanical linkage or external actuation. This fluid-actuated valving governs inlet and outlet timing directly from the hydraulic or pneumatic forces within the device.
[F04B 7/0208] the distribution member forming both the inlet and discharge distributor for one single pumping chamber -- This subclass covers piston machines where a single distribution component serves as both the inlet and outlet for fluid within a single pumping chamber, integrating valving and flow routing in one piece. It is used for positive-displacement pumps where the distributor element performs dual functions, simplifying construction.
[F04B 7/0216] and having an oscillating movement -- F04B 7/0216 pertains to piston-type positive-displacement machines for liquids that incorporate positively driven valving and employ an oscillating movement of the piston or plunger. It falls under the broader class of mechanical engineering devices for pumping fluids.
[F04B 7/0225] and having a slidable movement -- The F04B 7/0225 subclass covers positive-displacement liquid pumps in which the valve mechanism operates by a sliding, linear motion rather than a rotary or oscillating action. It applies to piston-type machines where the valving element moves back and forth to control flow.
[F04B 7/0233] a common distribution member forming a single discharge distributor for a plurality of pumping chambers -- This subclass covers piston pumps where a single discharge distributor serves several pumping chambers, providing a common distribution member that channels the fluid out of each chamber. It applies to positive-displacement machines that use positively driven valves to control flow.
[F04B 7/0241] and having an oscillating movement -- This subclass covers piston-type positive-displacement pumps in which the moving elements (pistons, diaphragms or similar) perform an oscillating, back-and-forth motion instead of a continuous rotary stroke, thereby driving the liquid through the valve system. It applies to machines whose valving is positively driven and whose liquid displacement is achieved by this reciprocating oscillation.
[F04B 7/025] and having a slidable movement -- F04B 7/025 covers piston-driven pumps or positive-displacement machines that incorporate a slidable movement in their valve mechanism. The classification applies to designs where a sliding component, such as a valve seat or cam, controls fluid intake or discharge as the piston operates, distinguishing them from revolving or fixed-valve types.
[F04B 7/0258] and having an orbital movement, e.g. elbow-pipe type members -- F04B 7/0258 pertains to positive-displacement liquid pumps where the pump elements move in an orbital, rather than purely reciprocating, manner, exemplified by elbow-pipe type members that impart a curved motion to the fluid. This subclass is used for piston-type machines whose valving is driven positively and whose geometry creates an orbital displacement.
[F04B 7/0266] the inlet and discharge means being separate members -- F04B 7/0266 covers piston-driven positive-displacement pumps in which the inlet and discharge ports are provided by distinct, separate members rather than a common housing or integrated component. This design isolates suction and delivery pathways, allowing independent optimization of flow entry and outflow characteristics.
[F04B 7/0275] and being deformable, e.g. membranes -- This CPC subclass relates to positive-displacement liquid machines that use deformable elements such as membranes to move fluid, for example diaphragm or bellows pumps. It applies to piston-type devices where the valving is positively driven and the displacement component can change shape during operation.
[F04B 7/0283] and having a rotating movement -- F04B 7/0283 pertains to piston machines or pumps that employ positively-driven valving and feature a rotating movement, such as rotary pistons or cam-actuated valves. It lies within mechanical engineering, positive-displacement machines for liquids, specifically piston machines with positively-driven valving.
[F04B 7/0291] the distribution being realised by moving the cylinder itself, e.g. by sliding or swinging -- F04B 7/0291 covers piston-driven positive-displacement liquid machines where the cylinder itself moves to achieve fluid distribution, such as by sliding linearly or swinging about an axis. This method replaces conventional valve mechanisms, using the cylinder's motion to control the flow path within the pump.
[F04B 7/04] in which the valving is performed by pistons and cylinders coacting to open and close intake or outlet ports -- F04B 7/04 covers piston-driven positive-displacement machines in which pistons and cylinders work together to open and close the intake or outlet ports, providing the valve action for the pump. This subclass deals specifically with valving that is achieved by the reciprocal motion of the pistons rather than separate mechanical or hydraulic valves.
[F04B 7/045] Two pistons coacting within one cylinder -- F04B 7/045 pertains to piston-type positive-displacement machines in which two pistons work together inside a single cylinder, sharing the same working chamber. The coordinated motion of the pistons enables fluid pumping or compression with improved efficiency and flow control.
[F04B 7/06] the pistons and cylinders being relatively reciprocated and rotated -- F04B 7/06 covers piston and cylinder arrangements where the pistons move back-and-forth while also rotating relative to the cylinder, a configuration used in positive-displacement machines such as certain rotary pumps and engines that combine reciprocating and rotary motion.
[F04B 9/00] Piston machines or pumps characterised by the driving or driven means to or from their working members -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pumps for liquids driven by mechanical means, e.g. cams, eccentrics or other means or by fluid means, e.g. hydraulically driven or pneumatically driven. In this group are also classified pumps being manually driven by a user.
[F04B 9/02] the means being mechanical -- This subgroup covers piston-type liquid displacement machines where the driving or driven elements are purely mechanical, such as gears, cams, levers or linkages that transmit motion to or from the working pistons. It excludes hydraulic or electronic actuation and focuses on conventional mechanical transmission methods.
[F04B 9/025] Driving of pistons coacting within one cylinder -- The entry F04B 9/025 covers the driving of pistons that coact within a single cylinder, describing mechanisms that link or synchronize multiple pistons to produce motion or power. It falls under positive-displacement liquid machines, specifically piston machines defined by their driving or driven means.
[F04B 9/04] the means being cams, eccentrics or pin-and-slot mechanisms -- This subgroup covers piston pumps or positive-displacement machines where the reciprocating motion is generated by cams, eccentrics or pin-and-slot linkages. These mechanisms convert continuous rotation into the linear movement of pistons or working members, providing the driving or driven means for the pump.
[F04B 9/042] the means being cams -- This subclass concerns piston or pump designs where the reciprocating motion of the working members is generated by cam mechanisms, including cam shafts, cam profiles and the associated linkages that convert rotary motion into piston movement for displacing liquid.
[F04B 9/045] the means being eccentrics -- The subclass F04B 9/045 covers piston machines or pumps that employ eccentrics as the driving or driven means, using an off-centre rotating element to convert rotary motion into the reciprocating motion of pistons or plungers. It pertains to the mechanical arrangement of the eccentric shaft, bearing, and associated components that generate the required displacement of liquid.
[F04B 9/047] the means being pin-and-slot mechanisms -- F04B 9/047 covers piston machines or pumps in which the driving or driven elements use a pin-and-slot arrangement to transmit motion to the working members, a mechanical linkage that converts rotary or reciprocating input into the required piston movement. This subclass falls under positive-displacement machines for liquids within the broader mechanical engineering field.
[F04B 9/06] the means including spring- or weight-loaded lost-motion devices -- F04B 9/06 classifies piston-type liquid displacement machines, such as pumps and compressors, that employ spring- or weight-loaded lost-motion devices to temporarily disengage or modify the motion of driving or driven members. These mechanisms provide timing control, overload protection, or efficiency improvement by allowing limited free movement before re-engagement.
[F04B 9/08] the means being fluid -- F04B 9/08 covers positive-displacement liquid machines whose driving or driven force is transmitted by a fluid medium, such as hydraulic or pneumatic actuation of pistons or other moving members. It includes designs where fluid power, rather than mechanical linkages, moves the working parts of pumps, compressors or similar devices.
[F04B 9/10] the fluid being liquid -- F04B 9/10 designates piston-type machines or pumps in which the working fluid is a liquid, focusing on the way the driving or driven elements transmit power to the moving members of the device. This subclass falls under positive-displacement machines for liquids within the broader mechanical-engineering field.
[F04B 9/103] having only one pumping chamber -- F04B 9/103 covers piston-type positive-displacement liquid pumps that contain a single pumping chamber, meaning the pump's working volume is formed by one cylinder and piston assembly rather than multiple chambers. This subclass applies to designs where the driving or driven mechanism acts on that lone chamber to move fluid.
[F04B 9/1035] the movement of the pump piston in the two directions being obtained by two single-acting liquid motors each acting in one direction -- This CPC subclass F04B 9/1035 covers piston pumps in which the reciprocating motion is created by two separate single-acting liquid motors, one driving the piston forward and the other returning it. The arrangement replaces a conventional crankshaft or other mechanical linkage, providing bidirectional drive through fluid power alone. It falls within the positive-displacement machines for liquids section of mechanical engineering.
[F04B 9/105] reciprocating movement of the pumping member being obtained by a double-acting liquid motor -- This subclass covers positive-displacement pumps in which the piston's reciprocating motion is produced by a double-acting liquid motor, i.e., fluid pressure drives the piston during both forward and return strokes. It applies to piston machines where the driving means is a liquid motor that acts on both sides of the working member.
[F04B 9/1053] one side of the double-acting liquid motor being always under the influence of the liquid under pressure -- This class relates to double-acting liquid motors where one side of the piston is permanently exposed to the pressurised fluid, providing a continuous force while the opposite side alternates between pressure and release during operation.

[F04B 9/107] rectilinear movement of the pumping member in the working direction being obtained by a single-acting liquid motor, e.g. actuated in the other direction by gravity or a spring -- F04B 9/107 relates to pumps in which the pumping member moves in a straight line during the working stroke by means of a single-acting liquid motor, while the return motion is achieved using gravity or a spring.
[F04B 9/1073] with actuation in the other direction by gravity -- This subgroup concerns piston pumps or other positive-displacement machines where the working member is driven in one direction by a mechanical means and returns in the opposite direction solely by the action of gravity.

This arrangement combines fluid-driven valve movement with external mechanical regulation to manage flow during each displacement cycle.
[F04B 9/109] having plural pumping chambers -- F04B 9/109 covers piston-driven positive-displacement pumps that contain multiple pumping chambers, enabling simultaneous or sequential displacement of liquid.

This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, positive-displacement machines for liquids, specifically piston machines distinguished by their driving or driven means.
[F04B 9/111] with two mechanically connected pumping members -- F04B 9/111 covers piston-type positive-displacement liquid machines that employ a pair of pumping members linked together so that the motion of one drives the other. [F04B 9/1115] the movement of the pumping pistons in only one direction being obtained by a single-acting piston liquid motor, e.g. actuation in the other direction by spring means -- This subgroup covers devices where the pistons of a liquid pump move in a single direction during the power stroke, driven by a single-acting piston motor, while the return stroke is provided by a spring or similar elastic element. It falls under positive-displacement machines for liquids within mechanical engineering.
[F04B 9/113] reciprocating movement of the pumping members being obtained by a double-acting liquid motor -- This CPC subgroup covers pumps whose pistons or other pumping members are driven in a reciprocating manner by a double-acting liquid motor that delivers power on both forward and return strokes. It includes arrangements where the hydraulic motor directly or indirectly creates the back-and-forth motion of the pump.
[F04B 9/115] reciprocating movement of the pumping members being obtained by two single-acting liquid motors, each acting in one direction -- This subclass covers pumps where the piston's back-and-forth motion is produced by two separate single-acting liquid actuators, each providing power in one stroke direction. It applies to reciprocating pumps that use paired liquid motors rather than a single motor or mechanical linkage.
[F04B 9/117] the pumping members not being mechanically connected to each other -- This CPC subclass covers positive-displacement liquid pumps whose individual pumping members operate independently, with no mechanical linkage between them. It applies to piston, diaphragm or similar pumps in which each chamber is driven separately, allowing asynchronous movement and simplifying the machine's construction.
[F04B 9/1172] the movement of each pump piston in the two directions being obtained by a double-acting piston liquid motor -- This CPC subclass concerns liquid motors that use a double-acting piston, where each piston stroke moves in both directions to provide reciprocal displacement of the fluid. It falls under positive-displacement machines for liquids within the mechanical engineering hierarchy.
[F04B 9/1174] with fluid-actuated inlet or outlet valve  mechanically controlled  F04B7/00 -- This subclass covers piston-type positive-displacement liquid pumps whose inlet or outlet valve is opened and closed by a valve that is actuated by fluid pressure, with the actuation mechanically linked to the pump's motion. It applies to machines in which the fluid-actuated valve timing is controlled by the moving parts of the pump rather than by separate hydraulic or electrical systems.
[F04B 9/1176] the movement of each piston in one direction being obtained by a single-acting piston liquid motor -- This subclass concerns piston machines where each piston is driven to move in only one direction by a single-acting liquid motor, defining a type of positive-displacement pump or motor.
[F04B 9/1178] the movement in the other direction being obtained by a hydraulic connection between the liquid motor cylinders -- F04B 9/1178 addresses piston-type liquid motors or pumps in which reverse motion is produced by hydraulically connecting the motor cylinders, so that the fluid discharged from one cylinder drives another cylinder in the opposite direction. This arrangement enables bidirectional operation without separate reversing mechanisms.
[F04B 9/12] the fluid being elastic, e.g. steam or air -- F04B 9/12 relates to piston-type displacement machines or pumps in which the working fluid is elastic, such as steam, compressed air or other gases. It covers designs where the compressibility of the fluid influences the piston's operation and performance.
[F04B 9/1207] using a source of partial vacuum or sub-atmospheric pressure -- F04B 9/1207 covers piston-type liquid displacement machines that are driven by a source of partial vacuum or sub-atmospheric pressure, rather than by positive pressure. It classifies pumps or compressors that create suction to move fluid, fitting within the broader group of positive-displacement machines for liquids in mechanical engineering.
[F04B 9/1215] the return stroke being obtained by a spring -- F04B 9/1215 covers piston-type positive-displacement machines, such as pumps or compressors, in which the piston's return stroke is generated by a spring rather than by external power or fluid pressure. This arrangement provides the recoiling motion needed to complete the pumping cycle.
[F04B 9/1222] the return stroke being obtained by an elastic fluid under pressure -- F04B 9/1222 covers piston pumps or other positive-displacement liquid machines in which the return stroke of the piston is driven by a pressurised elastic fluid, such as a hydraulic accumulator or spring-charged oil, rather than by mechanical linkage or external power.
[F04B 9/123] having only one pumping chamber -- F04B 9/123 refers to positive-displacement piston machines or pumps that employ a single pumping chamber. The classification includes designs where fluid is transferred by one cylinder or chamber during each operating stroke, distinguishing them from multi-chamber configurations.
[F04B 9/1235] the movement of the pump piston in the two directions being obtained by two single-acting piston fluid motors, each acting in one direction -- F04B 9/1235 covers positive-displacement pumps where the pump piston's reciprocal motion is generated by two separate single-acting piston fluid motors, one providing power for the forward stroke and the other for the return stroke.
[F04B 9/125] reciprocating movement of the pumping member being obtained by a double-acting elastic-fluid motor -- F04B 9/125 covers piston or pump machines whose reciprocating motion is produced by a double-acting elastic-fluid motor, meaning the working fluid drives the pump member alternately from both sides. This sub-class falls under positive-displacement liquid machines within mechanical engineering, specifying the use of a fluid-powered motor to generate the piston's back-and-forth movement.
[F04B 9/1253] one side of the double-acting piston fluid motor being always under the influence of the fluid under pressure
[F04B 9/1256] with fluid-actuated inlet or outlet valve  mechanically controlled  F04B7/00 -- This subclass covers piston-type pumps or compressors in which the inlet or outlet valve is opened and closed by a fluid-driven actuator that is itself controlled by a mechanical linkage or cam. The fluid actuation replaces direct mechanical linkage to the moving piston while the overall timing is set mechanically. It falls under CPC F04B 9/1256 within positive-displacement liquid machines.
[F04B 9/127] rectilinear movement of the pumping member in the working direction being obtained by a single-acting elastic-fluid motor, e.g. actuated in the other direction by gravity or a spring -- A positive-displacement pump in which the piston's linear travel is driven by a single-acting elastic-fluid motor, while the return stroke is provided by gravity or a spring, falls under CPC F04B 9/127. This arrangement uses a fluid-powered actuator for the pumping direction and a passive means for the opposite direction.
[F04B 9/1273] with actuation in the other direction by gravity -- This subclass refers to piston-type positive-displacement liquid pumps that use gravity to drive the piston or plunger back toward its starting position, providing the reverse motion without external power. It includes designs where the upward or downward stroke is caused by the weight of the moving part or the liquid column.
[F04B 9/1276] with fluid-actuated inlet or outlet valve  mechanically controlled  F04B7/00 -- A subclass of positive-displacement liquid machines covering piston-type pumps or compressors whose inlet or outlet valves are opened or closed by fluid pressure, while the valve motion is governed by a mechanical control mechanism. The code designates devices that combine fluid-actuated valve actuation with mechanically regulated operation.
[F04B 9/129] having plural pumping chambers -- F04B 9/129 refers to piston-driven machines or pumps that incorporate more than one pumping chamber, meaning the device contains multiple cylinders or chambers that work together to move liquid. This arrangement is used to increase flow capacity, balance operating forces, or improve efficiency in positive-displacement pumps.
[F04B 9/1295] having two or more pumping chambers in series -- F04B 9/1295 designates piston-type liquid pumps that contain two or more pumping chambers connected in series, allowing the output of one chamber to feed the next for higher pressure or flow. [F04B 9/131] with two mechanically connected pumping members -- F04B 9/131 covers positive-displacement pumps that employ two pumping members that are mechanically linked together, such as paired pistons, cylinders or diaphragms driven by a common shaft or linkage. [F04B 9/1315] the movement of the pumping pistons in only one direction being obtained by a single-acting piston fluid motor, e.g. actuation in the other direction by spring means -- F04B 9/1315 covers positive-displacement pumps in which the working pistons travel in only one direction under power from a single-acting fluid motor, while the opposite stroke is provided by a spring or similar return device, enabling a simple actuation cycle.
[F04B 9/133] reciprocating movement of the pumping members being obtained by a double-acting elastic-fluid motor -- The F04B 9/133 subclass covers positive-displacement pumps in which the reciprocating motion of the pistons or other pumping members is generated by a double-acting elastic-fluid motor, such as a hydraulic or pneumatic actuator that drives the work strokes in both directions. This arrangement provides the power source for the pump's reciprocating movement.
[F04B 9/135] reciprocating movement of the pumping members being obtained by two single-acting elastic-fluid motors, each acting in one direction -- This CPC subclass covers pumps in which the piston's reciprocating motion is generated by two single-acting elastic-fluid motors, each providing power in one direction only, so the forward and return strokes are driven by separate fluid-powered units without a conventional crank mechanism.
[F04B 9/137] the pumping members not being mechanically connected to each other -- This subclass covers piston-type positive-displacement pumps in which the individual pumping members operate independently and are not linked by gears, shafts or other mechanical connections, so each piston or diaphragm moves without being physically coupled to the others, allowing separate actuation of the working members.
[F04B 9/1372] the movement of each pump piston in the two directions is obtained by a double-acting piston fluid motor -- F04B 9/1372 relates to double-acting piston fluid motors in which each pump piston moves back and forth, providing power in both directions of its stroke. This arrangement is used in positive-displacement machines that transfer liquid by reciprocating pistons.
[F04B 9/1374] with fluid-actuated inlet or outlet valve  mechanically controlled  F04B7/00 -- F04B 9/1374 covers piston-type liquid displacement machines, such as pumps, in which the inlet or outlet valve is opened and closed by fluid pressure but the timing or movement of the valve is governed by a mechanical control mechanism. This subcategory falls under positive-displacement machines for liquids and specifies fluid-actuated valves that are mechanically regulated.
[F04B 9/1376] the movement of each piston in one direction being obtained by a single-acting piston fluid motor -- F04B 9/1376 covers piston machines or pumps in which each piston moves only in one direction, the motion being produced by a single-acting fluid motor that drives the piston. This subclass falls under positive-displacement machines for liquids within the mechanical engineering section of the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[F04B 9/1378] the movement in the other direction being obtained by an hydraulic connection between the fluid motor cylinders -- The code F04B 9/1378 covers piston or reciprocating machines where reverse motion is achieved by linking the cylinders of a fluid motor through a hydraulic circuit, allowing the fluid to power the opposite direction of movement without separate mechanical gearing. This arrangement is used in positive-displacement pumps and compressors to obtain bidirectional operation via fluid coupling rather than additional drive components.
[F04B 9/14] Pumps characterised by muscle-power operation -- This subclass covers positive-displacement pumps that are driven directly by human muscle power, such as hand, foot or arm operated pumps. It includes devices where the driving means is a lever, treadle or other manual mechanism acting on the pump's moving parts.
[F04C] ROTARY-PISTON, OR OSCILLATING-PISTON, POSITIVE-DISPLACEMENT MACHINES FOR LIQUIDS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pumps with rotary or oscillating pistons for liquids, for elastic fluids, or for combination of liquid and elastic fluid.  Positive displacement machines, i.e. devices which could be equally an engine or pump, with rotary or oscillating pistons for liquids only.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Positive displacement engines for liquids -> F03C - Positive displacement machines for liquids, or pumps in which the working-fluid is displaced by one or more reciprocating pistons or by flexible working members -> F04B  **Glossary:** - Pump: means a device for continuously raising, forcing, compressing, or exhausting fluid by mechanical means - Machine: means a device that could equally be both an engine and a pump and not a device which is restricted to an engine or one which is restricted to a pump - Positive displacement pumps: pumps using pistons or other mechanical members to displace a working fluid in a working chamber, the dynamic effect on the fluid being of minor importance - Positive displacement engines: the energy of a working fluid is transformed into mechanical energy, in which variations of volume created by the working fluid in a working chamber produce equivalent movement of mechanical members, e.g. pistons transmitting the energy, the dynamic effect of the fluid being of minor importance - Oscillating piston machine: means a positive-displacement machine in which a fluid-engaging, work-transmitting member oscillates, e.g. a vane piston swinging back and forth about a fixed axis - Reciprocating piston: means a fluid-engaging, work-transmitting member of an reciprocating-piston type machine or pump that slides alternately back and forth usually along a straight line or path - Rotary piston: means a fluid engaging, work-transmitting member of a rotary-piston machine or pump that can completely rotate about a fixed axis or about an axis moving along a circular or similar orbit when operating, e.g. rotor having vanes or teeth - Rotary piston machine: means a positive-displacement machine in which a liquid-engaging, work-transmitting member rotates about a fixed axis or about an axis moving along a circular or similar orbit, e.g. machine with a rotor having vanes or teeth - Cooperating members: means the "oscillating piston" or "rotary piston" and another member, e.g., the working-chamber wall, which assists in the pumping action or machine's action - Movement of the cooperating members: is to be interpreted as relative, so that one of the "cooperating members" may be stationary, even though reference may be made to its rotational axis, or both may move - Teeth or tooth equivalents: include lobes, projections or abutments - Internal axis type: means that the rotational axes of the inner and outer co-operating members remain at all times within the outer member, e.g., in a similar manner to that of a pinion meshing with the internal teeth of a ring gear - Working fluid: means the driven fluid in a pump or driving or driven liquid in a machine. The working fluid can be in a compressible, gaseous state, e.g. steam, called elastic fluid, a liquid state, or a state where there is coexistence of elastic fluid and liquid state
[F04C 11/00] Combinations of two or more machines or pumps, each being of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combinations of two or more machines or pumps, each being of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston type, such as multistage and parallel operating machines; Pumping installations  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Combinations of such pumps specially adapted for elastic fluids -> F04C23/00 - Combination of engines with pumps or compressors driven thereby -> F01C13/04
[F04C 11/001] of similar working principle -- F04C 11/001 covers arrangements that combine two or more rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement machines for liquids, where the units share a similar working principle. It is used to classify composite pump systems that operate on the same mechanical concept within the broader group of positive-displacement liquid machinery.
[F04C 11/003] having complementary function -- The F04C 11/003 entry covers arrangements that combine two or more positive-displacement machines of the rotary-piston or oscillating-piston type, where each unit performs a complementary function within the overall system, such as sequential pumping, pressurising, or mixing of liquids.
[F04C 11/005] of dissimilar working principle -- This CPC subclass covers positive-displacement machines for liquids that combine two or more pumps of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston type operating on different working principles. It applies to hybrid devices where dissimilar mechanisms are coupled.
[F04C 11/006] having complementary function -- A combination of two or more rotary-piston or oscillating-piston pumps arranged so that each unit performs a complementary role, such as one providing suction while another supplies pressure, integrated within a single assembly. This configuration enables coordinated operation of the pumps to achieve a desired overall fluid-handling function.
[F04C 11/008] Enclosed motor pump units -- This class covers compact units that combine a pump and its driving motor within a sealed housing, using rotary-piston or oscillating-piston mechanisms to displace liquids. The enclosure protects the moving parts from external contaminants and simplifies installation by integrating the two functions in a single package.
[F04C 13/00] Adaptations of machines or pumps for special use, e.g. for extremely high pressures -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Of such pumps specially adapted for elastic fluids -> F04C25/00 - Combination of engines with pumps or compressors driven thereby -> F01C13/04
[F04C 13/001] Pumps for particular liquids -- This subcategory covers pumps specifically designed for handling particular liquids, including adaptations for unique properties such as extreme viscosity, corrosiveness, or operation at very high pressures. It addresses specialized positive-displacement rotary or oscillating piston pumps tailored to these demanding applications.
[F04C 13/002] for homogeneous viscous liquids -- F04C 13/002 covers adaptations of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement machines designed to handle homogeneous viscous liquids, such as heavy oils or syrups, often requiring special construction to operate under very high pressures.
[F04C 13/004] with means for fluidising or diluting the material being pumped -- The subclass F04C 13/004 covers rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement pumps that incorporate devices to fluidise or dilute the material being pumped, such as agitators, injectors or spray nozzles. These features are intended to improve the handling of viscous, solid-laden or otherwise difficult fluids, especially in high-pressure or special-use applications.
[F04C 13/005] Removing contaminants, deposits or scale from the pump; Cleaning -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Prevention of deposits in the pump -> F04C2280/02
[F04C 13/007] Venting; Gas and vapour separation during pumping  preventing vapour lock in fuel pumps  F02M37/20 , in centrifugal pumps  F04D9/00 -- F04C 13/007 addresses adaptations of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement machines that provide venting and gas-vapour separation during pumping, preventing vapor lock in fuel pumps and similar centrifugal pump applications, especially under extremely high pressures.
[F04C 13/008] Pumps for submersible use, i.e. down-hole pumping -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Drawing well fluid out of a well with the adaptation for being below the earth's surface, and also pumps working in submersed conditions for other use.
[F04C 14/00] Control of, monitoring of, or safety arrangements for, machines, pumps or pumping installations -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of, monitoring of, or safety arrangements for, machines, pumps or pumping installations.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Of pumps or pumping installations specially adapted for elastic fluids -> F04C28/00
[F04C 14/02] specially adapted for several machines or pumps connected in series or in parallel -- F04C 14/02 relates to control, monitoring or safety arrangements that are specially adapted for use with several machines or pumps connected in series or in parallel.
[F04C 14/04] specially adapted for reversible machines or pumps -- The subclass F04C 14/04 deals with positive-displacement rotary-piston or oscillating-piston machines and pumps that are specially adapted for reversible operation, describing features that allow the unit to run in both directions. It also covers control, monitoring and safety arrangements specifically intended for such reversible machines or pumping installations.
[F04C 14/06] specially adapted for stopping, starting, idling or no-load operation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Measures or means for stopping, starting or idling a compressor or a pump; measures taken as a consequence or preceding starting, stopping or idling favouring the action, e.g. having reduced side sealing pressure at start.
[F04C 14/065] Capacity control using a multiplicity of units or pumping capacities, e.g. multiple chambers, individually switchable or controllable -- The entry covers arrangements for adjusting the output of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement pumps by employing several pump units, chambers or stages that can be switched on or off individually. Such capacity-control schemes allow fine regulation of flow or pressure by varying the number of active units.
[F04C 14/08] characterised by varying the rotational speed -- F04C 14/08 pertains to positive-displacement liquid pumps, especially rotary-piston or oscillating-piston types, where varying the rotational speed is used as a control, monitoring, or safety measure for the machine or its installation.
[F04C 14/10] characterised by changing the positions of the inlet or outlet openings with respect to the working chamber -- This subclass covers positive-displacement pumps, such as rotary-piston or oscillating-piston machines, whose inlet or outlet openings are shifted relative to the working chamber to regulate flow, enable monitoring, or provide safety functions. It includes designs where the position of the ports changes during operation to control the pumping process.
[F04C 14/12] using sliding valves -- F04C 14/12 covers the use of sliding valves in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement machines for liquids, focusing on how such valves control the fluid flow, enable monitoring, or provide safety functions within pumps and pumping installations.
[F04C 14/14] using rotating valves -- This subclass covers devices that employ rotating valves to regulate flow in positive-displacement pumps, including mechanisms for control, monitoring, or safety of pumping systems. It specifically relates to rotary-piston and oscillating-piston machines.
[F04C 14/16] using lift valves -- The classification F04C 14/16 deals with the use of lift valves for controlling, monitoring or providing safety functions in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement pumps for liquids. It falls under mechanical engineering, positive-displacement machines for liquids, and covers control and safety arrangements for such pumping installations.
[F04C 14/18] characterised by varying the volume of the working chamber -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Rotary-piston machines or pumps controlled by varying the volume of the working chamber also including those where the underlined expression is construed as changing the stroke ("stroke" as relative displacement between cooperating members defining the size of the working chamber as in reciprocating engines).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - By changing the positions of inlet or outlet openings -> F04C14/10
[F04C 14/185] by varying the useful pumping length of the cooperating members in the axial direction -- The CPC subclass F04C 14/185 covers rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement pumps where the useful pumping length of the cooperating members is varied in the axial direction. This variation is used as a means of controlling, monitoring, or providing safety for the pump or its installation.
[F04C 14/20] by changing the form of the inner or outer contour of the working chamber -- The class covers positive-displacement liquid machines in which the working chamber's inner or outer contour is altered during operation to vary its volume and shape. Such contour-changing mechanisms are used to control flow, improve efficiency, or provide safety features in pumps and similar devices.
[F04C 14/22] by changing the eccentricity between cooperating members -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Internal gear pumps with variable eccentricity (i.e. variable in direction and not in quantity)(no change of volume but only relative displacement of the working chamber to the inlet and outlet openings). -> F04C14/10
[F04C 14/223] using a movable cam -- This subclass covers devices that employ a movable cam to regulate or adjust the operation of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement pumps, including mechanisms for control, monitoring or safety. The cam can modify stroke length, timing or flow characteristics to improve performance or protect the installation.
[F04C 14/226] by pivoting the cam around an eccentric axis -- F04C 14/226 covers safety, monitoring or control devices for rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement liquid machines that operate by pivoting a cam around an eccentric axis. The classification is used for mechanisms that adjust pump timing or flow by shifting the cam's rotational centre, providing protective or regulatory functions within the pumping installation.
[F04C 14/24] characterised by using valves controlling pressure or flow rate, e.g. discharge valves -- The entry covers control, monitoring and safety features for positive-displacement liquid machines such as rotary- or oscillating-piston pumps, specifically those that employ valves to regulate pressure or flow rate, for example discharge valves. It falls under the mechanical engineering subclass for positive-displacement machines for liquids.
[F04C 14/26] using bypass channels -- The F04C 14/26 subclass concerns positive-displacement rotary- or oscillating-piston liquid machines that employ bypass channels to divert fluid flow for control, monitoring or safety purposes, such as reducing pressure spikes, enabling flow regulation or protecting pump components during abnormal operation.
[F04C 14/265] being obtained by displacing a lateral sealing face -- F04C 14/265 covers positive-displacement machines for liquids-such as rotary-piston or oscillating-piston pumps-where the sealing face is displaced laterally to create a moving seal. The entry details designs that shift the sealing element sideways during operation, improving sealing efficiency and reducing wear in pump chambers.
[F04C 14/28] Safety arrangements -- The F04C 14/28 classification covers safety-related devices and control systems for positive-displacement pumps and pumping installations, including mechanisms that monitor operation, prevent hazardous conditions, and ensure safe shutdown or protection of the machinery. It applies to rotary-piston, oscillating-piston, and other pump types used for moving liquids.
[F04C 15/00] Component parts, details or accessories of machines, pumps or pumping installations, not provided for in groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Component parts, details or accessories of machines, pumps or pumping installations, not provided for in groups F04C2/00 - F04C14/00 .  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Of pumps specially adapted for elastic fluids -> F04C29/00
[F04C 15/0003] Sealing arrangements in rotary-piston machines or pumps  sealing in general  F16J -- The entry covers the design and implementation of sealing systems for rotary-piston pumps and similar positive-displacement machines, including the selection, arrangement and detailing of seals, sealing surfaces and auxiliary components that prevent fluid leakage in such devices.
[F04C 15/0007] Radial sealings for working fluid -- F04C 15/0007 concerns radial sealing elements that retain the working fluid in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement machines for liquids. It applies to component parts, details or accessories of pumps and pumping installations that are not covered by any other specific group in the mechanical-engineering hierarchy.
[F04C 15/0011] of rigid material -- This subclass covers components, details or accessories made of rigid material that are used in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement liquid pumps, where the items are not listed elsewhere in the classification. It includes items such as casings, housings, shafts, and other structural parts that are fabricated from metals, ceramics or rigid polymers specifically for these pump types.
[F04C 15/0015] of resilient material -- F04C 15/0015 covers parts of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement pumps that are made of resilient material, such as flexible seals, gaskets, diaphragms and other elastic components that provide sealing or damping within the pump assembly.
[F04C 15/0019] Radial sealing elements specially adapted for intermeshing-engagement type machines or pumps, e.g. gear machines or pumps -- Radial sealing elements that are specially adapted for use in intermeshing-engagement machines or pumps, such as gear-type pumps, are covered. These components serve to prevent leakage between rotating parts and are classified as accessories of positive-displacement liquid machines not listed elsewhere.
[F04C 15/0023] Axial sealings for working fluid -- F04C 15/0023 covers axial sealing devices that prevent leakage of the working fluid along the shaft axis in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement pumps. It includes seals, glands and related hardware fitted to pump shafts, housings or stator components to contain the fluid within the pumping chamber.
[F04C 15/0026] Elements specially adapted for sealing of the lateral faces of intermeshing-engagement type machines or pumps, e.g. gear machines or pumps -- This subgroup covers specially adapted sealing elements for the lateral faces of intermeshing-engagement machines or pumps, such as gear machines and gear pumps. It includes component parts, details or accessories that are not listed in other groups of the rotary-piston positive-displacement machine classification.
[F04C 15/003] Sealings for working fluid between radially and axially moving parts -- F04C 15/003 covers sealing devices that prevent the escape of working fluid between components that move relative to each other in both radial and axial directions in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement machines, such as pumps, where the seal is not covered by other more specific groups.
[F04C 15/0034] for other than the working fluid, i.e. the sealing arrangements are not between working chambers of the machine -- F04C 15/0034 classifies sealing arrangements in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement liquid machines that are not for the working fluid, meaning the seals are placed outside the working chambers. It covers component parts, details or accessories of such pumps and pumping installations that are not otherwise provided for in the main groups.
[F04C 15/0038] Shaft sealings specially adapted for rotary-piston machines or pumps -- F04C 15/0038 covers shaft sealing devices that are specifically designed for use in rotary-piston machines or pumps. These seals protect the rotating shaft from fluid leakage while accommodating the unique motion and pressure conditions of such positive-displacement equipment. The classification includes seals, glands and accompanying accessories that are not covered in other detailed CPC groups.

[F04C 15/0053] Venting means for starting -- F04C 15/0053 covers venting devices that enable the start of positive-displacement pumps such as rotary or oscillating-piston machines. These components release trapped air or fluid to allow the pump to begin operating smoothly and are classified as accessories not otherwise specified.
[F04C 15/0057] Driving elements, brakes, couplings, transmission specially adapted for machines or pumps  brakes, couplings, transmissions  per se   F16 ,  B60 -- F04C 15/0057 covers component parts, details or accessories of positive-displacement liquid machines, specifically driving elements, brakes, couplings and transmissions that are specially adapted for the operation of such machines or pumps and are not listed in other groups.
[F04C 15/0061] Means for transmitting movement from the prime mover to driven parts of the pump, e.g. clutches, couplings, transmissions -- F04C 15/0061 pertains to component parts, details or accessories that convey motion from a pump's prime mover to its driven elements, such as clutches, couplings and other transmission devices used in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement liquid pumps.

[F04C 15/0073] Couplings between rotors and input or output shafts acting by interengaging or mating parts, i.e. positive coupling of rotor and shaft -- This subclass defines positive couplings that directly link a rotor to an input or output shaft by interengaging or mating parts, such as gear-like, spline or keyed connections, without intervening flexible elements. It applies to components that secure the rotor's rotation to the shaft in pumps or other positive-displacement machines.
[F04C 15/0076] Fixing rotors on shafts, e.g. by clamping together hub and shaft -- The entry covers devices that attach a pump rotor to its driving shaft, typically by clamping a hub onto the shaft to secure the rotor in positive-displacement liquid-pumping machines such as rotary-piston or oscillating-piston pumps. It includes component parts, details and accessories specifically designed for this fixing function.
[F04C 15/008] Prime movers -- F04C 15/008 covers components, details and accessories of prime movers - the power-providing devices for rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement pumps - that are not specified in other CPC groups. Items such as auxiliary shafts, couplings, bearings and control mechanisms for transmitting power to the pump fall within this group.
[F04C 15/0084] Brakes, braking assemblies -- F04C 15/0084 covers braking devices and assemblies used in positive-displacement liquid machines, such as rotary-piston or oscillating-piston pumps. It includes components that arrest, slow or control the rotation of pump shafts, pistons or other moving parts, as well as related accessories that are not classified elsewhere in the group.

[F04C 15/0092] Control systems for the circulation of the lubricant -- This CPC subclass covers devices and methods that regulate the flow of lubricating oil within rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement machines, such as pumps, valves, sensors and electronic controllers. It includes any accessory that monitors pressure, temperature or flow rate to maintain proper lubrication of moving parts.
[F04C 15/0096] Heating; Cooling  of machines or engines in general  F01P
[F04C 15/06] Arrangements for admission or discharge of the working fluid, e.g. constructional features of the inlet or outlet -- This subclass concerns the design of inlet and outlet features that admit or discharge the working fluid in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement pumps, including constructional elements such as valves, passages and other accessories not covered in other groups.
[F04C 15/062] Arrangements for supercharging the working space  similar arrangements for internal combustion engines  F02B33/00 ,  F02B37/00 -- This subclass covers designs that boost the pressure in the working chamber of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement liquid machines by means similar to supercharging used in internal-combustion engines, such as forced-induction, turbocharging or auxiliary compressors, to improve flow or power output.
