[F04C 27/005] Axial sealings for working fluid -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Especially scroll compressors where the axial sealing force is cancelled for capacity control purposes (e.g. forming an axial gap between scroll bodies).
[F04C 27/006] Elements specially adapted for sealing of the lateral faces of intermeshing-engagement type pumps, e.g. gear pumps -- The code F04C 27/006 covers components designed to seal the side faces of intermeshing-engagement pumps such as gear pumps, preventing leakage of elastic fluids. These sealing elements are adapted specifically for the lateral gaps between rotating gears or rotors within positive-displacement rotary-piston machines.
[F04C 27/007] Sealings for working fluid between radially and axially moving parts -- F04C 27/007 covers sealing arrangements used in rotary-piston pumps that handle elastic fluids, specifically designs that prevent leakage between components that move both radially and axially. The classification addresses seals that separate the working fluid from moving parts such as pistons, rotors or housings, ensuring reliable operation in positive-displacement liquid machines.
[F04C 27/008] for other than working fluid, i.e. the sealing arrangements are not between working chambers of the machine -- F04C 27/008 covers sealing arrangements in rotary-piston pumps that are specially adapted for elastic fluids but are positioned outside the working chambers, meaning the seals are not between the pump's working chambers. These seals protect the pump housing and drive components from contact with the fluid.
[F04C 27/009] Shaft sealings specially adapted for pumps -- F04C 27/009 covers shaft sealing solutions designed specifically for rotary-piston or oscillating-piston pumps that handle elastic liquids such as oils or greases. The classification addresses seal geometries and materials that maintain a leak-tight barrier despite the high pressure and flexing of the pump shaft. These sealings are optimized to accommodate the deformation of the fluid and the dynamic motion of the piston.
[F04C 27/02] Liquid sealing for high-vacuum pumps -- F04C 27/02 covers sealing arrangements in rotary-piston pumps that are specifically designed for handling elastic fluids, such as liquid sealing methods used in high-vacuum pumps. It falls under mechanical engineering, positive displacement machines for liquids, rotary-piston or oscillating-piston types.
[F04C 28/00] Control of, monitoring of, or safety arrangements for, pumps or pumping installations specially adapted for elastic fluids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of, monitoring of, or safety arrangements for, pumps or pumping installations specially adapted for elastic fluids.
[F04C 28/02] specially adapted for several pumps connected in series or in parallel -- This subclass covers control, monitoring and safety features for positive-displacement pumps handling elastic fluids when multiple units are arranged in series or in parallel. It applies to pump installations specially adapted for elastic liquids.
[F04C 28/04] specially adapted for reversible pumps
[F04C 28/06] specially adapted for stopping, starting, idling or no-load operation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Measures or means for stopping, starting or idling a compressor or pump; measures taken as a consequence or preceding starting, stopping or idling favouring the action, e.g. having reduced side sealing pressure at start.
[F04C 28/065] Capacity control using a multiplicity of units or pumping capacities, e.g. multiple chambers, individually switchable or controllable -- The F04C 28/065 entry covers systems that regulate pump flow by employing several parallel pumping units or chambers, each of which can be switched on or off or otherwise controlled to adjust overall capacity, especially for handling elastic fluids. It addresses methods for controlling, monitoring or providing safety in positive-displacement pumps by using multiple, independently operable modules. This allows precise capacity matching to varying demand or fluid characteristics.
[F04C 28/08] characterised by varying the rotational speed -- This subclass deals with positive-displacement rotary or oscillating-piston pumps handling elastic liquids, focusing on control, monitoring or safety features that adjust the pump's rotational speed to regulate flow or pressure.
[F04C 28/10] characterised by changing the positions of the inlet or outlet openings with respect to the working chamber -- In positive-displacement rotary or oscillating-piston pumps that handle elastic fluids, this subclass covers designs that alter the position of the inlet or outlet openings relative to the working chamber. By shifting these ports the pump can regulate flow, pressure or safety characteristics during operation.
[F04C 28/12] using sliding valves -- F04C 28/12 covers the use of sliding-type valves in positive-displacement machines for liquids, specifically pumps or pumping installations handling elastic fluids. It includes arrangements for controlling, monitoring or providing safety features that employ sliding valve mechanisms to regulate flow or pressure.
[F04C 28/125] with sliding valves controlled by the use of fluid other than the working fluid -- This subgroup covers pumps or pumping systems for elastic fluids that employ sliding valves whose actuation is performed by a control fluid different from the working fluid, focusing on the arrangement and safety aspects of such mechanisms.
[F04C 28/14] using rotating valves -- F04C 28/14 concerns rotating-valve mechanisms employed to control, monitor or provide safety for pumps and pumping installations that handle elastic fluids such as oils, polymers or greases. It falls under mechanical engineering, positive-displacement rotary-piston machines for liquids, focusing on flow-regulation arrangements using rotating valve elements.
[F04C 28/16] using lift valves -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Rotary-Piston pumps where a sliding valve controls an opening or port of non uniform section progressively (or a multiplicity of holes, passages forming as a whole an inlet or outlet port of a machine, pump). -> F04C28/12  **Glossary:** - Lift valve: valve with an ON-OFF action, i.e. opening or closing a passage in one movement
[F04C 28/18] characterised by varying the volume of the working chamber -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Rotary-piston pumps controlled by varying the volume of the working chamber also including those where the underlined expression is construed as changing the stroke ("stroke" as relative displacement between cooperating members defining the size of the working chamber as in reciprocating engines).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - By changing the positions of inlet or outlet openings -> F04C28/10
[F04C 28/185] by varying the useful pumping length of the cooperating members in the axial direction -- The code F04C 28/185 relates to positive-displacement pump systems for elastic fluids where the effective pumping length of the cooperating components is varied along the axial direction. Such variation is employed for control, monitoring or safety functions in rotary or oscillating-piston pumps.
[F04C 28/20] by changing the form of the inner or outer contour of the working chamber -- F04C 28/20 covers positive-displacement pumps, such as rotary-piston or oscillating-piston machines for liquids, where the working chamber's inner or outer contour is varied during operation to control flow or pressure, especially for elastic fluids. The change in shape provides built-in safety, monitoring or control functions for the pump system.
[F04C 28/22] by changing the eccentricity between cooperating members -- F04C 28/22 covers rotary or oscillating-piston positive-displacement pumps for elastic fluids where the eccentricity between cooperating members is altered to adjust performance and to provide control, monitoring, or safety functions.
[F04C 28/24] characterised by using valves controlling pressure or flow rate, e.g. discharge valves -- F04C 28/24 relates to positive-displacement pumps, especially rotary- or oscillating-piston types, that incorporate valves to regulate pressure or flow rate, such as discharge valves. The valves are used for control, monitoring or safety of pumping installations handling elastic fluids.
[F04C 28/26] using bypass channels -- F04C 28/26 covers control, monitoring or safety features for pumps or pumping installations that handle elastic fluids, specifically those that employ bypass channels to regulate flow, protect components or maintain operating conditions. It falls within mechanical engineering, positive-displacement machines for liquids, and rotary-piston or oscillating-piston pump types.
[F04C 28/265] being obtained by displacing a lateral sealing face -- This CPC subgroup covers control, monitoring or safety features for positive-displacement pumps that handle elastic fluids, specifically those in which the sealing face moves laterally during operation. It applies to rotary- or oscillating-piston machines where the lateral sealing face is displaced as part of the pump's design.
[F04C 28/28] Safety arrangements -- F04C 28/28 covers safety and monitoring devices for pumps or pumping installations that handle elastic fluids, such as oil or liquefied gas, in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement machines. It includes protective shut-off, pressure-relief, and control systems intended to prevent over-pressure or leakage.
[F04C 29/00] Component parts, details or accessories of pumps or pumping installations, not provided for in groups -- F04C 29/00 covers miscellaneous components, details and accessories of pumps or pumping installations that are not specifically listed in other groups of the CPC. It includes items such as special seals, mounting brackets, control devices and other ancillary parts for positive-displacement liquid pumps.
[F04C 29/0007] Injection of a fluid in the working chamber for sealing, cooling and lubricating  sealing only  F04C27/00 ; lubrication only  F04C29/02 ; cooling  F02B47/02 ,  F02D21/00 ,  F02M25/00 -- The CPC subgroup F04C 29/0007 covers components and accessories for rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement liquid pumps that introduce fluid into the working chamber specifically for sealing, cooling or lubricating functions. It details arrangements where the injected fluid serves only sealing, only lubrication, or only cooling, referencing related sub-groups for each purpose.
[F04C 29/0014] with control systems for the injection of the fluid -- F04C 29/0014 covers components, details or accessories for rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement pumps that incorporate control systems for injecting the fluid. The entry pertains to auxiliary parts that manage flow, timing or pressure during the pumping process, extending the basic pump hardware.
[F04C 29/0021] Systems for the equilibration of forces acting on the pump  interstice adjustment other than by fluid pressure  F01C21/102 -- F04C 29/0021 covers mechanical arrangements for balancing forces acting on the clearance or interstice of a positive-displacement pump, using methods other than hydraulic pressure. It includes devices that adjust or compensate these forces to improve pump performance and reduce wear.
[F04C 29/0028] Internal leakage control -- F04C 29/0028 covers devices and methods for reducing or managing internal leakage within rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement pumps. It includes seals, clearance-adjusting mechanisms, labyrinths and other components that prevent fluid passage between moving parts that are not otherwise classified.
[F04C 29/0035] Equalization of pressure pulses  silencing  F04C29/06 -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Silencing -> F04C29/06 - Vibration control -> F04C2270/12
[F04C 29/0042] Driving elements, brakes, couplings, transmissions specially adapted for pumps  brakes, couplings, transmissions  per se   F16 ,  B60 -- The CPC entry F04C 29/0042 covers driving elements, brakes, couplings and transmissions that are specially designed for use with positive-displacement pumps, particularly rotary-piston or oscillating-piston types. It includes components that transmit, control or interrupt power to the pump's moving parts.
[F04C 29/005] Means for transmitting movement from the prime mover to driven parts of the pump, e.g. clutches, couplings, transmissions -- F04C 29/005 covers the components that transfer power from a pump's prime mover to its driven elements, including clutches, couplings and other transmission devices. These parts are classified here when they are not specifically covered by other groups for pumps or pumping installations.
[F04C 29/0057] for eccentric movement -- F04C 29/0057 covers components, details or accessories of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement pumps that are designed specifically for providing or accommodating eccentric movement of the piston or related parts, where such items are not listed in other CPC groups. It relates to the mechanical elements that create the offset motion required for the pump's operation.
[F04C 29/0064] Magnetic couplings -- F04C 29/0064 covers magnetic couplings, devices that transmit rotary motion between a drive and a pump shaft by magnetic fields rather than a physical shaft. They are used as components or accessories in positive-displacement pumps to avoid seal wear and fluid leakage. The coupling typically consists of an internal and external magnet assembly that remains sealed from the pumped liquid.
[F04C 29/0071] Couplings between rotors and input or output shafts acting by interengaging or mating parts, i.e. positive coupling of rotor and shaft
[F04C 29/0078] Fixing rotors on shafts, e.g. by clamping together hub and shaft -- The entry covers methods and devices for attaching a rotor to a shaft in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement pumps, such as clamping the hub and shaft together. It includes auxiliary parts, details or accessories that secure the rotor to the rotating shaft, enabling reliable power transmission within the pump assembly.
[F04C 29/0085] Prime movers -- The F04C 29/0085 entry defines the prime movers that power positive-displacement pumps for liquids, covering the external engines, motors or turbines that drive rotary-piston or oscillating-piston pump units. It includes electric, internal-combustion, hydraulic or steam driven sources, but excludes the pump components themselves.
[F04C 29/0092] Removing solid or liquid contaminants from the gas under pumping, e.g. by filtering or deposition; Purging; Scrubbing; Cleaning -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Prevention of deposits in the pump -> F04C2280/02
[F04C 29/02] Lubrication -- F04C 29/02 covers components, details or accessories of pumps and pumping installations that are used for lubrication. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically positive-displacement rotary or oscillating-piston machines for liquids. The classification addresses lubrication systems integrated with pump hardware.
[F04C 29/021] Control systems for the circulation of the lubricant -- This group covers devices and arrangements that regulate the flow of lubricating oil in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement pumps and their installations, including valves, sensors, regulators and control circuits not elsewhere specified.
[F04C 29/023] Lubricant distribution through a hollow driving shaft  F04C29/025  takes precedence -- The class covers arrangements for supplying lubricant through the hollow rotating shaft that also transmits power in positive-displacement liquid pumps such as rotary-piston or oscillating-piston types. It includes design features that integrate lubrication channels within the drive shaft to feed bearings or moving parts of the pump.
[F04C 29/025] using a lubricant pump -- F04C 29/025 covers the use of a separate lubricant pump within hydraulic or other fluid-power installations, detailing how the pump supplies lubrication to moving components of the main pump or system. It includes arrangements, connections and control features specific to the lubricant feed.
[F04C 29/026] Lubricant separation -- F04C 29/026 covers components and accessories that separate lubricating oil from the fluid being pumped in positive-displacement pumps. The entry includes oil-water separators, centrifugal or coalescing devices and related arrangements that remove lubricant from the discharge stream.
[F04C 29/028] Means for improving or restricting lubricant flow -- This classification covers devices incorporated in rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement pumps that control the flow of lubricant, such as flow regulators, restrictors or enhancers, and other accessories not covered by other groups.
[F04C 29/04] Heating -- F04C 29/04 pertains to heating provisions for pumps or pumping installations, encompassing heating devices, jackets, coils or temperature-control elements integrated into pump components to warm the pumped liquid or the apparatus itself. It covers parts not classified in more specific groups.
[F04C 29/042] by injecting a fluid  injection of fluid for sealing, cooling or lubrication  F04C29/0007 -- This CPC subclass covers component parts, details or accessories of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement pumps in which a fluid is injected for sealing, cooling or lubrication. It is indexed under code F04C 29/042 within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy.
[F04C 29/045] of the electric motor in hermetic pumps -- F04C 29/045 covers the electric motor that drives hermetically sealed pumps, which are positive-displacement machines for liquids such as rotary-piston or oscillating-piston types. It pertains to the motor as a component part of the pump assembly, not included in other groups.
[F04C 29/047] Cooling of electronic devices installed inside the pump housing, e.g. inverters -- The CPC subclass F04C 29/047 concerns thermal management of electronic components, such as inverters, that are installed inside the housing of positive-displacement pumps. It covers cooling methods integrated within pump assemblies to dissipate heat from the electronics.
[F04C 29/06] Silencing -- F04C 29/06 pertains to silencing devices and measures for positive-displacement liquid pumps, such as mufflers, acoustic enclosures, vibration dampers and other accessories that reduce noise generated by rotary-piston or oscillating-piston pump installations.
[F04C 29/061] Silencers using overlapping frequencies, e.g. Helmholtz resonators -- F04C 29/061 covers silencers integrated into positive-displacement pump assemblies that reduce noise by using overlapping acoustic frequencies, such as Helmholtz resonator designs. The class applies to component parts, details or accessories of rotary-piston or oscillating-piston pumps not otherwise listed.
[F04C 29/063] Sound absorbing materials -- F04C 29/063 relates to sound-absorbing materials employed in the construction or retro-fitting of positive-displacement pumps and their accessories. It includes acoustic insulation, liners, baffles and other damping elements that reduce pump noise, but does not cover the pump's functional parts themselves.
[F04C 29/065] Noise dampening volumes, e.g. muffler chambers -- F04C 29/065 covers components that reduce acoustic emissions in positive-displacement liquid pumps, specifically chambers such as mufflers that attenuate noise generated by rotary-piston or oscillating-piston pump mechanisms. These parts are not listed elsewhere in the pump-accessories groups.
[F04C 29/066] with means to enclose the source of noise -- F04C 29/066 covers component parts, details or accessories of positive-displacement pumps or pumping installations that incorporate a housing or enclosure designed to contain the source of noise. The noise-enclosing means may be integrated with the pump casing, cover or auxiliary structure to reduce acoustic emissions.
[F04C 29/068] the silencing means being arranged inside the pump housing -- This subclass covers internal silencing devices mounted inside a pump housing to reduce acoustic emissions. It includes baffles, sound-absorbing liners or other noise-attenuating elements that form part of the pump structure rather than external accessories.
[F04C 29/12] Arrangements for admission or discharge of the working fluid, e.g. constructional features of the inlet or outlet -- F04C 29/12 covers the design of inlet and outlet features of positive-displacement rotary- or oscillating-piston liquid pumps, including how the working fluid is admitted to or discharged from the pump housing. It applies to component parts, details or accessories of pumping installations that are not specified in other groups.
[F04C 29/122] Arrangements for supercharging the working space  similar arrangements for internal combustion engines  F02B33/00 ,  F02B37/00
[F04C 29/124] with inlet and outlet valves specially adapted for rotary or oscillating piston pumps -- The code F04C 29/124 designates components, details or accessories that incorporate inlet and outlet valves specially adapted for rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement liquid pumps. It lies within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for positive-displacement machines, specifically the rotary-piston subgroup.
[F04C 29/126] of the non-return type -- F04C 29/126 covers components, details or accessories of non-return type rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement pumps that are not listed in other groups, such as check-valve mechanisms, bypass devices or other fittings designed to permit flow in only one direction within pump installations.
[F04C 29/128] of the elastic type, e.g. reed valves -- This CPC subgroup covers elastic components such as reed valves that are used as flexible flow-control or check elements in positive-displacement pumps and related pumping installations, where they are not classified elsewhere.
[F04C 3/00] Rotary-piston machines or pumps, with non-parallel axes of movement of co-operating members, e.g. of screw type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Rotary-piston machines or pumps, with non-parallel axes of movement of co-operating members, e.g. of screw type  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Rotary screw type piston pumps with parallel axes -> F04C2/16 - Rotary-piston pumps with the working-chamber walls at least partly resiliently deformable -> F04C5/00 - Rotary-piston pumps with non-parallel axes of movement of co-operating members specially adapted for elastic fluids -> F04C18/48
[F04C 3/02] the axes being arranged at an angle of 90 degrees -- F04C 3/02 covers rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement machines for liquids in which the cooperating members move on non-parallel axes that are arranged at a right-angle to each other, such as certain screw-type pumps.
[F04C 3/04] of intermeshing engagement type, i.e. with engagement of co-operating members similar to that of toothed gearing -- F04C 3/04 pertains to rotary-piston positive-displacement machines for moving liquids in which the cooperating members engage by intermeshing, comparable to toothed gearing. The classification includes screw-type pumps and other rotary mechanisms where the axes of the members are not parallel and motion is transferred through gear-like engagement.
[F04C 3/06] the axes being arranged otherwise than at an angle of 90 degrees -- This CPC subclass F04C 3/06 covers rotary-piston or screw-type positive-displacement liquid machines whose cooperating members move on non-parallel axes that are not arranged at a right angle. It is part of the broader group for positive-displacement machines for liquids within mechanical engineering.
[F04C 3/08] of intermeshing engagement type, i.e. with engagement of co-operating members similar to that of toothed gearing -- Rotary-piston positive-displacement machines in which the cooperating members engage by intermeshing like gear teeth, such as screw-type or other non-parallel axis pumps, are classified under CPC code F04C 3/08. The entry covers designs where the rotor and stator or similar elements mesh as a toothed gear to move liquid.
[F04C 3/085] the axes of cooperating members being on the same plane -- This subclass covers rotary-piston or oscillating-piston positive-displacement machines for liquids in which the cooperating members move on axes that lie in a single plane rather than being offset in three dimensions, such as screw-type arrangements with coplanar shafts.
[F04C 5/00] Rotary-piston machines or pumps with the working-chamber walls at least partly resiliently deformable -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Such pumps specially adapted for elastic fluids -> F04C18/00 - Peristaltic pumps -> F04B43/12
[F04C 7/00] Rotary-piston machines or pumps with fluid ring or the like -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Rotary-piston machines or pumps in which the rotary-piston is sealed by a mass of liquid rotating inside the housing.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Such pumps specially adapted for elastic fluids -> F04C19/00
[F04C 9/00] Oscillating-piston machines or pumps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Rotary-piston machines or pumps in which the rotary-piston moves back and forth inside the working chamber.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Rotary-piston pumps with coaxially mounted members having continuously-changing circumferential spacing between them -> F04C2/063 - Such pumps specially adapted for elastic fluids -> F04C21/00
[F04C 9/002] the piston oscillating around a fixed axis -- F04C 9/002 addresses positive-displacement pumps where a piston swings around a fixed axis to move liquid. The oscillating motion creates a reciprocating volume change that draws fluid in on one swing and expels it on the opposite swing, characteristic of oscillating-piston machines.
[F04C 9/005] the piston oscillating in the space, e.g. around a fixed point  rotary-piston machines or pumps with non-parallel axes of movement between co-operating members  F04C3/00 -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Rotary-piston machines or pumps with non-parallel axes of movement between co-operating members -> F04C3/00 - Rotary-piston machines or pumps in which the working fluid is compressed in a flexible chamber -> F04B43/1207
[F04C 9/007] the points of the moving element describing approximately an alternating movement in axial direction with respect to the other element -- This CPC subgroup covers oscillating-piston or positive-displacement machines for liquids in which the points of the moving element follow an approximately alternating axial movement relative to the other element, describing back-and-forth motion rather than continuous rotation.
[F04D] NON-POSITIVE-DISPLACEMENT PUMPS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non positive displacement pumps for liquids, for elastic fluids, or for liquids and elastic fluids, whether rotary or not having pure rotation.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Engine fuel-injection pumps -> F02M - Ion pumps -> H01J41/12 - Electrodynamic pumps -> H02K44/02  **Glossary:** - Engine: means a device for continuously converting fluid energy into mechanical power. Thus, this term includes, for example, steam piston engines or steam turbines, per se, or internal-combustion piston engines, but it excludes single-stroke devices - Pump: means a device for continuously raising, forcing, compressing, or exhausting fluid by mechanical or other means. Thus, this term includes fans or blowers - Machine: means a device which could equally be an engine and a pump, and not a device which is restricted to an engine or one which is restricted to a pump - Positive displacement: means the way the energy of a working fluid is transformed into mechanical energy, in which variations of volume created by the working fluid in a working chamber produce equivalent displacements of the mechanical member transmitting the energy - Non-positive displacement: means the way the energy of a working fluid is transformed into mechanical energy, by transformation of the energy of the working fluid into kinetic energy, and vice versa - Rotary-piston machine: means a positive-displacement machine in which a fluid-engaging work-transmitting member rotates about a fixed axis or about an axis moving along a circular or similar orbit. This definition applies also to engines and pumps
[F04D 1/00] Pumping liquids, or liquids and elastic fluids, by rotary pumps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Radial and mixed flow type pumps for non elastic fluids  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Shear force pumps -> F04D5/001 - Adaptation to specific liquids -> F04D7/00 - Priming of pumps -> F04D9/00
[F04D 1/003] Having contrarotating parts -- F04D 1/003 defines rotary positive-displacement pumps that incorporate two or more components rotating in opposite directions to transfer liquids or liquid-elastic mixtures. The contrarotating parts balance torque, reduce pulsation and improve efficiency, and are used in applications where smooth, controlled fluid delivery is required.
[F04D 1/006] double suction pumps -- Double suction pumps are rotary pumps that draw liquid into the pump through two suction inlets positioned opposite each other, improving balance and reducing axial thrust. They are used in applications requiring high flow rates and low vibration, such as water supply and industrial fluid handling.
[F04D 1/02] having non-centrifugal stages, e.g. centripetal -- F04D 1/02 covers rotary pumps that incorporate non-centrifugal stages, such as centripetal stages, within a generally positive-displacement pump design. These pumps move liquids or liquid-elastic-fluid mixtures and are classified under mechanical engineering.
[F04D 1/025] Comprising axial and radial stages -- F04D 1/025 covers rotary pumps that combine axial and radial stages in a single machine, enabling liquid and elastic-fluid displacement through both axial and radial flow paths. These hybrid-stage designs are employed where compact, efficient pumping of liquids is required.
[F04D 1/04] Helico-centrifugal pumps -- Helico-centrifugal pumps are a type of rotary pump that move liquids by imparting a helical motion combined with centrifugal force. They are used where moderate pressure and relatively high flow are required, typical in water supply, chemical processing and HVAC systems.
[F04D 1/06] Multi-stage pumps
[F04D 1/063] of the vertically split casing type -- The entry F04D 1/063 covers rotary liquid pumps that are not positive-displacement, specifically those whose pump casing is divided vertically into two halves. This vertical split casing design facilitates assembly, maintenance, or integration of pump components while handling liquids or liquid-elastic fluid mixtures. It falls under non-positive-displacement pumps within the broader class of mechanical engineering.
[F04D 1/066] the casing consisting of a plurality of annuli bolted together -- This subclass defines pump housings formed from several ring-shaped sections that are assembled and secured together with bolts, creating a segmented casing for rotary pumps that move liquids or liquid-elastic fluid mixtures.
[F04D 1/08] the stages being situated concentrically -- F04D 1/08 pertains to rotary pumps whose pumping stages are arranged concentrically around a common axis, so the fluid moves through successive impellers without axial displacement. This design is used to compactly increase pressure or flow while maintaining balance and reducing the overall length of the pump.
[F04D 1/10] with means for changing the flow-path through the stages, e.g. series-parallel, e.g. side loads -- The code F04D 1/10 classifies rotary pumps that move liquids or liquid-elastic mixtures and incorporate mechanisms to alter the flow path between stages, such as series-parallel configurations or side-load arrangements. It falls under non-positive-displacement pumps within mechanical engineering.
[F04D 11/00] Other rotary non-positive-displacement pumps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Any rotary non-positive displacement which does not have radial, axial or helico centrifugal flow.
[F04D 11/005] Swash-type impeller pumps -- This class covers rotary, non-positive-displacement pumps that use a swash-type impeller, where the impeller blades are set at an angle to create a sinusoidal motion that displaces fluid as the rotor turns. It is suited for low-pressure, high-flow applications such as fuel or hydraulic systems.
[F04D 1/12] Pumps with scoops or like paring members protruding in the fluid circulating in a bowl -- F04D 1/12 classifies rotary pumps that convey liquid by means of scoops, blades or similar protruding members that extend into the fluid contained in a rotating bowl. The scoops dip into the liquid, lift it, and discharge it as the bowl turns, a principle used in certain centrifugal and mixed-flow pump designs.
[F04D 1/14] Pumps raising fluids by centrifugal force within a conical rotary bowl with vertical axis -- F04D 1/14 covers pumps that raise fluids by centrifugal force generated in a conical rotary bowl that spins around a vertical axis, typically used for moving liquids and elastic fluids without positive displacement.
[F04D 13/00] Pumping installations or systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The combination of a pump with its drive system or its integration into a system either comprising several pumps or comprising storage systems  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Control of pumps -> F04D15/00
[F04D 13/02] Units comprising pumps and their driving means -- The entry covers integrated units that combine a pump with its driving mechanism, such as motor-pump assemblies or packaged pump sets, used in non-positive-displacement pumping installations. It classifies complete pump-driven systems rather than separate pump or drive components.
[F04D 13/021] containing a coupling -- F04D 13/021 covers pumping installations or systems that include a coupling for connecting components such as pumps, motors or drive shafts. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering, positive-displacement machines for liquids, and specifically non-positive-displacement pumps.
[F04D 13/022] a coupling allowing slip, e.g. torque converter -- F04D 13/022 pertains to couplings that permit slip between rotating parts in pumping installations, notably torque converters that smooth and transmit torque while allowing relative motion, thereby protecting and controlling drive performance in non-positive-displacement pump systems.
[F04D 13/023] for reducing start torque -- The code F04D 13/023 refers to pumping installations for reducing start torque. It falls under non-positive-displacement pumps in the mechanical engineering category. This classification is used in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[F04D 13/024] a magnetic coupling -- F04D 13/024 relates to pumping installations that transmit power by means of a magnetic coupling, which transfers torque between the drive and the pump without a direct mechanical seal. The magnetic coupling eliminates shaft sealing, reducing the risk of fluid leakage in non-positive-displacement pump systems.
[F04D 13/025] Details of the can separating the pump and drive area -- F04D 13/025 concerns the design of the sealed can that isolates the pump from its drive unit in non-positive-displacement pump installations, covering constructions, sealing methods and material choices for the separating housing that protects the pump chamber from mechanical vibrations and leakage.
[F04D 13/026] Details of the bearings -- The code F04D 13/026 covers specifications and design aspects of bearings used in non-positive-displacement pump systems, including bearing type, material, lubrication method, load capacity, and arrangement within the pump assembly. It addresses how bearings contribute to the reliability and performance of pumping installations.
[F04D 13/027] Details of the magnetic circuit -- F04D 13/027 covers the magnetic circuit of pump installations, describing how magnetic fields are created, guided and controlled in devices such as magnetically driven or coupled pumps. It includes the arrangement of permanent magnets, electromagnets, magnetic yokes and shielding that transmit torque to the fluid-moving parts without direct mechanical contact. The code is used to classify patents dealing with magnetic-drive pump technology within the broader class of non-positive-displacement pumps.
[F04D 13/028] the driving means being a planetary gear -- F04D 13/028 classifies pumping installations or systems in which a non-positive-displacement pump is driven by a planetary-gear transmission, detailing the use of gear sets that rotate around a common sun gear to provide the pump's driving power.
[F04D 13/04] the pump being fluid driven -- F04D 13/04 relates to pumps that are driven directly by the fluid they convey, using the fluid's own pressure or kinetic energy to operate without external power sources. Examples include hydraulic-ram pumps, vortex or vortex-type pumps, and other self-acting, fluid-driven displacement machines.
[F04D 13/043] the pump wheel carrying the fluid driving means -- The subclass F04D 13/043 deals with pump wheels that are driven by the fluid they convey, meaning the moving liquid itself provides the propulsion for the wheel. It applies to non-positive-displacement pump arrangements within broader mechanical-engineering pumping installations.
[F04D 13/046] the fluid driving means being a hydraulic motor of the positive displacement type -- This CPC entry F04D 13/046 covers pumping installations or systems where the fluid driving means is a hydraulic motor of the positive displacement type, used in non-positive-displacement pumps. It falls under mechanical engineering, positive-displacement machines for liquids, non-positive-displacement pumps.
[F04D 13/06] the pump being electrically driven -- This CPC subclass concerns pumping installations in which the motive power is supplied by an electric motor, covering electrically driven positive-displacement pumps and the associated drive and control components.
[F04D 13/0606] Canned motor pumps -- Canned motor pumps are hermetically sealed, non-positive-displacement pumps in which the drive motor is enclosed within the pump housing, eliminating the need for shaft seals and allowing the safe transfer of liquids, especially hazardous or corrosive fluids. The design integrates the motor and pump into a single, sealed unit, providing reliable operation with minimal leakage and maintenance. This class covers installations and systems that employ such sealed motor-pump assemblies.
[F04D 13/0613] Special connection between the rotor compartments -- The code F04D 13/0613 relates to pumping installations and specifies a special connection between the compartments of a pump rotor, such as ducts, passages or other links that enable fluid or mechanical interaction across rotor sections.
[F04D 13/062] pressure compensation between motor- and pump- compartment -- A pressure-compensation arrangement that balances fluid forces between the motor side and the pump side of a non-positive-displacement pump, ensuring stable operating conditions and preventing overload or cavitation. This feature is used in pumping installations where motor and pump compartments are coupled but require independent pressure control.
[F04D 13/0626] Details of the can -- F04D 13/0626 covers the design and construction details of the can, the pressure-tight housing that contains the moving parts of a non-positive-displacement pump. It relates to pumping installations or systems within the broader class of mechanical engineering for positive-displacement machines for liquids.
[F04D 13/0633] Details of the bearings -- F04D 13/0633 addresses the design, mounting and lubrication of bearings in pumping installations or systems that use non-positive-displacement pumps. It includes bearing types, materials, support structures and arrangements that influence pump efficiency, durability and vibration control, and falls under mechanical engineering, positive-displacement machines for liquids, and non-positive-displacement pumps.
[F04D 13/064] Details of the magnetic circuit -- F04D 13/064 refers to features of the magnetic circuit in pumping installations or systems, especially those employing electromagnetic principles for fluid movement. It covers design, arrangement, and material aspects of magnetic paths, cores, and windings that affect pump performance and control.
[F04D 13/0646] the hollow pump or motor shaft being the conduit for the working fluid -- This subclass covers pumping installations in which the pump or motor shaft is hollow and functions as a passage for the working fluid, allowing the fluid to flow through the rotating shaft as part of the pump's operation.
[F04D 13/0653] the motor being flooded -- F04D 13/0653 covers pumping installations in which the drive motor is intentionally placed in, or allowed to become, flooded by the liquid being moved. It relates to designs where the motor operates while immersed for cooling, protection or compact integration within the pump system.
[F04D 13/066] Floating-units -- Floating-units are buoyant pump assemblies or complete pumping installations that rest on or are suspended in a liquid, allowing them to rise and fall with the fluid level; they are commonly used in offshore, dredging or water-treatment applications where the pump must adapt to changing water heights.
[F04D 13/0666] the motor being of the plane gap type -- The entry covers pump installations that use a plane-gap motor, a type of electric motor where the rotor and stator are separated by a uniform air gap that provides a constant magnetic flux path. It applies to non-positive displacement pumps within the broader category of positive-displacement machines for liquids.
[F04D 13/0673] the motor being of the inside-out type -- F04D 13/0673 covers pumping installations that employ a motor of the inside-out type, where the rotor is mounted around the stator rather than inside it. The classification includes the design, arrangement and integration of such motors with non-positive-displacement pumps and related mechanical systems.
[F04D 13/068] Battery powered -- This CPC subclass covers pumping installations or systems that operate using battery power. It includes non-positive displacement pumps that are powered by portable or stationary batteries rather than mains electricity or other fuel sources.
[F04D 13/0686] Mechanical details of the pump control unit  pump control  F04D15/00 -- The F04D 13/0686 entry covers the mechanical design of the pump-control unit within non-positive-displacement pumping installations. It details components such as actuators, valves, and linkage mechanisms that regulate pump speed, flow, and pressure. The classification falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, positive-displacement machines for liquids, and non-positive-displacement pumps.
[F04D 13/0693] Details or arrangements of the wiring -- This CPC subclass concerns the electrical wiring layout and connections for pumping installations that use non-positive-displacement pumps, covering how cables, control circuits and power distribution are arranged within the system. It falls under mechanical engineering, positive-displacement machines for liquids, non-positive-displacement pumps, and pumping installations or systems.
[F04D 13/08] for submerged use -- F04D 13/08 covers pumping installations or systems that are intended for submerged use, meaning the entire pump operates fully underwater such as in wells, tanks, or underwater pipelines. It falls under non-positive-displacement pumps within the broader category of positive-displacement machines for liquids.
[F04D 13/083] and protected by a gas-bell -- F04D 13/083 relates to pumping installations or systems in which the pump is enclosed or protected by a gas-bell, a sealed chamber that uses gas pressure to isolate the moving parts from the environment. The classification covers designs where the gas-bell provides safety, reduces noise, or prevents contamination in non-positive-displacement pump arrangements.
[F04D 13/086] the pump and drive motor are both submerged -- F04D 13/086 covers pump installations where both the pump and its drive motor are placed entirely underwater, operating as a submersible unit. This arrangement eliminates the need for external shafts and is typical for applications such as borehole water extraction, oil-well pumping, and other submerged fluid-handling systems.
[F04D 13/10] adapted for use in mining bore holes -- F04D 13/10 covers pumping installations or systems that are specifically adapted for operation in mining bore holes. These devices are non-positive-displacement pumps designed to move liquids in the confined, high-pressure environment of underground drilling.
[F04D 13/12] Combinations of two or more pumps -- F04D 13/12 covers pumping installations that integrate two or more pumps, either in series, parallel, or hybrid arrangements, to increase flow, pressure, or reliability. It includes combined drive mechanisms, coordinated control, and shared housings for multiple pumps within a single system.
[F04D 13/14] the pumps being all of centrifugal type -- F04D 13/14 covers pumping installations or systems that employ only centrifugal pumps, specifying the complete arrangement, selection, and operation of such non-positive-displacement machines. It includes design considerations for the integration of multiple centrifugal pumps within a single installation. This subclass is used to classify patents dealing with fully centrifugal-type pump configurations.
[F04D 13/16] with storage reservoirs -- This subclass covers non-positive-displacement pump installations that include storage reservoirs for the fluid being moved, such as systems using tanks or surge vessels to regulate flow or pressure. It is situated under mechanical engineering, positive-displacement machines for liquids, non-positive-displacement pumps.
[F04D 15/00] Control, e.g. regulation, of pumps, pumping installations or systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Systems and schemes for controlling a pump, either matching the pump to the requirements of the system or avoiding harmful conditions for the pump
[F04D 15/0005] by using valves -- This subclass addresses the regulation of non-positive-displacement pumps by employing valves to control flow, pressure or speed within pumping installations, covering valve types, placement and control strategies used to manage pump performance.
[F04D 15/0011] by-pass valves -- The subclass F04D 15/0011 deals with by-pass valves that are employed to regulate the operation of non-positive displacement pumps or pumping installations. These valves provide an alternate flow path so that fluid can be diverted around the pump for control of pressure or flow.
[F04D 15/0016] mixing-reversing- or deviation valves -- The CPC class F04D 15/0016 covers valves that mix, reverse flow, or deviate fluid streams in non-positive-displacement pump systems, providing control over pump operation and hydraulic performance. These devices adjust the direction or proportion of flow to achieve mixing or alter pressure conditions within pumping installations.
[F04D 15/0022] throttling valves or valves varying the pump inlet opening or the outlet opening -- This CPC subclass deals with control of non-positive-displacement pumps by means of throttling or adjustable valves that vary the pump's inlet or outlet opening to regulate flow or pressure. Such valves are used to fine-tune pump performance in hydraulic and fluid-handling systems.
[F04D 15/0027] Varying behaviour or the very pump  F04D15/0055  and  F04D29/46  take precedence
[F04D 15/0033] By-passing by increasing clearance between impeller and its casing -- The code F04D 15/0033 covers pump control techniques that create a by-pass flow by enlarging the clearance between the impeller and its casing, effectively allowing fluid to leak around the impeller to reduce pressure or flow rate. This method is used for regulating the performance of non-positive-displacement pumps within mechanical engineering systems.
[F04D 15/0038] by varying the effective cross-sectional area of flow through the rotor
[F04D 15/0044] by introducing a gas -- F04D 15/0044 covers methods of controlling or regulating pumps or pumping systems by introducing a gas into the fluid flow. The gas is used to modify pressure or flow characteristics, allowing precise adjustment of pump performance.
[F04D 15/005] the pumps being of the circumferential flow type
[F04D 15/0055] Rotors with adjustable blades -- F04D 15/0055 refers to rotors whose blades can be adjusted in pitch or angle to modify flow or pressure in non-positive-displacement pump systems. The entry deals with control features that regulate the performance of pumps, pumping installations or related fluid-handling equipment.
[F04D 15/0061] responsive to temperature -- F04D 15/0061 relates to control techniques for non-positive-displacement pumps that adjust operation in response to temperature variations, covering regulation of pumping installations or systems based on thermal conditions.
[F04D 15/0066] by changing the speed, e.g. of the driving engine -- The code refers to methods for controlling pumps or pumping systems by varying the speed of the driving engine, such as changing motor rpm to adjust flow rate or pressure. This speed-adjustment approach is used to regulate the performance of non-positive-displacement pumps.
[F04D 15/0072] Installation or systems with two or more pumps, wherein the flow path through the stages can be changed, e.g. series-parallel -- It covers pump installations that use two or more pumps and allow the fluid path to be reconfigured, for example by switching between series and parallel arrangements to control flow distribution and system performance.
[F04D 15/0077] Safety measures  F04D15/02  takes precedence -- The CPC subclass F04D 15/0077 deals with safety measures for the control of non-positive-displacement pump installations, including protective devices, alarm systems and automatic shutdown functions. It is intended to take precedence over the broader control provisions of F04D15/02.
[F04D 15/0083] Protection against sudden pressure change, e.g. check valves -- The entry covers devices that safeguard non-positive-displacement pump systems from rapid pressure fluctuations, such as water-hammer or surge events. It includes mechanisms like check valves and other controls that automatically restrict flow or relieve excess pressure to protect the installation.
[F04D 15/0088] Testing machines -- The CPC entry F04D 15/0088 refers to machines that test pumps or pumping installations, assessing performance, efficiency, durability or control functions. It includes devices that simulate operating conditions, measure flow, pressure and power, and verify that pump systems meet required specifications. This class is situated under mechanical-engineering categories for non-positive-displacement pumps and their regulation.
[F04D 15/0094] Indicators of rotational movement -- F04D 15/0094 covers devices that detect or display the rotational movement of pump shafts or other rotating components in pumping systems. Such indicators are used to monitor speed, direction or phase of operation, aiding control, safety and maintenance of non-positive-displacement pumps.
[F04D 15/02] Stopping of pumps, or operating valves, on occurrence of unwanted conditions -- This CPC subclass covers devices or methods that automatically stop a pump or operate a valve when undesirable conditions such as over-pressure, dry-run, overheating or system faults occur. It falls under the control of non-positive-displacement pumps within mechanical engineering.
[F04D 15/0209] responsive to a condition of the working fluid  F04D15/029  takes precedence -- F04D 15/0209 covers control methods for non-positive-displacement pump installations that automatically adjust operation according to a condition of the working fluid such as pressure, temperature, flow rate or viscosity. The regulation may be performed by sensors, valves or electronic controllers to maintain desired performance or protect the system.
[F04D 15/0218] the condition being a liquid level or a lack of liquid supply -- This CPC subclass deals with control functions for non-positive-displacement pumps, covering regulation of pump installations or systems where the operating condition is the liquid level or a lack of liquid supply. It is nested under mechanical engineering → positive-displacement machines for liquids → non-positive-displacement pumps.
[F04D 15/0227] Lack of liquid level being detected using a flow transducer -- The code F04D 15/0227 pertains to methods and devices that detect a lack of liquid level by using a flow transducer, enabling control or regulation of pump installations. It is applied to non-positive-displacement pump systems where level detection is required for safe operation.
[F04D 15/0236] Lack of liquid level being detected by analysing the parameters of the electric drive, e.g. current or power consumption
[F04D 15/0245] responsive to a condition of the pump -- This subclass covers systems that adjust pump operation in response to measured conditions such as pressure, flow rate, or temperature. It includes sensors, controllers and feedback mechanisms that automatically regulate the pump to maintain desired performance.
[F04D 15/0254] the condition being speed or load -- F04D 15/0254 covers control measures that adjust pump operation based on speed or load conditions, applied to non-positive-displacement pumps and related pumping installations. The code addresses regulation methods that respond to these operational parameters.
[F04D 15/0263] the condition being temperature, ingress of humidity or leakage -- The CPC subclass F04D 15/0263 deals with control measures for pumps and pumping installations that address conditions such as temperature, ingress of humidity or leakage. It is part of the non-positive-displacement pump group within mechanical engineering and focuses on regulating these specific operational parameters.
[F04D 15/0272] the condition being wear or a position -- F04D 15/0272 covers control methods that monitor a pump's wear condition or its positional state and adjust operation accordingly, enabling regulation of non-positive-displacement pump systems based on wear-related or location-based parameters.
[F04D 15/0281] responsive to a condition not otherwise provided for -- This subgroup covers pump control methods that adjust operation in response to a condition not otherwise accounted for in the pump design, such as unexpected pressure spikes, temperature changes or flow variations, providing automatic regulation beyond standard set points.
[F04D 15/029] for pumps operating in parallel -- The code F04D 15/029 covers methods and devices for controlling pumps that operate in parallel, such as regulating flow, pressure or speed when several pumps run together in a system. It applies to non-positive-displacement pumping installations where coordinated control improves performance or energy efficiency.
[F04D 17/00] Pumping elastic fluids by rotary pumps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Radial and mixed-flow type pumps for elastic fluids.
[F04D 17/02] having non-centrifugal stages, e.g. centripetal -- A subclass of rotary pumps for elastic fluids that includes non-centrifugal stages, such as centripetal stages, within the broader group of positive-displacement machines. It covers designs where the pumping action is achieved without relying on centrifugal force, typically by directly moving the fluid with rotating elements.
[F04D 17/025] comprising axial flow and radial flow stages -- F04D 17/025 covers rotary pumps that move elastic fluids using both axial-flow and radial-flow stages within a single machine. These non-positive-displacement pumps combine the characteristics of axial and radial flow to handle liquids such as water or oil under varying pressure conditions.
[F04D 17/04] of transverse-flow type -- F04D 17/04 covers rotary pumps that move elastic fluids by a transverse-flow mechanism, displacing the fluid across the rotor axis rather than along it. Typical examples are gear, vane or screw-type pumps used in hydraulic systems.
[F04D 17/06] Helico-centrifugal pumps -- Helico-centrifugal pumps are rotary pumps that move elastic fluids using a helical flow path combined with centrifugal action. They belong to the non-positive-displacement pump group and are used where moderate pressure and smooth flow are required.
[F04D 17/08] Centrifugal pumps -- F04D 17/08 designates centrifugal pumps, a type of rotary pump that imparts kinetic energy to a fluid via a rotating impeller and converts it to increased pressure. These non-positive-displacement machines are used for moving liquids, especially elastic fluids, in applications ranging from water supply to chemical processing. The class covers the structure, operation and performance features of such pumps
[F04D 17/10] for compressing or evacuating -- This CPC subclass F04D 17/10 covers rotary non-positive-displacement pumps that compress or evacuate elastic fluids, such as liquids, by creating a pressure rise rather than moving a fixed volume. It includes designs where the pump imparts kinetic energy to the fluid and then converts it to pressure for flow.
[F04D 17/105] with double suction -- F04D 17/105 refers to rotary pumps designed for elastic fluids, such as rubber or polymer solutions, that incorporate a double-suction arrangement. In this configuration the impeller has two inlet openings on opposite sides, allowing simultaneous intake of fluid to balance forces, improve flow smoothness and reduce pulsation.
[F04D 17/12] Multi-stage pumps -- F04D 17/12 relates to multi-stage rotary pumps that convey elastic fluids, where the fluid passes through two or more impeller stages in series to raise pressure. These non-positive-displacement devices are employed where high head is required, such as in water supply, irrigation or industrial process systems.
[F04D 17/122] the individual rotor discs being, one for each stage, on a common shaft and axially spaced, e.g. conventional centrifugal multi- stage compressors -- F04D 17/122 covers multi-stage centrifugal compressors in which each stage has its own rotor disc mounted on a common shaft and spaced axially along it. The classification applies to rotary machines that pump elastic fluids without positive displacement, specifically designs where several disc rotors are arranged sequentially on a single shaft.
[F04D 17/125] the casing being vertically split -- F04D 17/125 covers rotary pumps for elastic fluids that incorporate a vertically split casing, allowing the pump housing to be opened along a vertical plane for assembly, inspection or maintenance. The split design facilitates handling of non-positive displacement pumps that move liquids such as oils, fuels or polymeric fluids.
[F04D 17/127] with radially spaced stages, e.g. for contrarotating type -- The F04D 17/127 subclass covers rotary pumps for elastic fluids that employ radially spaced stages, such as contrarotating pump arrangements. It is part of the non-positive-displacement pump group within mechanical engineering, describing designs where the pumping action is produced by rotating elements rather than by displacing a fixed volume.
[F04D 17/14] with means for changing the flow-path through the stages, e.g. series-parallel, e.g. side-loads, -- F04D 17/14 classifies rotary positive-displacement pumps that move elastic fluids and incorporate features to alter the flow path through the pump stages, such as series-parallel arrangements or side-load configurations, enabling variable fluid routing within the machine.
[F04D 17/16] for displacing without appreciable compression -- F04D 17/16 covers rotary pumps that move elastic fluids such as oil or water without causing significant compression of the fluid. These are non-positive-displacement devices, using the rotation of elements like gears, lobes or screws to create flow while the fluid's bulk modulus remains essentially unchanged.
[F04D 17/161] Shear force pumps -- Shear force pumps are non-positive-displacement rotary pumps that move elastic fluids by generating shear between rotating and stationary surfaces, rather than by trapping a fixed volume. The shear action creates a pressure gradient that propels the fluid through the pump, making the design well suited for low-viscosity or sensitive liquids. They fall under the CPC class F04D 17/161.
[F04D 17/162] Double suction pumps -- Double suction pumps are a type of rotary pump that draws fluid simultaneously from two opposite sides of the impeller, balancing axial forces and enhancing efficiency. They are used for moving elastic liquids such as water or oil, and fall under CPC code F04D 17/162.
[F04D 17/164] Multi-stage fans, e.g. for vacuum cleaners -- F04D 17/164 covers multi-stage fans that consist of two or more rotary impellers arranged in series to raise the pressure of a gas or vapor, such as the airflow generators used in vacuum cleaners and similar devices. The classification applies to fan constructions where each stage adds to the overall pressure rise, often employing axial or radial blades and may include housings, bearings, and drive mechanisms.
[F04D 17/165] Axial entry and discharge -- The F04D 17/165 subclass covers rotary pumps for elastic fluids where both the inlet and the outlet are aligned with the pump's axis. It includes designs that guide the fluid into the pump and expel it outward along the same axial direction, a configuration common in applications such as rubber or polymer handling.
[F04D 17/167] Operating by means of fibrous or porous elements  suction filters  F04D29/701 , e.g. with sponge rotors
[F04D 17/168] Pumps specially adapted to produce a vacuum -- F04D 17/168 covers pumps that are specifically designed to generate a vacuum rather than to deliver fluid flow. These include rotary pumps that create negative pressure for drawing liquids or gases, often used in applications such as vacuum cleaning, laboratory equipment, and industrial degassing. The class falls under non-positive-displacement pumps for elastic fluids.
[F04D 17/18] characterised by use of centrifugal force of liquids entrained in pumps -- This CPC subclass classifies rotary pumps that move elastic fluids by employing the centrifugal force acting on liquid entrained within the pump, typical of centrifugal and mixed-flow designs where the fluid is flung outward to generate flow.
[F04D 19/00] Axial-flow pumps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Axial flow type pumps for elastic fluids.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Combined axial-flow and radial-flow stages -> F04D17/025 - Pumps involving supersonic speeds in the pumped fluid -> F04D21/00
[F04D 19/002] Axial flow fans -- Axial-flow fans are rotating machines that generate a stream of air or gas parallel to the shaft axis by means of blades or impellers, directing the flow axially rather than radially. They are classified under mechanical engineering, non-positive-displacement pumps, specifically axial-flow pumps, with CPC code F04D 19/002.
[F04D 19/005] reversible fans -- F04D 19/005 covers reversible fans, a type of axial-flow pump that can operate in both directions to move air or fluid, allowing the same unit to provide thrust or suction as required. These devices are classified under mechanical engineering, positive-displacement machines for liquids, non-positive-displacement pumps, and specifically axial-flow pumps.
[F04D 19/007] multistage fans -- F04D 19/007 designates multistage fans, a type of axial-flow pump that employs two or more impeller stages in series to raise the pressure or volume of a fluid, commonly used for ventilation, cooling and industrial air-moving applications.
[F04D 19/02] Multi-stage pumps -- F04D 19/02 covers multi-stage pumps, which are axial-flow pumps built with two or more impeller stages in series to raise pressure or flow capacity beyond that of a single stage. These devices are classified under mechanical engineering, specifically non-positive-displacement pumps within the broader group of positive-displacement machines for liquids.
[F04D 19/022] with concentric rows of vanes; -- F04D 19/022 covers axial-flow pumps that employ concentric rows of vanes to move liquid along the pump axis, providing continuous flow without positive displacement. The arrangement of multiple vane rings improves efficiency and pressure handling in mechanical engineering applications.
[F04D 19/024] with contrarotating parts -- F04D 19/024 pertains to axial-flow pumps that employ two or more rotors or impellers turning in opposite directions. The contrarotating arrangement improves hydraulic efficiency, reduces slip and balances axial forces, and is used in non-positive-displacement pumping applications.
[F04D 19/026] with a plurality of shafts rotating at different speeds  F04D19/022  takes precedence -- This CPC subgroup F04D 19/026 relates to axial-flow pumps that use several shafts rotating at different speeds to control flow or pressure, a feature that refines the broader category of non-positive-displacement pumps. It builds on the preceding subgroup F04D 19/022.
[F04D 19/028] Layout of fluid flow through the stages -- F04D 19/028 classifies axial-flow pump designs that detail how the working fluid moves through the pump's successive stages, including the arrangement of inlet and outlet passages, flow-distribution components and the internal geometry that guides fluid from one stage to the next.
[F04D 19/04] specially adapted to the production of a high vacuum, e.g. molecular pumps -- F04D 19/04 covers axial-flow pumps that are specially designed to generate a high vacuum, for example molecular pumps. These devices belong to the non-positive-displacement pump group within mechanical engineering and are used to evacuate gases rather than to move liquids.
[F04D 19/042] Turbomolecular vacuum pumps -- F04D 19/042 covers turbomolecular vacuum pumps, which create high vacuum by using rapidly rotating blades to impart momentum to gas molecules, moving them from the inlet to the exhaust side. These pumps are non-positive-displacement, axial-flow devices that rely on molecular drag rather than mechanical displacement.
[F04D 19/044] Holweck-type pumps -- Holweck-type pumps are axial-flow machines that move fluid by means of helical grooves cut on a rotating cylinder and a stationary sleeve. The grooves act as drag surfaces, causing the liquid to be conveyed in the direction of the screw-like groove pattern, providing steady flow with relatively low pressure rise. These pumps are used where a compact, low-speed pump with smooth, pulsation-free delivery is required.
[F04D 19/046] Combinations of two or more different types of pumps -- F04D 19/046 covers axial-flow pumps that incorporate two or more different pump types in a single unit. The code lies within the CPC hierarchy for mechanical engineering, under positive-displacement machines for liquids, non-positive-displacement pumps.
[F04D 19/048] comprising magnetic bearings -- This subclass covers axial-flow pumps that are equipped with magnetic bearings, which support the rotating shaft without physical contact, reducing friction and wear. They fall under the non-positive-displacement pump group within mechanical engineering.
[F04D 21/00] Pump involving supersonic speed of pumped fluids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All pump for elastic fluids involving supersonic speed of pumped fluids.
[F04D 23/00] Other rotary non-positive-displacement pumps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Any rotary non positive displacement which does not have radial, axial or helico centrifugal flow, pumps adapted for conveying or for handling specific elastic fluid, and pumps with circumferential or transverse flow.
[F04D 23/001] Pumps adapted for conveying materials or for handling specific elastic fluids -- F04D 23/001 pertains to pumps that are specially adapted for moving particular materials or handling specific elastic fluids, such as viscous, compressible, or otherwise distinctive liquids, and includes design features that optimise performance for those substances.
[F04D 23/003] of radial-flow type -- F04D 23/003 covers rotary pumps that are not positive-displacement devices and that move fluid by radial flow, i.e., the fluid is directed outward from the rotating impeller toward the pump housing. These pumps rely on kinetic energy imparted by the impeller rather than by fixed volume chambers, and are used for a variety of low-pressure, high-flow applications.
[F04D 23/005] of axial-flow type -- F04D 23/005 classifies rotary non-positive-displacement pumps that move fluid by axial flow, meaning the liquid is propelled parallel to the pump shaft without a change in fluid volume. These pumps fall under mechanical engineering and are used where steady, low-pressure liquid transport is required.
[F04D 23/006] Creating a pulsating flow -- F04D 23/006 relates to methods and devices that produce a pulsating fluid flow in rotary non-positive-displacement pumps, typically by varying inlet or outlet pressure or using mechanical means to create periodic flow variations, often to improve pump performance or reduce cavitation.
[F04D 23/008] Regenerative pumps  for liquids or for liquids and elastic fluids  F04D5/002 -- Regenerative pumps are rotary non-positive-displacement devices that move liquids or liquids mixed with elastic fluids by imparting kinetic energy through a rotating impeller and a series of closely spaced chambers, creating a continuous flow at relatively low pressure but high volume. They are used in applications such as circulation, low-head boosting and hydraulic testing.
[F04D 25/00] Pumping installations or systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The combination of a pump for elastic fluids with its drive system or its integration into a system either comprising several pumps or comprising storage systems.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Pump installation control -> F04D27/00

[F04D 25/026] with a magnetic coupling -- F04D 25/026 classifies non-positive-displacement pump installations that use a magnetic coupling to transmit torque between the motor and the pump without a direct shaft seal, thereby eliminating leakage. The magnetic coupling allows sealed operation while maintaining the mechanical link required for fluid movement.
[F04D 25/028] the driving means being a planetary gear -- F04D 25/028 covers pumping installations or systems in which the pump's drive is a planetary-gear arrangement. [F04D 25/06] the pump being electrically driven -- F04D 25/06 relates to non-positive displacement pumps that are driven by an electric motor. [F04D 25/0606] the electric motor being specially adapted for integration in the pump -- This subclass defines pumps in which the drive is an electric motor specially designed to be built into the pump unit. [F04D 25/0613] the electric motor being of the inside-out type, i.e. the rotor is arranged radially outside a central stator -- F04D 25/0613 covers pumping installations that use an inside-out electric motor, where the rotor is positioned radially outside a central stator, to drive non-positive-displacement pumps. [F04D 25/062] Details of the bearings -- F04D 25/062 covers the design, construction and characteristics of bearings used in pumping installations or systems that belong to non-positive-displacement pumps. [F04D 25/0626] Details of the lubrication -- This subcategory covers the provision and management of lubrication within pumping installations for non-positive-displacement pumps, including methods, schedules, and system components that ensure reliable operation. [F04D 25/0633] Details of the magnetic circuit -- F04D 25/0633 classifies the magnetic-circuit features of pumping installations or systems, particularly in non-positive-displacement pumps such as those using electric or electromagnetic drives. It covers the design, arrangement and components of the magnetic circuit that generate the field required for pump operation.
[F04D 25/064] Details of the rotor -- The code F04D 25/064 covers technical information related to the rotor of non-positive-displacement pump installations or systems. It includes design features, geometry, materials and manufacturing aspects of the rotor that affect the pump's performance, such as flow patterns, sealing, and balance.
[F04D 25/0646] Details of the stator -- F04D 25/0646 covers the design and construction features of the stator in non-positive-displacement pump installations, such as geometry, winding, magnetic circuit and mounting arrangements. [F04D 25/0653] the motor having a plane air gap, e.g. disc-type -- This CPC subclass covers non-positive-displacement pump installations, specifically the type of motor that uses a plane air gap, for example disc-type rotating machines, within mechanical engineering of liquid displacement.
[F04D 25/066] Linear Motors -- F04D 25/066 covers linear motors employed in pumping installations or systems, specifically within non-positive-displacement pumps. [F04D 25/0666] a sensor is integrated into the pump/motor design -- The subclass F04D 25/0666 relates to pumping installations or systems in which a sensor is incorporated directly into the pump or motor construction. [F04D 25/0673] Battery powered -- A battery-powered non-positive-displacement pump used in pumping installations or systems. [F04D 25/068] Mechanical details of the pump control unit  pump control details  F04D27/00 -- The entry concerns the mechanical design of control units for non-positive-displacement pumps, covering components such as valves, speed governors, sensors and integrated control circuitry that regulate pump operation. [F04D 25/0693] Details or arrangements of the wiring -- F04D 25/0693 covers the detailed design and layout of electrical wiring in non-positive-displacement pump installations or systems. It includes the arrangement of cables, connectors and control circuitry that provide power and signals to pump components.
[F04D 25/08] the working fluid being air, e.g. for ventilation -- F04D 25/08 covers pumping installations or systems that move air rather than liquids, such as ventilation equipment. [F04D 25/082] the unit having provision for cooling the motor -- The code F04D 25/082 designates pump assemblies that include built-in provisions for cooling the drive motor, such as cooling jackets, fans or liquid-cooled circuits integrated into the unit. [F04D 25/084] hand fans -- Hand fans are manually operated devices that generate airflow by rotating blades attached to a handle, used for personal cooling or ventilation. [F04D 25/086] hand operated -- F04D 25/086 covers hand-operated pumping installations or systems that belong to the class of non-positive-displacement pumps, used for moving liquids without relying on a fixed volume per cycle.
