This is classified under mechanical engineering, positive-displacement machines for liquids, non-positive-displacement pumps, and specifically for cooling functions.
[F04D 29/5833] flow schemes and regulation thereto -- It pertains to the design of fluid flow pathways and control mechanisms used with non-positive-displacement pumps, covering schematics, regulation devices and associated accessories that manage how liquids are moved without a fixed volume per stroke.
[F04D 29/584] cooling or heating the machine  F04D29/5846 ,  F04D29/5853  take precedence -- This CPC subclass addresses the cooling or heating of non-positive-displacement pumps, focusing on details of component parts and accessories related to such thermal management. It includes the sub-entries F04D29/5846 and F04D29/5853, which take precedence over other material.
[F04D 29/5846] cooling by injection -- This subclass covers cooling methods in which a fluid is injected into a machine or component to absorb heat, such as coolant injection into a hydraulic chamber or bearing. It relates to accessories and parts for non-positive-displacement pumps used in mechanical engineering cooling applications.
[F04D 29/5853] heat insulation or conduction -- The CPC entry F04D 29/5853 covers thermal-insulation and heat-conduction elements that are part of or added to non-positive-displacement pumps. It includes insulated housings, jackets, liners or conductive components designed to control temperature of pump casings, shafts or fluid pathways, helping maintain performance and protect against overheating or heat loss.
[F04D 29/586] specially adapted for liquid pumps -- F04D 29/586 designates components, parts or accessories that are specially adapted for liquid pumps, particularly non-positive-displacement types. It includes modifications, fittings, auxiliary devices or other elements intended to enhance pump performance, control, installation or maintenance.
[F04D 29/5866] Cooling at last part of the working fluid in a heat exchanger -- This subclass covers methods and devices for cooling the final portion of the working fluid as it leaves a heat exchanger in non-positive-displacement pumps, including specific components and accessories that remove heat at the outlet.
[F04D 29/5873] flow schemes and regulation thereto -- The subclass F04D 29/5873 covers the design and arrangement of flow schemes and their regulation for non-positive-displacement pumps, including the associated control components and accessories. It relates to how fluid paths are organized and managed within these pump systems.
[F04D 29/588] cooling or heating the machine  F04D29/5886 ,  F04D29/5893  take precedence -- The F04D 29/588 subclass covers provisions for cooling or heating a non-positive-displacement pump, such as heat-exchange elements, cooling jackets, thermal insulation or heating devices integrated with the pump or its accessories. It includes designs that regulate the pump's operating temperature to improve performance or protect components, and is subordinated to the more specific sub-subclasses 29/5886 and 29/5893 which take precedence.
[F04D 29/5886] cooling by injection -- F04D 29/5886 relates to the cooling of non-positive-displacement pumps by injecting a fluid-often water, oil or gas-into the pump housing, flow passages or impeller region to remove heat generated during operation. The classification covers the design of injection nozzles, distribution manifolds and associated control components that form part of the pump's cooling system.
[F04D 29/5893] heat insulation or conduction -- The CPC code F04D 29/5893 covers parts, details or accessories of non-positive-displacement pumps that provide thermal management, such as insulation to limit heat loss or conduction paths that affect pump temperature control.
[F04D 29/60] Mounting -- F04D 29/60 covers the mounting of non-positive-displacement pumps, including brackets, supports, base plates and other fixtures that secure the pump to a foundation, frame or adjacent equipment. It also addresses coupling elements that align and transmit forces between the pump and driven machinery.
[F04D 29/601] specially adapted for elastic fluid pumps -- F04D 29/601 covers details, component parts and accessories that are specially adapted for pumps handling elastic fluids such as water within the non-positive-displacement pump group. It includes adaptations that improve sealing, wear resistance or performance for these fluid types.
[F04D 29/602] Mounting in cavities -- F04D 29/602 covers the mounting of pump units or their parts within recessed cavities of a supporting structure, such as housings, frames or castings. It includes the design of fittings, brackets and sealing arrangements that secure the pump in a cavity and facilitate its integration into a larger system.
[F04D 29/603] means for positioning from outside -- F04D 29/603 covers mechanisms that allow the external adjustment or positioning of pump components, such as shafts, impellers or housings, without disassembly. It applies to non-positive displacement pumps and includes external actuators, levers or hydraulic devices used to set operating clearances or alignment.
[F04D 29/604] means for removing without depressurising the cavity -- F04D 29/604 covers components, parts or accessories that empty a pump cavity or similar chamber without lowering its pressure, such as venting or drainage devices that keep the cavity sealed from the external atmosphere. It falls under mechanical engineering, positive-displacement machines for liquids, specifically non-positive-displacement pumps.
[F04D 29/605] specially adapted for liquid pumps -- F04D 29/605 covers the details, component parts and accessories that are specially adapted for liquid pumps within the non-positive-displacement pump group, such as specialized housings, shafts, seals or mounting devices designed to improve or modify pump performance.
[F04D 29/606] Mounting in cavities -- The code F04D 29/606 covers the way components, parts or accessories of non-positive-displacement pumps are mounted within cavities or recesses of the pump housing. It includes methods and features for securing elements such as impellers, bearings or seals inside built-in pockets to ensure proper alignment, stability and fluid-tight operation. This classification lies under mechanical engineering for positive-displacement machines for liquids, specifically non-positive-displacement pump mounting.
[F04D 29/607] means for positioning from outside -- F04D 29/607 covers devices that allow a pump's internal components or the pump itself to be positioned or adjusted from an external location. These mechanisms are intended for non-positive-displacement pumps and include external levers, hydraulic or pneumatic actuators, and other accessories that enable remote alignment, setting or repositioning without opening the pump housing.
[F04D 29/608] means for removing without depressurizing the cavity -- F04D 29/608 relates to components that allow fluid to be removed from a pump or other cavity without lowering its pressure, such as venting, bypass or shrouding mechanisms used in non-positive-displacement pump designs. These devices are typically integrated as accessories or detailed parts of the pump assembly.
[F04D 29/62] of radial or helico-centrifugal pumps
[F04D 29/622] Adjusting the clearances between rotary and stationary parts -- F04D 29/622 relates to devices for adjusting clearances between rotating and stationary components in non-positive displacement pumps, such as pistons or gear pumps. It covers methods, components or accessories that fine-tune the spacing to improve performance, reduce wear and maintain efficiency.
[F04D 29/624] especially adapted for elastic fluid pumps -- This classification covers component parts or accessories specially adapted for elastic fluid pumps, a type of non-positive-displacement pump used in mechanical engineering for moving liquids. It includes items such as flexible housings, seals, or drive mechanisms that accommodate the fluid's elasticity.
[F04D 29/626] Mounting or removal of fans -- F04D 29/626 classifies the mounting and removal of fans used in non-positive-displacement pumps, covering the methods, fixtures and accessories employed to attach, position, detach or replace the fan unit within the pump system.
[F04D 29/628] especially adapted for liquid pumps -- F04D 29/628 covers details, component parts or accessories designed for non-positive-displacement pumps that are especially adapted for handling liquids, such as specialized housings, shafts, seals or flow-control devices used in these types of machines.
[F04D 29/64] of axial pumps -- F04D 29/64 designates the parts and accessories of axial pumps, a subtype of non-positive-displacement pumps that move fluid by axial flow. The code falls under mechanical engineering, positive displacement machines for liquids, and covers detailed components such as impellers, casings, shafts and related fittings.
[F04D 29/642] by adjusting the clearances between rotary and stationary parts -- This subclass concerns non-positive-displacement pumps and includes details of components or accessories that achieve pump control by adjusting the clearances between rotary and stationary parts, such as varying gaps in housings, shafts or bearings to influence fluid displacement.
[F04D 29/644] especially adapted for elastic fluid pumps -- F04D 29/644 covers components or accessories especially adapted for elastic-fluid pumps, a type of non-positive-displacement pump used in mechanical engineering. The class applies to designs that accommodate the compressibility of elastic liquids in hydraulic or pneumatic systems.
[F04D 29/646] Mounting or removal of fans -- F04D 29/646 covers the installation, securing, adjustment, and removal of fans that are used in non-positive-displacement pump systems, detailing the mounting hardware, brackets, shafts, and related accessories required to attach or detach the fan from the pump or driven equipment.
[F04D 29/648] especially adapted for liquid pumps -- F04D 29/648 is a Cooperative Patent Classification entry for details, component parts, or accessories especially adapted for liquid pumps, placed in the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under positive-displacement machines for liquids and the non-positive-displacement pumps subclass.
[F04D 29/66] Combating cavitation, whirls, noise, vibration or the like -- This CPC subclass covers features, components or accessories for non-positive-displacement pumps that are designed to reduce or eliminate cavitation, whirl formation, noise, vibration and similar undesirable phenomena during operation. It includes methods, devices and structural modifications that mitigate these effects in liquid-handling equipment.
[F04D 29/661] especially adapted for elastic fluid pumps -- F04D 29/661 covers components, details or accessories specially designed for elastic fluid pumps, which are a type of non-positive-displacement pump used to move liquids without a fixed displacement per cycle. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, positive-displacement machines for liquids, non-positive-displacement pumps.
[F04D 29/662] Balancing of rotors  compensating imbalance  G01M1/36
[F04D 29/663] Sound attenuation -- F04D 29/663 pertains to components or accessories of non-positive-displacement pumps that are designed to reduce noise generated during operation. It includes acoustic liners, vibration isolators, mufflers and other sound-attenuation measures applied to pump housings, impellers or associated piping. The classification is used for inventions aimed at quieter pump systems.
[F04D 29/664] by means of sound absorbing material -- F04D 29/664 classifies non-positive-displacement liquid pumps that employ sound-absorbing material in their construction. It covers designs, components, parts or accessories such as liners, casings or inserts intended to dampen noise and vibration generated during pump operation.
[F04D 29/665] by means of resonance chambers or interference -- F04D 29/665 relates to accessories or component parts of non-positive-displacement pumps that generate fluid flow by employing resonance chambers or interference effects. Such designs use acoustic or vibrational phenomena to induce pumping without moving mechanical elements.
[F04D 29/666] by means of rotor construction or layout, e.g. unequal distribution of blades or vanes -- This subclass covers details, components or accessories of non-positive-displacement pumps that move fluid by a rotor whose blades or vanes are distributed unevenly, using the rotor's construction or layout to affect flow characteristics.
[F04D 29/667] by influencing the flow pattern, e.g. suppression of turbulence -- F04D 29/667 concerns non-positive-displacement pumps whose components or accessories alter the liquid flow pattern, for example by suppressing turbulence, using flow straighteners, guide vanes or surface features. These flow-control measures aim to improve efficiency, reduce noise and limit wear.
[F04D 29/668] damping or preventing mechanical vibrations -- The CPC subclass F04D 29/668 covers details, components or accessories for non-positive-displacement pumps that are specifically designed to dampen or prevent mechanical vibrations, helping to minimise oscillations and noise in pump operation. This entry falls under mechanical engineering, positive-displacement machines for liquids, and non-positive-displacement pumps.
[F04D 29/669] especially adapted for liquid pumps  F04D29/18  takes precedence -- F04D 29/669 classifies details, component parts, or accessories that are especially adapted for liquid pumps, falling under non-positive-displacement pumps in the positive-displacement machines for liquids group. It is a narrower subclass of F04D29/18, which takes precedence.
[F04D 29/68] by influencing boundary layers -- F04D 29/68 covers features of non-positive-displacement pumps that modify the boundary layer of fluid flow, such as surface textures or flow guides, to improve performance. The classification pertains to details, component parts, or accessories that influence the boundary layer in pump operation.
[F04D 29/681] especially adapted for elastic fluid pumps -- F04D 29/681 covers devices that are specially designed for elastic-fluid pumps, such as those using flexible membranes or diaphragms to move liquids. It includes adaptations of pump components, accessories, or parts that enhance the performance or suitability of these non-positive-displacement pumping systems.
[F04D 29/682] by fluid extraction -- F04D 29/682 covers pumps that transfer liquid by extracting it rather than using a positive-displacement mechanism, such as suction-type or venturi pumps. The subclass pertains to the details, component parts, and accessories employed in these non-positive-displacement pump designs.
[F04D 29/684] by fluid injection -- This group covers non-positive-displacement pumps that move liquids by injecting fluid into the pumping chamber, detailing the associated components and accessories.
[F04D 29/685] Inducing localised fluid recirculation in the stator-rotor interface -- F04D 29/685 covers designs or components that create a localized recirculating flow of fluid at the interface between a stator and a rotor in non-positive displacement pumps. The recirculation is used to improve sealing, reduce friction, or enhance cooling within the pump. It pertains to details, parts, or accessories that implement this flow-control technique.
[F04D 29/687] Plasma actuators therefore -- This CPC subclass covers plasma actuators used as components or accessories for non-positive-displacement liquid pumps, describing devices that employ plasma discharge to modify fluid flow, pressure or pump performance.
[F04D 29/688] especially adapted for liquid pumps -- F04D 29/688 covers specialized details, component parts or accessories that are especially adapted for liquid pumps, specifically within the non-positive-displacement pump segment of mechanical engineering. It includes adaptations that enhance pump performance, installation, or maintenance.
[F04D 29/70] Suction grids -- F04D 29/70 covers suction grids, which are accessory or component parts fitted to non-positive-displacement pumps to regulate the flow of liquid into the pump, prevent the entry of solids, and improve inlet distribution. The class pertains to design, construction and mounting of such grids.
[F04D 29/701] especially adapted for elastic fluid pumps -- F04D 29/701 contains information on pumps that are specially adapted for handling elastic fluids such as oil, gasoline or other liquids that show elastic behaviour, focusing on their components, parts and accessories. It is a subclass of non-positive-displacement pumps within the broader category of positive-displacement machines for liquids.
[F04D 29/703] specially for fans, e.g. fan guards -- F04D 29/703 covers accessories and component parts designed for fans, particularly protective devices such as fan guards. It falls within the mechanical engineering section for positive-displacement machines for liquids, specifically the non-positive-displacement pump subgroup, and addresses details that enhance fan safety or performance.
[F04D 29/705] Adding liquids -- This subclass covers components or accessories that introduce fluid into a pump or hydraulic system, such as inlet fittings, feed lines, dosing or metering devices, and related mechanisms for adding liquids to the working medium.
[F04D 29/706] Humidity separation -- F04D 29/706 classifies parts or accessories for non-positive-displacement pumps that remove or separate humidity from the conveyed liquid, including condensate-removal devices, moisture separators and related de-watering elements.
[F04D 29/708] specially for liquid pumps -- F04D 29/708 pertains to the details, component parts or accessories specially designed for liquid pumps that operate on a non-positive-displacement principle, such as centrifugal or axial-flow pumps. It covers elements like housings, impellers, seals, bearings and mounting devices that are specific to these pump types.
[F04D 3/00] Axial-flow pumps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Axial flow type pumps for non elastic fluids.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Adaptation to specific liquids -> F04D7/00 - Priming of pumps -> F04D9/00
[F04D 3/005] with a conventional single stage rotor -- This sub-group of CPC F04D 3 covers axial-flow pumps that employ a conventional single-stage rotor, meaning the fluid is moved by one row of rotating impeller blades without additional stages or special features. It applies to designs where the rotor is typical in construction and operation.
[F04D 3/02] of screw type -- F04D 3/02 covers axial-flow pumps that move fluid by means of a rotating screw or helical rotor, generating a continuous axial thrust to convey liquid along the pump's axis. These screw-type pumps provide smooth, low-pulsation flow and are suited for handling viscous or delicate fluids at modest pressures.
[F04D 31/00] Other non-positive-displacement pumps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pumping liquids and elastic fluids at the same time, i.e. one pump handles both elastic and non elastic fluids together.
[F04D 33/00] Non-positive-displacement pumps with other than pure rotation, e.g. of oscillating type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-positive-displacement pumps with other than pure rotation. Some examples are mechanisms imitating the movement of a bird wing or a fish tale, or the movement produced by humans when using a hand fan.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Wave generators -> F04D35/00
[F04D 35/00] Pumps producing waves in liquids, i.e. wave-producers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-positive-displacement pumps for the generation of waves.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Wave generators for baths and tubs -> A47K3/10
[F04D 5/00] Pumps with circumferential or transverse flow -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Shear force pumps and pumps with circumferential or transverse flow, also shear force pumps, although they are generally of radial type.  **Glossary:** - Shear force pump: a pump where the energy is transferred to the liquid by friction forces
[F04D 5/001] Shear force pumps -- Shear force pumps are a type of non-positive-displacement pump that transfers liquids by creating a shear field, usually with rotating or oscillating elements that generate circumferential or transverse flow without directly contacting the fluid. They fall under mechanical engineering for positive-displacement machines for liquids and are coded F04D 5/001.
[F04D 5/002] Regenerative pumps  for elastic fluids  F04D23/008
[F04D 5/003] of multistage type -- F04D 5/003 covers multistage pumps that transfer liquid by circumferential or transverse flow, i.e., pumps that use a series of impellers or rotors to raise pressure in a single unit. These are non-positive-displacement devices used where higher heads are needed than with single-stage versions.
[F04D 5/005] the stages being radially offset -- This subclass pertains to pumps that move fluid by circumferential or transverse flow and have multiple stages arranged with a radial offset between them, meaning each stage is displaced sideways relative to the adjacent one to alter the flow path or improve performance.
[F04D 5/006] the stages being axially offset -- F04D 5/006 refers to non-positive-displacement pumps that move fluid by circumferential or transverse flow, where successive pumping stages are positioned with an axial offset relative to each other. This arrangement lets the fluid be transferred between stages without a straight-line alignment, improving efficiency or reducing pulsation.
[F04D 5/007] Details of the inlet or outlet -- F04D 5/007 covers the design and configuration of the inlet and outlet sections of circumferential or transverse-flow pumps, which are non-positive-displacement machines used to move liquids. It includes features such as shape, size, positioning and internal geometry of ports, as well as arrangements that affect flow direction and pressure rise.
[F04D 5/008] Details of the stator, e.g. channel shape -- F04D 5/008 covers the geometrical details of the stator in non-positive-displacement pumps that move fluid circumferentially or transversely, such as the shape, dimensions and configuration of the internal channels that guide the liquid flow. It specifies how stator design influences pump performance.
[F04D 7/00] Pumps adapted for handling specific fluids, e.g. by selection of specific materials for pumps or pump parts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Adaptations which make a pump suitable for a specific liquid.
[F04D 7/02] of centrifugal type -- F04D 7/02 designates centrifugal pumps that are specially adapted for handling particular fluids, for example by using materials or component designs that are compatible with corrosive, viscous or otherwise demanding liquids. The subclass falls under the CPC hierarchy Mechanical Engineering - Positive-Displacement Machines for Liquids - Non-Positive-Displacement Pumps.
[F04D 7/04] the fluids being viscous or non-homogenous -- The CPC subclass F04D 7/04 covers pumps that are designed for fluids that are viscous or non-homogeneous, requiring special construction or material choices to handle such fluids effectively. It falls under non-positive-displacement pumps within the mechanical engineering hierarchy.
[F04D 7/045] with means for comminuting, mixing stirring or otherwise treating -- F04D 7/045 classifies pumps that are specially adapted to handle particular liquids by using selected materials and that include internal mechanisms for comminuting, mixing, stirring or otherwise treating the fluid during pumping.
[F04D 7/06] the fluids being hot or corrosive, e.g. liquid metals
[F04D 7/065] for liquid metal -- F04D 7/065 covers pumps designed to handle liquid metal, featuring materials and constructions resistant to the high temperature and corrosive properties of molten metals, suitable for applications such as casting, cooling, or metal processing.
[F04D 7/08] the fluids being radioactive -- F04D 7/08 relates to pumps specially adapted for handling radioactive fluids, requiring the use of materials and designs that resist radiation-induced degradation, minimize contamination, and ensure safe operation of the pump and its components.
[F04D 9/00] Priming -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All adaptations of a pump in order to prime the pump, to avoid vapor lock or to avoid loss of prime
[F04D 9/001] Preventing vapour lock  F04D9/041  takes precedence -- The subclass F04D9/001 covers measures for preventing vapor lock in non-positive-displacement pumps, typically by priming or other techniques that keep the pump full of liquid and avoid gas pockets that stop flow. It is situated under mechanical engineering, positive-displacement machines for liquids, non-positive-displacement pumps, priming, and is superseded by the more specific subclass F04D9/041.
[F04D 9/002] by means in the very pump  F04D9/041  takes precedence -- F04D 9/002 covers pumps that achieve priming by means incorporated directly in the pump itself, such as internal priming devices, suction valves or design features that enable self-priming without external aid. This subclass is superseded by the more specific F04D 9/041.
[F04D 9/003] separating and removing the vapour
[F04D 9/004] Priming of not self-priming pumps -- Priming of not self-priming pumps covers methods and devices that remove air and fill a pump with liquid before operation, enabling the pump to start functioning despite lacking the ability to draw fluid on its own. It includes techniques such as manual filling, vacuum assistance, and external priming systems used in positive-displacement machines for liquids.
[F04D 9/005] by adducting or recycling liquid  F04D9/006  takes precedence
[F04D 9/006] by venting gas or using gas valves -- The F04D 9/006 entry covers non-positive-displacement pumps that achieve priming by venting air or other gases, or by employing gas-valued control devices. It is applied to machines that move liquids where removal of trapped gas is essential for smooth operation, such as certain centrifugal and axial flow pumps.
[F04D 9/007] Preventing loss of prime, siphon breakers  stopping of pumps  F04D15/02 -- F04D 9/007 covers measures that keep a pump from losing its prime, including siphon-breaker devices and mechanisms that automatically stop the pump when priming is lost, thereby maintaining reliable fluid flow and preventing damage.
[F04D 9/008] by means in the suction mouth, e.g. foot valves -- The subclass F04D 9/008 refers to priming techniques for non-positive-displacement pumps that use devices installed in the suction inlet, such as foot valves, to admit fluid while preventing reverse flow and thereby assist the pump in establishing suction.
[F04D 9/02] Self-priming pumps -- Self-priming pumps are a type of non-positive-displacement pump that can draw liquid into the casing and begin operation without external priming, typically using a sealed chamber or internal recirculation to maintain suction. They are classified under CPC section F04D 9/02, within mechanical engineering for positive-displacement machines for liquids.
[F04D 9/04] using priming pumps -- The F04D 9/04 subclass covers pumps that incorporate priming functions to enable start-up when the pump is initially empty, such as mechanisms that introduce fluid, create suction or pre-fill the pump chamber. It applies to non-positive-displacement pump designs that use these priming methods to ensure reliable operation.
[F04D 9/041] the priming pump having evacuating action  F04D9/043  and  F04D9/06  take precedence -- F04D 9/041 covers priming pumps that incorporate an evacuating action, i.e., devices that remove air from the suction line to facilitate fluid flow. It falls under non-positive-displacement pumps and supersedes subclassifications F04D9/043 and F04D9/06.
[F04D 9/042] and means for rendering its in operative -- F04D 9/042 deals with non-positive-displacement pumps, focusing on priming arrangements and the components or methods that enable the pump to be rendered inoperative. It includes designs that supply initial fluid to start the pump and mechanisms that shut the pump down or disconnect it from the drive. This subclass falls under the broader category of mechanical engineering for positive-displacement machines for liquids.
[F04D 9/043] the priming pump being hand operated or of the reciprocating type -- F04D 9/043 covers priming pumps that are manually operated or use a reciprocating mechanism to start fluid flow. These devices belong to the non-positive-displacement pump subgroup of positive-displacement machines for liquids within mechanical engineering.
[F04D 9/044] Means for rendering the priming pump inoperative -- F04D 9/044 relates to mechanisms that disable the priming function of a pump, preventing it from automatically drawing liquid when the pump starts. Such means are used in non-positive-displacement pumps and may include mechanical stops, shut-off valves or control devices that render the priming pump inoperative.
[F04D 9/045] the means being liquid level sensors -- F04D 9/045 covers the sensors and detection devices that determine the liquid level within non-positive displacement pumps, primarily to assist priming operations. It includes mechanisms such as float-type switches, pressure-based transducers, capacitive or ultrasonic level detectors that signal when the pump is filled or empty.
[F04D 9/046] the means being floats -- This subclass concerns non-positive-displacement pumps whose priming is achieved by a floating device, i.e., the means for introducing liquid into the pump chamber are floats that rise with the liquid level and drive the pumping action.
[F04D 9/047] the means being flow sensors -- This subclass covers devices that detect the rate or direction of liquid flow in non-positive-displacement pumps, including sensors mounted in the pump housing or downstream of the moving parts. It encompasses mechanical, electronic or hydraulic means that generate a flow-measurement signal for control or monitoring purposes.
[F04D 9/048] the means being outlet pressure sensors -- F04D 9/048 relates to outlet pressure sensors in non-positive-displacement pumps, describing means for detecting the pressure at the pump's discharge point to monitor performance, regulate flow or trigger safety functions.
[F04D 9/049] by operator interventions -- F04D 9/049 covers priming of non-positive-displacement pumps that must be performed by the operator rather than automatically. It includes methods, devices or procedures where human intervention initiates or assists the removal of air or fluid to start pumping.
[F04D 9/06] of jet type -- This subclass covers jet-type non-positive-displacement pumps, in which a high-velocity fluid jet creates suction and entrains the liquid to be moved without any moving mechanical parts. The jet-type arrangement is typical of ejector or eductor pumps used for fluids such as water, chemicals, or gases.
[F04D 9/065] the driving fluid being a gas or vapour, e.g. exhaust of a combustion engine -- The subclass concerns non-positive-displacement pumps that are primed or driven by a gas or vapour, such as using the exhaust stream of a combustion engine to provide the moving force for liquid displacement. It applies to designs where the driving fluid is gaseous rather than liquid, covering a range of gas-driven pump arrangements within mechanical engineering.
[F04F] PUMPING OF FLUID BY DIRECT CONTACT OF ANOTHER FLUID OR BY USING INERTIA OF FLUID TO BE PUMPED -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines inducing a flow of a pumped fluid wherein the displaced fluid is directly in contact with an inducing fluid different from said displaced fluid.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Containers for dispensing liquid or semi-liquid contents by internal gaseous pressure, i.e. aerosol containers comprising propellant -> B65D83/14 - Conveying materials in bulk through troughs, pipes, or tubes by floating the materials, or by flows of gas, liquid, or foam -> B65G53/00 - Evacuating by sorption or thermal means -> F04B37/00  **Glossary:** - Pump: Device for continuously raising, forcing, compressing, or exhausting fluid by mechanical means - Machine: Device that could equally be both an engine and a pump and not a device which is restricted to an engine or one which is restricted to a pump - Positive displacement pumps: Pumps using pistons or other mechanical members to displace a working fluid in a working chamber, the dynamic effect on the fluid being of minor importance - Positive displacement engines: The energy of a working fluid is transformed into mechanical energy, in which variations of volume created by the working fluid in a working chamber produce equivalent movement of mechanical members, e.g. pistons transmitting the energy, the dynamic effect of the fluid being of minor importance - Oscillating piston machine: A positive-displacement machine in which a fluid-engaging, work-transmitting member oscillates, e.g. a vane piston swinging back and forth about a fixed axis - Reciprocating piston: A fluid-engaging, work-transmitting member of an reciprocating-piston type machine or pump that slides alternately back and forth usually along a straight line or path - Rotary piston: A fluid engaging, work-transmitting member of a rotary-piston machine or pump that can completely rotate about a fixed axis or about an axis moving along a circular or similar orbit when operating, e.g. rotor having vanes or teeth - Free-piston machine: A linear , "crankless" reciprocating piston machine in which the piston motion is not controlled by a crankshaft but determined by the interaction forces from the fluid pressure in the working chamber, a rebound device (e.g. a piston in a closed cylinder) and a load device (e.g. a gas compressor or a linear alternator) - Rotary piston machine: Positive-displacement machine in which a liquid-engaging, work-transmitting member rotates about a fixed axis or about an axis moving along a circular or similar orbit, e.g. machine with a rotor having vanes or teeth - Cooperating members: The "oscillating piston" or "rotary piston" and another member, e.g., the working-chamber wall, which assists in the pumping action or machine's action - Movement of the cooperating members: To be interpreted as relative, so that one of the "cooperating members" may be stationary, even though reference may be made to its rotational axis, or both may move - Teeth or tooth equivalents: Includes lobes, projections or abutments - Internal axis type: The rotational axes of the inner and outer co-operating members remain at all times within the outer member, e.g., in a similar manner to that of a pinion meshing with the internal teeth of a ring gear - Working fluid: Driven fluid in a pump or driving or driven liquid in a machine. The working fluid can be in a compressible, gaseous state, e.g. steam, called elastic fluid, a liquid state, or a state where there is coexistence of elastic fluid and liquid state
[F04F 1/00] Pumps using positively or negatively pressurised fluid medium acting directly on the liquid to be pumped -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines in which the displacing power comprises a pressurising force subjected upon a fluid other than the fluid to be pumped. The fluids may e.g. be a liquid, or a gas, e.g. steam, or also a mixture of a liquid with solids.
[F04F 10/00] Siphons -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines which cause a liquid to flow above the surface of a reservoir, without pumps, powered by the fall of the liquid as it flows down a tube under the pull of gravity, and is discharged at a level lower than the surface of the reservoir.
[F04F 10/02] Gravity-actuated siphons -- F04F 10/02 covers gravity-actuated siphons, devices that use the weight of a liquid column to create a continuous flow from a higher to a lower reservoir without external power. The classification includes siphon designs that rely on direct contact between fluids and the inertia of the moving liquid to initiate and maintain suction.
[F04F 1/02] using both positively and negatively pressurised fluid medium, e.g. alternating -- The code F04F 1/02 refers to pumps using both positively and negatively pressurised fluid medium. This includes pumps with alternating pressure. It is a subset of positive-displacement machines for liquids.
[F04F 1/04] generated by vaporising and condensing -- F04F 1/04 covers pumps that create suction or pressure by vaporising a liquid and then condensing the vapour, using the resulting pressure differential to move another fluid. These devices rely on the inertia of the condensing vapour rather than mechanical moving parts, and are employed in applications such as steam-jet ejectors and vapour-compression pumping systems.
[F04F 1/06] the fluid medium acting on the surface of the liquid to be pumped -- F04F 1/06 covers pumps that use a fluid medium in direct contact with the surface of the liquid to be moved, employing the pressure of that medium to impart motion to the pumped fluid. It includes devices where a positively or negatively pressurised jet of water, air or another fluid acts on the liquid surface to achieve pumping.
[F04F 1/08] specially adapted for raising liquids from great depths, e.g. in wells -- This subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification covers pumps specifically designed to lift liquids from great depths such as oil or water wells. It includes devices that use positively or negatively pressurised fluid, direct-contact or inertial mechanisms, adapted for the high head and pressure conditions encountered in deep-well applications.
[F04F 1/10] of multiple type, e.g. with two or more units in parallel -- This subclass covers pumping devices that comprise two or more identical or different pump units operating in parallel to increase capacity or provide redundancy. It includes arrangements where the units share a common inlet or discharge line and are driven by the same or separate power sources. Used for positive or negative pressure fluid actuation.
[F04F 1/12] in series -- F04F 1/12 designates pump arrangements in which two or more positive-displacement units are connected in series, the discharge of one acting as the suction of the next to raise overall pressure. This configuration is used when a higher head is required than a single stage can provide.
[F04F 1/14] adapted to pump specific liquids, e.g. corrosive or hot liquids -- F04F 1/14 covers positive-displacement pumps that are specially adapted for pumping particular liquids, such as corrosive or high-temperature fluids. The design uses materials and construction methods that resist chemical attack or heat, ensuring reliable operation when handling these demanding media.
[F04F 1/16] characterised by the fluid medium being suddenly pressurised, e.g. by explosion -- This subclass covers pumps that generate a sudden pressure rise in a driving fluid, often by an explosive or rapid combustion event, to move another liquid. The pressurised fluid directly contacts the pumped fluid, transferring its kinetic energy, and is employed in impulse or shock-wave pump designs.
[F04F 1/18] the fluid medium being mixed with, or generated from the liquid to be pumped -- F04F 1/18 pertains to positive-displacement pumps in which the drive fluid is mixed with, or generated from, the liquid being pumped so that the moving medium is derived from the pumped fluid itself. Such designs use the pumped liquid as part of the power source, eliminating a separate driving fluid.
[F04F 1/20] specially adapted for raising liquids from great depths, e.g. in wells -- F04F 1/20 covers pumps specially adapted for lifting liquids from great depths such as well extraction, typically using positive or negative pressure fluid acting directly on the fluid to be pumped. These devices are designed for high-head conditions and often use inertial or direct-contact fluid mechanisms.
[F04F 13/00] Pressure exchangers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines for exchanging pressure energy between relatively high and relatively low pressure fluid systems, wherein the fluid being may include gases (steam), liquids and pumpable mixtures of liquids and solids. The exchange of pressure energy is used for transfer of energy from one fluid stream to another.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Processes of separation using semi-permeable membranes, e.g. osmosis, reverse-osmosis -> B01D61/02
[F04F 3/00] Pumps using negative pressure acting directly on the liquid to be pumped -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines where the delivery outlet for evacuation is exposed to negative fluid pressure inside a working chamber.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Siphons -> F04F10/00
[F04F 5/00] Jet pumps, i.e. devices in which flow is induced by pressure drop caused by velocity of another fluid flow -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Jet-pumps, injectors, ejectors, or thermocompressors using the Venturi effect of a converging-diverging nozzle to convert the pressure energy of an inducing fluid to velocity energy which creates a low pressure zone that draws in and displaces a suction fluid. The inducing fluid may be a liquid, or a gas, e.g. steam. The displaced suction fluid may be a gas,e.g. steam, or a liquid, a slurry, or a dust-laden gas stream.
[F04F 5/02] the inducing fluid being liquid -- This subclass covers jet-pump configurations in which a moving liquid acts as the inducing fluid, creating a pressure drop that draws and transports another liquid. It falls under positive-displacement liquid machines that pump by direct contact with another fluid or by using the fluid's inertia.
[F04F 5/04] displacing elastic fluids -- F04F 5/04 covers devices that move or pump elastic fluids such as gases or liquid polymers by employing jet-pump principles where a high-velocity secondary fluid creates a pressure drop that draws the elastic fluid. These pumps operate by direct contact between the driving fluid and the elastic fluid, using momentum transfer rather than mechanical moving parts.
[F04F 5/06] of rotary type -- F04F 5/06 covers rotary-type jet pumps, a class of positive-displacement machines that move fluid by creating a high-velocity jet of another fluid. The rotating element generates a pressure drop that entrains the pumped fluid, allowing transfer without direct mechanical contact between the moving parts and the fluid being pumped.
[F04F 5/08] the elastic fluid being entrained in a free falling column of liquid -- F04F 5/08 relates to jet-pump arrangements in which an elastic fluid is drawn into a free-falling column of liquid, the falling liquid's inertia creating a pressure drop that entrains the secondary fluid. This subclass addresses devices that rely on the momentum of one fluid to pump another without mechanical moving parts.
[F04F 5/10] displacing liquids, e.g. containing solids, or liquids and elastic fluids -- The F04F 5/10 subclass covers devices that displace liquids which may contain solid particles or exhibit elastic behaviour, using jet-pump principles where a high-velocity fluid creates a pressure drop that draws the target fluid along. It includes systems that handle mixtures of liquids and solids or elastic fluids, addressing the specific challenges of moving such heterogeneous or non-Newtonian media.
[F04F 5/12] of multi-stage type -- F04F 5/12 classifies multi-stage jet pumps, which raise the pressure of a fluid by passing it through successive stages where the flow is induced by the kinetic energy of a driving fluid. These devices belong to the positive-displacement machines for liquids that pump by direct contact or inertia of the fluid being moved.
[F04F 5/14] the inducing fluid being elastic fluid -- F04F 5/14 designates jet-pump arrangements in which the motive fluid is an elastic (compressible) medium such as a gas or steam. The inducing fluid's velocity creates a pressure drop that draws the secondary liquid into the pump, allowing displacement without moving mechanical parts. This subclass falls under mechanical-engineering positive-displacement machines that pump fluid by direct contact or inertia.
[F04F 5/16] displacing elastic fluids -- F04F 5/16 covers machines that use an elastic fluid, such as a gas or high-velocity liquid, to displace another fluid, typically in jet-pump arrangements where the moving fluid creates a pressure drop that draws in the pumped fluid. It falls under positive-displacement pumps that operate by direct contact of one fluid with another or by using the inertia of the fluid being pumped.
[F04F 5/18] for compressing -- This subclass of the cooperative patent classification covers jet-pump arrangements that provide compression of the pumped fluid by using the velocity or pressure drop of a driving fluid. It falls under positive displacement machines for liquids, specifically devices that induce flow through direct-contact or inertia effects.
[F04F 5/20] for evacuating -- F04F 5/20 designates jet-pump devices that generate suction by exploiting the velocity of a driving fluid to evacuate another fluid, typically using the pressure drop caused by the moving fluid to pump or remove liquids or gases.
[F04F 5/22] of multi-stage type -- F04F 5/22 designates multi-stage jet pumps, devices that raise the pressure of a liquid by conveying it through successive stages where a high-velocity driving fluid creates a pressure drop that draws and accelerates the pumped fluid. The staged arrangement allows a higher overall pressure increase than a single-stage jet pump.
[F04F 5/24] displacing liquids, e.g. containing solids, or liquids and elastic fluids -- This subclass covers devices that move or transport liquids that may contain solid particles or elastic fluids, using jet pump principles where a motive fluid creates a pressure drop to entrain and convey the target fluid. It includes systems that handle mixtures of liquids and suspended solids or non-Newtonian fluids.
[F04F 5/26] of multi-stage type -- F04F 5/26 covers multi-stage jet pumps, which move a fluid by using the kinetic energy of a driving fluid to create a pressure drop that draws the pumped fluid through a series of successive jet stages, each stage boosting the pressure and flow. This arrangement belongs to the class of positive-displacement machines that pump by direct contact with another fluid or by exploiting the inertia of the fluid being pumped.
[F04F 5/28] Restarting of inducing action -- F04F 5/28 covers methods and devices for restarting the inducing action of jet pumps, where a secondary fluid flow is generated by the kinetic energy of a primary fluid. It deals with restoring the pressure-drop-driven flow after it has stopped or been disrupted.
[F04F 5/30] with axially-slidable combining nozzle -- F04F 5/30 covers jet-pump arrangements in which the mixing or combining nozzle is mounted so it can slide axially relative to the pump housing. This axial movement allows adjustment of the position where the driving fluid accelerates and entrains the pumped fluid, improving flow control or efficiency.
[F04F 5/32] with hinged flap in combining nozzle -- F04F 5/32 covers jet pumps that incorporate a hinged flap in the combining nozzle, where the moving flap opens or closes in response to pressure changes to direct or regulate the flow of the driving and pumped fluids. The feature improves flow control, efficiency or back-flow protection in devices that use the inertia of one fluid to pump another.
[F04F 5/34] characterised by means for changing inducing fluid source -- F04F 5/34 covers jet pumps that incorporate features for modifying the source of the inducing fluid. This includes mechanisms such as variable nozzles, auxiliary streams or adjustable inlets that change the flow conditions of the driving fluid, thereby influencing the pumping action.
[F04F 5/36] characterised by using specific inducing fluid -- Jet pumps that create a low-pressure zone by directing a specific inducing fluid to entrain the liquid being pumped fall under CPC group F04F 5/36. This subclass covers positive-displacement machines that use a secondary fluid's flow or inertia to move the primary fluid, with the inducing fluid being defined by its particular properties or composition.
[F04F 5/38] the inducing fluid being mercury vapour -- F04F 5/38 refers to jet pumps that use mercury vapor as the inducing fluid; the high-velocity vapor creates a pressure drop which draws the liquid to be pumped into the flow.
[F04F 5/40] the inducing fluid being oil vapour -- F04F 5/40 covers jet pumps that create flow by exploiting the kinetic energy of a second fluid, specifically when the inducing fluid is oil vapour. These devices use the pressure drop produced by the high-velocity oil vapour to draw and move another liquid.
[F04F 5/42] characterised by the input flow of inducing fluid medium being radial or tangential to output flow -- F04F 5/42 covers jet pumps in which the driving fluid enters the pump body from a direction that is radial or tangential relative to the direction of the pumped fluid. The design relies on the momentum of the inducing fluid to create a pressure drop that draws the output fluid through the pump. Such arrangements are used where compact, low-pressure boost is required.
[F04F 5/44] Component parts, details, or accessories not provided for in, or of interest apart from, groups -- F04F 5/44 relates to jet-pump systems and covers component parts, details or accessories that are not otherwise listed in the main subclass, such as auxiliary fittings, seals, nozzles or special elements that are of interest on their own rather than as part of the overall pump assembly.
[F04F 5/46] Arrangements of nozzles -- This CPC subclass F04F 5/46 covers the geometric and functional configuration of nozzles in jet-pump devices, where a moving fluid creates a pressure drop that draws and accelerates another fluid. It deals with arrangements that affect flow direction, mixing, or pressure recovery in positive-displacement machines that pump liquids by direct contact or fluid inertia.
[F04F 5/461] Adjustable nozzles -- F04F 5/461 covers jet pumps that create flow by a pressure drop produced by a high-velocity fluid, specifically focusing on adjustable nozzles that can be varied to control the jet geometry and pumping characteristics. Such adjustable nozzles allow the pump's performance to be tuned for different operating conditions.
[F04F 5/462] with provisions for cooling the fluid -- F04F 5/462 pertains to jet pumps that incorporate features to cool the fluid being pumped, for example by passing it through heat-exchange surfaces, mixing it with a cooler stream, or using dedicated cooling channels within the pump housing. The cooling can be applied to the working fluid itself or to the secondary fluid that creates the suction effect.
[F04F 5/463] with provisions for mixing -- This subclass covers jet pump designs that incorporate features for mixing the pumped fluid with another fluid or additives during operation. It applies to devices where a high-velocity fluid creates a pressure drop that draws in the target fluid, and the design includes elements such as mixing chambers, baffles or impellers to promote blending.
[F04F 5/464] with inversion of the direction of flow -- This subclass covers jet pump arrangements where the induced fluid flow is reversed relative to the driving fluid, so the pumped fluid moves opposite to the direction of the primary jet. It applies to devices that exploit a pressure drop created by a high-velocity stream to draw in and discharge fluid in the opposite direction.
[F04F 5/465] with supersonic flow  mixing of supersonic fluids  B01F25/20 -- F04F 5/465 covers jet-pump devices in which the driving fluid attains supersonic velocity and the resulting pressure drop mixes two supersonic streams, enabling high-speed fluid transfer or propulsion. The subclass is situated under mechanical engineering, positive displacement machines for liquids, and specifically deals with pumping by direct contact or fluid inertia using supersonic-flow mixing.
[F04F 5/466] with a plurality of nozzles arranged in parallel -- The classification covers jet-pump designs that employ several nozzles placed side by side so that multiple fluid streams are generated in parallel. This arrangement creates simultaneous jets that increase the overall flow rate or pressure lift compared with a single-nozzle configuration.
[F04F 5/467] with a plurality of nozzles arranged in series -- The subclass F04F 5/467 covers jet-pump arrangements that employ several nozzles placed one after another in the flow path, using the velocity of a driving fluid through each nozzle to create successive pressure drops that draw and accelerate the pumped fluid.
[F04F 5/468] with provisions for priming -- Jet pumps that incorporate means to prime the device, allowing the pump to start drawing fluid when initially empty, are covered by this subclass. The priming provisions may include self-acting valves, auxiliary reservoirs, or mechanisms that create an initial suction or pressure differential to initiate flow. This enables reliable operation of displacement machines that rely on direct-contact fluid acceleration.
[F04F 5/469] for steam engines -- F04F 5/469 covers jet-pump arrangements that are employed in steam-engine systems. It falls under the positive-displacement machines for liquids, specifically where fluid flow is induced by the pressure drop created by the high-velocity stream of another fluid. The entry relates to using steam-driven jet pumps for moving liquids.
[F04F 5/48] Control -- F04F 5/48 covers control arrangements for jet pumps, which create fluid flow by using the kinetic energy or pressure drop of a driving fluid to move a second fluid. The class includes devices for regulating speed, pressure, flow rate, or start-stop operation of such pumps.
[F04F 5/50] of compressing pumps -- F04F 5/50 covers jet pumps that compress a fluid by using the kinetic energy or pressure drop created by a second fluid flowing at high velocity. The secondary fluid may be in direct contact with the pumped fluid or act through inertia, producing a suction effect that moves the primary fluid.
[F04F 5/52] of evacuating pumps -- F04F 5/52 covers evacuating pumps, a type of jet pump that creates a vacuum by accelerating a motive fluid to draw gas from a vessel. The device relies on direct contact between the high-velocity fluid and the gas to be evacuated, using inertia and pressure drop rather than mechanical moving parts.
[F04F 5/54] Installations characterised by use of jet pumps, e.g. combinations of two or more jet pumps of different type -- F04F 5/54 covers installations that incorporate jet pumps, including systems that combine two or more jet pumps of different types. Jet pumps create flow by a pressure drop from the kinetic energy of a driving fluid, and this subclass addresses arrangements where such pumps are used together for fluid handling.
[F04F 7/00] Pumps displacing fluids by using inertia thereof, e.g. by generating vibrations therein -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines inducing a flow of fluid by generating vibration in a resonatable fluid, e.g. by acoustic energy.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Loudspeakers -> H04R1/00
[F04F 7/02] Hydraulic rams -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines using the fluid hammer effect to develop pressure that allows a portion of the input fluid to reach a higher pressure or to be moved. The fluid hammer effect is a pressure surge or wave resulting when a fluid in motion is forced to stop or change direction suddenly, i.e. momentum change.
[F04F 9/00] Diffusion pumps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Momentum transfer pumps using a high speed jet of vapour to direct gas molecules in the pump down into the bottom of the pump and out through an exhaust, e.g. to obtain a high vacuum.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Pumps for evacuation by absorption or adsorption -> F04B37/02
[F04F 9/02] of multi-stage type -- The F04F 9/02 subclass refers to diffusion pumps that employ a multi-stage arrangement, using successive vapor jets to incrementally raise the compression ratio and improve vacuum performance. Multi-stage designs allow the pump to handle lower pressures more efficiently than single-stage configurations.
[F04F 9/04] in combination with fore pumps, e.g. use of isolating valves -- F04F 9/04 covers diffusion vacuum pumps that are used together with a fore-pump, such as a rotary or scroll pump, often employing isolating valves to separate the stages. The classification addresses arrangements where the fore-pump prepares the inlet pressure and the diffusion pump provides high-vacuum performance.
[F04F 9/06] Arrangement of vapour traps -- F04F 9/06 covers the design and positioning of vapour traps in diffusion pumps, which are vacuum-generation devices that use a high-speed jet of oil vapour to capture and transport gas molecules. The sub-class addresses features that capture condensing vapour to protect the pump and improve vacuum quality.
[F04F 9/08] Control -- F04F 9/08 covers control features for diffusion pumps, a type of positive-displacement vacuum pump that moves fluid by direct contact with a high-velocity jet or by inertia of the pumped fluid. The subclass includes mechanisms for regulating pump speed, pressure, flow, temperature and start-stop sequencing.
[F04F 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines inducing a flow of fluid by means not provided for in other groups of this subclass, e.g. by magnetohydrodynamic effects, by electrostatic effects, or by electro-osmotic flow effects.
[F05] INDEXING SCHEMES RELATING TO ENGINES OR PUMPS IN VARIOUS SUBCLASSES OF CLASSES -- F05 is a Cooperative Patent Classification code within the Mechanical Engineering section that groups indexing schemes used to organize information about engines or pumps across a range of related subclasses, facilitating systematic searching and analysis of these devices in patent databases.
[F05B] INDEXING SCHEME RELATING TO WIND, SPRING, WEIGHT, INERTIA OR LIKE MOTORS, TO MACHINES OR ENGINES FOR LIQUIDS COVERED BY SUBCLASSES -- F05B is a CPC subclass that groups indexing schemes for machines or engines that move liquids using wind, spring, weight, inertia or similar motor principles. It falls under mechanical engineering and covers the classification of such power sources and their related devices.
[F05B 2200/00] Mathematical features -- The CPC subgroup F05B 2200/00, titled "Mathematical features," contains patents that focus on the use of mathematical methods, calculations, modelling or analysis for wind, spring, weight, inertia or similar motors that drive liquid-handling machines or engines. It covers theoretical formulations, simulations and optimisation techniques applied to the design, control or performance evaluation of these fluid-powered systems.
[F05B 2200/10] Basic functions -- F05B 2200/10 concerns the basic operating principles of wind, spring, weight, inertia or similar motors that drive machines or engines handling liquids. It indexes patents describing fundamental functions such as energy storage, release mechanisms and power transmission, without detailing particular device designs. This entry helps classify inventions that focus on the core functional concepts of these motor types.
[F05B 2200/11] Sum -- This subclass covers machines or engines for liquids that are driven by the combined action of wind, springs, weights, inertia or similar motors, detailing how multiple motive forces are summed to operate a pump. It falls within the mechanical-engineering indexing of engines or pumps and focuses on the aggregate use of such power sources.
[F05B 2200/12] Subtraction -- F05B 2200/12 designates the use of subtraction operations in the mathematical modelling, control or analysis of machines and engines for liquids that are driven by wind, springs, weights, inertia or similar motors. It falls within the mechanical-engineering indexing scheme for engines or pumps and addresses how difference calculations are applied to the performance or regulation of such fluid-handling devices.
[F05B 2200/13] Product -- F05B 2200/13 classifies the physical product generated by machines or engines that use wind, spring, weight, inertia or similar motors to move liquids. It applies to the end item-such as a pump, hydraulic unit or fluid-handling device-produced by these mechanically powered systems. This entry falls under mechanical engineering and indexing schemes for engines and pumps.
[F05B 2200/14] Division -- F05B 2200/14 is a CPC subclass in the mechanical-engineering section that deals with arrangements for dividing the output of wind, spring, weight, inertia or similar motors that drive liquid-handling machines. It covers devices and methods that split, distribute or partition the mechanical power or fluid flow produced by such engines, enabling multiple downstream operations or separate streams.
[F05B 2200/15] Inverse -- The CPC code F05B 2200/15 covers machines or engines for liquids that are driven by wind, springs, weights, inertia or comparable sources, where the operating principle relies on an inverse action of the fluid or mechanical movement. It classifies devices that generate output power by using the fluid-driven motion in a reversed or opposite manner to conventional pump or engine operation.
[F05B 2200/20] Special functions -- F05B 2200/20 is a CPC sub-group within the mechanical engineering section that deals with indexing schemes for wind, spring, weight, inertia or similar motors applied to machines or engines handling liquids. The entry groups patents that describe special functions or auxiliary control features of such motor-driven fluid devices, such as timing, regulation, or unconventional actuation methods.

It falls under mechanical engineering indexing schemes for engines or pumps that employ wind, spring, weight, or inertial drives. [F05B 2200/32] even -- The code F05B 2200/32 falls under the CPC class for positive-displacement pumps and identifies an indexing scheme for liquid-pumping machines powered by wind, spring, weight, inertia or similar motors, where the pump is characterised by an even, i.e., continuously uniform, operating behaviour or output.

[F05B 2210/00] Working fluid -- F05B 2210/00 refers to the working fluid used in machines or engines that move liquids, including hydraulic, pneumatic and other fluid-driven power sources. It categorises the substance that transmits energy, such as oils, water, gases or specialized liquids, within the broader indexing scheme for wind, spring, weight, inertia or like motors in mechanical engineering.
[F05B 2210/10] Kind or type -- F05B 2210/10 is an indexing subgroup in the CPC for mechanical engineering that classifies liquid-handling machines according to the kind or type of wind, spring, weight, inertia or similar motor that drives them. It does not describe a technical feature itself but serves to group patents by the nature of the driving motor within the broader F05B category for pumps and related engines.
[F05B 2210/11] liquid, i.e. incompressible -- F05B 2210/11 covers patents that involve a liquid used as the working fluid in machines or engines for liquids, specifically where the fluid is treated as incompressible. It applies to hydraulic, pump-type or other liquid-driven mechanisms indexed under mechanical engineering schemes for engines, pumps and inertia-type motors.
[F05B 2210/12] gaseous, i.e. compressible -- F05B 2210/12 covers machines that use a gaseous, compressible working fluid, such as wind-driven or inertia-type motors applied to liquid-handling engines. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering class for indexing schemes relating to engines or pumps and specifically to the subcategory for wind, spring, weight, inertia or like motors.
[F05B 2210/13] mixed, e.g. two-phase fluid -- The code refers to machines or engines that use a mixed or two-phase working fluid, such as a liquid-vapor combination, to transmit or convert energy in hydraulic, pneumatic or similar power systems. It belongs to the indexing scheme for wind, spring, weight, inertia or comparable motors applied to liquid-driven devices.
[F05B 2210/132] Pumps with means for separating and evacuating the gaseous phase -- This CPC subclass concerns pumps that incorporate internal or external devices for separating entrained gas from the working fluid and expelling it, such as gas separators, venting chambers or deaerators, enabling continuous liquid delivery in hydraulic or fluid-power systems.
[F05B 2210/14] Refrigerants with particular properties, e.g. HFC-134a -- F05B 2210/14 pertains to refrigerants selected for distinct thermodynamic, safety or environmental traits-such as the hydro-fluorocarbon HFC-134a-used as the working fluid in liquid-handling machines or engine systems.
[F05B 2210/16] Air or water being indistinctly used as working fluid, i.e. the machine can work equally with air or water without any modification -- F05B 2210/16 covers machines-such as compressors, pumps, or turbines-designed to run on either air or water as the working fluid without any structural or operational changes, allowing the device to switch between gas and liquid media freely.
[F05B 2210/18] Air and water being simultaneously used as working fluid -- F05B 2210/18 covers machines that employ both air and water at the same time as the working fluid, such as engines or pumps that combine the two to produce motion or power. It is part of the indexing scheme for wind, spring, weight, inertia or like motors applied to liquid-handling equipment.
[F05B 2210/20] Properties -- F05B 2210/20 is a CPC subclass that covers the properties of the working fluid used in liquid-driven machines or engines, especially those powered by wind, springs, weights, inertia or similar motors. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering group for indexing schemes relating to engines and pumps and focuses on how fluid characteristics affect performance.
[F05B 2210/30] Flow characteristics -- F05B 2210/30 covers the study and measurement of flow characteristics of the working fluid in hydraulic or fluid-power machines, such as pumps, motors and related engines. It includes analysis of velocity, turbulence, pressure distribution and other fluid-dynamic parameters that affect performance and efficiency. This subclass is part of the broader indexing scheme for engines and pumps within mechanical engineering.
[F05B 2210/301] with Mach-number kept constant along the flow -- F05B 2210/301 covers fluid machines in which the Mach number of the working fluid is deliberately kept constant throughout the flow path, such as certain compressors, turbines or pumps designed for high-speed gas streams. The classification falls under mechanical engineering indexing schemes for engines and pumps, specifically for wind, spring, weight or inertia-type motors operating with liquids.
[F05B 2210/302] Pressure kept constant along the flow -- In the CPC, class F05B covers machines that move liquids, such as pumps, compressors and hydraulic power units. Subgroup 2210/302 relates to designs where the pressure of the working fluid is kept essentially constant along the flow path, often by control devices or specific circuit arrangements.
[F05B 2210/40] Flow geometry or direction -- F05B 2210/40 covers the configuration and orientation of fluid flow paths in machines that use wind, spring, weight, inertia or similar motors to drive liquids, such as pumps and engines. It addresses the shape of channels, ducts, or passages that direct the working fluid, including bends, straight sections and flow-guiding elements.
[F05B 2210/401] upwards due to the buoyancy of compressed air -- The CPC subclass F05B 2210/401 covers machines that create upward motion by exploiting the buoyancy of compressed air as the working fluid, such as air-lift pumps or buoyancy-driven propulsion devices for liquid handling. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering indexing scheme for wind, spring, weight, inertia or similar motor concepts applied to engines or machines that move liquids.
[F05B 2210/402] Axial inlet and radial outlet -- F05B 2210/402 classifies machines, such as pumps or liquid-driven engines, in which the working fluid is introduced along the axis of the rotor and discharged perpendicular to that axis. The entry falls under the mechanical-engineering section that indexes wind, spring, weight, inertia or similar motor concepts for liquid-handling equipment.
[F05B 2210/403] Radial inlet and axial outlet -- This subgroup covers machines in which the working fluid enters the rotor or impeller from the radial direction and leaves along the axis, a flow arrangement commonly found in certain centrifugal and axial-flow pumps or turbines. It applies to hydraulic devices that use wind-, spring-, weight-or inertia-driven drives for moving liquids.
[F05B 2210/404] bidirectional, i.e. in opposite, alternating directions -- F05B 2210/404 classifies liquid-driven engines or machines whose working fluid moves alternately in opposite directions, i.e., bidirectional or alternating flow, within the indexing scheme for wind, spring, weight, inertia or similar motors.

[F05B 2220/60] making use of surplus or waste energy -- F05B 2220/60 covers methods and devices that capture and reuse surplus or waste energy in fluid-power machines, such as hydraulic or pneumatic systems, to improve overall efficiency. This includes regenerative braking, heat recovery, or other schemes that feed recovered energy back into the system.
[F05B 2220/602] with energy recovery turbines -- F05B 2220/602 covers machines that incorporate an energy-recovery turbine as part of a wind, spring, weight, inertia or similar motor driving a liquid-handling engine or pump. The classification is used for devices that capture kinetic energy from the motor's motion and convert it back into useful power, improving overall efficiency of the system.

This subclass is part of the broader indexing scheme for engines and pumps within mechanical engineering.

[F05B 2230/236] Diffusion bonding -- F05B 2230/236 concerns diffusion bonding, a solid-state joining method where components are pressed together at elevated temperature below their melting point to achieve metallurgical bonds. This entry is used by both the EPO and USPTO to catalogue inventions involving wind-driven power generation equipment.
[F05B 2240/211] with vertical axis -- The CPC subclass F05B 2240/211 covers machines or engines for liquids that use a rotating element oriented about a vertical axis, such as vertical-shaft pumps, turbines or wind-driven devices designed to move or pressurize fluids.
[F05B 2240/212] of the Darrieus type -- Group F05B2240/212 is impacted by reclassification into group F03D3/011 . Groups F05B2240/212 and F03D3/011 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F05B 2240/213] of the Savonius type -- Group F05B2240/213 is impacted by reclassification into group F03D3/009 . Groups F05B2240/213 and F03D3/009 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F05B 2240/214] of the Musgrove or "H"-type -- Group F05B2240/214 is impacted by reclassification into group F03D3/011 . Groups F05B2240/214 and F03D3/011 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F05B 2240/215] of the panemone or "vehicle ventilator" type -- Group F05B2240/215 is impacted by reclassification into group F03D3/009 . Groups F05B2240/215 and F03D3/009 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F05B 2240/216] of the anemometer type -- Group F05B2240/216 is impacted by reclassification into group F03D3/009 . Groups F05B2240/216 and F03D3/009 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F05B 2240/217] of the crossflow- or "Banki"- or "double action" type -- F05B 2240/217 classifies pumps that operate on the cross-flow, Banki or double-action principle, where liquid passes twice through the impeller producing two discharge streams. These devices are typically low-head, high-capacity machines used for moving liquids in applications such as irrigation, wastewater handling or industrial processing.
[F05B 2240/218] with horizontally hinged vanes -- Group F05B2240/218 is impacted by reclassification into groups F03D3/066 , F03D3/067 and F03D3/068 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[F05B 2240/221] with horizontal axis -- F05B 2240/221 designates pumps that operate with a rotor rotating about a horizontal axis. It falls under the mechanical engineering group for indexing schemes relating to engines or pumps, specifically those using wind, spring, weight, inertia or similar motors for liquid handling.
[F05B 2240/2211] of the multibladed, low speed, e.g. "American farm" type -- F05B 2240/2211 classifies multiblade, low-speed devices such as the "American farm"-type propellers used in wind, spring, weight or inertia motors that drive machines or engines handling liquids, fitting within the mechanical-engineering indexing scheme for engine-related indexing.
[F05B 2240/2212] perpendicular to wind direction -- F05B 2240/2212 is a subclass of mechanical engineering that indexes machines or engines for liquids using wind, spring, weight or inertia, specifically where the wind force is applied perpendicular to its direction. This classification groups devices that harness wind energy in a cross-wind arrangement to drive liquid-handling equipment.
[F05B 2240/2213] and with the rotor downwind from the yaw pivot axis -- This CPC subgroup deals with machines or engines for liquids in which the rotor is positioned on the downwind side of the yaw-pivot axis, a layout used in wind-driven pumps or turbines to benefit from cleaner airflow, improve efficiency and reduce turbulence around the pivot.
[F05B 2240/231] driven by aerodynamic lift effects -- The CPC code F05B 2240/231 designates machines or engines for liquids that are powered by aerodynamic lift, meaning the motion is obtained from lift forces generated by airflow rather than direct mechanical contact. It falls under mechanical engineering indexing for wind, spring, weight or inertia-type motors.
[F05B 2240/232] driven by drag -- F05B 2240/232 covers machines whose propulsion is provided by drag forces rather than rotating shafts, such as wind- or water-driven devices that move a fluid by the resistance of the moving body. It falls within the mechanical-engineering indexing scheme for engines and pumps, specifically for wind, spring, weight, inertia or similar motors used to operate liquid-handling machinery.
[F05B 2240/24] for turbines -- F05B 2240/24 designates turbines used as components of machines or engines for liquids that are powered by wind, spring, weight, inertia or similar motors. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering and indexes turbine arrangements that convert those forces into fluid motion.
[F05B 2240/241] of impulse type -- F05B 2240/241 covers impulse type mechanisms that use wind, spring, weight, inertia or similar energy sources to drive machines or engines handling liquids. It falls under mechanical engineering indexing schemes for engines and pumps, specifically for liquid moving devices powered by impulse actions.
[F05B 2240/2411] Pelton type -- F05B 2240/2411 is a CPC subclass for Pelton type hydraulic turbines, which are impulse water-wheel devices that convert the kinetic energy of a high-velocity jet into rotary motion. It falls under the mechanical-engineering group for indexing schemes relating to engines or pumps, specifically the scheme for wind, spring, weight, inertia or similar motors applied to machines handling liquids.
[F05B 2240/242] of reaction type
[F05B 2240/243] of the Archimedes screw type -- F05B 2240/243 covers liquid-moving machines that employ the Archimedes-screw principle, where a helical rotor transports fluid as it turns. The classification applies to engines or pumps powered by wind, springs, weights, inertia or comparable sources that drive such screw-type devices for moving liquids.
[F05B 2240/244] of the cross-flow, e.g. Banki, Ossberger type -- This subgroup covers cross-flow water turbines of the Banki or Ossberger design, where water enters the runner radially and exits axially, converting flow energy to mechanical power. It is used for small to medium hydro-electric installations and falls under the indexing scheme for wind-spring-weight-inertia type motors in liquid-driven machines.

Groups F05B2240/30 , F03D1/0641 and F03D1/0685 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.

[F05B 2240/37] Multiple rotors -- F05B 2240/37 covers machines that employ two or more rotors in a wind, spring, weight, inertia or comparable motor driving a liquid-handling engine, such as pumps or compressors, where the rotors work together to transfer energy or fluid. The classification is used for designs that synchronise multiple rotating elements within a single drivetrain to improve performance or balance.
[F05B 2240/372] coaxially arranged -- The F05B 2240/372 entry covers fluid-driven machines in which the driving and driven elements are aligned on the same axis, such as pumps, turbines or compressors with a coaxial motor arrangement. It pertains to designs that use wind, spring, weight, inertia or similar sources to power liquid-handling equipment, emphasizing the shared-axis configuration of the components.
[F05B 2240/374] Auxiliary rotors attached to blades of main rotor -- F05B 2240/374 covers auxiliary rotors mounted on the blades of a main rotor, used to modify airflow, increase lift or balance forces in devices such as wind-driven machines, helicopters or liquid-pumping engines. The feature is indexed under mechanical engineering schemes for wind, spring, weight, inertia or similar motor types.
[F05B 2240/40] Use of a multiplicity of similar components -- The CPC subgroup F05B 2240/40 covers inventions that use a large number of identical or substantially similar components-such as blades, pistons, rotors or other elements-within a liquid-handling engine, pump or similar machine to achieve the desired motion or flow.
[F05B 2240/50] Bearings -- Group F05B2240/50 is impacted by reclassification into groups F03D80/701 and F03D80/703 . Groups F05B2240/50 , F03D80/701 and F03D80/703 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
